topics.title: Engine Nomenclature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engine Nomenclature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ENGINE NOMENCLATURE** is the standardized system of technical terms, geometric parameters, dimensional ratios, and operational parameters used to completely identify, classify, and describe the constructional and functional characteristics of an internal/external combustion engine. It is the universal "language" of engine engineering that allows precise communication of an engine's physical size, thermodynamic capability, mechanical configuration, and operational behaviour without ambiguity.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBMUT404)**, Engine Nomenclature forms the foundational vocabulary upon which all subsequent modules — *Fuel Supply, Combustion, Cooling, Lubrication, and Transmission* — are built. Mastery of these terms is mandatory before a student can analyze thermodynamic cycles, perform volumetric/speed calculations, or interpret manufacturer specifications.

### 1.1.1 Broad Classification of Engines (The Root of Nomenclature)

Every engine is named/classified along **five orthogonal axes**:

| # | Classification Axis | The Two Primary Categories |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Combustion Location** | Internal Combustion (IC) Engine vs. External Combustion (EC) Engine |
| 2 | **Cycle of Operation** | 2-Stroke Cycle vs. 4-Stroke (Otto/Atkinson/Miller) Cycle |
| 3 | **Ignition Method** | Spark Ignition (SI) vs. Compression Ignition (CI) |
| 4 | **Thermodynamic Cycle** | Otto Cycle, Diesel Cycle, Dual Cycle, Sabathe Cycle, Atkinson, Miller |
| 5 | **Mechanical Arrangement** | Inline, V-type, Flat/Boxer, Radial, Wankel (Rotary) |

> [!NOTE]
> A complete engine nomenclature statement, as used in industry datasheets, follows the syntax: *"<Fuel Type> + <Cycle> + <Ignition> + <Arrangement> + <Displacement> + <Aspiration> Engine"*.
> **Example:** *Petrol, 4-Stroke, SI, Inline-4, 2.0 L, Turbocharged Engine.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

> [!TIP]
> **The "Engine as a Building" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are buying a house. Before moving in, you would ask: *How many rooms (cylinders)? What is the floor area (displacement)? How tall are the ceilings (bore)? How many floors (stroke ratio)? Is it built of brick or wood (construction type: OHV/DOHC)?* You would NOT buy a house described only as "a big building." You need **nomenclature** — a precise vocabulary.
>
> Similarly, when a manufacturer publishes "a **1.5 L, Inline-3, DOHC, Turbo-Diesel**," every term communicates a *quantitative* and *qualitative* engineering truth. **Engine Nomenclature is the dictionary that translates these alphanumeric codes into the engineering reality of metal, air, fuel, and fire.**

### 1.2.1 Why Engine Nomenclature is the "First Gear" of Engine Studies

- **Communication:** A single term (e.g., "bore") conveys an entire geometric reality.
- **Calculation Input:** All thermodynamic equations (compression ratio, mean effective pressure, indicated power) require *nomenclature-derived* parameters as inputs.
- **Diagnostics:** A mechanic hearing "low compression in cylinder #2" instantly knows to check piston rings, valves, or head gasket of *that specific cylinder*, thanks to firing-order nomenclature.
- **Comparison:** Engineers compare engines using a common nomenclature ("the 2.0 TSI produces more specific output than the 1.8 TSI").

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Engine Nomenclature — Terms Every KTU Student Must Know

### 1.3.1 Geometric (Dimensional) Nomenclature

| Term | Formal Definition | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Bore ($D$)** | The internal diameter of the engine cylinder. | **mm** or **cm** |
| **Stroke ($L$)** | The linear distance travelled by the piston between Top Dead Centre (TDC) and Bottom Dead Centre (BDC). | **mm** or **cm** |
| **TDC (Top Dead Centre)** | The upper-most position of the piston; also called *Outer Dead Centre (ODC)*. | — |
| **BDC (Bottom Dead Centre)** | The lower-most position of the piston; also called *Inner Dead Centre (IDC)*. | — |
| **Clearance Volume ($V_c$)** | The volume of the combustion chamber above the piston when at TDC. | **cm³** (cc) or **L** |
| **Swept Volume ($V_s$)** | The volume displaced by the piston in one stroke, $\pi D^2 L / 4$. | **cm³** or **L** |
| **Total Cylinder Volume ($V_t$)** | $V_c + V_s$. The volume when piston is at BDC. | **cm³** or **L** |
| **Engine Displacement (ED)** | Total swept volume of all cylinders. | **L** or **cc** |

### 1.3.2 Ratio Nomenclature (Dimensionless)

| Term | Definition | Typical SI Engine | Typical CI Engine |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compression Ratio ($r_k$ or $r_c$)** | Ratio of total volume to clearance volume. | 8 : 1 to 12 : 1 | 14 : 1 to 22 : 1 |
| **Stroke-to-Bore Ratio ($L/D$)** | Geometric descriptor of engine "character." | 0.8 – 1.0 (modern: <1 = "short stroke") | 1.0 – 1.3 (long stroke) |

### 1.3.3 Operational / Crank-Angle Nomenclature

- **Crank Angle ($\theta$):** Angle through which the crankshaft has rotated from a reference datum (usually TDC firing of cylinder #1).
- **Firing Interval:** Crank angle between consecutive power strokes. For a 4-stroke engine, it is $720°/N$ (N = number of cylinders). For a 2-stroke engine, it is $360°/N$.
- **Firing Order:** The specific sequence in which the cylinders deliver their power stroke. *Example: 1-3-4-2 for a typical Inline-4.*
- **Valve Overlap:** Crank angle (in degrees) during which both intake and exhaust valves are open simultaneously (occurs near TDC exhaust).
- **Ignition Advance / Retard:** Crank-angle position of the spark before/after TDC.

### 1.3.4 Constructional Nomenclature (Valvetrain)

- **OHV — Overhead Valve (Pushrod):** Valves in head, camshaft in block, driven by pushrods.
- **SOHC — Single Overhead Camshaft:** One camshaft per cylinder bank, mounted in the cylinder head.
- **DOHC — Dual Overhead Camshaft:** Two camshafts per bank (one for intake, one for exhaust valves).
- **VVT — Variable Valve Timing:** Continuous variation of valve opening/closing events with engine speed/load.
- **VVC — Variable Valve Control (Lift):** Variation in valve lift magnitude.

### 1.3.5 Performance Nomenclature (Will be expanded in later modules)

- **Indicated Power (IP):** Power developed inside the cylinder from gas pressure.
- **Brake Power (BP):** Power actually available at the crankshaft flywheel.
- **Friction Power (FP):** $FP = IP - BP$.
- **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$):** $BP / IP$.
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC):** Fuel mass per unit brake power per hour (kg/kWh).
- **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP):** A fictitious constant pressure that, if acted on the piston through the entire stroke, would produce the same work as the actual variable pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 1 of PBMUT404 specifically expects the student to *define, derive, and inter-relate* the geometric and ratio nomenclature (bore, stroke, displacement, clearance volume, compression ratio, $L/D$ ratio). The constructional and operational nomenclature is introduced here but developed in detail in Modules 2, 3, and 4.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Logical Structure of Engine Nomenclature

Engine nomenclature, although consisting of many terms, can be organized into **four conceptual layers** (from static geometry → dynamic operation):

> **Layer 1 — Pure Geometry (Static):** Bore, Stroke, Crank Radius, Connecting Rod Length, Piston Pin Offset.
> **Layer 2 — Volume Parameters (Static-derived):** Clearance Volume, Swept Volume, Total Cylinder Volume, Engine Displacement.
> **Layer 3 — Dimensionless Ratios (Static-comparative):** Compression Ratio, Stroke/Bore Ratio, Rod/Stroke Ratio.
> **Layer 4 — Crank-Angle / Time-Domain (Dynamic):** Firing Order, Valve Timing Diagram, Ignition Timing, Crank-angle resolution.

This hierarchical view is essential because **each layer is built from the layer below it**, and KTU examination questions test the student's ability to *move fluidly up and down this hierarchy.*

---

## 2.2 Deconstruction of Key Nomenclature Parameters

### 2.2.1 Bore ($D$) and Stroke ($L$) — The Two "Master" Dimensions

> **Why are these the master dimensions?**
> Because *every other volume and ratio* in the engine is mathematically derivable from $D$ and $L$ (plus the clearance volume, $V_c$, which is set by the head-gasket thickness and piston dish volume).

- **Bore ($D$):** Sets the *cross-sectional area* $A = \pi D^2/4$ of the cylinder, which is the area on which gas pressure acts.
- **Stroke ($L$):** Sets the *length of the lever arm* through which that pressure produces torque. Torque per cylinder $\approx$ (Mean Effective Pressure) $\times$ (Area) $\times$ (Stroke / 2).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** Increasing the **bore** increases the *area* (square law) and favours *high-rpm breathing*. Increasing the **stroke** increases the *leverage* and favours *low-end torque* but limits engine speed (higher piston mean velocity).

### 2.2.2 Swept Volume ($V_s$)

> **Definition:** The volume through which the piston sweeps as it moves from TDC to BDC.

**Derivation logic:**
Imagine the cylinder as a perfect right-circular cylinder of diameter $D$ and height equal to the stroke $L$. The piston is a flat disc of area $A = \pi D^2/4$ moving a linear distance $L$. By the volume formula for a cylinder, $V_s = A \times L$.

**Real-world meaning:** A larger $V_s$ per cylinder means more air–fuel mixture can be inducted per cycle, which generally means more power (subject to volumetric efficiency).

### 2.2.3 Clearance Volume ($V_c$)

> **Definition:** The small volume that remains above the piston at TDC and below the cylinder head.

**What constitutes $V_c$?**
1. The volume machined into the cylinder head (combustion chamber).
2. The volume of the head-gasket bore above the piston.
3. The volume of any piston dish/dome above the piston crown at TDC.
4. The small cylindrical slice between piston crown and cylinder head.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** $V_c$ is like the *air gap* inside a sealed syringe. If you push the plunger all the way in, you cannot eliminate this residual volume. Reducing it raises the compression ratio. KTU questions often require computing $V_c$ from a given compression ratio, or vice versa.

### 2.2.4 Compression Ratio ($r_k$)

> **Definition:** The ratio of the *total* cylinder volume (at BDC) to the *clearance* volume (at TDC).

This is the single most important *dimensionless* descriptor of an engine's thermodynamic character:
- **SI engines (petrol):** Limited to ~12:1 by "knock" (auto-ignition of the end-gas).
- **CI engines (diesel):** Can go to 22:1+ because the fuel is injected *after* compression (no end-gas knock problem).

### 2.2.5 The Stroke-to-Bore Ratio ($L/D$)

| $L/D$ Range | Engine Character | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **$L/D < 1$** (short stroke) | High revving, less piston friction, high power-to-displacement. | Modern high-performance petrol (Ferrari, F1) |
| **$L/D \approx 1$** (square) | Balanced torque and power. | Most modern production engines |
| **$L/D > 1$** (long stroke) | Strong low-end torque, slower revving, larger displacement. | Diesel trucks, tractors, older petrols |
| **$L/D \gg 1$** (very long) | High piston mean velocity, stress problems. | Largely obsolete |

### 2.2.6 Crank-Angle Nomenclature & Firing Interval

> **The "Why" behind firing interval:** For an engine to run smoothly (without torsional vibration and jerky torque delivery), its power strokes must be evenly spaced around the 2 or 4 crank revolutions.

For a **4-stroke, $N$-cylinder** engine, the total crank rotation per complete cycle is **$720°$** (since the cycle spans 2 revolutions). The firing interval is:

$$\text{Firing Interval} = \frac{720°}{N}$$

For a **2-stroke, $N$-cylinder** engine, the cycle spans only **$360°$**, so the interval is $360°/N$.

**Example:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke engine fires every $720°/4 = 180°$ of crank rotation. This is why the firing order (1-3-4-2) is designed so that no two adjacent cylinders fire consecutively (which would imbalance the crank).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield, Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value and ratio symbols use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (LaTeX) to avoid breaking the markdown pipe parser. In handwritten KTU exams, students will write the standard vertical bar as usual.

| # | Nomenclature Term | Formula (LaTeX) | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cross-sectional area of cylinder | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | m² or cm² | Piston face area |
| 2 | Swept volume per cylinder | $V_s = A \cdot L = \dfrac{\pi D^2 L}{4}$ | m³ or cc | Air–fuel charge volume per cycle |
| 3 | Total swept volume of engine | $V_D = N \cdot V_s$ | m³, L, or cc | **Engine Displacement** |
| 4 | Compression ratio | $r_k = \dfrac{V_t}{V_c} = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c} = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c}$ | dimensionless | Thermodynamic "severity" |
| 5 | Clearance volume (from $r_k$) | $V_c = \dfrac{V_s}{r_k - 1}$ | m³ or cc | Combustion-chamber volume |
| 6 | Stroke-to-bore ratio | $\dfrac{L}{D}$ | dimensionless | Engine character |
| 7 | Crank radius | $r = L/2$ | m or mm | Half of stroke |
| 8 | Mean piston speed | $\bar{V}_p = \dfrac{2 L N_{rpm}}{60} = \dfrac{L N_{rpm}}{30}$ | m/s | Indicator of piston speed stress |
| 9 | Firing interval (4-stroke) | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{720°}{N}$ | degrees | Crank angle between power strokes |
| 10 | Firing interval (2-stroke) | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | degrees | Crank angle between power strokes |
| 11 | Indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP) | $IMEP = \dfrac{W_{cycle}}{V_s}$ | Pa or bar | Equivalent constant pressure for one cycle |
| 12 | Indicated power (4-stroke) | $IP = \dfrac{p_{im} \cdot V_D \cdot N_{rpm} \cdot k}{2}$ | kW | $k$ = number of cylinders |
| 13 | Brake thermal efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | dimensionless | Energy conversion efficiency |
| 14 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of useful to indicated power |
| 15 | Specific fuel consumption (BSFC) | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Fuel economy metric |

### 2.3.1 Real-World Utility of This Nomenclature

1. **Engine Selection (Vehicle Design):** A tractor manufacturer picks a *long-stroke, low-rpm* diesel (high $L/D$, high $r_k$). A Formula-1 engineer picks a *short-stroke, high-rpm* petrol (low $L/D$, moderate $r_k$).
2. **Emission Compliance:** Compression ratio, bore, and stroke all directly affect flame-speed, knock-margin, and NOx formation (Module 4).
3. **Aftermarket Tuning:** Modifying $V_c$ by milling the head, decking the block, or using thinner head gaskets is a *nomenclature-based* modification of $r_k$.
4. **Diagnostics:** Crank-angle nomenclature lets a mechanic use an OBD-II scan tool to read "Cylinder 3 misfire at $320°$ after TDC."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Expectation:** In the End Semester Examination (ESE), derivation/modeling questions on engine nomenclature carry **7 marks** (out of 14 for the full question). Students must show **every algebraic step** with brief verbal justification. Skipping steps (e.g., writing only the final equation) typically leads to loss of 2–3 marks.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Swept Volume ($V_s$) From Bore and Stroke

**Problem Setup:** A cylinder has internal diameter $D$ and the piston travels a linear distance $L$ from TDC to BDC. Prove that the swept volume equals $\pi D^2 L / 4$.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Visualize the geometry.**
The cylinder is a hollow right-circular cylinder. The piston face, viewed from above, is a circle of diameter $D$. As the piston moves from TDC to BDC, it sweeps a *cylindrical shell* of height $L$.

**Step 2 — Compute the cross-sectional area of the piston.**
The piston is a circle of diameter $D$. Its area is:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

This is the standard area formula for a circle, $A = \pi r^2$, with $r = D/2$ substituted.

**Step 3 — Compute the swept volume.**
The swept volume is the *prism* of base area $A$ and height $L$:

$$
V_s = A \times L
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $A$ from Step 2 into Step 3.**

$$
V_s = \left( \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \right) \times L
$$

**Step 5 — Final canonical form.**

$$
\boxed{V_s = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4}}
$$

> **Units check:** $[D^2] = \text{m}^2$, $[L] = \text{m}$, so $[V_s] = \text{m}^3$. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Engine Displacement ($V_D$) for an N-Cylinder Engine

**Problem Setup:** An engine has $N$ identical cylinders, each with bore $D$ and stroke $L$. Derive the total engine displacement.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Per-cylinder swept volume (already derived).**

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the number of cylinders.**
The total displacement is the *sum* of $N$ identical swept volumes:

$$
V_D = N \times V_s
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
V_D = N \times \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Final canonical form.**

$$
\boxed{V_D = \frac{\pi \, N \, D^2 \, L}{4}}
$$

> **Numerical example (KTU style):** A 4-cylinder petrol engine has $D = 80 \text{ mm}$, $L = 90 \text{ mm}$. Compute displacement in **cc**.

**Solution:**

$$
V_D = \frac{\pi \times 4 \times (0.080)^2 \times (0.090)}{4} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_D = \pi \times (0.080)^2 \times (0.090) \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_D = \pi \times 0.0064 \times 0.090 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_D = \pi \times 0.000576 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_D \approx 0.001809 \text{ m}^3
$$

Convert to cc ($\text{1 m}^3 = 10^6 \text{ cm}^3$):

$$
V_D \approx 0.001809 \times 10^6 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V_D \approx 1809 \text{ cc} \approx 1.81 \text{ L}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Compression Ratio ($r_k$) and Its Algebraic Transformations

**Problem Setup:** Derive the three equivalent expressions for the compression ratio starting from first principles.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Define the ratio.**
By definition:

$$
r_k = \frac{V_t}{V_c}
$$

where $V_t$ is total volume (at BDC) and $V_c$ is clearance volume (at TDC).

**Step 2 — Express total volume.**
When the piston is at BDC, the cylinder contains the clearance volume *plus* the full swept volume:

$$
V_t = V_c + V_s
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
r_k = \frac{V_c + V_s}{V_c}
$$

**Step 4 — Split the fraction.**

$$
r_k = \frac{V_c}{V_c} + \frac{V_s}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c}
$$

**Step 5 — Final canonical form.**

$$
\boxed{r_k = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c}}
$$

This is the form most used by KTU students in derivation problems.

**Step 6 — Invert to solve for clearance volume.**
From Step 5, isolate $V_c$:

$$
r_k - 1 = \frac{V_s}{V_c}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_c = \frac{V_s}{r_k - 1}}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for swept volume in terms of $r_k$ and $V_c$.**

$$
\boxed{V_s = V_c \cdot (r_k - 1)}
$$

> **Numerical example:** A single-cylinder SI engine has $V_s = 500 \text{ cc}$ and $r_k = 10$. Find $V_c$.

$$
V_c = \frac{500}{10 - 1} = \frac{500}{9}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_c \approx 55.56 \text{ cc}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Mean Piston Speed ($\bar{V}_p$)

**Problem Setup:** An engine runs at $N$ rpm. Each piston travels the stroke $L$ twice per revolution (once up, once down). Derive the mean piston speed.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Distance travelled per revolution.**
The piston travels $2L$ per revolution (TDC → BDC → TDC).

**Step 2 — Distance per minute.**

$$
\text{Distance per minute} = 2L \cdot N
$$

**Step 3 — Distance per second (divide by 60).**

$$
\bar{V}_p = \frac{2L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{L \cdot N}{30}
$$

**Step 4 — Final canonical form.**

$$
\boxed{\bar{V}_p = \frac{L \cdot N}{30} \;\; \text{m/s (if } L \text{ in metres)}}
$$

> **Numerical example:** A motorbike engine has $L = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$ and runs at $N = 12{,}000 \text{ rpm}$.

$$
\bar{V}_p = \frac{0.060 \times 12000}{30} = 24 \text{ m/s}
$$

This is close to the practical limit of $\sim 25$ m/s for production engines.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Firing Interval for Multi-Cylinder Engines

**Problem Setup:** Derive the firing interval for (a) a 4-stroke $N$-cylinder engine and (b) a 2-stroke $N$-cylinder engine.

### Step-by-Step Deduction — Part (a) 4-Stroke

**Step 1 — Crank rotation per complete cycle.**
In a 4-stroke engine, one complete thermodynamic cycle (intake, compression, power, exhaust) requires **2 full revolutions** of the crank:

$$
\theta_{cycle} = 2 \times 360° = 720°
$$

**Step 2 — Number of cylinders.**
For smooth operation, the $N$ power strokes must be equally distributed around the $720°$.

**Step 3 — Firing interval.**

$$
\boxed{\Delta\theta_{4S} = \frac{720°}{N}}
$$

### Step-by-Step Deduction — Part (b) 2-Stroke

**Step 1 — Crank rotation per complete cycle.**
A 2-stroke engine completes its thermodynamic cycle in **1 full revolution**:

$$
\theta_{cycle} = 360°
$$

**Step 2 — Firing interval.**

$$
\boxed{\Delta\theta_{2S} = \frac{360°}{N}}
$$

> **KTU exam tip:** A common question reads: *"Why does a 4-cylinder 4-stroke engine fire every 180°?"* — The answer is $720°/4 = 180°$, AND this is one *firing per cylinder per 2 revolutions*, meaning each cylinder fires every 720° (one full cycle). Students often confuse these two numbers.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: "Which Nomenclature Term is Used Where?"

> [!TIP]
> This matrix is the **highest-yield revision tool** for KTU Module 1. In Part A 3-mark questions, students are often asked to "list any 5 engine nomenclature terms and state their use." Memorize this matrix.

| Nomenclature Term | Used in Calculating | Used in Describing | Module Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bore ($D$) | Displacement, IMEP, Heat release | Engine "size," breathing area | Module 1, 2 |
| Stroke ($L$) | Displacement, Piston speed, Torque | Engine "character" | Module 1, 2 |
| Displacement ($V_D$) | IP, BP, SFC, Engine rating | Engine "class" | Module 1, 2, 3 |
| Compression Ratio ($r_k$) | Otto/Diesel efficiency, knock margin | Engine "type" (SI vs CI) | Module 1, 2, 3, 4 |
| $L/D$ Ratio | Piston speed, Bore/stroke balance | "Square" vs "oversquare" | Module 1, 2 |
| Mean Piston Speed | Friction, valve-train stress | Engine "speed capability" | Module 2 |
| Firing Order | Crank balance, Vibration | "Smoothness" of engine | Module 2, 5 |
| Firing Interval | Cam profile design, Ignition timing | "Evenness" of torque | Module 2 |
| MEP | Indicated Work, Indicated Power | "Effectiveness" of cylinder | Module 2, 3 |
| BSFC | Fuel economy, Range | "Efficiency" of engine | Module 2, 3 |
| $\eta_m$ | BP from IP | "Mechanical health" of engine | Module 2, 3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Note for KTU Students:** Module 1 of PBMUT404 is largely descriptive and definitional. The diagrams below are designed to be *redrawn verbatim* in your answer scripts during the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE), where labelled diagrams carry **2 to 3 marks** within a 14-mark question.

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Hierarchical Architecture of Engine Nomenclature

This diagram maps the conceptual flow from the most fundamental geometric terms up to the dynamic performance descriptors.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef geo fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef vol fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef rat fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    classDef dyn fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef perf fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000

    A[BORE D and STROKE L]:::geo
    B[CRANK RADIUS r and CON ROD LENGTH]:::geo
    C[SWEPT VOLUME Vs equals pi D squared L over 4]:::vol
    D[CLEARANCE VOLUME Vc]:::vol
    E[TOTAL VOLUME Vt equals Vs plus Vc]:::vol
    F[ENGINE DISPLACEMENT VD equals N times Vs]:::vol
    G[COMPRESSION RATIO rk equals 1 plus Vs over Vc]:::rat
    H[STROKE BORE RATIO L over D]:::rat
    I[ROD STROKE RATIO l over L]:::rat
    J[CRANK ANGLE THETA]:::dyn
    K[FIRING INTERVAL DELTA THETA]:::dyn
    L[FIRING ORDER]:::dyn
    M[VALVE TIMING EVENTS]:::dyn
    N[INDICATED POWER IP]:::perf
    O[BRAKE POWER BP]:::perf
    P[MEAN EFFECTIVE PRESSURE MEP]:::perf
    Q[BSFC and MECHANICAL EFFICIENCY]:::perf

    A --> C
    B --> C
    A --> H
    B --> I
    C --> E
    C --> F
    D --> E
    C --> G
    D --> G
    C --> P
    F --> N
    F --> O
    J --> K
    K --> L
    J --> M
    N --> Q
    O --> Q
    P --> N
```

**How to interpret this diagram for the exam:**
- The **blue tier** = pure geometry; write these down first.
- The **orange tier** = volume parameters; derive these.
- The **purple tier** = dimensionless ratios; highlight these.
- The **green tier** = dynamic/time-domain terms.
- The **red tier** = performance metrics; calculated from all the above.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Schematic of an Engine's Functional Architecture

> [!TIP]
> Since true 3D cutaway drawings cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following *block-level functional architecture flow* shows how the nomenclature terms interact with the physical systems of an engine. KTU examiners accept block diagrams as a valid illustrative answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef cyl fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef crank fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef val fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef cool fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef lube fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef ign fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000

    P1[PISTON BORE D STROKE L]:::cyl
    P2[CYLINDER HEAD COMBUSTION CHAMBER Vc]:::cyl
    P3[CYLINDER BLOCK LINER]:::cyl
    P4[GASKET INTERFACE]:::cyl

    C1[CRANKSHAFT r equals L over 2]:::crank
    C2[CONNECTING ROD l]:::crank
    C3[FLYWHEEL FIRING ORDER]:::crank

    V1[INTAKE VALVE]:::val
    V2[EXHAUST VALVE]:::val
    V3[CAMSHAFT OHV SOHC DOHC]:::val
    V4[VALVE TIMING EVENTS]:::val

    F1[FUEL INJECTOR]:::ign
    S1[SPARK PLUG SI ENGINE]:::ign

    CO1[WATER JACKET COOLING]:::cool
    CO2[OIL GALLERY LUBRICATION]:::lube
    CO3[OIL PAN SUMP]:::lube

    P1 -- compression ratio rk --> P2
    P1 -- crank rotation --> C1
    C1 -- reciprocating motion --> C2
    C2 -- vertical movement --> P1
    V1 -- controlled by --> V3
    V2 -- controlled by --> V3
    V3 -- firing interval --> C3
    F1 -- CI Engine --> P2
    S1 -- SI Engine --> P2
    CO1 -- surrounds --> P3
    CO2 -- feeds bearings --> C1
    CO2 -- feeds --> C2
```

**Key relationships shown in the diagram:**
- Bore $D$ + Stroke $L$ + Clearance Volume $V_c$ together determine the **compression ratio $r_k$**.
- Crank rotation is converted to piston reciprocation through the **connecting rod** (linkage nomenclature).
- **Firing order** is the *output* of the valvetrain + cam + crank-angle nomenclature.
- **Cooling and lubrication** are auxiliary systems, but their *flow paths* are designed using the geometric nomenclature of the block (bore spacing, deck height).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Crank-Angle Nomenclature Map

This diagram visualizes the *crank-angle* terminology (TDC, BDC, Firing Interval) for a 4-cylinder 4-stroke engine — a very commonly tested figure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef ev fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef cy fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000

    A0[CRANK ANGLE 0 DEGREES TDC FIRING CYLINDER 1]:::cy
    A1[CRANK ANGLE 180 DEGREES TDC EXHAUST CYLINDER 1 EQUALS TDC INTAKE CYLINDER 3]:::cy
    A2[CRANK ANGLE 360 DEGREES TDC FIRING CYLINDER 3]:::cy
    A3[CRANK ANGLE 540 DEGREES TDC FIRING CYLINDER 4]:::cy
    A4[CRANK ANGLE 720 DEGREES TDC FIRING CYLINDER 2]:::cy
    A5[CRANK ANGLE 720 DEGREES RETURNS TO TDC OF CYLINDER 1]:::cy

    E1[EVENT 1 INTAKE CYLINDER 1 BDC at 180 degrees]:::ev
    E2[EVENT 2 COMPRESSION AND POWER CYLINDER 1]:::ev
    E3[EVENT 3 EXHAUST CYLINDER 1 BDC at 540 degrees]:::ev
    F1[FIRING INTERVAL 180 DEGREES]:::ev
    F2[COMPLETE CYCLE 720 DEGREES]:::ev

    A0 -- 180 degree interval --> A1
    A1 -- 180 degree interval --> A2
    A2 -- 180 degree interval --> A3
    A3 -- 180 degree interval --> A4
    A4 -- 180 degree interval --> A5

    A0 --- E1
    A0 --- E2
    A0 --- E3
    A0 --- F1
    A0 --- F2
```

> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *draw the firing order and crank-angle diagram*, draw a circle (representing the 720° crank rotation) and mark the firing events of each cylinder at the correct angular positions. Always **state the firing order explicitly** at the side of the diagram.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Classification of Engines (Nomenclature Root)

This flowchart gives the complete classification tree — a high-yield 3-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef root fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef l1 fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef l2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef l3 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000

    ROOT[ENGINE]:::root

    ROOT --> IC[INTERNAL COMBUSTION IC]:::l1
    ROOT --> EC[EXTERNAL COMBUSTION EC e.g. Stirling]:::l1

    IC --> SI[SPARK IGNITION PETROL]:::l2
    IC --> CI[COMPRESSION IGNITION DIESEL]:::l2

    SI --> S4[4 STROKE OTTO CYCLE]:::l3
    SI --> S2[2 STROKE OTTO CYCLE]:::l3
    CI --> C4[4 STROKE DIESEL CYCLE]:::l3
    CI --> C2[2 STROKE DIESEL CYCLE]:::l3

    S4 --> ARR1[ARRANGEMENT INLINE V FLAT ROTARY]:::l3
    S4 --> VAL1[VALVETRAIN OHV SOHC DOHC]:::l3
    S4 --> ASP1[ASPIRATION NA TURBO SUPER]:::l3
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part A of the ESE question paper contains short-answer questions of **3 marks each**, to be answered in **3 to 4 sentences** with a labelled diagram where applicable. The model answers below match the expected length and key-point density.

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PBMUT404, Module 1]
**Define the following engine nomenclature terms with their SI units: (i) Bore, (ii) Stroke, (iii) Engine Displacement.** *(3 Marks)*

### Model Answer:

**(i) Bore ($D$):** The internal diameter of the engine cylinder, measured across the working bore of the cylinder liner. It defines the *cross-sectional area* of the cylinder on which the combustion gas pressure acts to produce force on the piston.
**SI unit:** **metre (m)** or commonly **millimetre (mm)**.

**(ii) Stroke ($L$):** The linear distance traversed by the piston from **Top Dead Centre (TDC)** to **Bottom Dead Centre (BDC)**. The stroke determines the *swept volume* and is twice the crank radius.
**SI unit:** **metre (m)** or **millimetre (mm)**.

**(iii) Engine Displacement ($V_D$):** The total volume swept by *all* the pistons in one complete cycle of the engine, mathematically given by $V_D = N \times (\pi D^2 L / 4)$, where $N$ is the number of cylinders.
**SI unit:** **cubic metre (m³)**, but practically expressed in **litres (L)** or **cubic centimetres (cc)**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PBMUT404, Module 1]
**What is meant by the "firing interval" of a 4-stroke engine? Find the firing interval of a 6-cylinder 4-stroke engine in degrees of crank rotation.** *(3 Marks)*

### Model Answer:

**Firing Interval** is the angular displacement of the crankshaft (in degrees) between the *start of the power stroke* of one cylinder and the *start of the power stroke* of the next cylinder in the firing order. It is the inverse measure of how "smooth" the torque delivery of the engine is.

For a **4-stroke engine**, one complete thermodynamic cycle occupies **$720°$** of crank rotation (2 revolutions). The firing interval $\Delta\theta$ for an $N$-cylinder engine is:

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{720°}{N}
$$

For a **6-cylinder 4-stroke** engine, $N = 6$:

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{720°}{6} = 120°
$$

Thus, the firing interval is **$120°$** of crank rotation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule (ESE):** Each module carries *one full 14-mark question* with an internal choice (either "OR" between two sub-questions of the same module, or "Compulsory sub-parts"). Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two completely independent alternatives; the student may attempt *either one*. Each is worth **14 marks** and contains sub-parts (a) [7 marks] and (b) [7 marks].

---

### ❖ QUESTION A — 14 MARKS (Attempt Either A OR B)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model), PBMUT404, Module 1]**

#### (a) **(7 Marks)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, explain the major **engine nomenclature parameters** used to describe a 4-stroke SI engine. Include the geometric terms (bore, stroke), volume terms (clearance volume, swept volume), and ratio terms (compression ratio, $L/D$ ratio) in your answer.

#### Model Solution:

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Engine nomenclature is the standardized set of geometric, volumetric, and ratio terms that uniquely describes an engine. The major parameters are as follows.

**1. Geometric Nomenclature (2 marks):**

- **Bore ($D$):** Internal diameter of the cylinder. Determines piston area $A = \pi D^2/4$.
- **Stroke ($L$):** Distance between TDC and BDC. Determines crank radius $r = L/2$.
- **Top Dead Centre (TDC):** The piston's uppermost position.
- **Bottom Dead Centre (BDC):** The piston's lowermost position.

**2. Volume Nomenclature (2 marks):**

- **Swept Volume ($V_s$):** $V_s = \pi D^2 L / 4$. Volume displaced by one piston per stroke.
- **Clearance Volume ($V_c$):** Volume of combustion chamber + gasket + piston dish at TDC.
- **Total Cylinder Volume ($V_t$):** $V_t = V_c + V_s$.
- **Engine Displacement ($V_D$):** $V_D = N \times V_s$, the total swept volume of all $N$ cylinders.

**3. Ratio Nomenclature (2 marks):**

- **Compression Ratio ($r_k$):** $r_k = V_t / V_c = 1 + V_s / V_c$. For SI engines typically 8:1 to 12:1.
- **Stroke/Bore Ratio ($L/D$):** Less than 1 for high-rpm "short-stroke" engines; greater than 1 for "long-stroke" torque engines.

> *(A neat labelled diagram showing bore, stroke, TDC, BDC, $V_c$, and $V_s$ would carry the remaining 1 mark. Refer to Mermaid Diagram 4.1 in Section 4 of these notes for the schematic.)*

---

#### (b) **(7 Marks)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke SI engine has a **bore of 82 mm** and a **stroke of 90 mm**. The **clearance volume is 60 cm³**. Calculate: (i) Swept Volume, (ii) Compression Ratio, (iii) Engine Displacement, (iv) Stroke-to-Bore Ratio. Comment on the engine character based on $L/D$.

#### Model Solution:

**Given data (0.5 marks):**
$D = 82 \text{ mm} = 0.082 \text{ m}$, $L = 90 \text{ mm} = 0.090 \text{ m}$, $V_c = 60 \text{ cm}^3 = 60 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$, $N = 1$ cylinder.

**(i) Swept Volume (2 marks):**

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.082)^2 \times 0.090}{4} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi \times 0.006724 \times 0.090}{4} = \frac{0.0019015}{4} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_s \approx 4.754 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

Converting to cc: $V_s \approx 475.4 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 475 \text{ cc}$.

**(ii) Compression Ratio (2 marks):**

$$
r_k = 1 + \frac{V_s}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{475.4}{60} = 1 + 7.923
$$

$$
\boxed{r_k \approx 8.92 : 1}
$$

**Comment:** This is consistent with a typical 4-stroke SI engine (8:1 to 12:1).

**(iii) Engine Displacement (1.5 marks):**

$$
V_D = N \times V_s = 1 \times 475.4 \text{ cc}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_D \approx 475 \text{ cc} \approx 0.475 \text{ L}}
$$

**(iv) Stroke-to-Bore Ratio (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{L}{D} = \frac{90}{82} \approx 1.098
$$

**Comment on character (extra, completes the answer):** Since $L/D \approx 1.10 > 1$, this is a **mildly long-stroke (slightly "undersquare")** engine. Such an engine favours **low-to-mid range torque** over high-rpm power. The piston mean speed at, say, 5000 rpm would be $\bar{V}_p = (0.090 \times 5000)/30 = 15$ m/s, well within safe limits.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Alternative to Question A)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model), PBMUT404, Module 1]**

#### (a) **(7 Marks)** Define **compression ratio** and **stroke-to-bore ratio** for an engine. Explain how the choice of these ratios influences (i) the engine's power and torque characteristics, (ii) its fuel type suitability, and (iii) its mechanical/thermal stress.

#### Model Solution:

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **Compression Ratio ($r_k$):** $r_k = V_t / V_c = 1 + V_s / V_c$. A dimensionless ratio expressing how much the air (or air–fuel mixture) is compressed before combustion.
- **Stroke-to-Bore Ratio ($L/D$):** The ratio of the piston stroke to the cylinder bore. Classifies an engine as **"undersquare"** ($L/D > 1$), **"square"** ($L/D = 1$), or **"oversquare"** ($L/D < 1$).

**(i) Influence on Power and Torque (2 marks):**

- **Higher $r_k$** → Higher *theoretical* thermal efficiency (Otto: $\eta = 1 - 1/r_k^{\gamma-1}$) → higher power per unit fuel.
- **Higher $L/D$** (long stroke) → Higher *mean effective pressure* and *torque* at low rpm (longer lever arm) but lower peak rpm (piston-speed-limited).
- **Lower $L/D$** (short stroke) → Favors *high-rpm breathing* and higher *peak power* but lower low-end torque.

**(ii) Influence on Fuel Type Suitability (1.5 marks):**

- **SI (Petrol) Engines:** Limited to $r_k \approx 8$–$12$ due to **knock** (auto-ignition of end-gas).
- **CI (Diesel) Engines:** Can use $r_k = 14$–$22$+ because fuel is injected *after* compression → no knock.
- Therefore, **CI engines always have higher $r_k$** than SI engines of comparable size.

**(iii) Influence on Mechanical and Thermal Stress (1.5 marks):**

- **Higher $r_k$** → Higher peak cylinder pressure and temperature → greater stress on pistons, connecting rods, bearings, and cooling system.
- **Long stroke** ($L/D \gg 1$) → Higher mean piston speed at a given rpm → greater frictional loss, piston-ring wear, and reciprocating-mass inertia loading.
- **Engine designers balance** $r_k$ (for efficiency) against $L/D$ (for power-band width and durability).

> *Conclusion:* Compression ratio and $L/D$ are the two "tuning knobs" of engine nomenclature that determine whether the engine is a high-rpm petrol (low $L/D$, moderate $r_k$) or a low-rpm diesel (high $L/D$, high $r_k$).

---

#### (b) **(7 Marks)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine running at **3000 rpm** has a **bore of 75 mm** and a **stroke of 85 mm**. The **compression ratio is 9:1**. Calculate: (i) Engine Displacement in litres, (ii) Clearance volume per cylinder, (iii) Mean Piston Speed, (iv) Firing Interval in degrees.

#### Model Solution:

**Given data (0.5 marks):**
$N_{cyl} = 4$, $N_{rpm} = 3000$ rpm, $D = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$, $L = 85 \text{ mm} = 0.085 \text{ m}$, $r_k = 9$.

**(i) Engine Displacement in litres (2 marks):**

Swept volume per cylinder:

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.075)^2 \times 0.085}{4} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi \times 0.005625 \times 0.085}{4} = \frac{0.001502}{4} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_s \approx 3.755 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \approx 375.5 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Total engine displacement:

$$
V_D = 4 \times V_s = 4 \times 375.5 \text{ cc} = 1502 \text{ cc}
$$

Converting to litres:

$$
\boxed{V_D \approx 1.502 \text{ L}}
$$

**(ii) Clearance Volume per Cylinder (2 marks):**

Using $V_c = V_s / (r_k - 1)$:

$$
V_c = \frac{375.5 \text{ cc}}{9 - 1} = \frac{375.5}{8} \text{ cc}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_c \approx 46.94 \text{ cm}^3}
$$

**(iii) Mean Piston Speed (1.5 marks):**

$$
\bar{V}_p = \frac{L \cdot N_{rpm}}{30} = \frac{0.085 \times 3000}{30}
$$

$$
\boxed{\bar{V}_p = 8.5 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Comment:** This is a comfortable mean piston speed — typical of a mid-range passenger car engine at cruising rpm.

**(iv) Firing Interval (1 mark):**

For a 4-stroke engine:

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{720°}{N_{cyl}} = \frac{720°}{4} = 180°
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta\theta = 180° \text{ of crank rotation}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / COMMON PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Engine Nomenclature Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** if the displacement is given as a bare number without "cc," "L," or "m³." Always state the unit.
>
> 2. **Mixing up 2-stroke and 4-stroke firing intervals.** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder engine has a firing interval of $180°$ (NOT $90°$). The $90°$ would be for a 2-stroke 4-cylinder. This is the **single most common error** reported by KTU evaluators.
>
> 3. **Compression ratio formula reversal.** Students often write $r_k = 1 - V_s/V_c$ or $r_k = V_s/V_c$. The correct formula is $r_k = 1 + V_s/V_c = V_t/V_c$. Memorize the *additive* form.
>
> 4. **Confusing Bore with Stroke.** Bore is the *diameter* (across the cylinder). Stroke is the *length* (up–down travel). Students writing "bore = 90 mm and stroke = 75 mm" in Question B(b) above would lose **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Omitting the diagram.** For the 7-mark part (a) of a nomenclature question, a labelled diagram is worth **at least 1 mark**. Always include a schematic with bore, stroke, TDC, BDC, $V_c$, and $V_s$ clearly indicated.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions.** When computing mean piston speed, students should explicitly state "assuming single-cylinder" or "ignoring piston pin offset." Examiners reward 0.5 marks for this practice.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to comment on the result.** In the numerical part (b), KTU questions (especially those worth 7 marks) usually ask for a "comment" or "interpretation" of the calculated $L/D$ ratio or compression ratio. Simply writing the number without a comment can cost **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print/Save This List Before Every KTU Exam)**

- [x] **Bore ($D$)** = internal diameter of cylinder. Unit: **mm**.
- [x] **Stroke ($L$)** = TDC-to-BDC piston travel. Unit: **mm**.
- [x] **Swept volume per cylinder:** $V_s = \pi D^2 L / 4$.
- [x] **Total displacement:** $V_D = N \times V_s$. Unit: **L** or **cc**.
- [x] **Compression ratio:** $r_k = 1 + V_s / V_c$. SI: 8–12, CI: 14–22.
- [x] **Clearance volume:** $V_c = V_s / (r_k - 1)$.
- [x] **Stroke/bore ratio:** $L/D > 1$ (undersquare) → low-end torque; $L/D < 1$ (oversquare) → high-rpm power.
- [x] **Mean piston speed:** $\bar{V}_p = L \cdot N / 30$ (m/s). Typical limit $\sim 25$ m/s.
- [x] **Firing interval (4-stroke):** $720°/N$. **Firing interval (2-stroke):** $360°/N$.
- [x] **Firing order** for typical inline-4: **1-3-4-2** (or **1-2-4-3** for some engines).
- [x] **Common firing orders to remember:** Inline-3: 1-3-2. Inline-5: 1-2-4-5-3. Inline-6: 1-5-3-6-2-4. V8 (GM): 1-8-4-3-6-5-7-2.
- [x] **TDC = Top Dead Centre** (also ODC = Outer Dead Centre).
- [x] **BDC = Bottom Dead Centre** (also IDC = Inner Dead Centre).
- [x] **Crank radius** $r = L / 2$.
- [x] **Valvetrain abbreviations:** OHV, SOHC, DOHC, VVT, VVC.
- [x] **Engine classification axes (5):** Combustion location, cycle, ignition, thermodynamic cycle, arrangement.
- [x] **Common engine arrangements:** Inline (I3, I4, I5, I6), V (V6, V8, V10, V12), Flat/Boxer (F4, F6), W (W12, W16), Radial, Rotary (Wankel).
- [x] **SI vs CI quick test:** Petrol uses **spark plug** ($r_k \le 12$); Diesel uses **fuel injection into hot compressed air** ($r_k \ge 14$).
- [x] **Memorize the formula $1 \text{ m}^3 = 10^6 \text{ cm}^3 = 10^3 \text{ L}$** for unit conversions.
- [x] **Always include a labelled diagram** in any 7-mark nomenclature question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of IC Engines by Cylinder Arrangement and Valve Arrangement. Numbering of Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of IC Engines by Cylinder Arrangement and Valve Arrangement. Numbering of Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Internal Combustion (IC) Engine** is a prime mover that converts the chemical energy of a fuel into mechanical work through a thermodynamic cycle (Otto, Diesel, Dual, or Brayton) occurring *inside* the engine cylinder. The classification of IC engines by **cylinder arrangement** refers to the geometric configuration, orientation, and spatial layout of the cylinders with respect to the engine's central axis (crankshaft), directly affecting the engine's size, weight, balance, vibration characteristics, and packaging suitability for the vehicle.

The classification by **valve arrangement** refers to the location, orientation, and actuation mechanism of the intake and exhaust valves relative to the combustion chamber and the cylinder head architecture. The **numbering of cylinders** is the standardized convention used by manufacturers to assign numerical identities to each cylinder for service, firing order, balancing, and diagnostic reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> This topic forms the foundational vocabulary for the entire semester. Every later module (Fuel Systems, Cooling, Lubrication, Ignition) builds upon an assumed understanding of cylinder layout and valve geometry. Examiner weightage: **2–5 marks** (frequent short-answer and diagrammatic sub-parts).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an IC engine as a **team of workers lifting boxes in a synchronized rhythm**. Each cylinder is a "worker" with a piston, and the team must lift boxes (generate power) in a perfectly timed sequence. Now, how the workers are arranged in the room matters:

- If all workers stand in a **single straight line** along the conveyor belt → that's an **Inline engine**.
- If they form a **"V" shape** (two rows leaning toward each other) → that's a **V-engine**.
- If they lie flat on the floor in two opposite rows → that's a **Flat/Boxer engine**.
- If they stand in a circle around a central pivot → that's a **Radial engine**.

Similarly, the **valve arrangement** is like deciding *where the worker receives the box (intake air-fuel)* and *where they hand off the empty box (exhaust gases)*. The valves can be on the side, on the top, driven by pushrods, or directly by camshafts — each design is a different "valve arrangement."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Cylinder arrangement governs the engine's *external envelope* and *vibrational behavior*, while valve arrangement governs the engine's *breathing efficiency* and *power density*. A well-designed modern engine optimizes *both* simultaneously.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded)

- **Firing Order**: The specific sequence in which the spark plugs fire in each cylinder (e.g., 1-3-4-2 for a typical 4-cylinder inline engine).
- **Bank Angle**: The angle between the two cylinder banks in a V-engine (commonly **60°**, **72°**, **90°**).
- **Valve Train Components**: Camshaft, rocker arms, pushrods, lifters/tappets, timing belt/chain.
- **Cylinder Bore (D)**: Inner diameter of the cylinder, typically in **mm** (e.g., 80 mm).
- **Stroke Length (L)**: Distance traveled by the piston between TDC and BDC, typically in **mm** (e.g., 90 mm).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of IC Engines by Cylinder Arrangement

#### 2.1.1 Inline (Straight) Engine

- All cylinders are arranged in a single row along the length of the crankshaft.
- The crankshaft is oriented along the longitudinal axis of the vehicle (RWD) or transverse axis (FWD).
- **Number of cylinders:** 3, 4, 5, 6, 8 (even-numbered inline engines require balance shafts due to primary and secondary imbalance).
- **Advantages:** Simple design, lower manufacturing cost, ease of maintenance, compact in one direction, smooth for 4 and 6 cylinder configurations.
- **Disadvantages:** Long length makes it difficult to package transversely; heavy 6 and 8 inline engines cause front-weight bias in RWD cars.
- **Applications:** 4-cylinder inline (Tata Nexon, Maruti Swift), 6-cylinder inline (BMW inline-6), inline-3 (Tata Tiago).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why inline-3 is becoming popular:** Shorter length than inline-4 allows transverse mounting in small cars; modern balancer shafts cancel the inherent 1st-order vertical vibration.

#### 2.1.2 V-Type Engine

- Two banks of cylinders set at an angle (the **bank angle**) to a common crankshaft.
- Common bank angles: **60°** (V6 smoothness), **72°** (V8), **90°** (V8 cross-plane crank), **120°** (V12 perfect balance).
- **Advantages:** Shorter overall length than equivalent inline engines; better suited for high cylinder counts; lower bonnet height.
- **Disadvantages:** Greater mechanical complexity; two cylinder heads; wider engine bay requirement; more expensive.
- **Applications:** V6 (Nissan Altima), V8 (Ford Mustang), V12 (Ferrari, Lamborghini), W12 (Bugatti), VR6 (Volkswagen — narrow 15° bank angle).

#### 2.1.3 Flat (Boxer / Horizontally Opposed) Engine

- Two banks of cylinders lie horizontally opposite each other (180° apart) on either side of the crankshaft.
- Pistons in opposing cylinders move in mirror-image fashion, naturally cancelling primary and secondary vibrations.
- **Advantages:** Excellent primary and secondary balance; very low center of gravity; compact height.
- **Disadvantages:** Wider engine; complex lubrication (oil drainage issues); two cylinder heads increase service cost.
- **Applications:** Porsche 911 (flat-6), Subaru Boxer (flat-4), BMW Motorrad motorcycles (flat-twin), Chevrolet Corvair (flat-6, historical), Citroën 2CV.

#### 2.1.4 Radial Engine

- Cylinders (odd number, typically 3, 5, 7, 9) arranged radially around the central crankshaft, like spokes of a wheel.
- Almost exclusively air-cooled, with cylinders exposed for direct airflow.
- **Advantages:** Excellent power-to-weight ratio at high power outputs; large displacement in compact frontal area; very smooth.
- **Disadvantages:** Large frontal area; complex cooling; obsolete for road vehicles; exclusively used in **historical aircraft engines** (e.g., Pratt & Whitney R-2800, Wright R-3350).
- **Applications:** WWII-era fighter aircraft (P-47 Thunderbolt, Spitfire), helicopters (rotary radials).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Radial engines are *frequently* asked in Part A 3-mark questions as a contrast to inline/V-type. Remember: **"Radial = Aircraft, Flat = Porsche/Subaru, Inline = Mass-market cars, V = Luxury/Performance."**

#### 2.1.5 Wankel (Rotary) Engine

- Uses a triangular rotor in an epitrochoid housing instead of reciprocating pistons.
- Not strictly a cylinder arrangement, but classified under engine typology.
- **Applications:** Mazda RX-7, RX-8 (discontinued for mass production but still iconic).

### 2.2 Classification by Valve Arrangement

The **valve arrangement** is determined by:
1. **Location of valves** relative to the cylinder.
2. **Location of the camshaft(s)**.
3. **Actuation mechanism** (direct, indirect via pushrods/rockers).

#### 2.2.1 Side Valve (Flathead / L-Head) — Obsolete

- Valves are placed alongside the cylinder in the engine block, beside the piston.
- **Cam actuates** valves via simple tappets and pushrods located beside the cylinder.
- **Disadvantages:** Combustion chamber is asymmetric and large → poor breathing, low compression ratio, low power output.
- **Applications:** Vintage cars (Ford Model T, early Morris Minor).

#### 2.2.2 Overhead Valve (OHV) / Pushrod Engine

- Valves are located in the **cylinder head**, *above* the piston.
- Camshaft remains in the **engine block** (below the valves), and transmits motion **upward via pushrods and rocker arms**.
- **Configuration symbol:** *I-Head* engine.
- **Advantages:** Compact combustion chamber, high compression ratio, high volumetric efficiency, low engine height.
- **Applications:** American V8s (Chevrolet Small-Block, Chrysler Hemi pushrod era), most classic muscle cars.

> [!NOTE]
> **OHV = I-Head (also called "valve-in-head"):** This naming is a classic exam trap. KTU examiners often use "I-head" in question papers to test whether students know it is the same as OHV.

#### 2.2.3 Overhead Cam (OHC) — Single Cam

- The camshaft is located in the cylinder head, *above* the valves.
- **Single OHC (SOHC):** One camshaft actuates both intake and exhaust valves via rocker arms or direct bucket tappets.
- **Driven by:** Timing belt, timing chain, or gear train.
- **Advantages:** No pushrods → reduced reciprocating mass; higher RPM capability; more precise valve timing.
- **Applications:** Honda D-series, Maruti K-series, most modern 4-cylinders.

#### 2.2.4 Double Overhead Cam (DOHC) — Twin Cam

- **Two camshafts** in the cylinder head — one for intake valves, one for exhaust valves.
- Each camshaft operates its bank of valves via finger followers or direct bucket tappets.
- **Advantages:** Best breathing efficiency, accommodates 4-valves-per-cylinder (4VPC) or 5VPC pent-roof designs, highest specific output.
- **Disadvantages:** Most expensive and complex; wider cylinder head.
- **Applications:** All modern high-performance engines (BMW, Audi, Mercedes, Ferrari, Honda VTEC, Toyota VVT-i).

#### 2.2.5 Hemispherical Combustion Chamber (Hemi Head)

- A *combustion chamber shape* that uses a roughly hemispherical dome with valves arranged in a "pent-roof" pattern.
- **Can be OHV or DOHC** — the term "Hemi" refers to *chamber geometry*, not valve actuation.
- **Applications:** Chrysler Hemi V8, Porsche, Jaguar, Ferrari V12.

#### 2.2.6 Desmodromic Valve Actuation

- Valves are *closed mechanically* (via a second rocker arm), not by springs.
- Eliminates valve float at very high RPMs.
- **Applications:** Ducati L-twin motorcycles, Mercedes W196 F1 (1955), high-revving racing engines.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cylinder Arrangement Metrics

| Parameter / Quantity | Formula or Definition | Standard Value / Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cylinder Displacement Volume $V_c$ | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L$ | **cc** or **liters** | Swept volume of one cylinder |
| Total Engine Displacement $V_d$ | $V_d = V_c \times n$ (where $n$ = number of cylinders) | **cc** or **liters** | Total swept volume of engine |
| Engine Configuration Code | e.g., **I-4**, **V-6**, **V-8**, **F-6**, **W-12** | Dimensionless | Quick identification of layout |
| Valve Count Symbol | e.g., **DOHC 16V** = 4V/cyl × 4 cyl = 16 valves | Dimensionless | Breathing capacity indicator |
| Firing Interval (4-stroke) | $\theta_{\text{fire}} = \dfrac{720°}{n}$ | **degrees of crank rotation** | Determines smoothness |
| Firing Order | Empirical sequence (e.g., 1-3-4-2 for I-4) | Dimensionless | Critical for NVH |
| Volumetric Efficiency $\eta_{vol}$ | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual mass of charge}}{\text{Theoretical mass at atmospheric density}}$ | **Ratio (typically 0.85 – 1.05 for modern engines)** | Breathing effectiveness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note on Pipe Symbol:** All absolute-value or modular notations in the formula sheet are written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to prevent Markdown table breakage. No raw $\vert$ character is used inside table cells.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Classifications

- **Powertrain Packaging:** The choice of inline vs. V vs. flat determines the *engine bay envelope*, *front overhang*, and *crash crumple zone* available.
- **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Inline-6 and flat engines have inherently perfect primary balance; V-engines and inline-5 require balance shafts.
- **Serviceability:** Inline engines are easiest to service; boxer engines require removing the bottom cover; V/DOHC engines demand more skill.
- **Manufacturing Cost:** Inline SOHC ≈ lowest cost → V DOHC ≈ highest cost in the spectrum.
- **Performance Mapping:** DOHC 4VPC engines support the highest specific output (kW/L) in modern road cars.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Firing Interval and Power Delivery

The total thermodynamic cycle of a **4-stroke engine** spans **720°** of crankshaft rotation (two complete revolutions). This is because the 4-stroke cycle has four piston strokes: Intake, Compression, Power, Exhaust — each requiring **180°** of crank rotation.

**Step 1: Identify the constraint.**  
For smooth torque delivery, cylinders should fire as *uniformly spaced events* across the cycle. With $n$ cylinders, the firing interval in degrees of crank rotation is:

$$
\theta_{\text{fire}} = \dfrac{720°}{n}
$$

**Step 2: Apply to a 4-cylinder inline engine (the most common case).**  
Substitute $n = 4$:

$$
\theta_{\text{fire}} = \dfrac{720°}{4} = 180° \text{ of crank rotation}
$$

This means: **one cylinder fires every 180°** of crank rotation, i.e., every half-revolution. With a smooth firing order like **1-3-4-2**, the torque impulses are evenly distributed.

**Step 3: Verify the balance for a 6-cylinder inline engine.**  
Substitute $n = 6$:

$$
\theta_{\text{fire}} = \dfrac{720°}{6} = 120° \text{ of crank rotation}
$$

The firing events occur every 120°, which is closer together than the 180° interval of an inline-4. This gives a 6-cylinder its characteristic **effortless, silky-smooth power delivery** with no perceivable gaps in torque.

**Step 4: Derivation for a 2-stroke engine.**  
A 2-stroke engine completes the entire cycle in **360°** of crank rotation. Therefore:

$$
\theta_{\text{fire, 2-stroke}} = \dfrac{360°}{n}
$$

For a 2-cylinder 2-stroke: $\theta_{\text{fire}} = 180°$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cylinder Arrangements

The following table provides a side-by-side engineering comparison for rapid exam revision:

| Property | Inline (I) | V-Type | Flat (Boxer) | Radial |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Crankshaft Orientation** | Single plane | Two banks at angle | Two banks 180° | Cylinders radiate outward |
| **Length** | Longest | Medium | Medium | Short (height-wise) |
| **Width** | Narrow | Wider | Widest | Widest |
| **Height** | Tall | Shorter than inline | Lowest (low CG) | Tallest |
| **Primary Balance** | Best for 3, 4, 6 cyl | Depends on bank angle + crank | Perfect for even cylinders | Perfect |
| **Typical Use** | Mass-market cars | Luxury, performance | Sports cars, aircraft-derived | Aircraft (historical) |
| **Serviceability** | Easiest | Moderate | Difficult (bottom cover) | Difficult (radial) |
| **Cooling** | Water | Water | Air or water | Almost always air-cooled |
| **Modern Example** | Maruti Swift 1.2L K12 | Audi 4.0L V8 TFSI | Porsche 911 3.0L flat-6 | Pratt & Whitney R-2800 |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Valve Arrangements

| Property | Side Valve (L-head) | OHV (Pushrod) | SOHC | DOHC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Camshaft Location** | Block side | Block center | Cylinder head | Cylinder head (×2) |
| **Drive Mechanism** | Pushrod | Pushrod + rocker | Belt/chain → cam | Belt/chain → 2 cams |
| **Max Practical RPM** | ~3500 | ~5500 | ~7000 | ~9000+ |
| **Compression Ratio Limit** | ~8:1 | ~10:1 | ~12:1 | ~14:1+ |
| **Valves per Cylinder** | 2 | 2 (rarely 4) | 2 or 4 | 4 or 5 (Pent-roof) |
| **Specific Output (kW/L)** | Low (~30) | Medium (~50) | High (~75) | Very high (~100+) |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest |
| **Example** | Ford Model T | Chevrolet SBC 350 | Honda D15B | BMW S65 V8 |

### 3.4 Derivation: Why DOHC 4VPC Outperforms SOHC 2VPC

The airflow through an intake valve can be modeled as an **orifice flow**:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \cdot \Delta p}
$$

Where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (≈ 0.6 to 0.8 for sharp-edged valves)
- $A_v$ = effective valve curtain area
- $\rho$ = density of incoming charge
- $\Delta p$ = pressure drop across the valve

**Step 1: Compute the area for 2 valves vs. 4 valves.**  
For a 4-cylinder engine with bore $D$, the maximum total valve curtain area scales as:

$$
A_{v,\text{total}} = n_v \cdot \pi \cdot d_v \cdot L_v
$$

Where $n_v$ is the number of valves and $d_v$ is the valve seat diameter. Doubling valves from 2 to 4 (per cylinder) increases $A_{v,\text{total}}$ by a factor approaching **2×** (limited by the bore circumference constraint of $\pi D$).

**Step 2: Relate to volumetric efficiency.**  
From continuity, $\eta_{vol}$ at a given engine speed $N$ scales as:

$$
\eta_{vol} \propto A_{v,\text{total}}
$$

Therefore, **DOHC 4VPC engines can achieve higher peak $\eta_{vol}$** and sustain it over a wider RPM range, leading to higher specific output.

**Step 3: Conclude the engineering trade-off.**  
DOHC 4VPC engines dominate modern high-performance applications *because* the breathing advantage outweighs the added mechanical complexity, friction, and cost. This is precisely why KTU Module 1 emphasizes this classification — it directly leads to Modules 3 and 4 (Fuel Systems, Ignition) where valve timing (VVT, VVT-i, i-VTEC) is introduced.
