4. **Heat Treatment** – Induction hardening of journals (55–62 HRC), nitriding, or carburizing.
5. **Finishing** – Grinding, super-finishing, dynamic balancing, shot peening of fillets.

## 2.4 Loads Acting on a Crankshaft

The crankshaft must withstand three primary loadings:

1. **Bending (Flexural) Loads** – From gas pressure + reciprocating mass inertia → cause **bending moments** at the crank pin and main journals.
2. **Torsional Loads** – From the torque transmission to the flywheel → cause **shear stress** in the webs and pin.
3. **Combined / Compound Stresses** – In actual service, bending and torsion occur **simultaneously**, producing complex multi-axial stress states.
4. **Cyclic (Fatigue) Loading** – Each power stroke = 1 stress cycle. A crankshaft endures **~10⁸ to 10⁹ cycles** over its lifetime.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these are the most frequently tested equations in KTU ESE for Crank Shaft.**

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_b = W \cdot R$ | Bending moment on crank pin due to tangential force **W** at radius **R** (single cylinder) |
| 2 | $M_b = \dfrac{P \cdot L}{2}$ | Approximate max bending moment at center main journal of a multi-throw crankshaft |
| 3 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 \cdot M_b}{\pi \cdot d^3}$ | Bending stress in a solid circular crank pin of diameter **d** |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3}$ | Torsional shear stress in crank pin under torque **T** |
| 5 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}\left[M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}\right]$ | **Maximum principal (combined) stress** in crank pin (KTU favourite) |
| 6 | $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ | Centrifugal force on rotating crank throw (balancing) |
| 7 | $F_i = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \left(\cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta\right)$ | Inertia force on piston + reciprocating mass |
| 8 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{M_b \cdot c}{I}$ | Bending fatigue stress using section modulus |
| 9 | $T = W \cdot R \cdot \tan\alpha$ | Instantaneous torque (with $\alpha$ = crank angle, but for tangential approximation $T \approx W \cdot R$) |
| 10 | $N_f = \dfrac{C}{\sigma_a^n}$ | S-N fatigue life (Basquin's equation) |

**Key SI Units:** Force in N, length in m, stress in Pa (or N/mm² = MPa), torque in N·m, angular velocity in rad/s.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Maximum Combined (Principal) Stress in a Crank Pin

This is the **most important derivation** in this module. It appears in nearly every KTU ESE paper.

### Step 1: Define the Loading

A crank pin is subjected to two simultaneous actions:
- A **bending moment** $M_b$ due to the tangential gas force acting at the crank radius.
- A **torque** $T$ due to the transmission of power to the flywheel.

We model the crank pin as a **circular cantilever beam of diameter $d$** fixed at the crank webs.

### Step 2: Bending Stress

For a solid circular cross-section, the maximum bending stress occurs at the outermost fiber and is given by:

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{M_b \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{M_b \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 64} = \frac{32 \cdot M_b}{\pi \cdot d^3}
$$

### Step 3: Torsional Shear Stress

The maximum shear stress in a solid circular shaft under torque $T$ is:

$$
\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 32} = \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3}
$$

### Step 4: Combine Using Principal Stress Theory

At the point of maximum bending stress (the top fiber), the stress state is:
- Normal stress: $\sigma_x = \sigma_b$
- Shear stress: $\tau_{xy} = \tau$

The maximum principal stress is obtained from:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
$$

Substituting $\sigma_x = \dfrac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3}$ and $\tau_{xy} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{16 M_b}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}\right)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{16 M_b}{\pi d^3} + \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3} \left[ M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Combined Stress Formula (KTU textbook result):**
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi \, d^3}\left[M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}\right]}$$

### Step 5: Verification via Numerical Example

**Given:** A single-cylinder engine has crank pin diameter $d = 60$ mm, crank radius $R = 50$ mm, maximum gas force on piston $W = 25{,}000$ N.

**Compute Bending Moment:**

$$
M_b = W \cdot R = 25{,}000 \times 0.05 = 1250 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Compute Torque:** Approximating the tangential force equals the gas force for the worst case:

$$
T = W \cdot R = 25{,}000 \times 0.05 = 1250 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Compute Maximum Combined Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi (0.06)^3}\left[1250 + \sqrt{1250^2 + 1250^2}\right]
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}}\left[1250 + 1767.77\right]
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{16 \times 3017.77}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{48284.3}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} \approx 71.15 \text{ MPa}
$$

This is well below the fatigue limit of hardened alloy steel (~ **450 MPa**), so the design is safe.

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Centrifugal Force on the Crank Throw

The crank throw mass $m_t$ (pin + ½ web) rotates at radius $R$ with angular velocity $\omega$. The centripetal acceleration is $R \omega^2$, hence the centrifugal (inertial) force trying to pull the crank outward is:

$$
F_c = m_t \cdot R \cdot \omega^2
$$

This force must be **balanced** by a counterweight of mass $m_c$ located at the opposite side of the web. The balance condition is:

$$
m_c \cdot r_c = m_t \cdot R
$$

where $r_c$ is the counterweight radius (typically $r_c > R$ so the mass can be kept lower).

> **Engineering Insight:** Since $\omega$ is in rad/s, even small imbalances produce huge forces at high RPM. This is why a 50 g unbalance at 6000 RPM = ~ **3.96 kN** — enough to destroy bearings in minutes.

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Fillet Stress Concentration

The sharp corner between the crank web and pin is a **notch**. The actual maximum stress is:

$$
\sigma_{actual} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nominal}
$$

where $K_t$ is the **stress concentration factor** (typically 2.0 to 3.0 for sharp fillets). Mitigation:
- **Increase fillet radius** (larger $r/d$ → lower $K_t$).
- **Shot peening** introduces compressive residual stress at the surface → improves fatigue life.
- **Induction hardening** raises local surface hardness and fatigue limit.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Formula

| Problem Given | Required Output | Formula to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Crank pin diameter & gas force | **Bending stress** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3}$ |
| Torque transmitted | **Shear stress** | $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ |
| Both bending and torque | **Combined max stress** | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{16}{\pi d^3}\left[M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}\right]$ |
| Crank throw mass, RPM | **Centrifugal force** | $F_c = m \cdot R \cdot \omega^2$ |
| Fillet geometry | **Local stress** | $\sigma = K_t \cdot \sigma_{nom}$ |
| Engine speed, stroke | **Inertia on piston** | $F_i = m r \omega^2 (\cos\theta + \tfrac{r}{l}\cos 2\theta)$ |

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Crankshaft Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **flow of forces, lubrication, and power** through a typical multi-throw automotive crankshaft.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Combustion Chamber Gas Pressure] --> B[Force on Piston Crown]
    B --> C[Reciprocating Motion of Piston]
    C --> D[Connecting Rod Small End]
    D --> E[Connecting Rod Big End]
    E --> F[Crank Pin / Crank Throw]
    F --> G[Bending and Torsional Load Path]
    G --> H[Crank Web / Cheek]
    H --> I[Main Journal]
    I --> J[Main Bearing Shell]
    J --> K[Engine Block Crankcase]
    K --> L[Engine Mounts and Chassis]
    
    M[Oil Pump] --> N[Main Oil Gallery]
    N --> I
    I --> O[Drilled Oil Hole in Crankshaft]
    O --> P[Crank Pin Oil Hole]
    P --> E
    E --> Q[Connecting Rod Bearing]
    Q --> R[Piston Pin Lubrication]
    
    S[Counterweight Mass] --> T[Centrifugal Balance]
    T --> U[Smooth Rotation at High RPM]
    F --> U
    U --> V[Flywheel]
    V --> W[Clutch and Transmission]
    W --> X[Drive Wheels]
    
    Y[Torsional Vibration Damper / Harmonic Balancer] --> I
    Y --> Z[Damper Pulley]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Crank Throw Assembly]
        A1[Gas Force W applied by Con Rod] --> A2[Crank Pin]
        A2 --> A3[Bending Moment Mb on Pin]
        A2 --> A4[Torque T on Pin]
        A3 --> A5[Crank Web Fillets High Stress Zone]
        A4 --> A5
        A5 --> A6[Main Journals]
        A6 --> A7[Main Bearings]

    end

```

## 4.3 Physical Description (Verbal Schematic) of a 4-Cylinder Inline Crankshaft

Since Mermaid cannot render true engineering drawings, here is a precise **text schematic** showing the layout of a 4-cylinder inline crankshaft (4 main bearings, 4 crank pins, **firing order 1-3-4-2**):

```
                FRONT OF ENGINE (PULLEY SIDE)
                        │
        ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
        │     Main Journal #1 (Front)   │  ← Thrust bearing (limits axial play)
        └───────────────┬───────────────┘
                        │
            ╔═══════════╧═══════════╗
            ║   Crank Web (Front)   ║
   ┌────────╫──── Crank Pin #1 ────╫────────┐
   │        ║     (Piston #1)      ║        │  0°  — Firing
   │        ╚═══════════╤═══════════╝        │
   │                    │                    │
        ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
        │    Main Journal #2 (Center)  │
        └───────────────┬───────────────┘
                        │
            ╔═══════════╧═══════════╗
            ║   Crank Web (Center)  ║
   ┌────────╫──── Crank Pin #2 ────╫────────┐
   │        ║     (Piston #3)      ║        │  180°
   │        ╚═══════════╤═══════════╝        │
   │                    │                    │
        ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
        │    Main Journal #3            │
        └───────────────┬───────────────┘
                        │
            ╔═══════════╧═══════════╗
            ║   Crank Web (Rear)    ║
   ┌────────╫──── Crank Pin #3 ────╫────────┐
   │        ║     (Piston #4)      ║        │  360°
   │        ╚═══════════╤═══════════╝        │
   │                    │                    │
        ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
        │    Main Journal #4 (Rear)    │
        └───────────────┬───────────────┘
                        │
        ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
        │   Flywheel Mounting Flange    │
        └───────────────────────────────┘
                        │
                REAR OF ENGINE (FLYWHEEL SIDE)
```

> **Note on Firing Order:** Crank pins #1, #4 are in the same plane (180° from pins #2, #3) so that pistons 1 & 4 move together, and pistons 2 & 3 move together. This naturally gives the 1-3-4-2 firing order when paired with a 4-stroke cam profile.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any four main parts of a crankshaft and state the function of crank webs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four main parts of a crankshaft are:
1. **Main journals** – Cylindrical surfaces supported in the main bearings of the crankcase; they support the crankshaft radially.
2. **Crank pins (crank throws)** – Offset cylindrical surfaces that connect to the big end of the connecting rod; they convert reciprocating motion to rotary motion.
3. **Crank webs (cheeks)** – Flat masses connecting the main journal to the crank pin.
4. **Counterweights** – Masses placed opposite the crank pin to balance centrifugal forces.

**Function of crank webs:** Crank webs connect the main journal to the crank pin and provide the **rigidity (stiffness)** required to transmit combustion force from the crank pin to the main journal without excessive bending deflection. They also provide the mounting surface for **counterweights**.

> **Mark Split (KTU pattern):** 4 parts × 0.5 = 2 marks + function = 1 mark.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What are the materials commonly used for crankshafts? Why is forging preferred over casting?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Common materials:**
- **Forged alloy steels** – e.g., AISI 4340, EN36, SAE 1053.
- **Ductile (nodular) cast iron** – for low-cost mass-production engines.
- **Bill steel and microalloyed steels** in modern applications.

**Why forging is preferred:**
1. **Superior grain flow** – Forging aligns the steel's grain structure along the load paths, giving higher fatigue strength.
2. **Higher tensile and yield strength** due to refined microstructure.
3. **Better impact resistance** at high RPMs.
4. **Greater reliability** for safety-critical rotating components.

> **Mark Split:** Materials list = 1.5 marks; 2 reasons for forging = 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**A. (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a crankshaft used in a multi-cylinder engine. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke engine has a bore of 100 mm, stroke of 120 mm, and develops a mean effective pressure of 8 bar. The crank pin diameter is 60 mm. Determine the maximum combined stress in the crank pin. Take connecting rod length = 240 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution A(a):

**Construction of a multi-cylinder crankshaft (with reference to a 4-cylinder inline engine):**

1. **Main Journals:** Five cylindrical surfaces supported in five main bearings. The centerline of all main journals defines the crankshaft's axis of rotation. The front main journal acts as a **thrust bearing** to limit axial movement.
2. **Crank Pins:** Four crank pins (one per cylinder), each offset from the main journal axis by a radial distance equal to half the stroke (**R = 60 mm**). The pins are arranged in pairs: pins 1 & 4 are coaxial, and pins 2 & 3 are coaxial, with a 180° angular offset between the two pairs.
3. **Crank Webs:** Eight crank webs (two per crank throw) provide rigidity and connect the crank pin to the adjacent main journals.
4. **Counterweights:** Projections integral with the webs, located opposite the crank pin. They balance the centrifugal force of the rotating throw and partially the reciprocating piston mass.
5. **Oil Galleries:** Internal drilled passages connecting each main journal to the corresponding crank pin for lubrication of the big-end bearing.
6. **Fillet Radii:** Generous radii at all transitions to minimize stress concentration.
7. **Front Nose:** Carries the timing chain sprocket, vibration damper pulley, and crankshaft position sensor reluctor.
8. **Rear Flange:** Bolts to the flywheel.

**Working:**
During the power stroke, gas pressure pushes the piston down, transmitting force through the connecting rod to the crank pin. The crank pin, being offset, applies a tangential force to the crank web, producing a torque. This torque rotates the entire crankshaft, and via the flywheel, transmits power to the transmission. The counterweights ensure smooth, vibration-free rotation at all engine speeds.

**Sketch Description (verbal):**
Draw a horizontal line representing the main journal axis. Mark 5 main journals. Between journals 1–2, 2–3, 3–4, 4–5, place crank pins with offset "R". Add webs and counterweights. Label all parts.

> **Mark Split:** Construction (sketch + 6 labels) = 4 marks; Working = 3 marks.

---

#### Model Solution A(b):

**Given:**
- Bore $D = 100$ mm = 0.10 m
- Stroke $L_s = 120$ mm = 0.12 m → Crank radius $R = 0.06$ m
- Mean effective pressure $p_m = 8$ bar = $8 \times 10^5$ N/m²
- Crank pin diameter $d = 60$ mm = 0.06 m
- Connecting rod length $l = 240$ mm = 0.24 m → $n = l/R = 4$

**Step 1: Calculate the Mean Force on the Piston:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
W = p_m \times A = 8 \times 10^5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 6283.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum Tangential Force (Approximation):**
For a conservative design, the maximum tangential force is taken equal to the gas force on the piston (worst case at TDC).

$$
W_{t, max} \approx W = 6283.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Bending Moment on the Crank Pin:**

$$
M_b = W_{t, max} \times R = 6283.2 \times 0.06 = 377.0 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4: Torque on the Crank Pin:**

$$
T = W_{t, max} \times R = 377.0 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5: Maximum Combined Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left[M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}\right]
$$

$$
\sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{377.0^2 + 377.0^2} = 377.0 \sqrt{2} = 533.05 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi (0.06)^3}[377.0 + 533.05]
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}} \times 910.05
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{16 \times 910.05}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{14560.8}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 21.46 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $\sigma_{max} \approx 21.46$ MPa. Since this is far below the fatigue strength of forged alloy steel (~450 MPa), the crank pin is **safe in operation**.

> **Mark Split:** Force calculation = 2 marks, Bending moment = 1 mark, Torque = 1 mark, Stress formula substitution = 2 marks, Final answer = 1 mark.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**B. (a)** Explain the various types of crankshafts with neat sketches. Compare forged steel and cast iron crankshafts. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** A crankshaft of a single-cylinder 4-stroke diesel engine has a stroke of 150 mm, crank pin diameter 80 mm, and maximum gas force of 45 kN. The connecting rod length is 350 mm. Calculate: (i) Bending stress in the crank pin, (ii) Shear stress due to torsion, (iii) Maximum combined principal stress. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution B(a):

**Types of Crankshafts:**

**1. Based on Number of Throws:**
- **Single-throw crankshaft:** Has one crank pin and is used in single-cylinder engines (motorcycles, pumps, small generators).
- **Multi-throw crankshaft:** Has multiple crank pins, used in multi-cylinder engines. In inline engines, crank pins are arranged along a single axis.

**2. Based on Construction:**
- **Integral (one-piece) crankshaft:** Forged or cast as a single unit. Standard for automotive engines due to strength and reliability.
- **Built-up crankshaft:** Crank pins are shrunk-fit or bolted into a separate "spider" or multi-throw web assembly. Used in large 2-stroke marine diesel engines (e.g., 80 cm bore ship engines).

**3. Based on Arrangement:**
- **Inline crankshaft** for inline 3, 4, 6-cylinder engines.
- **V-type crankshaft** for V6, V8 engines — with crank pins offset at 90° between banks.
- **Flat (boxer) crankshaft** for horizontally opposed engines.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Forged Steel Crankshaft | Cast Iron (Ductile) Crankshaft |
|---|---|---|
| Material | AISI 4340, EN36, alloy steel | SG iron (spheroidal graphite iron) |
| Strength | Higher tensile and fatigue strength | 70–80% of forged steel |
| Manufacturing cost | Higher (forging dies, machining) | Lower (cast + finish machined) |
| Weight | Slightly heavier for same strength | Can be lighter by design |
| Damping | Lower internal damping | Better vibration damping (graphite) |
| Application | Passenger cars, performance engines, CV joints | Low-cost mass-market engines, some trucks |
| Grain structure | Continuous grain flow → better fatigue | Random — lower fatigue resistance |

> **Mark Split:** Types = 3 marks; Comparison = 4 marks (8 points × 0.5).

---

#### Model Solution B(b):

**Given:**
- Stroke $L_s = 150$ mm → $R = 0.075$ m
- Crank pin diameter $d = 80$ mm = 0.08 m
- Maximum gas force $W = 45$ kN = 45,000 N
- Connecting rod length $l = 350$ mm = 0.35 m

**Step 1: Bending Moment on the Crank Pin:**

$$
M_b = W \cdot R = 45{,}000 \times 0.075 = 3375 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(i) Bending Stress:**

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 3375}{\pi \times (0.08)^3}
$$

$$
\sigma_b = \frac{108{,}000}{\pi \times 5.12 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{108{,}000}{1.6085 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_b \approx 67.14 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 2: Torque on the Crank Pin:**

$$
T = W \cdot R = 3375 \text{ N·m}
$$

**(ii) Torsional Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 3375}{\pi \times (0.08)^3}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac{54{,}000}{1.6085 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau \approx 33.57 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Combined Principal Stress:**

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left[M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}\right]
$$

$$
\sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2} = \sqrt{3375^2 + 3375^2} = 3375\sqrt{2} = 4772.9 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\sigma_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi (0.08)^3}\left[3375 + 4772.9\right] = \frac{16 \times 8147.9}{1.6085 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 81.04 \text{ MPa}}
$$

> **Mark Split (each sub-part):** Formula = 0.5, Substitution = 0.5, Final answer = 0.5 (per sub-question × 3 = 4.5 marks); Identification and approach = 2.5 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (most common):** Students often mix mm and m. **Always convert bore, stroke, and diameter to metres** before substituting into any stress formula. Forgetting this loses **2–3 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Confusing piston area calculation:** Use $A = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2$ (bore, not stroke) — a frequent trap in the question.
> 3. **Forgetting the square root in combined stress:** Many students write $M_b + T$ instead of $M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}$. The principal stress formula **must** include the square root — this is a 2-mark deduction in KTU scripts.
> 4. **Mean effective pressure units:** 1 bar = $10^5$ N/m². Always show this conversion.
> 5. **Failing to label the sketch:** A neat sketch without labels = 0 marks for that part. Always label **all parts of the crankshaft** in any 7-mark descriptive question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this in your mind before every KTU ESE attempt:**

- [x] **Crankshaft** converts reciprocating piston motion into rotary motion; transmits torque to flywheel.
- [x] **Key parts:** Main journals, crank pins, crank webs, counterweights, oil holes, fillets, front nose, rear flange.
- [x] **Material of choice:** Forged alloy steel (AISI 4340 / EN36) — best strength-to-cost ratio.
- [x] **Integral crankshafts** are standard in modern automotive engines; built-up types for marine diesels.
- [x] **Crank radius** $R$ = ½ stroke.
- [x] **Firing order** of a 4-cylinder inline engine (typical): **1-3-4-2**, with pins 1-4 in one plane and 2-3 in the other (180° apart).
- [x] **Critical stress location:** The **fillet** between the crank web and crank pin.
- [x] **Combined stress formula** (memorize verbatim):
  $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi d^3}\left[M_b + \sqrt{M_b^2 + T^2}\right]$$
- [x] **Centrifugal force on rotating throw:** $F_c = m R \omega^2$ — must be balanced by counterweights.
- [x] **Lubrication path:** Oil pump → main gallery → main journal → drilled passage → crank pin → big-end bearing.
- [x] **Improvement methods** for fatigue life: Larger fillet radius, shot peening, induction hardening, superfinishing.
- [x] **Common failure modes:** Fillet fatigue cracking, bearing wear, oil hole cracking, torsional fatigue.
- [x] **Always convert units** to SI (metres, Newtons, Pascals) before formula substitution.
- [x] **Torsional vibration damper** is mounted on the front of the crankshaft to control torsional oscillations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flywheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Flywheel # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **flywheel** is a heavy, rotating mechanical disc or rim mounted rigidly on the crankshaft of an internal combustion (IC) engine, between the engine and the transmission (clutch assembly). Its primary function is to **store rotational kinetic energy** during the power stroke and **release it** during the idle strokes (intake, compression, exhaust), thereby smoothing out the fluctuating torque output and maintaining a near-uniform angular velocity of the crankshaft. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A flywheel is treated as a **rotating energy reservoir**. It does not add energy to the system; it merely *redistributes* the energy already supplied by the combustion of fuel across the full 720° (4-stroke) or 360° (2-stroke) cycle. In engineering notation, the **mean kinetic energy** stored in a flywheel rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ is: $$ E = \frac{1}{2} \, I \, \omega^{2} $$ where $I$ is the **mass moment of inertia** of the flywheel about the axis of rotation (kg·m²) and $\omega$ is the **angular velocity** in rad/s. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – The "Mechanical Battery":** > Imagine a cyclist riding a bicycle up a hill. During pedaling (the power stroke), the cyclist's legs deliver a strong, sudden push. Without anything to absorb this push, the bike would jerk forward violently. However, the **spinning wheels act as a flywheel** — they store some of the pedaling energy as rotational kinetic energy and release it smoothly when the cyclist stops pedaling or coasts downhill. The wheels keep spinning, providing a steady forward motion. > > In an IC engine, only **one cylinder fires per power stroke** (in a 4-cylinder, every 180°). The flywheel is the "bicycle wheel" of the engine: it absorbs the impulsive torque from combustion and returns it during the three idle strokes, keeping the crankshaft turning smoothly at a nearly constant RPM. ## 1.3 Secondary Functions of a Flywheel 1. **Energy smoothing** – bridges the gap between power and idle strokes. 2. **Crankshaft support** – acts as a large bearing surface and adds rotational stiffness. 3. **Clutch mounting platform** – the rear face provides a friction surface for the clutch disc (in manual transmission vehicles) or a ring gear for the starter motor. 4. **Engine starting assistance** – the starter motor ring gear (machined into the outer rim) is used to crank the engine; the flywheel's inertia helps the engine pass through the compression stroke. 5. **Torsional vibration damping** – reduces high-frequency crankshaft vibrations. 6. **Balance mass contribution** – a small portion of the rotating assembly imbalance is compensated. ## 1.4 Standard Materials & Metrics | Property | Typical Value / Material | Remarks | |----------|--------------------------|---------| | Material | **Cast iron**, **ductile iron**, **forged steel**, **aluminum alloys** (light-duty) | Cast iron is most common due to cost & damping | | Density ($\rho$) | Cast iron: **7200 kg/m³**, Steel: **7850 kg/m³** | Used in rim stress calculations | | Outer diameter | **300 mm – 500 mm** (passenger cars) | Truck flywheels can exceed 600 mm | | Mass | **8 kg – 25 kg** (passenger cars) | Heavier for higher-torque diesel engines | | Rim velocity ($v$) | Limited to **30 – 70 m/s** | Beyond ~100 m/s, rim may burst | | Working temperature | –40 °C to **+200 °C** (clutch face) | Thermal fatigue from clutch slip | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight:** A heavier flywheel = smoother engine operation + slower throttle response (heavier to spin up/down). A lighter flywheel = faster revving + more pronounced torque pulsations. This is why **diesel engines** (high torque, low RPM) use **heavier** flywheels and **racing engines** use **lightweight** chromoly or aluminum flywheels. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Need for a Flywheel – Why Torque Fluctuates In a multi-cylinder IC engine, the **indicated torque** (torque from gas pressure) varies cyclically with crank angle, while the **load torque** (resistance from the driven machinery) is assumed constant (or slowly varying). This mismatch creates a **fluctuation of energy** that must be absorbed or released by the flywheel. During the **power stroke**, the indicated torque exceeds the load torque → **excess energy is stored** in the flywheel (speed increases). During the **idle strokes**, the indicated torque is less than the load torque → **deficit energy is withdrawn** from the flywheel (speed decreases). ## 2.2 Key Definitions (Exam-Critical Vocabulary) Let $N_{max} = N_2$ and $N_{min} = N_1$ (in rpm). Then: **Mean speed:** $$ N = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2} $$ **Coefficient of fluctuation of speed ($C_S$ or $C_N$):** $$ C_S = \frac{N_2 - N_1}{N} = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\omega} $$ **Coefficient of fluctuation of energy ($C_E$):** $$ C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{W} $$ where $\Delta E$ is the **maximum fluctuation of energy** (Joules) and $W$ is the **work done per cycle** (Joules). ## 2.3 Master Equation: Energy Fluctuation of a Flywheel For a flywheel with mass moment of inertia $I$ rotating at mean angular speed $\omega$: **Maximum kinetic energy stored:** $$ E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \, I \, \omega_2^{2} $$ **Minimum kinetic energy stored:** $$ E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} \, I \, \omega_1^{2} $$ **Maximum fluctuation of energy:** $$ \Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} \, I \left( \omega_2^{2} - \omega_1^{2} \right) $$ Using the identity $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$: $$ \Delta E = \frac{1}{2} \, I (\omega_2 - \omega_1)(\omega_2 + \omega_1) $$ Since $\omega_2 - \omega_1 = C_S \cdot \omega$ and $\omega_2 + \omega_1 \approx 2\omega$: $$ \boxed{\Delta E = I \, \omega^{2} \, C_S} $$ This is the **most important equation** in flywheel design and is tested almost every semester in KTU examinations. ## 2.4 Rim-Type Flywheel Simplification If the flywheel mass $m$ is assumed concentrated at the rim of mean radius $R$ (radius of gyration $k \approx R$): $$ I = m k^{2} \approx m R^{2} $$ $$ \Delta E = m R^{2} \omega^{2} C_S $$ Since the **rim velocity** is $v = R \omega$: $$ \boxed{\Delta E = m \, v^{2} \, C_S} $$ This is the designer's quick-equation for sizing a flywheel mass once the permissible rim velocity is fixed. ## 2.5 Centrifugal (Hoop) Stress in the Flywheel Rim The spinning rim is subjected to centrifugal force. For a thin rim rotating at velocity $v$: $$ \sigma_t = \rho \, v^{2} \quad \text{(Pa, or N/m}^2\text{)} $$ where $\rho$ is the density of the rim material. > [!IMPORTANT] > The hoop stress grows with the **square** of rim speed. This is why rim velocity — not RPM — is the true design limit. Doubling the speed quadruples the stress. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Remarks | |---|----------|--------|---------|------|---------| | 1 | Mean speed | $N$ | $(N_1 + N_2)/2$ | rpm | Arithmetic mean of extremes | | 2 | Fluctuation of speed coeff. | $C_S$ | $(N_2 - N_1)/N$ | — | Dimensionless; typically **0.01 to 0.05** | | 3 | Fluctuation of energy coeff. | $C_E$ | $\Delta E / W$ | — | Used when work per cycle is known | | 4 | Max. energy fluctuation | $\Delta E$ | $I \omega^{2} C_S$ | J | Core design equation | | 5 | Mass moment of inertia | $I$ | $m k^{2}$ | kg·m² | $k$ = radius of gyration | | 6 | Rim-type simplification | $\Delta E$ | $m v^{2} C_S$ | J | Valid when $k = R$ | | 7 | Rim velocity | $v$ | $\pi D N / 60$ | m/s | $D$ = mean rim diameter (m) | | 8 | Centrifugal (hoop) stress | $\sigma_t$ | $\rho v^{2}$ | Pa | Limiting design criterion | | 9 | Mean kinetic energy | $E$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | J | Energy at mean speed | | 10 | Max KE / Min KE ratio | $E_{max}/E_{min}$ | $(\omega_2 / \omega_1)^{2}$ | — | For finding speed ratio | > [!WARNING] > **Notation Trap:** Some textbooks (Khurmi, Shigley) use the symbol $C_E$ for **coefficient of fluctuation of energy**, while others (Bhandari, KTU preferred) reserve $C_E$ for the same but use $\Delta E$ as the **maximum** fluctuation. **Always** state the definition before using the symbol in your exam answer. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Engine design**: Determines flywheel mass to limit $C_S$ within acceptable vibration limits. - **Punch presses & shears**: Energy released by flywheel performs intermittent high-power tasks. - **Hybrid vehicles**: The KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery System) uses a small **electric flywheel** (gyrobus) or ultracapacitor as a modern analog. - **Wind turbines**: The rotor itself acts as a flywheel, smoothing out gust-induced power swings. - **Power plants**: Large diesel gensets use massive flywheels to bridge momentary fuel interruptions. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Fluctuation of Energy ($\Delta E = I \omega^{2} C_S$) **Step 1 – Start with stored kinetic energy at any instant.** The rotational kinetic energy of the flywheel at angular velocity $\omega$ is: $$ E = \frac{1}{2} \, I \, \omega^{2} $$ **Step 2 – Write energy at maximum and minimum speeds.** At the maximum speed $\omega_2$ (end of power stroke), the energy is: $$ E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \, I \, \omega_2^{2} $$ At the minimum speed $\omega_1$ (end of idle strokes), the energy is: $$ E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} \, I \, \omega_1^{2} $$ **Step 3 – Subtract to get the maximum fluctuation of energy.** $$ \Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} I \left( \omega_2^{2} - \omega_1^{2} \right) $$ **Step 4 – Factor the difference of squares.** $$ \Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_2 - \omega_1)(\omega_2 + \omega_1) $$ **Step 5 – Introduce the mean angular speed.** By definition, the mean speed is: $$ \omega = \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_1 + \omega_2 = 2\omega $$ **Step 6 – Introduce the coefficient of fluctuation of speed.** By definition: $$ C_S = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\omega} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_2 - \omega_1 = C_S \, \omega $$ **Step 7 – Substitute back.** $$ \Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (C_S \, \omega)(2\omega) = I \omega^{2} C_S $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta E = I \, \omega^{2} \, C_S} \qquad \text{(Q.E.D.)} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Centrifugal (Hoop) Stress in a Thin Rim **Step 1 – Consider a thin rim segment of length $L$ subtending an angle $d\theta$ at the centre.** Mass of the segment: $$ dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot L = \rho \cdot A \cdot R \, d\theta $$ where $A$ is the rim cross-sectional area and $R$ is the mean radius. **Step 2 – The segment undergoes uniform circular motion. Net inward force required is the centripetal force.** $$ dF_c = dm \cdot \omega^{2} R = \rho A R^{2} \omega^{2} \, d\theta $$ **Step 3 – This centripetal force is supplied by the hoop (tensile) stress $\sigma_t$ acting on the two cut faces, each of area $A$.** The two radial components of the stress (each face has stress $\sigma_t$ pulling on it) provide: $$ 2 \sigma_t A \sin(d\theta/2) \approx \sigma_t A \, d\theta $$ (using $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$ for small angles). **Step 4 – Equate centripetal force to stress resultant.** $$ \sigma_t A \, d\theta = \rho A R^{2} \omega^{2} \, d\theta $$ **Step 5 – Cancel $A \, d\theta$ from both sides.** $$ \sigma_t = \rho R^{2} \omega^{2} = \rho (R\omega)^{2} $$ **Step 6 – Recognize the rim velocity.** $$ v = R \omega $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_t = \rho \, v^{2}} \qquad \text{(Q.E.D.)} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rim-Type Mass in terms of Permissible Velocity Rearranging the rim-type energy equation for mass $m$: $$ m = \frac{\Delta E}{v^{2} \, C_S} $$ This is the **direct design equation**: given a target $\Delta E$, a maximum allowable $v$, and a specified $C_S$, one solves for $m$. The corresponding $D$ is then: $$ D = \frac{60 v}{\pi N} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A 4-stroke 4-cylinder IC engine develops **30 kW** at a mean speed of **1500 rpm**. The maximum fluctuation of energy is **1/15 of the work done per cycle**. The fluctuation of speed is **±2% of the mean speed**. The mean rim diameter is **0.6 m**. Find: (a) The mass moment of inertia of the flywheel. (b) The mass of the flywheel if treated as a rim. (c) The hoop stress in the rim, given $\rho = 7200$ kg/m³ for cast iron. **Solution:** **Part (a) – Moment of inertia.** Work done per cycle: $$ W = \frac{30\,000 \text{ W}}{1500/60 \times 2} \quad (\text{4-stroke: 1 cycle = 2 rev}) $$ $$ W = \frac{30\,000}{50} = 600 \text{ J} $$ Maximum fluctuation of energy: $$ \Delta E = C_E \cdot W = \frac{1}{15} \times 600 = 40 \text{ J} $$ Mean angular velocity: $$ \omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 50 \pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s} $$ Coefficient of fluctuation of speed: $$ C_S = \frac{N_2 - N_1}{N} = 2\% + 2\% = 0.04 $$ Solving for $I$ from $\Delta E = I \omega^{2} C_S$: $$ I = \frac{\Delta E}{\omega^{2} C_S} = \frac{40}{(157.08)^{2} \times 0.04} $$ $$ I = \frac{40}{24\,674 \times 0.04} = \frac{40}{986.96} \approx 0.0405 \text{ kg·m}^{2} $$ **Part (b) – Mass of rim-type flywheel.** Mean rim radius: $$ R = D/2 = 0.3 \text{ m} $$ $$ I = m R^{2} \Rightarrow m = \frac{I}{R^{2}} = \frac{0.0405}{(0.3)^{2}} = \frac{0.0405}{0.09} = 0.45 \text{ kg} $$ **Part (c) – Hoop stress in the rim.** Rim velocity: $$ v = R \omega = 0.3 \times 157.08 = 47.12 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ \sigma_t = \rho v^{2} = 7200 \times (47.12)^{2} = 7200 \times 2220.3 \approx 15.99 \text{ MPa} $$ **Sanity check:** Cast iron ultimate tensile strength is 150–400 MPa, so a working stress of ~16 MPa gives a factor of safety of ~10 — extremely safe. ✓ | Quantity | Value | Unit | |----------|-------|------| | Work per cycle $W$ | 600 | J | | $\Delta E$ | 40 | J | | $C_S$ | 0.04 | — | | $\omega$ | 157.08 | rad/s | | Moment of inertia $I$ | 0.0405 | kg·m² | | Rim mass $m$ | 0.45 | kg | | Rim velocity $v$ | 47.12 | m/s | | Hoop stress $\sigma_t$ | 15.99 | MPa | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture – Flywheel in Engine Powertrain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Engine Block
Cylinders 1 to N] -->|Power Stroke
Torque Pulse| B[Crankshaft] B --> C[Rear Main Bearing] C --> D[Flywheel Assembly] D --> D1[Rotating Mass
Stores Kinetic Energy] D --> D2[Ring Gear
Starter Motor Engagement] D --> D3[Clutch Friction Face
or Flexplate] D3 --> E[Clutch Disc
Manual Transmission] D3 --> F[Torque Converter
Automatic Transmission] E --> G[Gearbox Input Shaft] F --> G G --> H[Driveshaft / Wheels] subgraph FL[Flywheel Internal Layers] D1 D2 D3 end ``` ## 4.2 Energy Flow Block Diagram – How the Flywheel Smooths Torque ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Power Stroke Begins] --> Q1{Indicated Torque
Greater than
Load Torque?} Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Excess Energy Stored
in Flywheel Kinetic Energy
Speed Rises to N2] S1 --> P2[Idle Stroke Starts] P2 --> Q2{Indicated Torque
Less than
Load Torque?} Q2 -- Yes --> S2[Energy Withdrawn
from Flywheel
Speed Falls to N1] S2 --> P1 Q1 -- No --> P2 Q2 -- No --> P1 S1 -.Maximum KE.-> K1[Emax = 0.5 I omega2 squared] S2 -.Minimum KE.-> K2[Emin = 0.5 I omega1 squared] K1 --> DIF[DeltaE = Emax minus Emin
= I omega squared Cs] K2 --> DIF ``` ## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph X[Flywheel Cross Section] A1[Outer Rim
Stores Most Mass
High Centrifugal Stress] --> A2[Web Disc
Connects Rim to Hub
Transmits Torque] A2 --> A3[Central Hub
Bolted to Crankshaft Flange] A3 --> A4[Ring Gear
Pressed onto Rim
Driven by Starter Motor] end STRESS[Rim Hoop Stress
sigma subscript t = rho v squared] -.applies to.-> A1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Notice the block diagrams above deliberately avoid complex mechanical sketches. In your answer sheet, you may draw either (i) a simple labelled cross-section, or (ii) a torque vs. crank-angle diagram showing fluctuation — both earn full credit. Always **label** axes (Torque on y-axis, Crank angle on x-axis) and mark $T_{max}$, $T_{min}$, $T_{mean}$, and the shaded "excess" and "deficit" areas (which correspond to $\Delta E$). ## 4.4 Torque Diagram Convention (Textual Description) - **y-axis:** Indicated torque $T_i$ (N·m) - **x-axis:** Crank angle $\theta$ (degrees) - A horizontal line at $T_{mean}$ represents the constant load torque. - The wavy curve is the indicated torque (positive during power stroke, near zero during idle strokes). - The area **above** $T_{mean}$ enclosed by the curve = maximum **excess** energy (energy stored in flywheel). - The area **below** $T_{mean}$ enclosed by the curve = maximum **deficit** energy (energy released by flywheel). - By construction, the maximum excess = maximum deficit = $\Delta E$. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Define the term "coefficient of fluctuation of speed" for a flywheel. State its typical range for IC engines.** **Model Answer:** The coefficient of fluctuation of speed ($C_S$ or $C_N$) is defined as the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed (difference between maximum and minimum speeds) to the mean speed of the flywheel. $$ C_S = \frac{N_2 - N_1}{N} = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\omega} $$ It is a **dimensionless** quantity. For IC engines, its typical range is **0.01 to 0.05** (1% to 5%). For precision machinery (e.g., printing presses), it can be as low as **0.001**, while for pumps and punches it may be 0.05–0.10. *(3 marks – 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for typical range)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **List any four functions of a flywheel in an IC engine.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Energy storage and smoothing** – stores kinetic energy during the power stroke and releases it during idle strokes, maintaining uniform speed. 2. **Clutch mounting** – provides a flat friction surface (or ring gear for automatic transmissions) for the clutch assembly. 3. **Starting assistance** – the ring gear on the outer periphery engages with the starter motor pinion to crank the engine. 4. **Torsional vibration damping** – reduces high-frequency vibrations of the crankshaft. *(3 marks – ½ mark each; mention 4 functions. Add ½ mark for a brief note on one of them.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum fluctuation of energy** of a flywheel in terms of its mass moment of inertia, angular velocity, and coefficient of fluctuation of speed. State any two assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-stroke 4-cylinder IC engine running at **1800 rpm** develops **22 kW**. The mean diameter of the flywheel is **0.45 m**, and the permissible rim speed is **30 m/s**. The coefficient of fluctuation of speed is **0.04**. Determine: **(i)** the moment of inertia, **(ii)** the mass of the flywheel (rim-type), and **(iii)** the hoop stress if the rim is made of cast iron ($\rho = 7200$ kg/m³). **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer to Question A **Part (a) — Derivation:** **Assumptions:** 1. The flywheel is a rigid, perfectly balanced rotating disc. 2. The mean angular velocity $\omega$ is constant and the fluctuation is small (so $\omega_1 + \omega_2 \approx 2\omega$). **Derivation (Full Steps):** The kinetic energy of a flywheel at angular velocity $\omega$ is: $$ E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} $$ At maximum and minimum speeds: $$ E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_2^{2}, \qquad E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_1^{2} $$ Maximum fluctuation of energy: $$ \Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_2^{2} - \omega_1^{2}) $$ Factor: $$ \Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_2 - \omega_1)(\omega_2 + \omega_1) $$ Substitute $(\omega_2 + \omega_1) = 2\omega$ and $(\omega_2 - \omega_1) = C_S \omega$: $$ \Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (C_S \omega)(2\omega) $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta E = I \, \omega^{2} C_S} $$ *(7 marks – 1 for assumptions, 4 for derivation, 2 for boxed final equation with units and meaning.)* --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $N = 1800$ rpm, $P = 22$ kW, 4-stroke, $D = 0.45$ m, $v_{max} = 30$ m/s, $C_S = 0.04$, $\rho = 7200$ kg/m³. **Step 1 – Mean angular velocity:** $$ \omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1800}{60} = 60\pi \approx 188.5 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 2 – Work done per cycle (4-stroke = 1 power stroke per 2 revolutions):** $$ W = \frac{P \times 60}{N/2} = \frac{22\,000 \times 60}{900} = 1466.67 \text{ J} $$ **Step 3 – Rim velocity check & adjustment.** Given $v = 30$ m/s. **Step 4 – Maximum fluctuation of energy.** (Use $\Delta E = m v^{2} C_S$ with the rim form, but first compute via $W$ for cross-check.) For design, assume $\Delta E$ is not given directly. We use the relation $\Delta E = I \omega^{2} C_S$. To find $I$, we need $\Delta E$. From the rim velocity, $R = D/2 = 0.225$ m. If we treat the flywheel as a rim, the moment of inertia is $I = mR^2$. **Method:** For a *given* design problem with $C_S$ and $v$, the mass is computed using the relationship between $\Delta E$ and $W$. Without an explicit $C_E$ given, we use the standard design formulation: $$ m = \frac{\Delta E}{v^{2} C_S}, \qquad \Delta E = C_E \cdot W $$ If we assume $C_E = \frac{1}{15}$ (typical for 4-cylinder engines), then: $$ \Delta E = \frac{1466.67}{15} \approx 97.78 \text{ J} $$ $$ m = \frac{97.78}{(30)^{2} \times 0.04} = \frac{97.78}{36} \approx 2.72 \text{ kg} $$ $$ I = m R^{2} = 2.72 \times (0.225)^{2} = 2.72 \times 0.0506 \approx 0.138 \text{ kg·m}^{2} $$ **Step 5 – Hoop stress:** $$ \sigma_t = \rho v^{2} = 7200 \times 900 = 6.48 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 6.48 \text{ MPa} $$ | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | $\omega$ | 188.5 rad/s | | $W$ | 1466.67 J | | $\Delta E$ | 97.78 J | | Mass $m$ | 2.72 kg | | $I$ | 0.138 kg·m² | | $\sigma_t$ | 6.48 MPa | *(7 marks – distributed across given-data identification, formula selection, substitution, and final values.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[Internal Choice Alternative]** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction of a **solid disc flywheel** and a **rim-type flywheel**. Compare them. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke engine has a mean speed of **240 rpm** and the indicated torque varies from **+200 N·m to –50 N·m** in one cycle. The work done per cycle is **6000 J**. Calculate: **(i)** the coefficient of fluctuation of energy, **(ii)** the required moment of inertia of the flywheel if $C_S = 0.05$, and **(iii)** the diameter of the rim if the permissible rim velocity is **25 m/s** and the flywheel is rim-type. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer to Question B **Part (a) — Solid Disc vs. Rim-Type Flywheel:** **Solid disc flywheel:** - Made of a single thick disc of cast iron or steel, keyed/bolted to the crankshaft flange. - Used in low-power applications and small engines. - **Advantages:** Simple, cheap, easy to manufacture, self-balancing. - **Disadvantages:** Heavier for the same moment of inertia (mass distributed at small radii contributes little to $I$). **Rim-type flywheel:** - Comprises a heavy **rim** (most mass concentrated at large radius), a thin **web/arm disc**, and a **hub** mounted on the crankshaft. - Mass concentrated at the rim maximizes $I = mR^{2}$ for a given $m$. - **Advantages:** Higher moment of inertia per unit mass, lower material cost, can be fitted with a ring gear and clutch face. - **Disadvantages:** More complex to cast; rim may burst at high speeds; spokes transmit torque and must be designed for shear. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Solid Disc | Rim Type | |---------|-----------|----------| | Mass concentration | Uniform | At rim | | $I$ per kg | Low | High | | Manufacturing | Simple | Complex | | Cost | Low | Moderate | | Typical use | Small engines | Cars, industrial | *(7 marks – 2 for solid, 2 for rim, 2 for comparison table, 1 for neat sketch description.)* --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $N = 240$ rpm, $T_{max} = 200$ N·m, $T_{min} = -50$ N·m, $W = 6000$ J, $C_S = 0.05$, $v = 25$ m/s, 4-stroke. **Step 1 – Mean angular velocity:** $$ \omega = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 8\pi \approx 25.13 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 2 – Coefficient of fluctuation of energy:** $$ C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{W} $$ We compute $\Delta E$ from the torque diagram. For a single cylinder, the maximum excess torque area = maximum deficit torque area = $\Delta E$. A simple approximation for a single-cylinder engine with sinusoidal torque variation: $$ \Delta E = \frac{1}{2} (T_{max} - T_{mean}) \times \theta_{excess} + \frac{1}{2} (T_{mean} - T_{min}) \times \theta_{deficit} $$ Assuming the areas of excess and deficit are equal (which they must be for steady-state operation), and approximating the angular extent of each to be $\pi$ radians (half-cycle each), we have: $$ T_{mean} = \frac{T_{max} + T_{min}}{2} = \frac{200 + (-50)}{2} = 75 \text{ N·m} $$ Excess torque magnitude = $200 - 75 = 125$ N·m Deficit torque magnitude = $75 - (-50) = 125$ N·m (Symmetric, as expected.) Each half-cycle (excess or deficit) spans $\pi$ radians: $$ \Delta E = \frac{1}{2} \times 125 \times \pi = 62.5\pi \approx 196.35 \text{ J} $$ $$ C_E = \frac{196.35}{6000} \approx 0.0327 $$ *(Alternative: some textbooks compute $\Delta E$ differently for a single cylinder. Use whichever the lecturer specifies. Many problems give $\Delta E$ directly.)* **Step 3 – Required moment of inertia:** $$ I = \frac{\Delta E}{\omega^{2} C_S} = \frac{196.35}{(25.13)^{2} \times 0.05} = \frac{196.35}{631.65 \times 0.05} = \frac{196.35}{31.58} \approx 6.22 \text{ kg·m}^{2} $$ **Step 4 – Diameter of the rim (rim-type):** For rim-type: $I = m R^{2}$ and $\Delta E = m v^{2} C_S$. We need $R$ or $D$. From $v = R \omega$: $$ R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{25}{25.13} \approx 0.995 \text{ m} $$ $$ D = 2R \approx 1.99 \text{ m} $$ **Sanity check:** Such a large flywheel is plausible for a slow-speed single-cylinder engine (e.g., a large marine auxiliary). The $C_E$ value (~3.3%) is also typical. | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | $\omega$ | 25.13 rad/s | | $\Delta E$ | 196.35 J | | $C_E$ | 0.0327 | | $I$ | 6.22 kg·m² | | $D$ | 1.99 m | *(7 marks – distributed across identification of mean torque, energy calculation, $C_E$, $I$, and $D$.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Mixing up $C_S$ and $C_E$** — they are not interchangeable. $C_S$ relates to *speed*; $C_E$ relates to *energy per cycle*. Using one in place of the other in $\Delta E = I \omega^{2} C_S$ will lead to a wrong answer. > 2. **Forgetting to halve the RPM for 4-stroke engines** — a 4-stroke engine completes only **one power cycle per 2 revolutions**. Many students forget this and compute $W$ incorrectly. > 3. **Using $N$ directly instead of $\omega$** — always convert rpm to rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ before substituting into $I \omega^{2} C_S$. > 4. **Wrong sign for deficit torque** — if the question gives torque values including negative values (engine motoring), do not drop the sign. > 5. **Confusing $k$ (radius of gyration) with $R$ (rim radius)** — for a rim-type flywheel, $k \approx R$, but for a solid disc, $k = R/\sqrt{2}$. The KTU expected answer uses $k = R$ only for rim-type. > 6. **Unit mismatches** — $N$ must be in rpm when computing $W$ from $P$ (kW), but $\omega$ must be in rad/s when computing $I \omega^{2} C_S$. Be very careful. > 7. **Skipping the diagram** — a labelled torque-vs-angle diagram in the answer fetches easy 1–2 marks, even on numerical questions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - A **flywheel** is a rotating energy reservoir attached to the crankshaft of an IC engine. - Its primary function is to **smooth out torque fluctuations** between power and idle strokes. - **$C_S$** = (max speed – min speed) / mean speed. Typical for IC engines: **0.01 – 0.05**. - **$C_E$** = max energy fluctuation / work per cycle. Typical: **0.05 – 0.20** (small engines higher). - **Master equation:** $\Delta E = I \omega^{2} C_S$ — the single most important formula in this module. - **Rim-type simplification:** $\Delta E = m v^{2} C_S$ — used when mass is concentrated at the rim. - **Rim velocity:** $v = \pi D N / 60 = R \omega$ — typically capped at **30–70 m/s** in design. - **Hoop stress in rim:** $\sigma_t = \rho v^{2}$ — grows with the **square** of velocity. - **Work per cycle:** $W = P \times 60 / (N/n_{stroke})$ where $n_{stroke} = 1$ for 2-stroke, $2$ for 4-stroke. - **Mean speed:** $N = (N_1 + N_2)/2$ — the design point about which speed fluctuates. - **More cylinders = smoother torque = lighter flywheel** (a V8 needs a smaller flywheel than a single-cylinder engine of the same power). - **Diesel engines use heavier flywheels** than petrol engines due to lower RPM and higher torque pulsations. - **Aluminum flywheels** (lighter) are used in **racing** for faster revving; cast iron (heavier) for road vehicles. - **Clutch face** is machined on the rear face of the flywheel in manual-transmission vehicles. - **Ring gear** is pressed/bolted onto the outer rim for starter motor engagement. - Always **label the torque-vs-angle diagram** in exam answers; it is worth 1–2 easy marks. - Always **state assumptions** before derivations (rigid body, small fluctuation, $k = R$ for rim-type, etc.). - Watch for **unit conversions**: rpm ↔ rad/s, mm ↔ m, kW ↔ W. - The flywheel does **not add energy** — it only *redistributes* kinetic energy within the cycle.

topics.title: Main Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Main Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Main Bearings** are the precision-engineered, semi-cylindrical support bearings (also called *crankshaft main bearings* or *main journal bearings*) that house the main journals of the crankshaft inside the engine's cylinder block (sump side) and bearing caps. Their primary function is to **support the rotating crankshaft, transmit combustion and inertial loads from the crank webs to the engine structure, and provide a hydrodynamic oil film that permits near-frictionless rotation** of the crankshaft at speeds typically ranging from **600 rpm (idle) to 6000+ rpm (redline)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> *Main bearings are plain hydrodynamic journal bearings fitted between the main journals of the crankshaft and the engine block/bearing caps. They locate the crankshaft radially, support combustion and inertia loads, and permit rotation with a pressurized oil film.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are spinning a heavy iron rod (the **crankshaft**) inside a heavy iron frame (the **engine block**). If the rod just scrapes the frame, friction will weld them together, generate extreme heat, and destroy both. 

Now imagine placing **two ultra-smooth, oil-slicked half-shells** — one below the rod and one above — so the rod actually "floats" on a thin pressurized cushion of oil, like a **hydroplaning car tire on a wet road**. That's exactly what a main bearing does. It never directly touches the crankshaft during normal operation; a microscopic oil wedge (typically **5 to 50 micrometres** thick) separates the two metal surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Main bearings are the "chairs" on which the crankshaft sits. The crankshaft never touches them directly — it floats on engine oil. The bearings are sacrificial: they wear out so the expensive crankshaft does not.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of Main Bearings

- **Radial support** of the crankshaft against combustion pressure and inertia forces.
- **Axial (thrust) location** of the crankshaft (via a flanged half-bearing, usually at the centre main).
- **Hydrodynamic lubrication film generation** to minimize friction and wear.
- **Damping of vibrations** and shock loads from combustion.
- **Heat dissipation** — the oil film carries away frictional heat to the sump.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded)

| Metric | Typical Value (Passenger Car Petrol Engine) |
|---|---|
| Number of main bearings | **4, 5, or 6** (typically one more than cylinders) |
| Bearing diameter (D) | **60 – 80 mm** |
| Bearing axial length (L) | **20 – 35 mm** |
| L/D ratio | **0.4 – 0.6** |
| Radial oil clearance | **0.025 – 0.075 mm** |
| Bearing pressure | **7 – 20 MPa** |
| Oil film thickness (h₀) | **5 – 50 µm** |
| Oil supply pressure | **2 – 4 bar** (≈ 200–400 kPa) |
| Oil temperature at bearing | **80 – 120 °C** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** A V6 engine almost always has **4 main bearings** (not 6), because a 60° V6 shares bearing journals between connecting rods. A V8 typically has **5 main bearings**. A flat-4 (boxer) has **5 main bearings** (4 main + 1 centre thrust).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Main Bearings

Main bearings are categorized as follows (memorize this for KTU 2024 ESE):

**A. By Type of Lubrication**
1. **Hydrodynamic (Plain / Sleeve) Bearings** — Used in virtually all IC engines. Oil film generated by rotation.
2. **Roller / Ball Bearings** — Used only in very small / specialty engines (e.g., model engines, some motorbike crankshafts in pre-mix 2-strokes with separate crank bearing housings). Not standard for automotive main bearings.

**B. By Load Direction Supported**
1. **Radial Bearings** — Support perpendicular (radial) loads.
2. **Thrust Bearings** — Locate the crankshaft axially. The centre main bearing in most engines has an **integral flanged thrust face** (a pair of semicircular thrust collars machined on either side of the bearing shell).

**C. By Construction (Shell Type)**
1. **Solid (Monolithic) Bearings** — Single-piece cylindrical bushing (rare in modern auto).
2. **Split Bearings (Shell Bearings)** — Two halves (upper and lower) bolted together via a bearing cap. **This is the universal automotive construction.**

## 2.2 Detailed Construction of a Main Bearing Assembly

A modern automotive main bearing consists of the following components (top-down):

1. **Bearing Cap** — A high-strength cast iron or forged steel cap that clamps the lower half-shell against the engine block. It is bolted with **two cap bolts** (or studs) torqued to precise values (typically **60 – 110 N·m**).
2. **Lower Bearing Shell (Half-Shell)** — The bottom half of the bearing, often featuring an **oil hole and oil groove** for lubrication supply.
3. **Upper Bearing Shell (Half-Shell)** — The top half, set into the cylinder block's machined saddle.
4. **Bearing Lining** — A multi-layer metallic coating (see §2.3).
5. **Crankshaft Main Journal** — The polished cylindrical surface of the crankshaft.
6. **Thrust Flanges** (on centre bearing only) — Machined shoulders that bear against the crankshaft thrust faces to limit axial movement.
7. **Locating Tang / Notch** — A small protrusion or cutout that prevents the shell from rotating in its housing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The **upper half-shell** is usually the **loaded half** (in most engines, the lower main journal of the crankshaft is loaded due to combustion forces pushing the crank down). The upper shell therefore has a **thinner overlay** because the lower half receives the heaviest hydrodynamic pressure. *Some manufacturers reverse this for specific designs — the key is consistency.*

## 2.3 Bearing Lining Materials — The KTU 2024 Memory Table

| Material | Composition | Hardness (HB) | Fatigue Strength | Embeddability | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **White Metal (Babbitt)** | Sn (≈ 90%) + Sb + Cu | 20 – 30 | Low–Medium | **Excellent** | Older engines, high-speed low-load |
| **Copper-Lead** | Cu (≈ 70%) + Pb | 30 – 60 | High | Good | Diesel engines, heavy duty |
| **Aluminium Alloy** | Al + Sn (≈ 20%) | 45 – 80 | Very High | Poor–Fair | Modern petrol, high-performance |
| **Tri-Metal** | Cu-Pb core + Pb-Sn-Cu overlay | 50 – 80 | Very High | Excellent | High-performance diesel, racing |
| **Overlay (Electroplate)** | Pb-Sn (≈ 85% Pb) | 10 – 15 | Low | **Excellent** | Always 0.005–0.025 mm coating |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Modern bearings are typically **tri-metal** or **aluminium-tin** with a **lead-bronze overlay** only **0.005 – 0.025 mm** thick. The overlay is sacrificial — it embeds dirt particles, preventing them from scoring the crankshaft.

## 2.4 The Hydrodynamic Oil Film — Operational Logic

Step-by-step explanation of how the oil film forms (this is **the most heavily tested concept** in KTU 2024 exams for this module):

1. **At Rest (Engine Off):** The crankshaft journal rests on the bearing shell under gravity. Direct metal-to-metal contact exists.
2. **Cranking / Start-Up:** Engine oil pump delivers oil through the **main oil gallery** → through the **block cross-drillings** → through the **oil hole in the bearing shell**. The journal begins to climb the bearing surface as it rotates (wedge action).
3. **Hydrodynamic Lift-Off:** As rotational speed increases, oil is dragged by viscous adhesion into the converging wedge between journal and shell. Pressure builds up (**Reynolds Equation** governs this) until it exceeds the load on the bearing. The journal lifts off and "floats."
4. **Full-Film Operation:** Above the **minimum film thickness (h₀)**, the journal is fully supported by oil. Contact is theoretically zero. This is the **designed operating regime**.
5. **Boundary / Mixed Lubrication:** During cold starts, oil is cold/viscous but film can be thin; this regime is to be **minimized** by proper oil selection and warm-up.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HALLMARKS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **definitive equations** the KTU 2024 board examiner expects for this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Projected Bearing Area** | $A = L \times D$ | mm² | L = axial length, D = journal diameter |
| 2 | **Mean Bearing Pressure** | $P_m = \dfrac{W}{L \times D}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | W = load on bearing in Newtons |
| 3 | **Sliding Velocity** | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | N = crank rpm |
| 4 | **PV Product (Heat-Load Index)** | $PV = P_m \times V$ | MPa·m/s | Critical parameter for material selection |
| 5 | **Specific Loading (Petrov's Law, basic friction)** | $\mu = \dfrac{2 \pi^2 \eta N}{P_m \cdot 60} \cdot k$ | dimensionless | Used in full-film hydrodynamic model |
| 6 | **Sommerfeld Number** | $S = \dfrac{\mu N}{P_m} \cdot \left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | For journal-bearing design |
| 7 | **Radial Clearance** | $c = D_b - D_j$ | mm | D_b = bearing bore, D_j = journal diameter |
| 8 | **Clearance Ratio** | $\psi = \dfrac{c}{D}$ | dimensionless | Typical: 0.001 to 0.002 |
| 9 | **Oil-Flow Requirement (per bearing)** | $Q \approx 0.5 \cdot \pi D \cdot c \cdot n \cdot L$ | m³/s | Approximate oil-film volumetric flow |
| 10 | **Thrust Bearing Face Pressure** | $P_t = \dfrac{F_{axial}}{A_{thrust}}$ | MPa | For centre flanged main bearing |

> **Engineering Utility:** The **PV product** is the single most important index. It is the universal benchmark for bearing material selection. White metal is limited to **PV ≈ 30–60 MPa·m/s**, while modern aluminium alloys tolerate **PV ≈ 100–200 MPa·m/s**. KTU numerical problems frequently ask: *"Given W, D, L, N, compute Pm, V, PV."*

## 2.6 Failure Modes of Main Bearings (Board-Favourite Topic)

| Failure Mode | Cause | Symptom |
|---|---|---|
| **Fatigue Cracking** | Cyclic load exceeds endurance limit | Spalling of lining |
| **Smearing** | Oil film collapse, metal-to-metal contact | Glossy, dragged metal |
| **Embedding** | Hard dirt particles press into soft overlay | Local pitting |
| **Cavitation / Erosion** | Sub-atmospheric pressure in diverging film region | Pinhole pock marks |
| **Corrosion** | Acidic oil (sulphur from fuel) attacks Pb/Cu | Etched surfaces |
| **Overheating (Pickup)** | Loss of oil pressure or blockage | Wiping, welding |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Solved Model)

> **A four-cylinder, four-stroke petrol engine has a main bearing with the following data:**
> - Journal diameter: $D = 70$ mm
> - Bearing axial length: $L = 28$ mm
> - Radial clearance: $c = 0.060$ mm
> - Maximum load per cylinder: $W = 4500$ N
> - Engine speed: $N = 3000$ rpm
> - Oil viscosity at operating temp: $\eta = 0.012$ Pa·s
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Projected bearing area
> (b) Mean bearing pressure
> (c) Sliding (peripheral) velocity
> (d) PV product
> (e) Sommerfeld number
> (f) Comment on the suitability of the bearing for white metal (allowable PV = 50 MPa·m/s).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

### (a) Projected Bearing Area $A$

We define projected area as the axial length times the journal diameter, treated as a rectangle in side projection.

$$
A = L \times D
$$

**Substitute the given numerical values** (with explicit unit conversion to keep all quantities in SI / mm² as required):

$$
A = 28 \text{ mm} \times 70 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
A = 1960 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Convert to SI (m²)** for use in pressure calculations (1 mm² = 10⁻⁶ m²):

$$
A = 1960 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 1.96 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

---

### (b) Mean Bearing Pressure $P_m$

By definition, mean bearing pressure is the radial load divided by the projected area.

$$
P_m = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{W}{A}
$$

**Substitute W and A:**

$$
P_m = \frac{4500 \text{ N}}{1960 \text{ mm}^2}
$$

$$
P_m = 2.296 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 2.296 \text{ MPa}
$$

> **Note on units:** 1 N/mm² = 1 MPa, so the conversion is direct.

---

### (c) Sliding (Peripheral) Velocity $V$

The journal surface speed is the circumference traversed per unit time:

$$
V = \frac{\pi D N}{60}
$$

**Substitute** ($D = 70$ mm = $0.070$ m, $N = 3000$ rpm):

$$
V = \frac{\pi \times 0.070 \times 3000}{60}
$$

$$
V = \frac{\pi \times 210}{60}
$$

$$
V = \frac{659.73}{60}
$$

$$
V = 10.996 \text{ m/s} \approx 11.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

---

### (d) PV Product

The PV product is the product of mean pressure and peripheral velocity — a measure of the heat-generation intensity at the bearing.

$$
PV = P_m \times V
$$

**Substitute** ($P_m = 2.296$ MPa, $V = 10.996$ m/s):

$$
PV = 2.296 \times 10.996
$$

$$
PV = 25.24 \text{ MPa·m/s}
$$

---

### (e) Sommerfeld Number $S$

For a short journal bearing, the dimensionless Sommerfeld number is given by:

$$
S = \frac{\mu N}{P_m} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2
$$

where:
- $\mu$ = absolute viscosity of oil = 0.012 Pa·s (Note: in some textbooks, $\mu$ is replaced by $\eta$)
- $N$ = rotational speed in rev/s = 3000/60 = 50 rps
- $r$ = journal radius = $D/2$ = 35 mm = 0.035 m
- $c$ = radial clearance = 0.060 mm = 60 × 10⁻⁶ m = $6.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $P_m$ = 2.296 MPa = 2.296 × 10⁶ Pa

**Step 1 — Compute the radius-to-clearance ratio:**

$$
\frac{r}{c} = \frac{0.035}{6.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 583.33
$$

**Step 2 — Square it:**

$$
\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = (583.33)^2 = 3.4028 \times 10^{5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the viscosity-speed-pressure ratio:**

$$
\frac{\mu N}{P_m} = \frac{0.012 \times 50}{2.296 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{0.6}{2.296 \times 10^{6}} = 2.613 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$
S = 2.613 \times 10^{-7} \times 3.4028 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
S = 8.89 \times 10^{-2} \approx 0.0889
$$

---

### (f) Suitability of White Metal (PV Check)

The computed PV product was **25.24 MPa·m/s**, well within the allowable limit for white metal (**50 MPa·m/s**). The bearing is therefore **suitable** for a white-metal lining under these operating conditions. A small safety margin remains for transient overloads (e.g., full-throttle acceleration).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Insight:** A full-marks solution must explicitly state the **allowable PV** of the chosen material and compare. Numerical result alone = 5/7. The concluding statement with material justification = 7/7.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU 2024 Numerical Set-Ups

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Equations |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure check | W, D, L | $P_m$ | $P_m = W/(L \cdot D)$ |
| Speed check | D, N | $V$ | $V = \pi D N/60$ |
| Material selection | W, D, L, N | PV vs. material chart | $PV = P_m \cdot V$ |
| Clearance verification | D, oil grade, N | $c_{min}$ for full-film | $h_0 \propto (c \cdot \mu N / P_m)^{0.5}$ |
| Oil flow sizing | D, c, N, L | $Q$ | $Q \approx 0.5 \pi D c n L$ |
| Friction power loss | $\mu, W, N, c$ | $P_{friction}$ | $P_f = \mu \cdot W \cdot V$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Main Bearing Assembly Hierarchy

This block diagram maps the components of a complete main bearing assembly and their relationships. Purely alphanumeric node IDs and clean labels are used to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[EngineBlock] --> B1[SaddleMachinedSurface]
    B1 --> C1[UpperBearingShell]
    A2[BearingCap] --> C2[LowerBearingShell]
    A1 --> A2
    C1 --> D1[BearingLiningOverlay]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[CrankshaftMainJournal]
    E1 --> F1[HydrodynamicOilFilm]
    A1 --> G1[MainOilGallery]
    G1 --> G2[CrossDrillingInBlock]
    G2 --> G3[OilHoleInUpperShell]
    G3 --> F1
    E1 --> H1[ThrustFlangeOnCentreMain]
    H1 --> I1[AxialLocationOfCrankshaft]
    C1 --> J1[LocatingTangAntiRotation]
    C2 --> J1
    A2 --> K1[CapBoltsTorquedToSpec]
    K1 --> L1[ClampLoad]
    L1 --> C2
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Oil-Flow Architecture Through a Main Bearing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[OilPump] --> O2[MainOilGalleryInBlock]
    O2 --> O3[CrossDrillAtEachMainBearing]
    O3 --> O4[OilHoleInUpperHalfShell]
    O4 --> O5[OilGrooveOnShell]
    O5 --> O6[ConvergingWedgeJournalRegion]
    O6 --> O7[HydrodynamicPressureBuildUp]
    O7 --> O8[FloatingJournalSupportedOnFilm]
    O8 --> O9[OilDrainsToSumpViaLowerShellGap]
    O9 --> O10[CrankcaseSump]
    O10 --> O1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture: Main Bearing Design Considerations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[LoadOnBearing] --> D2[BearingPressure]
    D2 --> D3[MaterialSelection]
    D3 --> D4{BabbittOrTriMetal}
    D4 --> D5[OverlayThickness]
    D5 --> D6[EmbeddabilityCheck]
    D1 --> D7[EngineSpeed]
    D7 --> D8[SlidingVelocity]
    D2 --> D9[PVProduct]
    D8 --> D9
    D9 --> D10{PVWithinLimit}
    D10 -->|Yes| D11[DesignAcceptable]
    D10 -->|No| D12[IncreaseDorL]
    D12 --> D2
    D7 --> D13[OilViscosity]
    D13 --> D14[MinimumFilmThickness]
    D14 --> D15{FullFilmRegime}
    D15 -->|Yes| D11
    D15 -->|No| D16[BoundaryLubricationWarning]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Bearing Failure Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[ObservedBearingFailure] --> F2{VisualPattern}
    F2 -->|ShinyStreaks| F3[SMEARING]
    F2 -->|PitsAndCraters| F4[FATIGUESPALLING]
    F2 -->|PinpointPits| F5[CAVITATION]
    F2 -->|EtchedSurfaces| F6[CORROSION]
    F2 -->|EmbeddedParticles| F7[DIRTINDENTATION]
    F2 -->|WipedLining| F8[OILSTARVATION]
    F3 --> F9[CauseLowOilPressureOrColdStart]
    F4 --> F10[CauseOverloadOrMaterialInferior]
    F5 --> F11[CauseDivergingFilmRegion]
    F6 --> F12[CauseAcidicOilOrContaminatedOil]
    F7 --> F13[CausePoorFiltration]
    F8 --> F14[CauseBlockedOilPassage]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: List any three functions of main bearings in an IC engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, 1 mark per point):**
1. They support the crankshaft radially and transmit combustion and inertia loads to the engine block.
2. They provide a hydrodynamic oil film that permits near-frictionless rotation of the crankshaft.
3. They locate the crankshaft axially (via the thrust flanged centre main bearing) and assist in heat dissipation through the oil flow.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Avoid vague answers like *"to reduce friction."* Use the precise KTU 2024 wording: *"hydrodynamic oil film generation"* and *"axial location of crankshaft."*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Q: Differentiate between white metal (Babbitt) and copper-lead bearing materials in two points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | White Metal (Babbitt) | Copper-Lead |
|---|---|---|
| Embeddability | **Excellent** (soft material, absorbs dirt) | Good but inferior to Babbitt |
| Fatigue strength | **Lower** (~30 MPa) | **Higher** (~70 MPa) |
| Typical application | Older engines, high-speed, low-load | Diesel, heavy-duty, high-load |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]

**Q: (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and materials used in main bearings of an IC engine. (7 marks)
**(b)** A main bearing of a 6-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has the following specifications: bore = 80 mm, stroke = 90 mm, maximum gas pressure = 4 MPa, journal diameter = 75 mm, bearing length = 32 mm, engine speed = 2800 rpm. Calculate the (i) load on the bearing, (ii) mean bearing pressure, (iii) sliding velocity, and (iv) PV product. Comment on the suitability of a copper-lead lining (allowable PV = 90 MPa·m/s). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **Construction (3 marks):** A main bearing consists of a **split bearing** (two half-shells) — the upper half fits in the cylinder block saddle, the lower half in the bearing cap. Both halves are lined with bearing material. The cap is bolted with two high-tensile studs. A locating **tang** prevents rotation of the shell. The centre main bearing has **thrust flanges** for axial location.
2. **Materials (3 marks):** Modern bearings use a **tri-metal** or **bimetal** construction: a **steel backing** (3–6 mm thick) for strength, an **intermediate lining** (Cu-Pb or Al-Sn, 0.25–0.5 mm) for fatigue resistance, and a thin **Pb-Sn overlay** (0.005–0.025 mm) for embeddability. White metal (Babbitt, Sn-Sb-Cu) is used in low-load high-speed engines.
3. **Sketch description (1 mark):** Upper and lower shells sandwiching the journal, oil hole, oil groove, cap bolts, tang, thrust flanges.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $D_j = 75$ mm, $L = 32$ mm, $N = 2800$ rpm, max gas pressure $p_g = 4$ MPa, bore $B = 80$ mm, stroke $S = 90$ mm, 4-stroke, 6-cylinder.

**(i) Load on the bearing:**
For a 6-cylinder 4-stroke engine, the maximum load occurs when one cylinder is at TDC firing. The inertial and gas forces are shared across the main bearings; the worst-case bearing load is taken as the gas force on one cylinder (conservative KTU assumption for main bearing in a 4-stroke):

$$
F_{gas} = p_g \times \frac{\pi}{4} B^2
$$

$$
F_{gas} = 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 80^2 = 4 \times 5026.55 = 20106 \text{ N}
$$

Round to **W ≈ 20 100 N** ≈ **20.1 kN** for the most loaded main bearing.

**(ii) Mean bearing pressure:**

$$
P_m = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{20106}{32 \times 75} = \frac{20106}{2400}
$$

$$
P_m = 8.378 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iii) Sliding velocity:**

$$
V = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.075 \times 2800}{60}
$$

$$
V = \frac{659.73}{60} = 10.996 \text{ m/s} \approx 11.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iv) PV product:**

$$
PV = 8.378 \times 10.996 = 92.12 \text{ MPa·m/s}
$$

**Comment:** Allowable PV for Cu-Pb is **90 MPa·m/s**. Computed PV = **92.12 MPa·m/s** slightly exceeds this limit. **The copper-lead lining is marginally unsuitable**; either an **aluminium alloy** (allowable PV ~150 MPa·m/s) or **tri-metal** should be selected, or L should be increased.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]

**Q: (a)** Describe the various types of bearing materials used in automotive engines with their comparative properties. (7 marks)
**(b)** Explain the phenomenon of hydrodynamic lubrication in a journal bearing. Derive the Petroff equation for friction torque and discuss its limitations. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Materials in tabular form (4 marks):**
Refer to the materials table in §2.3 — present a clean 4-row table (White metal, Cu-Pb, Al-alloy, Tri-metal) with composition, hardness, fatigue strength, embeddability, and application.

**Comparative discussion (3 marks):**
- White metal: best embeddability, worst fatigue.
- Cu-Pb: best for diesel, moderate embeddability.
- Al-alloy: highest fatigue strength, modern petrol engines.
- Tri-metal: combines Cu-Pb strength with Pb-Sn overlay embeddability — used in racing and high-performance diesel.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Hydrodynamic lubrication explanation (3 marks):**
When the journal rotates, oil is dragged by viscous shear into the converging wedge between the journal and bearing. Pressure builds up per Reynolds equation; once it exceeds the load, the journal lifts and floats. Full-film hydrodynamic lubrication is established above a critical speed.

**Petroff's equation derivation (3 marks):**
For a concentric journal of radius $r$ rotating at $N$ rev/s in a bearing of clearance $c$, the shear stress is:

$$
\tau = \eta \cdot \frac{V}{c} = \eta \cdot \frac{2 \pi r N}{c}
$$

The friction force over the projected area $A = 2 r L$ is:

$$
F_f = \tau \cdot A = \eta \cdot \frac{2 \pi r N}{c} \cdot 2 r L = \frac{4 \pi \eta r^2 L N}{c}
$$

The friction torque $T_f = F_f \cdot r$:

$$
T_f = \frac{4 \pi \eta r^3 L N}{c}
$$

The coefficient of friction (Petroff):

$$
\mu = \frac{T_f}{W \cdot r} = \frac{2 \pi^2 \eta r N}{P_m \cdot c} \quad \text{(with } W = P_m \cdot 2 r L\text{)}
$$

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Assumes concentric journal — does not account for eccentricity.
2. Assumes full-film, no side leakage — ignores axial flow.
3. Cannot predict load-carrying capacity or minimum film thickness.
4. Only valid for very low loads and very high speeds (limiting case).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (7–9 Range Stuck in 5–6):**
> 1. **Forgetting unit consistency:** Mixing mm and m in the same equation (e.g., computing $V = \pi D N / 60$ with $D$ in mm — KTU strictly deducts **1 mark** per numerical error).
> 2. **Skipping the "comment" step:** Numerical problems on PV products **always** require a concluding comment on material suitability. Missing this = loss of **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing "upper" and "lower" shell loading:** The upper shell is **typically the loaded half** in most automotive engines (because combustion pushes the crank down, the crank journal is pushed UP into the upper shell). Stating the opposite = **½ mark** deduction.
> 4. **Calling main bearings "ball/roller" bearings:** KTU strictly marks this wrong. Main bearings are **plain hydrodynamic journal bearings** in 100% of automotive engines. Roller bearings in engine crankshafts are practically non-existent.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch in 7-mark answers:** KTU 2024 mandates a **neat sketch** for full marks. A verbal description alone forfeits at least **1.5 marks**.
> 6. **Misnaming the thrust location:** Only the **centre main bearing** carries the thrust. The front and rear mains do not. Many students incorrectly say "all mains" — KTU deducts **1 mark**.

---
