# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimum Runner Length for Ram Effect

> **Problem:** Derive the expression for the optimum length of an intake runner in a 4-stroke SI engine such that the reflected rarefaction wave arrives at the intake valve just as it opens at BDC.

### Conceptual Setup
1. The intake valve closes at the end of the induction stroke (BDC).
2. A positive pressure pulse travels from the valve towards the open end (plenum) at the local speed of sound.
3. It reflects as a **negative (rarefaction) pressure pulse** and travels back.
4. The valve opens again at the **next BDC** (one full engine cycle later for a 4-stroke, i.e., **two crank revolutions**).
5. The rarefaction should arrive **exactly** at this moment to suck in additional charge (ram effect).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Speed of sound in the gas inside the runner (assuming intake temperature T_i in Kelvin):

$$
a = \sqrt{\gamma \, R_{s} \, T_{i}}
$$

**Step 2:** For a 4-stroke engine running at N revolutions per minute, the time between two successive induction events (intake valve opening at BDC) is the time for **two full crank revolutions**:

$$
t_{c} = \frac{2 \times 60}{N} = \frac{120}{N} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

**Step 3:** During this time, the pressure wave must travel from the valve, to the plenum, reflect, and return — i.e., traverse the runner **twice**:

$$
\text{Total distance travelled} = 2 \, L
$$

**Step 4:** Equating the time taken by the wave with the time between two intake events:

$$
\frac{2 \, L}{a} = t_{c}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $t_{c} = \dfrac{120}{N}$:

$$
\frac{2 \, L}{a} = \frac{120}{N}
$$

**Step 6:** Solving for L:

$$
L = \frac{a \cdot 120}{2 \, N} = \frac{60 \, a}{N}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,L_{\text{opt}} = \frac{60 \, a}{N}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion to f_r (resonance frequency):** Since $f_{r} = \dfrac{N}{120}$ for a 4-stroke engine (one intake event per 2 crank revs), this can equivalently be written as:
> $$L_{\text{opt}} = \frac{a}{4 \, f_{r}}$$
> This is the **quarter-wave tuning** formula. The runner acts as a closed-open pipe (closed at valve, open at plenum), supporting a quarter wavelength.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Helmholtz Resonance of an Inlet Plenum

> **Problem:** Derive the resonant frequency of the plenum–runner system treated as a Helmholtz oscillator.

### Conceptual Setup
A Helmholtz resonator consists of:
- A cavity of volume V_c (the plenum)
- A neck of cross-sectional area A_r and effective length L_e (the runner)

When air in the neck oscillates, the air in the cavity acts as a "spring", producing a characteristic resonance.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Effective mass of air in the neck (with end-correction for the open end):

$$
m_{e} = \rho \, A_{r} \, L_{e}
$$

**Step 2:** Acoustic compliance (springiness) of the air in the cavity:

$$
C = \frac{V_{c}}{\rho \, c^{2}} \quad \text{where} \quad c = \sqrt{\gamma R_{s} T}
$$

**Step 3:** Resonant angular frequency of a mass-spring system:

$$
\omega_{H} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{m_{e} \, C}}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$
\omega_{H} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho \, c^{2}}{V_{c} \, \rho \, A_{r} \, L_{e}}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{A_{r} \, L_{e} \, V_{c}}}
$$

**Step 5:** Since $\omega = 2 \pi f$:

$$
2 \pi f_{H} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{A_{r} \, L_{e} \, V_{c}}}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,f_{H} = \frac{c}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{A_{r}}{V_{c} \, L_{e}}}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A larger plenum volume V_c → lower f_H → useful for low-RPM torque. A smaller plenum → higher f_H → useful for high-RPM power. Variable geometry works by dynamically altering V_c and/or L_e to track engine speed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop in a Manifold Runner

> **Problem:** Calculate the pressure drop in an intake runner of length L = 0.4 m, diameter D = 40 mm, with a mean air velocity V = 60 m/s. Assume smooth pipe and 1 bend with K = 0.3. Take air at 320 K, ρ = 1.1 kg/m³, μ = 1.85 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s.

### Step 1 — Reynolds Number

$$
Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1.1 \times 60 \times 0.040}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
Re = \frac{2.64}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.427 \times 10^{5}
$$

The flow is fully turbulent (Re > 4000).

### Step 2 — Friction Factor (Blasius)

$$
f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{(1.427 \times 10^{5})^{0.25}}
$$

$$
Re^{0.25} = (1.427 \times 10^{5})^{0.25} \approx 19.40
$$

$$
f = \frac{0.316}{19.40} \approx 0.0163
$$

### Step 3 — Total Pressure Drop (Darcy + Minor Loss)

$$
\Delta P = \frac{\rho V^{2}}{2}\left( f \cdot \frac{L}{D} + K \right)
$$

$$
\frac{\rho V^{2}}{2} = \frac{1.1 \times 60^{2}}{2} = \frac{1.1 \times 3600}{2} = 1980 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
f \cdot \frac{L}{D} = 0.0163 \times \frac{0.4}{0.040} = 0.0163 \times 10 = 0.163
$$

$$
K + f(L/D) = 0.3 + 0.163 = 0.463
$$

$$
\Delta P = 1980 \times 0.463 = 916.7 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.917 \text{ kPa}
$$

### Result

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta P \approx 0.92 \text{ kPa (or about 0.9\% of atmospheric)}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A well-designed manifold keeps ΔP below **1–2% of ambient** (≈ 1–2 kPa) to avoid significant volumetric efficiency loss. The above design is well within acceptable limits.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs

| Design Parameter | Increase Effect | Decrease Effect | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Runner Length L | More low-end torque, higher ram effect at lower RPM | Loss of top-end power | 0.2 – 0.6 m |
| Runner Diameter D | Higher peak flow (top-end) | Slower air velocity → loss of low-end | 30 – 60 mm |
| Plenum Volume V_c | Smoother pulse, broader torque | Slow transient response | 0.5 – 5 L |
| Bend Radius | Lower bend loss (K) | Requires more space | ≥ 2D |
| Surface Finish | Lower friction (f) | Higher cost | Ra < 1.6 μm |
| Heated Manifold (SI) | Better fuel vaporisation, cold-start | Risk of vapour lock, knock | 60 – 100 °C |

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Inlet Manifold — SI vs CI** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q:** Differentiate between the inlet manifolds used in **SI engines** and **CI engines**, with emphasis on charge composition, temperature, and material.

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | SI Engine Inlet Manifold | CI Engine Inlet Manifold |
|---|---|---|
| Charge carried | Air + fuel (mixture) | Air only |
| Wall temperature | **Heated (hot spot)** to vaporise fuel | **Cool or insulated** to keep air dense |
| Material | Cast aluminium / cast iron with heat passages | Aluminium (no heating passages) |
| Typical temperature | 60 – 100 °C | Near ambient (35 – 60 °C) |
| Fuel delivery | Upstream (in manifold) via carb / port injection | Not in manifold; fuel injected into cylinder |
| Risk | Vapour lock, hot start problems | Cold-start requires glow plugs |

---

### **Question 2: Log Manifold vs Tubular Header** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

> **Q:** Compare **log-type exhaust manifold** and **tubular header** in terms of construction, performance, and typical application.

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Log Manifold | Tubular Header |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Cast or stamped, short unequal pipes merging into a single chamber | Individual bent steel tubes of **equal length** welded into a collector |
| Primary length | Unequal, hence unequal pulse timing | Equal, hence **uniform pulse arrival** |
| Scavenging efficiency | Low to moderate | High |
| Cost | Low (mass production) | High (welding, bending) |
| Typical use | Family sedans, commercial vehicles | Performance cars, racing engines |
| Weight | Heavier (cast) | Lighter (tubes) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice between **Option A** and **Option B** (Module-wise). Both options carry equal marks and students answer either.

---

### **Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q:** (a) Explain the **design considerations of an inlet manifold** for a multi-cylinder SI engine, with reference to charge distribution, ram effect, and fuel vaporisation. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q:** (b) With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a **variable length intake manifold (VLIM)**. How does it improve engine performance across the RPM range? *(7 marks)*

### **Model Answer — Part (a):**

**1. Charge Distribution (uniformity):**
- The plenum volume should be large enough to dampen cylinder-to-cylinder pulsations.
- All runners should have **equal length and diameter** to ensure equal flow resistance.
- Sharp bends, sudden expansions, and asymmetric branch points must be avoided.

**2. Ram Effect (Inertial Tuning):**
- Pressure wave theory: a positive pressure pulse reflects from the plenum and returns as a rarefaction to assist induction.
- Optimum runner length:
$$
L = \frac{60 \, a}{N}
$$
- This effect is strongly RPM-dependent, so a single fixed length is a compromise.

**3. Fuel Vaporisation (Hot Spot):**
- A heated section near the carburettor (the "hot spot") vaporises fuel droplets.
- Achieved by passing exhaust gases through a small chamber surrounding the manifold, or by coolant passages.
- Wall temperature maintained at **60–100 °C** to balance vaporisation vs. vapour-lock risk.

**4. Surface Finish and Material:**
- Smooth internal surfaces (Ra < 1.6 μm) reduce friction losses.
- Aluminium alloy is preferred for its light weight and good heat dissipation.

**5. Mounting and NVH:**
- The manifold should be isolated from the engine block to reduce transmitted vibration.

### **Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Construction of VLIM:**
- Two sets of runners are provided: a **long set** and a **short set**.
- A set of **butterfly valves** (or a drum-type valve) controlled by a vacuum actuator (or electric solenoid) opens/closes the secondary set based on engine RPM and load.
- At low RPM, only the **long runners** are active → high ram effect → strong low-end torque.
- At high RPM, the **short runners** open in parallel → reduced effective length → high-RPM volumetric efficiency.

**Working Principle:**
- ECU receives signals from the **RPM sensor** and **MAP sensor**.
- At a threshold (typically 3500–4500 rpm), the actuator opens the secondary valves, switching from long-runner to short-runner mode.
- The change is **seamless** to the driver but produces a noticeable torque "step" in some engines.

**Performance Benefits:**
- Extends the useful torque curve by 30–40%.
- Eliminates the traditional trade-off between low-end torque and top-end power.
- Improves fuel economy at low loads.

> **Diagram (described in text since physical drawings not possible):**
> ```
> [Plenum] -- long runner -- [Cylinder 1]
>              \  short runner (with butterfly) -- [Cylinder 1]
> [Plenum] -- long runner -- [Cylinder 2]
>              \  short runner (with butterfly) -- [Cylinder 2]
> ... (continued for all 4 cylinders)
> ```

---

### **Question B (Option 2) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q:** (a) Describe the **construction and working of an exhaust manifold** in a CI engine. Explain the role of **pulse tuning** in improving volumetric efficiency. *(7 marks)*
>
> **Q:** (b) Define **scavenging**. With the help of pressure-time diagrams, explain how the exhaust manifold helps in scavenging a 4-stroke CI engine. *(7 marks)*

### **Model Answer — Part (a):**

**1. Construction of Exhaust Manifold in CI Engine:**
- Made of **cast iron** or **high-grade stainless steel** to withstand temperatures of 600 – 900 °C.
- Consists of **individual primary tubes** (one per cylinder) that merge into a **collector** (log section).
- The collector is connected via a flange to the **turbocharger turbine inlet** (if turbocharged) or to the **catalytic converter / silencer**.
- **Heat shields** are mounted externally to protect the bonnet and surrounding components.
- Modern designs use **thin-wall stainless steel** to reduce weight and improve thermal fatigue life.

**2. Working:**
- Exhaust gases leave the cylinder at the end of the power stroke when the exhaust valve opens.
- The high-pressure pulse (≈ 3 – 5 bar) travels down the primary tube, reaches the collector, and partially reflects back as a **rarefaction pulse**.
- A small portion of the pulse energy is used to drive the turbocharger turbine (in turbocharged engines).
- The remainder continues to the after-treatment devices.

**3. Pulse Tuning:**
- In a CI engine, exhaust pulses are **strong** due to high compression ratios (17:1 typical).
- The intervals between successive exhaust events (for a 4-stroke 4-cylinder) are uniform (180° of crank rotation for adjacent cylinders in firing order).
- By choosing the right **primary tube length**, the rarefaction wave from one cylinder's exhaust pulse can arrive at another cylinder's exhaust port exactly when its exhaust valve is about to open, helping to **draw out** the burnt gases.
- This "**exhaust gas extraction**" leaves more room for fresh charge in the next cycle, improving **volumetric efficiency** by 5–10% and reducing pumping losses.

**Optimum Primary Tube Length (Pulse Tuning):**
$$
L_{p} = \frac{a_{exh} \cdot \Delta t_{pulse}}{2}
$$

where $\Delta t_{pulse}$ is the crank angle interval between successive exhaust events converted to time.

### **Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Definition:**
> **Scavenging** is the process of **expelling burnt exhaust gases** from the cylinder and **replacing them with fresh charge** during the valve overlap period (in 4-stroke) or throughout the cycle (in 2-stroke).

**Role of Exhaust Manifold in 4-Stroke Scavenging:**

A typical 4-stroke engine has a brief **valve overlap period** (≈ 10–30° of crank rotation) where both intake and exhaust valves are open simultaneously. Effective scavenging depends on:

1. **Intake manifold ram effect** (low pressure in cylinder)
2. **Exhaust pulse energy** (high pressure wave arrives at exhaust port)

**Pressure-Time Diagram Description:**

```
Pressure
  (bar)
   5  |         *  *  *  *            ← Exhaust valve opens
   4  |        *        *
   3  |       *          *
   2  |      *            *
   1  |     *              *
   0  |----*----------------*---*-----  ← Atmospheric reference
      |                          
      |  ___    ___    ___    ___        ← Intake stroke
      |     \__/   \__/   \__/   \__
      |________________________________
        0°   180°  360°  540°  720°
                Crank Angle
```

**Key observations:**
- The exhaust pressure spike (point of exhaust valve opening) is high but falls rapidly.
- A properly tuned exhaust manifold ensures that the **rarefaction phase** of the reflected pulse coincides with the **intake valve opening**, lowering cylinder back-pressure.
- This reduces the **pump work** required by the piston during the gas-exchange process and increases net indicated work.

**Numerical Gain:**
- A well-tuned exhaust manifold can improve **brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)** by 3–5% and increase **peak torque** by 5–8 Nm in a 2-litre diesel engine.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing SI vs CI inlet manifold requirements** (–1 to –2 marks). Many students wrongly say "CI manifolds are also heated".
> 2. **Forgetting units in formula-based answers** (–1 mark). Always state L in **m**, a in **m/s**, N in **rpm**.
> 3. **Mixing up ram tuning (intake) with pulse tuning (exhaust)** (–2 marks). They are related but conceptually distinct.
> 4. **Drawing poor/sketchy diagrams** in 7-mark sub-questions (–1 mark). Always label: plenum, runner, throttle, cylinder, valve.
> 5. **Omitting the role of plenum volume** when discussing volumetric efficiency (–1 mark).
> 6. **Failing to mention material selection** (cast iron vs aluminium vs stainless steel) in 7-mark design questions (–1 mark).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Must-Know Points for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ Inlet manifold in **SI engine carries fuel + air**; in **CI engine carries only air**.
- ✅ SI inlet manifold has a **hot spot**; CI manifold is **cool/insulated**.
- ✅ **Plenum** damps pulsations and provides stable pressure; **runners** feed individual cylinders.
- ✅ **Ram effect** optimum runner length: $L = \dfrac{60 \, a}{N}$ (4-stroke).
- ✅ **Helmholtz resonance** of plenum-runner system: $f_H = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{A_r}{V_c L_e}}$.
- ✅ **VLIM** switches between long and short runners to extend torque curve.
- ✅ **Exhaust manifold** made of cast iron or stainless steel; withstands up to **900 °C**.
- ✅ **Log manifold** = cheap, unequal lengths; **Tubular header** = expensive, equal lengths, better scavenging.
- ✅ **Pulse tuning** in CI engines uses exhaust wave energy to help scavenging.
- ✅ **Volumetric efficiency** is the key parameter governed by manifold design.
- ✅ Typical pressure drop in a well-designed manifold is **< 2% of atmospheric** (< 2 kPa).
- ✅ **Speed of sound in air (300 K)** = 347 m/s; **in exhaust gas (600 K)** ≈ 517 m/s.
- ✅ **Reynolds number** in runners is **> 10⁵** (fully turbulent); use Blasius equation: $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$.
- ✅ Modern trends: **Variable geometry**, **3D-printed manifolds**, **integrated with turbo and intercooler**.
- ✅ Bernoulli's equation (incompressible form) used for low-speed flow analysis; for high-speed pulse propagation, use **acoustic/wave theory**.
- ✅ Always state the **units** and **assumptions** before plugging values into a formula.
- ✅ KTU expects: **Definition + Diagram + Working + 1 Numerical / Formula** in any 7-mark question on manifolds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valve Actuating Mechanisms. Air Intake System in Petrol and Diesel Engines: Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMUTUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Valve Actuating Mechanisms. Air Intake System in Petrol and Diesel Engines: Layout # 🔧 Module 1 — Valve Actuating Mechanisms & Air Intake System Layout ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Valve Actuating Mechanism — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Valve Actuating Mechanism (Valvetrain)** is the assembly of mechanical components responsible for the **timed opening and closing of the intake and exhaust valves** of an internal combustion engine, converting the **rotational motion of the camshaft** into a precisely controlled **linear reciprocating motion** of the valves, synchronized with the piston position via the **timing chain/belt**. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Outcome: *CO1 — Understand the constructional features of modern automotive engines*), the valvetrain is treated as a **subsystem** that directly governs **volumetric efficiency, breathing capacity, and power output** of the engine. **Primary Functions of the Valvetrain:** - Open the **intake valve** at the correct crank angle to admit air (or air-fuel mixture). - Hold the valve open for the required **duration** (valve lift event). - Close the valve **gas-tight** before combustion begins. - Open the **exhaust valve** at BDC to release burnt gases. - Coordinate **valve overlap** between IVO (Intake Valve Open) and EVO (Exhaust Valve Open). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Door & Pulley" Model > [!NOTE] > Think of a valve as a **door** in a hallway, and the camshaft lobe as a **person pushing that door open with a stick (pushrod/rocker arm)** at exactly the right moment when a runner (piston) needs to pass through. The **valve spring** is the automatic door-closer that slams the door shut the moment the person removes the stick. The **timing belt/chain** is the "schedule" ensuring the person pushes the door open at precisely the right millisecond — neither too early nor too late. ### 1.3 Air Intake System — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Air Intake System** of an IC engine is the **plumbing network** that delivers clean, metered air (or air-fuel mixture in SI engines) from the ambient atmosphere to the engine cylinders at the **lowest possible restriction** while providing **filtration, silencing, and (in turbocharged engines) pressurization**. **Key Sub-Components (Universal):** - **Air Filter / Cleaner** — removes particulates (efficiency **>99.5%** for typical paper elements). - **Intake Duct / Hose** — channels air to the manifold. - **Resonator / Helmholtz Silencer** — attenuates intake noise. - **Intake Manifold** — distributes air to individual cylinders. - **Throttle Body (SI only)** — meters airflow via an electronically controlled butterfly valve. - **Surge Tank / Plenum** — smooths pulsations in multi-cylinder engines. **Standard Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield):** | Parameter | Typical Value (Petrol) | Typical Value (Diesel) | |---|---|---| | Intake Pressure | 0.3 – 1.0 bar (NA) / 1.5 – 2.5 bar (Turbo) | 1.5 – 3.0 bar (Turbo CRDi) | | Air-Fuel Ratio | **14.7 : 1 (stoichiometric)** | **18 – 70 : 1 (lean)** | | Volumetric Efficiency | 85 – 95% (NA) | 90 – 110% (forced induction) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Valve Actuating Mechanisms > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme specifically tests the **identification, advantages/disadvantages, and application** of each valvetrain type. Memorize the location of the **camshaft relative to the cylinder head**. ### 2.1.1 (A) Side Valve (L-Head) — *Historical / Obsolete* - Camshaft placed **beside the cylinder block**, in the crankcase. - Valves located in the block beside the piston. - **Driven by:** Short pushrods and simple rockers. - ❌ Disadvantages: Long flow path → poor volumetric efficiency, high heat loss, detonation-prone. - ✅ Use: Vintage cars, some low-cost utility engines (e.g., old Fiat, vintage BMC). ### 2.1.2 (B) Overhead Valve (OHV / Pushrod Engine) - Camshaft remains in the **crankcase (block)**, but valves are in the **cylinder head**. - **Driven by:** Tappet/Lifter → Pushrod → Rocker Arm → Valve. - **Transmission Method:** Mechanical pushrod linkage. - ✅ Advantages: Compact cam, robust, cheap, good low-end torque. - ❌ Disadvantages: Higher reciprocating mass, limited RPM, complex geometry. - 🔧 Application: American V8s (Chevy small-block), Mahindra mHawk, Tata Dicor (older). ### 2.1.3 (C) Overhead Cam (OHC / Cam-in-Head) - Camshaft mounted **directly inside the cylinder head**, above the valves. - **Driven by:** Timing belt/chain (or gears) from crankshaft. - **Sub-types:** - **SOHC** (Single Overhead Cam) — one cam operates both intake & exhaust via rocker arms (e.g., Honda i-VTEC SOHC). - **DOHC** (Double Overhead Cam) — separate cams for intake & exhaust (e.g., Maruti K-series, most modern bikes). - ✅ Advantages: Lower reciprocating mass, higher RPM, better breathing, direct-acting bucket tappets possible. - ❌ Disadvantages: More complex timing drive, taller engine, costlier. - 🔧 Application: Maruti Suzuki, Honda, Toyota, BMW, almost all modern passenger cars. ### 2.1.4 (D) Desmodromic Valve System - **Positive closure** — both opening and closing are mechanically actuated (no spring). - ✅ Used in: **Ducati, Mercedes F1, MotoGP bikes** — eliminates valve float at extreme RPM. - ❌ Disadvantages: Complexity, weight, cost. ### 2.1.5 (E) Variable Valve Timing (VVT) & Lift Systems - **VTEC** (Honda) — uses multiple cam profiles switched by oil pressure. - **Vanos / Valvetronic** (BMW) — continuously variable lift & timing. - **i-VTEC, VVT-i, MultiAir, Cam-Phaser** — modern ECU-controlled phasers. ## 2.2 Detailed Component Breakdown — Valvetrain | Component | Function | Material (Typical) | |---|---|---| | **Camshaft** | Rotates at ½ crankshaft speed (4-stroke), lobes open valves | Chilled cast iron / forged steel | | **Cam Lobe** | Profile determines lift, duration, and timing | Hardened steel (surface) | | **Tappet / Lifter** | Translates cam rotation to linear motion | Hardened steel, roller-tipped in OHC | | **Pushrod** (OHV only) | Transfers motion from lifter to rocker | Steel tube, hollow for oil passage | | **Rocker Arm** | Pivots to push valve down (in OHV) | Forged steel, needle-bearing pivot | | **Valve Spring** | Returns valve to closed seat; retains contact | Chrome-silicon steel wire, helical | | **Valve Stem Seal** | Prevents oil from entering combustion chamber | Viton / PTFE rubber | | **Timing Belt/Chain** | Synchronizes camshaft with crankshaft | Rubber with fiberglass (belt) / Steel (chain) | | **Bucket Tappet** (DOHC) | Direct-acting, no rocker needed | Hardened steel, shim-adjustable | ## 2.3 Valve Geometry — Critical Parameters > [!IMPORTANT] > These parameters appear frequently in KTU numerical problems and 14-mark questions. Memorize the formula sheet below. ### 2.3.1 Valve Timing Diagram (4-Stroke Engine) A complete **720°** crank cycle is divided into: - **Intake Stroke:** 0° → 180° (TDC to BDC). - **Compression Stroke:** 180° → 360°. - **Power Stroke:** 360° → 540°. - **Exhaust Stroke:** 540° → 720°. However, due to **inertia of gases** and **flow dynamics**, valves do NOT open exactly at TDC/BDC. **Standard Valve Timing Events (Typical Petrol, in degrees of crank rotation):** | Event | Abbreviation | Typical Angle (BTDC / ABDC / ATDC) | |---|---|---| | Intake Valve Opens | **IVO** | 10° – 20° BTDC | | Intake Valve Closes | **IVC** | 40° – 70° ABDC | | Exhaust Valve Opens | **EVO** | 40° – 60° BBDC | | Exhaust Valve Closes | **EVC** | 10° – 20° ATDC | | **Valve Overlap** | IVO − EVC | 10° – 40° | > [!NOTE] > **Valve Overlap** = Period when **both** intake and exhaust valves are **simultaneously open** at the end of exhaust stroke / beginning of intake stroke. Higher overlap → better high-RPM breathing but worse idling & emissions. Tuned for **mid-range torque** in road cars. ### 2.3.2 Valve Lift (h) The maximum displacement of the valve from its seat, controlled by the cam lobe profile. $$h = r + r\cos(\theta_{max}) \quad \text{(for circular cam profile)}$$ Where: - $r$ = base circle radius of cam - $\theta_{max}$ = maximum cam rotation angle from base circle For a **tangent cam** (most common in OHV): $$h = 2r(1 - \cos\theta_{max}/2)$$ ### 2.3.3 Valve Velocity & Acceleration $$v = \frac{dh}{dt} = \omega \frac{dh}{d\theta} \quad [\text{m/s}]$$ $$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \omega^2 \frac{d^2h}{d\theta^2} \quad [\text{m/s}^2]$$ Where $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ for the **camshaft** in rad/s (N = camshaft RPM = ½ × crank RPM). ## 2.4 Air Intake System — Detailed Architecture ### 2.4.1 Petrol (SI) Engine Intake System Layout **Layout (Path of Air):** **Atmosphere → Air Filter → Air Flow Meter (MAF) → Throttle Body → Intake Manifold (with Fuel Injectors) → Surge Tank → Cylinder Head Port → Intake Valve → Combustion Chamber** **Sub-Component Detail:** 1. **Air Filter Assembly** — Pleated paper element, housing with snorkel. 2. **Mass Air Flow Sensor (MAF)** — Hot-film or hot-wire type; measures mass of air entering engine. Critical for ECU fuel calculation. 3. **Throttle Body** — Contains: - **Butterfly valve** (electronically controlled in drive-by-wire systems). - **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)**. - **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** — bypass air around closed throttle for idle. 4. **Intake Manifold** — Distributes air to each cylinder. Modern designs use **tuned-length runners** (variable length manifolds — Toyota, Honda) for optimizing torque across RPM range. 5. **Fuel Injectors** (in MPFI) — mounted at cylinder head port, spray fuel onto back of intake valve. 6. **Surge Tank / Plenum** — smooths pressure pulsations in multi-cylinder engines. 7. **PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation) Valve** — recirculates blow-by gases into intake. ### 2.4.2 Diesel (CI) Engine Intake System Layout **Key Difference:** Diesel engines operate with **excess air** (λ > 1 always), so **NO throttle body is required** in naturally aspirated diesels. **Layout (Turbocharged CRDi):** **Atmosphere → Air Filter → Turbocharger Compressor → Intercooler (Charge Air Cooler) → Intake Manifold (with swirl flaps in some designs) → Cylinder Head Port → Intake Valve (or Direct Injection into Chamber) → Combustion Chamber** **Sub-Component Detail:** 1. **Air Filter** — Heavy-duty (diesel environments are dustier). 2. **Turbocharger** — Centrifugal compressor driven by exhaust turbine. Boost pressure typically **1.5 – 2.5 bar**. 3. **Wastegate / VNT (Variable Nozzle Turbine)** — controls max boost pressure. 4. **Intercooler (Charge Air Cooler)** — Reduces temperature of compressed air (denser charge). - For every **10°C reduction** in intake air temperature, density increases by **~3%**, improving power. 5. **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) Cooler** — Recirculates cooled exhaust gas to reduce NOₓ emissions. 6. **Intake Manifold with Swirl Flaps** — In indirect-injection diesels, swirl flaps create **tumble/swirl** in cylinder to aid fuel-air mixing. ### 2.4.3 Critical Differences — SI vs CI Intake Systems | Feature | Petrol (SI) | Diesel (CI) | |---|---|---| | Throttle Body | ✅ Yes (restricts air to control load) | ❌ No (load controlled by fuel quantity) | | Fuel Injection Point | Intake port (MPFI) or direct (GDI) | Direct into cylinder (CRDi) | | Air-Fuel Ratio | Stoichiometric 14.7:1 (λ=1) | Lean (λ > 1.5) | | Turbocharger | Optional (most modern are) | Almost universal | | Intercooler | Common on turbo petrols | Standard on turbo diesels | | Swirl/Tumble Control | Limited | Critical for combustion | | EGR | For emissions | Mandatory for Euro 6/BS-VI | | Intake Pressure | Atmospheric (NA) / Boosted (Turbo) | Always boosted (modern CRDi) | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Module 1 **frequently asks**: *"Why is there no throttle in a diesel engine?"* — Answer: Diesel engines control **load by varying fuel quantity**, not air quantity. Air is always supplied in excess. A throttle would only create pumping loss without benefit. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Cam Profile Maximum Lift for Symmetric Tangent Cam > [!NOTE] > This is a **high-frequency KTU numerical problem** (3-mark or 14-mark part (a)). Students must know the steps. ### Problem Setup A symmetric tangent cam profile has a **base circle radius** $r$ and **roller follower** of radius $R_r$. The cam rotates through a total lift angle of $2\theta_f$ (where $\theta_f$ is the flank angle from the base circle). Determine the **maximum lift of the follower**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Define the geometry. The cam's base circle is the smallest circle inscribed in the cam profile (radius $r$). The roller follower of radius $R_r$ rolls on the cam. **Step 2:** The lift begins when the roller just touches the base circle. The roller center is at height $r + R_r$ above the cam axis. **Step 3:** As the cam rotates by angle $\theta$, the roller center moves along a path. The vertical rise (lift) of the roller center from the base circle position is: $$\text{Lift} = (r + R_r) - (r + R_r)\cos\theta$$ Wait — this is for a **circular arc cam** with offset roller. For a **tangent cam** with flank angle $\theta_f$: **Step 4:** The roller center moves along a straight tangent line. The vertical component of roller center displacement from the base circle line is: $$h(\theta) = R_r \left(\frac{1 - \cos\theta}{\cos\theta_f}\right) + r(1 - \cos\theta) \quad \text{... (for tangent cam with flat flank)}$$ For maximum lift at the **nose** of the cam (i.e., when $\theta = \theta_f$, the cam is at the flank-to-nose transition): $$h_{max} = R_r \left(\frac{1 - \cos\theta_f}{\cos\theta_f}\right) + r(1 - \cos\theta_f)$$ **Step 5:** Simplifying using the trigonometric identity $\frac{1-\cos\theta}{\cos\theta} = \sec\theta - 1$: $$h_{max} = R_r(\sec\theta_f - 1) + r(1 - \cos\theta_f)$$ **Step 6:** For a **knife-edge follower** ($R_r = 0$), this reduces to: $$h_{max} = r(1 - \cos\theta_f)$$ **Step 7:** For a **circular cam** with a flat follower (the most common textbook case), the lift is: $$h_{max} = 2r(1 - \cos\theta_f)$$ > This occurs because the maximum lift happens when the cam rotates by $2\theta_f$ (from one side of the nose to the other), giving the maximum vertical displacement of $2r$ minus the chord length $2r\cos\theta_f$. **Step 8: Final Result:** $$\boxed{h_{max} = 2r(1 - \cos\theta_f) \quad \text{[for circular cam with flat follower]}}$$ **Step 9: Units Check:** - $r$ has units of [m] - $\cos\theta_f$ is dimensionless - $\Rightarrow h_{max}$ has units of [m] ✓ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Valvetrain for Engine Type > [!NOTE] > Use this **decision matrix** to answer KTU's 14-mark descriptive questions on "Compare OHV, OHC, and DOHC valvetrains" or "Justify the valvetrain used in [specific car]." | Selection Criterion | Best Valvetrain Choice | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | **High RPM (>6000)** | DOHC + VVT | Lowest reciprocating mass, best breathing | | **Low-End Torque (Diesel)** | OHV | Compact cam, robust pushrods survive high cylinder pressures | | **Compact Engine (City Car)** | SOHC | Single cam, simpler, cheaper than DOHC | | **Motorcycle / 2-wheeler** | DOHC | High power-to-weight, high RPM operation | | **Heavy-Duty Truck** | OHV (Pushrod) | Mechanical simplicity, durability | | **Racing / F1** | DOHC + Desmodromic | No valve float, positive control | ## 3.3 Numerical Example: Volumetric Efficiency & Air Mass Flow > [!NOTE] > This numerical appears in KTU University Exams for Module 1. **Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine running at 3000 RPM has a **bore = 80 mm**, **stroke = 90 mm**, and **volumetric efficiency = 85%**. The air-fuel ratio is **14.7:1** and the intake air density is **1.2 kg/m³**. Calculate: - (a) The **mass of air inducted per minute**. - (b) The **mass of fuel required per minute**. **Step 1: Displacement Volume per Cylinder** $$V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2 \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.080)^2 \times 0.090$$ $$V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.090 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.000576$$ $$V_c = 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 / \text{cylinder}$$ **Step 2: Total Displacement Volume (4 cylinders)** $$V_d = 4 \times V_c = 4 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-4} = 1.810 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3: Theoretical Air Volume per Minute (4-stroke, so ½ cycle for induction)** For a 4-stroke engine, induction happens once every 2 revolutions. Therefore: $$N_{ind} = \frac{3000}{2} = 1500 \text{ induction strokes/min}$$ $$V_{theoretical} = V_d \times N_{ind} = 1.810 \times 10^{-3} \times 1500 = 2.715 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$ **Step 4: Actual Air Volume per Minute (Apply Volumetric Efficiency)** $$V_{actual} = \eta_{vol} \times V_{theoretical} = 0.85 \times 2.715 = 2.308 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$ **Step 5: Mass of Air per Minute** $$\boxed{m_{air} = \rho_{air} \times V_{actual} = 1.2 \times 2.308 = 2.769 \text{ kg/min}}$$ **Step 6: Mass of Fuel per Minute (A/F = 14.7)** $$m_{fuel} = \frac{m_{air}}{A/F} = \frac{2.769}{14.7}$$ $$\boxed{m_{fuel} = 0.1884 \text{ kg/min}}$$ **Step 7: Sanity Check** - For a 1.8L engine at 3000 RPM, this is realistic (real engines consume ~2-3 kg/min of air at this point). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Below are **professional Mermaid block diagrams** depicting the valvetrain and air intake system architecture as required by KTU Module 1. ## 4.1 Diagram 1: OHV (Pushrod) Valve Actuating Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CRANK_CASE["CRANKCASE (Cam in Block)"] CAM["Camshaft with Lobe
Rotates at 1/2 crank speed"] TAPPET["Tappet / Lifter
Follows cam profile"] PUSHROD["Pushrod
Hollow steel tube"] end subgraph CYL_HEAD["CYLINDER HEAD"] ROCKER["Rocker Arm
Pivots on stud"] VALVE["Intake Valve
Opens into port"] SPRING["Valve Spring
Returns valve closed"] SEAT["Valve Seat
In cylinder head"] end subgraph TIMING["TIMING DRIVE"] CRANK["Crankshaft Sprocket
1x speed"] CHAIN["Timing Chain / Belt
2:1 ratio"] end CRANK -->|drives| CHAIN CHAIN -->|drives| CAM CAM -->|lobe pushes up| TAPPET TAPPET -->|linear push| PUSHROD PUSHROD -->|pushes up| ROCKER ROCKER -->|pivots down| VALVE VALVE -->|lifts off| SEAT SPRING -.->|closes| VALVE style CRANK_CASE fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style CYL_HEAD fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style TIMING fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style CAM fill:#ffcc99 style ROCKER fill:#ffcc99 style VALVE fill:#ff9999 ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2: DOHC (Double Overhead Cam) Valvetrain ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HEAD["CYLINDER HEAD (Cam in Head)"] CAM_IN["Intake Camshaft
Drives intake valves"] CAM_EX["Exhaust Camshaft
Drives exhaust valves"] BUCKET["Bucket Tappet
Direct acting"] VALVE_IN["Intake Valve"] VALVE_EX["Exhaust Valve"] SPRING_IN["Intake Valve Spring"] SPRING_EX["Exhaust Valve Spring"] end subgraph TIMING_DOHC["TIMING DRIVE (End of Head)"] CRANK2["Crankshaft"] BELT["Timing Belt / Chain
2:1 ratio"] PULLEY["Camshaft Sprocket"] end CRANK2 -->|drives| BELT BELT -->|drives| PULLEY PULLEY -->|drives| CAM_IN PULLEY -->|drives| CAM_EX CAM_IN -->|lobe pushes| BUCKET BUCKET -->|direct lift| VALVE_IN VALVE_EX -->|direct lift from exhaust cam| CAM_EX SPRING_IN -.->|closes| VALVE_IN SPRING_EX -.->|closes| VALVE_EX style HEAD fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style TIMING_DOHC fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32 style CAM_IN fill:#99ccff style CAM_EX fill:#ff9999 style VALVE_IN fill:#99ccff style VALVE_EX fill:#ff9999 ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Petrol (SI) Engine Air Intake System Layout ```mermaid flowchart LR ATM["Ambient
Atmosphere"] --> SNORKEL["Snorkel
Intake Duct"] SNORKEL --> AIRBOX["Air Filter
Box Assembly"] AIRBOX --> MAF["MAF Sensor
Mass Air Flow"] MAF --> THROTTLE["Throttle Body
Butterfly Valve
+ TPS + IACV"] THROTTLE --> PLENUM["Plenum / Surge
Tank"] PLENUM --> MANIFOLD["Intake Manifold
Tuned Runners"] MANIFOLD --> INJECTOR["Fuel Injector
MPFI at Port"] INJECTOR --> PORT["Intake Port
in Cylinder Head"] PORT --> IVALVE["Intake Valve
OHC or OHV"] IVALVE --> CC["Combustion
Chamber"] PCV["PCV Valve
Crankcase Vent"] -.->|blow-by gases| PLENUM EVAP["EVAP Canister
Fuel Vapour"] -.->|purge| THROTTLE style ATM fill:#e3f2fd style AIRBOX fill:#fff9c4 style THROTTLE fill:#ffccbc style PLENUM fill:#c8e6c9 style MANIFOLD fill:#c8e6c9 style CC fill:#ffcdd2 style IVALVE fill:#ff9999 ``` ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Diesel (CI) Engine Air Intake System Layout (Turbo CRDi) ```mermaid flowchart LR ATM2["Ambient
Atmosphere"] --> SNK2["Snorkel /
Intake Duct"] SNK2 --> AF2["Air Filter
Heavy Duty"] AF2 --> COMP["Turbocharger
Compressor Wheel
Boost 1.5-2.5 bar"] COMP --> WG["Wastegate /
VNT Control"] WG -.->|bleeds exhaust| TURBINE TURBINE["Exhaust Turbine
Driven by exhaust gas"] -.->|drives| COMP COMP --> INTERCOOLER["Intercooler
Charge Air Cooler
Reduces T by 50-100 C"] INTERCOOLER --> EGR["EGR Cooler
Mixes cooled exhaust"] EGR --> IMAN["Intake Manifold
with Swirl Flaps
(IDI Diesel)"] IMAN --> PORT2["Intake Port"] PORT2 --> DVALVE["Intake Valve
or DI directly"] DVALVE --> DCC["Combustion
Chamber
High Compression"] INJ["Common Rail
Injector"] -->|high pressure 2000 bar| DCC style ATM2 fill:#e3f2fd style AF2 fill:#fff9c4 style COMP fill:#ffccbc style INTERCOOLER fill:#b3e5fc style IMAN fill:#c8e6c9 style DCC fill:#ffcdd2 style DVALVE fill:#ff9999 style INJ fill:#d1c4e9 ``` ## 4.5 Diagram 5: Valve Timing Diagram (Cam Angle vs Crank Angle) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TIMING_DIAG["VALVE TIMING - 720 DEG CYCLE"] T1["0 deg - TDC
IVO 12 BTDC
Exhaust closing"] T2["180 deg - BDC
IVC 50 ABDC
Intake stroke ends"] T3["360 deg - TDC
Compression complete"] T4["540 deg - BDC
EVO 55 BBDC
Exhaust begins"] T5["720 deg - TDC
EVC 15 ATDC
Cycle restart"] end T1 -->|Intake stroke 0-180| T2 T2 -->|Compression 180-360| T3 T3 -->|Power 360-540| T4 T4 -->|Exhaust 540-720| T5 T5 -->|Cycle restart| T1 OV["VALVE OVERLAP
IVO 12 BTDC to EVC 15 ATDC
= 27 deg overlap"] -.->|shown between| T5 OV -.->|and| T1 style TIMING_DIAG fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00 style OV fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define valve overlap. State its effect on engine performance.** **Model Answer:** > **Valve Overlap** is the angular duration (in degrees of crank rotation) during which **both the intake valve and the exhaust valve are simultaneously open**, occurring at the end of the exhaust stroke and the beginning of the intake stroke. Mathematically: $$\theta_{overlap} = \text{IVO angle (BTDC)} + \text{EVC angle (ATDC)}$$ **Effects on Engine Performance:** - ✅ **Beneficial:** At high RPM, the inertia of the incoming fresh charge helps **sweep out residual exhaust gases** (internal EGR scavenging), improving volumetric efficiency and high-end power. - ❌ **Detrimental:** At low RPM/idle, overlap causes **fresh charge to escape** through the still-open exhaust valve, increasing HC emissions, worsening idle quality, and reducing low-end torque. - Modern engines use **VVT (Variable Valve Timing)** to optimize overlap across the RPM range. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is a throttle body not used in a diesel engine intake system?** **Model Answer:** A **throttle body** is not required in a diesel engine because of the following fundamental reason: - Diesel engines operate on the principle of **compression ignition** where combustion is initiated by injecting fuel into **hot compressed air**. - **Load control** in a diesel engine is achieved by **varying the quantity of fuel injected** per cycle — the air quantity is always supplied in **excess** (Air-Fuel ratio ranges from **18:1 to 70:1**, always lean with λ > 1). - Introducing a throttle would only **restrict air flow** and create **pumping losses** (parasitic loss to overcome manifold vacuum), reducing thermal efficiency. - A throttle would also disturb the **swirl and tumble** motion that is critical for proper air-fuel mixing in diesel combustion chambers. - Therefore, the diesel intake manifold is designed for **unrestricted air flow** from the turbocharger/intercooler to the cylinder. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B offers **internal choice**. Two fully-worked alternatives are provided below. ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **With neat sketches, describe the construction and working of an Overhead Cam (OHC) valvetrain. Compare it with the Overhead Valve (OHV) system in terms of (i) reciprocating mass, (ii) RPM capability, (iii) complexity, and (iv) typical applications. [14 Marks]** #### Sub-Part (a): Construction & Working of OHC [7 Marks] **Construction:** 1. **Camshaft(s)** — Mounted inside the cylinder head, above the valves. In SOHC, a single cam operates both intake and exhaust valves via rocker arms. In DOHC, separate camshafts for intake and exhaust allow direct bucket-tappet actuation. 2. **Cam Lobes** — Precision-machined eccentric profiles that determine valve lift, duration, and timing. 3. **Drive Mechanism** — A **timing belt (toothed rubber)** or **timing chain (steel)** connects the crankshaft sprocket to the camshaft sprocket at a **2:1 ratio** (cam rotates at half crank speed in 4-stroke engines). On dual-overhead-cam engines, both cams are driven by the same belt/chain using an idler pulley. 4. **Tappets (Lifters)** — Either **bucket-type** (cylindrical, shim-adjusted, used in direct-acting DOHC) or **finger followers** (in some SOHC designs). 5. **Valve Springs** — Concentric helical springs (often dual springs with unequal pitch to avoid surge) that close the valve when the cam rotates past the lobe nose. 6. **Valve Stem Seals** — Viton or PTFE rubber seals preventing oil from entering the combustion chamber. **Working Principle:** - As the **crankshaft rotates** at engine speed, the **timing belt/chain** transmits motion to the camshaft at **½ engine speed**. - The **cam lobe** rotates and its **eccentric profile** pushes up the **tappet/bucket** directly below it. - In **SOHC**, the tappet pushes a **rocker arm** which pushes the valve down, opening it against the spring. - In **DOHC**, the bucket tappet pushes the valve directly, eliminating the rocker arm and reducing reciprocating mass. - When the cam rotates past the lobe nose, the **valve spring** returns the valve to its closed seat. - The cam profile is precisely synchronized such that intake valves open during the intake stroke and exhaust valves open during the exhaust stroke. #### Sub-Part (b): Comparison Table — OHC vs OHV [7 Marks] | Parameter | OHV (Pushrod) | OHC (Cam-in-Head) | |---|---|---| | **(i) Reciprocating Mass** | **Higher** (lifter + pushrod + rocker + valve = 4 components in motion) | **Lower** (lifter + valve only; bucket-tappet in DOHC eliminates rocker) | | **(ii) RPM Capability** | Limited (~5000–5500 RPM) due to valve float | **High** (7000–9000+ RPM) due to low inertia | | **(iii) Complexity** | **Simple** timing drive (single chain to block) but complex pushrod geometry | **Complex** belt routing, multiple sprockets, idlers; but simpler valve train | | **(iv) Typical Applications** | American V8s (Chevy, Ford), older Mahindra diesel, low-end torque engines | Maruti K-series, Honda i-VTEC, BMW, Toyota VVT-i, motorcycles | | **(v) Volumetric Efficiency** | Lower (longer flow path, pushrod restricts port) | **Higher** (straight port design, no obstruction) | | **(vi) Cost** | Lower | Higher (more components) | | **(vii) Lubrication** | Oil splash to lifters | Pressurized oil to bucket tappets via galleries | **Conclusion:** OHC is preferred for modern high-performance, high-RPM engines; OHV remains in use for low-cost, high-torque applications. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain in detail the layout of the air intake system in a modern multi-point fuel-injected (MPFI) petrol engine. Describe the function of each component. How does this differ from the air intake system of a turbocharged common-rail diesel engine? [14 Marks]** #### Sub-Part (a): MPFI Petrol Engine Intake System Layout [7 Marks] **Layout (with component functions):** 1. **Air Intake Snorkel** — A duct positioned to draw **cool, dust-free air** from outside the engine bay, often with a resonator to reduce intake noise. 2. **Air Filter Assembly** — Contains a **pleated paper filter element** that removes particulates (dust, debris) with efficiency **>99.5%**. Has a precisely sized surface area to minimize flow restriction. 3. **Mass Air Flow (MAF) Sensor** — Senses the **mass of air** entering the engine using either a **hot-wire** or **hot-film** element. Output signal goes to the ECU, which calculates the precise fuel quantity for stoichiometric combustion (A/F = 14.7:1). 4. **Intake Air Temperature (IAT) Sensor** — Embedded in the intake tract. ECU uses this to **correct air density** (warmer air = less dense = less fuel). 5. **Throttle Body** — Contains a **butterfly valve** that regulates airflow into the engine. In **drive-by-wire** systems, it is opened by an electric motor based on accelerator pedal position (no mechanical cable). Includes: - **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** — reports valve angle to ECU. - **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** — bypasses air around the closed throttle for cold-start and idle. 6. **Surge Tank / Plenum** — A **reservoir chamber** that smooths pressure pulsations in the intake, especially important in 4-cylinder engines where intake events occur every 180°. 7. **Intake Manifold** — Distributes air from the plenum to each cylinder through **individually tuned runners**. Modern designs use **variable-length intake manifolds** (e.g., Toyota's ACIS) to optimize torque at different RPMs. 8. **Fuel Injectors (MPFI)** — One **electromagnetic injector per cylinder**, mounted at the intake port, spraying fuel onto the back of the intake valve. Operates at **3–4 bar** pressure, pulsed by ECU. 9. **Intake Ports** — Carefully shaped channels in the cylinder head that generate **tumble and swirl** motion in the cylinder to enhance mixing. 10. **PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation) Valve** — Recirculates **blow-by gases** (combustion gases that leak past piston rings into the crankcase) back into the intake to be re-burned, reducing emissions. 11. **EVAP (Evaporative Emission Control) Canister** — Absorbs fuel vapors from the tank and purges them into the intake manifold under controlled conditions. #### Sub-Part (b): Comparison with Turbocharged CRDi Diesel Intake System [7 Marks] | Component / Feature | MPFI Petrol | Turbo CRDi Diesel | |---|---|---| | **Throttle Body** | Present, meters airflow for load control | **Absent** — load controlled by fuel quantity; air unrestricted | | **MAF Sensor** | Critical, ECU uses for stoichiometric A/F | Used for boost control and EGR management | | **Turbocharger** | Optional (mainly on performance petrols) | **Standard**, provides 1.5–2.5 bar boost | | **Intercooler** | Optional, on turbo petrols | **Standard**, cools compressed air by 50–100°C | | **EGR System** | For emissions only | **Mandatory** for BS-VI/Euro 6 NOₓ limits | | **Fuel Injector Location** | Intake port (MPFI) or direct (GDI) | **Direct** into cylinder at 2000+ bar | | **Intake Manifold** | Tuned for resonance, lower pressure | Designed for **swirl generation**, holds higher pressure | | **Swirl/Tumble Flaps** | Rare | **Common**, especially in IDI diesels | | **Surge Tank/Plenum** | Yes, for pulsation damping | Yes, must withstand boost pressure | | **Air-Fuel Ratio** | Stoichiometric 14.7:1 (λ=1) | Always lean (λ = 1.5–3.5) | | **PCV System** | Yes | Yes (closed system) | | **Emission Control** | Three-way catalyst (TWC) | DOC + DPF + SCR + EGR (aftertreatment heavy) | **Key Conceptual Difference:** Petrol intake systems **regulate air to control load** (with throttle), while diesel intake systems **supply maximum air continuously** (no throttle, high boost) and regulate load by **fuel quantity** alone. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 1:** > > 1. **Confusing IVO and EVO** — Many students mix up intake and exhaust valve opening events. **Remember:** IVO is **before TDC at the start of intake** (BTDC); EVO is **before BDC at the end of power** (BBDC). > > 2. **Forgetting the 2:1 ratio** — When asked for camshaft speed, students forget that a 4-stroke camshaft rotates at **half the crankshaft speed**. A 3000 RPM engine has a cam turning at **1500 RPM**. > > 3. **Writing "throttle in diesel"** — This is a **sure sign of incomplete understanding** to examiners. Diesel engines **do not use a throttle body** (naturally aspirated CRDi). Always state the reason: load is controlled by fuel quantity, not air. > > 4. **Skipping units in numericals** — Even if the calculation is right, **forgetting to write "kg/min" or "m³"** loses 0.5–1 mark. Always include units. > > 5. **Confusing "air-fuel ratio" and "fuel-air ratio"** — A/F = 14.7 (air-rich); F/A = 1/14.7 ≈ 0.068 (fuel-lean). Examiners test this swap. > > 6. **In valve timing diagram, not showing valve overlap** — A drawing without the overlap region is **incomplete** and loses 1–2 marks in 14-mark questions. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1** ### 🔹 Valve Actuating Mechanism - ✅ **OHV (Pushrod)** — Cam in block, valves in head, driven by pushrod & rocker. Robust, low-RPM, low-cost. Used in older V8s and heavy-duty diesels. - ✅ **OHC** — Cam in head. **SOHC** = one cam; **DOHC** = two cams. Lower mass, higher RPM, modern cars. - ✅ **Desmodromic** — Positive valve closure (no spring). Used in Ducati, F1. - ✅ **VVT / VTEC / Vanos** — Variable cam timing systems. Optimize performance across RPM. - ✅ **Cam rotates at ½ crank speed** in 4-stroke engines. - ✅ **Valve timing events:** IVO (BTDC), IVC (ABDC), EVO (BBDC), EVC (ATDC). - ✅ **Valve overlap** = IVO + EVC. Improves high-RPM scavenging, worsens idle. - ✅ **Maximum lift for circular cam:** $h_{max} = 2r(1 - \cos\theta_f)$. ### 🔹 Air Intake System — Petrol (SI) - ✅ Atmosphere → Snorkel → Air Filter → MAF → Throttle Body → Plenum → Manifold → Injector → Port → Intake Valve → Chamber. - ✅ **Throttle body present** to control load by air metering. - ✅ **Stoichiometric** A/F = 14.7:1 (λ = 1). - ✅ Components: MAF, TPS, IACV, PCV, EVAP, tuned runners. ### 🔹 Air Intake System — Diesel (CI) - ✅ **No throttle body** — load controlled by fuel quantity. - ✅ Always **excess air** (A/F = 18:1 to 70:1, λ > 1). - ✅ Turbo + Intercooler standard; EGR mandatory for emission norms. - ✅ **CRDi** = Common Rail Direct Injection at 2000+ bar. - ✅ Swirl/tumble generation critical for combustion. ### 🔹 Quick Numerical Formulas - ✅ $V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times L \times n$ (n = no. of cylinders) - ✅ $V_{theoretical} = V_d \times \frac{N}{2}$ (4-stroke) - ✅ $V_{actual} = \eta_{vol} \times V_{theoretical}$ - ✅ $m_{air} = \rho \times V_{actual}$ - ✅ $m_{fuel} = m_{air} / (A/F)$ ### 🔹 Examiners' High-Yield Keywords - "**Reciprocating mass**" (always in valvetrain comparison questions) - "**Valve float**" (when springs can't close valve in time) - "**Pumping loss**" (why no throttle in diesel) - "**Stoichiometric**" (always for petrol 3-way catalyst) - "**Boost pressure**" and "**Volumetric efficiency**" (always coupled) --- > **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — VALVE ACTUATING MECHANISMS & AIR INTAKE SYSTEM LAYOUT** > *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | Course Code: PBMUT404 | B.Tech Automobile / Mechanical Engineering*

topics.title: Components
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Engine and Transmission Components** refer to the complete set of mechanical, electromechanical, fluid-handling, and structural parts that collectively form the **Powertrain System** of a road vehicle. The powertrain is defined as the system that **generates, transmits, and delivers mechanical power** from the fuel source to the driving wheels.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an automobile is treated as an integration of **three major sub-systems**:

1. **Engine (Power Unit)** – Converts chemical energy of fuel into mechanical rotational energy.
2. **Transmission (Power Delivery Unit)** – Transfers and modulates the engine's torque/speed to the drive wheels.
3. **Auxiliary Systems** – Lubrication, cooling, fuel supply, ignition, intake, and exhaust — which keep the engine functional, efficient, and emissions-compliant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The Human Body**
> Think of a car like a human body:
> - The **Engine** is the **heart** — it pumps out energy (blood) instead of blood.
> - The **Clutch** is a **joint** that engages/disengages the energy flow.
> - The **Gearbox** is the **muscle gearing** — like the biceps that trade speed for strength (force).
> - The **Differential** is a **wrist** — letting the two wheels turn at different speeds when turning.
> - The **Cooling system** is **perspiration**, and the **Lubrication system** is **synovial fluid** in the joints.
> - The **Chassis** is the **skeleton**, and the **Body** is the **skin**.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following universal metrics govern automotive components under KTU syllabus:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Output | $P_s$ | **0.5 – 1.5** | kW/kg |
| Compression Ratio (SI) | $r_c$ | **8 – 12** | — |
| Compression Ratio (CI) | $r_c$ | **14 – 22** | — |
| Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth}$ | **25 – 40%** | — |
| Swept Volume | $V_s$ | **0.4 – 6.0** | L |

## 1.4 Classification of Automotive Components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Classification (Direct KTU Syllabus Hit)**
> 
> 1. **Engine Components**
>    - *Stationary:* Cylinder block, cylinder head, crankcase, manifolds, gaskets.
>    - *Moving:* Piston, piston rings, connecting rod, crankshaft, camshaft, valves, tappets.
>    - *Auxiliary engine parts:* Flywheel, vibration damper, timing gear, oil pan (sump).
> 2. **Transmission Components**
>    - *Clutch Assembly* (single-plate, multi-plate, centrifugal, hydraulic).
>    - *Gearbox* (sliding mesh, constant mesh, synchromesh, automatic/AMT/CVT/DCT).
>    - *Propeller Shaft* (with universal joints).
>    - *Final Drive & Differential* (bevel gear, crown wheel & pinion).
>    - *Drive Axles & Wheels* (live axle, dead/dead axle).
> 3. **Auxiliary Subsystems**
>    - Lubrication, Cooling, Fuel Supply, Ignition, Starting, Intake & Exhaust, Governing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Engine Components — Operational Breakdown

### A. Cylinder Block
- **Function:** The main structural casting that houses the cylinders, water jackets, and crankshaft.
- **Material:** Cast iron (CI) historically; modern trend is **aluminium alloy** for weight reduction.
- **Key Takeaway:** All other engine components are mounted onto the block — it is the "spine" of the engine.

### B. Cylinder Head
- **Function:** Closes the cylinder from the top, contains the **valves**, **combustion chamber**, **spark plug** (SI) / **injector** (CI), and ports.
- **Critical:** The head gasket seals combustion gases between block and head.

### C. Piston
- **Function:** Reciprocates inside the cylinder, converting gas pressure into linear force.
- **Material:** Aluminium alloy (lightweight, high thermal conductivity).
- **Design Variants:** Flat, dished, and bowl-in-piston.

### D. Piston Rings
- **Compression Rings (Top 1 or 2):** Seal combustion gases above the piston.
- **Oil Control Ring (Bottom):** Scrapes excess oil from cylinder walls back to the sump.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**C-O-C**" — *Compression* above, *Oil control* below.

### E. Connecting Rod
- Connects piston (via gudgeon/wrist pin) to the crankshaft (via big-end bearing).
- Subjected to both **tensile (combustion)** and **compressive (compression stroke)** loads — a classic case of **reversal of stress**.

### F. Crankshaft
- Converts reciprocating motion of the piston into rotary motion.
- Contains **crankpins, crank webs, and counterweights**.
- Output: drives the **flywheel** at one end and **timing gear** at the other.

### G. Camshaft
- Operates the **inlet and exhaust valves** through a precisely timed lobe profile.
- Driven from the crankshaft by **timing chain, belt, or gear** (typically **1:2 ratio** — camshaft rotates at half engine speed in 4-stroke engines).

### H. Valves
- **Inlet Valve:** Allows air (SI) or air-fuel mixture (CI) into the cylinder.
- **Exhaust Valve:** Expels burnt gases after combustion.
- **Design:** Typically poppet valves, with exhaust valves often **hollow and sodium-cooled** to handle high temperatures.

### I. Flywheel
- A heavy rotating disc that **stores kinetic energy** and smooths out power delivery between piston strokes.
- **Key Role:** Provides inertia for starting and absorbs torsional vibrations.

## 2.2 Transmission Components — Operational Breakdown

### A. Clutch
- A friction device that **temporarily disconnects** the engine from the gearbox to allow smooth gear changes.
- **Types (KTU High-Yield):**
  - Single-plate dry clutch (most common in passenger cars).
  - Multi-plate clutch (motorcycles, heavy vehicles).
  - Centrifugal clutch (small engines, scooters).
  - Hydraulic clutch (actuated by fluid pressure).

### B. Gearbox
- Provides **torque multiplication** and **speed reduction** to match driving conditions.
- **Gear Ratios (Illustrative):**

$$G = \frac{N_{\text{input}}}{N_{\text{output}}} = \frac{T_{\text{output}}}{T_{\text{input}}}$$

- **Types:** Sliding mesh → Constant mesh → Synchromesh (modern standard) → Automatic (AMT/AT/CVT/DCT).

### C. Propeller Shaft
- Long rotating shaft transmitting power from gearbox (rear) to the differential (rear axle) in a RWD vehicle.
- Always used with **universal (Hooke) joints** at both ends to handle angular misalignment.

### D. Differential
- Allows the **inner and outer drive wheels** to rotate at different speeds during cornering.
- Contains **bevel gears, crown wheel & pinion, and side gears**.

$$N_{\text{left}} + N_{\text{right}} = 2 \cdot N_{\text{crown}}$$

### E. Drive Axle
- **Live Axle:** Carries power + load (RWD, AWD).
- **Dead Axle:** Only carries load (front wheels of RWD vehicle).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Cheat-Sheet — All Must-Know Equations**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot n$ | Swept volume (n = number of cylinders) | m³ |
| 2 | $V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot (L + l_c)$ | Clearance volume | m³ |
| 3 | $r_c = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | Compression ratio | — |
| 4 | $\eta_{th} = \frac{W}{Q_{in}}$ | Thermal efficiency | — |
| 5 | $\text{BMEP} = \frac{T \cdot 2\pi}{V_s}$ | Brake mean effective pressure | Pa |
| 6 | $G = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}$ | Gear ratio (ideal, no losses) | — |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{mech}} = \frac{BP}{IP}$ | Mechanical efficiency | — |
| 8 | $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Power from torque | W |
| 9 | $v_p = L \cdot \frac{N}{30}$ | Mean piston speed | m/s |
| 10 | $\text{SFC} = \frac{m_f}{BP \cdot t}$ | Specific fuel consumption | kg/kWh |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Powertrain Components

- **SI Engines:** Lower compression, uses spark ignition, requires throttle body.
- **CI Engines:** Higher compression, no throttle, uses fuel injectors.
- **Modern Vehicles:** DCT (Dual Clutch Transmission) and CVT are widely used for fuel economy and comfort.
- **Electric/Hybrid:** Component list expands to include **battery pack, inverter, electric motor, and BMS** — these may appear in KTU module extensions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Swept Volume & Compression Ratio

**Given:**
- Bore (cylinder diameter) = $D$
- Stroke (piston travel) = $L$
- Clearance gap (head–piston TDC) = $l_c$
- Number of cylinders = $n$

**Step 1: Area of one piston face**
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 2: Swept volume (volume displaced by one piston per stroke)**
$$V_{s,\text{one}} = A \times L = \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4}$$

**Step 3: Total swept volume of engine**
$$V_s = n \times \frac{\pi D^2 L}{4}$$

**Step 4: Clearance volume (volume at TDC)**
$$V_c = \frac{\pi D^2 l_c}{4}$$

**Step 5: Compression ratio** — ratio of total cylinder volume at BDC to volume at TDC
$$r_c = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \frac{\frac{\pi D^2 L}{4} + \frac{\pi D^2 l_c}{4}}{\frac{\pi D^2 l_c}{4}}$$

**Step 6: Cancellation** — $\frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ cancels from numerator and denominator
$$r_c = \frac{L + l_c}{l_c} = 1 + \frac{L}{l_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a 4-stroke SI engine with $r_c = 10$ and $L = 80$ mm, the clearance gap $l_c = \frac{L}{r_c - 1} = \frac{80}{9} = 8.89$ mm — a remarkably tight physical dimension that demands precision machining.

## 3.2 Derivation: Gear Ratio and Torque-Speed Trade-off

**Given:**
- Input shaft speed (engine side) = $N_1$
- Output shaft speed (wheel side) = $N_2$
- Input torque = $T_1$
- Output torque = $T_2$

**Step 1: Define gear ratio** — ratio of teeth on driven gear to teeth on driver gear, equivalently ratio of speeds
$$G = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1}$$

**Step 2: For an ideal (lossless) gear train, power is conserved**
$$P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute power equation $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$**
$$\frac{2\pi N_1 T_1}{60} = \frac{2\pi N_2 T_2}{60}$$

**Step 4: Simplify and rearrange**
$$N_1 T_1 = N_2 T_2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = G$$

**Step 5: Final relationship** — A gear reduction ($G > 1$) increases torque and reduces speed
$$T_2 = G \cdot T_1 \quad\text{and}\quad N_2 = \frac{N_1}{G}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** This is the **mechanical equivalent of a lever** — you trade rotational speed for rotational force. First gear in a typical car has $G \approx 3.5$, meaning the wheel torque is 3.5× engine torque.

## 3.3 Derivation: Differential Speed Relationship

**Given:** A standard automotive differential with two side gears (left and right wheels) and a crown wheel rotating at $N_c$.

**Step 1: When vehicle moves straight**, both wheels must rotate at the same speed. If the carrier is locked, both side gears turn with the crown wheel.

**Step 2: When turning**, the differential action allows the two side gears to rotate at different speeds while their *average* equals the carrier speed.

**Step 3: Mathematical relationship** (Euler's classical result for bevel differentials)
$$N_{\text{left}} + N_{\text{right}} = 2 \cdot N_{\text{carrier}}$$

**Step 4: Example calculation** — If $N_{\text{carrier}} = 300$ rpm and outer wheel turns at 360 rpm, the inner wheel turns at:
$$N_{\text{inner}} = 2(300) - 360 = 240 \text{ rpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world note:** This is why one tyre on a car can spin freely on gravel while the other remains stationary — that's the differential in action.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Functions

| Component | Primary Function | Energy Form | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Piston | Force transmission | Pressure → Linear | Seizure, scuffing |
| Connecting Rod | Link & convert motion | Linear → Rotational | Buckling, fatigue |
| Crankshaft | Power output | Reciprocating → Rotary | Crank-shaft failure |
| Camshaft | Valve actuation | Rotary → Linear (timed) | Lobe wear |
| Flywheel | Energy storage | Rotational KE | Cracking, imbalance |
| Clutch | Torque connect/disconnect | Friction | Plate wear, slip |
| Gearbox | Torque/speed ratio | Mechanical | Tooth breakage |
| Differential | Speed difference | Mechanical | Gear whine, seizure |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Powertrain Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[FUEL SUPPLY SYSTEM]
