<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Modeled]**

### Q1. List the major components of a four-stroke SI engine and state the function of the **flywheel**.

**Model Answer:**
The major components of a 4-stroke SI engine are: cylinder block, cylinder head, piston, piston rings, connecting rod, crankshaft, camshaft, inlet valve, exhaust valve, spark plug, flywheel, and manifolds.

**Function of Flywheel:** The flywheel is a heavy rotating disc mounted on the crankshaft. It **stores rotational kinetic energy** during the power stroke and returns it during the idle strokes (intake, compression, exhaust), thereby ensuring smooth and uniform rotation of the crankshaft. It also helps in starting the engine by providing the initial inertia.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between a **live axle** and a **dead axle**. Where are they used?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Live Axle | Dead Axle |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Transmits power + carries load | Only carries load |
| Rotation | Rotates with the wheels | Stationary |
| Used in | Rear axle of RWD / 4WD vehicles, both front and rear of AWD | Front wheels of RWD vehicles |
| Example | Rear axle of a Maruti Swift (old) | Front axle of a Tata truck |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Modeled 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the layout of an automotive powertrain. List the major components of the **transmission system** and briefly explain the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The powertrain of an automobile is the complete system that generates, transmits, and delivers power from the engine to the driving wheels. The block diagram is as follows:

```
[ENGINE] → [CLUTCH] → [GEARBOX] → [PROPELLER SHAFT] → [DIFFERENTIAL] → [DRIVE WHEELS]
```

**Major Transmission Components:**

1. **Clutch:** A friction device that connects/disconnects the engine from the gearbox. Allows smooth gear changes and prevents engine stalling at rest.
2. **Gearbox:** Provides multiple gear ratios to match engine torque/speed with vehicle load and speed conditions. Modern gearboxes use synchromesh for clash-free shifting.
3. **Propeller Shaft:** A long rotating shaft (with universal joints at both ends) that transmits power from the gearbox output to the differential in RWD vehicles.
4. **Final Drive & Differential:** The **final drive** (crown and pinion) reduces speed and increases torque. The **differential** divides torque between two wheels and allows them to rotate at different speeds during cornering.
5. **Drive Axles (Half Shafts):** Connect the differential output to the drive wheels.

**Conclusion:** The transmission system multiplies engine torque, allows speed variation, reverses direction (via reverse gear), and disconnects power smoothly when required.

---

#### (b) Explain the construction and working of a **single-plate dry clutch** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A single-plate dry clutch consists of:
- **Flywheel** (driving member) — bolted to the engine crankshaft.
- **Clutch plate (driven member)** — a circular disc with friction lining on both faces, mounted on a splined hub that slides on the gearbox input shaft.
- **Pressure plate** — presses the clutch plate against the flywheel via **coil springs** (or diaphragm spring in modern designs).
- **Release (throw-out) bearing** — pushes the pressure plate forward when the pedal is pressed, disengaging the clutch.
- **Clutch pedal** — actuates the release mechanism via mechanical linkage or hydraulic fluid.

**Working:**
- **Engaged Position (Pedal Released):** Springs force the pressure plate against the friction plate, which in turn is clamped against the flywheel. Friction transmits torque from the engine to the gearbox input shaft.
- **Disengaged Position (Pedal Pressed):** The release bearing pushes the pressure plate forward, compressing the springs. The friction plate is released, breaking the contact. No torque is transmitted, allowing smooth gear changes.

**Diagrammatic Representation (Textual Layout):**
```
        [FLYWHEEL]
         |  |  |
   [FRICTION PLATE]
         |  |  |
     [PRESSURE PLATE]
        [SPRINGS]
       [RELEASE BEARING] ← from [CLUTCH PEDAL]
```

**Conclusion:** The single-plate dry clutch is the most widely used clutch in passenger cars due to its simplicity, efficiency (~98%), and ease of maintenance.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) Explain the working of a **four-stroke SI engine** with P-V and Valve Timing diagrams. List its major **engine components** and state the material used for the piston. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of a 4-Stroke SI Engine:**

The cycle completes in **two crankshaft revolutions (720°)** through four distinct strokes:

1. **Intake Stroke (0°–180°):** Inlet valve opens, piston moves from TDC to BDC. Fresh air-fuel mixture is drawn into the cylinder due to the partial vacuum created.
2. **Compression Stroke (180°–360°):** Both valves close. Piston moves from BDC to TDC, compressing the mixture (typical ratio **8:1 to 12:1**).
3. **Power Stroke (360°–540°):** At ~25° before TDC, the spark plug fires. Combustion raises pressure to **30–60 bar**, pushing the piston down. This is the only stroke producing work.
4. **Exhaust Stroke (540°–720°):** Exhaust valve opens, piston moves up, expelling burnt gases.

**Valve Timing Diagram (Degrees of Crank Rotation):**
- Inlet Valve Opens (IVO): ~10°–20° before TDC
- Inlet Valve Closes (IVC): ~30°–40° after BDC
- Exhaust Valve Opens (EVO): ~40°–50° before BDC
- Exhaust Valve Closes (EVC): ~10°–15° after TDC

**Major Engine Components (as listed earlier):** Cylinder block, head, piston, rings, connecting rod, crankshaft, camshaft, valves, flywheel, manifolds, spark plug.

**Piston Material:** **Aluminium alloy** (typically Al-Si alloys) — chosen for its low density (~2.7 g/cm³), high thermal conductivity, and good machinability. Cast iron pistons are used in heavy-duty diesel engines.

---

#### (b) With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **synchromesh gearbox**. State two advantages over a constant-mesh gearbox. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A synchromesh gearbox is a constant-mesh gearbox fitted with **synchronizer units** that equalize the speeds of the gear and the shaft before engagement, preventing clash and dog-tooth wear. Key parts:
- **Main shaft (input)** with all gears free to rotate.
- **Counter shaft (layshaft)** with all gears fixed, meshing with main shaft gears.
- **Synchronizer assembly** on the main shaft — consists of a **sleeve**, **brass synchro-rings (cones)**, and **dog clutch teeth**.
- **Selector fork** moved by the gear lever to slide the synchronizer sleeve.

**Working:**
1. When the driver moves the gear lever, the selector fork pushes the synchronizer sleeve toward the desired gear.
2. The brass synchro-ring (cone) makes frictional contact with the gear, creating a **frictional drag**.
3. This drag **equalizes the rotational speeds** of the sleeve and the target gear.
4. Once speeds match, the sleeve slides over the dog teeth of the gear, locking it positively to the main shaft.
5. Torque is transmitted without any clash or grinding.

**Diagram (Textual Layout):**
```
   [GEAR-A] [GEAR-B] [GEAR-C] [GEAR-D]
       ↕         ↕        ↕
  [SYNCHRO-1] [SYNCHRO-2] [SYNCHRO-3]
       ↕         ↕        ↕
       [MAIN SHAFT] ← input from clutch
```

**Two Advantages over Constant-Mesh Gearbox:**
1. **Silent and clash-free gear shifting** — synchronizers equalize speeds before engagement.
2. **Reduced wear of dog teeth** — engagement is friction-based initially, not impact-based, increasing gearbox life.
3. (Bonus) **Easier gear selection** for the driver, especially for novice drivers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing functions of compression rings and oil control rings** — remember "**C above, O below**" or use the COC mnemonic.
> 2. **Writing compression ratio formula incorrectly** — it is $r_c = (V_s + V_c)/V_c$, not $V_s/V_c$.
> 3. **Forgetting that camshaft rotates at half engine speed** in 4-stroke engines — this is a free 1-mark question, often missed.
> 4. **Mixing up "constant mesh" and "synchromesh"** — constant mesh has gears always meshed (sliding dog clutches); synchromesh has synchronizers added. Both have gears always in mesh.
> 5. **Omitting units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit**.
> 6. **Skipping the neat sketch** in clutch/gearbox questions — **1–2 marks reserved** for diagram in KTU scheme.
> 7. **Using "engine" and "motor" interchangeably** — KTU strictly uses "engine" for IC engine and "motor" for electric machine.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Components**
> 
> ✅ The **engine** is the power unit; **transmission** delivers power to wheels; **auxiliary systems** support engine function.
> 
> ✅ **Cylinder Block** = spine; **Cylinder Head** = top closure; **Piston** = force generator; **Crankshaft** = rotary output.
> 
> ✅ Piston rings: **Compression (top)** seal gas; **Oil control (bottom)** scrapes oil.
> 
> ✅ **Camshaft runs at half engine speed** in 4-stroke engines — driven by timing chain/belt/gear.
> 
> ✅ **Flywheel** stores KE and smooths rotation; also aids in starting.
> 
> ✅ **Clutch** = friction device for engage/disengage; **Gearbox** = torque multiplier and speed reducer.
> 
> ✅ **Differential** allows wheel speed difference during turning: $N_L + N_R = 2N_c$.
> 
> ✅ **Live axle** transmits power + carries load; **Dead axle** only carries load.
> 
> ✅ **Compression Ratio:** $r_c = 1 + L/l_c$ (geometry form) or $r_c = (V_s + V_c)/V_c$ (volume form).
> 
> ✅ **Gear Ratio:** $G = N_1/N_2 = T_2/T_1$ (ideal).
> 
> ✅ **Piston material:** Aluminium alloy (SI) and Cast iron (heavy CI).
> 
> ✅ **Power equation:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ in Watts.
> 
> ✅ Always label axes of P-V and valve timing diagrams and provide units in numerical answers.
> 
> ✅ Modern trends: **DCT and CVT** transmissions, **aluminium blocks**, **turbocharging**, and **hybrid powertrains** — keep an eye on KTU 2024 elective modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Filter: Types and Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Filter: Types and Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Air Filter** is a critical ancillary component of an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** induction system, designed to remove airborne particulate contaminants (dust, dirt, pollen, soot, and debris) from the atmospheric air before it enters the engine cylinders via the carburetor or fuel injection system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBMUT404):**
> An automotive air filter is a mechanical device employing a porous filter medium of specific micron rating to ensure that the **Air-Fuel (A/F) ratio** remains stoichiometric and that the volumetric efficiency of the engine is not compromised by intake air contamination.

**Standard Air Contamination Threshold (KTU Reference):**
- Atmospheric dust concentration: **0.1 to 1.0 mg/m³** (urban) and up to **10 mg/m³** (off-road)
- Filter efficiency requirement: **≥ 99%** for particles **≥ 5 microns**
- Permissible pressure drop across a clean filter: **≤ 25 mbar (2500 Pa)**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Nose of the Car":**
> Think of the air filter as the **"nose"** of a car engine. Just as human nose hair and mucous trap dust and germs before air reaches the lungs, the air filter traps dirt particles before air reaches the engine "lungs" (combustion chambers). If you breathe dirty air, you fall sick; if an engine breathes dirty air, its pistons, rings, and cylinder walls wear out prematurely.

**Why is an air filter non-negotiable?**
- 1 gram of dust entering an engine can cause **~40%** increase in cylinder wear.
- A modern passenger car consumes **~10,000 to 15,000 liters of air per 100 km**.
- Without filtration, abrasive silica particles score the cylinder walls.

## 1.3 Functional Requirements of an Air Filter (KTU Board Favourite)

1. **High Filtration Efficiency** (≥ 99% for particles > 5 μm)
2. **Low Restriction to Air Flow** (minimal pressure drop)
3. **High Dust Holding Capacity**
4. **Long Service Life**
5. **Moisture and Heat Resistance**
6. **Compact Size & Lightweight**
7. **Easy Replacement / Servicing**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Stage-wise Filtration

A modern automotive air filter operates on **three simultaneous filtration mechanisms**:

1. **Inertial Impaction** — Larger particles (≥ 10 μm), due to mass, cannot follow the curved air streamlines around the filter fibers and strike the fibers directly.
2. **Interception** — Medium particles (1–10 μm) follow streamlines but get caught when they brush against a fiber.
3. **Diffusion (Brownian Motion)** — Submicron particles (≤ 1 μm) have random motion and randomly collide with fibers.

> [!NOTE]
> The **most penetrating particle size (MPPS)** is typically **0.1 – 0.3 μm**. Filters are tested against this critical size.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Symbol / Expression | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V}_a$ | kg/s | Sizing filter area |
| 2 | Volumetric Air Flow | $\dot{V}_a = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot N \cdot n_c}{2 \cdot 60}$ | m³/s | Designing intake restriction |
| 3 | Filter Efficiency | $\eta_f = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Filtration quality |
| 4 | Pressure Drop (Darcy) | $\Delta P = \frac{\mu \cdot v \cdot L}{k \cdot A}$ | Pa | Restriction analysis |
| 5 | Air Density (ICAO) | $\rho_a = 1.225 \left(\frac{293}{T}\right)$ | kg/m³ | Altitude correction |
| 6 | Stoichiometric A/F | $(\text{A/F})_{stoich} = 14.7 : 1$ | ratio | Petrol engine basis |
| 7 | Diesel A/F Ratio | $(\text{A/F})_{diesel} = 18 : 1$ to $22 : 1$ | ratio | Diesel engine basis |
| 8 | Filter Life Index | $T_{life} = \frac{C_{cap}}{\dot{m}_d}$ | hours | Service interval |
| 9 | Engine Air Demand | $\dot{m}_a = \frac{\text{BSFC} \cdot P_e}{3600 \cdot \eta_{th}}$ | kg/s | Matching filter capacity |
| 10 | Restriction Penalty | $\eta_{vol,loss} \approx k \cdot \Delta P_{filter}$ | %/Pa | Performance loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Convention:** $\dot{V}_a$ = air volume flow, $V_d$ = displacement, $N$ = RPM, $n_c$ = number of cylinders, $C_{in}/C_{out}$ = inlet/outlet dust concentration, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $k$ = permeability of filter medium, $C_{cap}$ = dust capacity, $\dot{m}_d$ = dust ingestion rate.

## 2.3 Real-world Engineering Utility

- **Emission Control:** Filtered air prevents unburned hydrocarbon formation due to incomplete combustion.
- **BS-VI/Euro 6 Compliance:** Clean intake air is essential for precise Lambda ($\lambda$) sensor readings and SCR catalyst longevity.
- **Heavy-duty applications:** Construction equipment and trucks use **two-stage filtration** (primary + safety element) to extend service intervals to **100,000+ km**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Air Filter Pressure Drop Across a Pleated Element

> **Scenario:** A pleated paper air filter element has the following specifications:
> - Filter area (flat) = $A_f = 0.25$ m²
> - Pleat depth = $h_p = 25$ mm = 0.025 m
> - Number of pleats = $N_p = 100$
> - Pleat pitch = $p = 5$ mm
> - Air velocity through filter = $v = 1.5$ m/s
> - Permeability of paper medium = $k = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²
> - Dynamic viscosity of air = $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s

### Step 1 — Effective Filtration Area Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{eff} &= A_f \times \left( \frac{\text{Pleat length}}{\text{Pleat pitch}} \right) \\
A_{eff} &= 0.25 \times \left( \frac{2 \times h_p}{p} \right) \times N_p \times p \\
A_{eff} &= 0.25 \times \left( \frac{2 \times 0.025}{0.005} \right) \\
A_{eff} &= 0.25 \times 10 \\
A_{eff} &= 2.5 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The pleated geometry multiplies the flat filter area by the ratio of pleat length to pleat pitch. This 10× area increase drastically reduces face velocity and pressure drop.

### Step 2 — Darcy's Law Application

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{\mu \cdot v \cdot L}{k \cdot A_{eff}} \\
\Delta P &= \frac{1.81 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 1.5 \cdot L}{2 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 2.5} \\
\Delta P &= \frac{2.715 \times 10^{-5} \cdot L}{5.0 \times 10^{-9}} \\
\Delta P &= 5430 \cdot L \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** For a typical filter medium thickness $L = 0.4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m:

### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 5430 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} \\
\Delta P &= 2.172 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The total pressure drop of 2.172 Pa is well within the permissible 25 mbar (2500 Pa) limit, confirming the design is safe.

### Step 4 — Restriction Penalty on Volumetric Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{vol,loss} &\approx k_r \cdot \Delta P \\
\eta_{vol,loss} &\approx 0.001\% / \text{Pa} \times 2.172 \text{ Pa} \\
\eta_{vol,loss} &\approx 0.0022\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This negligible loss confirms the pleated design preserves engine breathing capacity.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Filter Types

| Parameter | Paper (Dry) | Oil Bath (Wet) | Foam (PU) | Cotton Gauze | Cyclonic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Filtration Efficiency | **99%** | 95–98% | 92–96% | 96–98% | 85–95% |
| Pressure Drop | Low | Medium | Medium-High | Low | Very Low |
| Service Life | 20,000 km | 10,000 km | Reusable | Reusable | 50,000+ km |
| Cost | Low | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Maintenance | Replace | Oil change | Wash + Oil | Wash + Oil | Self-cleaning |
| Application | Passenger cars | Old vehicles, tractors | Motorcycles, off-road | Performance cars | Heavy trucks, mining |
| Moisture Tolerance | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| Micron Rating | 5–10 μm | 10–20 μm | 15–25 μm | 5–8 μm | 2–5 μm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Air Filter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> B[Air Cleaner Housing]
    B --> C[Inlet Snorkel Duct]
    C --> D[Pre-Filter Screen]
    D --> E[Main Filter Element]
    E --> F{Filter Type Selector}
    F -->|Paper Element| G1[Pleated Cellulose Media]
    F -->|Oil Bath| G2[Mesh Element in Oil Reservoir]
    F -->|Foam Type| G3[Polyurethane Foam with Oil]
    F -->|Cotton Gauze| G4[Oiled Cotton Pleats]
    F -->|Cyclonic| G5[Centrifugal Vane Chamber]
    G1 --> H[Clean Air Outlet]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I[Throttle Body / Carburetor]
    I --> J[Intake Manifold]
    J --> K[Engine Combustion Chamber]
    L[Vacuum Operated Valve] -.-> E
    M[Dust Collection Bowl] -.-> G2
    N[Hot Air Intake Duct for Cold Start] -.-> B
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Paper Element Air Filter Construction (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Dirty Air Inlet] --> B1[Housing Cover with Clips]
    B1 --> C1[Rubber Sealing Gasket]
    C1 --> D1[Top Retainer Plate]
    D1 --> E1[Pleated Paper Filter Element]
    E1 --> F1[Bottom Support Mesh]
    F1 --> G1[Center Outlet Tube]
    G1 --> H1[Rubber Mounting Grommet]
    H1 --> I1[Clean Air to Throttle Body]
    J1[Dust Drainage Valve at Bottom] -.-> E1
    K1[Vacuum Operated Hot Air Flap] -.-> B1
```

## 4.3 Constructional Details of Major Air Filter Types

### (A) Paper (Dry) Element Filter — Most Common in Modern Cars
- **Media:** Cellulose fiber paper, often resin-impregnated.
- **Geometry:** Pleated accordion-fold (depth 20–40 mm) for high surface area.
- **Frame:** Plastic end caps with polyurethane sealant.
- **Housing:** Polymer or metal box with snap-fit clips.
- **Service:** Replace at **20,000 km or 12 months** (whichever earlier).
- **Examples:** Bosch, Mann-Filter, Mahle.

### (B) Oil Bath (Wet) Filter — Vintage & Heavy Vehicles
- **Construction:** Mesh element dipped in a reservoir of engine oil.
- **Air Path:** Dirty air enters top, passes through oil-soaked mesh, dirt sticks to oil, clean air exits.
- **Service:** Replace oil every **5,000–10,000 km**; clean mesh in kerosene.
- **Used in:** Tractors, Jeeps (Mahindra), older commercial vehicles.

### (C) Foam (Polyurethane) Filter
- **Media:** Open-cell polyurethane foam, typically 10–15 mm thick.
- **Working:** Foam is oiled before installation; oil acts as the sticky surface for dust.
- **Applications:** Royal Enfield, KTM Duke, off-road bikes.
- **Service:** Wash in solvent, re-oil, and reinstall every **5,000 km**.

### (D) Cotton Gauze Filter (Reusable)
- **Media:** Multiple layers of oiled cotton gauze (e.g., K&N).
- **Advantage:** Lasts the lifetime of the vehicle; washable.
- **Used in:** Performance cars, racing applications.

### (E) Cyclonic Air Cleaner
- **Working:** Air enters tangentially, creating a vortex; heavier dust particles are thrown outward into a collection cup; clean air moves to the center.
- **Self-Cleaning:** No filter media replacement.
- **Used in:** Volvo trucks, Scania, mining dump trucks, Tata Prima.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention the **two-stage filtration** system used in heavy commercial vehicles — a **primary element** (outer, replaceable) and a **safety element** (inner, only replaced when primary fails). This is a frequently asked question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**"List any three functional requirements of an automotive air filter."**

**Model Answer:**
1. High filtration efficiency (**≥ 99%** for particles > 5 μm).
2. Low restriction to air flow with pressure drop **≤ 25 mbar**.
3. High dust holding capacity for extended service life.

*(Any three from syllabus list)* — **3 Marks**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**"What is the role of an air filter in an IC engine?"**

**Model Answer:**
The air filter removes airborne contaminants from the intake air, ensuring clean air reaches the combustion chamber. It prevents abrasive wear of cylinder walls, pistons, and valves, and contributes to maintaining the **stoichiometric A/F ratio of 14.7:1** for petrol engines. It also ensures compliance with **BS-VI emission norms** by enabling complete combustion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**
**(a) Explain the construction and working of a paper element air filter with a neat sketch. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Compare oil bath, paper element, and cyclonic air cleaners. (7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### (a) Paper Element Air Filter — Construction & Working

**Construction:**
1. **Housing** — A polymer or metallic rectangular/cylindrical box with a top cover secured by clips or bolts.
2. **Filter Element** — Pleated cellulose paper sealed between two end caps (top plastic retainer and bottom metal mesh support).
3. **Sealing Gasket** — Rubber gasket prevents bypass leakage of unfiltered air.
4. **Inlet Duct** — A snorkel-shaped duct draws air from a cool, dust-free zone (above the engine bay).
5. **Vacuum-Operated Hot Air Flap** — In cold climates, draws warm air from around the exhaust manifold.
6. **Outlet Tube** — Central tube delivers clean air to the carburetor/throttle body.

**Working:**
- Atmospheric air enters through the inlet snorkel.
- It passes through the pleated paper medium; **inertial impaction, interception, and diffusion** trap the dust particles.
- Clean air exits through the central outlet tube to the throttle body.
- Vacuum-operated flap regulates inlet temperature.

**Neat Sketch:** *(Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 above)*

#### (b) Comparison: Oil Bath vs Paper Element vs Cyclonic

| Feature | Oil Bath | Paper Element | Cyclonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Filtration Mechanism | Oil adhesion | Mechanical + electrostatic | Centrifugal force |
| Efficiency | 95–98% | 99% | 85–95% |
| Maintenance | Oil change, mesh cleaning | Element replacement | Dust cup emptying |
| Service Interval | 5,000–10,000 km | 20,000 km | 50,000+ km |
| Cost | Low | Low | High |
| Application | Tractors, old vehicles | Modern passenger cars | Trucks, mining |

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**
**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the working of a cyclonic air cleaner. State its advantages. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Explain the constructional features of a foam-type air filter. Why is it preferred for off-road vehicles? (7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### (a) Cyclonic Air Cleaner

**Construction:**
- Tangential air inlet creates a **spiral/swirl motion**.
- Cylindrical body with dust collection cup at the bottom.
- Central clean air outlet tube (vortex finder).
- No filter media — uses **centrifugal force**.

**Working:**
1. Air enters tangentially → high-velocity vortex forms.
2. Heavier dust particles are flung outward against the wall.
3. Particles fall into the dust collection cup due to gravity.
4. Clean air spirals upward through the central outlet.

**Advantages:**
- No filter media to replace — **maintenance-free**.
- Suitable for extremely dusty environments.
- Long service life (**> 100,000 km**).
- Used in mining, construction, and heavy commercial vehicles.

#### (b) Foam-Type Air Filter

**Construction:**
- **Open-cell polyurethane foam** wrapped around a plastic cage.
- Foam is **saturated with special filter oil** before installation.
- Mounted in a housing with sealing flanges.

**Working:**
- Air passes through oiled foam; dust sticks to the oil film.
- Foam can be washed, re-oiled, and reused.

**Why Preferred for Off-Road Vehicles:**
1. **High dust load tolerance** — foam holds more dirt per unit volume.
2. **Reusability** — washable, reducing spares in remote areas.
3. **Moisture resistance** — performs well in humid/muddy conditions.
4. **Mechanical durability** — foam doesn't tear like paper in rough use.
5. **Lightweight** — critical for motorcycles and ATVs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Avoid these mistakes:**
> 1. ❌ Confusing the **MPPS (Most Penetrating Particle Size)** with the largest filterable particle.
> 2. ❌ Writing "air filter purifies air" — it **filters**, not purifies (purification involves chemical processes).
> 3. ❌ Forgetting to mention the **vacuum-operated hot air flap** in paper filter construction.
> 4. ❌ Confusing **oil bath** with **foam** filters — both use oil, but foam is reusable and dry-type oiled.
> 5. ❌ Not mentioning the **stoichiometric A/F ratio (14.7:1)** when discussing the role of air filter.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Air Filter**

- ⭐ **Purpose:** Removes airborne dust to protect engine from abrasive wear.
- ⭐ **Three Filtration Mechanisms:** Inertial Impaction, Interception, Brownian Diffusion.
- ⭐ **MPPS:** 0.1–0.3 μm — most difficult particle size to filter.
- ⭐ **Five Major Types:** Paper (Dry), Oil Bath, Foam, Cotton Gauze, Cyclonic.
- ⭐ **Most Common Type Today:** Pleated paper element (dry).
- ⭐ **Best for Heavy Dust:** Cyclonic (self-cleaning) + safety element (two-stage).
- ⭐ **Pressure Drop Limit:** **≤ 25 mbar** for a clean filter.
- ⭐ **Filtration Efficiency:** **≥ 99%** for particles > 5 μm.
- ⭐ **Stoichiometric A/F:** 14.7:1 (petrol), 18–22:1 (diesel).
- ⭐ **Pleat Geometry:** Increases effective area by **8 to 10 times** the flat area.
- ⭐ **Two-Stage Filtration:** Primary (outer, replaceable) + Safety (inner, backup).
- ⭐ **Service Interval (Paper):** 20,000 km or 12 months.
- ⭐ **Hot Air Flap:** Vacuum-operated, used in cold climates to prevent carburetor icing.
- ⭐ **Volumetric Efficiency Loss:** Proportional to filter restriction (≈ 0.001 % per Pa).
- ⭐ **Modern Trend:** Integrated air filter-housing units with mass air flow (MAF) sensors for ECU feedback.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Air Filter: Types and Construction**
> **Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020 Aligned) | PBMUT404**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Fuel Feed Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Components of Fuel Feed Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> The **Fuel Feed System** in an internal combustion engine is the integrated assembly of mechanical, electromechanical, and hydraulic components responsible for **storing, filtering, pressurizing, metering, and delivering** liquid or gaseous fuel from the fuel tank to the engine's combustion chambers at the correct pressure, flow rate, and timing for efficient and clean combustion.

The fuel feed system is a **sub-system of the broader Engine Management System (EMS)** and operates between two endpoints: the **fuel reservoir (tank)** and the **combustion chamber (intake port / direct injection cylinder)**.

### 1.1.1 Primary Functional Objectives

| S.No | Objective | Engineering Significance |
|:----:|:----------|:-------------------------|
| 1 | **Storage of fuel** | Capacity for ~400–600 km driving range |
| 2 | **Filtration** | Removal of particulates ≥ 10 µm |
| 3 | **Pressurization** | 3–4 bar (MPI) or 200+ bar (GDI/Diesel CRDi) |
| 4 | **Metering** | Stoichiometric AFR control (14.7:1 gasoline) |
| 5 | **Delivery timing** | PFI: port injection; DI: in-cylinder direct |
| 6 | **Vapor management** | EVAP system to capture HC vapors |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Human Circulatory System":**
> Think of the fuel feed system exactly like the **human blood circulatory system**. The **fuel tank** is the *heart's reservoir* (stomach/glycogen). The **fuel pump** is the *heart* (provides pressure). The **fuel lines** are *arteries and veins* (conduits). The **fuel filter** is the *kidneys* (purification). The **injectors** are the *capillaries* that deliver oxygenated blood (fuel-air mix) to the cells (combustion chambers). The **pressure regulator** is the *autonomic nervous system* (maintains homeostasis of pressure).
>
> Just as a clogged artery or weak heart reduces the body's efficiency, a weak pump or blocked filter will starve the engine of fuel — causing power loss, misfire, and eventual component damage.

### 1.2.1 Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board Numerical Values)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Gasoline Stoichiometric AFR**: **14.7 : 1** (by mass)
> - **Diesel Stoichiometric AFR**: **14.5 : 1**
> - **Typical Injection Pressure (PFI)**: **3.5 bar**
> - **Typical Injection Pressure (GDI)**: **100–350 bar**
> - **Common Rail Diesel (CRDi)**: **1600–2500 bar**
> - **Filter Mesh Size (In-line)**: **10–30 µm**
> - **Pump Flow Rate (Electric)**: **80–200 L/h**
> - **Fuel Tank Capacity (Sedan)**: **45–70 L**
> - **Fuel Density (Gasoline)**: **0.74–0.77 kg/L**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of the Fuel Feed System

The fuel feed system can be logically decomposed into **six (6) sequential functional stages**. Each stage has dedicated components that perform a specific physical transformation on the fuel.

### Stage 1: Storage (Fuel Tank)
- Holds liquid fuel under atmospheric or slight pressure.
- Contains a **fuel strainer (sock filter)** at the pickup tube to trap large debris (≥ 100 µm).
- Has a **vent valve** to allow air exchange as fuel is consumed.
- Houses a **fuel level sender unit (float arm + variable resistor)** that drives the dashboard fuel gauge.

### Stage 2: Suction & Pressurization (Fuel Pump)
- **Mechanical Pump (older carbureted engines)**: Diaphragm-type, driven by camshaft via pushrod; produces ~0.3–0.7 bar.
- **Electric Pump (modern MPI/GDI)**: Submerged in-tank brushless DC motor; produces **3.0–6.5 bar**.
- **High-Pressure Pump (Diesel CRDi)**: Radial piston pump driven by camshaft; produces **1600+ bar**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In-Tank Submerged Pump Principle:** The pump is placed *inside* the fuel tank to ensure it always operates in a flooded condition, eliminating cavitation. Surrounding fuel also cools the pump's electric motor.

### Stage 3: Filtration (Fuel Filter)
- **Primary Filter (In-tank sock)**: Coarse mesh (~100 µm).
- **Secondary Filter (In-line cartridge)**: Fine filtration (**10–30 µm**).
- Modern filters use **pleated synthetic media** (cellulose or polyester) with high surface area to handle high flow rates with low pressure drop.

### Stage 4: Pressure Regulation (Fuel Pressure Regulator)
- **Vacuum-referenced diaphragm regulator** (older MPI systems).
- Maintains a constant **pressure differential across the injector** regardless of manifold vacuum.
- Excess fuel returns to the tank via the **return line**.

> **In modern returnless systems**, pressure regulation is done by the **ECU** commanding the pump's PWM duty cycle (no mechanical regulator).

### Stage 5: Distribution (Fuel Rail & Injectors)
- **Fuel Rail**: A common accumulator tube supplying equally to all injectors.
- **Injectors**: Solenoid or piezo-electric valves, electronically pulsed by the ECU.
- Injection duration (**pulse width**) determines fuel quantity.

### Stage 6: Return / Venting (Return Line + EVAP)
- **Return line**: Returns excess fuel to the tank (in return-type systems).
- **EVAP system**: Captures fuel vapors in a charcoal canister and purges them into the intake.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** No vertical pipe `|` character is used inside any cell. All mathematical "such that" or "divides" notations use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

| S.No | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | KTU Application |
|:----:|:-------------------|:-----------------|:--------------|:----------------|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_f = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate of fuel | kg/s | Pumping capacity |
| 2 | $P_{inj} = P_{rail} - P_{manifold}$ | Net pressure drop across injector | bar | Injector sizing |
| 3 | $Q_{inj} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$ | Injector flow equation | L/min | Pulse width calc |
| 4 | $AFR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$ | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis) | dimensionless | Mixture control |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Lambda (excess air factor) | dimensionless | ECU fuel trim |
| 6 | $t_{inj} = \dfrac{m_{req}}{Q_{inj}/\Delta t}$ | Injector pulse width | ms | Fuel mapping |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{filter} = \dfrac{128 \mu L \dot{V}}{\pi d^4}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille pressure drop in filter | Pa | Filter design |
| 8 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho g H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Pump head pressure (ideal) | Pa | Pump selection |
| 9 | $V_{tank} \geq R_{range} \cdot \dot{m}_f$ | Tank sizing for given range | L | Vehicle design |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{EVAP} = k \cdot A \cdot (P_{tank} - P_{atm})$ | Vapor leakage rate | g/day | EVAP diagnostics |

### 2.2.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs** use these formulas during **calibration** of the Engine Control Unit (ECU) to build the fuel map (a 3D lookup table of RPM vs. Load vs. Pulse Width).
- **Service technicians** use pressure-drop formulas to diagnose clogged filters (a high $\Delta P$ indicates blockage).
- **Emission engineers** use $\lambda$ to ensure Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC) operates near $\lambda = 1 \pm 0.01$ for optimum HC/CO/NOx conversion.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Injector Flow Rate (Bernoulli-Based Orifice Flow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this derivation matters for KTU Board Exams:** Injector flow equations are tested in 7-mark sub-parts, and understanding the **square-root relationship** between pressure drop and flow is essential for fuel map problems.

### Step 1 — Start with Bernoulli's Equation

For an incompressible fluid flowing through an injector orifice, apply Bernoulli between the upstream (rail) point (1) and the downstream (intake port) point (2):

$$
P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_f v_1^2 + \rho_f g z_1 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_f v_2^2 + \rho_f g z_2
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Geometric and Boundary Assumptions

- The fuel rail is large → $v_1 \approx 0$ (stagnant upstream).
- The injector is horizontal → $z_1 = z_2$ (no height change).
- Therefore, the equation simplifies to:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_f v_2^2
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Exit Velocity

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_f}}
$$

Let $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ denote the pressure differential across the injector.

### Step 4 — Introduce the Discharge Coefficient

The real flow is less than the ideal flow because of viscous losses, vena contracta, and turbulence. The **discharge coefficient $C_d$** accounts for these:

$$
C_d = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{ideal}} \quad ; \quad 0.6 \leq C_d \leq 0.95
$$

### Step 5 — Write the Volumetric Flow Rate Equation

$$
Q_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{orifice} \cdot v_2
$$

Substituting the expression for $v_2$:

$$
Q_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_{orifice} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_f}}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Compact Form (THE KTU BOARD FORMULA)

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{inj} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_f}}\,}
$$

**Textual logic**: The injector flow is proportional to the **square root of the pressure drop** and **linearly proportional to the orifice area**. Doubling $\Delta P$ increases flow by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ times — a key reason why diesel systems use *enormous* pressures (2000 bar) to achieve reasonable flow through small orifices.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pulse Width (Injector ON-Time) Calculation

### Step 1 — Define Required Fuel Mass

The engine requires a fuel mass $m_{req}$ per combustion cycle:

$$
m_{req} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{AFR_{target}}
$$

### Step 2 — Relate Mass to Volumetric Flow

$$
m_{req} = \rho_f \cdot Q_{inj} \cdot t_{inj}
$$

where $t_{inj}$ is the injector pulse width in seconds.

### Step 3 — Solve for Pulse Width

$$
t_{inj} = \dfrac{m_{req}}{\rho_f \cdot Q_{inj}}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Injector Flow Equation

$$
t_{inj} = \dfrac{m_{req}}{\rho_f \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_f}}}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplify (Rearrange)

$$
t_{inj} = \dfrac{m_{req}}{C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_f \cdot \Delta P}}
$$

**Textual logic**: To deliver the same fuel mass at *higher* rail pressure, the ECU must command a *shorter* pulse width. This is why GDI systems can achieve more precise fueling at low loads.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Component-to-Failure Mode Map

| Component | Primary Function | Common Failure Mode | Symptom | Diagnostic Test |
|:----------|:-----------------|:--------------------|:--------|:----------------|
| **Fuel Pump** | Pressurize fuel | Motor burnout, relay failure | Engine no-start, stalling | Pressure gauge at Schrader valve |
| **Fuel Filter** | Remove particulates | Clogging (media saturation) | Power loss under load | $\Delta P$ measurement |
| **Fuel Pressure Regulator** | Maintain rail pressure | Diaphragm rupture | Black smoke, rich mixture | Vacuum line check |
| **Injector** | Atomize & meter fuel | Coil failure, tip coking | Misfire, rough idle | Noid light / resistance test |
| **Fuel Rail** | Distribute equal pressure | Internal blockage | Cylinder-specific fuel starvation | Pressure drop test per cylinder |
| **Fuel Tank** | Store fuel | Rust, contamination | Sediment in filter | Sump drain inspection |
| **Return Line** | Return excess fuel | Kinking, blockage | Pressure spikes | Visual + pressure test |
| **EVAP Canister** | Trap HC vapors | Charcoal saturation | Hard starting, CEL P0440 | Smoke machine test |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A 4-cylinder gasoline engine runs at 3000 RPM with a volumetric efficiency of 0.88, displacement of 2.0 L, $\lambda = 1.0$, gasoline density 0.75 kg/L, and rail pressure 3.5 bar. The injector orifice has $C_d = 0.85$ and $A = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m². Calculate the injector pulse width per cycle.

### Step 1: Air Mass Flow Rate (4-stroke engine)

For a 4-stroke, each cylinder fires once every 2 revolutions, so total cycles/sec = $\dfrac{3000}{2 \times 60} = 25$ cycles/s.

$$
\dot{V}_{air} = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{2}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{air} = 0.88 \cdot 0.002 \text{ m}^3 \cdot 25 = 0.044 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 2: Air Mass Flow (using $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³)

$$
\dot{m}_a = 0.044 \cdot 1.2 = 0.0528 \text{ kg/s}
$$

### Step 3: Fuel Mass Flow (Stoichiometric AFR = 14.7)

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{0.0528}{14.7} = 3.59 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

### Step 4: Fuel Mass per Cycle (1 injector serves 1 cylinder, 25 cycles/s for 4 cylinders = 100 injector events/s)

Total injector events per second = $\dfrac{4 \times 3000}{2 \times 60} = 100$ events/s.

$$
m_{per \, event} = \dfrac{3.59 \times 10^{-3}}{100} = 3.59 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 5: Injector Volumetric Flow at $\Delta P = 3.5 \times 10^5$ Pa

$$
Q_{inj} = 0.85 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 3.5 \times 10^5}{750}}
$$

$$
Q_{inj} = 2.125 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \sqrt{933.33} = 2.125 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 30.55
$$

$$
Q_{inj} = 6.49 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 64.9 \text{ mL/s}
$$

### Step 6: Pulse Width

$$
t_{inj} = \dfrac{3.59 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg}}{750 \text{ kg/m}^3 \cdot 6.49 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

$$
t_{inj} = \dfrac{3.59 \times 10^{-5}}{4.87 \times 10^{-2}} = 7.37 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \approx 0.74 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $t_{inj} \approx \mathbf{0.74 \text{ ms}}$ — a realistic value for a PFI injector at mid-load.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Rationale:** A physical pictorial drawing of all fuel feed components is impractical in Mermaid (reserved keywords block complex paths). Instead, a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** clearly shows the **signal/energy/fluid flow** between each component — exactly what KTU board examiners expect in 7-mark "explain with block diagram" questions.

```mermaid

> **Use this ASCII schematic in 7-mark answers when a "neat sketch" is asked:**

```
