---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the working of an in-tank electric fuel pump. Why is it preferred over mechanical pumps in modern MPI engines?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An in-tank electric fuel pump is a **brushless DC motor-driven centrifugal pump submerged in fuel**. The motor spins an impeller that creates suction at the inlet and pressurizes fuel at the outlet to **3.0–4.5 bar**. The fuel surrounds the motor, providing cooling and silent operation, while always keeping the pump flooded (no cavitation).
>
> It is preferred because: (i) it operates regardless of engine speed, (ii) it eliminates vapor lock, (iii) it is quieter and more reliable, and (iv) its pressure output is **ECU-controllable via PWM** for returnless systems. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three functions of a fuel filter and state the typical micron rating of a modern in-line gasoline filter.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Functions of the fuel filter:
> 1. **Removes particulates** (dirt, rust, scale) to protect the injectors and pump from wear.
> 2. **Maintains fuel cleanliness** to ensure consistent injector spray pattern.
> 3. **Prevents abrasive damage** to high-precision injector needle valves.
>
> Typical micron rating of an in-line gasoline filter: **10–30 µm**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

> **Q A. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the components of a **Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) fuel feed system** of a gasoline engine. Discuss the role of the **fuel pressure regulator** and **fuel rail**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q A. (b)** Derive the equation for the **volumetric flow rate of fuel through a multi-hole injector orifice** using Bernoulli's principle, and explain the physical significance of the **discharge coefficient $C_d$**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q A. (a)

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram** above (printed on answer sheet as a labeled block diagram).

**Step-by-step explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Fuel Tank (1 mark):** Stores 45–70 L of gasoline. Includes a vent valve for atmospheric pressure equalization and a level sender unit for the dashboard gauge.

2. **In-Tank Strainer (½ mark):** Coarse sock filter (~100 µm) at the suction pipe to trap large debris.

3. **Electric Fuel Pump (1 mark):** Submerged brushless DC pump delivering fuel at **3.0–4.5 bar**. Pump speed is PWM-controlled by the ECU.

4. **In-Line Filter (1 mark):** Pleated cellulose/polyester cartridge, **10–30 µm**, removes fine particulates to protect injectors.

5. **Fuel Rail (1½ marks):** A common accumulator tube that holds fuel at uniform pressure for all cylinders. Acts as a **hydraulic capacitor**, dampening pressure pulsations from individual injector openings. Each injector taps off the rail.

6. **Fuel Pressure Regulator (1½ marks):** A **vacuum-referenced diaphragm regulator** maintains a *constant* pressure differential $\Delta P = P_{rail} - P_{manifold}$. When rail pressure exceeds the setpoint, the diaphragm lifts and excess fuel returns to the tank. This ensures injector flow depends only on pulse width and is independent of engine load.

7. **Injectors + ECU (½ mark):** Four solenoid injectors mounted at the intake port spray atomized fuel at 3.5 bar; ECU controls pulse width based on $\lambda$, MAP, RPM, and coolant temperature sensors.

> **Conclusion:** The MPFI system delivers stoichiometric AFR to each cylinder independently, improving fuel economy and reducing emissions.

---

#### Model Solution — Q A. (b)

**Full derivation (as in Section 3.1):**

Starting from Bernoulli's equation between upstream (rail) and downstream (port) points of the injector:

$$
P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2
$$

Apply $v_1 \approx 0$ and let $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_f}}
$$

Introduce discharge coefficient $C_d$ (0.6–0.95) to account for real-world losses:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{inj} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_f}}\,}
$$

**Physical significance of $C_d$ (3 marks):**

- $C_d$ is the **ratio of actual to ideal (theoretical) flow rate** through an orifice.
- It accounts for: **(i) vena contracta** (the flow area at the vena contracta is smaller than the geometric orifice area), **(ii) viscous friction losses** along the orifice walls, and **(iii) turbulent dissipation** at the inlet edges.
- A perfectly sharp-edged orifice has $C_d \approx 0.61$; a well-rounded nozzle achieves $C_d \approx 0.95$.
- In injector design, $C_d$ is **experimentally calibrated** to ensure the ECU's fuel map is accurate.

> **Examiner's Note:** Students often forget that $\Delta P = P_{rail} - P_{manifold}$, **not** $P_{rail}$ alone. The vacuum-referenced regulator exists *precisely* to keep $\Delta P$ constant.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

> **Q B. (a)** Compare the **fuel feed system of a carbureted engine** with that of a **Multi-Point Injection (MPI) system** in terms of components, pressure, and metering principle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q B. (b)** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder gasoline engine has displacement 1.6 L, runs at 2500 RPM, $\eta_{vol} = 0.85$, $\lambda = 1.0$, and uses an injector with $C_d = 0.82$, $A = 3.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m², and $\Delta P = 3.2$ bar. Calculate the **injector pulse width per cycle**. (Take $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\rho_{gasoline} = 740$ kg/m³, $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q B. (a)

| Parameter | Carbureted System | MPFI System |
|:----------|:-------------------|:-------------|
| **Pressurization** | Mechanical pump (~0.4 bar) | Electric in-tank pump (3.0–4.5 bar) |
| **Filtration** | Single in-line filter | Two-stage (sock + in-line) |
| **Metering** | Venturi + calibrated jets | ECU-controlled solenoid injectors |
| **Fuel Delivery** | Continuous drip into venturi | Pulsed spray into intake port |
| **Pressure Regulation** | Needle & seat (float bowl) | Vacuum-referenced regulator |
| **Mixture Distribution** | Unequal (manifold design dependent) | Equal (one injector per port) |
| **Cold Start Aid** | Choke (butterfly) | ECU extra pulse + idle air control |
| **Emission Control** | Limited (EGR only) | Lambda closed-loop + EVAP + TWC |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** MPFI offers superior **fuel economy (~15% better), emissions (50% lower HC/CO), and drivability** due to per-cylinder electronic fuel metering and lambda closed-loop control.

---

#### Model Solution — Q B. (b)

**Step 1: Air volumetric flow rate (4-stroke):**

$$
\dot{V}_{air} = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{2} = 0.85 \cdot 0.0016 \cdot \dfrac{2500}{2 \cdot 60}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{air} = 0.85 \cdot 0.0016 \cdot 20.833 = 0.02833 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2: Air mass flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_a = 0.02833 \cdot 1.2 = 0.034 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 3: Fuel mass flow (stoichiometric):**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{0.034}{14.7} = 2.313 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4: Number of injection events per second:**

$$
n_{inj} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 2500}{2 \cdot 60} = 83.33 \text{ events/s}
$$

**Step 5: Fuel mass per injection event:**

$$
m_{per\,event} = \dfrac{2.313 \times 10^{-3}}{83.33} = 2.776 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 6: Injector volumetric flow rate:**

$$
Q_{inj} = 0.82 \cdot 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 3.2 \times 10^5}{740}}
$$

$$
Q_{inj} = 2.46 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \sqrt{864.86} = 2.46 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 29.41
$$

$$
Q_{inj} = 7.235 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 72.35 \text{ mL/s}
$$

**Step 7: Pulse width:**

$$
t_{inj} = \dfrac{2.776 \times 10^{-5}}{740 \cdot 7.235 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{2.776 \times 10^{-5}}{5.354 \times 10^{-2}} = 5.18 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $t_{inj} \approx \mathbf{0.518 \text{ ms}}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (where students lose 2–3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 4-stroke factor of 2:** Many students write $\dot{V} = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot N$ instead of $\eta_{vol} \cdot V_d \cdot (N/2)$. The 4-stroke engine fires once every 2 revolutions. **−1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $\Delta P$ with $P_{rail}$:** The injector flow equation uses the **differential pressure across the injector**, not the absolute rail pressure. In a vacuum-referenced system, $\Delta P = P_{rail} - P_{manifold}$. **−1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Wrong number of injection events:** For a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke engine at 2500 RPM, total injections/sec = (4 cylinders × 2500 RPM) / (2 rev/cycle × 60 s/min) = **83.33/s**, not 2500/s. **−1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Skipping unit conversions:** $\Delta P = 3.2$ bar must be converted to **Pa** ($3.2 \times 10^5$ Pa) before substituting. **−½ mark.**
>
> 5. **Not stating $C_d$ significance:** In the derivation question, $C_d$ deserves **3 marks** by itself. Many students write the formula but skip its physical meaning. **−3 marks.**

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — "Components of Fuel Feed Systems"**

- [ ] **Fuel Feed System = 6 stages:** Storage → Suction → Filtration → Pressurization → Metering → Distribution.
- [ ] **Fuel Tank** stores fuel; contains a **vent valve** and **level sender**.
- [ ] **Fuel Pump types:** (i) Mechanical diaphragm (carbureted, 0.4 bar), (ii) In-tank electric DC (MPI, 3.5 bar), (iii) High-pressure radial piston (CRDi, 1600+ bar).
- [ ] **In-tank pump** is always **flooded** to prevent cavitation; surrounding fuel **cools** the motor.
- [ ] **Filter micron ratings:** Sock/strainer = **100 µm**, In-line = **10–30 µm**.
- [ ] **Fuel Rail** is a **common accumulator** that ensures equal pressure to all injectors; acts as a hydraulic capacitor.
- [ ] **Pressure Regulator** is **vacuum-referenced** to maintain **constant $\Delta P$** across injectors regardless of manifold vacuum.
- [ ] **Injector flow equation:** $Q_{inj} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_f}$ — **square-root dependence** on pressure.
- [ ] **ECU** controls injectors via **pulse width modulation (PWM)**, typically **1–10 ms** at mid-load.
- [ ] **4-stroke engine:** fires once per **2 revolutions** → total injection events/s = (cyl × RPM) / 120.
- [ ] **AFR (gasoline) = 14.7 : 1** (stoichiometric); **$\lambda = 1$** is the closed-loop target.
- [ ] **EVAP system** captures fuel vapors in a **charcoal canister** to prevent HC release to atmosphere.
- [ ] **Returnless systems** use **PWM pump control** by ECU; return-type systems have a mechanical regulator + return line.
- [ ] **Diesel CRDi** pressures: **1600–2500 bar**; uses **piezo injectors** with response times of ~100 µs.
- [ ] **Filter diagnostics:** high $\Delta P$ across filter = **clogged cartridge** → replace.
- [ ] **Injector diagnostics:** resistance check (solenoid: 2–5 Ω), spray pattern test, leak-down test.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Components of Fuel Feed Systems**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carburetion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Carburetion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Carburetion** is the process of atomizing, vaporizing, and mixing fuel with air in the correct proportion to form a combustible mixture suitable for combustion in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine. The device that performs this operation is called a **carburetor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBMUT404 Module 2):**
> *Carburetion is the process of preparing a homogeneous, combustible air-fuel mixture outside the engine cylinder in a device called a carburetor. The process must deliver a stoichiometric (chemically correct) air-fuel ratio of approximately 15:1 by mass for petrol under ideal conditions, while compensating for varying operating conditions such as cold start, idling, cruising, acceleration, and full load.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making **filter coffee** using a traditional South Indian filter. The upper chamber holds the coffee powder, and the lower chamber collects the decoction. Hot water is poured from the top, and the small holes at the bottom allow the water to slowly seep through the powder, picking up its essence as it travels.

A **carburetor works on a very similar principle**:
- The **air** entering the engine acts like the **hot water**.
- The **fuel** in the float chamber acts like the **coffee powder**.
- The **venturi/throttle** acts like the **filtration point** where the air picks up fuel droplets.

Just like you adjust the water flow and coffee quantity to get a strong, medium, or light decoction depending on the mood of the guests, a carburetor adjusts the air-fuel mixture to match the engine's demand — whether starting in the cold morning, idling at a traffic signal, cruising on the highway, or accelerating uphill.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics for Carburetion:**
> - Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Petrol): **AFR_s ≈ 14.7 : 1** (by mass)
> - Calorific Value of Petrol: **≈ 44 MJ/kg**
> - Density of Air (at 1 atm, 25°C): **ρ_air ≈ 1.184 kg/m³**
> - Density of Petrol: **ρ_f ≈ 680–740 kg/m³**
> - Lower Heating Value (LHV) of gasoline: **≈ 44,000 kJ/kg**

### 1.3 Why is Carburetion Necessary?

For an SI engine, the air-fuel mixture must be:
1. **Homogeneous** — uniformly mixed
2. **Atomized** — fuel in fine droplet form for rapid vaporization
3. **In correct proportion** — neither too rich nor too lean

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Note:** Modern vehicles have largely replaced carburetors with **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI/MPFI)** systems due to stricter emission norms (Bharat Stage VI) and better fuel economy. However, understanding carburetion is essential as it forms the conceptual foundation for all modern fuel delivery systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Essential Elements of a Simple Carburetor

A simple (ideal) carburetor must contain the following components to deliver the correct air-fuel mixture:

**Step 1 — Air Intake & Flow Restriction:**
- Air is drawn into the engine through a pipe containing a **throttle valve** (for quantity control) and a **venturi** (for pressure reduction).

**Step 2 — Fuel Reservoir:**
- A **float chamber** maintains a constant level of fuel just below the discharge nozzle.

**Step 3 — Fuel Discharge:**
- A **fuel discharge nozzle** (also called a jet) opens into the venturi. The pressure difference between the float chamber and the venturi sucks fuel into the air stream.

**Step 4 — Mixing:**
- The venturi converts kinetic energy (high velocity) into low pressure, drawing fuel into the air stream where it atomizes and mixes.

**Why?** The venturi creates a **low-pressure region** based on Bernoulli's principle. Since the float chamber is at atmospheric pressure, fuel is pushed into the venturi by this pressure differential.

### 2.2 Working Principle — The Venturi Effect

A venturi is a converging-diverging duct. As the cross-sectional area reduces, the air velocity increases, and according to **Bernoulli's equation**, the static pressure drops:

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$

> **How it works:** A decrease in static pressure at the throat of the venturi creates a pressure difference (ΔP) between the float chamber (atmospheric) and the throat. This ΔP is what drives the fuel out of the nozzle.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air velocity at venturi throat | $v_a = C_d \sqrt{\frac{2(P_a - P_v)}{\rho_a}}$ | Velocity from pressure drop | m/s |
| Fuel discharge velocity | $v_f = C_{df} \sqrt{\frac{2(P_a - P_v)}{\rho_f}}$ | Velocity from same ΔP | m/s |
| Mass flow rate of air | $\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot A_a \cdot \rho_a \cdot v_a$ | Air mass per second | kg/s |
| Mass flow rate of fuel | $\dot{m}_f = C_{df} \cdot A_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot v_f$ | Fuel mass per second | kg/s |
| Air-Fuel Ratio | $(A/F) = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{C_d \cdot A_a}{C_{df} \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_f}{\rho_a}}$ | The fundamental mixing equation | Dimensionless |
| Simplified AFR | $(A/F) = \frac{C_d \cdot A_a}{C_{df} \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_f}{\rho_a}}$ | Combines geometry & densities | — |
| Fuel-Air Ratio | $(F/A) = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{\dot{m}_a}$ | Inverse of A/F | — |
| Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \frac{\text{Actual mass}}{\text{Ideal mass at NTP}}$ | Measures filling | % |
| Stagnation / Critical Pressure Ratio | $\frac{P_v}{P_a} = 0.528$ (isentropic) | Choked flow limit | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM NOTE:** In the simplified A/F ratio equation, both the air and fuel have the **same pressure differential** (P_a − P_v). This is the key assumption in the ideal simple carburetor. Always verify this assumption before applying the formula.

### 2.4 Air-Fuel Ratio Requirements at Different Operating Conditions

| **Operating Condition** | **Required A/F Ratio** | **Nature of Mixture** |
|---|---|---|
| Cold Starting | 1 : 1 to 3 : 1 (very rich) | Extremely rich |
| Idling | 9 : 1 to 12 : 1 | Rich |
| Cruising / Part Load | 15 : 1 to 17 : 1 | Near stoichiometric |
| Acceleration (open throttle) | 12 : 1 to 13 : 1 | Slightly rich |
| Full Load (WOT) | 12 : 1 to 13 : 1 | Rich (max power) |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The compensation devices (choke, idling jet, power jet, accelerator pump) are designed to deliver these varied A/F ratios. In modern electronic systems, the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** does this precisely using sensor feedback — but the underlying physics remains the same as the carburetor.

### 2.5 Compensation Devices (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Choke Valve** — A butterfly valve at the carburetor inlet. It restricts air during cold starting, enriching the mixture.
2. **Idling System** — Provides a rich mixture at low throttle openings via the idle port.
3. **Main Metering System** — Supplies fuel during normal cruising using the main jet.
4. **Power Enrichment System (Economiser/Power Jet)** — Adds extra fuel at full throttle for high power.
5. **Accelerating Pump (Pumper Jet)** — Injects an extra squirt of fuel during sudden throttle opening to prevent bogging.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Air-Fuel Ratio of a Simple Carburetor

**Given:**
- Air enters the venturi with pressure P_a and density ρ_a.
- At the venturi throat, the pressure drops to P_v.
- Fuel at the discharge nozzle is at the same atmospheric pressure P_a (float chamber is open to atmosphere).

**To Derive:** The fundamental A/F ratio equation in terms of geometry and density.

---

**Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation between the upstream air and the venturi throat.**

$$P_a + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a v_1^2 = P_v + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a v_a^2$$

**Reasoning:** The upstream air velocity v₁ is negligibly small compared to the throat velocity v_a, so the v₁² term can be dropped.

$$\therefore P_a = P_v + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a v_a^2$$

**Step 2: Solve for the throat air velocity v_a.**

Rearranging the equation:

$$\frac{1}{2}\rho_a v_a^2 = P_a - P_v$$

$$v_a^2 = \frac{2(P_a - P_v)}{\rho_a}$$

Taking the square root and introducing the discharge coefficient C_d to account for viscous and turbulence losses:

$$v_a = C_d \sqrt{\frac{2(P_a - P_v)}{\rho_a}}$$

> **Note:** The discharge coefficient C_d typically ranges from **0.85 to 0.95** for well-designed venturis.

**Step 3: Apply the same Bernoulli's principle to the fuel nozzle.**

The fuel in the float chamber is at pressure P_a, and the nozzle exit is at the venturi throat pressure P_v. The same pressure differential drives the fuel.

$$v_f^2 = \frac{2(P_a - P_v)}{\rho_f}$$

Introducing the fuel discharge coefficient C_df:

$$v_f = C_{df} \sqrt{\frac{2(P_a - P_v)}{\rho_f}}$$

**Reasoning:** The pressure differential (P_a − P_v) is the **same** for both air and fuel because the float chamber is vented to atmosphere and the discharge nozzle exits at the venturi throat.

**Step 4: Express the mass flow rates.**

For air:

$$\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot A_a \cdot \rho_a \cdot v_a$$

Substituting the velocity expression:

$$\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot A_a \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \sqrt{\frac{2(P_a - P_v)}{\rho_a}}$$

$$\dot{m}_a = C_d^2 \cdot A_a \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a (P_a - P_v)}$$

For fuel:

$$\dot{m}_f = C_{df}^2 \cdot A_f \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_f (P_a - P_v)}$$

**Step 5: Form the Air-Fuel Ratio.**

$$(A/F) = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f}$$

$$(A/F) = \frac{C_d^2 \cdot A_a \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a (P_a - P_v)}}{C_{df}^2 \cdot A_f \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_f (P_a - P_v)}}$$

The term $\sqrt{2(P_a - P_v)}$ cancels out from numerator and denominator:

$$(A/F) = \frac{C_d^2 \cdot A_a}{C_{df}^2 \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

**Step 6: Simplify the relation.**

Taking the inverse of both sides to see the F/A ratio:

$$(F/A) = \frac{C_{df}^2}{C_d^2} \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_a} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_f}{\rho_a}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** This equation proves that for a **simple, ideal carburetor**, the A/F ratio is **independent of engine speed and throttle position**. The air velocity and fuel velocity both increase proportionally with the pressure drop. This is why compensation devices are **mandatory** — without them, a simple carburetor cannot deliver variable A/F ratios.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Problem-Solving Approach for KTU

**KTU Pattern Problem:** A simple carburetor has the following data. Find the A/F ratio.

- Diameter of air passage at throat: D_a = 25 mm
- Diameter of fuel nozzle: D_f = 1.5 mm
- C_d = 0.85, C_df = 0.70
- ρ_a = 1.184 kg/m³
- ρ_f = 720 kg/m³

**Solution Template (for any such KTU problem):**

**Step A — Compute the cross-sectional areas.**

$$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} D_a^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0015)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step B — Apply the fundamental A/F equation.**

$$(A/F) = \frac{C_d^2 \cdot A_a}{C_{df}^2 \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$(A/F) = \frac{(0.85)^2 \cdot (4.909 \times 10^{-4})}{(0.70)^2 \cdot (1.767 \times 10^{-6})} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.184}{720}}$$

$$(A/F) = \frac{0.7225 \cdot 4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{0.49 \cdot 1.767 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot \sqrt{0.001644}$$

$$(A/F) = \frac{3.547 \times 10^{-4}}{8.658 \times 10^{-7}} \cdot 0.04055$$

$$(A/F) = 409.7 \cdot 0.04055$$

$$(A/F) \approx 16.6 : 1$$

> **Interpretation:** This A/F ratio of 16.6 is close to stoichiometric for petrol (14.7) but slightly lean, which is acceptable for cruising conditions.

---

### 3.3 Effect of Altitude on Carburetor Performance

**Step 1:** Air density ρ_a decreases with altitude.

$$\rho_a = \rho_{a,0} \cdot e^{-\frac{gh}{RT}}$$

where h = altitude, g = gravity, R = gas constant, T = temperature.

**Step 2:** From the A/F equation, since $\sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$ is in the numerator, decreasing ρ_a will **decrease the A/F ratio**.

$$(A/F) \propto \sqrt{\rho_a}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, at higher altitudes, the mixture becomes **richer** (lower A/F) because less air is drawn per stroke but the same fuel quantity is delivered.

**Engineering Solution:** Carburetors are equipped with an **altitude compensator** (or the driver manually adjusts the fuel) to restore the correct A/F ratio. In modern EFI systems, the **MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensor** automatically corrects for this.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Simple Carburetor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> A1[Choke Valve Block]
    A1 --> A2[Venturi Throat Block]
    A2 --> A3[Throttle Valve Block]
    A3 --> A4[Engine Manifold Block]

    F0[Fuel Tank Supply] --> F1[Fuel Strainer]
    F1 --> F2[Float Chamber Reservoir]
    F2 --> F3[Float Valve Mechanism]
    F3 --> F2
    F2 --> F4[Main Fuel Discharge Nozzle]
    F4 --> A2

    A2 -. Low Pressure Suction .-> F4

    A1 --> C1[Compensation: Choke Circuit]
    F4 --> C2[Compensation: Main Jet Circuit]
    A3 --> C3[Compensation: Idling Circuit]
    A3 --> C4[Compensation: Accelerator Pump]
    F4 --> C5[Compensation: Power Jet Economiser]
```

### 4.2 Operational Flow Diagram: Air-Fuel Delivery Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Start: Driver turns key] --> step2[Choke valve partially closed]
    step2 --> step3[Restricted air flow creates vacuum]
    step3 --> step4[Fuel lifted from float chamber]
    step4 --> step5[Rich mixture delivered for cold start]

    step5 --> step6[Engine warms up]
    step6 --> step7[Choke gradually opens]
    step7 --> step8[Venturi effect provides normal suction]
    step8 --> step9[Main jet delivers cruising mixture]

    step9 --> step10{Throttle Position?}
    step10 -->|Idle| step11[Idle port supplies rich mixture]
    step10 -->|Cruise| step12[Main jet only]
    step10 -->|Accelerate| step13[Accelerator pump squirt]
    step10 -->|Full Load| step14[Power jet economiser activates]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of Compensation Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SC[Starting Compensation]
        SC1[Choke Valve]
        SC2[Choke Unloader]
    end

    subgraph IC[Idling Compensation]
        IC1[Idle Port]
        IC2[Idle Adjustment Screw]
    end

    subgraph MC[Main Compensation]
        MC1[Main Metering Jet]
        MC2[Emulsion Tube]
    end

    subgraph PC[Power Compensation]
        PC1[Power Jet Economiser]
        PC2[Mechanical or Vacuum Operated]
    end

    subgraph ACC[Acceleration Compensation]
        ACC1[Accelerator Pump]
        ACC2[Pump Discharge Nozzle]
    end

    SC --> MC
    IC --> MC
    MC --> PC
    MC --> ACC
```

### 4.4 Carburetor Type Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CT[Carburetor Types] --> T1[Updraft]
    CT --> T2[Downdraft]
    CT --> T3[Sidedraft]
    CT --> T4[Constant Choke]
    CT --> T5[Variable Venturi]

    T1 --> D1[Air flows upward. Old design. Rare today.]
    T2 --> D2[Most common. Air flows down. Used in Maruti 800 era vehicles.]
    T3 --> D3[Used in motorcycles and racing. Compact design.]
    T4 --> D4[Fixed venturi, variable jet. Simple and reliable.]
    T5 --> D5[Modern hydraulic carburetor. Variable venturi area.]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define carburetion. List any four essential elements of a simple carburetor.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Carburetion is the process of atomizing, vaporizing, and mixing fuel with air in the correct proportion outside the engine cylinder to form a combustible mixture for a spark ignition engine.

Four essential elements of a simple carburetor are:
1. **Float chamber** — to maintain a constant fuel level
2. **Fuel discharge nozzle** — to deliver fuel into the air stream
3. **Venturi** — to create a low-pressure region for fuel suction
4. **Throttle valve** — to control the quantity of mixture entering the engine

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing the four elements)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
*Explain why a simple carburetor cannot deliver a constant A/F ratio across all operating conditions.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A simple carburetor relies on a single venturi and a single fuel nozzle. From the A/F ratio equation, $(A/F) \propto \sqrt{\rho_a / \rho_f}$, the ratio depends only on geometry and densities — **not on engine speed or throttle position**. This means the A/F ratio remains constant, which is unsuitable for varying conditions:
- At cold start, the engine needs a very rich mixture (A/F ≈ 3:1)
- At idling, a rich mixture (A/F ≈ 10:1)
- At cruising, a stoichiometric mixture (A/F ≈ 15:1)
- At full load, a slightly rich mixture (A/F ≈ 12:1)

Since the simple carburetor cannot deliver these variable mixtures, **compensation devices** (choke, idle jet, main jet, power jet, accelerator pump) are mandatory additions.

*(1 mark for identifying the limitation, 2 marks for the explanation with examples)*

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of a simple carburetor. Explain the role of the venturi in fuel discharge. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A simple carburetor consists of a float chamber, a fuel discharge nozzle, a venturi, and a throttle valve. The float chamber maintains fuel at a constant level just below the nozzle tip. The venturi is a converging-diverging passage in the air intake. The throttle valve controls the air flow into the engine.

**Working Principle:**
- The engine piston draws air through the venturi during the suction stroke.
- At the venturi throat, the cross-section reduces, increasing air velocity and reducing static pressure (Bernoulli's effect).
- The pressure at the throat (P_v) becomes lower than the atmospheric pressure (P_a) in the float chamber.
- This pressure differential (ΔP = P_a − P_v) forces fuel out of the nozzle.
- The high-velocity air atomizes the fuel, forming a combustible mixture.
- This mixture passes through the throttle and enters the engine cylinder.

**Role of the Venturi:**
The venturi is the heart of the carburetor. It converts the pressure energy of the air into kinetic energy. As velocity increases, pressure drops. This pressure drop is the driving force for fuel delivery. Without the venturi, no fuel would be drawn into the air stream. **(1 mark for sketch description, 4 marks for working, 2 marks for venturi role explanation)**

**(b)** Derive the air-fuel ratio equation for a simple carburetor in terms of jet areas, discharge coefficients, and fluid densities. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Assumptions:*
1. The float chamber is vented to atmosphere (fuel at P_a).
2. Steady, incompressible flow.
3. The fuel nozzle discharges at the venturi throat.
4. The pressure differential (P_a − P_v) is the same for both air and fuel.

*Derivation:*

Apply Bernoulli's equation between the upstream air and the venturi throat (ignoring upstream velocity):

$$P_a = P_v + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a v_a^2 \Rightarrow v_a = C_d \sqrt{\frac{2(P_a - P_v)}{\rho_a}}$$

For the fuel, the same pressure differential acts across the nozzle:

$$v_f = C_{df} \sqrt{\frac{2(P_a - P_v)}{\rho_f}}$$

Mass flow rates:

$$\dot{m}_a = C_d^2 \cdot A_a \cdot \sqrt{2\rho_a (P_a - P_v)}$$

$$\dot{m}_f = C_{df}^2 \cdot A_f \cdot \sqrt{2\rho_f (P_a - P_v)}$$

Forming the ratio:

$$(A/F) = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{C_d^2 \cdot A_a}{C_{df}^2 \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

> **Conclusion:** The A/F ratio of a simple carburetor is independent of engine speed and pressure differential — it is a function only of geometry and fluid properties. **(2 marks for assumptions, 4 marks for derivation, 1 mark for conclusion)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Pattern - Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain with diagrams the function of the following compensation devices in a modern carburetor:
   (i) Choke valve
   (ii) Accelerator pump
   (iii) Power jet economiser
   (iv) Idling system **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Choke Valve (2 marks):**
A butterfly valve located at the carburetor air inlet, upstream of the venturi. During cold starting, the choke is closed (partially), restricting air entry. This enriches the mixture (A/F ≈ 3:1 to 5:1) since fuel flow remains largely unchanged while air decreases. As the engine warms, the choke is gradually opened by an automatic mechanism (thermal or vacuum-operated).

**(ii) Accelerator Pump (2 marks):**
A mechanically or vacuum-operated pump that injects an extra squirt of fuel into the venturi when the throttle is opened suddenly. Without it, sudden acceleration would cause a momentary lean mixture (bogging), because the fuel flow cannot increase as rapidly as air flow. The accelerator pump compensates for this lag.

**(iii) Power Jet Economiser (2 marks):**
A supplementary fuel circuit that activates only at wide-open throttle (WOT). It is typically vacuum-operated or mechanically linked to the throttle. It adds extra fuel to enrich the mixture for maximum power output (A/F ≈ 12:1). At part loads, it remains inactive, ensuring economy.

**(iv) Idling System (1 mark):**
Consists of an idle port and idle adjustment screw located just below the throttle valve. At idle, manifold vacuum draws fuel through the idle port. The screw adjusts the air-fuel ratio for smooth low-speed running.

**(b)** A simple carburetor has a throat diameter of 28 mm and a fuel nozzle diameter of 1.8 mm. The discharge coefficients are C_d = 0.82 and C_df = 0.65. The densities of air and fuel are 1.18 kg/m³ and 740 kg/m³ respectively. Calculate:
   (i) The area of the air passage at the throat.
   (ii) The area of the fuel nozzle.
   (iii) The air-fuel ratio of the mixture. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Area of air passage at throat (2 marks):**

$$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} D_a^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.028)^2 = 6.158 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**(ii) Area of fuel nozzle (2 marks):**

$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} D_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0018)^2 = 2.545 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

**(iii) Air-Fuel Ratio (3 marks):**

$$(A/F) = \frac{C_d^2 \cdot A_a}{C_{df}^2 \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

$$(A/F) = \frac{(0.82)^2 \cdot (6.158 \times 10^{-4})}{(0.65)^2 \cdot (2.545 \times 10^{-6})} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.18}{740}}$$

$$(A/F) = \frac{0.6724 \cdot 6.158 \times 10^{-4}}{0.4225 \cdot 2.545 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot \sqrt{0.001595}$$

$$(A/F) = \frac{4.141 \times 10^{-4}}{1.075 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot 0.03994$$

$$(A/F) = 385.2 \cdot 0.03994$$

$$\boxed{(A/F) \approx 15.4 : 1}$$

This is very close to the stoichiometric ratio of 14.7, suitable for normal cruising. The slight lean bias is acceptable and gives good fuel economy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the discharge coefficients (C_d and C_df):** The discharge coefficients are squared in the A/F equation because they appear in both velocity and mass flow terms. Many students write C_d instead of C_d² — this costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing throat diameter with piston/cylinder diameter:** The venturi throat is much smaller than the cylinder. Do not equate them.
> 3. **Mixing up A/F and F/A ratios:** Always read the question carefully. If the question asks for "fuel-air ratio," invert your final answer.
> 4. **Forgetting assumptions in derivations:** Always state the 4 key assumptions (atmospheric float chamber, steady flow, same ΔP, etc.) before deriving. Marks are explicitly allotted for assumptions.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** Densities must be in kg/m³, areas in m² (convert mm to m!). This is a common source of calculation errors.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Carburetion** = Process of preparing a combustible air-fuel mixture outside the engine cylinder.
- **Simple carburetor** elements: Float chamber, venturi, fuel nozzle, throttle valve.
- **Venturi effect** (Bernoulli's principle) is the driving force for fuel suction.
- **Stoichiometric A/F ratio for petrol = 14.7:1** (by mass).
- **Required A/F varies** with operating condition (cold start, idle, cruise, acceleration, WOT).
- **A/F equation:** $(A/F) = \dfrac{C_d^2 \cdot A_a}{C_{df}^2 \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$
- **Key insight:** A/F is **independent of engine speed** in a simple carburetor — hence compensation devices are needed.
- **Compensation devices:** Choke, idling system, main jet, power jet economiser, accelerator pump.
- **Altitude effect:** A/F decreases (mixture becomes richer) as ρ_a decreases.
- **Carburetor types:** Updraft, downdraft, sidedraft, constant choke, variable venturi.
- **Discharge coefficients are squared** in the final A/F equation.
- **Bernoulli's equation** is the foundation — $P + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2 = \text{constant}$.
- **Modern vehicles** use EFI/MPFI, but the underlying physics is rooted in carburetion principles.
- **Density values** to memorize: ρ_air ≈ 1.184 kg/m³, ρ_petrol ≈ 720 kg/m³.
- **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Always include units in final answers and state assumptions clearly in derivations.
- **Pitfall:** Do not confuse throat area (A_a) with cylinder bore area.
- **Practical application:** Solex carburetor (India), SU carburetor (UK), Holley (USA) — all use the same fundamental principles.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Air-Fuel Mixtures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties of Air-Fuel Mixtures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Properties of Air-Fuel Mixtures — Module 2

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Air-Fuel Mixture** is a homogeneous gaseous combination of atmospheric air (oxidizer) and vaporized liquid fuel (hydrocarbon) prepared in the correct proportion to support controlled combustion inside the cylinder of a Spark Ignition (SI) or Compression Ignition (CI) engine. The **Properties of Air-Fuel Mixtures** refer to the thermophysical and thermochemical characteristics — such as composition ratio, density, specific heats, molecular mass, gas constant, calorific value, and combustion behavior — that govern engine power output, fuel economy, emissions, and knocking tendency.

The **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F or AFR)** is defined as the ratio of the mass of air to the mass of fuel in the mixture:

$$A/F = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)ₛ**: The chemically ideal ratio at which all the carbon and hydrogen in the fuel are completely burnt using exactly the available oxygen, leaving no free oxygen and no unburnt fuel. For gasoline: **(A/F)ₛ ≈ 14.7:1**, for diesel: **(A/F)ₛ ≈ 14.5:1**, for methane: **(A/F)ₛ ≈ 17.2:1**.

### Key Mixture Terminology

| Term | Definition | Typical Value (Gasoline) |
|------|------------|--------------------------|
| **Stoichiometric Mixture** | Chemically correct ratio for complete combustion | A/F = 14.7 |
| **Lean Mixture** | Excess air; AFR > stoichiometric | A/F > 14.7 |
| **Rich Mixture** | Deficient air; AFR < stoichiometric | A/F < 14.7 |
| **Equivalence Ratio (Φ)** | (F/A)actual / (F/A)stoich | Φ = 1 (stoich), >1 rich, <1 lean |
| **Relative Air-Fuel Ratio (λ)** | (A/F)actual / (A/F)stoich | λ = 1/Φ |
| **Air-Equivalence Ratio** | Same as λ, the inverse of Φ | λ = 1 stoich, >1 lean |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Recipe Analogy"** — Think of an air-fuel mixture as a recipe for baking a cake. If you put in too much flour and not enough water, the cake turns out dry and burnt on the edges (rich mixture, incomplete combustion, CO, soot). If you put in too much water and not enough flour, the cake is thin, weak, and slow to bake (lean mixture, low power, high NOx). The **stoichiometric ratio** is the perfect recipe — every molecule of fuel finds exactly the oxygen partner it needs, producing a clean, efficient burn. In an engine, we deliberately drift from "perfect" to either side:
> - **Rich** (more fuel): cooler combustion, more power, protects cylinder walls.
> - **Lean** (more air): cleaner exhaust, better fuel economy, but risk of misfire and slow burn.

The molecular weight of atmospheric air is **M_air = 28.97 kg/kmol** and its density at NTP (Normal Temperature and Pressure: 0°C, 101.325 kPa) is **ρ_air = 1.293 kg/m³**. These constants form the foundation of all mixture property calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Air Requirement for Combustion

Consider **1 kg of fuel** with the following ultimate analysis by mass:
- **C** = mass fraction of Carbon
- **H** = mass fraction of Hydrogen
- **O** = mass fraction of Oxygen (already present in fuel)
- **S** = mass fraction of Sulphur (negligible for gasoline/diesel)

### Step-by-Step Combustion Logic

**Step 1 — Carbon burns to CO₂:**
$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$
Each kg of C requires **8/3 kg of O₂** (since 12 kg C reacts with 32 kg O₂).

**Step 2 — Hydrogen burns to H₂O:**
$$\text{2H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{2H}_2\text{O}$$
Each kg of H requires **8 kg of O₂** (since 4 kg H reacts with 32 kg O₂).

**Step 3 — Sulphur burns to SO₂:**
$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2$$
Each kg of S requires **1 kg of O₂** (32/32).

**Step 4 — Subtract the oxygen already bound within the fuel:**
The fuel itself contains O which partially supplies its own oxygen demand. So the **net** oxygen that must come from atmospheric air is reduced by the mass of O in the fuel.

### 2.2 Master Formula: Theoretical Oxygen & Air Required

For 1 kg of fuel, the **minimum (theoretical) mass of O₂** required is:

$$m_{O_2, th} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O \quad [\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg fuel}]$$

Since atmospheric air contains only **23.3% O₂ by mass** (i.e., 1 kg of O₂ comes with 3.32 kg of N₂, so 1 kg O₂ corresponds to **4.32 kg of air**), the **theoretical mass of air required per kg of fuel** is:

$$A_0 = 23 \times \left(\frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O\right) / 100 \times 100/23.3$$

Simplified into the classical KTU formula:

$$A_0 = \frac{100}{23} \left(\frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O\right) \quad [\text{kg air} / \text{kg fuel}]$$

For most liquid hydrocarbon fuels where S ≈ 0 and O is small:

$$A_0 \approx \frac{100}{23}\left(\frac{8}{3}C + 8H - O\right) \approx 11.6\,C + 34.8\,H - 4.35\,O \quad [\text{kg air/kg fuel}]$$

### 2.3 Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio

$$\boxed{(A/F)_s = \frac{A_0}{1} = A_0}$$

For a typical gasoline **C₈H₁₇** (octane) with molecular weight 114 kg/kmol, theoretical calculation gives **(A/F)ₛ ≈ 15.13**, but the empirical industry-standard value used in practice is **14.7:1** to account for incomplete vaporization and minor impurities.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------------------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Air-Fuel Ratio | $A/F = m_{air}/m_{fuel}$ | dimensionless | Mass-based, primary parameter |
| 2 | Fuel-Air Ratio | $F/A = 1/(A/F)$ | dimensionless | Used for SI engine calculations |
| 3 | Theoretical O₂ per kg fuel | $m_{O_2} = (8/3)C + 8H + S - O$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel | Based on ultimate analysis |
| 4 | Theoretical Air Required | $A_0 = (100/23)[(8/3)C + 8H + S - O]$ | kg air / kg fuel | Stoichiometric basis |
| 5 | Stoichiometric A/F (gasoline) | $(A/F)_s \approx 14.7$ | — | Empirical engine value |
| 6 | Stoichiometric A/F (diesel) | $(A/F)_s \approx 14.5$ | — | Empirical engine value |
| 7 | Equivalence Ratio | $\Phi = (F/A)_{actual}/(F/A)_{stoich}$ | — | Φ > 1 → rich; Φ < 1 → lean |
| 8 | Relative Air-Fuel Ratio (λ) | $\lambda = (A/F)_{actual}/(A/F)_{stoich} = 1/\Phi$ | — | λ > 1 → lean; λ < 1 → rich |
| 9 | Actual Air Supplied | $A = \lambda \cdot A_0$ | kg air / kg fuel | For excess air operation |
| 10 | Molecular Weight of Air | $M_{air} = 28.97$ | kg/kmol | Universal constant |
| 11 | Density of Air (NTP) | $\rho_{air} = 1.293$ | kg/m³ | At 0°C, 101.325 kPa |
| 12 | Universal Gas Constant | $R_u = 8.314$ | kJ/(kmol·K) | Applies to all ideal gases |
| 13 | Gas Constant of Air | $R_{air} = R_u/M_{air} = 0.287$ | kJ/(kg·K) | $R_{air} = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K |
| 14 | Gas Constant of Fuel Vapor | $R_f = R_u/M_f$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Depends on fuel M_f |
| 15 | Gas Constant of Mixture | $R_m = (m_a R_a + m_f R_f)/(m_a + m_f)$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Mass-weighted average |
| 16 | Molecular Weight of Mixture | $M_m = (m_a + m_f)/(m_a/M_a + m_f/M_f)$ | kg/kmol | Harmonic mass average |
| 17 | Density of Mixture (ideal gas) | $\rho_m = p/(R_m T)$ | kg/m³ | From ideal gas law |
| 18 | Specific Heat (c_v) Mixture | $c_{v,m} = (m_a c_{v,a} + m_f c_{v,f})/(m_a + m_f)$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Mass-weighted |
| 19 | Specific Heat (c_p) Mixture | $c_{p,m} = (m_a c_{p,a} + m_f c_{p,f})/(m_a + m_f)$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Mass-weighted |
| 20 | Theoretical Combustion Products (per kg fuel) | $CO_2: 11/3 C;\; H_2O: 9H;\; SO_2: 2S;\; N_2: 7.52 A_0$ | kg | By mass balance |
| 21 | Volumetric Efficiency (η_v) | $\eta_v = V_{actual}/V_{swept}$ | dimensionless | Air-charging efficiency |
| 22 | Combustion Efficiency (η_c) | $\eta_c = E_{released}/E_{supplied}$ | dimensionless | Energy conversion |
| 23 | Energy per kg of Charge | $E_{charge} = \eta_c \cdot Q_{LHV}/(A/F + 1)$ | kJ/kg | For 1 kg of mixture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Exams**: Always write A₀ as a function of fuel composition (C, H, O, S). When only the empirical (A/F)ₛ is given, use 14.7 for SI engines and 14.5 for CI engines. The equivalence ratio Φ and relative AFR λ are **inverse** of each other — a common board-exam trap.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emission Control**: Three-way catalytic converters function efficiently only when λ remains within a narrow band of **1.00 ± 0.01** (called the lambda window).
- **Engine Calibration**: Modern Electronic Control Units (ECUs) compute λ in real time from the oxygen sensor (lambda sensor) feedback and adjust injector pulse width accordingly.
- **Combustion Knock Mitigation**: In SI engines, rich mixtures (λ ≈ 0.85–0.9) absorb more heat and suppress detonation, protecting the piston.
- **Diesel Performance**: CI engines always operate lean (λ ≈ 1.2 to 2.5) because fuel is injected directly into hot compressed air, eliminating the need for stoichiometric pre-mixing.
- **Rocket & Gas Turbine Applications**: The same A/F principles govern propellant design, where λ determines exhaust velocity, specific impulse, and combustion stability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gas Constant of an Air-Fuel Mixture

**Given**: An air-fuel mixture consisting of $m_a$ kg of air and $m_f$ kg of fuel vapor at temperature T and pressure p.

**Step 1 — Apply Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**:
The total pressure equals the sum of the partial pressures of air and fuel vapor components:

$$p = p_a + p_f$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Ideal Gas Equation to Each Component**:
For air occupying volume V:

$$p_a V = m_a R_{air} T \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_a = \frac{m_a R_{air} T}{V}$$

For fuel vapor occupying the same volume V:

$$p_f V = m_f R_f T \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_f = \frac{m_f R_f T}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Add Partial Pressures**:

$$p = p_a + p_f = \frac{T}{V}\left(m_a R_{air} + m_f R_f\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the Single-Component Ideal Gas Law**:
The total mixture obeys:

$$p V = m_m R_m T$$

where $m_m = m_a + m_f$ is the total mass of the mixture and $R_m$ is the gas constant of the mixture.

**Step 5 — Equate and Solve for R_m**:

$$m_m R_m T = T\left(m_a R_{air} + m_f R_f\right)$$

Dividing both sides by $m_m T$:

$$\boxed{R_m = \frac{m_a R_{air} + m_f R_f}{m_a + m_f}}$$

This shows that **R_m is a mass-weighted average** of the individual gas constants.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Molecular Weight of an Air-Fuel Mixture

**Step 1 — Use Avogadro's Law**: For an ideal gas, the number of kilomoles is $n = m/M$.

**Step 2 — Total Number of Kilomoles in Mixture**:

$$n_m = n_a + n_f = \frac{m_a}{M_{air}} + \frac{m_f}{M_f}$$

**Step 3 — Total Mass of Mixture**: $m_m = m_a + m_f$.

**Step 4 — Molecular Weight of Mixture**:

$$\boxed{M_m = \frac{m_m}{n_m} = \frac{m_a + m_f}{\dfrac{m_a}{M_{air}} + \dfrac{m_f}{M_f}}}$$

This is a **harmonic-type average** because it weighs by moles, not by mass.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern): Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio from Ultimate Analysis

> **Problem**: A liquid hydrocarbon fuel has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 0.84, H = 0.14, O = 0.01, S = 0.01. The air contains 23.2% O₂ and 76.8% N₂ by mass. Calculate the **(a)** theoretical O₂ required per kg of fuel, **(b)** theoretical air required per kg of fuel, and **(c)** stoichiometric air-fuel ratio.

**Part (a) — Theoretical O₂ Required**:

Using the master equation:

$$m_{O_2, th} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$$

Substitute values (C = 0.84, H = 0.14, S = 0.01, O = 0.01):

$$m_{O_2, th} = \frac{8}{3}(0.84) + 8(0.14) + (0.01) - (0.01)$$

Compute each term:

$$= \frac{8 \times 0.84}{3} + 1.12 + 0.01 - 0.01 = 2.24 + 1.12 + 0.00 = 3.36 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Part (b) — Theoretical Air Required**:

Since air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass:

$$A_0 = \frac{m_{O_2, th}}{0.232} = \frac{3.36}{0.232}$$

$$A_0 = 14.483 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Part (c) — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio**:

$$(A/F)_s = \frac{A_0}{1} = 14.48$$

The mixture is slightly leaner than standard gasoline because of the higher H fraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Board Mark Loss**: Students often forget the **N₂ carry** in air. Atmospheric air is not pure oxygen — every kg of O₂ comes with (76.8/23.2) = 3.31 kg of N₂, meaning 1 kg O₂ is supplied by 4.31 kg of air. Use 23.2% or 23% consistently.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Mixture Gas Constant & Molecular Weight

> **Problem**: A 4-stroke SI engine running at WOT draws in an air-fuel mixture with A/F = 14. The fuel has molecular weight **M_f = 110 kg/kmol** and gas constant **R_f = 0.0756 kJ/(kg·K)**. The air has M_a = 28.97 kg/kmol, R_a = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K). Calculate **(a)** the molecular weight of the mixture, **(b)** the gas constant of the mixture, and **(c)** the density of the mixture at 350 K and 1 bar.

**Part (a) — Molecular Weight of Mixture**:

Take 1 kg of fuel and 14 kg of air. So $m_a = 14$, $m_f = 1$, $m_m = 15$ kg.

$$M_m = \frac{m_a + m_f}{\dfrac{m_a}{M_a} + \dfrac{m_f}{M_f}} = \frac{14 + 1}{\dfrac{14}{28.97} + \dfrac{1}{110}}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\frac{14}{28.97} = 0.4833, \quad \frac{1}{110} = 0.00909$$

$$\text{Sum} = 0.4924$$

$$M_m = \frac{15}{0.4924} = 30.46 \;\text{kg/kmol}$$

**Part (b) — Gas Constant of Mixture**:

$$R_m = \frac{m_a R_a + m_f R_f}{m_a + m_f} = \frac{14 \times 0.287 + 1 \times 0.0756}{15}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$14 \times 0.287 = 4.018, \quad 1 \times 0.0756 = 0.0756$$

$$\text{Sum} = 4.094 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

$$R_m = \frac{4.094}{15} = 0.2729 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Part (c) — Density of Mixture**:

Convert pressure: 1 bar = 100 kPa.

Using $p = \rho_m R_m T$:

$$\rho_m = \frac{p}{R_m T} = \frac{100}{0.2729 \times 350}$$

$$\rho_m = \frac{100}{95.52} = 1.047 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

This is **lower** than pure air (1.293 kg/m³ at NTP) because the fuel vapor is heavier but contributes a small mass fraction while significantly increasing the molar volume.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Check with R and M**: For an ideal gas, $R = R_u/M$, so $R_m$ should equal $8.314/30.46 = 0.2729$ ✓ — This is an excellent self-consistency check during board exams.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Composition & Properties of an Air-Fuel Mixture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ATMOSPHERIC AIR 21% O2 79% N2 by volume]:::in --> A1[M_a 28.97 kg/kmol]
    A --> A2[R_a 0.287 kJ kg K]
    A --> A3[rho_a 1.293 kg m3 at NTP]

    F[LIQUID FUEL HYDROCARBON]:::in --> F1[Ultimate Analysis C H O S]
    F --> F2[M_f from chemical formula]
    F --> F3[R_f R_u divided by M_f]

    A1 --> MIX
    A2 --> MIX
    A3 --> MIX
    F1 --> MIX
    F2 --> MIX
    F3 --> MIX

    MIX[AIR FUEL MIXTURE m_a kg air plus m_f kg fuel]:::mix
    MIX --> M1[Mixture Molecular Weight M_m mass harmonic mean]
    MIX --> M2[Mixture Gas Constant R_m mass weighted average]
    MIX --> M3[Mixture Density rho_m from p R T]
    MIX --> M4[Mixture Specific Heat c_p_m and c_v_m]
    MIX --> M5[Stoichiometric A F ratio 14.7 SI 14.5 CI]
    MIX --> M6[Equivalence Ratio phi F A actual by F A stoich]
    MIX --> M7[Relative A F lambda A F actual by A F stoich 1 by phi]

    M5 --> E1[LEAN MIXTURE lambda greater than 1]
    M5 --> E2[STOICHIOMETRIC MIXTURE lambda equals 1]
    M5 --> E3[RICH MIXTURE lambda less than 1]

    E1 --> O1[High NOx Clean Exhaust Low Power]
    E2 --> O2[Ideal Combustion Best Catalytic Efficiency]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Combustion Process & Mixture Quality Effects

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Engine Operating Point]:::start --> A1[Throttle Position and RPM]
    A1 --> A2[Manifold Pressure and Air Density]
    A2 --> A3[Mass of Air Inducted m_a]
    A3 --> A4[ECU Computes Required Fuel m_f]
    A4 --> A5[Injector Pulse Width]

    A5 --> A6[AFR Sensor Measures Lambda]
    A6 --> A7{Feedback Loop}
    A7 --> A4
    A7 -->|IF lambda>1 LEAN| R1[Reduce Fuel Increase NOx]
    A7 -->|IF lambda<1 RICH| R2[Add Fuel Increase CO and HC]
    A7 -->|IF lambda=1 STOIC| R3[Optimal Catalytic Conversion]

    A6 --> A8[Combustion Chamber]
```

## 4.3 Mixture Classification Table

| Mixture Type | λ Range | Φ Range | AFR (Gasoline) | Combustion Behavior | Use Case |
|--------------|---------|---------|----------------|---------------------|----------|
| Extremely Rich | < 0.6 | > 1.67 | < 8.8 | Smoky, raw fuel, misfire | Cold start, high load nitromethane racing |
| Rich | 0.6 – 0.85 | 1.18 – 1.67 | 8.8 – 12.5 | Power zone, anti-knock | WOT operation, race tuning |
| Slightly Rich | 0.85 – 1.0 | 1.0 – 1.18 | 12.5 – 14.7 | Best power, slight fuel waste | Performance driving |
| **Stoichiometric** | **1.0** | **1.0** | **14.7** | **Complete combustion** | **Catalyst operation** |
| Slightly Lean | 1.0 – 1.10 | 0.91 – 1.0 | 14.7 – 16.2 | Best economy, hot exhaust | Highway cruise, eco-mode |
| Lean | 1.10 – 1.30 | 0.77 – 0.91 | 16.2 – 19.1 | Lower power, higher NOx | Diesel part-load, HCCI |
| Extremely Lean | > 1.30 | < 0.77 | > 19.1 | Misfire, unstable burn | Lean-burn GDI research |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART — A (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the following terms: (a) Air-Fuel Ratio, (b) Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio, (c) Equivalence Ratio.

**Model Answer**:

**(a) Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)**: It is the ratio of the mass of air to the mass of fuel in the combustible mixture supplied to the engine cylinder. Mathematically, **AFR = m_air / m_fuel**. It is a dimensionless number; for gasoline engines it is typically 14.7:1 under stoichiometric conditions.

**(b) Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio**: It is the chemically correct ratio at which all the carbon (C) and hydrogen (H) in the fuel are completely burnt using exactly the available oxygen, leaving no free O₂ and no unburnt fuel. For gasoline **(AFR)ₛ = 14.7:1**, for diesel **(AFR)ₛ = 14.5:1**.

**(c) Equivalence Ratio (Φ)**: It is the ratio of the actual fuel-air ratio to the stoichiometric fuel-air ratio, **Φ = (F/A)actual / (F/A)stoich**. When Φ = 1, the mixture is stoichiometric; Φ > 1 indicates a rich mixture, and Φ < 1 indicates a lean mixture.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the significance of the relative air-fuel ratio (λ) in modern engine management systems? State the typical lambda window for three-way catalytic converter operation.

**Model Answer**:

The **relative air-fuel ratio (λ)** is defined as the ratio of the actual AFR to the stoichiometric AFR, **λ = (A/F)actual / (A/F)stoich**. It is the inverse of the equivalence ratio (λ = 1/Φ). Its significance includes:

1. **Real-Time Engine Control**: λ is measured continuously by the **oxygen (lambda) sensor** in the exhaust, providing feedback to the ECU for closed-loop fuel injection control.
2. **Emission Compliance**: The **three-way catalytic converter** converts CO, HC, and NOx simultaneously into CO₂, H₂O, and N₂ only when λ is held within a narrow window of **1.00 ± 0.01** (i.e., 0.99 ≤ λ ≤ 1.01).
3. **Performance Optimization**: Depending on the operating mode (idling, cruise, WOT), the ECU targets different λ values to balance power, economy, and emissions.

---

## PART — B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Between Question A and Question B

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** A gasoline substitute fuel has the following ultimate analysis by mass: **C = 0.86, H = 0.13, O = 0.005, S = 0.005, Ash = 0.005**. Air contains 23.2% O₂ and 76.8% N₂ by mass. The fuel is supplied at an actual air-fuel ratio of 18:1 in a 4-stroke SI engine.

**(a)** Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel and the percentage of excess air supplied.

**(b)** Determine the molecular weight, gas constant, and density of the air-fuel mixture at the start of the compression stroke, given the conditions in the cylinder are **T = 350 K, p = 1 bar**. The fuel vapor has **M_f = 100 kg/kmol**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Stoichiometric Air and % Excess Air** [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Theoretical O₂ per kg fuel**:

$$m_{O_2,th} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O = \frac{8}{3}(0.86) + 8(0.13) + 0.005 - 0.005$$

Compute each term:

$$= \frac{6.88}{3} + 1.04 + 0.005 - 0.005 = 2.293 + 1.04 = 3.333 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical (Stoichiometric) Air**:

$$A_0 = \frac{m_{O_2,th}}{0.232} = \frac{3.333}{0.232} = 14.366 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Air Supplied** (AFR = 18:1):

$$A_{actual} = 18 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Excess Air Supplied**:

$$A_{excess} = A_{actual} - A_0 = 18 - 14.366 = 3.634 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage of Excess Air**:

$$\%\,\text{Excess Air} = \frac{A_{actual} - A_0}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{3.634}{14.366} \times 100 = 25.30\%$$

This corresponds to a **relative AFR λ = 18/14.366 = 1.253**, i.e., a **lean** mixture.

---

**Part (b) — Mixture Molecular Weight, Gas Constant, Density** [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Take 1 kg of fuel; mass of air is 18 kg; total mixture mass = 19 kg**.

**Step 2 — Molecular Weight of Mixture**:
Given M_a = 28.97 kg/kmol, M_f = 100 kg/kmol.

$$M_m = \frac{m_a + m_f}{\dfrac{m_a}{M_a} + \dfrac{m_f}{M_f}} = \frac{18 + 1}{\dfrac{18}{28.97} + \dfrac{1}{100}}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\frac{18}{28.97} = 0.6213, \quad \frac{1}{100} = 0.0100, \quad \text{Sum} = 0.6313$$

$$M_m = \frac{19}{0.6313} = 30.10 \;\text{kg/kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Gas Constant of Mixture**:
R_a = 0.287 kJ/(kg·K), R_f = R_u/M_f = 8.314/100 = 0.08314 kJ/(kg·K).

$$R_m = \frac{m_a R_a + m_f R_f}{m_a + m_f} = \frac{18 \times 0.287 + 1 \times 0.08314}{19}$$

Compute numerator:

$$18 \times 0.287 = 5.166, \quad 1 \times 0.08314 = 0.08314, \quad \text{Sum} = 5.249$$

$$R_m = \frac{5.249}{19} = 0.2763 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Cross-check**: $R_u/M_m = 8.314/30.10 = 0.2762$ ✓

**Step 4 — Density of Mixture at 350 K, 1 bar**:
Convert pressure: 1 bar = 100 kPa.

$$\rho_m = \frac{p}{R_m T} = \frac{100}{0.2763 \times 350} = \frac{100}{96.71} = 1.034 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

The mixture density is lower than that of pure air (1.293 kg/m³) due to thermal expansion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]** A 4-stroke SI engine is supplied with a stoichiometric air-fuel mixture of **AFR = 14.7:1**. The fuel used has a calorific value (Lower Heating Value) of **44,000 kJ/kg**. The molecular weight of air is **28.97 kg/kmol** and of fuel vapor is **100 kg/kmol**.

**(a)** Compute the **relative air-fuel ratio (λ)**, **equivalence ratio (Φ)**, and the **mass of combustion products formed per kg of fuel** (assume complete combustion with CₐH_b fuel where C = 0.86, H = 0.14).

**(b)** Compute the **molecular weight**, **gas constant**, and **density** of the **combustion products** at 1000 K and 1 bar, given the molar composition of products as: CO₂ = 14.5%, H₂O = 12.5%, N₂ = 73.0%.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Ratios and Combustion Product Masses** [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Relative AFR**:

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} = \frac{14.7}{14.7} = 1.00$$

**Step 2 — Equivalence Ratio**:

$$\Phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{1.00} = 1.00$$

The mixture is **stoichiometric** — chemically correct.

**Step 3 — Theoretical O₂ per kg fuel**:

$$m_{O_2,th} = \frac{8}{3}(0.86) + 8(0.14) = 2.293 + 1.120 = 3.413 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of N₂ supplied with air**:

For A/F = 14.7, mass of air = 14.7 kg/kg fuel, of which O₂ = 3.413 kg and N₂ = 14.7 - 3.413 = 11.287 kg.

**Step 5 — Mass of CO₂ formed**:
C → CO₂: 12 kg C gives 44 kg CO₂, so 1 kg C gives 44/12 = 3.667 kg CO₂.

$$m_{CO_2} = 0.86 \times \frac{44}{12} = 0.86 \times 3.667 = 3.154 \;\text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 6 — Mass of H₂O formed**:
2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O: 4 kg H gives 36 kg H₂O, so 1 kg H gives 9 kg H₂O.

$$m_{H_2O} = 0.14 \times 9 = 1.260 \;\text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 7 — Mass of N₂ (inert)**:

$$m_{N_2} = 11.287 \;\text{kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 8 — Total mass of products**:

$$m_{products} = 3.154 + 1.260 + 11.287 = 15.701 \;\text{kg/kg fuel}$$

(Mass is conserved: 1 kg fuel + 14.7 kg air = 15.7 kg products ✓)

---

**Part (b) — Molecular Weight, Gas Constant, Density of Products** [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Molecular Weights of Components**:
- M_CO₂ = 44 kg/kmol
- M_H₂O = 18 kg/kmol
- M_N₂ = 28 kg/kmol

**Step 2 — Mole Fractions in Products** (using given volume/mole percentages since for ideal gas, mole fraction = volume fraction):
- x_CO₂ = 0.145
- x_H₂O = 0.125
- x_N₂ = 0.730

**Step 3 — Molecular Weight of Product Mixture** (mole-weighted average):

$$M_{prod} = \sum x_i M_i = 0.145 \times 44 + 0.125 \times 18 + 0.730 \times 28$$

Compute each term:

$$= 6.380 + 2.250 + 20.440 = 29.070 \;\text{kg/kmol}$$

**Step 4 — Gas Constant of Product Mixture**:

$$R_{prod} = \frac{R_u}{M_{prod}} = \frac{8.314}{29.070} = 0.2860 \;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 5 — Density at 1000 K, 1 bar (100 kPa)**:

$$\rho_{prod} = \frac{p}{R_{prod} T} = \frac{100}{0.2860 \times 1000} = \frac{100}{286.0} = 0.3497 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

This low density at 1000 K confirms the high-temperature gas expansion typical of combustion products leaving the cylinder.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**: 
> 1. **Most common mistake in Part A derivations** — Forgetting to include the **N₂ carry** in air, which inflates the O₂ mass by 4×. Students often confuse 1 kg of O₂ with 1 kg of air. Always divide by 0.232 (or 0.23) to convert O₂ mass to air mass.
> 2. **Equivalence ratio vs. Relative AFR inversion** — A 2-mark trap. Φ = 1/λ. If a question states λ = 1.2, you MUST report Φ = 0.833, not 1.2. Mismatched units cost full marks.
> 3. **Molecular weight averaging confusion** — The mixture **molecular weight** uses **mole fractions** (harmonic-like mass mean), while the **specific heat** and **gas constant** use **mass fractions** (direct mass-weighted average). Mixing these up in a 7-mark problem typically results in a 3-mark penalty.
> 4. **Neglecting ash and sulphur** — For gasoline/diesel where the problem states S and ash are present but very small, students sometimes drop them; KTU key typically expects their inclusion. Always include all terms given.
> 5. **Unit conversions in density** — Pressure in kPa, R in kJ/(kg·K), T in K directly give ρ in kg/m³. Mixing bar and Pa loses 1 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Stoichiometric A/F**: Gasoline **14.7:1**; Diesel **14.5:1**; Methane **17.2:1**; Hydrogen **34.3:1** (memorize the first two).
- **Oxygen calculation formula**: $m_{O_2,th} = (8/3)C + 8H + S - O$ kg O₂ per kg fuel — derive from molecular stoichiometry.
- **Air calculation**: $A_0 = m_{O_2,th} / 0.232$ (since air is 23.2% O₂ by mass).
- **Equivalence Ratio**: $\Phi = (F/A)_{actual}/(F/A)_{stoich}$ → Φ > 1 = rich, Φ < 1 = lean.
- **Relative AFR**: $\lambda = (A/F)_{actual}/(A/F)_{stoich} = 1/\Phi$ → λ > 1 = lean, λ < 1 = rich.
- **Mixture Molecular Weight**: harmonic mean of component molecular weights, weighted by **mole** fractions.
- **Mixture Gas Constant**: $R_m = (m_a R_a + m_f R_f)/(m_a + m_f)$ — mass-weighted average.
- **Mixture Density**: $\rho_m = p / (R_m T)$ — derived from the ideal gas law.
- **Lambda window for 3-way catalyst**: $\lambda = 1.00 \pm 0.01$ — narrowest tolerance in engine calibration.
- **SI engines** typically run near stoichiometric (catalyst operation); **CI engines** always run **lean** with $\lambda = 1.2$ to $2.5$.
- **Conservation of mass**: 1 kg fuel + A kg air = (1 + A) kg products, useful as a self-check on stoichiometric calculations.
- **Universal gas constant**: $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) = 8314 J/(kmol·K); **air** has $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K).
- **NTP reference**: $T = 273.15$ K, $p = 101.325$ kPa, $\rho_{air} = 1.293$ kg/m³ — used as a baseline for all engine intake calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mixture Requirements for Steady State and Transient Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mixture Requirements for Steady State and Transient Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Mixture Requirement" in an Automotive Engine?

In an internal combustion (IC) engine, the **air–fuel (A/F) mixture** is the heart of every combustion event. The phrase *Mixture Requirements* refers to the **exact proportion of air to fuel** the engine demands at any given instant to deliver:

- Maximum efficiency
- Lowest exhaust emissions
- Smoothest driveability
- Adequate power output

This requirement is **NOT constant**. It continuously changes as the driver presses the accelerator, releases it, starts the engine cold, climbs a hill, or idles at a signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Mixture Requirement is the volumetric or mass-based ratio of air to fuel (A/F ratio) demanded by a spark-ignition (SI) or compression-ignition (CI) engine to achieve desired torque, fuel economy, and emission targets under a given combination of engine speed, load, coolant temperature, and ambient conditions.*

The A/F ratio is non-dimensionalized into **Equivalence Ratio (Φ)** or **Lambda (λ)**:

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoichiometric}}$$

| λ Value | Mixture State | Engine Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| λ = 1.00 | Stoichiometric (chemically correct) | Best for 3-way catalytic converter |
| λ < 1.00 | **Rich** (excess fuel) | More power, cooler exhaust |
| λ > 1.00 | **Lean** (excess air) | Better economy, higher combustion temp |

For **gasoline (SI engines)**, the stoichiometric mass ratio is **14.7 : 1**.
For **diesel (CI engines)**, since combustion always uses excess air, λ ranges between **1.3 and 2.0+**.

---

## 1.2 Steady-State vs. Transient Operation — Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine driving a Maruti Swift on National Highway 66 from Kochi to Kasaragod.**
> - For 90% of the trip, you cruise at 80 km/h on a flat road. Your foot is steady on the accelerator, the engine RPM is constant, the coolant is hot, and the fuel flow is **steady-state**.
> - Suddenly, you overtake a truck. You press the accelerator. RPM shoots up, manifold pressure changes, fuel flow surges, coolant is still hot. This 2–3 second event is a **transient**.
> - Next morning, you cold-start the car. The engine shivers, RPM is high (fast-idle), fuel is heavily enriched. This is also a **transient**.

**Steady-State Operation:**
A condition where engine speed, load, and temperature are held **constant for several seconds or more**. The A/F ratio demanded is predictable, repeatable, and lies on the engine's **calibration map**.

**Transient Operation:**
A condition where speed, load, throttle position, or temperature is **changing rapidly (within milliseconds to a few seconds)**. The A/F ratio must be *actively corrected* by the fuel system, otherwise the engine stumbles, stalls, smokes, or over-emits.

> [!NOTE]
> A modern engine spends about **70–80% of its life in steady state** and **20–30% in transient conditions**, yet **60% of all emissions are generated during transients**. This is why transient mixture control is the most researched area in engine management.

---

## 1.3 Why A/F Ratio Varies — The Three Big Reasons

1. **Volumetric Efficiency (η_vol) changes** with RPM and manifold pressure → more or less air enters the cylinder → fuel must be matched.
2. **Combustion thermodynamics** demand different mixture strengths for *power* (rich), *economy* (lean), and *cold start* (very rich).
3. **Aftertreatment devices** (3-way catalytic converter, DPF, GPF) only work efficiently at a specific λ window.

---

## 1.4 Quick Reference of Standard Constants

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric A/F (gasoline) | **14.7 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Stoichiometric A/F (diesel) | **14.5 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Stoichiometric A/F (methanol) | **6.4 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Stoichiometric A/F (ethanol) | **9.0 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Stoichiometric A/F (CNG) | **17.2 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Stoichiometric A/F (LPG) | **15.5 : 1** | kg air / kg fuel |
| Lower Heating Value of gasoline | **44,000** | kJ/kg |
| Calorific value of air (at stoichiometric) | **3,050** | kJ/kg air |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Steady-State Mixture Requirements of a Spark-Ignition (SI) Engine

The classic **Henderson–Penzien / Heywood A/F map** for a naturally aspirated SI engine is plotted against **Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)** on the X-axis and **Engine Speed (N)** on the Y-axis. The lambda contours form a "bull's-eye" pattern with the richest region at **Wide-Open Throttle (WOT)** and the leanest region at **light cruise**.

### Region-Wise Breakdown

**Region 1 — Idle (λ ≈ 0.85 to 0.95)**
- Throttle is nearly closed, manifold vacuum is high (> 50 kPa below atmospheric).
- Residual exhaust gases (EGR) make up 15–20% of in-cylinder charge, diluting fresh charge.
- To maintain stable combustion, mixture is kept **slightly rich**.
- Without enrichment, the engine would misfire and stall due to slow flame propagation.

**Region 2 — Light Cruise / Part Load (λ ≈ 1.00 to 1.20)**
- Driver wants fuel economy.
- Throttle is partially open, manifold vacuum moderate.
- Mixture is biased **lean of stoichiometric**.
- Modern closed-loop systems (with oxygen sensor / lambda sensor) hold λ = 1.00 ± 0.02 to maximize 3-way catalyst efficiency.

**Region 3 — Wide-Open Throttle / Full Load (λ ≈ 0.85 to 0.90)**
- Maximum power demanded (e.g., overtaking, hill climbing).
- A **rich mixture** is required for three reasons:
   1. **Power enrichment:** Rich mixtures have higher flame speeds (up to λ = 0.88).
   2. **Exhaust cooling:** Excess fuel absorbs more heat during vaporization, preventing exhaust valve and piston crown overheating.
   3. **Knock suppression:** Rich mixtures have lower peak temperatures, reducing end-gas auto-ignition.

**Region 4 — High-Speed Light Load (λ ≈ 1.05 to 1.15)**
- Lean for economy; modern direct-injection engines can push to λ = 1.5 in stratified mode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Pattern:** When asked to "explain the steady-state A/F map," students MUST draw or describe the 3D lambda surface with four operating zones. Marks are awarded for identifying the **rich idle, lean cruise, rich WOT, lean high-speed** regions explicitly.

---

## 2.2 Steady-State Mixture Requirements of a Compression-Ignition (CI / Diesel) Engine

Unlike SI engines, diesel engines always operate with **excess air** because:
- Fuel is injected into hot compressed air; combustion is diffusion-controlled, not flame-front-controlled.
- The mixture is prepared inside the cylinder during the injection event.

The diesel A/F ratio (overall) ranges from **18 : 1 (full load)** to **70 : 1 (idle / no load)**.

| Operating Mode | A/F Ratio | λ Range | Comments |
|---|---|---|---|
| Idle / No load | 60 – 70 : 1 | 4.1 – 4.8 | Excess air extremely high; some unburned HC + white smoke |
| Part load | 30 – 50 : 1 | 2.1 – 3.4 | Quality governing region |
| Full load | 18 – 22 : 1 | 1.24 – 1.52 | Smoke limit; max BMEP |
| Smoke-limited WOT | ~ 15 : 1* | ~ 1.0* | *Theoretical minimum before visible smoke |

> [!NOTE]
> **Smoke Limit:** The minimum λ below which the diesel engine emits visible black smoke due to fuel-rich zones in the diffusion flame. This is the **physical hard limit** of diesel mixture requirement at full load.

Diesel engines use **Quality Governing** (vary fuel quantity per cycle) rather than **Quantity Governing** (vary air quantity per cycle as in SI engines using throttle).

---

## 2.3 Transient Mixture Requirements

Transient operations demand **active fuel compensation** because the engine's mechanical and thermodynamic response is not instantaneous. Below is the exhaustive list of major transient events.

### 2.3.1 Cold Start

- Fuel condenses on cold intake port walls and cylinder walls (wall-wetting effect).
- Only **30–40% of injected fuel** actually enters the combustion chamber.
- ECU commands a **massively rich mixture (λ ≈ 0.4 to 0.6)** until coolant temperature exceeds 40 °C.
- Enrichment ratio can reach **2× to 5× the stoichiometric fuel mass**.

### 2.3.2 Warm-Up

- After first fire, enrichment is gradually tapered.
- Choke (older carburetor engines) or idle air control valve + cold-start injector (modern systems) regulates the air-fuel balance.
- Fast-idle RPM is maintained 200–400 RPM above normal idle to overcome high friction at low temperature.

### 2.3.3 Acceleration (Tip-In / Throttle Opening)

- Sudden throttle opening causes a brief **fuel deficit** because:
  - Manifold pressure rises rapidly.
  - Fuel film on port walls absorbs extra fuel (wall-wetting lag).
- Solutions:
  - **Accelerator pump** in carbureted engines squirts an extra fuel shot.
  - **Acceleration enrichment pulse** in EFI systems adds extra injection time for 0.5–2 seconds.
  - **Direct Injection** drastically reduces this lag because fuel is injected directly into the cylinder.

### 2.3.4 Deceleration (Tip-Out / Throttle Closing)

- Throttle snaps shut; manifold vacuum spikes.
- Residual fuel film on port walls vaporizes and creates a brief **over-rich condition**.
- ECU cuts fuel entirely (**fuel cut-off / deceleration fuel shut-off**) when RPM > 1500 and TPS < 1%, restoring fuel when RPM drops to ~ 1100–1200 (overrun cutoff threshold).

### 2.3.5 Hot Restart / Hot Soak

- After a hot run, fuel in the lines vaporizes (vapor lock).
- On restart, air is drawn in but liquid fuel is scarce, causing **over-lean condition and stall**.
- Modern systems use a **purge valve** and **vapor canister (charcoal canister)** to trap and recycle fuel vapors back into the intake.

### 2.3.6 Altitude Compensation

- Air density drops ~ 12% per 1000 m of altitude.
- A naturally aspirated engine receives less mass of air at the same throttle opening.
- Carburetors deliver **too much fuel** at altitude (no compensation).
- EFI systems use a **barometric pressure (MAP) sensor** to scale the base fuel pulse width proportionally.

### 2.3.7 Hot Day / Cold Day / Rain Compensation

- ECU uses **Intake Air Temperature (IAT)** sensor and **Coolant Temperature (CTS)** sensor to trim fuel delivery.
- Hot air is less dense → less fuel.
- Cold air is denser → more fuel.

---

## 2.4 Fuel Compensation Methods — The Engineering Toolbox

| Compensation Method | System Type | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Choke plate | Carburetor | Restricts air, enriches mixture during cold start |
| Accelerator pump | Carburetor | Mechanical fuel squirt during tip-in |
| Power valve | Carburetor | Adds extra fuel at WOT |
| Idle air control valve (IACV) | EFI | Controls idle speed and cold fast-idle |
| Coolant temp sensor trim | EFI | Enriches as coolant is cold |
| Intake air temp sensor trim | EFI | Compensates for air density changes |
| MAP sensor scaling | EFI | Adjusts for altitude and load changes |
| Decel fuel cut-off | EFI | Stops fuel during overrun |
| Wall-wetting model (Bonitz / Aquino) | EFI | Predicts fuel film dynamics for compensation |
| Barometric pressure sensor | EFI | Altitude compensation |
| Wide-band O2 sensor feedback | EFI | Closed-loop control keeps λ ≈ 1.00 |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoichiometric}}$ | Lambda (equivalence ratio normalized) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Phi = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}}$ | Equivalence ratio (inverse of lambda) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{(A/F)_{target}}$ | Base fuel mass flow rate calculation | kg/s |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2}$ | Air mass flow into SI engine (4-stroke) | kg/s |
| 5 | $T_{inj} = K \cdot \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\dot{m}_{inj,ref}} \cdot f(T_{cool},T_{air},P_{man})$ | Injection pulse width (simplified) | ms |
| 6 | $E_{enrichment} = 1 + k_1 e^{-t/\tau_1} + k_2 e^{-t/\tau_2}$ | Transient enrichment (dual exponential) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{vol} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air,actual}}{\rho_{air} \cdot V_d \cdot N/2}$ | Volumetric efficiency definition | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\rho_{air} = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$ | Ideal gas law for air density | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_{fuel,rich} = \dot{m}_{fuel,stoich} \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda_{rich}}$ | Rich mixture fuel mass | kg/s |
| 10 | $\dot{V}_{fuel,per\,cycle} = \frac{V_d \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air}}{N/2 \cdot (A/F)}$ | Volumetric fuel per cycle | m³/cycle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Symbol Convention:**
> - $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)
> - $V_d$ = displacement volume (m³)
> - $N$ = engine speed (rev/s, NOT RPM in formula)
> - $P_{man}$ = manifold absolute pressure (kPa)
> - $T_{cool}$ = coolant temperature (°C or K)
