# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Air-Fuel Ratio from Volumetric Efficiency

**Given:**
A 4-stroke SI engine with displacement $V_d = 1.2$ L (1.2 × 10⁻³ m³), running at $N = 3000$ RPM. Intake air density $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³. Volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol} = 0.85$. Target A/F ratio = 14.7 (stoichiometric).

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to revolutions per second.**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= 3000 \text{ RPM} \\
  &= \frac{3000}{60} \text{ rev/s} \\
  &= 50 \text{ rev/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the air mass flow rate formula for a 4-stroke engine.**

The factor of 2 in the denominator accounts for one intake event per **two** crank revolutions.

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{air} &= \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2} \\
              &= 0.85 \cdot 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3 \cdot (1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3) \cdot \frac{50}{2} \\
              &= 0.85 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.0012 \cdot 25 \\
              &= 0.85 \cdot 0.036 \\
              &= 0.0306 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute fuel mass flow rate for stoichiometric combustion.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{fuel} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} \\
               &= \frac{0.0306 \text{ kg/s}}{14.7} \\
               &= 2.082 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \\
               &= 2.082 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to fuel volume flow rate (assume fuel density = 750 kg/m³).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{fuel} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}} \\
               &= \frac{2.082 \times 10^{-3}}{750} \\
               &= 2.776 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
               &= 2.776 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Fuel mass per cycle (per cylinder, single intake event every 2 revs).**

Number of intake events per second per cylinder (assume 4-cylinder, 4-stroke):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Intake events/s per cylinder} &= \frac{N}{2} = 25 \\
\dot{m}_{fuel,per\,cycle} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{4 \cdot 25} = \frac{2.082 \times 10^{-3}}{100} \\
                           &= 2.082 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/cycle per cylinder} \\
                           &= 20.82 \text{ mg/cycle per cylinder}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:** A 1.2 L 4-cylinder engine at 3000 RPM demands 20.82 mg of fuel per cylinder per cycle at stoichiometric A/F.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Enrichment Ratio During Cold Start

**Problem:** Engine coolant temperature is $-10$ °C. The cold-start enrichment factor $E$ is given by the empirical relation:

$$E = 1 + 4.5 \cdot e^{-0.045(T_{cool} - T_{ref})}$$

where $T_{ref} = 40$ °C is the reference temperature at which no enrichment is required. Calculate the enrichment factor and the resulting fuel mass.

**Step 1 — Compute the exponent.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{cool} - T_{ref} &= -10 - 40 = -50 \text{ °C} \\
-0.045 \cdot (-50) &= 2.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute enrichment factor.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 1 + 4.5 \cdot e^{2.25} \\
  &= 1 + 4.5 \cdot 9.4877 \\
  &= 1 + 42.694 \\
  &= 43.694
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** Students often confuse the direction of the exponent. In the formula above, enrichment is **highest at low $T_{cool}$**, so the term $(T_{cool} - T_{ref})$ is **negative**, and the negative sign in front of the coefficient makes the exponent **positive** → exponential growth.

**Step 3 — Calculate fuel mass with enrichment.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{fuel,cold} &= E \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel,stoich} \\
                    &= 43.694 \cdot 2.082 \times 10^{-3} \\
                    &= 9.097 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s} \\
                    &= 90.97 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}$$

This is **unrealistic for a real engine** — it shows that the formula is only valid near the reference temperature. Real ECU strategies clamp $E$ at a maximum of 4–5× for cold start.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Closed-Loop Lambda Feedback Correction

**Problem:** A wide-band O₂ sensor reports current λ = 1.08 (lean) in closed loop. The integral control algorithm updates the long-term fuel trim (LTFT) using:

$$FT_{new} = FT_{old} + K_I \cdot (\lambda_{target} - \lambda_{meas})$$

Given $FT_{old} = 1.00$, $K_I = 5$, $\lambda_{target} = 1.00$, $\lambda_{meas} = 1.08$. Find $FT_{new}$ after 1 iteration.

**Step 1 — Compute the error.**

$$\begin{aligned}
e &= \lambda_{target} - \lambda_{meas} \\
  &= 1.00 - 1.08 \\
  &= -0.08
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the update law.**

$$\begin{aligned}
FT_{new} &= FT_{old} + K_I \cdot e \\
          &= 1.00 + 5 \cdot (-0.08) \\
          &= 1.00 - 0.40 \\
          &= 0.60
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

$FT_{new} = 0.60$ means the ECU will multiply the base fuel pulse width by 0.60, which would actually move the system to **excessively rich** in one step. A real PI controller uses both $K_P$ and $K_I$ with much smaller gains (typically 0.01–0.1) for stability.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Altitude Compensation

**Problem:** An engine is calibrated at sea level ($\rho_{air,SL} = 1.225$ kg/m³). Find the fuel scaling factor $K_{alt}$ required when the engine operates at 3000 m altitude, where air density drops to $\rho_{air,3000} = 0.905$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Compute density ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_{alt} &= \frac{\rho_{air,3000}}{\rho_{air,SL}} \\
        &= \frac{0.905}{1.225} \\
        &= 0.7388
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Interpret.**

The fuel mass flow should be reduced to **73.88% of sea-level value** to maintain stoichiometry. If the engine had no compensation, the mixture would be **excessively rich** (1/0.7388 = 1.353 times too rich).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world consequence:** Pre-EFI carbureted cars driving from Kochi (sea level) to Munnar (~ 1600 m) would lose ~ 20% power and waste fuel. Modern EFI cars compensate automatically via the MAP sensor.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: A/F Ratio Across Operating Modes

| Operating Mode | SI Engine λ | SI A/F | CI Engine λ | CI A/F | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cold start | 0.40 – 0.60 | 5.9 – 8.8 | ~ 4.0 | ~ 58 | Wall-wetting compensation |
| Cold idle | 0.85 – 0.95 | 12.5 – 14.0 | ~ 3.5 | ~ 50 | Combustion stability |
| Warm idle | 0.90 – 1.00 | 13.2 – 14.7 | ~ 3.0 | ~ 43 | Stoichiometric target |
| Light cruise | 1.00 – 1.20 | 14.7 – 17.6 | 2.5 – 3.0 | 36 – 43 | Economy bias |
| Part load | 1.00 – 1.15 | 14.7 – 16.9 | 2.0 – 2.5 | 29 – 36 | Quality governing |
| Full load (WOT) | 0.85 – 0.90 | 12.5 – 13.2 | 1.3 – 1.5 | 19 – 22 | Power + smoke limit |
| Wide-open throttle, max RPM | 0.85 | 12.5 | 1.3 | 19 | Max power |
| Decel fuel cut | λ undefined (no fuel) | — | Fuel cut at low load | — | Overrun emission control |
| Acceleration (tip-in) | 0.80 – 0.95 | 11.8 – 14.0 | 1.5 – 2.0 | 22 – 29 | Wall-wetting compensation |
| Hot restart | 0.85 – 1.00 | 12.5 – 14.7 | 2.5 – 3.0 | 36 – 43 | Vapor lock recovery |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual / Definition Type)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the term "Equivalence Ratio" (Φ) and "Lambda" (λ) for an engine's air-fuel mixture. State the stoichiometric value of A/F ratio for gasoline.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Equivalence Ratio (Φ) is defined as the actual fuel-to-air ratio of the mixture divided by the stoichiometric fuel-to-air ratio:

$$\Phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoichiometric}}$$

Lambda (λ) is the reciprocal of the equivalence ratio, i.e., the actual air-to-fuel ratio divided by the stoichiometric air-to-fuel ratio:

$$\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoichiometric}} = \frac{1}{\Phi}$$

For gasoline (assumed as iso-octane C₈H₁₈), the stoichiometric mass A/F ratio is **14.7 : 1**.

- Φ = 1.0 / λ = 1.0 → stoichiometric
- Φ > 1.0 / λ < 1.0 → rich mixture
- Φ < 1.0 / λ > 1.0 → lean mixture

**Mark Distribution:** Definition of λ (1), Definition of Φ (1), Stoichiometric A/F (1).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three transient operating conditions of an SI engine and explain why the A/F ratio must be compensated during transients.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three transient conditions are:
1. **Cold Start:** Cold cylinder walls condense injected fuel (wall-wetting). Only 30–40% of fuel enters the combustion chamber, so fuel must be enriched by 2–5× stoichiometric.
2. **Acceleration (Tip-in):** Manifold pressure rises suddenly. Extra fuel is needed because the fuel film on port walls absorbs part of the injected pulse.
3. **Deceleration (Tip-out / Overrun):** Throttle closes rapidly; excess vaporized fuel from the port walls creates over-rich condition. ECU performs **deceleration fuel cut-off** above ~ 1500 RPM.

**Why compensation is needed:**
During transients, the engine's air and fuel responses are not synchronized — air response is nearly instantaneous (faster), but fuel delivery has mechanical and wall-wetting lag. Without active compensation, the engine stumbles, stalls, over-emits, or knocks.

**Mark Distribution:** Three transients (1.5 marks), Reason for compensation (1.5 marks).

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Full A/F Map and Diesel vs SI Comparison

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**Q.A.(a)** Draw and explain the steady-state air-fuel ratio (lambda) map for a 4-stroke SI engine as a function of BMEP and engine speed. Label the four operating regions with their typical lambda values and justify why each region demands a particular mixture strength. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A.(b)** Compare the steady-state mixture requirements of SI and CI engines. Why does a CI engine never operate at stoichiometric A/F? **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — SI Engine Lambda Map**

The lambda map of a 4-stroke SI engine plots **Engine Speed (N)** on the Y-axis and **Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)** on the X-axis. The contours of constant lambda form a characteristic "bull's-eye" pattern with the following four regions:

| Region | Speed | Load (BMEP) | λ Range | A/F Range | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Idle Zone** | Low (700–900 RPM) | Near zero (0–2 bar) | 0.85 – 0.95 | 12.5 – 14.0 | High EGR dilution; rich mixture ensures stable combustion against low flame speed. |
| **Part-Load Cruise** | Medium (1500–3000 RPM) | Low–medium (2–6 bar) | 1.00 – 1.20 | 14.7 – 17.6 | Lean for fuel economy; closed-loop control with 3-way catalyst. |
| **WOT Full Load** | Wide range (1500–6000 RPM) | High (6–12 bar) | 0.85 – 0.90 | 12.5 – 13.2 | Rich for max power; lower peak temp prevents knock; vaporization cools exhaust valves. |
| **High-Speed Light Load** | High (4000–6500 RPM) | Low (1–3 bar) | 1.05 – 1.15 | 15.4 – 16.9 | Best BSFC region; homogeneous lean or stratified mode in GDI. |

**Key Justification (board-required reasoning):**
1. **Power enrichment at WOT** is required because the chemical energy release rate per unit mass peaks near λ = 0.88.
2. **Economy enrichment at cruise** is needed because BSFC (brake specific fuel consumption) reaches its minimum around λ = 1.05–1.10.
3. **Idle enrichment** is essential because residual gas fraction (RGF) is 18–25% at idle, diluting the fresh charge.
4. **Knock protection** at high-load high-RPM is achieved by richening to reduce end-gas temperature.

**Part (b) — SI vs CI Steady-State Mixture Requirements**

| Parameter | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Combustion mode | Premixed (flame front) | Diffusion (spray controlled) |
| Throttle | Yes (for load control) | No throttle (load by fuel quantity) |
| A/F ratio range | 10 : 1 to 20 : 1 | 18 : 1 to 70 : 1 |
| Typical λ range | 0.7 to 1.3 | 1.2 to 4.8 |
| Stoichiometric operation | Common (λ = 1.00 in closed loop) | Not possible (no spark to ignite lean mixture reliably) |
| Idle mixture | Rich (λ = 0.85–0.95) | Excessively lean (λ = 4+) |
| WOT mixture | Rich (λ = 0.85) | Smoke-limited rich (λ = 1.3) |
| Load control method | Quantity governing (throttle) | Quality governing (fuel rack) |
| Mixture preparation location | Intake manifold / port / cylinder | Direct into cylinder |
| Emission control at WOT | Power enrichment trade-off | EGR + DPF + SCR for NOx/PM |

**Why CI never operates at stoichiometric A/F:**

1. **Premixed charge does not exist** — fuel is injected late, mixes with air during combustion, not before. Local rich zones are inevitable.
2. **Smoke formation** occurs if λ drops below ~ 1.3 due to fuel-rich pockets in the diffusion flame where soot forms and is not fully oxidized.
3. **No throttling** → wide-open intake always → large excess air always present in the cylinder.
4. **Combustion is mixing-controlled**, not reaction-controlled. The rate-limiting step is the rate at which fuel vapor mixes with air, not the chemical kinetics.
5. **Three-way catalysts** are ineffective on diesel exhaust due to lean operation; diesel needs **DPF, SCR, LNT** for aftertreatment.

**Mark Distribution (Part b):** Comparison table (3 marks), Five reasons (1 mark each = 5 marks), but capped at 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Transient Compensation Strategies

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**Q.B.(a)** Explain the concept of "Wall Wetting" in port fuel injection. How does it affect mixture preparation during cold start and acceleration? Describe the methods used to compensate for wall-wetting effects in modern EFI systems. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B.(b)** A 4-cylinder, 1.2 L SI engine operates at 3000 RPM with volumetric efficiency 85% and intake air density 1.2 kg/m³. Calculate (i) the mass flow rate of air, (ii) the stoichiometric fuel mass flow rate, and (iii) the mass of fuel per cycle per cylinder. If the ECU applies a cold-start enrichment factor of 2.5, recalculate (ii) and (iii). **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — Wall Wetting Explanation**

**Definition:**
Wall wetting is the phenomenon in which **fuel droplets from the injector impinge on, mix with, and form a thin liquid film on the walls of the intake port and intake valve**. This liquid film does NOT vaporize instantaneously; it is gradually entrained back into the air stream as the air flow increases.

**Effect during cold start:**
- Port and valve surfaces are cold (often below 50 °C).
- Wall-wetting fraction is very high: only **30–40% of injected fuel** enters the cylinder as vapor; the rest stays as a film.
- ECU compensates by adding **extra fuel (enrichment) of 2× to 5×** the stoichiometric base value.
- This extra fuel saturates the wall film and provides the air-fuel ratio needed for combustion.

**Effect during acceleration (tip-in):**
- Throttle opens, manifold pressure rises, air flow increases rapidly.
- The **increased air shear** strips fuel from the port walls → sudden over-rich condition.
- Alternatively, in some systems, the rising air flow causes **temporary fuel starvation** as the wall film re-equilibrates.
- ECU adds an **acceleration enrichment pulse** (a few hundred milliseconds of extra injection) to maintain target A/F.

**Compensation Methods in Modern EFI:**
1. **Wall-wetting model** (Aquino or Hendricks model) embedded in ECU software — uses a first-order differential equation with parameters $X$ (wall film fraction) and $\tau$ (time constant).
2. **Coolant temperature-based enrichment** — lookup table in ECU ROM.
3. **TPA (Throttle Position Acceleration) rate** — detects tip-in rate and adds a scheduled pulse.
4. **Methanol/water injection** in some high-performance engines to clean fuel film and cool intake.
5. **Direct Injection (GDI)** — bypasses port wall wetting entirely, fuel injected directly into cylinder.

**Wall-Wetting Model (Aquino):**
$$\dot{m}_{ff} = X \cdot \dot{m}_{fi} - \frac{X}{\tau} \cdot m_{f} + \frac{d m_{f}}{dt}$$

where $\dot{m}_{ff}$ = fuel mass flow leaving wall film, $X$ = fraction of injected fuel hitting wall, $\tau$ = time constant of film evaporation, $m_f$ = mass of fuel in film.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem**

**Given:**
- $V_d = 1.2$ L $= 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $N = 3000$ RPM $= 50$ rev/s
- $\eta_{vol} = 0.85$
- $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $A/F = 14.7$ (stoichiometric)
- 4 cylinders
- Cold-start enrichment $E = 2.5$

**Step 1 — Air mass flow rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{air} &= \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2} \\
              &= 0.85 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 25 \\
              &= 0.85 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 0.03 \\
              &= 0.0306 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric fuel mass flow rate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{fuel,stoich} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{14.7} \\
                      &= \frac{0.0306}{14.7} \\
                      &= 2.082 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \\
                      &= 2.082 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Fuel mass per cycle per cylinder:**

Total intake events per second = $\frac{N}{2} = 25$ per cylinder (since each cylinder fires every 2 revolutions in 4-stroke).

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{fuel,per\,cyl,per\,cycle} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel,stoich}}{25} \\
                                    &= \frac{2.082 \times 10^{-3}}{25} \\
                                    &= 8.327 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg} \\
                                    &= 83.27 \text{ mg/cycle}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — With cold-start enrichment (E = 2.5):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{fuel,cold} &= E \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel,stoich} \\
                    &= 2.5 \cdot 2.082 \times 10^{-3} \\
                    &= 5.205 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \\
                    &= 5.205 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{fuel,per\,cyl,per\,cycle,cold} &= 2.5 \cdot 83.27 \text{ mg} \\
                                        &= 208.18 \text{ mg/cycle}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Air mass flow rate = **0.0306 kg/s**
- (ii) Stoichiometric fuel flow = **2.082 g/s**; Cold-start fuel flow = **5.205 g/s**
- (iii) Fuel per cycle per cylinder = **83.27 mg**; Cold-start = **208.18 mg**

**Mark Distribution (Part b):** Air mass flow (1.5), Stoichiometric fuel (1.5), Per cycle (1.5), Cold-start recalculation (2.5).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Units Mistake in Numerical:** Many students use $N$ in RPM directly in the formula $\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho \cdot V_d \cdot N/2$. This is **WRONG**. $N$ must be in **rev/s (i.e., divide RPM by 60)** before substituting. This single mistake costs **1.5 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing 4-stroke and 2-stroke factor:** The denominator is 2 for 4-stroke, 1 for 2-stroke. Students often write the formula without the divisor and lose marks.
>
> 3. **A/F direction confusion:** A/F = 14.7 means **14.7 kg air per 1 kg fuel**, NOT 1 kg air per 14.7 kg fuel. To find fuel from air: $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{air} / 14.7$.
>
> 4. **Wall-wetting explanation incomplete:** Simply saying "fuel condenses on walls" gets only 1 mark. To get full marks, students must mention the **time constant**, the **enrichment factor range (2–5×)**, and at least **one compensation method** (e.g., Aquino model).
>
> 5. **Cold-start A/F ratio confusion:** Some students write "cold start A/F is 30:1" — this is wrong. Cold start is **rich** (low A/F, like 5:1 to 8:1), not lean.
>
> 6. **Missing MAP sensor role in altitude compensation:** Many students explain altitude compensation but forget to mention the **barometric / MAP sensor** as the sensing element. Lose 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Diesel vs gasoline stoichiometric A/F:** Students often say "diesel A/F is 14.7" — correct stoichiometric value, but the diesel engine **operates at much higher A/F** (18–70) due to excess air. The distinction between **stoichiometric** and **actual operating A/F** must be made.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### A — Definitions You MUST Memorize

- ☑ **λ (Lambda)**: Actual A/F divided by stoichiometric A/F; λ = 1 = stoichiometric, λ < 1 = rich, λ > 1 = lean.
- ☑ **Φ (Equivalence Ratio)**: 1/λ; Φ = 1 = stoichiometric, Φ > 1 = rich.
- ☑ **Steady State**: Engine speed, load, and temperature held constant for several seconds.
- ☑ **Transient**: Operating condition changing within milliseconds to a few seconds.
- ☑ **Wall Wetting**: Liquid fuel film formation on port and valve surfaces in PFI engines.
- ☑ **Quality Governing** (Diesel): Vary fuel quantity per cycle for load control.
- ☑ **Quantity Governing** (SI): Vary air quantity (via throttle) for load control.
- ☑ **Smoke Limit**: Minimum A/F ratio for diesel below which visible smoke forms.
- ☑ **Power Enrichment**: Richening of mixture at WOT for power and knock protection.
- ☑ **Decel Fuel Cut-Off (DFCO)**: ECU cuts injector pulse when RPM > threshold and TPS = 0%.

### B — Numerical Constants You MUST Memorize

- ☑ Stoichiometric A/F (gasoline) = **14.7 : 1**
- ☑ Stoichiometric A/F (diesel) = **14.5 : 1** (theoretical)
- ☑ Air density at STP = **1.225 kg/m³**
- ☑ Universal gas constant R for air = **287 J/(kg·K)**
- ☑ Gasoline LCV = **44,000 kJ/kg**
- ☑ Cold-start enrichment range = **2× to 5× stoichiometric**
- ☑ Power enrichment λ at WOT = **0.85 to 0.90**
- ☑ Idle λ = **0.85 to 0.95**
- ☑ Lean cruise λ = **1.00 to 1.20**
- ☑ Diesel smoke limit λ ≈ **1.3**
- ☑ Diesel idle λ ≈ **4.0 to 4.8**

### C — Key Sensor / Actuator Roles

- ☑ **MAP Sensor** → measures manifold pressure → base fuel calculation.
- ☑ **MAF Sensor** → measures mass air flow → speed-density or mass-flow method.
- ☑ **O₂ / Lambda Sensor** → closed-loop feedback → keeps λ = 1.00 ± 0.02.
- ☑ **CTS (Coolant Temp Sensor)** → cold-start and warm-up enrichment.
- ☑ **IAT (Intake Air Temp Sensor)** → density compensation.
- ☑ **TPS (Throttle Position Sensor)** → acceleration enrichment detection.
- ☑ **CKP / CMP Sensors** → injection timing trigger.

### D — Formulas You MUST Be Able to Apply in 3 Minutes

- ☑ $\dot{m}_{air} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{2}$ (4-stroke)
- ☑ $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{(A/F)}$
- ☑ $\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$
- ☑ $E = 1 + k_1 e^{-t/\tau_1} + k_2 e^{-t/\tau_2}$ (transient enrichment, qualitative)
- ☑ $\rho_{air} = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$ (ideal gas)

### E — KTU-Favored Long-Answer Structure

For any 7-mark sub-question, the model answer should follow:
1. **Definition** (1 mark)
2. **Diagram / Table** (2 marks)
3. **Step-by-step reasoning with formulas** (3 marks)
4. **Real-world example or application** (1 mark)

### F — Common Mistakes to Avoid (Recall from Warning Box)

- ☑ Convert RPM → rev/s before plugging into formulas.
- ☑ Watch the direction of A/F: 14.7 air per 1 fuel.
- ☑ Stoichiometric ≠ Actual operating A/F.
- ☑ Wall wetting is specific to PFI, not GDI.
- ☑ Mention MAP sensor explicitly for altitude.
- ☑ Use **4-stroke divisor 2** explicitly in air flow formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Carburettor with Circuits. Fuel Supply System in Diesel Engines: Components of Diesel Injection System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Carburettor with Circuits. Fuel Supply System in Diesel Engines: Components of Diesel Injection System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Simple Carburettor — Formal Definition

A **carburettor** is a mechanical, air-pressure-actuated device used in **Spark Ignition (S.I.) petrol engines** to atomize, meter, and mix liquid gasoline with incoming air in the correct proportion (Stoichiometric Air-Fuel ratio ≈ 14.7:1) to deliver a combustible mixture to the engine cylinders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** A *Simple* carburettor (also called **elementary carburettor**) is the theoretical, idealized version containing only a float, float chamber, jet, venturi, and throttle. It is the **building block** for understanding the more complex *compound/practical carburettor circuits* taught later.

**Fundamental components of an elementary carburettor:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Float Chamber** | Stores fuel at a constant, atmospheric head |
| **Float & Needle Valve** | Maintains a steady fuel level in the float chamber |
| **Venturi Tube** | Convergent–divergent duct that accelerates air and drops its pressure |
| **Main Metering Jet (Fuel Jet)** | Restricts fuel flow from float chamber to discharge nozzle |
| **Discharge Nozzle** | Delivers fuel into the airstream at the throat of the venturi |
| **Throttle Valve (Butterfly Valve)** | Controls engine load by varying the mass of mixture admitted |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Tap and Suction Pipe" 💧

Imagine you are sipping water through a straw from a glass:

- **The straw** is the venturi — your strong suction (fast-moving air) creates a low-pressure zone.
- **The water** is the fuel — it gets pulled *up* from the glass because the pressure above the water in the glass (atmospheric) is now higher than the pressure in the straw.
- **The float chamber** is the glass — it keeps a constant water level, so the "head" of liquid never changes regardless of how much you've sipped.
- **The narrow fuel jet** is the size of the straw's tip — a smaller tip lets less liquid out (lean mixture), a bigger tip (rich mixture).

This is exactly how a simple carburettor works: **moving air "sucks" fuel from a low-pressure fuel pool.**

---

## 1.3 Diesel Fuel Supply System — Formal Definition

The **Diesel Fuel Supply System** is the high-pressure hydraulic network that delivers metered, atomized diesel fuel into the highly compressed (≈ 30–40 bar) hot air charge inside the cylinder of a **Compression Ignition (C.I.) engine** at the precise instant of injection.

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike petrol, diesel is **not premixed with air** outside the cylinder. The mixing occurs *inside* the combustion chamber during the injection event. Hence the diesel system must generate very high pressures (typically **200 to 2000 bar** in modern CRDi systems) to ensure fine atomization.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The High-Pressure Perfume Sprayer" 🌫️

Think of an aerosol spray can:

- You press the nozzle (the **injector**), the pressurized liquid (diesel from the **injection pump**) escapes through a tiny orifice and instantly breaks up into a fine mist that mixes with surrounding air.
- A small **straw** (the fuel line from the tank) cannot deliver fuel fast enough under high pressure — so we use a hand-pump action (the **lift pump** plus **injection pump**) to first *prime* and then *force-charge* the system.
- A fine **filter** is essential to keep the tiny nozzle hole from getting clogged.

This mirrors the diesel fuel path: **Tank → Filter → Lift Pump → Injection Pump → High-Pressure Pipe → Injector → Combustion Chamber**, with surplus fuel returning to tank.

---

## 1.5 Key Engineering Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlight)

- **Stoichiometric A/F ratio (Petrol):** $\mathbf{14.7 : 1}$
- **Stoichiometric A/F ratio (Diesel):** $\mathbf{14.5 : 1}$
- **Range of A/F ratio for practical SI engines:** $12:1$ (maximum power) to $18:1$ (maximum economy)
- **Typical injection pressure (older IDI diesels):** $\mathbf{100\text{–}350\ bar}$
- **Modern Common-Rail Diesel Injection (CRDi):** $\mathbf{1500\text{–}2000\ bar}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Simple (Elementary) Carburettor

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Piston intake stroke creates suction** in the intake manifold.
2. Atmospheric air rushes in, passes through the **venturi throat**.
3. By the **Continuity equation**, velocity $V_a$ increases; by **Bernoulli's equation**, the static pressure $P_a$ drops below atmospheric.
4. The **float chamber** is vented to atmosphere (or partially vented), so the fuel surface is at pressure $P_{atm}$.
5. A pressure difference $(\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_a)$ now exists between the float chamber and the venturi throat.
6. This pressure difference drives fuel up the discharge nozzle into the airstream.
7. The discharged fuel is **atomized** (broken into fine droplets) by the high-velocity air and **vaporizes** to form a combustible mixture.

### The Core 'Why'

The venturi is the **heart** of a carburettor. Without it, you would need an external fuel pump just to push fuel into the manifold. The venturi cleverly *harnesses* the kinetic energy of the moving air to do this work for you.

---

## 2.2 Quantitative Analysis — Air and Fuel Flow

Let us derive the theoretical fuel-air ratio relationships as required by KTU's analytical questions.

### Air Flow Through the Venturi

Let:
- $A_a$ = cross-sectional area of venturi throat (m²)
- $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge for air (typically 0.85)
- $\rho_a$ = density of air (≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at NTP)
- $P_1$ = upstream (atmospheric) pressure = $P_{atm}$
- $P_2$ = throat pressure
- $\Delta P_a = P_1 - P_2$ = pressure drop at venturi

The **actual mass flow rate of air** is:

$$m_a = C_d \cdot A_a \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_a \cdot \Delta P_a}$$

### Fuel Flow Through the Jet

Let:
- $A_f$ = effective area of the fuel jet (m²)
- $C_{df}$ = coefficient of discharge for fuel (typically 0.7)
- $\rho_f$ = density of fuel (≈ **750 kg/m³** for petrol)
- $h_f$ = height of fuel above the jet (small in modern designs)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

The total head driving fuel is the combination of **suction head** $(\Delta P_a / \rho_f \cdot g)$ plus the **static head** $h_f$:

$$m_f = C_{df} \cdot A_f \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_f \cdot (\Delta P_a + \rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_f)}$$

### Resultant Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)

$$\frac{m_a}{m_f} = \frac{C_d \cdot A_a}{C_{df} \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{\rho_f \cdot g \cdot h_f}{\Delta P_a}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** As engine speed increases, $\Delta P_a$ grows (because air velocity grows), so the second term under the square root approaches unity. This means at high speeds, the $h_f$ term becomes **negligible** — and the A/F ratio depends purely on the **jet geometry** $\dfrac{A_a}{A_f}$. This is why jet sizing is the primary calibration parameter.

---

## 2.3 Practical Limitations of the Simple Carburettor

| Operating Condition | Required A/F | What Simple Carburettor Delivers | Problem |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Starting (Cold)** | Very rich (≈ 5:1) | Lean (≈ 12:1) | Engine fails to start |
| **Idling** | Slightly rich (≈ 12:1) | Lean (incorrect) | Stalling |
| **Cruising** | ≈ 14.7:1 | ≈ 14.7:1 | ✓ Correct |
| **Full Load (Power)** | Rich (≈ 12:1) | Lean (≈ 16:1) | Loss of power |
| **Acceleration** | Slightly rich (≈ 12:1) | Lean (no extra fuel) | Flat spot / hesitation |

> A *simple* carburettor can only deliver a **fixed proportion** of fuel to air. To handle all five conditions above, we must add **auxiliary circuits**.

---

## 2.4 Carburettor Circuits (The Practical Carburettor)

A *compound (or multi-circuit) carburettor* is simply a simple carburettor augmented with several auxiliary circuits, each handling one operating regime:

### 2.4.1 Starting Circuit (Choke Circuit)
- A **choke valve** is fitted in the air horn upstream of the venturi.
- When closed (engine cold), it **restricts airflow** → manifold vacuum rises sharply.
- The reduced air mass but same fuel discharge makes the mixture **rich**.
- **Mechanism:** Pulled-out (manual choke) or thermostatically opened (automatic choke using a bimetallic spring).

### 2.4.2 Idling Circuit
- Operates at **closed throttle**, where manifold vacuum is very high (≈ 0.4–0.5 bar below atmospheric).
- A small **idling jet** delivers fuel from the float chamber to a port located **just below the throttle edge**.
- High vacuum sucks fuel out of this port even when main jet sees no flow.
- An **idling air bleed** mixes a small amount of air with the fuel to atomize it.

### 2.4.3 Main Metering Circuit
- Active during **normal cruising**. This is essentially the *simple carburettor* with the main jet.
- Includes an **emulsion tube** (main well) that bleeds air into the main jet — this **progressively weakens** the mixture as airflow grows, approaching stoichiometric ratio.

### 2.4.4 Accelerating Pump Circuit
- Solves the **flat spot** during sudden throttle opening.
- When the throttle is opened rapidly, air rushes in *immediately* (low inertia), but fuel (with higher inertia) lags behind → momentary lean misfire.
- A **pump plunger**, mechanically linked to the throttle shaft, **injects a squirt of extra fuel** directly into the venturi.
- Check-valve system ensures injection is unidirectional and instantaneous.

### 2.4.5 Power Enrichment Circuit (Economizer)
- Activated near **full throttle** to provide an extra-rich mixture for maximum power.
- A **vacuum-operated piston/diaphragm** lifts a valve in the main jet → effective jet area increases → more fuel.
- Modern power valves can also be **mechanically actuated** by throttle position.

### 2.4.6 Economizer / Part-Load Mixture Control
- Through the **emulsion tube air bleed**, the main circuit is calibrated to deliver a slightly *leaner-than-stoichiometric* ratio (≈ 16:1) during cruising to improve fuel economy.
- The emulsion tube allows extra air to enter the main well, effectively enlarging the jet area without changing the main-jet size.

---

## 2.5 Components of a Modern Diesel Fuel Injection System

The system must perform three jobs: **metering**, **timing**, and **atomization**.

### Stage 1: Low-Pressure Supply Stage
| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Fuel Tank** | Storage, with a strainer at the outlet |
| **Sediment Bowl / Primary Filter** | Removes water and large particles |
| **Lift Pump (Feed Pump)** | Plunger/diaphragm pump delivering low pressure (≈ **0.3–0.7 bar**), supplies fuel to injection pump |
| **Secondary (Fine) Filter** | Removes micro-particles (≤ 5 μm) to protect precision injectors |
| **Overflow / Return Line** | Returns excess fuel to the tank; air-bleeds the system |

### Stage 2: High-Pressure Generation Stage
The **Injection Pump** pressurizes fuel to 100–2000 bar depending on type.

### Stage 3: High-Pressure Distribution & Injection Stage
| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **High-Pressure Pipes (Delivery Valves)** | Carry pressurized fuel from pump to injector; the delivery valve in the pump element controls **retraction** of fuel column (anti-dribble) |
| **Nozzle Holder / Injector Body** | Houses the spring and needle; contains the high-pressure passage |
| **Nozzle (Spray Nozzle)** | The tip that atomizes fuel into the chamber — single-hole, multi-hole (DIN), or pintle type |
| **Nozzle Spring** | Sets the **opening pressure** (≈ 175–350 bar typical) |
| **Spring Pressure Adjusting Screw** | Calibrates opening pressure |
| **Overflow Pipe (from injector)** | Returns leakage fuel to tank |

### Stage 4: Control & Timing Stage
- **Governor** — regulates injection quantity by adjusting the effective stroke of the pump plunger based on engine speed and load.
- **Advance/Retard mechanism** — varies the injection timing based on engine speed.

---

## 2.6 Types of Diesel Injection Pumps (KTU Syllabus)

| Pump Type | Key Feature | Typical Pressure |
|---|---|---|
| **Inline (Jerk) Pump** | One plunger per cylinder, mounted in line | 350–550 bar |
| **Distributor (Rotary) Pump** | Single rotating plunger serves all cylinders sequentially | 550–800 bar |
| **Common Rail (CRDi)** | High-pressure accumulator rail feeds electronically-actuated injectors | 1500–2000 bar |
| **Unit Injector (UI)** | Pump + nozzle in a single unit per cylinder | 1000+ bar |
| **Electronic Unit Injector (EUI)** | Solenoid-controlled, very precise metering | 2000+ bar |

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_a = C_d A_a \sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta P_a}$ | Mass flow rate of air through venturi |
| 2 | $m_f = C_{df} A_f \sqrt{2 \rho_f \left(\Delta P_a + \rho_f g h_f\right)}$ | Mass flow rate of fuel through jet |
| 3 | $\dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{C_d A_a}{C_{df} A_f}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}\cdot\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{\rho_f g h_f}{\Delta P_a}}}$ | Air–Fuel ratio in terms of geometry and pressures |
| 4 | $\dfrac{m_a}{m_f} \big|_{h_f=0} = \dfrac{C_d A_a}{C_{df} A_f}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$ | A/F ratio when fuel head is negligible |
| 5 | $\Delta P_a = P_1 - P_2$ | Pressure drop at venturi (driving force) |
| 6 | $P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_a^2 = P_1$ | Bernoulli equation (horizontal venturi, neglecting height) |
| 7 | $V_a = \dfrac{\dot{V}_a}{A_a}$ | Air velocity at venturi throat |
| 8 | $\text{Indicated Power} = \eta_{th} \cdot (\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV})$ | Relates fuel flow to engine power |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{vol}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot N / 2}$ | Volumetric efficiency (for 4-stroke) |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\text{BP}}{\text{CV} \cdot \eta_{bt}}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption relation |
| 11 | $h_f = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_2}{\rho_f \cdot g}$ | Theoretical fuel head equivalent to venturi suction |
| 12 | $\text{Best Power A/F} \approx 12:1$ | Rich mixture for maximum power |
| 13 | $\text{Best Economy A/F} \approx 16:1$ | Lean mixture for maximum efficiency |
| 14 | Stoichiometric A/F for petrol | $\mathbf{14.7 : 1}$ |
| 15 | Stoichiometric A/F for diesel | $\mathbf{14.5 : 1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The same Bernoulli principle is used in **aircraft carburettors, paint spray guns, Bunsen burners, water jet pumps, and Venturi scrubbers** for pollution control. Understanding this principle is foundational across all fluid systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Air-Fuel Ratio of a Simple Carburettor

This is the **single most important derivation** in the SI engine module. KTU has asked this in Dec 2018, Jun 2021, and Dec 2023. We will derive it from first principles using continuity, Bernoulli, and the orifice equation.

### Step 0 — Setup and Assumptions

- Air and fuel flows are **steady, incompressible, and inviscid** (Bernoulli applies).
- The venturi throat is at section **2-2**, upstream at **1-1**.
- The float chamber vent is at atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$.
- The fuel discharge nozzle tip is at the **same elevation** as the float chamber fuel surface (so we can isolate just the pressure-driven component first, then add the static head $h_f$ as a correction).
- Fuel is treated as incompressible with density $\rho_f$.

---

### Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between Section 1-1 and Section 2-2 for Air

Bernoulli along a horizontal streamline (height terms cancel because both sections are at the same elevation, approximately):

$$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_1^2 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_2^2$$

We now define $\Delta P_a = P_1 - P_2$ (this is the pressure drop that does the useful work of lifting fuel):

$$\Delta P_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a \left(V_2^2 - V_1^2\right)$$

---

### Step 2 — Use Continuity to Relate $V_1$ and $V_2$

Let $A_1$ = cross-section upstream (where velocity ≈ 0 in the float vent, but in the air horn it is finite), $A_2 = A_a$ = throat area. For simplicity, treat $V_1 \approx 0$ in the still reservoir (stagnation case at carburettor entry):

$$\Delta P_a \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_a^2$$

where $V_a = V_2$ is the air velocity at the venturi throat.

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Theoretical Air Mass Flow Rate

For a **sharp-edged orifice** at the venturi, the actual mass flow rate is reduced by the **coefficient of discharge** $C_d$:

$$\dot{m}_{a,\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot \rho_a \cdot A_a \cdot V_a$$

Substitute $V_a$ from Step 2:

$$V_a = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P_a}{\rho_a}}$$

Therefore:

$$m_a = C_d \cdot A_a \cdot \rho_a \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P_a}{\rho_a}} = C_d \cdot A_a \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_a \cdot \Delta P_a}$$

> **This is Formula 1 from our Cheat Sheet.** It tells us the air mass flow rate scales with the **square root of venturi pressure drop**.

---

### Step 4 — Compute the Theoretical Fuel Mass Flow Rate

The fuel is driven by **two heads**:

1. The **suction head** caused by the pressure drop in the venturi, equal to a column of fuel of height:

$$h_{f,\text{suction}} = \dfrac{\Delta P_a}{\rho_f \cdot g}$$

2. The **static head** $h_f$ — the difference in elevation between the float chamber fuel surface and the discharge nozzle tip.

The total equivalent head (in metres of fuel) is:

$$h_{\text{total}} = h_f + \dfrac{\Delta P_a}{\rho_f \cdot g}$$

The theoretical fuel velocity at the jet exit (Torricelli's law, just like water draining from a tank):

$$V_f = \sqrt{2 g h_{\text{total}}} = \sqrt{2 g \left(h_f + \dfrac{\Delta P_a}{\rho_f \cdot g}\right)} = \sqrt{2 g h_f + \dfrac{2 \Delta P_a}{\rho_f}}$$

The actual fuel mass flow rate (with coefficient of discharge $C_{df}$):

$$m_f = C_{df} \cdot A_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot V_f = C_{df} \cdot A_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot \sqrt{2 g h_f + \dfrac{2 \Delta P_a}{\rho_f}}$$

Pull the $\rho_f$ inside the square root:

$$m_f = C_{df} \cdot A_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_f \cdot \left(g h_f + \dfrac{\Delta P_a}{\rho_f}\right)}$$

$$m_f = C_{df} \cdot A_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_f \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P_a + \rho_f g h_f}{\rho_f}}$$

$$\boxed{m_f = C_{df} \cdot A_f \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_f \cdot \left(\Delta P_a + \rho_f g h_f\right)}}$$

> **This is Formula 2 from our Cheat Sheet.**

---

### Step 5 — Divide to Get the Air-Fuel Ratio

$$\dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{C_d \cdot A_a \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta P_a}}{C_{df} \cdot A_f \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_f \left(\Delta P_a + \rho_f g h_f\right)}}$$

Cancel the common factor $\sqrt{2}$:

$$\dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{C_d \cdot A_a}{C_{df} \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a \cdot \Delta P_a}{\rho_f \left(\Delta P_a + \rho_f g h_f\right)}}$$

Factor $\Delta P_a$ out of the denominator term:

$$\dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{C_d \cdot A_a}{C_{df} \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_f} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P_a}{\Delta P_a + \rho_f g h_f}}$$

Divide numerator and denominator inside the inner square root by $\Delta P_a$:

$$\dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{C_d \cdot A_a}{C_{df} \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{\rho_f g h_f}{\Delta P_a}}}$$

> **This is the Master A/F Equation** — Formula 3 in the Cheat Sheet.

---

### Step 6 — Special Case: $h_f = 0$ (Modern Carburettors)

In modern downdraught carburettors, the float chamber is designed to be at **almost the same height** as the fuel discharge nozzle. So $h_f \to 0$:

$$\left.\dfrac{m_a}{m_f}\right\vert_{h_f=0} = \dfrac{C_d \cdot A_a}{C_{df} \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** A change in **jet area $A_f$** is the only calibration knob. Halving $A_f$ doubles the A/F ratio (leaner). Doubling $A_a$ also doubles the A/F ratio.

---

### Step 7 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Question:** A simple carburettor has a venturi throat diameter of **25 mm**, a main jet diameter of **1.2 mm**, $C_d = 0.85$, $C_{df} = 0.7$, $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\rho_f = 750$ kg/m³. Assuming $h_f = 0$ and $\Delta P_a = 0.05 \cdot P_{atm}$ (i.e., 5% of atmospheric, where $P_{atm} = 10^5$ Pa), compute the air and fuel flow rates and the A/F ratio.

**Solution:**

**Step 7.1 — Areas:**

$$A_a = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$A_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.0012)^2 = 1.131 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 7.2 — Pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P_a = 0.05 \times 10^5 = 5000\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 7.3 — Air mass flow rate:**

$$m_a = 0.85 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 1.2 \times 5000}$$

$$= 0.85 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{12000}$$

$$= 0.85 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 109.545$$

$$\boxed{m_a = 4.572 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{kg/s} \approx 45.7\ \text{g/s}}$$

**Step 7.4 — Fuel mass flow rate (with $h_f = 0$):**

$$m_f = 0.7 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{2 \times 750 \times 5000}$$

$$= 0.7 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{7{,}500{,}000}$$

$$= 0.7 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times 2738.61$$

$$\boxed{m_f = 2.169 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/s} \approx 2.17\ \text{g/s}}$$

**Step 7.5 — Air-Fuel ratio:**

$$\dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{45.72}{2.17} \approx 21.07 : 1$$

> **KTU Examiner Note:** This is **leaner than stoichiometric** (14.7:1). To bring the mixture down to stoichiometric, we would need to *enlarge* the fuel jet diameter. Solving for the required $A_f$:

$$A_{f,\text{req}} = A_f \times \dfrac{A/F_{\text{current}}}{A/F_{\text{desired}}} = 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{21.07}{14.7}$$

$$A_{f,\text{req}} = 1.621 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2 \Rightarrow d_{f,\text{req}} = 1.437\ \text{mm}$$

---

### Step 8 — Verification Using the Master Equation

$$\dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{0.7 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1.2}{750}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0}}$$

$$= 527.2 \times \sqrt{0.0016} = 527.2 \times 0.04 = 21.09$$

> ✓ Matches our direct calculation to within rounding error. **The derivation is consistent.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Suitability of Each Circuit

| Operating Regime | Throttle Position | Engine Vacuum | Required A/F | Active Circuit | Solution Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold Start** | Closed | Low (atmospheric intake) | Very rich (5:1) | **Choke** | Restricts air → maintains rich mixture |
| **Warm-Up** | Slightly open | Moderate | Rich (10–12:1) | **Choke (partly open) + Idling** | Progressive choke opening |
| **Idle** | Almost closed | Very high | Slightly rich (12:1) | **Idling** | Fuel drawn from idle port below throttle |
| **Cruising** | Partially open | Moderate | ≈ 14.7 (or lean) | **Main (with emulsion)** | Emulsion tube adds air to weaken mixture |
| **Acceleration** | Opening fast | Momentarily high | Slightly rich (12:1) | **Accelerating Pump** | Mechanical plunger squirts extra fuel |
| **Full Load (Power)** | Wide open | Low | Rich (12:1) | **Power (Economizer)** | Power valve increases jet area |
| **Overrun / Deceleration** | Sudden closing | High | Lean (cut-off) | **Dashpot** (deceleration) | Avoids backfiring |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Each answer must be precise and direct (≈ 4–5 lines, with one labelled diagram/sketch if asked). KTU values crisp definitions.**

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2022]**
Explain the function of the **float chamber** in a simple carburettor. Why is it vented to the atmosphere?

**Model Answer:**
The float chamber is a small fuel reservoir attached to the carburettor body that stores fuel at a **constant level**. The level is maintained by a **hollow float** (usually brass or plastic) that rises and falls with the fuel level, actuating a **needle valve** at the tank inlet. When fuel is consumed, the float drops, the needle opens, and fresh fuel enters. When the set level is reached, the float lifts the needle and cuts off the flow.

The float chamber is **vented to atmosphere** (or partially vented in compensated designs) so that the fuel surface is always at **atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$**. This ensures a **constant pressure head** is available at the discharge nozzle — independent of the fluctuations in manifold pressure. Without this atmospheric reference, the pressure differential driving the fuel through the jet would vary erratically, and the A/F ratio could not be maintained.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List the **five essential circuits** of a practical carburettor and state the operating condition each handles.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Circuit | Operating Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Starting (Choke) Circuit** | Cold engine start; provides very rich mixture (≈ 5:1) |
| 2 | **Idling Circuit** | Engine at idle / no load; provides slightly rich mixture (≈ 12:1) |
| 3 | **Main Metering Circuit** | Normal cruising; delivers near-stoichiometric (≈ 14.7:1) |
| 4 | **Accelerating Pump Circuit** | Sudden throttle opening; provides temporary enrichment to prevent hesitation |
| 5 | **Power Enrichment (Economizer) Circuit** | Full throttle / maximum load; provides rich mixture (≈ 12:1) for maximum power |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Style, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat labelled sketch, explain the **working principle of a simple carburettor**. Derive the expression for the **air-fuel ratio** in terms of venturi and jet areas, densities, and the fuel head $h_f$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A simple carburettor is to be designed for a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine with displacement of **1.5 litres** running at **3000 rpm**. The volumetric efficiency is **82%**, the A/F ratio is to be maintained at **15:1**, and the air density is **1.2 kg/m³**. Compute the required **venturi throat diameter** and **fuel-jet diameter** given $C_d = 0.85$, $C_{df} = 0.7$, $\rho_f = 750$ kg/m³, and assume $h_f = 0$. Also compute the venturi pressure drop $\Delta P_a$ if the air velocity at the throat is **85 m/s**.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Sketch:** Refer to Section 4.1 diagram.

**Working Principle (in six steps):**
1. During the **intake stroke**, the descending piston creates a low pressure (vacuum) in the intake manifold.
2. Atmospheric air rushes in through the air horn and accelerates as it passes through the **converging venturi throat**.
3. By Bernoulli's principle, the increased air velocity produces a **pressure drop** at the throat: $P_2 < P_1 = P_{atm}$.
4. The **float chamber** is vented to atmosphere, so its fuel surface remains at $P_{atm}$.
5. The pressure difference $\Delta P_a = P_{atm} - P_2$ pushes fuel up the discharge nozzle, where it is **atomized** into the airstream.
6. The fuel-air mixture flows past the **throttle valve** into the engine cylinders.

**Derivation of A/F Ratio:** (As already derived exhaustively in Section 3.1, Steps 1–6)

$$\boxed{\dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{C_d \cdot A_a}{C_{df} \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{\rho_f g h_f}{\Delta P_a}}}}$$

> **Examiner's tip:** Always state the assumptions explicitly — *steady flow, incompressible fluids, negligible fuel inertia, isothermal flow*. KTU's evaluator looks for these.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Total air mass flow rate required**

For a 4-stroke engine, the number of intake strokes per minute = N/2 = 1500.
Swept volume per cycle per cylinder = $V_d/4 = 1.5/4 = 0.375$ L = $3.75 \times 10^{-4}$ m³.

Theoretical air volume per minute:

$$\dot{V}_{a,\text{theo}} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot N}{2} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 3000}{2} = 2.25\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

Actual volume (using volumetric efficiency):

$$\dot{V}_{a,\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{vol}} \cdot \dot{V}_{a,\text{theo}} = 0.82 \times 2.25 = 1.845\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

Convert to kg/s:

$$m_a = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V}_{a,\text{actual}} = 1.2 \times \dfrac{1.845}{60} = 1.2 \times 0.03075$$

$$\boxed{m_a = 0.0369\ \text{kg/s} = 36.9\ \text{g/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Required fuel mass flow rate**

$$m_f = \dfrac{m_a}{A/F} = \dfrac{0.0369}{15} = 0.00246\ \text{kg/s} = 2.46\ \text{g/s}$$

**Step 3 — Required venturi area $A_a$**

From the air mass flow equation (with the pressure drop to be computed in Step 5):

We have $V_a = 85$ m/s (given in the problem), so use the simpler form:

$$m_a = C_d \cdot A_a \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_a$$

$$A_a = \dfrac{m_a}{C_d \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_a} = \dfrac{0.0369}{0.85 \times 1.2 \times 85}$$

$$A_a = \dfrac{0.0369}{86.7} = 4.256 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$\boxed{A_a = 4.256 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 \Rightarrow d_a = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_a}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 4.256 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi}} = 0.02328\ \text{m} \approx 23.3\ \text{mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Required fuel jet area $A_f$**

Using the Master A/F equation with $h_f = 0$:

$$\dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{C_d \cdot A_a}{C_{df} \cdot A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$$

$$15 = \dfrac{0.85 \times 4.256 \times 10^{-4}}{0.7 \times A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1.2}{750}}$$

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{1.2}{750}} = \sqrt{0.0016} = 0.04$$

$$15 = \dfrac{0.85 \times 4.256 \times 10^{-4}}{0.7 \times A_f} \times 0.04$$

$$15 = \dfrac{1.447 \times 10^{-5}}{0.7 \times A_f}$$

$$A_f = \dfrac{1.447 \times 10^{-5}}{15 \times 0.7} = 1.378 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$\boxed{d_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 1.378 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi}} = 0.001325\ \text{m} \approx 1.32\ \text{mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Venturi pressure drop**

Using Bernoulli (assuming $V_1 \approx 0$ at the air horn entry):

$$\Delta P_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V_a^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times 85^2$$

$$\Delta P_a = 0.6 \times 7225 = 4335\ \text{Pa} \approx 0.043\ \text{bar}$$

> **Verification using air mass flow formula:**
> $m_a = C_d \cdot A_a \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta P_a} = 0.85 \times 4.256 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 1.2 \times 4335}$
> $= 0.85 \times 4.256 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{10404}$
> $= 0.85 \times 4.256 \times 10^{-4} \times 101.99$
> $= 0.0369$ kg/s ✓ **Matches Step 1.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe with a circuit-level block diagram the **five main circuits** of a multi-circuit carburettor. Explain the role of the **emulsion tube** in the main metering circuit.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a block diagram, list and explain the **components of a modern diesel fuel injection system**. Differentiate clearly between an **inline jerk pump**, a **rotary distributor pump**, and a **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)** system with respect to construction, working pressure, and metering control.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2.

**Five Circuits (with description):**

1. **Starting Circuit (Choke):** A butterfly valve fitted in the air horn above the venturi. During cold start, it is **partially closed**, restricting the air inflow. The unchanged fuel flow combined with reduced air makes the mixture **very rich** (≈ 5:1), which is needed because cold fuel does not vaporize well. The choke is progressively opened as the engine warms up (manually via a cable or automatically via a bimetallic spring thermostat).

2. **Idling Circuit:** A small auxiliary fuel passage that draws fuel from the float chamber via an **idling jet** and delivers it to a **port located just below the throttle edge**. At idle, the throttle is nearly closed, so manifold vacuum is high. This high vacuum sucks fuel from the idle port. A small **idling air bleed** admits air to emulsify the fuel. The mixture richness is adjusted via the **idle mixture screw**.

3. **Main Metering Circuit:** Active during normal cruising. Fuel is drawn through the **main jet** and discharged at the venturi. The **emulsion tube** (also called the main well) is a perforated tube that bleeds air into the main jet well. This extra air is mixed with the fuel before it reaches the discharge nozzle. As airflow increases, the emulsion tube allows progressively more air to enter — this **weakens the effective fuel-to-air ratio** at high loads, preventing the mixture from becoming overly rich.

4. **Accelerating Pump Circuit:** A **mechanically-actuated plunger pump** linked to the throttle shaft. When the throttle is opened suddenly, the pump plunger is forced down, squirting an **extra dose of fuel** directly into the venturi through a discharge nozzle. This counteracts the **flat spot** that occurs because fuel inertia cannot match sudden air inflow. A check valve prevents reverse flow, and a dashpot provides smooth action.

5. **Power Enrichment Circuit (Economizer):** Activated at **wide-open throttle** to provide a rich mixture (≈ 12:1) for maximum power. A **vacuum-operated diaphragm or piston** mechanically lifts a needle/piston in the main jet, effectively **increasing the jet area**. The vacuum signal comes from the intake manifold — at high load, manifold vacuum drops below the spring preload, and the piston moves.

**Role of the Emulsion Tube (extended explanation):**
In a *simple* carburettor, the A/F ratio varies with engine speed because the fuel head $h_f$ effect diminishes as $\Delta P_a$ grows. This causes the mixture to become **leaner at high speeds**, which is opposite of what we want. The emulsion tube solves this by **drawing calibrated amounts of air into the main jet well** through calibrated holes. The pre-mixed air-fuel emulsion in the well has a lower effective density than pure fuel, so the actual mass of fuel discharged is reduced. As airflow grows (higher $\Delta P_a$), more air bleeds through the emulsion tube, progressively weakening the mixture. This compensates for the natural weakening tendency and keeps the A/F ratio close to stoichiometric over a wide operating range.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Block Diagram of Diesel Fuel System:** Refer to Section 4.3 and 4.4.

**Components of a Modern Diesel Fuel Injection System:**

| S.No. | Component | Detailed Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fuel Tank** | Storage with capacity for ≥ 1 hour of operation |
| 2 | **Fuel Strainer / Sediment Bowl** | Removes coarse contaminants and water |
| 3 | **Lift Pump (Feed Pump)** | Plunger or diaphragm type; supplies fuel at low pressure (0.3–0.7 bar) to the injection pump |
| 4 | **Fine Fuel Filter** | Removes particles down to 5 μm to protect precision injector parts |
| 5 | **Injection Pump** | Generates high pressure (100–2000 bar) and meters fuel quantity |
| 6 | **Delivery Valve** | Atomizer fitting inside pump; provides rapid cutoff of injection and prevents dribble |
| 7 | **High Pressure Pipes** | Thick, small-bore steel tubes carrying high-pressure fuel from pump to injector |
| 8 | **Governor** | Mechanical / hydraulic / electronic; controls the effective plunger stroke → fuel quantity |
| 9 | **Timing Advance Device** | Advances injection timing with increasing speed (often automatic via centrifugal weights) |
| 10 | **Injector / Nozzle Holder** | Houses the needle, spring, and the high-pressure passage |
| 11 | **Nozzle Spring + Adjusting Screw** | Sets the **nozzle opening pressure** (NOP), typically 175–350 bar |
| 12 | **Spray Nozzle** | Atomizes fuel — single-hole, multi-hole, or pintle type |
| 13 | **Leak-off Pipe** | Returns small leakage past the nozzle needle to the tank |
| 14 | **Fuel Return Line** | Returns excess fuel from pump and injectors to the tank; also air-bleeds the system |

**Comparison Table: Inline vs. Distributor vs. CRDi Pumps:**

| Parameter | **Inline (Jerk) Pump** | **Rotary Distributor Pump** | **Common Rail (CRDi)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | One pumping element per cylinder; elements mounted in-line | Single pumping element rotates and distributes fuel sequentially | Separate high-pressure pump + accumulator rail + solenoid injectors |
| **Max Pressure** | 350 – 550 bar | 550 – 800 bar | 1500 – 2000 bar (some up to 2500 bar) |
| **Metering Control** | Mechanical governor varies plunger stroke | Centrifugal governor rotates plunger; roller ring controls stroke | ECU controls solenoid valve timing |
| **Timing Control** | Mechanical advance (centrifugal) | Mechanical advance | Electronic — fully variable |
| **Application** | Heavy-duty trucks, tractors, gensets | Passenger cars (older), light commercial | Modern passenger cars, BS-VI/Euro 6 vehicles |
| **Advantages** | Robust, simple, easy to service | Compact, fewer parts, cheaper | Ultra-high pressure, multiple injections per cycle, lowest emissions |
| **Disadvantages** | Bulky, heavy, lower max pressure | Limited pressure, single-point failure risk | Very expensive, complex electronics |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Carburettor & Diesel Injection Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ factor** in the orifice equation. KTU allocates a full 2 marks for the correct flow-rate formula. Writing $m_a = C_d A_a \rho_a V_a$ without justification costs marks — *show the Bernoulli derivation first.*
> 2. **Mixing up $C_d$ and $C_{df}$** — air discharge coefficient is typically 0.85, fuel is 0.65–0.7. **Do not** use the same value for both.
> 3. **Ignoring the $h_f$ term** when it is given as non-zero in the problem. This leads to a 10–15% error in A/F.
> 4. **In the numerical:** Forgetting to convert $V_d$ from litres to m³ (multiply by $10^{-3}$) and RPM to per-second ($N/60$). These unit-conversion errors are the #1 cause of mark loss in numericals.
> 5. **In diesel questions:** Conflating "lift pump pressure" (low) with "injection pressure" (high). The lift pump is a *feed* pump, not the *injection* pump.
> 6. **CRDi question trap:** Many students forget that the *Common Rail* is a pressurized accumulator and not a distribution pipe — fuel is *not* pumped sequentially; it is *sprayed sequentially* from injectors fed by the always-pressurized rail.
> 7. **Drawing the float chamber without showing the vent to atmosphere** → loses 1 mark on the sketch.
> 8. **In derivations:** Skipping the assumption of incompressible flow. Always list the assumptions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> **Rapid-revision checklist (read this the night before the exam):**

- ✅ A **simple carburettor** has only a float chamber, venturi, main jet, and throttle. It cannot handle all operating conditions.
- ✅ **Air flow** is driven by manifold vacuum; **fuel flow** is driven by the **venturi pressure drop** plus the small static head $h_f$.
- ✅ The **master A/F equation** is:
  $$\dfrac{m_a}{m_f} = \dfrac{C_d A_a}{C_{df} A_f} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{\rho_f g h_f}{\Delta P_a}}}$$
- ✅ With $h_f = 0$, the A/F ratio depends only on **jet geometry** and the square root of the **density ratio**.
- ✅ **5 Carburettor circuits:** Starting (Choke) → Idling → Main (Emulsion) → Accelerating Pump → Power (Economizer).
- ✅ **Emulsion tube** weakens mixture at high airflow by bleeding air into the main jet.
- ✅ **Accelerating pump** prevents the "flat spot" during sudden throttle opening.
- ✅ **Choke** = air restriction, gives rich mixture for cold start.
- ✅ **Diesel fuel path:** Tank → Strainer → Lift Pump → Filter → Injection Pump → Delivery Valve → HP Pipe → Injector → Nozzle → Cylinder.
- ✅ **Injection pressure** is **100–2000 bar** (diesel) vs. **0.5–1.0 bar** (petrol carburettor venturi).
- ✅ **CRDi** uses a pressurized common rail; fuel pressure is decoupled from engine speed and injection timing is fully electronic.
- ✅ **Inline pump** = one plunger per cylinder; **Rotary** = one plunger rotates; **Unit Injector** = pump and nozzle in one.
- ✅ **Delivery valve** prevents secondary injection and dribble.
- ✅ **Nozzle opening pressure (NOP)** is set by the nozzle spring — adjustable via shims/screws.
- ✅ **Governors** control the *quantity* of injection; **advance mechanisms** control the *timing*.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F** = 14.7:1 (petrol) or 14.5:1 (diesel).
- ✅ **Best power** ≈ 12:1 (rich); **best economy** ≈ 16:1 (lean).
- ✅ The **lift pump** delivers low pressure; the **injection pump** delivers high pressure — never confuse them.
- ✅ For **4-stroke engines**, the number of intake events per second = $N/2$ (not $N$).
- ✅ Always convert **litres → m³** (× $10^{-3}$) and **minutes → seconds** (÷ 60) in numericals.

---

> 🏁 **End of Notes — Module 2, Topic: Simple Carburettor with Circuits & Diesel Fuel Supply System**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020), PBMUT404 — Automotive Engines and Transmission.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diesel Air Filters: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Diesel Air Filters: Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Air Filter** in a diesel engine is an essential **intake system component** that cleans atmospheric air before it enters the engine cylinders. It removes suspended particulate contaminants such as **dust, dirt, soot, pollen, sand, and carbon particles**, ensuring that only purified air reaches the combustion chamber.

In the KTU 2024 scheme terminology, a diesel air filter is classified as a **particulate separation device** operating on the principles of **inertial impaction, interception, diffusion, and electrostatic attraction**. For a typical diesel engine, the air-fuel ratio ranges from **18:1 to 70:1**, meaning a heavy-duty diesel consumes **15–20 kg of air for every 1 kg of fuel**. Contaminated air causes **cylinder wall scoring, piston ring wear, valve seat damage, and lubricant contamination**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** According to the PBMUT404 syllabus, the types of diesel air filters constitute a high-frequency topic. Students must be able to identify, classify, and explain the constructional and operational features of dry, oil-bath, cyclone, paper element, foam, and two-stage air filters.

## 1.2 Key Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Filtration Efficiency | 99.0 – 99.9 | % |
| Air Flow Capacity | 5 – 30 | m³/min |
| Initial Pressure Drop | 2 – 5 | kPa |
| Maximum Allowable Pressure Drop | 15 – 25 | kPa |
| Dust Holding Capacity | 200 – 1000 | g |
| Service Life (Paper Element) | 20,000 – 50,000 | km |

> [!NOTE]
> A **2% reduction** in airflow due to a clogged filter can result in a **3–5% drop in engine power output** and a measurable **increase in specific fuel consumption (SFC)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine breathing through a dusty cloth versus breathing clean air — your lungs would clog, your breathing efficiency would drop, and over time, you would become sick. Now, scale this up to a diesel engine:

- The **engine cylinder** is like your lungs.
- The **air filter** is like a high-quality dust mask.
- The **dust and dirt in atmospheric air** are the harmful particles.
- A **clogged filter** is like a mask covered in mud — you can still breathe, but with great effort and reduced airflow.

Think of a diesel engine as a "hungry giant" that swallows **massive volumes of air**. Without a proper filter, the giant is forced to inhale sharp dust particles, which act like microscopic sandpaper grinding the cylinder walls, pistons, and valves from the inside.

## 1.4 Why Air Filtration is Critical for Diesel Engines

- **High Air Consumption:** Diesels ingest 20+ times more air by mass than fuel.
- **High Cylinder Pressures (up to 200 bar):** Any abrasive particle accelerates wear.
- **Modern CRDi Injectors:** Tolerate only ultra-clean air; even 5 μm particles damage precision nozzles.
- **Emission Compliance:** BS-VI/Euro-VI standards demand clean combustion; contaminated air increases smoke and particulate emissions.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Context:** A single grain of sand (about 100 μm) entering a turbocharged diesel engine can cause **₹50,000–₹1,00,000 worth of damage** to the turbocharger bearings and cylinder walls within 500 operating hours.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles of Air Filtration

Air filtration in diesel engines relies on **four primary mechanisms**, often working simultaneously in modern multi-stage filters:

### Mechanism 1: Inertial Impaction
- Larger particles (≥ 5 μm) cannot follow the curved streamlines of air around filter fibers.
- Due to their inertia, they **collide with and stick to the filter media**.
- Effective for: Dust, sand, large soot particles.

### Mechanism 2: Interception
- Medium-sized particles (1–5 μm) follow air streamlines but are large enough to be **physically intercepted** when streamlines pass close to a fiber.
- They are captured when the particle center lies within one particle radius of the fiber surface.

### Mechanism 3: Diffusion (Brownian Motion)
- Sub-micron particles (< 0.5 μm) exhibit **random Brownian motion**.
- Their erratic path increases the probability of colliding with filter fibers.
- Dominant mechanism for the smallest particles; efficiency increases at **lower air velocities**.

### Mechanism 4: Electrostatic Attraction
- Some filter media (especially **electret non-woven fabrics**) carry a static charge.
- Charged fibers attract **oppositely charged or polarized particles** even at very low airflow velocities.

## 2.2 Classification of Diesel Air Filters

Diesel air filters are broadly classified by **filtration medium** and **operating principle**:

### Type 1: Dry Type Air Filter (Paper Element)
- **Medium:** Pleated cellulose or synthetic paper treated with resin.
- **Construction:** Cylindrical or panel-shaped element with accordion-like pleats.
- **Operation:** Air passes through the porous paper; particles are trapped on the outer surface.
- **Advantages:** High filtration efficiency (≥ 99.5%), lightweight, low cost, disposable.
- **Disadvantages:** Clogs quickly in dusty environments; cannot be cleaned effectively.
- **Application:** Modern passenger cars, light commercial vehicles, BS-VI diesel engines.

### Type 2: Oil Bath (Wet) Air Filter
- **Medium:** Mesh or wire gauze bathed in engine oil.
- **Construction:** A bowl at the bottom holds oil; the filter element is positioned above.
- **Operation:** Incoming air is directed downward, makes a sharp U-turn through the oil, and then passes through the mesh. Dust sticks to the oil.
- **Advantages:** Reusable, can be cleaned and re-oiled, robust in very dusty conditions.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher pressure drop, oil carryover into intake can foul sensors, heavier.
- **Application:** Older commercial vehicles, tractors, off-road equipment.

### Type 3: Cyclone (Inertial) Air Filter
- **Medium:** No physical filter medium; uses centrifugal force.
- **Construction:** A vortex chamber with tangential air entry and a central exhaust tube.
- **Operation:** Air enters tangentially, creating a spinning vortex. Heavier particles are flung outward against the wall by centrifugal force and fall into a dust collection bin. Clean air exits through the center.
- **Advantages:** No consumables, handles very high dust loads, ideal as a pre-cleaner.
- **Disadvantages:** Low efficiency for fine particles (< 10 μm), used mainly as a first stage.
- **Application:** Heavy earth-moving machinery (JCB, Caterpillar, Tata Hitachi), mining equipment.

### Type 4: Foam (Polyurethane) Air Filter
- **Medium:** Open-cell polyurethane foam saturated with special filter oil.
- **Construction:** Foam ring or panel wrapped around a plastic/metal support cage.
- **Operation:** Air passes through the foam pores; the oil-coated foam traps particles.
- **Advantages:** Reusable, washable, good balance of efficiency and durability, oil retention captures fine dust.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires periodic re-oiling, less efficient than paper for sub-micron particles.
- **Application:** Off-road motorcycles, ATVs, small diesel generators, two-wheeler diesel engines.

### Type 5: Two-Stage Air Filter (Combined System)
- **Stage 1:** Cyclone pre-cleaner removes 70–85% of coarse dust.
- **Stage 2:** Dry paper or foam element removes the remaining fine particles.
- **Advantages:** Extended service life (often 2–3× longer), optimized efficiency, reduced maintenance.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher cost, more complex installation, larger packaging.
- **Application:** Heavy-duty trucks (Tata, Ashok Leyland, Mahindra), buses, modern CRDi engines.

### Type 6: Oil-Coated Mesh Air Filter
- **Medium:** Multiple layers of wire mesh coated with viscous oil.
- **Operation:** Air flows radially outward or axially through the mesh; oil captures and retains particles.
- **Application:** Older two-stroke diesel engines, marine auxiliary engines.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Equations for Air Filter Analysis**

| Equation | Formula | Variable Description | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Filtration Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100$ | $C_{in}$ = upstream concentration, $C_{out}$ = downstream concentration | Measures filter performance |
| Overall Efficiency (Two-Stage) | $\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - \eta_1)(1 - \eta_2)$ | $\eta_1, \eta_2$ = individual stage efficiencies | Multi-stage filter design |
| Air Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_{air} \times Q$ | $\rho_{air}$ $\approx$ **1.2 kg/m³**, $Q$ = volumetric flow | Sizing filter capacity |
| Pressure Drop (Darcy's Law) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\mu \cdot v \cdot L}{k}$ | $\mu$ = viscosity, $v$ = velocity, $L$ = thickness, $k$ = permeability | Predicting filter loading |
| Filter Life (Dust Loading) | $t_{life} = \dfrac{C_{dust}}{m_{dust\_rate}}$ | $C_{dust}$ = dust holding capacity, $m_{dust\_rate}$ = ingestion rate | Maintenance scheduling |
| Centrifugal Force (Cyclone) | $F_c = \dfrac{m \cdot v^2}{r}$ | $m$ = particle mass, $v$ = tangential velocity, $r$ = vortex radius | Cyclone pre-cleaner design |
| Air-Fuel Ratio (Diesel) | $A/F = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}}$ | Typical **18:1 to 70:1** | Combustion stoichiometry |
| Reynolds Number (Filter Media) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d_f}{\mu}$ | $d_f$ = fiber diameter, $v$ = face velocity | Flow regime identification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention in KTU Exams:** Air filter efficiency is always expressed as a **percentage (%)**, pressure drop in **kilopascals (kPa)** or **millimeters of water column (mmWC)**, and flow rates in **Cubic Meters Per Minute (m³/min)** or **Cubic Feet Per Minute (CFM)**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Diesel Generator Sets:** Continuous-duty gensets in hospitals and data centers require **99.97% efficient (HEPA-grade) air filters** to ensure 24×7 reliability.
- **Mining & Construction:** Caterpillar 793 mining trucks use **three-stage filtration** (cyclone + paper + safety element) to survive dust concentrations of **> 1,000 mg/m³**.
- **BS-VI Compliance:** Modern Maruti Suzuki and Tata diesel engines use **mass airflow (MAF) sensors** downstream of the air filter; even minor contamination affects ECU readings and emissions.
- **Marine Engines:** Two-stroke marine diesels (Wärtsilä, MAN B&W) use **self-cleaning air filters** with reverse-pulse jet mechanisms.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **safety element** is a critical design feature in modern diesel air filters. It is a backup filter positioned after the main element. If the main element ruptures (e.g., from water ingestion), the safety element prevents catastrophic engine damage. KTU questions often ask why a safety element is mandatory in CRDi engines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Overall Filtration Efficiency of a Two-Stage Air Filter

**Given:** A two-stage diesel air filter system has a cyclone pre-cleaner with efficiency $\eta_1$ and a paper main element with efficiency $\eta_2$. The dust concentration entering the system is $C_{in}$.

**To Find:** The overall system efficiency $\eta_{overall}$.

### Step 1: Define Stage 1 (Cyclone) Output Concentration
The cyclone removes a fraction $\eta_1$ of incoming dust, so the concentration exiting stage 1 is:

$$
C_1 = C_{in} \cdot (1 - \eta_1)
$$

**Logic:** If the cyclone is 80% efficient ($\eta_1 = 0.80$), then 20% of the dust passes through.

### Step 2: Define Stage 2 (Paper Element) Output Concentration
The paper element operates on the concentration $C_1$ and removes a fraction $\eta_2$ of *that*:

$$
C_{out} = C_1 \cdot (1 - \eta_2)
$$

### Step 3: Substitute $C_1$ into the Output Equation
Combining both stages:

$$
C_{out} = C_{in} \cdot (1 - \eta_1) \cdot (1 - \eta_2)
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Efficiency Definition
By definition, overall efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute $C_{out}$
$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{C_{in} - C_{in}(1 - \eta_1)(1 - \eta_2)}{C_{in}}
$$

### Step 6: Simplify
$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1 - \eta_1)(1 - \eta_2)
$$

### Step 7: Final Expanded Form (for KTU numerical problems)

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_1 + \eta_2 - \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2
$$

**Logic Check:** If $\eta_1 = 0.85$ and $\eta_2 = 0.99$:
$$
\eta_{overall} = 0.85 + 0.99 - (0.85 \times 0.99) = 1.84 - 0.8415 = 0.9985
$$

This means the two-stage system achieves **99.85% efficiency**, compared to 99% for the paper element alone.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cyclone Pre-Cleaner Centrifugal Separation

**Given:** A cyclone pre-cleaner with vortex radius $r = 0.15$ m, air tangential velocity $v = 20$ m/s, and a dust particle of diameter $d = 50$ μm and density $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³ (typical silica dust).

**To Find:** The centrifugal force on a single dust particle and the particle's settling velocity.

### Step 1: Calculate the Mass of a Single Dust Particle
Assuming a spherical particle:

$$
m = \rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot d^3
$$

$$
m = 2500 \times \frac{\pi}{6} \times (50 \times 10^{-6})^3
$$

$$
m = 2500 \times 0.5236 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-13}
$$

$$
m = 1.636 \times 10^{-10} \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Centrifugal Force
$$
F_c = \frac{m \cdot v^2}{r}
$$

$$
F_c = \frac{1.636 \times 10^{-10} \times (20)^2}{0.15}
$$

$$
F_c = \frac{6.544 \times 10^{-8}}{0.15}
$$

$$
F_c = 4.363 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law)
For a particle falling through the boundary layer, assuming laminar flow ($Re < 1$):

$$
v_s = \frac{d^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_{air})}{18 \cdot \mu}
$$

Where:
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³ (negligible compared to $\rho_p$)
- $\mu_{air} = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s (dynamic viscosity)

$$
v_s = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 9.81 \times (2500 - 1.2)}{18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
v_s = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 9.81 \times 2498.8}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
v_s = \frac{6.127 \times 10^{-5}}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
v_s = 0.188 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 50 μm dust particle settles at 18.8 cm/s inside the cyclone, ensuring rapid separation and collection in the dust bin. Smaller particles (5 μm) settle **100× slower**, explaining why cyclones are effective for coarse dust but inefficient for fine particulates.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Air Filter Pressure Drop and Engine Power Loss

**Given:** A diesel engine consumes $\dot{m}_{air} = 0.5$ kg/s of air. A clogged air filter causes a pressure drop $\Delta P_{filter} = 8$ kPa.

**To Find:** The power consumed in overcoming this pressure drop.

### Step 1: Recall the Compressible Flow Work Equation
The power required to push air through a restriction is:

$$
P_{loss} = \dot{m}_{air} \times \frac{\Delta P}{\rho_{air}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Known Values
$$
P_{loss} = 0.5 \times \frac{8000}{1.2}
$$

$$
P_{loss} = 0.5 \times 6666.67
$$

$$
P_{loss} = 3333.33 \text{ W} \approx 3.33 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Percentage Power Loss
For a 100 kW engine:

$$
\% \text{ Power Loss} = \frac{3.33}{100} \times 100 = 3.33\%
$$
