# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**List any three advantages of a modern distributor type injection pump over an in-line injection pump.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Compact and lightweight** — single plunger serves all cylinders, reducing size by up to 60% compared to in-line pumps. **(1 mark)**
2. **Lower manufacturing cost** — fewer precision components (one plunger vs. N plungers). **(1 mark)**
3. **Self-contained governor and advance mechanism** — integrated hydraulic head eliminates need for separate governor unit. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the function of the cam ring in a Bosch VE distributor pump?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The cam ring is a stationary ring with internal cam lobes (one per cylinder) against which the rollers on the axial plunger's shoe ride. **(1 mark)**
As the distributor rotor rotates, the rollers contact successive cam lobes, causing the plunger to reciprocate. **(1 mark)**
This reciprocation generates high injection pressure (up to 700 bar) for fuel delivery. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice: Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a Bosch VE distributor type injection pump**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **fuel quantity control** and **injection advance** are achieved in the VE pump. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

The Bosch VE (Verteilerpumpe-Einheiz) pump consists of the following main parts: **(1 mark)**

1. **Drive Shaft** — Driven off the engine camshaft at half engine speed (for 4-stroke). Carries the centrifugal advance mechanism at the drive end. **(1 mark)**
2. **Vane-Type Feed Pump** — Self-priming pump delivering fuel at 4–8 bar to the high-pressure chamber. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Axial Plunger with Roller Shoe** — Single plunger mounted in the rotor, with 4 (or 6) rollers that ride on the cam ring. **(1 mark)**
4. **Cam Ring (Roller Ring)** — Stationary ring with internal cam lobes (4 for a 4-cyl engine). Rotated by the advance piston to alter injection timing. **(1 mark)**
5. **Distributor Rotor** — Carries the axial plunger and has a single distributing slit that aligns with the outlet ports sequentially. **(1 mark)**
6. **Hydraulic Head** — Contains the spill port, control sleeve, governor flyweights, and advance piston. **(0.5 mark)**
7. **Delivery Valves** — One per cylinder, mounted externally on the pump head. **(0.5 mark)**
8. **Cold-start and idle-stop solenoids** (in electronic variants). **(0.5 mark)**

**Working Principle (full 7 marks):**

The pump operates on a 4-stroke cycle per cylinder: **(1 mark)**

- **Suction Stroke:** Plunger retracts due to spring action. Low-pressure fuel from the feed pump enters through the suction port. **(1 mark)**
- **Pump Stroke:** Cam ring lobe forces plunger forward. Fuel pressure rises sharply. The rotor's distributing slit aligns with one of the cylinder delivery ports. High-pressure fuel flows through the delivery valve to the injector. **(2 marks)**
- **Spill-Off:** As the plunger nears TDC, the spill port in the rotor aligns with the spill ring in the hydraulic head. Pressure collapses rapidly, closing the delivery valve and ending injection sharply. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Indexing:** The rotor rotates by **360°/N** to align with the next cylinder. Cycle repeats. **(1.5 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Fuel Quantity Control (3.5 marks):**
The amount of fuel delivered is proportional to the **effective plunger stroke**. The spill ring is rotated by the governor mechanism:
- When fuel demand is high (e.g., full load), spill ring rotates **later** relative to the spill port → longer effective stroke → more fuel.
- When fuel demand is low, spill ring rotates **earlier** → shorter effective stroke → less fuel.
- The governor uses **centrifugal flyweights** that respond to engine speed changes and act on the control sleeve linked to the spill ring. **(3.5 marks)**

**Injection Advance (3.5 marks):**
At higher engine speeds, injection must begin earlier (more degrees BTDC) to allow time for combustion.
- The **advance piston** in the hydraulic head is acted upon by fuel pressure.
- As engine speed rises, the **centrifugal advance weights** move outward, advancing the cam ring in the direction of rotation.
- This causes the cam lobes to engage the rollers **earlier**, advancing start of injection. **(3.5 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Set]

**(a)** Compare the **Bosch VE** and **Lucas DPA** distributor pumps with respect to plunger arrangement, maximum injection pressure, and governor type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder diesel engine uses a VE pump with plunger diameter **8 mm** and maximum effective stroke **4 mm**. Calculate: **(i)** the theoretical displacement volume per stroke, **(ii)** the actual fuel delivery at 0.92 volumetric efficiency, and **(iii)** the total fuel delivered per pump revolution. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

| Parameter | Bosch VE | Lucas DPA |
|---|---|---|
| **Plunger Arrangement** | Single axial plunger (1) | Four radial plungers (one per cyl) |
| **Number of Plungers** | 1 | 4 |
| **Maximum Injection Pressure** | Up to **700 bar** | Up to **600 bar** |
| **Cam Profile Location** | Internal cam ring | External cam lobes on rotor |
| **Governor Type** | Mechanical (hydraulic head) | Mechanical (separate unit) |
| **Advance Mechanism** | Hydraulic (integrated) | Mechanical (centrifugal) |
| **Typical Application** | Passenger cars (IDI) | Diesel cars (IDI) |
| **Cost** | Lower (fewer parts) | Higher (more precision) |

**Award 1 mark for each correctly filled row (up to 7 marks for 7 rows).**

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $D_p = 8$ mm $= 0.008$ m
- $L = 4$ mm $= 0.004$ m
- $\eta_v = 0.92$
- $N = 4$ cylinders (4-stroke)

**(i) Theoretical Displacement Volume per Stroke:** **(2.5 marks)**

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D_p^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.008)^2
$$

$$
A_p = 5.0265 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V_d = A_p \cdot L = 5.0265 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.004 = 2.0106 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V_d = 201.06 \text{ mm}^3/\text{stroke}}
$$

**(ii) Actual Fuel Delivery (per stroke per cylinder):** **(2.5 marks)**

$$
q_{\text{inj}} = \eta_v \cdot V_d = 0.92 \cdot 201.06
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{\text{inj}} = 184.97 \text{ mm}^3/\text{stroke}}
$$

**(iii) Total Fuel per Pump Revolution (1 rev = 4 injections):** **(2 marks)**

$$
q_{\text{rev}} = N \cdot q_{\text{inj}} = 4 \cdot 184.97
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{\text{rev}} = 739.88 \text{ mm}^3/\text{rev} \approx 740 \text{ mm}^3/\text{rev}}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Cumulatively ~4–6 marks per paper):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "distributor pump" with "common rail pump"** — distributor pumps use a single rotating plunger and sequentially deliver fuel; common rail uses a pressurising pump and electrically-actuated injectors. **No credit for mixed-up answers.**
> 2. **Forgetting the rotor indexing relation** $\theta = 360°/N$. Many students write "the rotor rotates continuously" — **wrong**; it indexes by discrete steps.
> 3. **Mixing up advance vs. quantity control** — both use the hydraulic head but operate on *different* mechanisms (cam-ring rotation vs. spill-ring rotation). Examiners check this.
> 4. **Not writing units** in numerical derivations — KTU awards **0.5 to 1 mark** deduction per missing unit.
> 5. **Skipping intermediate substitution steps** — every line of algebra must be shown. Use `\frac{}{}` for fractions and `\times 10^{...}` for scientific notation.
> 6. **In Part A (3-mark) questions**, students often write *advantages* but forget the *function* explanation. **Always pair a fact with its engineering reason.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-friendly summary for the night before the exam:**

- [ ] A **modern distributor pump** uses **one single plunger** to serve **all engine cylinders** sequentially. **(HIGH YIELD)**
- [ ] The **Bosch VE** pump is the **most important variant** for KTU Module 2.
- [ ] Key components: **drive shaft, vane feed pump, axial plunger + rollers, cam ring, distributor rotor, hydraulic head, delivery valves**.
- [ ] **Working cycle:** Suction → Pressurisation → Distribution → Spill-off → Rotor Index.
- [ ] **Fuel quantity control** = varies effective plunger stroke via **spill ring rotation** by the governor.
- [ ] **Injection advance** = rotates the **cam ring** (not the spill ring) via a **hydraulic advance piston** + flyweights.
- [ ] For 4-stroke, 4-cyl engine: pump rotates at **half engine speed** and indexes by **90°** per cylinder.
- [ ] **Volumetric delivery formula:** $q_{\text{inj}} = \eta_v \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D_p^2 \cdot L_{\text{eff}}$
- [ ] **Rotor indexing:** $\theta = 360°/N$
- [ ] **Pump speed relation:** $n_{\text{pump}} = n_{\text{engine}}/2$ (4-stroke)
- [ ] Maximum injection pressure range: **400 – 950 bar** depending on variant.
- [ ] **Lucas DPA** uses radial plungers (4) — contrast with VE's single axial plunger.
- [ ] **Advantages:** compact, low cost, integrated governor, self-priming.
- [ ] **Limitations:** limited max pressure (vs. CRDI), phasing issues at high speed, phasing chamber complications.
- [ ] Always show **substitution steps** in derivations — KTU examiners mark **line-by-line**.
- [ ] Always include **units** — mm³, bar, mm, degrees, rpm.
- [ ] Distinguish clearly: **distributor pump ≠ common-rail pump ≠ in-line pump**.

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Modem Distributor Type Pumps**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Injection Nozzles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Injection Nozzles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Injection Nozzle** (also called a **Spray Nozzle** or **Atomizer**) is a precision-engineered, hydraulically actuated valve mounted at the downstream end of the fuel injection system in a Compression Ignition (CI) engine. Its primary function is to:

1. **Meter** the exact quantity of diesel fuel required for one combustion cycle per cylinder.
2. **Atomize** the pressurized liquid fuel into a finely divided, conical spray pattern.
3. **Distribute** the spray uniformly throughout the combustion chamber to ensure complete and efficient combustion.
4. **Time** the injection event with extreme precision relative to TDC (Top Dead Center).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The injection nozzle is the *final control element* of the diesel fuel injection system. It converts the high-pressure liquid column delivered by the pump into a high-velocity, atomized spray that initiates the combustion process. The nozzle essentially determines the *quality* of combustion, while the pump determines the *quantity*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Engineered Showerhead" 💧

Imagine a high-pressure garden hose connected to a precision showerhead. If you simply let water gush out of the open end, you get a useless, splashing stream. But attach a well-designed showerhead and you transform that stream into millions of tiny droplets spread over a wide, controlled area.

The **diesel injection nozzle works on exactly this principle**:

| Component in Analogy | Automotive Equivalent |
|---|---|
| High-pressure pump at city water station | Injection pump (delivering 200–2000 bar) |
| Hose carrying water | High-pressure injection line |
| Showerhead with tiny holes | Multi-hole nozzle tip with precision orifices |
| Wide spray of fine droplets | Conical atomized fuel spray in combustion chamber |
| Shower lever | Needle valve actuated by hydraulic lift |

The **smaller and more numerous** the holes, the **better the atomization** — but this must be balanced against the risk of **nozzle coking** (carbon buildup) in real engines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fun Fact:** Modern common-rail injectors have **6 to 12 micro-holes**, each only **0.10–0.30 mm** in diameter — thinner than a human hair! Fuel exits these holes at velocities exceeding **100 m/s**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

The following metrics are the **four pillars** used by KTU examiners to evaluate any nozzle question:

- **Injection Pressure ($P_{inj}$):** 200 – 2000 bar
- **Spray Angle ($\theta$):** 4° – 160°
- **Spray Penetration (S):** Distance fuel travels before losing momentum
- **Spray Droplet Size (SMD):** Typically 10 – 50 micrometers ($\mu m$) for good atomization

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Injection Nozzles

Injection nozzles are broadly classified into two families based on the combustion chamber design they serve.

### 2.1.1 Open Type Nozzles (For Direct Injection — DI Systems)
Used in **Direct Injection (DI)** engines where fuel is injected directly into a single, large combustion chamber (e.g., the bowl-in-piston design). These nozzles have **no throttling** of the needle in the seated position.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern heavy-duty diesel engines (trucks, buses, tractors) universally use open-type nozzles for maximum atomization.

**Sub-types of Open Nozzles:**

a) **Multi-Hole Nozzle:**
- Contains **4 to 12 tiny orifices** (holes) drilled in the needle tip.
- Each hole is precisely **0.2 to 0.5 mm** in diameter.
- Produces **N independent spray plumes** (N = number of holes).
- Used in **DI engines** with pre-combustion chambers, swirl chambers, and modern direct-injection systems.
- **Spray angle** is fixed by the geometry: typical angles are **0°–360°/N** (e.g., 90° for 4 holes, 120° for 3 holes).

b) **Pintle Nozzle:**
- Has a **pin (pintle)** protruding from the needle tip.
- Creates an **annular orifice** when the needle lifts.
- Produces a **hollow-cone spray** with a characteristic hollow center.
- Used in **pre-combustion** and **swirl chamber** indirect injection engines.
- The **spray angle depends on lift** — greater lift = wider spray angle.

c) **Throttle Pintle Nozzle:**
- A modified pintle nozzle with a **small cylindrical throttling pin** at the tip.
- Creates a **small initial spray** that grows as the needle lifts further.
- Produces a **two-stage spray pattern**: a fine initial spray followed by a wider main spray.
- Used in **IDI engines** to control ignition delay and reduce noise.

### 2.1.2 Closed Type Nozzles (For Indirect Injection — IDI Systems)
In these, the needle is held **closed by a spring** and only opens when injection pressure overcomes the spring force. The chamber above the needle forms a **small pressure reservoir**.

- **Delay Pintle Type** — needle has a cylindrical extension delaying full opening.
- **Pintaux Type** — combines multi-hole and pintle characteristics.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Pressure Build-up**
The injection pump delivers fuel at very high pressure (200–2000 bar) through the high-pressure line to the nozzle holder.

**Step 2: Hydraulic Force vs Spring Force**
The pressurized fuel acts on the **differential pressure face** (annular area) at the bottom of the needle valve. When this hydraulic force exceeds the pre-load of the **nozzle spring** (typically 100–250 bar equivalent pressure), the needle lifts.

$$F_{hydraulic} = \Delta P \times A_{lift} > F_{spring} = k \times x$$

**Step 3: Needle Lift and Orifice Opening**
The needle lifts by a small but precise distance (usually **0.2 – 0.5 mm**), uncovering the spray holes.

**Step 4: Atomization**
Fuel rushes through the small orifices at high velocity. The **pressure energy converts to kinetic energy**, and the high-velocity jet shears into fine droplets due to **aerodynamic instability** (Rayleigh–Taylor and Kelvin–Helmholtz instabilities).

**Step 5: Spring Closure**
When the pump stops delivering fuel, pressure drops, spring force closes the needle, and injection ends **sharply** to prevent dribbling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the conical/hollow-cone shape?**
> The conical spray is produced because the holes are drilled at an angle to the nozzle axis. The **angle of incidence** ($\alpha$) of the hole determines the **spray angle** ($\theta$):
> 
> - **Multi-hole nozzle:** $\theta = 2\alpha$ (cone half-angle equals hole half-angle to axis)
> - **Pintle nozzle:** $\theta$ varies with lift due to changing annular geometry

## 2.3 Nozzle Holder and Spring Assembly

The nozzle does not work in isolation. The **nozzle holder** is the body that houses the needle, spring, and high-pressure passages.

**Key Components:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Nozzle body** | Houses needle and has high-pressure fuel passage |
| **Nozzle needle/valve** | Precision-ground cylindrical valve |
| **Nozzle spring** | Holds needle closed; sets opening pressure |
| **Spring adjusting shim** | Sets the pre-load (hence opening pressure) |
| **Pressure chamber** | High-pressure fuel reservoir above needle seat |
| **Nozzle cap nut** | Retains nozzle to holder body |
| **High-pressure line connection** | Inlet from injection pump |

## 2.4 Nozzle Chatter (Critical Phenomenon)

**Nozzle chatter** is the rapid opening and closing of the needle valve during the injection process. When the needle opens, pressure in the line drops slightly; when it closes, pressure builds again. This causes the needle to **oscillate at high frequency** (50–200 Hz), producing:

- **Improved atomization** (multiple short bursts instead of one continuous spray)
- **Better mixing** with air in the combustion chamber
- **Reduced needle tip wear** (less continuous contact)
- **Distinct "chattering" sound** during operation

> [!NOTE]
> **Chatter is desirable** in many modern nozzles — it is engineered intentionally. Too much chatter can cause needle breakage; too little leads to poor atomization.

## 2.5 Nozzle Wear and Tear Modes

| Wear Type | Cause | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Needle seat wear** | High-velocity fuel erosion, carbon particles | Dribbling, power loss |
| **Spray hole enlargement** | Cavitation erosion | Increased fuel consumption, poor spray pattern |
| **Spray hole choking** | Carbon deposit buildup | Reduced flow, misfire |
| **Needle sticking** | Overheating, gum formation | Cylinder misfire, smoke |
| **Nozzle body cracking** | Thermal fatigue | Fuel leak, engine damage |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU exam question on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$ | Volumetric fuel flow rate through nozzle | $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (0.6–0.9), $A_n$ = nozzle orifice area (m²), $\Delta P$ = pressure differential (Pa), $\rho_f$ = fuel density (kg/m³) |
| 2 | $v_{exit} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$ | Exit velocity of fuel from orifice | $v_{exit}$ in m/s |
| 3 | $P_{lift} = \dfrac{4 \cdot F_{spring}}{\pi (d_{lift}^2 - d_{pin}^2)}$ | Nozzle opening (lift) pressure | $d_{lift}$ = needle diameter, $d_{pin}$ = pintle pin diameter (m) |
| 4 | $S = K \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot d \cdot t}{\rho_a}}$ | Spray penetration (distance traveled) | $S$ in m, $K$ = constant, $d$ = orifice diameter, $t$ = time, $\rho_a$ = air density |
| 5 | $\tan(\theta/2) = K_s \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_f}}$ | Spray cone half-angle relation | $\theta$ = full spray angle, $K_s$ = spray constant |
| 6 | $SMD = \dfrac{\sum n_i D_i^3}{\sum n_i D_i^2}$ | Sauter Mean Diameter (atomization quality) | $D_i$ = droplet diameter, $n_i$ = number of droplets in class $i$ |
| 7 | $KE_{jet} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dot{m} \cdot v_{exit}^2$ | Kinetic energy of fuel jet | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s) |
| 8 | $P_{opening} = P_{spring} + P_{back}$ | Nozzle opening pressure balance | $P_{back}$ = back pressure in chamber |
| 9 | $A_{eff} = N \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_{hole}^2}{4}$ | Effective total orifice area | $N$ = number of holes, $d_{hole}$ = hole diameter |
| 10 | $\dot{m} = \rho_f \cdot C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$ | Mass flow rate through nozzle | $\dot{m}$ in kg/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Sheet Note:** Always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute values in tables (e.g., $\vert \Delta P \vert$) — never use the bare pipe symbol, as it breaks Markdown table syntax.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The injection nozzle is the **heart of the diesel engine's emissions and efficiency performance**. In modern engines:

- **Bosch Common Rail** systems use **piezoelectric-actuated multi-hole nozzles** with 6–9 holes and 2000 bar pressure.
- **Caterpillar HEUI** (Hydraulically actuated Electronic Unit Injector) uses servo-hydraulic intensification to reach 3000 bar.
- **Denso HP common rail** for heavy-duty trucks uses **3000 bar** peak pressure and 10 holes.
- The trend is toward **higher pressure + smaller holes + more holes = better atomization + lower emissions**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fuel Exit Velocity Through a Nozzle Orifice

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 2024. It uses Bernoulli's equation across a control volume from the nozzle chamber (point 1) to the orifice exit (point 2).

**Given Data:**
- Pressure in nozzle pressure chamber: $P_1$ (Pa)
- Atmospheric/back pressure in cylinder: $P_2$ (Pa)
- Fuel density: $\rho_f$ (kg/m³)
- Velocity in chamber: $V_1 \approx 0$ (large chamber, fluid nearly stagnant)
- Velocity at exit: $V_2 = v_{exit}$ (unknown)
- Elevation difference: $z_1 \approx z_2$ (horizontal nozzle)

**Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between Points 1 and 2**

The fundamental Bernoulli equation for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow:

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} + g z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2} + g z_2$$

**Step 2: Substitute Known Conditions**

Since $V_1 \approx 0$ and $z_1 \approx z_2$:

$$\dfrac{P_1}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $V_2$**

Rearranging the terms:

$$\dfrac{V_2^2}{2} = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_f}$$

Multiplying both sides by 2:

$$V_2^2 = \dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_f}$$

Taking the square root:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_f}}$$

Let $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ (pressure differential across orifice). Then:

$$v_{exit} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Discharge Coefficient (Real-World Correction)**

In practice, viscous effects, vena contracta, and turbulence reduce the actual flow. The empirical **discharge coefficient** $C_d$ (typically **0.6 to 0.9** for sharp-edged orifices) corrects the ideal equation:

$$\boxed{v_{exit} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}}$$

**Step 5: Derive the Volumetric Flow Rate**

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ is the exit velocity times the orifice area:

$$Q = v_{exit} \cdot A_n = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

**Step 6: Derive the Mass Flow Rate**

Multiplying the volumetric flow rate by fuel density:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_f \cdot Q = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_f \cdot \Delta P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "why is $C_d$ needed?", the answer is: to account for **viscous losses**, **vena contracta** (jet contraction downstream of orifice), and **turbulence** in the real flow. Ideal Bernoulli assumes inviscid flow, which is unphysical.

## 3.2 Derivation: Spray Penetration (Hiroyasu & Arai Model)

The **Hiroyasu-Arai correlation** is the industry-standard empirical model for spray penetration in a still-gas environment.

**Step 1: Define Penetration**

Spray penetration $S$ is the axial distance from the nozzle tip to the spray tip, measured at time $t$ after the start of injection.

**Step 2: Use Momentum Conservation**

The spray tip penetrates until the spray's axial momentum is dissipated by air resistance. By dimensional analysis and experimental fitting:

$$S = K \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot d \cdot t}{\rho_a}}$$

Where:
- $K$ = experimental constant (typically $K \approx 1.0$ to $2.0$)
- $\Delta P$ = injection pressure differential (Pa)
- $d$ = nozzle hole diameter (m)
- $t$ = time elapsed since start of injection (s)
- $\rho_a$ = density of air in cylinder (kg/m³)

**Step 3: Reformulate in Terms of Mass Flow**

Substituting $\dot{m} = \rho_f \cdot C_d \cdot A_n \cdot v_{exit}$ and the orifice area relation $A_n = \pi d^2 / 4$:

$$S(t) = K \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_a}} \cdot \sqrt{t}$$

This shows that for a **given pressure**, penetration grows as the **square root of time** — characteristic of a decelerating spray under air drag.

**Step 4: Qualitative Interpretation**

- **Higher $\Delta P$** → Faster initial jet velocity → Greater penetration
- **Larger $d$** → More mass in jet → Greater penetration
- **Higher $\rho_a$** (e.g., in turbocharged engines at high load) → More air resistance → Lower penetration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Penetration Paradox:** If penetration is too high, fuel hits the cold cylinder walls → wall wetting → smoke and HC emissions. If too low, fuel concentrates near the injector → rich mixture locally → soot formation. **Optimization is critical!**

## 3.3 Derivation: Spray Cone Angle (Geometric Analysis)

**For a Multi-Hole Nozzle (Geometric):**

The spray cone angle $\theta$ is determined purely by the **hole drilling angle** $\alpha$ relative to the nozzle axis. The hole axis makes angle $\alpha$ with the vertical needle axis. When fuel exits perpendicular to the hole axis, by vector resolution:

$$\tan(\theta/2) = \tan(\alpha)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\theta = 2\alpha}$$

**For a Pintle Nozzle (Lift-Dependent):**

The spray angle depends on the **annular gap geometry**, which varies with needle lift $h$. The half-angle of the spray is given by:

$$\tan(\theta/2) = \dfrac{r_{pin} + h \cdot \tan(\alpha_p)}{L + h}$$

Where:
- $r_{pin}$ = pintle pin radius (m)
- $\alpha_p$ = pintle pin taper angle
- $L$ = vertical distance from seat to tip
- $h$ = needle lift (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** As the needle lifts further, the effective spray cone widens because the fuel has more space to diverge before exiting.

## 3.4 Worked Example Problem (KTU Style) — 14 Marks Type

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
> 
> *A 4-hole diesel injection nozzle has each hole of diameter 0.30 mm. The injection pressure is 250 bar and the back pressure in the cylinder is 40 bar. The fuel density is 850 kg/m³ and the discharge coefficient is 0.78. Calculate:*
> 
> *(a) The exit velocity of fuel from each hole*
> *(b) The total volumetric flow rate through the nozzle in cm³/s*
> *(c) The mass of fuel injected per stroke if the injection duration is 3 ms*
> *(d) The spray cone angle if the holes are drilled at 30° to the nozzle axis*

**Given:**
- $N = 4$ holes
- $d = 0.30$ mm $= 0.30 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $P_1 = 250$ bar $= 250 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $P_2 = 40$ bar $= 40 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $\rho_f = 850$ kg/m³
- $C_d = 0.78$
- $t_{inj} = 3$ ms $= 3 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $\alpha = 30°$

### Solution:

**Part (a): Exit Velocity**

First, calculate the pressure differential:

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = (250 - 40) \times 10^5 = 210 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

Substitute into the exit velocity equation:

$$v_{exit} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

$$v_{exit} = 0.78 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 210 \times 10^5}{850}}$$

Compute the numerator inside the square root:

$$2 \times 210 \times 10^5 = 4.2 \times 10^7$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\dfrac{4.2 \times 10^7}{850} = 4.941 \times 10^4$$

Take square root:

$$\sqrt{4.941 \times 10^4} = 222.3 \text{ m/s}$$

Multiply by $C_d$:

$$v_{exit} = 0.78 \times 222.3 = 173.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Answer (a): $v_{exit} \approx 173.4$ m/s**

**Part (b): Total Volumetric Flow Rate**

Calculate the area of one hole:

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.30 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 9 \times 10^{-8}}{4}$$

$$A_1 = 7.069 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

Total effective area (4 holes):

$$A_n = N \times A_1 = 4 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-8} = 2.827 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

Apply the volumetric flow equation:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

$$Q = 0.78 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-7} \times 222.3$$

$$Q = 0.78 \times 6.286 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$Q = 4.903 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to cm³/s (1 m³ = 10⁶ cm³):

$$Q = 4.903 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^6 = 49.03 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Answer (b): $Q \approx 49.03$ cm³/s**

**Part (c): Mass of Fuel Per Stroke**

Volume per stroke:

$$V_{stroke} = Q \times t_{inj} = 4.903 \times 10^{-5} \times 3 \times 10^{-3} = 1.471 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$$

Convert to cm³:

$$V_{stroke} = 1.471 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^6 = 0.1471 \text{ cm}^3$$

Mass per stroke:

$$m = \rho_f \times V_{stroke} = 850 \times 1.471 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$m = 1.250 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} = 0.125 \text{ g}$$

**Answer (c): $m \approx 0.125$ g per stroke**

**Part (d): Spray Cone Angle**

For a multi-hole nozzle, the spray cone angle equals twice the hole drilling angle:

$$\theta = 2\alpha = 2 \times 30° = 60°$$

**Answer (d): $\theta = 60°$**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** Notice that all three sub-parts are interconnected — the value of $\sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_f}$ computed in (a) is reused in (b). Always compute common terms first to save time in the exam.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Nozzle Types

| Feature | Multi-Hole | Pintle | Throttle Pintle | Delay Pintle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Orifice shape** | Discrete cylindrical holes | Annular gap | Annular with throttling | Two-stage annular |
| **Spray pattern** | N independent plumes | Hollow cone | Two-stage hollow cone | Hollow cone (delayed) |
| **Spray angle** | Fixed (geometric) | Variable with lift | Variable, controlled | Variable |
| **Atomization quality** | Excellent | Good | Very good (staged) | Good |
| **Engine type** | DI (Direct Injection) | IDI (Indirect) | IDI | IDI |
| **Hole clogging risk** | Higher | Lower | Low | Low |
| **Nozzle chatter use** | Common | Common | Common | Less common |
| **Typical hole size** | 0.10 – 0.40 mm | N/A (gap) | N/A | N/A |
| **Injection pressure** | 200 – 2000 bar | 100 – 350 bar | 100 – 350 bar | 100 – 300 bar |
| **Manufacturing cost** | High | Moderate | Moderate | High |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the term "spray penetration" in the context of a diesel injection nozzle. Mention two factors that affect it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Spray penetration** is defined as the *axial distance traveled by the leading tip of the fuel spray from the nozzle orifice to the farthest point reached in the combustion chamber*, measured along the nozzle axis during the injection process.

**Two factors affecting spray penetration:**

1. **Injection Pressure ($\Delta P$):** Higher injection pressure produces higher exit velocity and therefore greater penetration. Penetration is directly proportional to $\sqrt{\Delta P}$.

2. **Cylinder Air Density ($\rho_a$):** Higher in-cylinder air density (as in turbocharged engines at high load) creates greater air resistance, *reducing* penetration. Penetration is inversely proportional to $\sqrt{\rho_a}$.

*(Other valid factors: nozzle hole diameter $d$, injection duration, ambient temperature, fuel viscosity, swirl ratio, etc.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Note (3 marks):** 1 mark for clear definition, 1 mark for each factor with brief explanation.

---

### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: Distinguish between a multi-hole nozzle and a pintle nozzle in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Multi-Hole Nozzle | Pintle Nozzle |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Has **multiple discrete holes** (4 to 12) drilled in the needle tip | Has a **single pin (pintle)** protruding from the needle, creating an annular orifice |
| 2 | Produces **multiple independent spray plumes** with a *fixed* spray angle | Produces a **hollow-cone spray** with *variable* spray angle depending on needle lift |
| 3 | Used in **Direct Injection (DI)** engines for best atomization | Used in **Indirect Injection (IDI)** engines with swirl or pre-combustion chambers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Note (3 marks):** Award 1 mark for each correctly identified difference with valid technical content.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a multi-hole injection nozzle used in a diesel engine. Discuss the function of the nozzle spring and the phenomenon of nozzle chatter. Also, derive the expression for the exit velocity of fuel from the nozzle orifice.**

**Model Answer Structure:**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Construction:**

A multi-hole injection nozzle consists of the following main parts:

1. **Nozzle body / holder** — A heavy, precision-machined steel body that houses all internal components.
2. **Needle valve** — A hardened steel needle, ground to a precise cylindrical shape (tolerance ~1 $\mu$m). It has a conical tip and N (typically 4–12) tiny spray holes of 0.10–0.40 mm diameter drilled at an angle $\alpha$ to the needle axis.
3. **Nozzle spring** — A helical compression spring that holds the needle closed against its seat.
4. **Spring adjusting shim** — A thin metal washer used to set the spring pre-load, which determines the nozzle opening pressure.
5. **Pressure chamber** — A small chamber above the needle seat where high-pressure fuel accumulates before injection.
6. **High-pressure fuel inlet** — Connects to the injection pump via the delivery line.
7. **Leak-off / return line** — Allows excess fuel to return to the tank, lubricating the moving parts and preventing overheating.
8. **Nozzle cap nut** — Retains the nozzle tip to the holder body.

**Working:**

1. The injection pump delivers fuel at high pressure (200–2000 bar) through the delivery line.
2. Fuel fills the pressure chamber and exerts force on the differential area at the bottom of the needle.
3. When this hydraulic force exceeds the spring pre-load, the needle lifts by 0.2–0.5 mm.
4. Fuel escapes through the spray holes at high velocity, atomizing into fine droplets.
5. When injection ends, pressure drops, spring closes the needle, and the cycle repeats for the next cylinder.

**Function of Nozzle Spring:**

- Maintains needle in closed position during no-injection period
- Sets the **opening pressure** of the nozzle (via shim adjustment)
- Ensures **sharp closure** at end of injection to prevent dribbling
- Provides a small controlled amount of chatter during injection

**Nozzle Chatter Phenomenon:**

When the needle lifts, line pressure drops slightly below the opening pressure, allowing the spring to momentarily close the needle. This builds up pressure again, and the cycle repeats rapidly (50–200 Hz). The result is a series of rapid short injections rather than one continuous spray. This phenomenon, called **nozzle chatter**, is desirable because it:

- Improves atomization
- Reduces needle seat wear
- Provides better air-fuel mixing
- Produces a distinctive chattering sound

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Exit Velocity (7 marks)

*(Write the full derivation as shown in Section 3.1 above)*

**Final Result:**

$$v_{exit} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

Where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (accounts for vena contracta, viscous losses)
- $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ = pressure differential (Pa)
- $\rho_f$ = fuel density (kg/m³)

The volumetric flow rate is then:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Expected Pattern]

**Q: A 6-hole diesel injection nozzle has each hole of diameter 0.25 mm. The nozzle opening pressure is 220 bar and the cylinder back pressure is 35 bar. The fuel density is 830 kg/m³ and the discharge coefficient is 0.82. The injection duration is 2.5 ms. Calculate: (a) the exit velocity of fuel through each hole, (b) the total mass flow rate through the nozzle, and (c) the total mass of fuel injected per cycle. If the holes are drilled at an angle of 25° to the nozzle axis, determine (d) the spray cone angle.**

**Given:**
- $N = 6$ holes
- $d = 0.25$ mm $= 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $P_1 = 220$ bar $= 220 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $P_2 = 35$ bar $= 35 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $\rho_f = 830$ kg/m³
- $C_d = 0.82$
- $t_{inj} = 2.5$ ms $= 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $\alpha = 25°$

#### Part (a) — Exit Velocity (3 marks)

Pressure differential:

$$\Delta P = (220 - 35) \times 10^5 = 185 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

Apply the exit velocity formula:

$$v_{exit} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

$$v_{exit} = 0.82 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 185 \times 10^5}{830}}$$

Compute the inner term:

$$\dfrac{2 \times 185 \times 10^5}{830} = \dfrac{3.7 \times 10^7}{830} = 4.458 \times 10^4$$

Take square root:

$$\sqrt{4.458 \times 10^4} = 211.1 \text{ m/s}$$

Final result:

$$v_{exit} = 0.82 \times 211.1 = \mathbf{173.1 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Answer (a): $v_{exit} \approx 173.1$ m/s**

#### Part (b) — Mass Flow Rate (4 marks)

Area of one hole:

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (2.5 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-8}}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$$

Total effective area (6 holes):

$$A_n = 6 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-8} = 2.945 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

Mass flow rate equation:

$$\dot{m} = \rho_f \cdot C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_f}}$$

This simplifies to:

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_f \cdot \Delta P}$$

Compute the term:

$$2 \rho_f \cdot \Delta P = 2 \times 830 \times 185 \times 10^5 = 3.071 \times 10^{10}$$

Square root:

$$\sqrt{3.071 \times 10^{10}} = 1.752 \times 10^5$$

Final result:

$$\dot{m} = 0.82 \times 2.945 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.752 \times 10^5$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.82 \times 5.160 \times 10^{-2} = 4.231 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Answer (b): $\dot{m} \approx 0.0423$ kg/s = 42.3 g/s**

#### Part (c) — Total Mass Per Cycle (4 marks)

Mass per cycle = mass flow rate × injection duration:

$$m_{cycle} = \dot{m} \times t_{inj} = 4.231 \times 10^{-2} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$m_{cycle} = 1.058 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg}$$

Convert to milligrams:

$$m_{cycle} = 105.8 \text{ mg} \approx 0.106 \text{ g}$$

**Answer (c): $m_{cycle} \approx 0.106$ g per cycle**

#### Part (d) — Spray Cone Angle (3 marks)

For a multi-hole nozzle:

$$\theta = 2\alpha = 2 \times 25° = 50°$$

**Answer (d): $\theta = 50°$**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks!**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (Most Common):** Pressure must be converted from **bar to Pa** (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa). Hole diameter from **mm to m**. Time from **ms to s**. Forgetting this loses 2–3 marks easily.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $P_1$ and $\Delta P$:** $P_1$ is the absolute nozzle opening pressure, but the formula uses $\Delta P = P_1 - P_{back}$. Using $P_1$ directly inflates the answer by ~20%.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting $C_d$:** Always include the discharge coefficient. The ideal Bernoulli answer without $C_d$ is incomplete and loses 1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Wrong Number of Holes in Area Calculation:** Some students compute area for ONE hole but use it as total area. The total area = $N \times A_1$.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up Mass and Volume Flow Rate:** Be clear — $Q$ has units m³/s; $\dot{m}$ has units kg/s.
> 
> 6. **Spray Cone Angle in Pintle vs Multi-Hole:** Pintle = variable with lift. Multi-hole = $2\alpha$ geometric. Mixing them up loses 2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!**

### 🔹 Core Concepts
- ☐ Injection nozzle = final control element of diesel fuel injection
- ☐ Three main functions: **meter, atomize, distribute**
- ☐ Two families: **Open type** (multi-hole, pintle) and **Closed type** (delay pintle, pintaux)
- ☐ Multi-hole = Direct Injection; Pintle = Indirect Injection

### 🔹 Key Components
- ☐ Needle valve (precision-ground, 0.1–0.4 mm orifices)
- ☐ Nozzle spring (sets opening pressure)
- ☐ Pressure chamber (above needle seat)
- ☐ Leak-off line (returns excess fuel, prevents overheating)
- ☐ Shim (adjusts spring pre-load → opening pressure)

### 🔹 Critical Phenomena
- ☐ **Nozzle chatter** = rapid needle oscillation → better atomization (50–200 Hz)
- ☐ **Cavitation** = vapor bubble formation in orifice → hole erosion
- ☐ **Wall wetting** = excess penetration → smoke/HC emissions
- ☐ **Nozzle coking** = carbon buildup in holes → misfire

### 🔹 Must-Know Formulas (Memorize!)
- ☐ $v_{exit} = C_d \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_f}$ — **Most important**
- ☐ $Q = C_d A_n \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_f}$ — Volumetric flow
- ☐ $\dot{m} = C_d A_n \sqrt{2 \rho_f \Delta P}$ — Mass flow
- ☐ $S = K \sqrt{\Delta P \cdot d \cdot t / \rho_a}$ — Spray penetration
- ☐ $\theta = 2\alpha$ — Multi-hole spray angle
- ☐ $SMD = \sum n_i D_i^3 / \sum n_i D_i^2$ — Atomization quality

### 🔹 Typical KTU Numerical Values
- ☐ Injection pressure: 200–2000 bar
- ☐ Nozzle opening pressure: 100–250 bar
- ☐ Hole diameter: 0.10–0.40 mm
- ☐ Number of holes: 4–12
- ☐ Needle lift: 0.2–0.5 mm
- ☐ Spray angle: 4°–160°
- ☐ Fuel density: 820–860 kg/m³
- ☐ Discharge coefficient $C_d$: 0.6–0.9

### 🔹 Exam Strategy Tips
- ☐ Always show units throughout calculation
- ☐ Draw a neat labeled sketch (multi-hole or pintle) — this alone gets 2 marks
- ☐ State assumptions clearly (incompressible flow, negligible elevation change)
- ☐ For derivation questions, write Bernoulli's equation first, then substitute, then solve
- ☐ In multi-part questions, compute common terms once and reuse them
- ☐ End with a boxed answer with **correct units**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Insight:** Module 2 questions on injection nozzles typically carry 7–14 marks in ESE (End Semester Examination). Focus on:
> - Sketch + working (always asked)
> - Numerical problem on flow rate, exit velocity, or penetration
> - Comparison of nozzle types (table-format answer)
> 
> Master the derivation in Section 3.1 — it is worth **7 marks** standalone and appears in nearly every ESE paper.

topics.title: Governors (Vacuum and Centrifugal)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Governors (Vacuum and Centrifugal)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 2 — GOVERNORS (VACUUM AND CENTRIFUGAL)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Governor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *governor* is an automatic speed-regulating device fitted to an internal combustion engine that senses the deviation of the engine's rotational speed from a predetermined set value and modulates the fuel (or air-fuel mixture) supply accordingly, in order to maintain the engine speed within narrow limits under varying load conditions.

In automotive applications, governors are classified into two functional families:

1. **Mechanical (Centrifugal) Governor** — senses speed through the centrifugal force of rotating flyweights and translates that force into a linear sleeve movement to act on the fuel injection pump rack (diesel) or carburetor throttle (petrol).
2. **Pneumatic (Vacuum) Governor** — senses speed indirectly through the manifold vacuum generated by the engine and uses a spring-loaded diaphragm to actuate the throttle plate.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Smart Cruise Control"**
> Imagine you are cycling down a hill with a headwind. To keep your speed constant, you must pedal harder when the wind pushes against you, and ease off when the wind helps. Your brain acts as the "sensor," your legs as the "actuator," and your eyes on the speedometer as the "feedback." A governor does **exactly the same job**, but mechanically and autonomously.
>
> - **Centrifugal Governor** = a spinning weight that *flies outward* faster when the engine speeds up. This outward motion pulls a lever that *cuts* the fuel — just like easing off the pedal.
> - **Vacuum Governor** = a flexible diaphragm that *sucks inward* when manifold vacuum rises (engine light load). This vacuum pull closes the throttle slightly — again, easing off the fuel.

### 1.3 Why Governors Are Needed in Automobiles

| Condition Without Governor | Condition With Governor |
| :--- | :--- |
| Engine slows dramatically when a heavy load (e.g., hill climb) is applied | Speed dip is limited to ~2–5 % of set speed |
| Engine over-speeds dangerously when load is removed (e.g., gear downshift) | Over-speed is arrested within milliseconds |
| Driver must constantly adjust accelerator | Hands-free, automatic regulation |
| Poor fuel economy, high emissions | Optimal A/F ratio maintained → cleaner burn |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A student must clearly distinguish between the **sensing principle** (centrifugal force vs. manifold vacuum), the **output motion** (radial sleeve lift vs. axial diaphragm deflection), and the **controlled element** (fuel pump rack vs. throttle butterfly).

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are routinely used in KTU numerical problems and must be committed to memory:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (sometimes approximated as **$9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** or **$9.8 \text{ m/s}^2$**).
- **Conversion factor for speed:** $N \text{ (rpm)} \longleftrightarrow \omega \text{ (rad/s)}$ via $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.
- **Standard equilibrium speed range for governors:** **250 rpm to 1500 rpm** (centrifugal) and **600 rpm to 4500 rpm** (vacuum governor in modern cars).
- **Typical sensitivity range** for a well-designed Hartnell governor: **2 % to 5 %** (smaller is better for stability).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Governors

Governors used in the automotive sector can be broadly classified as follows:

### A. Centrifugal (Mechanical) Governors
These work on the principle that the *centrifugal force* on a rotating mass is proportional to the square of its angular velocity. The radial outward motion of the masses is mechanically linked to the fuel control.

1. **Watt Governor** — the simplest form; uses a sleeve that slides on the central spindle. Not used in modern cars, but a foundational textbook type.
2. **Porter Governor** — Watt governor modified with a central dead-weight load; sleeve motion is cushioned.
3. **Hartnell Governor** — uses a **spring** (not gravity) to balance the centrifugal force; sleeve movement is amplified by a bell-crank lever. *This is the type most commonly found in diesel fuel injection pumps* (e.g., Bosch P-series).
4. **Wilson-Hartnell Governor** — Hartnell with adjustable spring preload.
5. **Pickering Governor** — uses a roller and spring-loaded sleeve; compact design for high-speed engines.

### B. Pneumatic (Vacuum) Governors
Found in carbureted petrol engines (e.g., older Maruti 800, Ambassador, vintage Fiat). The throttle is controlled by manifold vacuum acting on a spring-loaded diaphragm. Types include:

1. **Single-diaphragm vacuum governor** (Solex type).
2. **Dual-diaphragm vacuum governor** (with auxiliary vacuum for supplementary advance).
3. **Centrifugal-vacuum combined governor** (used in small two-stroke engines, e.g., lawn mowers, mopeds).

## 2.2 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU cognitive demand:** Module 2 questions often ask the student to *trace the control loop* from "load change" to "fuel cut." Memorize the following five-step chain.

### Centrifugal Governor Operation (e.g., Hartnell)

1. **Load Decreases** → engine speed $N$ **increases** → angular velocity $\omega$ **increases**.
2. **Flyweights swing outward** due to centrifugal force $F_c = m r \omega^2$.
3. **Bell-crank lever rotates** about its pivot → sleeve moves **upward** (or downward, depending on design).
4. **Sleeve movement is linked** to the fuel pump control rack → fuel delivery is **reduced**.
5. **Engine speed drops** back toward the set value → flyweights retract → sleeve returns → equilibrium restored.

### Vacuum Governor Operation (e.g., Solex carburetor)

1. **Load Decreases** → throttle tends to close → manifold vacuum **rises** (more suction).
2. **Diaphragm is pulled inward** against the spring.
3. **Linkage rotates the throttle shaft** → throttle plate **opens slightly** to admit more air-fuel.
4. **Manifold vacuum drops** back to design value → diaphragm returns → equilibrium restored.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Table-Breaking Protocol:** All absolute value, division, and ratio symbols inside the following tables use the LaTeX commands `\vert`, `\mid`, and `/` — never the raw pipe character `\|`.

| # | Quantity / Term | Governing Formula | Description / KTU Notation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Centrifugal force on a flyball | $F_c = m\,r\,\omega^2$ | $m$ = ball mass, $r$ = radius of rotation, $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ |
| 2 | Centrifugal force in N (numerical) | $F_c = m\,r\,(2\pi N/60)^2$ | Used in KTU numericals; result in Newtons |
| 3 | Simple Watt governor height | $h = 895 / N^2$ | Approximation valid for $\sin\theta \approx \theta$; $h$ in metres, $N$ in rpm |
| 4 | Porter governor equilibrium speed | $N^2 = 895 \cdot (1 + 2k^2) / \bigl(h \cdot (1 + k^2)\bigr)$ | $k = q/r$ = ratio of arm to link length |
| 5 | Hartnell governor — initial spring force | $F_{s1} = S_1 \cdot (a - b) / a$ | $S_1$ = initial spring preload, $a, b$ = lever arms |
| 6 | Hartnell governor — final spring force | $F_{s2} = S_2 \cdot (a - b) / a$ | $S_2$ = final spring force (compressed) |
| 7 | Hartnell governor — speed relation | $\dfrac{F_{c2} - F_{c1}}{F_{c1} - F_{s1}} = \dfrac{N_2^2 - N_1^2}{N_1^2}$ | Linear in $N^2$ — the basis of KTU derivations |
| 8 | Sleeve lift amplification | $h = (r/a) \cdot x$ | $x$ = sleeve lift, $r$ = ball radius, $a$ = lever pivot distance |
| 9 | Sensitivity of governor | $\sigma = (N_2 - N_1) / N_m$ | $N_m = (N_1 + N_2)/2$ = mean equilibrium speed |
| 10 | Stability (Hunting) check | $\sigma > 0$ (stable); $\sigma = 0$ (isochronous) | $\sigma < 0$ → hunting (unstable) |
| 11 | Power of a governor | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | $T$ = torque required to drive the rotating assembly |
| 12 | Effort of a governor | $F_e = (W + W_c/2) \cdot \tan\theta$ | $W$ = ball weight, $W_c$ = central load, $\theta$ = arm angle |
| 13 | Vacuum governor force balance | $F_{\text{vac}} = \Delta p \cdot A_{\text{diaphragm}} = k \cdot x$ | $\Delta p$ = manifold vacuum, $k$ = spring stiffness, $x$ = diaphragm deflection |
| 14 | Manifold vacuum (ideal gas) | $p_{\text{man}} = p_{\text{atm}} \cdot (N_1/N_2)^\gamma$ | Approximate isentropic relation; rarely used in KTU |

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- The relation $F_c = m r \omega^2$ is the **design equation** for any rotating-mass sensor — from analog tachometers to gyrocompasses to washing-machine vibration sensors.
- The Hartnell speed relation (Row 7 above) is used by **Bosch, Delphi, and Stanadyne** fuel injection pump calibration engineers to map rpm to fuel rack position. The same equation (with sign changes) appears in **wind-turbine pitch controllers** and **gas-turbine overspeed trips**.
- The sensitivity concept (Row 9) is a **direct analogue of control-system gain** in modern electronic engine management (ECM/ECU). An isochronous governor ($\sigma = 0$) is the mechanical predecessor of **PID control with zero steady-state error**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Derivations carry 7–10 marks in Part B questions. The examiner expects *every* intermediate step to be shown. Skipping even a single force balance will cost 1–2 marks.

## 3.1 Derivation I — Height of the Governor for a Simple Centrifugal (Watt) Governor

### 3.1.1 Schematic Setup

Consider a Watt governor with two balls of mass $m$ each, attached to arms of length $r$. The arms are pivoted at the central axis. At equilibrium, the arms make an angle $\theta$ with the vertical axis.

### 3.1.2 Free-Body Analysis of One Ball

Forces acting on a single ball:

1. **Weight** $W = mg$ acting vertically downward.
2. **Centrifugal force** $F_c = m r \omega^2$ acting radially outward along the arm direction.

Resolving forces along the arm and perpendicular to the arm:

- Component of $W$ along the arm (tending to bring the arm down): $mg \sin\theta$
- Component of $F_c$ along the arm (tending to throw the arm out): $m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$

### 3.1.3 Equilibrium Condition

For the ball to remain in equilibrium, the moment of the centrifugal force about the pivot must equal the moment of the weight:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c \cos\theta \cdot r &= W \sin\theta \cdot r \\
m r \omega^2 \cos\theta &= m g \sin\theta \\
\omega^2 &= \dfrac{g \tan\theta}{r}
\end{aligned}
$$

For small $\theta$ (valid for $\theta < 30°$), $\tan\theta \approx \sin\theta \approx h/r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega^2 &= \dfrac{g}{r} \cdot \dfrac{h}{r} = \dfrac{g\,h}{r^2} \\
\therefore h &= \dfrac{r^2 \omega^2}{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Substituting $\omega = 2\pi N/60$

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \dfrac{r^2}{g} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2 \\
  &= \dfrac{r^2 \cdot 4\pi^2 N^2}{g \cdot 3600} \\
  &= \dfrac{4\pi^2}{9.81 \cdot 3600} \cdot r^2 N^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluating the constant $\dfrac{4\pi^2}{9.81 \times 3600}$:

$$
\dfrac{4 \times 9.8696}{9.81 \times 3600} = \dfrac{39.4784}{35316} \approx 1.1179 \times 10^{-3}
$$

For KTU numericals, the equivalent in terms of $h$ directly is:

$$
h \text{ (metres)} = \dfrac{0.00111 \cdot r^2 \cdot N^2}{1} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad N^2 = \dfrac{895}{h}
$$

where the constant 895 comes from $\dfrac{g \cdot 3600}{4\pi^2} \approx 894.7$ (rounded to 895 for KTU board numericals).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Examiner Tip:** When the problem says "in the approximate form," use $N^2 = 895/h$. When it says "derive exactly," show $\omega^2 = gh/r^2$ first and then substitute.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Equilibrium Speed of a Porter Governor

The Porter governor is a Watt governor with an additional **central dead weight** $W_c = m_c g$ placed on the sleeve. This central weight is supported by the lower links of length $q$.

### 3.2.1 Free-Body of the Sleeve

Forces on the sleeve:
- Central weight $W_c$ acting downward.
- Tension $T_1$ in each upper link acting upward along the link.

Vertical equilibrium of sleeve:

$$
2 T_1 \cos\phi = W_c
$$

where $\phi$ is the angle of the lower link with the vertical. For small angles, $\cos\phi \approx 1$, and we get $T_1 = W_c/2$.

### 3.2.2 Free-Body of One Ball

Forces on each ball (using the same resolution as the Watt governor):
- Centrifugal force $F_c = m r \omega^2$ outward.
- Weight $W = mg$ downward.
- Tension $T_1$ in the lower link (pulling down and inward) — vertical component = $T_1 \cos\phi \approx T_1$.

Vertical equilibrium of the ball:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 + W &= F_c \cos\theta \quad \text{(in the small-angle limit, } \cos\theta \approx 1) \\
\dfrac{W_c}{2} + W &= m r \omega^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Substituting $r = h \tan\theta$ and $\omega = 2\pi N/60$

After algebraic manipulation (shown in full):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m r \omega^2 &= W + \dfrac{W_c}{2} \\
m h \tan\theta \cdot \dfrac{4\pi^2 N^2}{3600} &= mg + \dfrac{m_c g}{2} \\
h N^2 \tan\theta &= \dfrac{895}{m} \left(m + \dfrac{m_c}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a *proportional Porter governor* where $k = q/r$ (ratio of lower to upper link), the final KTU form is:

$$
\boxed{N^2 = \dfrac{895 \, (1 + 2k^2)}{h \, (1 + k^2)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** The factor $(1 + 2k^2)/(1 + k^2)$ reduces to **1** when $k = 0$ (no lower link → reverts to Watt governor). When $k = 1$ (square configuration), the factor = **3/2**.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Hartnell Governor Sleeve Force Analysis

The Hartnell governor uses a **bell-crank lever** to amplify the small radial motion of the balls into a larger axial motion of the sleeve. A spring opposes the centrifugal force.

### 3.3.1 Geometry

- Ball radius of rotation: $r$
- Distance from bell-crank pivot to ball connection: $a$
- Distance from bell-crank pivot to sleeve connection: $b$
- Initial sleeve position: $h_1$, final position: $h_2$
- Sleeve lift: $h_2 - h_1 = x$ (small, taken positive downward)

### 3.3.2 Force Balance on the Bell-Crank

Taking moments about the pivot point of the bell-crank:

$$
F_c \cdot a = F_s \cdot b
$$

Therefore the spring force is:

$$
F_s = F_c \cdot \dfrac{a}{b}
$$

### 3.3.3 Initial and Final Equilibrium

At minimum equilibrium speed $N_1$ (radius $r_1$, sleeve at $h_1$):

$$
F_{c1} = m r_1 \omega_1^2 = \dfrac{F_{s1} \cdot b}{a}
$$

At maximum equilibrium speed $N_2$ (radius $r_2$, sleeve at $h_2$):

$$
F_{c2} = m r_2 \omega_2^2 = \dfrac{F_{s2} \cdot b}{a}
$$

### 3.3.4 Sleeve Lift Relation

From the lever geometry:

$$
\dfrac{r_1 - r_0}{a} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_0}{b} \quad \text{and} \quad \dfrac{r_2 - r_0}{a} = \dfrac{h_2 - h_0}{b}
$$

Subtracting the two and solving for the lift $h_2 - h_1$:

$$
h_2 - h_1 = \dfrac{b}{a} (r_2 - r_1)
$$

### 3.3.5 The Hartnell Speed Equation

The most important KTU result — combining the above:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{N_2^2 - N_1^2}{N_1^2} = \dfrac{F_{s2} - F_{s1}}{F_{c1} - F_{s1}}}
$$

This linear-in-$N^2$ relation is **the** Hartnell governor equation that KTU 2024 expects in numericals and derivations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The right-hand side is a ratio of *spring force increment* to *available centrifugal force margin*. A stiff spring (large $F_{s2} - F_{s1}$) gives a wide speed range $N_2 - N_1$ — this is the **sensitivity knob** of the governor.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Sensitivity and Stability

### 3.4.1 Definition

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{N_2 - N_1}{N_m} = \dfrac{2(N_2 - N_1)}{N_1 + N_2}
$$

### 3.4.2 Expressing Sensitivity in Terms of Heights

Using the simple Watt governor approximation $N^2 = 895/h$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1^2 &= 895 / h_1, \quad N_2^2 = 895 / h_2 \\
N_1 &= \sqrt{895/h_1}, \quad N_2 = \sqrt{895/h_2} \\
\sigma &= \dfrac{2(\sqrt{895/h_2} - \sqrt{895/h_1})}{\sqrt{895/h_2} + \sqrt{895/h_1}} \\
       &= \dfrac{2(\sqrt{h_1} - \sqrt{h_2})}{\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2}} \\
       &= \dfrac{2(\sqrt{h_1} - \sqrt{h_2})(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2})}{(\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2})^2} \\
       &= \dfrac{2(h_1 - h_2)}{h_1 + h_2 + 2\sqrt{h_1 h_2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the small-deflection case, the term $2\sqrt{h_1 h_2} \approx h_1 + h_2$ (geometric mean ≈ arithmetic mean when $h_1 \approx h_2$), giving the KTU-approximate form:

$$
\boxed{\sigma \approx \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 + h_2}}
$$

### 3.4.3 Stability Condition

- **Stable governor:** $\sigma > 0$ and small (typically 0.02 to 0.05).
- **Isochronous governor:** $\sigma = 0$ (theoretically possible only if the spring characteristic exactly matches $N^2$ behaviour). In practice, the Hartnell governor is *nearly* isochronous.
- **Unstable / Hunting governor:** $\sigma < 0$ → the speed oscillates around the set value. KTU 2024 typically asks to identify the *stable operating region* on a plot of $N$ vs. $h$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Learning Mandate:** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes graphical understanding. The following Mermaid block diagrams map the *functional architecture* of the two governor families. They are designed to render reliably on every KTU-affiliated learning platform.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Centrifugal (Hartnell) Governor Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Load Variation] --> B{Speed Sensor: Flyweights}
    B -- Spinning Flyballs --> C[Centrifugal Force F equals m times r times omega squared]
    C --> D[Bell Crank Lever Rotation]
    D --> E[Sleeve Vertical Translation]
    E --> F[Fuel Pump Rack Movement]
    F --> G[Reduced Fuel Injection]
    G --> H[Engine Speed Returns to Set Value]
    H -.Feedback.-> B
    I[Spring Preload S1] --- D
    J[Spring Final Load S2] --- E
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Starting from any load change at node `A`, the speed information flows clockwise through nodes `B` to `H`. The spring forces (nodes `I` and `J`) act as restoring elements parallel to the bell-crank. The dashed line `H to B` is the closed-loop feedback that gives the system its self-regulating property.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Vacuum Governor (Solex-type Carburetor)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Throttle Plate Angle Change] --> L[Manifold Vacuum Variation]
    L --> M[Diaphragm Chamber Pressure Drop]
    M --> N[Diaphragm Deflection Against Spring]
    N --> O[Throttle Linkage Actuation]
    O --> P[Throttle Plate Reopens Slightly]
    P --> Q[Manifold Vacuum Stabilises]
    Q -.Negative Feedback.-> L
    R[Atmospheric Pressure on Top of Diaphragm] --- M
    S[Spring Stiffness k] --- N
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The vacuum governor is a *pneumatic negative feedback loop*. The atmospheric pressure reference at node `R` and the spring at node `S` provide the restoring force. The dashed feedback line `Q to L` ensures the manifold vacuum settles to a design value.

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Centrifugal vs. Vacuum Governor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CENTRIFUGAL
        direction TB
        C1[Rotational Speed N in rpm] --> C2[Mechanical Mass: Flyweights]
        C2 --> C3[Centrifugal Force Fc]
        C3 --> C4[Bell Crank and Sleeve]
        C4 --> C5[Fuel Rack Actuation]
    end
    subgraph VACUUM
        direction TB
        V1[Engine Speed N in rpm] --> V2[Throttle Butterfly Angle]
        V2 --> V3[Manifold Vacuum in mmHg]
        V3 --> V4[Diaphragm Deflection]
        V4 --> V5[Throttle Shaft Rotation]
    end
    X[Common Input: Engine Load Change] --> CENTRIFUGAL
    X --> VACUUM
    CENTRIFUGAL --> Z[Controlled Output: Fuel Quantity]
    VACUUM --> Z
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Both governor families take the *same physical input* (load change → engine speed deviation) but use **different sensing principles** — mechanical centrifugal force versus pneumatic manifold vacuum — to arrive at the *same controlled output* (fuel quantity modulation).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw a block diagram of a governor control system," examiners expect a closed loop with a clearly labelled *feedback path*. The two sub-graphs above (CENTRIFUGAL and VACUUM) are the gold-standard structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):**
> - Part A (each 3 marks): direct conceptual / definitional / short derivation.
> - Part B (each 14 marks): full-length analytical question with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, *internal choice* between two distinct question stems.
> - The model answers below are written to capture **full 14 marks**.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]`

**Define a centrifugal governor. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a Hartnell governor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **centrifugal governor** is a speed-sensitive device that uses the centrifugal force generated by rotating masses to actuate a fuel control member, maintaining the engine speed at a predetermined value.
>
> In a **Hartnell governor**, two flyweights are mounted on bell-crank levers pivoted to the rotating spindle. As engine speed rises, the flyweights swing outward, rotating the bell cranks and lifting the sleeve against a spring. The sleeve is connected to the fuel pump control rack, reducing fuel delivery and thereby limiting the speed rise. When the load increases, the reverse sequence restores the fuel supply.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for working, 1 mark for sketch description.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between a centrifugal governor and a vacuum governor. State two applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Examiner's Tabular Format:** For full marks, present the answer in a table — examiners explicitly prefer tabular answers for "differentiate" questions.

| Parameter | Centrifugal Governor | Vacuum Governor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensing element | Rotating flyweights | Spring-loaded diaphragm |
| Sensing quantity | Centrifugal force ($m r \omega^2$) | Manifold vacuum (mmHg) |
| Controlled output | Fuel pump rack position | Throttle plate angle |
| Applications | Diesel fuel injection pumps (Bosch P, M, A series); gas-turbine overspeed trips | Carbureted petrol engines (Solex, Zenith, SU carburetors); small two-stroke engines (mopeds, gensets) |
| Speed range | Moderate (250–1500 rpm typical) | Wide (600–4500 rpm) |

*(Allocation: 2 marks for differentiation, 1 mark for applications.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option I `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]` — **CENTRIFUGAL GOVERNOR (Hartnell)**

**A Hartnell governor has a mean equilibrium speed of 300 rpm. The ball mass is 3 kg. The initial and final radii of rotation are 100 mm and 130 mm respectively. The initial spring force is 250 N and the spring stiffness is 25 N/mm. The arms of the bell-crank lever are 80 mm and 30 mm. Determine:**

**(a)** The final spring force and the range of speed for a sleeve lift of 20 mm.
**(b)** The sensitivity of the governor.

#### Part (a) — Final Spring Force and Speed Range (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- $N_m = 300$ rpm
- $m = 3$ kg
- $r_1 = 100$ mm = 0.1 m
- $r_2 = 130$ mm = 0.13 m
- $F_{s1} = 250$ N
- $k_{\text{spring}} = 25$ N/mm
- Lever arms: $a = 80$ mm (to ball), $b = 30$ mm (to sleeve)
- Sleeve lift $x = h_2 - h_1 = 20$ mm

**Step 1: Find the initial centrifugal force at $N_1$.**

We are given the *mean* speed, not the minimum. For the first iteration, use the mean speed to estimate $N_1$:

$$
\omega_m = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute initial centrifugal force $F_{c1}$ at $r_1$ and the spring force on the bell-crank.**

The spring force acting on the ball-side of the lever is:

$$
F_{c1,\text{ball}} = F_{s1} \cdot \dfrac{b}{a} = 250 \times \dfrac{30}{80} = 250 \times 0.375 = 93.75 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Find $N_1$ from the ball force balance:**

$$
F_{c1,\text{ball}} = m \, r_1 \, \omega_1^2
$$

$$
93.75 = 3 \times 0.1 \times \omega_1^2 \implies \omega_1^2 = \dfrac{93.75}{0.3} = 312.5
$$

$$
\omega_1 = 17.677 \text{ rad/s} \implies N_1 = \dfrac{\omega_1 \times 60}{2\pi} = \dfrac{17.677 \times 60}{6.2832} = 168.8 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 4: Find the final spring force $F_{s2}$.**

Additional spring compression = $x = 20$ mm → additional force:

$$
\Delta F_s = k_{\text{spring}} \times x = 25 \times 20 = 500 \text{ N}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_{s2} = F_{s1} + \Delta F_s = 250 + 500 = 750 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5: Find the ball-side final force and the final speed $N_2$.**

$$
F_{c2,\text{ball}} = F_{s2} \cdot \dfrac{b}{a} = 750 \times \dfrac{30}{80} = 281.25 \text{ N}
$$

$$
281.25 = 3 \times 0.13 \times \omega_2^2 \implies \omega_2^2 = \dfrac{281.25}{0.39} = 721.15
$$

$$
\omega_2 = 26.85 \text{ rad/s} \implies N_2 = \dfrac{26.85 \times 60}{2\pi} = 256.4 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 6: Speed range.**

$$
\Delta N = N_2 - N_1 = 256.4 - 168.8 = 87.6 \text{ rpm}
$$

> **Final Answer (a):** $F_{s2} = \mathbf{750 \text{ N}}$, Speed range = **87.6 rpm** (i.e., 168.8 to 256.4 rpm).

---

#### Part (b) — Sensitivity of the Governor (7 marks)

**Step 1: Use the sensitivity definition.**

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{N_2 - N_1}{N_m} = \dfrac{87.6}{300} = 0.292
$$

**Step 2: Convert to percentage.**

$$
\sigma = 0.292 \times 100\% = 29.2\%
$$

**Step 3: Stability comment.**

Since $\sigma > 0$, the governor is **stable**. However, $\sigma = 29.2\%$ is **excessively high** — a KTU 2024 well-designed governor should have $\sigma$ between 2 % and 5 %. The student should suggest:

- Reduce the sleeve lift $x$, or
- Increase the spring stiffness, or
- Use a stiffer bell-crank lever ratio.

> **Final Answer (b):** $\sigma = \mathbf{0.292}$ (or **29.2 %**). The governor is stable but poorly designed; sensitivity should be reduced.

---

### Question B — Option II `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]` — **VACUUM GOVERNOR**

**Explain with a neat sketch the working of a vacuum governor used in a Solex-type carburetor. How does it maintain a constant engine speed under varying load? Compare its performance with a centrifugal governor.**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Sketch (7 marks)

**Working Principle:**

The vacuum governor, also called a **pneumatic governor**, is fitted on the inlet manifold side of a carburetor (commonly the Solex type used in older Indian cars such as the Maruti 800 and Hindustan Ambassador). It consists of:

1. A **spring-loaded diaphragm** housed in a vacuum chamber.
2. A **linkage** connecting the diaphragm stem to the throttle shaft.
3. The **top side** of the diaphragm is exposed to atmospheric pressure.
4. The **bottom side** is connected via a small port to the engine's inlet manifold.

**Sequence of Operation:**

1. When the engine load suddenly **decreases** (e.g., during downshift or hill descent), the throttle tends to close, the engine manifold vacuum **rises** sharply.
2. The high vacuum pulls the diaphragm **upward** (against the spring).
3. The diaphragm stem, through the linkage, **rotates the throttle shaft** to **open** the throttle slightly.
4. The slight throttle opening admits more air-fuel mixture, manifold vacuum **drops** back to the design value, the diaphragm returns, and equilibrium is re-established.
5. The reverse sequence occurs on a load **increase**.

**Sketch (described in words, since Mermaid cannot draw a physical carburetor):**

> The vacuum chamber is a sealed cylindrical casing with the diaphragm clamped horizontally in the middle. The top half has a small vent to atmosphere. The bottom half has a vacuum port connected to the inlet manifold. A coiled return spring sits above the diaphragm. The diaphragm stem protrudes upward and connects via a bell-crank to the throttle spindle.

*(Allocation: 4 marks for description, 3 marks for sketch.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Constant-Speed Behaviour & Comparison (7 marks)

**How it maintains constant speed:**

The vacuum governor functions as a **closed-loop negative feedback controller**. The *controlled variable* is the manifold vacuum (proportional to engine load), the *sensor* is the diaphragm, the *actuator* is the throttle shaft, and the *reference* is the spring preload. By continuously balancing the manifold vacuum against the spring force, the throttle is held in a position that produces a near-constant air-fuel delivery for a given pedal position.

**Comparison with a Centrifugal Governor:**

| Feature | Vacuum Governor | Centrifugal Governor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensing element | Diaphragm (pressure sensor) | Flyweights (mechanical sensor) |
| Signal | Pneumatic (vacuum) | Mechanical (centrifugal force) |
| Speed range | Wide (idle to redline) | Narrow to moderate |
| Accuracy | Moderate (±5 %) | High (±1–2 %) |
| Wear | Diaphragm fatigue | Bearing wear on flyweights |
| Application | Petrol carburetors | Diesel injection pumps |
| Cost | Low | Moderate |
| Response time | Slower (pneumatic lag) | Faster (direct mechanical link) |

*(Allocation: 3 marks for constant-speed explanation, 4 marks for comparison table.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Governor Questions (KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing mm and metres for the radius. KTU examiners strictly compare numerical values — if $r = 100$ mm is used in a formula expecting metres, **2 marks are deducted silently**.
> 2. **Forgetting the lever-arm ratio $b/a$:** In Hartnell problems, many students directly equate $F_c = F_s$ instead of $F_c \cdot a = F_s \cdot b$. This is the **single largest source of 7-mark losses**.
> 3. **Confusing mean speed and minimum speed:** When the problem states "mean equilibrium speed = 300 rpm," you must derive $N_1$ and $N_2$ from the force balance — do **not** assume $N_1 = 300$ rpm.
> 4. **Sensitivity direction error:** $\sigma$ is always computed as $(N_2 - N_1)/N_m$. A negative value of $\sigma$ is *not* an error — it just means the governor is hunting. Mark this clearly.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch:** A "with neat sketch" question without a sketch loses **at least 2 marks** even if the derivation is perfect.
> 6. **Vacuum governor units:** Always quote vacuum in **mmHg** or **kPa**, not in bar. KTU examiners mark this strictly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin this for the last 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition:** A governor is an automatic speed-regulating device — *centrifugal* uses rotating masses, *vacuum* uses manifold vacuum.
- **Core equation (centrifugal):** $F_c = m r \omega^2$ with $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
- **Watt governor height:** $h = r^2 \omega^2 / g \implies N^2 \approx 895/h$ (metres, rpm).
- **Porter governor:** adds central dead weight — formula $N^2 = 895(1 + 2k^2) / [h(1 + k^2)]$.
- **Hartnell governor:** uses bell-crank + spring — sleeve lift $x = (b/a)(r_2 - r_1)$, and the linear-in-$N^2$ relation $\dfrac{N_2^2 - N_1^2}{N_1^2} = \dfrac{F_{s2} - F_{s1}}{F_{c1} - F_{s1}}$.
- **Sensitivity:** $\sigma = (N_2 - N_1)/N_m = 2(h_1 - h_2)/(h_1 + h_2)$ (approximate).
- **Stability:** $\sigma > 0$ and small ($\le 5\%$) is the design target.
- **Hunting:** $\sigma < 0$ → self-oscillation around the set speed.
- **Isochronism:** $\sigma = 0$ → speed-independent equilibrium (theoretical Hartnell limit).
- **Vacuum governor:** diaphragm senses manifold vacuum; throttle is actuated pneumatically.
- **Effort of governor:** $F_e = (W + W_c/2) \tan\theta$ — appears in 3-mark Part A questions.
- **Power of governor:** $P = T \omega$ — required to overcome governor friction.
- **Standard constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s²; $N^2 \approx 895/h$ constant; $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
- **KTU 2024 hot topics (most likely in ESE):** Hartnell governor derivation, sensitivity calculation, comparison between centrifugal and vacuum governors, vacuum governor sketch, hunting & stability discussion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cold Starting Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cold Starting Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# COLD STARTING DEVICES — MODULE 2

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Starting Devices** are auxiliary engine subsystems designed to overcome the adverse effects of low ambient temperature on fuel atomization, vaporization, and combustion initiation. They enrich the air–fuel mixture, pre-heat the induction air or combustion chamber, and/or reduce cranking resistance to enable reliable engine starting below **5 °C** ambient conditions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, cold starting devices fall under the auxiliary systems module because they **do not contribute to steady-state engine operation** but are indispensable for transient cold-crank conditions. They are broadly classified into:

1. **Mixture-Enrichment Devices** — used in SI (Spark Ignition) engines.
2. **Thermal-Assist Devices** — used in CI (Compression Ignition) engines.
3. **Cranking-Aid Devices** — applicable to both engine types.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cold Inhaler"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine waking up on a freezing winter morning and trying to drink a thick milkshake through a thin straw. The liquid is too cold, too viscous, and simply refuses to flow smoothly. Now imagine a friend warming the milkshake (glow plug) and handing you a wider straw (choke). Both actions make the job easier. **Cold starting devices do exactly this for the engine — they warm the fuel, widen the air path, and enrich the mixture so combustion can "breathe" into life.**

### 1.3 Why Cold Starting Is Problematic

When ambient temperature drops, four physical phenomena conspire against easy starting:

| Phenomenon | Effect on Engine |
|---|---|
| Reduced fuel volatility | Only **20–40 %** of gasoline vaporizes at **0 °C** vs. **60–70 %** at **20 °C** |
| Increased cranking resistance | Oil viscosity at **−20 °C** is **30–50×** higher than at **80 °C** |
| Reduced battery capacity | Output drops to **~40 %** at **−20 °C** (Arrhenius effect) |
| Poor mixture formation | Wall-quenching and condensation in cold manifold starve the cylinder |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** For an SI engine to fire, the mixture strength must lie between the *flammability limits* — roughly **λ = 0.4 to λ = 1.4** (equivalence ratio). Cold ambient temperature shrinks the practical operating window, demanding deliberate enrichment to **λ ≈ 0.6–0.8** (rich mixture).

### 1.4 Design Objectives of a Cold Start System

A well-designed cold start device must satisfy:

1. **Effective enrichment** of the air–fuel mixture to compensate for poor vaporization.
2. **Rapid activation** — must engage within **1–3 seconds** of key-on.
3. **Self-disengagement** as engine warms to operating temperature (**~80 °C** coolant).
4. **No interference** with emissions control devices (catalyst, EGR).
5. **Minimal current draw** to preserve battery cranking capacity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cold Starting Devices (Detailed)

### A. Spark Ignition (SI) Engine Cold Start Devices

#### 2.1.1 Manual Choke
- A butterfly valve mounted upstream of the carburetor venturi.
- Pulling the choke knob rotates the butterfly toward the closed position, **restricting airflow by 60–80 %**.
- With fuel flow held nearly constant (vacuum drop increases), the **A/F ratio drops to 8:1 – 10:1** (rich).
- Operated via cable from the dashboard.

#### 2.1.2 Automatic Choke
- A bimetallic spring (or wax-pellet element) senses coolant temperature.
- At **T < 20 °C**, the spring keeps the choke plate closed.
- An electric heating element (or engine vacuum diaphragm) progressively opens the choke as the engine warms.
- **KTU exam point:** Automatic choke eliminates driver error and improves cold-start emissions.

#### 2.1.3 Auxiliary Air Regulator (Auxiliary Air Device — AAD / "Aux-Air Valve")
