- A bypass air passage around the closed throttle plate, controlled by a **bi-metallic spring** or **wax element**.
- Provides additional air during cold idle to raise idle speed to **1200–1500 rpm** (vs. normal **700–800 rpm**).
- Closes progressively as engine temperature rises.

#### 2.1.4 Cold Start Injector (Electronic Fuel Injection Systems)
- An additional electromagnetic injector mounted upstream of the intake manifold.
- Activated for **1–8 seconds** by an electronic control unit (ECU) signal triggered by the **thermo-time switch**.
- Injects **5–30 mL** of supplementary fuel directly into the manifold.
- The **thermo-time switch** is a bimetallic device that interrupts injection if coolant **T > 35 °C** or after a maximum time limit to prevent flooding.

#### 2.1.5 Fast-Idle Cam / Fast-Idle Mechanism
- Linked to the choke shaft; opens the throttle slightly during cold operation.
- Engine runs at elevated RPM to generate more heat quickly.

---

### B. Compression Ignition (CI) Engine Cold Start Devices

#### 2.2.1 Glow Plugs (Sheathed-Type)
- A **heater element** (coil of nickel-chromium or platinum wire) enclosed in a metal sheath filled with **magnesium oxide (MgO)** insulator.
- Energized by the battery for **5–15 seconds** before cranking.
- Tip temperature reaches **850–1100 °C** within **5 s**.
- **KTU exam point:** Modern "self-regulating" (SR) glow plugs use a **positive temperature coefficient (PTC)** element that limits max temperature automatically.

#### 2.2.2 Sheath-Type vs. Rod-Type Glow Plugs

| Parameter | Sheath Type | Rod Type |
|---|---|---|
| Heat-up time | **4–6 s** | **8–12 s** |
| Tip temperature | **1000–1100 °C** | **800–900 °C** |
| Durability | High | Moderate |
| Modern use | Common-rail, IDI, DI | Older IDI engines |

#### 2.2.3 Intake Manifold / Grid Heater
- An electrically heated grid mounted in the intake manifold.
- Air passing through is warmed by **80–150 °C** before entering cylinders.
- More common in larger commercial-vehicle diesel engines.

#### 2.2.4 Flame-Start System
- A small fuel injector sprays diesel onto a glow element located in the intake manifold.
- The fuel ignites on the hot element, creating a flame jet that preheats the induction air.
- Used in heavy-duty diesel engines (Mercedes-Benz, MAN).

#### 2.2.5 Ether / Starting Fluid Injection
- Pressurized canister of **diethyl ether (C₂H₅OC₂H₅)** with very low auto-ignition temperature (**160 °C** vs. diesel **250 °C**).
- Injected into the intake manifold during cranking.
- Highly volatile, low cetane requirement → ignites readily on compression.
- **Caution:** Ether has poor lubricity and can damage pistons, rings, and bearings with prolonged use.

#### 2.2.6 Block Heater / Coolant Heater
- An electric resistance heater installed in a coolant passage or against the engine block.
- Plugged into mains power (110 V / 230 V AC) for **2–4 hours** before starting.
- Keeps engine temperature above **30 °C**, eliminating the need for any in-cylinder assist.

---

### C. Universal (Both SI and CI) Cranking Aids

- **Battery Warmers** — Insulated blankets with heating elements.
- **Oil Dilution / Synthetic Low-Viscosity Oils** (0W-30, 0W-40).
- **Crankcase Ventilation Heaters.**
- **Hydraulic Lash Adjuster Pre-lubrication (auto-start-stop vehicles).**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key formulas for cold-starting calculations — save this table.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A/F_{\text{stoich}} = 14.7$ | Stoichiometric A/F for gasoline (C₈H₁₈) | ratio |
| 2 | $A/F_{\text{cold}} = 8 \text{ to } 12$ | Cold-start enrichment ratio | ratio |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{(F/A)_{\text{actual}}}{(F/A)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Equivalence ratio (alternate) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $T_{\text{tip}} = T_0 + \dfrac{V^2 \, t}{m \, c_p \, R}$ | Glow plug tip temperature (transient) | °C or K |
| 6 | $P_{\text{heater}} = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | Electrical power dissipation in glow plug | W |
| 7 | $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Heat required to warm air charge | J |
| 8 | $P_{\text{crank}} = \dfrac{2 \pi \, N \, T}{60}$ | Cranking power requirement | W |
| 9 | $T_{\text{crank}} = T_0 \, \left(\dfrac{\eta_v}{\eta_{v,0}}\right) \, e^{k \Delta T}$ | Cold-crank torque degradation factor | N·m |
| 10 | $\eta_v = \dfrac{m_a}{\rho_a \, V_d}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless |
| 11 | $C_{\text{bat}}(T) = C_0 \left[1 - \alpha (T_0 - T)\right]$ | Battery capacity vs. temperature | Ah |
| 12 | $t_{\text{inject}} = K \cdot e^{-\beta T}$ | Cold-start injector pulse width (empirical) | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Formula 1–4 are used in emission and mixture-quality studies. Formula 5–6 are essential for glow-plug thermal design. Formula 11 (battery temperature model) is heavily tested in KTU ESE and GATE examinations.

---

## 2.3 Operational Sequence of a Typical Cold Start (CI Diesel with Glow Plugs)

1. **Key ON** (position II): Glow plug relay closes, current (**6–14 A** per plug) flows through heater coils.
2. **Wait Indicator** on dashboard illuminates; pulses until tips reach **≥ 850 °C** (typically **3–8 s**).
3. **Crank** (position III): Starter motor engages, pistons compress air to **30–35 bar** and **700–900 °C**.
4. **Combustion** initiates on fuel injected onto the hot glow plug sheath; pre-chamber or direct-injection flame propagates.
5. **Post-Start Heating:** Glow plugs remain energized for **30–120 s** after start to stabilize idle and reduce white smoke (unburnt fuel).
6. **Coolant Reaches 75–85 °C:** ECU de-energizes glow plug relay; system goes dormant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cold-Start Fuel Enrichment Requirement

### Problem Setup
A 4-cylinder SI engine has a displacement of **1.6 L** and a stoichiometric requirement of **A/F = 14.7**. At **0 °C**, only **40 %** of the injected fuel vaporizes before the intake valve. The cylinder needs at least **70 %** of the fuel in vapor form to fire reliably. Calculate the cold-start equivalence ratio and the **required cold-start A/F** if the maximum usable vapor fraction is **70 %**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Define vapor fraction requirement**

Let $f_v$ = vapor fraction at 0 °C = 0.40 (40 %).

Required vapor fraction for firing: $f_{v,\text{req}}$ = 0.70 (70 %).

**Step 2: Mass balance on fuel**

$$
m_{\text{vap,actual}} = f_v \cdot m_f
$$

$$
m_{\text{vap,required}} = f_{v,\text{req}} \cdot m_{f,\text{cold}}
$$

For successful firing, the *vapor mass entering the cylinder* from stoichiometric fuel must be the same as the vapor mass from the enriched cold fuel. Therefore:

$$
f_v \cdot m_f = f_{v,\text{req}} \cdot m_{f,\text{cold}}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for cold-start fuel mass ratio**

$$
m_{f,\text{cold}} = \dfrac{f_v}{f_{v,\text{req}}} \cdot m_f
$$

$$
m_{f,\text{cold}} = \dfrac{0.40}{0.70} \cdot m_f = 0.5714 \, m_f
$$

Wait — this says we need *less* fuel. The logic is inverted. The correct interpretation: At 0 °C, the same amount of *liquid* fuel must be sprayed, but only 40 % vaporizes. So we need to inject **more liquid fuel** so that 70 % of a *larger* liquid pool still gives us enough vapor:

$$
f_v \cdot m_{f,\text{cold}} = f_{v,\text{req}} \cdot m_{f,\text{stoich}}
$$

$$
m_{f,\text{cold}} = \dfrac{f_{v,\text{req}}}{f_v} \cdot m_{f,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{0.70}{0.40} \cdot m_{f,\text{stoich}}
$$

$$
m_{f,\text{cold}} = 1.75 \, m_{f,\text{stoich}}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the cold-start A/F ratio**

Since air mass $m_a$ is unchanged, the new ratio is:

$$
(A/F)_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{m_a}{1.75 \, m_{f,\text{stoich}}} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{1.75} = \dfrac{14.7}{1.75}
$$

$$
(A/F)_{\text{cold}} = 8.40
$$

**Step 5: Equivalence ratio**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{cold}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}} = \dfrac{8.40}{14.7} = 0.571
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A cold start at 0 °C requires the mixture to be enriched to **A/F ≈ 8.4:1**, corresponding to **λ ≈ 0.57** (rich mixture). This matches the empirical KTU/SAE value of 8:1 to 10:1 cited in textbooks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Glow Plug Tip Temperature vs. Time

### Problem Setup
A sheathed glow plug has the following specifications:
- Heating coil resistance: **R = 0.30 Ω**
- Battery voltage: **V = 12 V**
- Sheath mass: **m = 8.0 g = 0.008 kg**
- Specific heat of sheath steel: **c_p = 500 J/(kg·K)**
- Ambient temperature: **T₀ = 25 °C**
- Heat loss coefficient to intake air: **h = 0.15 W/K** (assumed constant)

Find the time required for the tip to reach **850 °C** and verify the energy balance.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute electrical power input**

$$
P = \dfrac{V^2}{R} = \dfrac{(12)^2}{0.30} = \dfrac{144}{0.30} = 480 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2: Energy balance on sheath (transient lumped model)**

The sheath gains heat from electrical input and loses heat by convection to intake air:

$$
m \, c_p \, \dfrac{dT}{dt} = P - h (T - T_0)
$$

**Step 3: Solve the first-order linear ODE**

Rearranging in standard form:

$$
\dfrac{dT}{dt} + \dfrac{h}{m \, c_p} T = \dfrac{P + h T_0}{m \, c_p}
$$

Let:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{m \, c_p}{h} = \dfrac{0.008 \times 500}{0.15} = \dfrac{4.0}{0.15} = 26.67 \text{ s}
$$

Steady-state temperature:

$$
T_\infty = T_0 + \dfrac{P}{h} = 25 + \dfrac{480}{0.15} = 25 + 3200 = 3225 \text{ °C}
$$

(Theoretical max — actual tip is limited by radiation losses and material melting.)

**Step 4: Apply the solution**

$$
T(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) \, e^{-t/\tau}
$$

**Step 5: Solve for time to reach 850 °C**

$$
850 = 3225 - (3225 - 25) \, e^{-t/26.67}
$$

$$
850 = 3225 - 3200 \, e^{-t/26.67}
$$

$$
3200 \, e^{-t/26.67} = 3225 - 850 = 2375
$$

$$
e^{-t/26.67} = \dfrac{2375}{3200} = 0.7422
$$

$$
-\dfrac{t}{26.67} = \ln(0.7422) = -0.2983
$$

$$
t = 0.2983 \times 26.67 = 7.95 \text{ s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The glow plug reaches **850 °C** in approximately **8 seconds**, which is consistent with manufacturer datasheet values of **5–10 s**. The simple lumped model is valid because the Biot number is small (internal conduction ≫ surface convection).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Device Selection by Engine Type and Temperature

| Temperature Range | SI Engine (Petrol) | CI Engine (Diesel) |
|---|---|---|
| **+20 to +40 °C** | No assist needed | No assist needed |
| **+5 to +20 °C** | Automatic choke / fast idle | Glow plug pre-heat **3 s** |
| **−10 to +5 °C** | Cold start injector (2–4 s pulse) | Glow plug **8 s** + post-heat **30 s** |
| **−20 to −10 °C** | Choke + cold start injector + crankcase heater | Glow plug **15 s** + grid heater + block heater |
| **< −20 °C** | Ether injection + battery warmer | Ether + block heater (4 h preheat) + 0W-30 oil |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Cold Start Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Key ON at Low Temperature] --> B{Engine Type}
    B -- SI / Petrol --> C[ECU Energizes Cold Start Injector]
    B -- CI / Diesel --> D[Energize Glow Plug Relay]
    C --> C1[Thermo-Time Switch Closed?]
    C1 -- Yes T less than 35C --> C2[Inject 5 to 30 mL Extra Fuel]
    C1 -- No --> C3[Skip Cold Start Injection]
    C2 --> C4[Crank Engine]
    C4 --> C5[Fast Idle Cam Maintains 1200 rpm]
    D --> D1[Current Flows Through PTC Element]
    D1 --> D2[Tip Reaches 850 C in 8 s]
    D2 --> D3[Wait Indicator OFF]
    D3 --> D4[Crank Engine]
    D4 --> D5[Post Start Heat 30 to 60 s]
    C3 --> C4
    C5 --> F[Engine Reaches 80 C]
    D5 --> F
    F --> G[System Deactivates]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block Architecture of Diesel Cold Start System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER[Power Supply]
        BATT[Battery 12V 60Ah]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Control Unit]
        ECU[Engine ECU]
        TSW[Thermo Time Switch]
        RELAY[Glow Plug Relay 70A]
    end
    subgraph PLUGS[Heating Elements]
        GP1[Glow Plug Cyl 1]
        GP2[Glow Plug Cyl 2]
        GP3[Glow Plug Cyl 3]
        GP4[Glow Plug Cyl 4]
    end
    subgraph SENSE[Sensors]
        CTEMP[Coolant Temp Sensor]
        IND[Dashboard Wait Indicator]
    end
    BATT --> RELAY
    ECU --> RELAY
    TSW --> ECU
    CTEMP --> ECU
    RELAY --> GP1
    RELAY --> GP2
    RELAY --> GP3
    RELAY --> GP4
    ECU --> IND
    CTEMP --> RELAY
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: SI Engine Cold Start Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INTAKE[Intake Manifold]
        AIR[Ambient Air]
        CHOKE[Choke Butterfly Valve]
        AUX[Aux Air Valve Bypass]
        THROT[Throttle Plate]
        VENTURI[Venturi]
    end
    subgraph FUEL[Fuel Delivery]
        PUMP[Fuel Pump]
        MAIN[Main Jet]
        CSI[Cold Start Injector]
    end
    AIR --> CHOKE --> THROT --> VENTURI
    CHOKE -.Restricted.-> VENTURI
    AUX -.Extra Air.-> THROT
    PUMP --> MAIN --> VENTURI
    PUMP --> CSI --> VENTURI
    TSW2[Thermo Time Switch] -.Control Signal.-> CSI
    BIMETAL[Bi-metallic Spring] -.Mechanical Link.-> CHOKE
    BIMETAL -.Mechanical Link.-> AUX
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the function of an automatic choke in an SI engine. How does it disengage as the engine warms up?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) An automatic choke is a device that **enriches the air–fuel mixture during cold starting** by restricting the air passage through a butterfly valve in the carburetor.
- (1 mark) It is operated by a **bi-metallic spring or wax-pellet element** that responds to coolant temperature. At low temperatures, the spring keeps the choke plate closed, providing a rich mixture.
- (1 mark) As engine coolant temperature rises above **20–25 °C**, the bimetallic strip twists (or wax element expands), gradually opening the choke plate to allow normal air–fuel ratio. The system becomes fully open at **~75 °C** coolant temperature.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why are glow plugs necessary in a diesel engine? Mention any two types.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1.5 marks) A diesel engine relies on **compression ignition**. In cold weather, the compressed air loses heat to the cold cylinder walls, and the temperature at TDC may fall below the **auto-ignition temperature of diesel (~250 °C)**. Glow plugs raise the in-cylinder local temperature to enable reliable ignition of the first few cycles.
- (0.75 marks) **Rod-type glow plug:** A simple exposed heating coil; slower heat-up.
- (0.75 marks) **Sheath-type glow plug:** Coil enclosed in a metal sheath filled with MgO insulator; faster, more durable, and commonly used in modern common-rail diesel engines.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** list devices without explaining the **operational principle** — students lose up to **4 marks** per sub-question for this.
> 2. **Always** include numerical values (temperatures, A/F ratios, current ratings) — generic descriptions fetch only 50 % credit.
> 3. **Draw a labelled sketch or block diagram** wherever asked — a missing diagram can cost **2–3 marks**.
> 4. For glow plug questions, **always state both the heating element AND the sheath material** — examiners specifically test this.
> 5. Do **not** confuse *cold start injector* (SI petrol) with *glow plug* (CI diesel) — this is the single most common KTU mistake.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Resit Pattern]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **sheath-type glow plug** used in a CI engine. Mention the materials used and the typical operating temperatures.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
1. **Central heating coil:** Made of **nickel–chromium (NiCr)** or **platinum (Pt)** resistance wire, wound in a helical shape.
2. **Insulating filler:** **Magnesium oxide (MgO)** powder, which is electrically insulating but thermally conductive. It transmits heat from coil to sheath.
3. **Metal sheath:** A closed-end tube made of **Inconel (Ni-Cr-Fe alloy)** or stainless steel, capable of withstanding **1100 °C** and corrosive combustion gases.
4. **Terminal post & connection stud:** Connects the coil to the battery via the glow plug relay.
5. **Sealing washer & body:** Provides gastight mounting in the cylinder head.

**Working (2.5 marks):**
1. When ignition is switched ON, the **glow plug relay** closes, applying **12 V DC** across the coil.
2. The current (**6–14 A** per plug) heats the coil to **1000–1100 °C** within **4–6 seconds**.
3. The hot sheath protrudes into the **pre-combustion chamber** (or the swirl chamber), raising the local air temperature to ensure diesel auto-ignition on the first compression stroke.
4. After engine start, the ECU keeps the relay closed for **30–120 seconds** of *post-heat* to reduce white smoke and stabilize idle.
5. When coolant temperature reaches **75–80 °C**, the relay opens and the glow plug is de-energized.

**Materials & Temperatures (1 mark):**
- Coil: NiCr (or Pt), MgO insulator, Inconel sheath.
- Operating tip temperature: **850–1100 °C**.
- Cold-start preheat time: **4–8 s**; post-heat: **30–120 s**.

**Diagram (essential — 0.5 mark reserved):** Sketch of the glow plug showing the four key parts above.

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Discuss the various **cold starting devices used in SI (petrol) engines** with emphasis on the **cold start injector** and **auxiliary air device**. Why is mixture enrichment critical at sub-zero temperatures?

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Reasons for enrichment (2 marks):**
1. **Reduced fuel volatility:** At **0 °C**, only **~40 %** of fuel vaporizes vs. **~65 %** at **20 °C**. A lean mixture will not ignite.
2. **Wall quenching:** Cold intake manifold walls cause fuel to condense, leaving the cylinder too lean.
3. **Lower battery voltage and slower cranking** reduce the air charge, demanding richer mixture.
4. Stoichiometric mixture is unfirable until **T > 5 °C**; cold engines need **A/F = 8:1 to 10:1** (λ ≈ 0.6–0.7).

**Cold Start Injector — Construction & Working (2.5 marks):**
1. It is an additional **electromagnetic solenoid injector** mounted upstream of the intake manifold (often near the throttle body).
2. On cold cranking, the ECU energizes it for **1–8 seconds** based on coolant temperature.
3. The **thermo-time switch** (a bimetallic thermal element) interrupts the signal if coolant > **35 °C** or after a **maximum duration** (to prevent flooding).
4. It injects **5–30 mL** of supplementary fuel directly into the air stream, ensuring a vapor-rich charge reaches each cylinder.
5. As coolant warms, the injection pulse width decreases progressively to zero.

**Auxiliary Air Device (Aux-Air Valve) (2 marks):**
1. A bypass air passage around the closed throttle plate.
2. Contains a **bi-metallic spring** or **wax-pellet** actuator.
3. At cold temperatures, the valve is open, allowing extra air to bypass the throttle — this raises idle speed to **1200–1500 rpm**.
4. The higher RPM **generates more combustion heat** and **warms the catalyst faster** (emissions benefit).
5. As engine warms, the bimetallic element gradually closes the bypass, reducing idle to normal **700–800 rpm**.

**Other SI cold-start devices (0.5 mark):**
- Manual/automatic choke, fast-idle cam, intake air heater (modern vehicles).

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **electronic cold-start injection system** used in modern multi-point fuel-injected (MPFI) petrol engines. Explain the role of the **thermo-time switch**.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
- Battery → Main Relay → ECU
- ECU receives inputs from: Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS), Crank Position Sensor, Throttle Position Sensor.
- ECU drives: Cold Start Injector (via dedicated driver transistor) and Fuel Pump Relay.
- Thermo-Time Switch wired in series with the cold start injector coil.
- The arrangement: Key ON → ECU checks CTS → if T < 35 °C and cranking detected → energize cold start injector.

**Working Sequence (3 marks):**
1. Driver turns key to START. ECU reads coolant temperature.
2. If **T < 35 °C** AND engine is cranking, ECU sends a **PWM signal** to the cold start injector.
3. The **thermo-time switch** is a bimetal-actuated switch, also sensing coolant temperature. It closes only if T < 35 °C. It is wired in **series** with the injector — acting as a redundant safety.
4. The injector delivers **5–30 mL** of additional fuel over a pulse width of **1–8 seconds**.
5. As the engine cranks, the ECU refines the pulse width based on cranking RPM and CTS feedback.

**Role of Thermo-Time Switch (1 mark):**
- Acts as a **redundant temperature-sensing safety** independent of the ECU.
- Prevents flooding by **limiting injection duration** (max **8 s**) and **cutting off at high temperature** (T > 35 °C).
- Provides a **fail-safe backup** if the ECU malfunctions.

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A 1.6 L, 4-cylinder petrol engine has a stoichiometric A/F ratio of 14.7. At an ambient temperature of **−10 °C**, only **30 %** of the injected fuel vaporizes. Determine:
   1. The **required cold-start A/F ratio** to ensure 70 % vapor delivery.
   2. The **equivalence ratio λ** for this cold-start mixture.
   3. The **percentage enrichment** over stoichiometric.
   4. Comment on whether a **choke** or a **cold start injector** is preferable at this temperature.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}$ = 14.7
- Vapor fraction at −10 °C: $f_v$ = 0.30
- Required vapor fraction: $f_{v,\text{req}}$ = 0.70
- Stoichiometric fuel mass: $m_{f,\text{stoich}}$
- Cold fuel mass: $m_{f,\text{cold}}$

**Part (1) — Required Cold-Start A/F (3 marks):**

Apply vapor mass balance:

$$
f_v \cdot m_{f,\text{cold}} = f_{v,\text{req}} \cdot m_{f,\text{stoich}}
$$

$$
0.30 \cdot m_{f,\text{cold}} = 0.70 \cdot m_{f,\text{stoich}}
$$

$$
m_{f,\text{cold}} = \dfrac{0.70}{0.30} \cdot m_{f,\text{stoich}} = 2.333 \, m_{f,\text{stoich}}
$$

Since air mass remains the same, the A/F ratio is inversely proportional to fuel mass:

$$
(A/F)_{\text{cold}} = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}{2.333} = \dfrac{14.7}{2.333} = 6.30
$$

$$
\boxed{(A/F)_{\text{cold}} = 6.30 : 1}
$$

**Part (2) — Equivalence Ratio λ (1.5 marks):**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{cold}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}} = \dfrac{6.30}{14.7} = 0.429
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda = 0.43}
$$

**Part (3) — Percentage Enrichment (1.5 marks):**

$$
\% \text{ Enrichment} = \left(\dfrac{m_{f,\text{stoich}} - m_{f,\text{cold}}}{m_{f,\text{stoich}}}\right) \times 100
$$

Wait — enrichment is *additional* fuel, so:

$$
\% \text{ Enrichment} = \left(\dfrac{m_{f,\text{cold}} - m_{f,\text{stoich}}}{m_{f,\text{stoich}}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
= (2.333 - 1) \times 100 = 133.3 \%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Enrichment} = 133.3 \%}
$$

**Part (4) — Device Selection (1 mark):**
- At **−10 °C**, the required A/F (6.3:1) is **below the practical range of a choke alone** (choke typically gives 8:1 to 10:1).
- Therefore, a **cold start injector** is preferred, **supplemented by an automatic choke** for additional enrichment. Pure choke use at this temperature risks **flooding, plug fouling, and excessive emissions** due to over-rich mixture beyond the lambda window.
- Modern MPFI systems use **electronic cold start injection with ECU-controlled pulse width**, which is the cleanest and most controllable approach.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist for last-minute KTU preparation.**

- ✅ Cold starting devices solve the **poor vaporization, high cranking torque, and reduced battery capacity** at low temperatures.
- ✅ SI engines use **choke, cold start injector, auxiliary air device, fast-idle cam**.
- ✅ CI engines use **glow plugs (sheath or rod type), grid heaters, ether injection, block heaters**.
- ✅ **Sheath-type glow plug** = NiCr/Pt coil + MgO insulator + Inconel sheath. Tip reaches **850–1100 °C** in **4–8 s**.
- ✅ **Cold start injector** delivers **5–30 mL** extra fuel, controlled by **ECU + thermo-time switch** in series.
- ✅ **Thermo-time switch** is a bimetallic safety device that opens at **T > 35 °C** or after **max 8 s**.
- ✅ **Auxiliary air device** raises cold idle to **1200–1500 rpm** for faster warm-up.
- ✅ Cold-start A/F ratio: **8:1 to 10:1** (mild cold) down to **6:1 to 8:1** (severe cold).
- ✅ Equivalence ratio λ: **0.4 to 0.7** during cold start (rich).
- ✅ **Ether** (auto-ignition T = 160 °C) is used in extreme cold for diesel engines, but damages lubrication.
- ✅ **Block heater** plugged to mains AC is the most reliable but slowest (2–4 h preheat).
- ✅ **PTC (positive temperature coefficient) glow plugs** are self-regulating and prevent overheating.
- ✅ Always relate numerical A/F, λ, and % enrichment calculations to the formula: $m_{f,\text{cold}} = (f_{v,\text{req}} / f_v) \times m_{f,\text{stoich}}$.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Do not interchange SI and CI cold-start devices — KTU examiners deduct marks for this.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turbocharging and Supercharging in IC Engines. Electronically Controlled Engines: MPFi Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Turbocharging and Supercharging in IC Engines. Electronically Controlled Engines: MPFi Engines ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Forced Induction — The Foundational Concept **Supercharging** is the process of supplying air (or air-fuel mixture) to an internal combustion engine at a pressure **greater than atmospheric pressure** (i.e., greater than **101.325 kPa** or **1.01325 bar**) by using an external compressor. This external compressor is mechanically driven by the engine itself, typically via a belt, gear, chain, or directly from the crankshaft. **Turbocharging** is a specific, highly efficient form of supercharging where the compressor is driven not by the engine's mechanical output, but by a turbine that harvests energy from the engine's **exhaust gas stream**. Because it recovers otherwise wasted exhaust energy, turbocharging is considered a form of **exhaust energy recovery** or **waste-gate turbocharging**. > [!NOTE] > **Strict Terminology (KTU Board Convention):** > - **Supercharger** → Compressor driven mechanically by the engine (parasitic load). > - **Turbocharger** → Compressor driven by an exhaust-driven turbine (energy recovered). > - **Forced Induction** = Umbrella term covering both. > - The pressurized intake charge is called the **Boost**, measured as **Boost Pressure** in bar or kPa. ### 1.2 Why Do We Need Forced Induction? — The Intuitive Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Blowing on a Fire" Analogy:** > Imagine you are trying to keep a campfire alive. When the fire is small, you blow on it gently. The stronger (and faster) you blow, the more vigorously the fire burns. A supercharger or turbocharger is essentially "blowing" compressed air into the engine cylinders — packing more oxygen molecules into the same cylinder volume. More oxygen + same amount of fuel = **more powerful combustion = more torque and power** from an engine of identical displacement. The power output of an engine is fundamentally proportional to the **mass of air inducted per cycle**: $$P_{engine} \propto \dot{m}_{air} \propto \rho_{air} \times V_{displaced}$$ By increasing the **density (ρ)** of the intake charge through compression, we cram more air (and therefore more fuel energy) into the same displacement volume, yielding substantially more power from an engine of the same physical size. ### 1.3 Engine Power Without Forced Induction — The Ceiling A naturally aspirated engine loses volumetric efficiency as RPM rises due to: - Frictional pressure drops across the throttle valve - Short time available for cylinder filling - Heat transfer to the intake charge from hot cylinder walls > [!NOTE] > **Standard Benchmarks used in KTU Numerical Problems:** > - **Ambient Pressure, P₁ = 1.013 bar** (Sea-level standard) > - **Ambient Temperature, T₁ = 300 K** (27 °C) > - **Ratio of specific heats for air, γ = 1.4** > - **Specific heat at constant pressure, cp = 1.005 kJ/kg·K** > - **Gas constant for air, R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K** > - **Mechanical efficiency range, ηₘ = 0.80 – 0.90** for automotive engines ### 1.4 Definition of MPFi Engine **MPFi (Multi-Point Fuel Injection)** is an electronic fuel injection architecture in which **each engine cylinder is equipped with its own dedicated electromagnetic fuel injector** positioned directly upstream of the intake valve (in the intake port or manifold runner close to the valve head). The quantity of fuel injected is precisely controlled by the **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** through **pulse-width modulation (PWM)** of the injector solenoid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Distinction KTU Examiners Love to Test:** > - **SPFi (Single-Point Fuel Injection)** → ONE injector upstream of the throttle body (like a carburetor replacement). > - **MPFi (Multi-Point Fuel Injection)** → ONE injector PER cylinder, located at the intake port. > - **GDi (Gasoline Direct Injection)** → Injector sprays fuel DIRECTLY into the combustion chamber. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Justification for Forced Induction The **Indicated Power (IP)** of a 4-stroke engine is given by: $$IP = \frac{\eta_v \times \rho_a \times V_d \times N \times LCV}{\left(\frac{A}{F}\right) \times 2 \times 60} \quad \text{[kW]}$$ Where the air density $\rho_a$ at manifold conditions is: $$\rho_a = \frac{P_{man}}{R \times T_{man}}$$ By raising $P_{man}$ from 1.013 bar to (say) 1.8 bar through forced induction, the air density increases almost proportionally, and so does the mass of fuel that can be burned (limited by stoichiometry), thereby raising IP. ### 2.2 Thermodynamic Compression — The Core Physics For an **ideal adiabatic (isentropic) compression** of air from state 1 (ambient) to state 2 (boost pressure), the temperature rise is governed by: $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$$ The **work of compression per unit mass** required by the supercharger is: $$w_c = c_p (T_2 - T_1) = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right] \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ The **power required to drive the supercharger**: $$\boxed{\dot{W}_{sc} = \dot{m}_a \times w_c = \dot{m}_a \times c_p \times T_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]} \quad \text{[kW]}$$ ### 2.3 Volumetric Efficiency in Forced Induction Volumetric efficiency is redefined for forced induction as: $$\eta_v = \frac{\text{Actual mass of air inducted}}{\text{Mass of air at INLET (manifold) conditions filling displaced volume}}$$ For a supercharged engine, ηv is typically higher (often 1.0 to 1.15) because the higher manifold pressure actively pushes air in, overcoming throttling losses. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Mandatory Formula Table for Forced Induction Problems:** | # | Formula / Equation | Symbols & Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | T in K, P in bar or kPa, γ = 1.4 | Adiabatic temperature rise across compressor | | 2 | $w_c = c_p T_1 [(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$ | w in kJ/kg, cp = 1.005 kJ/kg·K | Compressor specific work | | 3 | $\dot{W}_{sc} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p T_1 [(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$ | Ẇ in kW, ṁ in kg/s | Power to drive supercharger | | 4 | $\rho_2/\rho_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{1/\gamma}$ | ρ in kg/m³ | Density ratio across compressor | | 5 | $P_{super}/P_{na} \approx (P_2/P_1)^{1/\gamma} \times (\eta_{v,super}/\eta_{v,na})$ | Power ratio dimensionless | Power augmentation ratio | | 6 | $BSFC_{ratio} = BSFC_{super}/BSFC_{na}$ | kg/kWh | Specific fuel consumption comparison | | 7 | $P_2 \cdot V_2^{\gamma} = P_1 \cdot V_1^{\gamma}$ | P in kPa, V in m³ | Isentropic relation | | 8 | $\dot{m}_a = \eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot N / (2 \times 60)$ | ṁ in kg/s | Air mass flow rate (4-stroke) | | 9 | $\eta_{mech} = IP/BP$ | dimensionless | Mechanical efficiency | | 10 | $t_{dwell} = \text{injector opening time (ms)}$ | time in ms | MPFi pulse width parameter | | 11 | $Q_{inj} = \dot{V}_{inj} \times t_{dwell} \times N_{inj}$ | Q in mm³/stroke | Injected fuel quantity per stroke | | 12 | $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7:1$ (gasoline) | dimensionless | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL KTU Mark-Saver Rule:** In all formula tables, never use the bare vertical pipe $\vert x \vert$ notation. Use the LaTeX command $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table renderer. This is a verified KTU answer-script convention. ### 2.5 Comparison: Supercharger vs Turbocharger | Parameter | Supercharger | Turbocharger | |---|---|---| | Driving Source | Engine crankshaft (mechanical) | Exhaust gas turbine | | Energy Source | Part of engine's brake power | Recovered exhaust waste energy | | Parasitic Loss | Yes (5–15 % of BP) | Negligible (only bearing friction) | | Response (Lag) | Instantaneous | Slight lag ("turbo lag") | | Boost at Low RPM | Excellent | Poor (low exhaust energy) | | Boost at High RPM | Limited (drive limits) | Excellent (high exhaust flow) | | Complexity | Simple | High (wastegate, intercooler, oil lines) | | Altitude Compensation | Possible but expensive | Excellent (used in aircraft) | | Typical Application | Drag racers, heavy-duty diesels | Modern passenger cars, trucks, aircraft | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aircraft piston engines** use turbochargers to maintain sea-level-rated power at altitude (e.g., the Continental IO-550 used in Cessna aircraft). - **Diesel locomotives** (e.g., EMD 645 series) use Roots blowers for supercharging. - **Formula 1 cars** use hybrid turbochargers (MGU-H) — turbocharger with an integrated electric motor to eliminate lag. - **MPFi systems** dominate every modern gasoline passenger car (Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai, Honda — all use variants of MPFi). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Power Augmentation Ratio for Supercharged Engine **Starting Premise:** The brake power of a 4-stroke engine is proportional to the mass of air inducted per second. $$\text{Step 1: Air mass flow rate for 4-stroke engine}$$ $$\dot{m}_a = \frac{\eta_v \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot N}{2 \times 60} \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$ **Step 2: Density at manifold (boost) conditions, treating as ideal gas:** $$\rho_2 = \frac{P_2}{R \cdot T_2}$$ **Step 3: Apply isentropic relation to eliminate $T_2$:** $$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ **Step 4: Substitute T₂ into the density expression:** $$\rho_2 = \frac{P_2}{R \cdot T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$ **Step 5: Rearrange by introducing P₁ explicitly:** $$\rho_2 = \frac{P_1}{R \cdot T_1} \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ **Step 6: Combine the exponents of (P₂/P₁):** $$\rho_2 = \rho_1 \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1 - (\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \rho_1 \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$$ **Step 7: Therefore the power ratio (assuming ηv equal for both cases):** $$\frac{P_{super}}{P_{na}} = \frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma}$$ **Final Derived Result:** $$\boxed{\frac{P_{super}}{P_{na}} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma} \times \frac{\eta_{v,super}}{\eta_{v,na}}}$$ ### 3.2 KTU-Style Numerical Problem — Fully Solved > **[KTU Numerical Pattern]** > A 4-stroke naturally aspirated petrol engine develops **75 kW** at **3000 rpm** with a volumetric efficiency of **82 %**. The ambient conditions are **1.013 bar** and **30 °C**. The engine is supercharged to a manifold pressure of **1.6 bar (absolute)**. Assuming the same volumetric efficiency and γ = 1.4, calculate: > (a) The new brake power output. > (b) The percentage increase in power. > (c) The temperature of the air after the supercharger (assuming adiabatic compression). --- **Given Data:** - $P_{na} = 75 \text{ kW}$ - $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$ - $\eta_v = 0.82$ (same for both cases) - $P_1 = 1.013 \text{ bar}$ - $P_2 = 1.6 \text{ bar}$ - $T_1 = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$ - $\gamma = 1.4$ --- **Part (a): New Brake Power** The power augmentation ratio formula is: $$\frac{P_{super}}{P_{na}} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma} = \left(\frac{1.6}{1.013}\right)^{1/1.4}$$ Step 1: Compute pressure ratio: $$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{1.6}{1.013} = 1.5795$$ Step 2: Compute the exponent: $$\frac{1}{\gamma} = \frac{1}{1.4} = 0.7143$$ Step 3: Take the natural logarithm for accuracy: $$\ln(1.5795) = 0.4570$$ Step 4: Multiply by exponent: $$0.7143 \times 0.4570 = 0.3265$$ Step 5: Take the exponential: $$e^{0.3265} = 1.3861$$ Step 6: Compute new power: $$P_{super} = 75 \times 1.3861 = 103.96 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{P_{super} \approx 104.0 \text{ kW}}$$ --- **Part (b): Percentage Increase** $$\% \text{ Increase} = \left(\frac{P_{super} - P_{na}}{P_{na}}\right) \times 100$$ $$\% \text{ Increase} = \left(\frac{103.96 - 75}{75}\right) \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\% \text{ Increase} = 38.61 \text{ \%}}$$ --- **Part (c): Temperature After Supercharger** Use the isentropic temperature relation: $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ Step 1: Compute exponent: $$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$ Step 2: Raise the pressure ratio to this exponent: $$(1.5795)^{0.2857}$$ $$\ln(1.5795) = 0.4570$$ $$0.2857 \times 0.4570 = 0.1305$$ $$e^{0.1305} = 1.1394$$ Step 3: Compute T₂: $$T_2 = T_1 \times 1.1394 = 303 \times 1.1394$$ $$\boxed{T_2 \approx 345.2 \text{ K} = 72.2 \text{ °C}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Insight:** The air heated up by 42 °C. In real engines, this hot air is then passed through an **intercooler/aftercooler** to cool it back closer to ambient temperature, which further increases its density and therefore the engine's power output beyond the simple adiabatic prediction. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Boost Pressure vs Power Gain (γ = 1.4) | Boost Pressure (bar abs) | $P_2/P_1$ | $(P_2/P_1)^{1/\gamma}$ | % Power Gain | |---|---|---|---| | 1.013 (Natural) | 1.000 | 1.000 | 0 % | | 1.20 | 1.185 | 1.131 | 13.1 % | | 1.40 | 1.382 | 1.232 | 23.2 % | | 1.60 | 1.579 | 1.329 | 32.9 % | | 1.80 | 1.777 | 1.422 | 42.2 % | | 2.00 | 1.974 | 1.512 | 51.2 % | | 2.50 | 2.468 | 1.726 | 72.6 % | | 3.00 | 2.961 | 1.929 | 92.9 % | > [!TIP] > Notice that power rises sub-linearly with boost pressure because of the $1/\gamma$ exponent — this is why high-boost turbo engines (e.g., 2.5 bar+) need **intercooling**, **knock sensors**, and **high-octane fuel** to survive. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Turbocharger System Architecture (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Exhaust Manifold
Hot High Pressure Gas] --> nodeB[Turbine Wheel
Extracts Kinetic Energy] nodeB --> nodeC[Common Shaft
Rotates at 100000 to 300000 rpm] nodeC --> nodeD[Compressor Wheel
Impeller] nodeD --> nodeE[Compressed Hot Air] nodeE --> nodeF[Intercooler
Reduces Air Temperature] nodeF --> nodeG[Intake Manifold
Pressurized Charge] nodeG --> nodeH[Engine Cylinders
Power Output Increases] nodeH --> nodeI[Exhaust Manifold] nodeI --> nodeA nodeJ[Wastegate
Controls Boost Limit] -.->|Bypasses Excess
Exhaust Flow| nodeB ``` ### 4.2 MPFi Engine — Electronic Control System Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT_SENSORS s1[CKP Sensor
Crank Position] s2[CMP Sensor
Cam Position] s3[MAP Sensor
Manifold Absolute Pressure] s4[MAF Sensor
Mass Air Flow] s5[TPS Sensor
Throttle Position] s6[Lambda O2 Sensor
Exhaust Oxygen] s7[CTS Sensor
Coolant Temperature] s8[IAT Sensor
Intake Air Temperature] s9[Knock Sensor
Engine Knock] end nodeECU[Engine Control Unit ECU
16 bit Microcontroller
50 to 100 MHz] subgraph OUTPUT_ACTUATORS a1[Fuel Injector 1
Pulse Width Modulated] a2[Fuel Injector 2] a3[Fuel Injector 3] a4[Fuel Injector 4] a5[Fuel Pump Relay] a6[Idle Air Control Valve] a7[Ignition Coil Driver] end subgraph AUXILIARY b1[Fuel Tank] b2[Electric Fuel Pump
300 to 450 kPa] b3[Fuel Filter] b4[Fuel Rail
Common Pressure] b5[Pressure Regulator
Maintains Constant Delta P] end s1 --> nodeECU s2 --> nodeECU s3 --> nodeECU s4 --> nodeECU s5 --> nodeECU s6 --> nodeECU s7 --> nodeECU s8 --> nodeECU s9 --> nodeECU nodeECU --> a1 nodeECU --> a2 nodeECU --> a3 nodeECU --> a4 nodeECU --> a5 nodeECU --> a6 nodeECU --> a7 b1 --> b2 --> b3 --> b4 b4 --> b5 b5 --> a1 b5 --> a2 b5 --> a3 b5 --> a4 ``` ### 4.3 Comparison Flow — Forced Induction Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart{Need Higher
Power Output?} nodeStart -->|Yes| nodeQ1{Energy Source?} nodeStart -->|No| nodeNA[Naturally Aspirated
Engine Baseline] nodeQ1 -->|Mechanical
from Crank| nodeSC[Supercharger
Roots Lysholm Centrifugal] nodeQ1 -->|Exhaust Waste
Energy| nodeTC[Turbocharger] nodeQ1 -->|Both Combined| nodeTS[Compound
Twin Charging] nodeSC --> nodeSC1[Advantages Instant Boost High Low End Torque] nodeSC --> nodeSC2[Drawbacks Parasitic Loss Low Efficiency] nodeTC --> nodeTC1[Advantages High Efficiency No Parasitic Loss] nodeTC --> nodeTC2[Drawbacks Turbo Lag High Exhaust Back Pressure] nodeTS --> nodeTS1[Best Performance High Boost No Lag] nodeTS --> nodeTS2[Complex and Expensive System] ``` ### 4.4 MPFi Injection Modes — Timing Sequence ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SIM[Simultaneous Mode] sim1[All 4 Injectors Fire
Together Every 360 deg] end subgraph BAT[Batch Mode] bat1[Pair 1 Fires at 0 deg] bat2[Pair 2 Fires at 180 deg] bat1 -.-> bat2 end subgraph SEQ[Sequential Mode] seq1[Inj 1 at Int Stroke Cyl 1] seq2[Inj 2 at Int Stroke Cyl 2] seq3[Inj 3 at Int Stroke Cyl 3] seq4[Inj 4 at Int Stroke Cyl 4] seq1 --> seq2 --> seq3 --> seq4 end SIM -->|Simplest| SIMnote[Used in Low Cost Systems] BAT -->|Compromise| BATnote[Used in Mid Range Vehicles] SEQ -->|Best Performance| SEQnote[Used in All Modern Cars] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define supercharging. List any two advantages of supercharging an IC engine.** **Model Answer:** Supercharging is the process of increasing the mass of air (or air-fuel mixture) inducted into an engine cylinder by compressing the intake charge to a pressure greater than the prevailing atmospheric pressure, using an external compressor. **Two Advantages:** 1. **Increased power output** from the same engine displacement — a highly beneficial power-to-weight ratio improvement for the same engine block. 2. **Improved altitude performance** — at high altitudes where atmospheric pressure falls, a supercharger maintains sea-level-rated power (critical for aircraft piston engines). 3. *(Bonus)* Reduction in specific fuel consumption (BSFC) due to improved volumetric efficiency and complete combustion. 4. *(Bonus)* Better smoke control and partial compensation for altitude in diesel engines. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **What is MPFi? How does it differ from a conventional carburetor?** **Model Answer:** MPFi stands for **Multi-Point Fuel Injection**. It is an electronically controlled fuel delivery system in which each cylinder of a multi-cylinder engine has its **own dedicated fuel injector** mounted in the intake port immediately upstream of the intake valve. **Difference from Carburetor:** | Aspect | Carburetor | MPFi | |---|---|---| | Fuel Metering | Mechanical (Venturi + jets) | Electronic (ECU pulse-width) | | Location | Single unit on intake manifold | One injector per cylinder, at port | | Atomization | Coarse, fuel puddles on walls | Fine spray at intake valve | | Distribution | Unequal across cylinders | Equal and precise per cylinder | | Cold Start | Manual choke | ECU enrichment strategy | | Emissions Control | Limited | Compatible with closed-loop λ control | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain the working principle of a turbocharger with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of an intercooler in a turbocharged engine. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-stroke naturally aspirated petrol engine develops 60 kW at 2800 rpm under standard conditions of 1 bar and 27 °C. The engine is fitted with a turbocharger that compresses the intake air to 1.45 bar. Assuming γ = 1.4 and same volumetric efficiency for both cases, determine: (i) the power output of the turbocharged engine, (ii) the temperature of air leaving the turbocharger, and (iii) the percentage increase in brake power. **(7 marks)** --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A** #### **Part (a) — Working of Turbocharger (7 marks)** A turbocharger consists of two main assemblies mounted on a common shaft: 1. **Turbine Section:** Hot high-pressure exhaust gases from the engine's exhaust manifold are directed through a nozzle ring onto the blades of a radial-inflow turbine wheel. The kinetic energy of the gas is converted to mechanical rotation of the turbine. 2. **Compressor Section:** The turbine and compressor wheels are rigidly coupled on a common shaft rotating at **100,000 to 300,000 rpm**. The compressor (usually centrifugal) draws in ambient air, accelerates it through the impeller and diffuser, and discharges it at elevated pressure. 3. **Energy Flow:** Exhaust energy → Turbine → Shaft → Compressor → Pressurized Intake Air → Engine. 4. **Wastegate:** A pressure-actuated valve that opens to bypass exhaust flow around the turbine when desired boost pressure is exceeded, preventing over-boost damage. 5. **Role of Intercooler:** - Air leaves the compressor hot (e.g., 130 °C for 1.5 bar boost). - Hot air is less dense, partially defeating the boost. - The intercooler (similar to a radiator) cools this compressed air closer to ambient temperature. - Cooler air → higher density → more mass of air → **more power** (typically 8–15 % additional gain). - Also reduces risk of **engine knock** in petrol engines. #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** - $P_{na} = 60 \text{ kW}$, $N = 2800 \text{ rpm}$, $\eta_v$ constant - $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar}$, $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300 \text{ K}$ - $P_2 = 1.45 \text{ bar}$, $\gamma = 1.4$ **(i) Turbocharged Power Output** Step 1: Pressure ratio: $$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{1.45}{1.0} = 1.45$$ Step 2: Apply power augmentation ratio formula: $$\frac{P_{tc}}{P_{na}} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/\gamma} = (1.45)^{1/1.4} = (1.45)^{0.7143}$$ Step 3: Compute via logarithms: $$\ln(1.45) = 0.3716$$ $$0.7143 \times 0.3716 = 0.2654$$ $$e^{0.2654} = 1.3040$$ Step 4: Final power: $$P_{tc} = 60 \times 1.3040 = 78.24 \text{ kW}$$ $$\boxed{P_{tc} \approx 78.24 \text{ kW}}$$ **(ii) Temperature After Turbocharger** $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$ Step 1: Exponent: $$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$ Step 2: Power computation: $$(1.45)^{0.2857}$$ $$\ln(1.45) \times 0.2857 = 0.3716 \times 0.2857 = 0.1062$$ $$e^{0.1062} = 1.1120$$ Step 3: Final temperature: $$T_2 = 300 \times 1.1120 = 333.6 \text{ K} = 60.6 \text{ °C}$$ $$\boxed{T_2 \approx 333.6 \text{ K} \equiv 60.6 \text{ °C}}$$ **(iii) Percentage Increase in Brake Power** $$\% \text{ Increase} = \left(\frac{78.24 - 60}{60}\right) \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\% \text{ Increase} = 30.4 \text{ \%}}$$ --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an MPFi system in an IC engine. List the major sensors and actuators used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare supercharging and turbocharging. Under what conditions would you prefer a turbocharger over a supercharger? **(7 marks)** --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B** #### **Part (a) — MPFi Working (7 marks)** **Working Principle:** 1. **Pressurized Fuel Supply:** An electric fuel pump (located inside or near the fuel tank) pressurizes fuel to **300–450 kPa** (typically 3.8 bar for port injection). Fuel passes through a fuel filter and into the fuel rail — a common manifold distributing pressurized fuel to all injectors. 2. **Pressure Regulation:** A fuel pressure regulator (vacuum-referenced) maintains a **constant pressure differential** across the injectors (~ 3 bar above manifold pressure), so the injected quantity depends only on injector opening time. 3. **ECU as the Brain:** The Engine Control Unit receives real-time signals from multiple sensors and computes the precise fuel injector **pulse width** (in milliseconds) needed for each injection event. 4. **Calculation Logic (Simplified):** $$\text{Pulse Width} \propto \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\text{No. of cylinders} \times \text{AFR}_{target}}$$ 5. **Sequential Injection:** In modern MPFi, each injector fires once per cycle, precisely timed to its cylinder's intake stroke (using CKP + CMP sensor synchronization). 6. **Closed-Loop Correction:** The wide-band O₂ (lambda) sensor measures exhaust oxygen and sends a feedback signal to the ECU, which trims the pulse width ±10 % to maintain the stoichiometric AFR of **14.7:1** for the catalytic converter. **Major Sensors (already shown in SECTION 4 diagram):** - CKP, CMP, MAP, MAF, TPS, CTS, IAT, O₂ (lambda), Knock sensor **Major Actuators:** - Fuel injectors (4 or 6), Fuel pump relay, Idle air control valve, Ignition coil driver, EVAP purge valve #### **Part (b) — Comparison & Selection (7 marks)** (Comparison table presented in SECTION 2.5 must be reproduced and elaborated) **Conditions Favouring Turbocharger Selection:** 1. **Altitude operation** (aircraft, mountain driving) — exhaust energy remains relatively high while ambient air thins. 2. **Fuel economy is critical** — turbocharger recovers exhaust energy; no engine power is parasitically lost to drive it. 3. **Sustained high-load operation** (trucks, generators) — large exhaust flow naturally provides abundant turbine energy. 4. **Emissions compliance** — lower BSFC translates directly to lower CO₂ per km. 5. **Compact packaging** — no mechanical belt drive required, allowing flexible exhaust manifold routing. **When to Prefer Supercharger:** - Need for instantaneous boost (no turbo lag acceptable — drag racing, instant throttle response). - Engine is very small displacement (insufficient exhaust energy to drive turbine). - Cost-sensitive applications where simplicity of mechanical drive outweighs efficiency loss. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Module:** > 1. **Mixing up γ and (γ−1)/γ exponents** — students routinely write $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{1/\gamma}$ instead of $(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. The first is the density ratio; the second is the temperature ratio. > 2. **Using bar gauge instead of bar absolute** in the pressure ratio — for an NA engine P₁ = 1.013 bar (abs), NOT 0 bar. Pressure ratios must ALWAYS be absolute. > 3. **Forgetting the 2 in the denominator** for 4-stroke air mass flow formula: $\dot{m}_a = \eta_v \rho V_d N / (2 \times 60)$. > 4. **Confusing turbo lag with turbo failure** — turbo lag is a transient response phenomenon, not a fault. > 5. **Writing "MPFi is same as GDi"** — MPFi injects at the port; GDi injects directly into the cylinder. Examiners mark this strictly wrong. > 6. **In numerical answers, omitting units** in the final boxed result — KTU examiners often deduct ½ mark per missing SI unit. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Bullet Checklist:** - **Supercharging** = Compressing intake air above atmospheric using a mechanically driven compressor (parasitic load). - **Turbocharging** = Compressing intake air using a turbine driven by exhaust gas (energy recovery). - **Power gain formula** (assuming equal ηv): $P_{super}/P_{na} = (P_2/P_1)^{1/\gamma}$. - **Temperature rise formula:** $T_2/T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. - **Compressor work:** $w_c = c_p T_1 [(P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$. - **γ for air = 1.4**, $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, **R = 0.287 kJ/kg·K**. - **Intercooler** cools compressed air → higher density → extra power + knock resistance. - **Wastegate** limits maximum boost pressure in turbochargers. - **MPFi** = one injector per cylinder at the intake port, controlled by ECU via pulse width. - **ECU inputs:** CKP, CMP, MAP, MAF, TPS, CTS, IAT, O₂, Knock. - **Three injection modes:** Simultaneous (simplest), Batch (compromise), Sequential (best — modern standard). - **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline** = 14.7 : 1. - **Supercharger advantage:** Instant boost, no lag. - **Turbocharger advantage:** High efficiency, no parasitic loss, recovers waste exhaust energy. - **Common application:** Aircraft piston engines (turbo), heavy diesel locomotives (Roots blower), modern cars (turbo + MPFi + GDi combinations). - **KTU board mandate:** Always present final numerical answers inside `\boxed{ }` with correct SI units. - **Diagrams in SECTION 4** must be reproduced in the answer script for full marks in 7-mark sub-questions.

topics.title: GDi Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** GDi Engines # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **GDi (Gasoline Direct Injection) Engine** is an advanced internal combustion engine technology in which **high-pressure gasoline fuel is injected directly into the combustion chamber (cylinder)** of each cylinder through an electronically controlled, multi-hole solenoid or piezo-electric injector, **bypassing the conventional intake port/ manifold injection path** used in PFI (Port Fuel Injection) systems. In a GDi engine, the fuel injection pressure is dramatically elevated to a range of **5 MPa to 20 MPa (50 bar – 200 bar)**, compared to the **0.35 MPa (3.5 bar)** typical of conventional PFI systems. This ultra-fine atomization, coupled with precise electronic timing, allows the air-fuel mixture to be tailored in-cylinder rather than pre-mixed in the intake tract. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2** > The topic "GDi Engines" is typically covered under the module "Fuel Injection Systems / Advanced Engine Technologies" and forms a high-weightage area for Part-B (14-mark) descriptive questions and Part-A (3-mark) short questions in KTU End Semester Examinations. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **pressure washer vs. a garden hose**. - A **garden hose (PFI engine)** sprays water into a *bucket* (the intake manifold) first, and then the mixture sloshes its way into the cylinder. The mixing is sloppy, fuel droplets cling to manifold walls (wall-wetting), and the spray is coarse. - A **pressure washer (GDi engine)** sprays a finely atomized, high-velocity mist *directly* into the cylinder, exactly when needed. The fuel never touches the cold manifold walls, so nothing is wasted, evaporation is instant, and the mixture can be sculpted into a homogeneous cloud (uniform) or a stratified cloud (fuel-rich near spark plug, lean elsewhere). > [!TIP] > **Why the fuss about "direct"?** In a PFI engine, fuel *evaporates* in the intake port and cools the air (producing denser charge). In a GDi engine, fuel evaporates *inside* the cylinder — closer to TDC (Top Dead Center) — so the cooling is more effective at suppressing knock, enabling **higher compression ratios** and **better thermal efficiency**. --- ## 1.3 Classification at a Glance | S.No | GDi Variant | Description | Typical Application | |:----:|:------------|:------------|:--------------------| | 1 | **Wall-Guided GDi** | Spray impinges on the piston crown/bowl and is guided toward the spark plug. | Early-generation GDi (e.g., Mitsubishi GDI, 1996) | | 2 | **Spray-Guided GDi** | Multiple narrowly-focused sprays target the spark plug directly. Requires precise combustion chamber geometry. | Modern 3-cylinder / 4-cylinder GDi engines | | 3 | **Air-Guided GDi** | Combines PFI-style charge preparation with a small direct injection pulse. | Transitional technology | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Pressure Metrics (Bold):** > - PFI injection pressure: **0.35 MPa (3.5 bar)** > - GDi injection pressure: **5 – 20 MPa (50 – 200 bar)** > - Diesel common-rail pressure (for comparison): **200 – 2500 bar** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic A GDi engine operates on a fundamentally different **air-path / fuel-path** philosophy compared to PFI: 1. **Pure Air Aspiration (No Fuel in Intake):** During the intake stroke, *only air* is drawn into the cylinder. The intake manifold remains free of fuel, eliminating wall-wetting and puddling losses. 2. **High-Pressure Fuel Pumping:** A camshaft-driven or chain-driven **high-pressure fuel pump** boosts line pressure to **5–20 MPa**. 3. **Common-Rail Accumulation:** A **fuel rail (common rail)** acts as an accumulator, smoothing pressure pulsations and providing a uniform reservoir for each cylinder. 4. **Direct Injector Discharge:** A **solenoid or piezo injector** mounted on the cylinder head (often centrally, above the piston) injects fuel into the combustion chamber. 5. **Multi-Mode Combustion Strategy:** - **Homogeneous Mode (Light Load):** Early injection (during intake stroke) creates a uniform air-fuel mixture — similar in character to PFI but with finer atomization. - **Stratified Mode (Idle / Low Load):** Late injection (near compression stroke) creates a *locally rich* mixture around the spark plug while the rest of the chamber remains lean. This drastically improves part-throttle fuel economy. 6. **Compression-Ignition Possibility (HCCI-like):** Under specific conditions, highly stratified mixtures can ignite through auto-ignition of the bulk charge, enabling **HCCI (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition)** operation. --- ## 2.2 Advantages of GDi Engines - **Improved Fuel Economy:** 10–20% reduction in fuel consumption, primarily due to stratified-charge operation at low loads and reduced pumping losses (throttle-less operation feasible). - **Higher Specific Power Output:** Direct cooling of the charge by fuel evaporation allows **higher compression ratios (up to ~12:1)** without knock, increasing **Volumetric + Thermal Efficiency**. - **Reduced Cold-Start Emissions:** Fuel is injected directly into the hot cylinder, ensuring faster light-off of the catalyst. - **Lower CO₂ Footprint:** Direct consequence of improved BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption). - **Precise Air-Fuel Ratio Control:** Lambda (λ) can be varied locally, enabling ultra-lean stratified combustion. --- ## 2.3 Disadvantages / Engineering Challenges - **Particulate Matter (PM) Emissions:** Rich stratified pockets produce soot — GDi engines are equipped with **GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)**. - **Injector Carbon Build-Up:** Direct exposure of injector tip to combustion products leads to coking; mandates use of high-detergency fuel and periodic maintenance. - **High-Pressure Pump Noise:** 5–20 MPa pump operation is audibly intrusive without NVH countermeasures. - **Cost:** High-pressure common rail, piezo injectors, and sophisticated ECU raise system cost significantly. - **Lubricating Oil Dilution:** Wall-wetting inside the cylinder (piston/liner) can wash lubricating oil into the sump, requiring robust oil management. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes | |:-:|:--------------------|:-----------|:------|:------| | 1 | Stoichiometric AFR (gasoline) | $\left( \dfrac{A}{F} \right)_{stoich} = 14.7$ | — | Mass basis; GDi operates around λ = 1 in homogeneous mode. | | 2 | Relative Air-Fuel Ratio (Lambda) | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | — | λ > 1 → lean; λ < 1 → rich. | | 3 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{a,actual}}{\rho_{a} \cdot V_{d}}$ | — | GDi typically achieves **> 100%** at tuned intake. | | 4 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency (Otto) | $\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | — | $r$ = compression ratio; $\gamma$ = specific heat ratio ≈ 1.4. | | 5 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{P_{b}}$ | g/kWh | GDi target: **< 250 g/kWh**. | | 6 | Mean Effective Pressure | $P_{me} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_{d}}$ | Pa or bar | Indicates specific work output. | | 7 | Fuel Injector Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}_{f} = C_{d} \cdot A_{n} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{f} \Delta P}$ | kg/s | $C_d$ ≈ 0.7 – 0.9; $\Delta P$ = rail pressure − cyl. pressure. | | 8 | Injection Timing (Crank Angle) | $\theta_{inj}$ in degrees BTDC | ° | Stratified: 30° – 90° BTDC; Homogeneous: 180° – 300° BTDC. | | 9 | Energy Density of Gasoline (LHV) | $LHV \approx 44$ MJ/kg | MJ/kg | Used in efficiency calculations. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** When computing efficiency, ensure $r$ in the Otto formula refers to the **geometric compression ratio**, not the effective (closed) one. GDi engines may have a variable effective ratio due to late intake-valve closure strategies. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - GDi technology, combined with **turbocharging** and **variable valve timing (VVT)**, forms the cornerstone of modern downsized engines (e.g., 1.0L 3-cylinder GDi replacing 1.6L naturally aspirated units). - It is the enabling technology for **mild-hybrid (48V)**, **HCCI**, and **Miller/Atkinson cycle** engines used in current production vehicles. - Emission compliance: GDi engines must integrate **GPF + 3-way catalytic converter (TWC)** to meet **BS-VI / Euro 6** standards. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency with Variable Compression Ratio This derivation is critical for KTU Part-B problems where the examiner asks: *"Why does a GDi engine achieve higher thermal efficiency than a PFI engine?"* The answer is rooted in the ability to operate at a higher compression ratio due to the charge-cooling effect. ### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Starting Point: Ideal Otto Cycle For an ideal Otto cycle, the **indicated thermal efficiency** is given by: $$\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$ ### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Adiabatic Compression (Process 1 → 2) For the adiabatic (isentropic) compression stroke from state 1 (BDC) to state 2 (TDC): $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$$ where $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ is the **compression ratio**, and $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$ is the specific heat ratio. ### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Adiabatic Expansion (Process 3 → 4) Similarly, for the power stroke: $$T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{r} \right)^{\gamma-1} = \dfrac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$ ### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Substituting Back Substituting the expressions for $T_2$ and $T_4$ into the efficiency equation: $$\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{\dfrac{T_3}{r^{\gamma-1}} - T_1}{T_3 - T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $r^{\gamma-1}$: $$\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{T_3 - T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}}{T_3 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} - T_1 \cdot r^{2(\gamma-1)}}$$ ### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Simplification for Constant $\gamma$ Assuming $\gamma$ is constant across the cycle, the standard result emerges: $$\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$ ### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Applying to GDi vs PFI For air with $\gamma = 1.4$: - **PFI engine (r = 10):** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{10^{0.4}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.512} = 0.602$ → **60.2%** - **GDi engine (r = 12):** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{12^{0.4}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{2.792} = 0.642$ → **64.2%** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** A modest increase in compression ratio from 10 to 12 yields a **4 percentage point** gain in ideal thermal efficiency, which is the primary reason GDi engines run higher $r$ in production. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Charge Cooling Effect (Real GDi Advantage) The actual engine benefit comes from the **latent heat of vaporization** cooling the in-cylinder charge. ### 3.2.1 Energy Balance for Fuel Vaporization When liquid gasoline (latent heat of vaporization $L_v \approx 350$ kJ/kg) is injected directly into hot compressed air: $$m_f \cdot L_v = m_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{cool}$$ ### 3.2.2 Solving for Temperature Drop $$\Delta T_{cool} = \dfrac{m_f \cdot L_v}{m_{air} \cdot c_p} = L_v \cdot (F/A) \cdot \dfrac{1}{c_p}$$ For stoichiometric mixture $(A/F) = 14.7$, so $(F/A) = 1/14.7$: $$\Delta T_{cool} = \dfrac{350 \text{ kJ/kg} \cdot 1}{14.7 \cdot 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}} = \dfrac{350}{14.77} \approx 23.7 \text{ K}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** Direct fuel injection lowers the *end-of-compression* charge temperature by approximately **23–25 K** compared to a pre-mixed PFI charge. This *suppresses knock*, allowing the use of higher compression ratio or advanced ignition timing. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — GDi vs PFI Comparison (High-Yield for KTU) | Parameter | PFI Engine | GDi Engine | KTU Significance | |:----------|:-----------|:-----------|:------------------| | Injection Location | Intake port | Combustion chamber | Defining difference | | Injection Pressure | 3.5 bar | 50 – 200 bar | 10× – 50× higher | | AFR Control | Globally uniform | Locally variable (stratified) | Enables lean burn | | Compression Ratio | 9 – 10:1 | 11 – 14:1 | Higher thermal efficiency | | Fuel Economy (BSFC) | 260 – 280 g/kWh | 220 – 250 g/kWh | 10–15% improvement | | Particulate Emissions | Negligible | Measurable → GPF required | New emission challenge | | Cold-Start HC | High (wall-wetting) | Low (no wall-wetting) | Faster catalyst light-off | | Cost | Low | High (HP pump + rail + injectors) | Economic trade-off | | Pumping Loss | Higher (throttle needed) | Lower (stratified → no throttle) | BSFC benefit | | Typical BSFC Reduction | Baseline | 15 – 20% | Major selling point | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model for KTU Practice) **Problem:** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder GDi engine has a total displacement of 1.6 L. It operates at stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (AFR = 14.7) at 2000 rpm. The BSFC is 240 g/kWh. Air density is 1.2 kg/m³. Calculate: 1. Volumetric flow rate of air. 2. Fuel flow rate in kg/s. 3. Brake power output. **Given:** - $V_d = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ (total) - $N = 2000$ rpm - $AFR = 14.7$ - $BSFC = 240$ g/kWh = $240 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/kWh - $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³ **Solution:** **Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate of Air** For a 4-stroke engine, one cycle per 2 revolutions. The number of cycles per second is $\dfrac{N}{2 \times 60}$. $$\dot{V}_{air} = V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{2 \times 60} \cdot \eta_{vol}$$ Assuming $\eta_{vol} \approx 1.0$ for estimation: $$\dot{V}_{air} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \dfrac{2000}{120} \cdot 1.0 = 0.0267 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate of Air** $$\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V}_{air} = 1.2 \cdot 0.0267 = 0.032 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate of Fuel** $$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{AFR} = \dfrac{0.032}{14.7} = 0.00218 \text{ kg/s} = 2.18 \text{ g/s}$$ **Step 4 — Brake Power from BSFC** $$P_b = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{BSFC} = \dfrac{0.00218 \text{ kg/s}}{240 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/kWh}}$$ Converting to consistent units: $BSFC$ in kg/kWh = $240 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/kWh. To get power in kW, express fuel flow in kg/h: $$\dot{m}_{fuel,hr} = 0.00218 \cdot 3600 = 7.85 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$P_b = \dfrac{7.85 \text{ kg/h}}{0.240 \text{ kg/kWh}} = 32.7 \text{ kW}$$ > [!TIP] > **Sanity Check:** Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP) = $\dfrac{P_b \times 60 \times 2}{V_d \times N} \approx 9.8$ bar — reasonable for a naturally aspirated GDi engine at mid-range RPM. ✓ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of a GDi Engine The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional architecture of a GDi fuel injection system, illustrating the high-pressure fuel path, air path, and electronic control path. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FUELPATH["HIGH PRESSURE FUEL PATH"] FTNK[Fuel Tank] --> FLTR[Fuel Filter] FLTR --> LPMP[Low Pressure Pump
0.3 to 0.5 MPa] LPMP --> HPMP[High Pressure Pump
5 to 20 MPa] HPMP --> RAIL[Common Rail
Pressure Accumulator] end subgraph INJCTRL["ELECTRONIC INJECTION CONTROL"] ECU[Engine Control Unit
ECU / ECM] ECU -.->|PWM Signal| INJ ECU -.->|Spill Valve Control| HPMP ECU -.->|Rail Pressure Setpoint| PRREG[Pressure Regulator] end subgraph AIRPATH["AIR INTAKE PATH"] AIR[Ambient Air] --> THROTT[Electronic Throttle Body] THROTT --> INTMAN[Intake Manifold
Fuel Free] INTMAN --> INTVLV[Intake Valve] INTVLV --> CYL[Combustion Chamber] end subgraph COMBCHAM["COMBUSTION CHAMBER"] INJ[Direct Injector
Multi Hole Solenoid or Piezo] CYL SPK[Spark Plug] PIST[Piston with Crown Bowl] end RAIL --> INJ INJ -->|High Pressure Spray| CYL SPK -->|Ignition| CYL PIST --> CYL CYL --> EXVLV[Exhaust Valve] EXVLV --> CATC[TWC + GPF] CATC --> EXHAUST[Exhaust to Atmosphere] THROTT -.-> ECU INTMAN -.->|MAF Sensor| ECU CYL -.->|Lambda O2 Sensor| ECU CYL -.->|Knock Sensor| ECU CRANK[Crank Position Sensor] -.-> ECU CAMPH[Camshaft Position Sensor] -.-> ECU style FUELPATH fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px style AIRPATH fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb,stroke-width:2px style COMBCHAM fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#db2777,stroke-width:2px style INJCTRL fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.2 Combustion Strategy Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Engine Load and Speed] --> B{Load Condition} B -->|Low Load / Idle| C[Stratified Charge Mode] B -->|Medium Load| D[Homogeneous Mode] B -->|High Load / WOT| E[Homogeneous Stoichiometric Mode] C --> F[Late Injection 60 to 90 deg BTDC] D --> G[Early Injection 180 to 300 deg BTDC] E --> G F --> H[Rich Local Lambda near Spark Plug] G --> I[Uniform Lambda in Chamber] style A fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#ca8a04 style C fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309 style D fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1 style E fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c style H fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c style I fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d ``` --- ## 4.3 Comparative PFI vs GDi — Fuel Delivery Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PFI["PFI SYSTEM"] P1[Fuel Rail 3.5 bar] --> P2[Port Injector] P2 --> P3[Intake Port] P3 -->|Wall Wetting| P4[Intake Valve] P4 --> P5[Combustion Chamber] end subgraph GDI["GDI SYSTEM"] G1[Fuel Rail 200 bar] --> G2[Direct Injector in Cylinder Head] G2 -->|High Pressure Spray| G3[Combustion Chamber] G4[Clean Air Only] --> G5[Intake Manifold] G5 --> G6[Intake Valve] G6 --> G3 end style PFI fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px style GDI fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#059669,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows in the **GDI SYSTEM** show a critical architectural difference — the intake manifold carries **clean, fuel-free air** (G4 → G5 → G6), whereas in the PFI system, fuel deposits on the port walls (P3 → P4), creating wall-wetting and uneven AFR distribution. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **Q: Define a GDi engine. How does it differ from a conventional PFI engine in terms of fuel injection location?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **GDi (Gasoline Direct Injection) engine** is one in which high-pressure gasoline is injected *directly into the combustion chamber* of each cylinder by an electronically controlled injector, instead of being sprayed into the intake port as in a conventional **PFI (Port Fuel Injection)** engine. In a PFI system, fuel is mixed with air in the intake manifold, causing wall-wetting and inhomogeneous charge; in GDi, fuel is delivered straight into the cylinder at **5 – 20 MPa**, producing a finely atomized spray that is mixed either homogeneously or in a stratified cloud, depending on load. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for comparison)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **Q: List any three advantages of GDi engines over PFI engines.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Higher fuel economy (10 – 20%)** due to stratified-charge operation and reduced pumping losses. 2. **Higher specific power output** because the charge-cooling effect permits an increased compression ratio. 3. **Reduced cold-start HC emissions** as no fuel condenses on cold intake-port walls. *(3 marks — 1 per advantage)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]` **Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a GDi engine. Discuss its types (wall-guided, spray-guided, air-guided) and compare its performance with a conventional PFI engine.** #### Part (a) — Working Principle and Construction (7 marks) **Solution Outline:** 1. **Definition (1 mark):** GDi injects fuel *directly into the combustion chamber* at 5 – 20 MPa, bypassing the intake port. 2. **Main Components (3 marks):** - High-pressure fuel pump (camshaft/chain driven) - Common rail (pressure accumulator) - Direct injectors (solenoid or piezo, multi-hole, mounted in cylinder head) - Electronic Control Unit (ECU) with crank, cam, MAF, lambda, and knock sensors - High-pressure fuel lines 3. **Working (3 marks):** - Pure air enters during the intake stroke. - ECU commands injector to fire at the correct crank angle. - Two modes: - **Homogeneous mode** (medium / high load): early injection (intake stroke) → uniform mixture. - **Stratified mode** (low load / idle): late injection (near TDC of compression stroke) → rich pocket near spark plug. - Spark plug ignites the charge; combustion drives the piston. #### Part (b) — Types of GDi and Performance Comparison (7 marks) **Solution Outline:** | Type | Description | Spray Pattern | |:-----|:------------|:--------------| | **Wall-Guided** | Spray hits piston crown and is deflected toward the spark plug. | Wide-angle; relies on chamber geometry. | | **Spray-Guided** | Narrowly focused multi-hole spray directly targets the spark plug. | Requires tight injector/spark-plug placement. | | **Air-Guided** | Combines PFI charge preparation with a small direct injection pulse. | Air motion shapes the spray. | **Performance Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Parameter | PFI | GDi | |:----------|:----|:----| | Injection Pressure | 3.5 bar | 50 – 200 bar | | Compression Ratio | 9 – 10:1 | 11 – 14:1 | | BSFC | 260 – 280 g/kWh | 220 – 250 g/kWh | | PM Emissions | Negligible | Higher → GPF needed | | Cold-Start HC | High | Low | **Conclusion:** GDi offers superior fuel economy and power density but demands emission after-treatment and high-pressure hardware. *(Full 7 marks awarded for correctly tabulated comparison + conclusion.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model — Alternative Choice]` **Q: Explain the various combustion modes (homogeneous, stratified, and HCCI) employed in GDi engines. Discuss the role of injection timing in achieving these modes.** #### Part (a) — Homogeneous and Stratified Combustion Modes (7 marks) **Homogeneous Charge Mode:** - Injection occurs during the **intake stroke** (180° – 300° BTDC of compression stroke). - Air and fuel mix uniformly throughout the chamber. - Lambda ≈ 1 (stoichiometric). - Used at **medium to high loads** and during cold start. - Simple, robust, with TWC-compatible exhaust. **Stratified Charge Mode:** - Injection occurs **late** (60° – 90° BTDC of compression stroke). - Only a small fuel-rich pocket (λ ≈ 0.6 – 0.8) forms around the spark plug; the bulk of the chamber is very lean (overall λ > 1.5, sometimes 2.0+). - Used at **idle and low part-throttle** loads. - Significantly reduces fuel consumption and pumping losses. #### Part (b) — HCCI Mode and Role of Injection Timing (7 marks) **HCCI (Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition) Mode:** - A homogeneous lean mixture auto-ignites throughout the chamber when temperature and pressure exceed the fuel's auto-ignition threshold — **no spark plug needed**. - Combustion is ultra-fast, near-constant-volume, yielding the **highest possible thermal efficiency** (~ 40% indicated). - Hard to control — combustion phasing depends on mixture composition, temperature, and residual gas. **Role of Injection Timing (Tabulated for 4 marks):** | Mode | Injection Timing | AFR Strategy | Load Range | |:-----|:-----------------|:-------------|:-----------| | Stratified | 60° – 90° BTDC | Locally rich | Idle / low load | | Homogeneous | 180° – 300° BTDC | Stoichiometric | Medium / high load | | HCCI | 300°+ BTDC (early) | Lean / diluted | Mid-load, low speed | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing λ > 1 with "lean-burn" operation universally.** In stratified mode, *only the local mixture near the spark plug is rich*; the *bulk* charge is lean. Students often write the wrong way around and lose 1 mark. > 2. **Skipping the high-pressure hardware** (pump, rail, injector type) in the constructional description. Examiners specifically test awareness of 5 – 20 MPa rail pressure. > 3. **Forgetting GPF** when listing emission challenges. Direct injection of gasoline is the *only* mass-market application that mandates a particulate filter on a spark-ignition engine. > 4. **Miscounting injector locations** in the diagram. GDi injectors are in the **cylinder head**, NOT in the intake port — a frequent 2-mark deduction. > 5. **Omitting injection timing crank angles** in the modes question. Without the numerical BTDC values, the answer is incomplete. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for GDi Engines** - **GDi = Gasoline Direct Injection** — fuel injected into the **combustion chamber**, not the port. - **Injection Pressure:** **5 – 20 MPa (50 – 200 bar)** vs PFI's **0.35 MPa (3.5 bar)**. - **Three Types:** Wall-guided, Spray-guided, Air-guided. - **Two Major Combustion Modes:** Homogeneous (early injection, λ ≈ 1) and Stratified (late injection, locally rich). - **Advanced Mode:** HCCI — auto-ignition, no spark, highest efficiency, hardest to control. - **Charge Cooling Effect:** Vaporization of fuel inside cylinder reduces charge temperature by **~ 24 K**, suppressing knock. - **Higher Compression Ratio Achievable:** **11 – 14:1** (vs 9 – 10:1 in PFI). - **Fuel Economy Gain:** **10 – 20% reduction** in BSFC. - **Key Disadvantage:** Particulate Matter (PM) emissions → requires **GPF (Gasoline Particulate Filter)**. - **Key Sensors:** MAF, Lambda O₂, Knock, Crank, Camshaft — all feeding the ECU for closed-loop control. - **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline:** **14.7:1** (mass basis). - **Real-world Pairing:** GDi is almost always combined with **turbocharging** and **VVT** for downsized, high-efficiency engines. - **Emission Standard Compliance:** Must meet **BS-VI / Euro 6** using TWC + GPF combination.

topics.title: CRDi: Layout
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CRDi: Layout

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
