**Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)** is an advanced diesel fuel injection architecture in which a single, high-pressure accumulator (called the **Common Rail**) supplies pressurized fuel simultaneously to all engine cylinders via electronically controlled solenoid or piezo-electric injectors. Unlike conventional jerk-pump or unit-injection systems where injection pressure is generated *per cylinder per cycle*, CRDi decouples pressure generation from injection timing, allowing the **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** to independently control injection **pressure**, **timing**, **quantity**, and the **number of injection events per cycle** (pilot, main, and post injections).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Layout" in this context refers to the **functional arrangement, fluid path, and component interconnection** of the CRDi system — from fuel tank pickup to combustion in the cylinder. Students must be able to draw and label the complete layout, identify the function of each component, and explain the signal/fuel flow path.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of CRDi as a **"Centralized Water Tower supplying multiple taps with a Digital Brain."**

- The **water tower (common rail)** is always full of water at a constant high pressure, regardless of which tap is open.
- Each **tap (injector)** can be opened independently, at any time, for any duration — controlled electronically.
- The **digital brain (ECU)** decides when, how long, and how many times each tap opens, based on sensor inputs (accelerator pedal position, engine speed, coolant temperature, etc.).

**In contrast**, an old jerk-pump system is like having a separate hand-pump for every tap — pressure is generated only when that specific pump is pumped, making fast, multiple, or precisely-timed injections nearly impossible.

### Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded)
- **Common Rail Pressure (Modern Common Rail):** **1,600 – 2,500 bar** (Bosch 3rd gen reaches 2,500 bar; latest piezo systems up to **2,700 bar**)
- **Pilot Injection Pressure:** Typically **250 – 400 bar**
- **Nozzle Opening Pressure (NOP) for solenoid injectors:** around **200 – 300 bar**
- **Injection Duration:** As low as **~0.1 ms** per event (piezo injectors)
- **Number of injections per cycle:** Up to **9** (modern multi-event systems)
- **Fuel temperature in rail:** Maintained around **60 – 90 °C**

> [!NOTE]
> A "bar" is **not** an SI unit but is widely accepted in automotive engineering; 1 bar = 100 kPa. 2,000 bar = 200 MPa.

### Layman's Summary
In CRDi, the engine's job of creating high pressure is done **once** in a central pump and stored in a thick steel pipe (the rail). Each cylinder's injector is just an **electronic valve** — fast, precise, and programmable. This enables cleaner combustion, lower noise, and better fuel economy.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of a CRDi System

The CRDi layout consists of three major subsystems, each with a clear function:

#### A. Low-Pressure Circuit (Supply Side)
Responsible for delivering clean fuel from the tank to the high-pressure pump.
1. **Fuel Tank** — stores diesel fuel.
2. **Fuel Strainer / Pre-Filter** — coarse sediment removal at the tank outlet.
3. **Lift Pump (Feed/Transfer Pump)** — usually an electric 12 V/24 V pump that delivers fuel at **3 – 5 bar** to the suction side of the high-pressure pump. It also bleeds air and returns excess fuel to the tank via a pressure regulator.
4. **Fuel Filter (Fine Filtration)** — water separator + fine filter (typically **3 – 5 µm** rating) to protect the precision HP pump and injectors.

#### B. High-Pressure Circuit (Generation & Storage)
5. **High-Pressure Pump (HP Pump)** — usually a **radial-piston pump** with 3 pistons (each piston has its own pumping element) driven off the camshaft at half engine speed (or 1:1 in some designs). It generates **up to 2,000 – 2,500 bar**.
   - Each piston has an inlet valve (mechanical) and an outlet (delivery) valve.
   - The pump has an integrated **fuel metering unit (FMU / SCV — Suction Control Valve)** that regulates fuel quantity drawn into the pump chambers, controlling rail pressure indirectly.
6. **Common Rail (Accumulator)** — a high-pressure stainless steel tube (volume typically **20 – 60 cm³**) that stores pressurized fuel and dampens pressure oscillations. It maintains a nearly constant pressure throughout injection.
7. **Pressure Limiter / Pressure Control Valve** — a safety valve that opens at ~2,500 – 2,700 bar to vent excess fuel back to the tank, protecting components.
8. **Rail Pressure Sensor (RPS)** — a strain-gauge sensor that reports instantaneous rail pressure to the ECU.

#### C. Injection Circuit (Delivery Side)
9. **Injectors (Solenoid or Piezo-Electric)** — one per cylinder, mounted directly in the cylinder head. Each injector contains:
   - A **Nozzle** (multi-hole, typically 5 – 9 holes).
   - A **Nozzle Needle** held closed by a spring.
   - A **Control Valve (Solenoid coil or Piezo stack)** that, when energized, opens a small dump orifice, allowing the needle-lift pressure to drop, which lifts the needle and begins injection.
10. **High-Pressure Injection Pipes (Injector Lines)** — short, equal-length rigid steel lines connecting rail to each injector.
11. **Fuel Return Lines** — return fuel from injectors (leakage from control chamber) and from the rail's pressure limiter to the tank, often through a fuel cooler.

#### D. Electronic Control Unit (ECU) & Sensors
12. **ECU (Engine Control Module)** — the brain. Performs > 100 million operations/sec for injection calculations.
13. **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)** — for crank angle & RPM.
14. **Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP)** — to identify firing order and injection phase.
15. **Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (APP)** — drive-by-wire input.
16. **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS), Intake Air Temperature Sensor (IAT), Fuel Temperature Sensor.**
17. **Boost Pressure Sensor (MAP)** — for turbocharged engines.
18. **Air Mass Flow Sensor (MAF)** — measures intake air mass for precise stoichiometric ratio.

### 2.2 Operational Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic)
1. Driver presses accelerator → APP sensor sends signal to ECU.
2. ECU consults a pre-loaded **3-D calibration map** (RPM × Pedal Position × Coolant Temp → fueling quantity, timing, rail pressure).
3. ECU commands the **Fuel Metering Unit (FMU)** to meter correct fuel into the HP pump, setting desired rail pressure.
4. ECU waits for the correct crank angle (from CKP/CMP), then energizes the relevant injector solenoid/piezo for a calculated **duration** (= desired fuel quantity at known pressure).
5. The injector opens → fuel sprays into the combustion chamber at very high pressure → fine atomization.
6. Multiple injections may occur: **Pilot (1–2) → Main (1) → After (1–3)** to reduce noise and emissions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **directly testable** in KTU University Examinations under Module 2.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks / Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------------------|---------------------|-------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Hydraulic Power of HP Pump | $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{Q \cdot p}{\eta_v}$ | W | $Q$ = flow (m³/s), $p$ = rail pressure (Pa), $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency (~0.9) |
| 2 | Volumetric Fuel Flow from HP Pump | $Q_{HP} = V_{disp} \cdot N_{pump} \cdot \eta_v$ | m³/s | $V_{disp}$ = total pump displacement per rev (m³/rev), $N_{pump}$ = pump rpm |
| 3 | Pump Speed (Cam-driven) | $N_{pump} = \dfrac{N_{engine}}{2}$ | rpm | For 4-stroke with single cam lobe per HP-pump piston |
| 4 | Fuel Mass per Injection Cycle | $m_{inj} = \rho_{fuel} \cdot C_d \cdot A_{nozzle} \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta p / \rho_{fuel}} \cdot t_{inj}$ | kg | $\Delta p$ = rail pressure − cylinder back pressure |
| 5 | Rail Pressure Decay (Quasi-steady) | $p_{rail}(t) = p_0 - \dfrac{m_{inj} \cdot c^2}{2 V_{rail}} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Pa | $V_{rail}$ = rail volume, $\tau$ = damping time constant |
| 6 | Mean Effective Pressure (Indicated) | $IMEP = \dfrac{W_{cycle}}{V_d}$ | Pa | $W_{cycle}$ = work per cycle, $V_d$ = swept volume |
| 7 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_f}{P_b}$ | kg/(kW·s) | $m_f$ = fuel mass rate, $P_b$ = brake power |
| 8 | Rail Pressure Energy Stored | $E_{rail} = \dfrac{1}{2} p \cdot V_{rail}$ | J | Approx. for isothermal energy storage |
| 9 | Injection Quantity per Stroke | $Q_{inj} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_n^2}{4} \cdot v_{needle} \cdot N_{holes} \cdot C_d \cdot t_{inj}$ | m³ | $d_n$ = nozzle hole diameter, $v_{needle}$ = needle lift velocity |
| 10 | Pressure Wave Speed in Rail | $c = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + (d/E \cdot t) \cdot (K/D)}}$ | m/s | $K$ = bulk modulus of fuel, $D$ = rail diameter, $t$ = wall thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes `|` are used inside table cells** — modulus bars are written as $\vert x \vert$ or \mid for column separation safety.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Passenger Cars & Light Commercial Vehicles:** Vast majority of modern diesels (Tata, Mahindra, Hyundai, Toyota, BMW) use CRDi.
- **Heavy-Duty Trucks & Buses:** Use scaled-up CRDi with rail pressures up to 2,500 bar.
- **Marine Diesel (Two-stroke):** MAN B&W, Wärtsilä use electronically controlled common rail for very large engines.
- **Agricultural Tractors:** Use CRDi for better torque control.
- **Off-Highway & Stationary Engines:** For reduced emissions compliance (BS-VI, Euro 6, EPA Tier 4 Final).

### 2.5 Advantages vs. Disadvantages (For KTU 2-mark Questions)

**Advantages:**
- High and constant injection pressure → better atomization.
- Multiple injections possible → lower noise, lower emissions (NOx, PM).
- Better cold-start performance.
- Higher specific power output.
- ECU-controlled → easy integration with ABS, ESP, ASR.

**Disadvantages:**
- Very high-pressure components are expensive.
- Requires extremely clean fuel (water & dirt cause injector failure).
- Complex ECU calibration & diagnosis.
- Return lines can cause fuel aeration at high altitudes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydraulic Power Required by the High-Pressure Pump

The HP pump must raise fuel from a low feed pressure $p_{in}$ to the common-rail pressure $p_{rail}$. The work done per unit mass (specific work) is the integral of specific volume with respect to pressure (assuming incompressible flow is a useful first-order approximation since diesel bulk modulus is ~1.5–2.0 GPa).

**Step 1 — Define the system.**
A piston of displacement $V_d$ compresses fuel from inlet pressure $p_{in}$ to delivery pressure $p_{rail}$.

**Step 2 — Write the work done per stroke (assuming incompressible).**
For an incompressible fluid, the work done per unit volume equals the pressure rise:

$$W_{stroke} = (p_{rail} - p_{in}) \cdot V_d$$

**Step 3 — Convert to power by multiplying with pump speed.**
The HP pump runs at $N_{pump}$ revolutions per second (after converting from rpm), and each cylinder fires once per revolution (for a single-acting pump). The number of effective pumping strokes per second is:

$$N_{strokes} = N_{pump} \cdot n_{cyl}$$

where $n_{cyl}$ is the number of HP-pump pistons (typically **3** for radial-piston pumps).

**Step 4 — Compute hydraulic power delivered to the fluid.**

$$P_{hyd} = W_{stroke} \cdot N_{strokes} = (p_{rail} - p_{in}) \cdot V_d \cdot N_{pump} \cdot n_{cyl}$$

**Step 5 — Account for volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$.**
The actual displaced volume is reduced by leakage past pistons:

$$V_{actual} = V_d \cdot \eta_v$$

Since $p_{in} \ll p_{rail}$ (3 bar vs. 2,000 bar), we may neglect $p_{in}$ to obtain the standard KTU exam form:

$$P_{hyd} \approx p_{rail} \cdot V_d \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot N_{pump} \cdot \eta_v$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example for a 4-cylinder, 3-piston radial pump.**

Given:
- $V_d$ per piston = 1.0 cm³ = $1 \times 10^{-6}$ m³
- $N_{pump} = N_{engine} / 2 = 2{,}000$ rpm at 4,000 engine rpm = **33.33 rev/s**
- $n_{cyl} = 3$ pistons
- $p_{rail} = 2{,}000$ bar = $2 \times 10^{8}$ Pa
- $\eta_v = 0.9$

Compute:
$$P_{hyd} = 2 \times 10^{8} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 3 \cdot 33.33 \cdot 0.9$$

$$P_{hyd} = 2 \times 10^{8} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 3 \cdot 33.33 \cdot 0.9$$

Let me compute step by step:
- $2 \times 10^{8} \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6} = 200$ (W·s/m³ · m³ = W·s... actually units: Pa · m³ = J)
- $200 \cdot 3 = 600$ J/rev (per pumping stroke of one piston)
- Per second: $600 \cdot 33.33 = 19{,}998$ J/s = ~20 kW
- Times $\eta_v$: $20{,}000 \cdot 0.9 = 18{,}000$ W = **18 kW**

**Final Answer:** $P_{hyd} \approx 18 \text{ kW}$

> This represents only the *useful* hydraulic energy; the actual mechanical input to the pump is higher due to mechanical friction and is calculated as $P_{mech} = P_{hyd} / \eta_{mech}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Per-Injection Fuel Mass vs. Rail Pressure

**Step 1 — Start with the Bernoulli-based discharge equation** for an injector nozzle:

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_{fuel} \cdot \Delta p}$$

where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (≈ 0.7 – 0.85 for multi-hole nozzles)
- $A_n$ = total nozzle area = $N_{holes} \cdot \pi d_n^2 / 4$
- $\rho_{fuel}$ ≈ 835 kg/m³ (diesel at 40 °C)
- $\Delta p = p_{rail} - p_{cyl}$ (cylinder gas pressure)

**Step 2 — Multiply by injection duration $t_{inj}$** to get mass per injection event:

$$m_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_{fuel} \cdot \Delta p} \cdot t_{inj}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for injection duration** to deliver a target mass $m_{target}$ (this is the ECU's main computation):

$$t_{inj} = \dfrac{m_{target}}{C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_{fuel} \cdot \Delta p}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check.**
Given:
- $C_d = 0.8$, $A_n = 0.2 \text{ mm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ m²
- $\rho_{fuel} = 835$ kg/m³
- $p_{rail} = 1{,}800$ bar, $p_{cyl} = 40$ bar
- $\Delta p = 1{,}760$ bar = $1.76 \times 10^{8}$ Pa
- $m_{target} = 30$ mg = $3 \times 10^{-5}$ kg (typical main injection dose)

Compute:
- $\sqrt{2 \cdot 835 \cdot 1.76 \times 10^{8}} = \sqrt{2.94 \times 10^{11}} = 5.42 \times 10^{5}$ m/s (this is the theoretical velocity)
- Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = 0.8 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 835 \cdot 5.42 \times 10^{5} / 1$ — let me redo cleanly:

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho \cdot \Delta p}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.8 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot 835 \cdot 1.76 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\sqrt{2 \cdot 835 \cdot 1.76 \times 10^{8}} = \sqrt{2.939 \times 10^{11}} \approx 5.42 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.8 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 5.42 \times 10^{5} = 0.8 \cdot 0.1084 = 0.0867 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute injection time:**

$$t_{inj} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0867} \approx 3.46 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.346 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{inj} \approx 0.35$ ms — a typical value for a single main injection event.

> [!NOTE]
> Pilot injections typically have $m_{target} = 1$–$2$ mg and $t_{inj} \approx 0.1$ ms, occurring 1–3 degrees of crank angle before the main injection.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: CRDi vs. Conventional Jerk Pump vs. Unit Injector

| Feature | CRDi | Jerk / Inline Pump | Unit Injector (EUI) |
|---------|------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Pressure generation | Central, continuous | Per cylinder, on cam | Per cylinder, on cam |
| Peak Pressure (bar) | 1,600 – 2,500 | 600 – 1,000 | 1,500 – 2,000 |
| Max Injections/cycle | 7 – 9 | 1 | 1 – 2 (with HEUI: 3) |
| Pressure control | Electronic (RPS + FMU) | Mechanical governor | Mechanical + Electronic |
| Cold start | Excellent | Difficult | Moderate |
| Noise level (dB) | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| Cost | High | Lowest | High |
| Fuel filter demand | Very fine (3 µm) | Standard (10 µm) | Fine (5 µm) |
| ECU dependency | Essential | None | Optional |
| BS-VI / Euro 6 compliance | Yes (mandatory) | No | No (with HEUI: borderline) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CRDi System Layout (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the complete **fluid path** (low pressure → high pressure → injection → return) and the **signal path** (sensors → ECU → actuators) of a CRDi system. This is the exact layout a KTU examiner expects a student to draw and label.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    %% Low Pressure Circuit
    TANK[Fuel Tank]
    STRAIN[Strainer / Pre-Filter]
    LIFT[Lift Pump 3-5 bar]
    REGFUEL[Pressure Regulator]
    FILTER[Fine Fuel Filter 3-5 micrometer]

    %% High Pressure Circuit
    HPPUMP[High Pressure Pump Radial 3 Piston 2000 bar]
    FMU[Fuel Metering Unit SCV]
    RAIL{{Common Rail Accumulator Volume 20-60 cm3}}
    RPS[Rail Pressure Sensor]
    PLV[Pressure Limiter Valve 2500 bar]

    %% Injection Circuit
    INJ1[Injector Cyl 1 Solenoid]
    INJ2[Injector Cyl 2 Solenoid]
    INJ3[Injector Cyl 3 Solenoid]
    INJ4[Injector Cyl 4 Solenoid]
    ENG[Diesel Engine Cylinders]

    %% Electronic Control
    ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    CKP[Crankshaft Sensor]
    CMP[Camshaft Sensor]
    APP[Accelerator Pedal Sensor]
    CTS[Coolant Temp Sensor]
    IAT[Intake Air Temp Sensor]
    MAP[Boost Pressure Sensor]
    MAF[Air Mass Flow Sensor]

    %% Flow Path
    TANK --> STRAIN --> LIFT --> REGFUEL --> FILTER --> HPPUMP
    HPPUMP -- High Pressure 2000 bar --> RAIL
    RPS --> RAIL
    PLV --> RAIL
    RAIL --> INJ1
    RAIL --> INJ2
    RAIL --> INJ3
    RAIL --> INJ4
    INJ1 --> ENG
    INJ2 --> ENG
    INJ3 --> ENG
    INJ4 --> ENG

    %% ECU Connections
    CKP --> ECU
    CMP --> ECU
    APP --> ECU
    CTS --> ECU
    IAT --> ECU
    MAP --> ECU
    MAF --> ECU
    RPS --> ECU
    ECU -- SCV command --> FMU
    FMU --> HPPUMP
    ECU -- Injector drive pulse --> INJ1
    ECU -- Injector drive pulse --> INJ2
    ECU -- Injector drive pulse --> INJ3
    ECU -- Injector drive pulse --> INJ4

    %% Return Path
    LIFT -- Excess fuel return --> TANK
    INJ1 -- Leakage fuel return --> TANK
    INJ2 -- Leakage fuel return --> TANK
    INJ3 -- Leakage fuel return --> TANK
    INJ4 -- Leakage fuel return --> TANK
    PLV -- Overpressure relief --> TANK
    RPS -- Vent --> TANK
```

### 4.2 Signal & Fuel Flow Separation (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSOR_BLOCK[Input Sensors]
      S1[CKP CMP]
      S2[APP Pedal]
      S3[Temperature Sensors]
      S4[Pressure and Air Sensors]
    end

    SENSOR_BLOCK --> ECU_CENTER[ECU Control Unit]
    ECU_CENTER -- Pressure setpoint --> FMU_BLOCK[Fuel Metering Unit]
    FMU_BLOCK --> HP_PUMP_BLOCK[High Pressure Pump]
    HP_PUMP_BLOCK --> RAIL_BLOCK[Common Rail]
    ECU_CENTER -- Injection timing and duration --> INJ_BLOCK[Injectors 1 to N]
    RAIL_BLOCK --> INJ_BLOCK
    INJ_BLOCK --> COMBUSTION[Combustion Chamber]
    COMBUSTION -- Exhaust --> EGR_TURBO[Turbo and EGR System]
    EGR_TURBO -- Intake air feedback --> S4
```

### 4.3 Pressure Stage Map (Visual Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1 Tank Atmospheric 0 bar]
    P2[Stage 2 Lift Pump 3-5 bar]
    P3[Stage 3 HP Pump Outlet 2000 bar]
    P4[Stage 4 Rail 1600-2000 bar]
    P5[Stage 5 Nozzle Exit 1500-1800 bar differential]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The pressure between the rail and the combustion chamber is the **injection pressure differential** — this is what atomizes the fuel. Modern systems target **> 1,500 bar differential** for proper atomization of BS-VI diesel into particles < 10 µm.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **List the major components of a CRDi system in the order of fuel flow from tank to cylinder.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Fuel tank → fuel strainer → electric lift pump → pressure regulator → fine fuel filter → radial-piston high-pressure pump → common rail (accumulator) → high-pressure injector lines → solenoid injectors → combustion chamber. *(1 mark for order, 1 mark for naming ≥ 7 components, 1 mark for mention of "low pressure" vs "high pressure" stages)*

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is the function of the Fuel Metering Unit (FMU) / Suction Control Valve (SCV) in a CRDi system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The FMU/SCV is a **proportionally controlled valve** on the suction side of the high-pressure pump. It meters the quantity of fuel allowed into the HP-pump chambers, thereby indirectly controlling the **common-rail pressure**. The ECU commands the SCV based on the difference between **desired rail pressure** (from the 3-D calibration map) and **actual rail pressure** (from the Rail Pressure Sensor). At low loads, SCV throttles inflow → less fuel is compressed → rail pressure drops. *(2 marks for function, 1 mark for ECU control loop)*

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 2]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat layout, explain the working of a Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) system used in a modern diesel engine. Label all major components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the ECU in controlling injection parameters. Explain pilot, main, and post injections with diagrams. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Layout & Component Functions (7 marks):**

A typical CRDi system consists of three circuits:

**Low-pressure circuit (1.5 marks):**
- **Fuel tank** — stores diesel.
- **Pre-filter / strainer** — removes coarse debris.
- **Electric lift pump** — supplies fuel at 3–5 bar.
- **Fine fuel filter (3–5 µm)** — protects HP pump and injectors from contamination.
- **Pressure regulator** — returns excess fuel to the tank.

**High-pressure circuit (3 marks):**
- **High-pressure (radial piston) pump** — driven by the camshaft at half engine speed; generates up to 2,000 bar. Has 3 pistons.
- **Fuel Metering Unit (SCV)** — controls quantity of fuel entering the HP pump → controls rail pressure.
- **Common rail** — high-pressure accumulator (20–60 cm³ volume) storing fuel at constant pressure.
- **Rail pressure sensor** — provides feedback to ECU.
- **Pressure limiter valve** — safety relief at 2,500 bar.

**Injection circuit (2.5 marks):**
- **High-pressure injection lines** — equal-length rigid steel pipes from rail to injectors.
- **Solenoid injectors** — one per cylinder; electronically actuated. Contain control valve, nozzle needle, and spring.
- **Return lines** — leakage fuel and overpressure relief return to the tank.

**Working (1 mark):**
The lift pump supplies clean low-pressure fuel to the HP pump. The HP pump compresses fuel to ~2,000 bar and delivers it to the rail. The rail maintains constant pressure. Based on sensor inputs, the ECU energizes the appropriate injector solenoid at the correct crank angle and for the correct duration. Fuel sprays into the combustion chamber with fine atomization, igniting due to high cylinder temperature and pressure.

**Sketch:** (Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram — a hand-drawn version with arrows and labels scores full marks.)

---

**(b) ECU Control & Multiple Injections (7 marks):**

**ECU control loop (2 marks):**
The ECU reads inputs from CKP, CMP, APP, CTS, MAP, MAF, and Rail Pressure Sensor. It runs algorithms every 2–5 ms and outputs commands to:
1. The FMU/SCV (rail pressure setpoint).
2. The four injector solenoids (injection timing, duration, and number).

**Injection parameters controlled (2 marks):**
1. **Injection pressure** — varies from 250 bar (idle) to 2,000 bar (full load).
2. **Injection timing** — usually 4–12° BTDC, advanced at low loads, retarded at high loads.
3. **Injection quantity** — calculated as $Q = BSFC \times P_{target} / (N \cdot N_{cyl})$.
4. **Number of injections** — pilot + main + after, controlled independently.

**Pilot, Main, Post Injections (3 marks):**
- **Pilot injection:** Small mass (1–2 mg) injected **~10–15° BTDC** before main. Pressure ~300 bar. Purpose: small pre-combustion → raises cylinder pressure gradually → **reduces combustion noise** and reduces NOx.
- **Main injection:** The largest dose (15–40 mg). Pressure 1,600–2,000 bar. Determines power output.
- **After (post) injection:** Small dose injected **~20–50° ATDC**. Purpose: **regenerates the Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** by raising exhaust temperature, and reduces emissions.

**Diagram (optional):** A typical crank-angle vs. fuel-rate plot showing 1–2 pilot pulses, 1 main pulse, 1–2 after pulses.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) system with emphasis on the high-pressure pump and the common rail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare CRDi with conventional jerk-pump and unit-injection systems. Why is CRDi preferred for BS-VI emission norms? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working — HP Pump & Common Rail (7 marks):**

**High-Pressure Pump (4 marks):**
- **Type:** Radial-piston pump with **3 pistons** arranged radially (120° apart).
- **Drive:** Coupled to the engine camshaft; rotates at **half engine speed** (gear ratio 1:2) for 4-stroke engines.
- **Construction:**
  - Camshaft with 3 cam lobes pushes each piston inward during one revolution.
  - Each piston has a **suction (intake) valve** (opens toward pump chamber from feed line) and a **delivery (outlet) valve** (opens toward rail).
  - The **Fuel Metering Unit (SCV)** is mounted on the suction side; it is a normally-closed proportional valve.
- **Working:**
  1. As the cam lobe moves away, piston retracts → low pressure opens SCV → fuel fills chamber.
  2. As the cam lobe pushes the piston inward, pressure rises → SCV closes → fuel is forced through delivery valve into the rail.
  3. Pressure continues to rise up to rail pressure (~2,000 bar) and stays there.
  4. The rail absorbs pressure pulses and maintains near-constant pressure for injection.

**Common Rail (3 marks):**
- **Construction:** A high-pressure stainless-steel tube (or forged steel) of volume **20–60 cm³**, with:
  - A feed port from the HP pump.
  - 4–6 outlet ports to injector lines.
  - A pressure sensor port.
  - A pressure-limiter valve port.
- **Function:**
  1. Stores fuel at constant high pressure.
  2. Decouples pressure generation from injection timing.
  3. Damps pressure oscillations (acting as an **accumulator**).
- **Working:** The rail pressure is maintained within ±20 bar of the setpoint by ECU controlling the SCV. Pressure limiter opens at 2,500 bar as a safety.

---

**(b) Comparison & BS-VI Justification (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):** (Refer to Section 3.3 Analytical Matrix.)

**Why CRDi is preferred for BS-VI (3 marks):**
1. **Multiple injections** — pilot + main + post → enables in-cylinder emission reduction (lower peak temperatures → lower NOx; controlled combustion → lower PM).
2. **High, controlled pressure** → fine atomization → complete combustion → lower CO and HC.
3. **EGR integration** — ECU can coordinate injection timing with EGR rate for optimal NOx-PM trade-off.
4. **DPF regeneration** — post injections raise exhaust temperature to burn soot off the DPF without separate burners.
5. **Real-time correction** — ECU can adjust injection based on sensor feedback (e.g., lambda, rail pressure).
6. Conventional jerk-pump has only **1 injection/cycle** at **lower pressure (~600 bar)** — cannot achieve the necessary combustion control for BS-VI/Euro 6.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU CRDi Layout Questions:**
> 1. **Drawing the layout but missing arrow direction** — examiners expect clearly marked arrows for fuel flow direction. **(-1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing SCV (Suction Control Valve) with PRV (Pressure Relief Valve)** — SCV is on the suction (low-pressure) side; PRV is on the rail (high-pressure) side. **(-1 to -2 marks)**
> 3. **Writing "Common Rail pressure is 200 bar"** — it is **1,600 – 2,500 bar** in modern systems. **(-2 marks)**
> 4. **Forgetting to mention ECU and sensor inputs** — a CRDi layout is incomplete without the ECU and at least 4 sensors. **(-1 mark)**
> 5. **Writing "fuel injector" without specifying solenoid or piezo** — KTU expects the type. **(-1 mark)**
> 6. **Missing return lines** in the layout — return is essential to complete the circuit. **(-1 mark)**
> 7. **Confusing pilot injection with pre-injection in SI engines** — pilot injection is a *diesel-specific* term. **(-1 mark)**
> 8. **Forgetting to label common rail volume** in numericals. **(-0.5 mark)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **CRDi = Common Rail Direct Injection** — pressure generation is decoupled from injection event.
- [x] **Three circuits:** Low-pressure (3–5 bar), High-pressure (up to 2,500 bar), Injection circuit.
- [x] **HP pump** = Radial-piston, 3-pistons, cam-driven at **½ engine speed** for 4-stroke.
- [x] **SCV (FMU)** = Suction Control Valve, controls rail pressure indirectly by metering fuel into HP pump.
- [x] **Rail** = accumulator, volume 20–60 cm³, smooths pressure pulsations.
- [x] **Rail Pressure** = **1,600 – 2,500 bar** in modern systems; older systems: 1,350–1,600 bar.
- [x] **Injectors:** Solenoid (1,600 bar) or Piezo (2,000+ bar); Piezo is faster (multiple injections up to 9/cycle).
- [x] **Pilot injection** = 1–2 mg, low pressure, **reduces combustion noise**.
- [x] **Main injection** = largest mass, determines torque.
- [x] **Post injection** = regenerates DPF; occurs ATDC.
- [x] **ECU** = brain; processes CKP, CMP, APP, MAP, MAF, CTS, IAT, RPS inputs.
- [x] **Key advantage:** Multiple injections + high pressure → low emissions, low noise, high efficiency.
- [x] **Fuel filter:** 3–5 µm — extremely clean fuel is mandatory.
- [x] **Formula to remember:** $P_{hyd} = p_{rail} \cdot V_d \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot N_{pump} \cdot \eta_v$ and $m_{inj} = C_d A_n \sqrt{2\rho \Delta p}\, t_{inj}$.
- [x] **BS-VI / Euro 6 compliance:** CRDi is **mandatory** for light-duty diesel vehicles.
- [x] **Memorize component order:** Tank → Strainer → Lift Pump → Regulator → Filter → HP Pump → Rail → Injector → Cylinder.
- [x] **Return path:** Injector leakage + Pressure limiter relief + Lift pump excess → back to tank.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components and Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Components and Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Engine Components — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> An **internal combustion (IC) engine** is a heat engine in which the combustion of a fuel (typically gasoline, diesel, or alternative fuel) occurs with an oxidizer (usually air) in a combustion chamber that is an integral part of the working fluid flow circuit. The high-pressure, high-temperature gases produced drive a piston, rotary blade, or rotor, producing mechanical work.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **"Components and Working"** refers to the systematic study of:
- **Structural components** of a reciprocating IC engine (the stationary and moving parts)
- **Working principles** of four-stroke and two-stroke cycles
- **Functional subsystems** — fuel supply, ignition, cooling, and lubrication

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Bicycle Pump Analogy:**
> Imagine a bicycle pump. When you push the handle down, the air inside is compressed (this is the **compression stroke**). When you release a small burst of air, it pushes back against the piston (this represents the **power stroke**). Now, replace your hand with a controlled explosion (combustion of fuel), and the bicycle pump with a sturdy steel cylinder — you have an IC engine! The explosion pushes the piston down with tremendous force, which turns the crankshaft, and ultimately your wheels.

## 1.3 Key Engine Performance Constants (KTU High-Yield Metrics)

The following are **standard KTU-mandated metrics** every student must memorize:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compression Ratio** | $r$ or $\varepsilon$ | SI: 6–12, CI: 14–22 | dimensionless |
| **Mean Effective Pressure** | $P_{me}$ | 8–12 (SI), 7–10 (CI) | bar |
| **Indicated Thermal Efficiency (Otto)** | $\eta_{th,Otto}$ | 50–60% | % |
| **Indicated Thermal Efficiency (Diesel)** | $\eta_{th,Diesel}$ | 35–45% | % |
| **Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption** | ISFC | 0.30–0.40 (SI), 0.22–0.30 (CI) | kg/kW·h |
| **Firing Order (4-cyl)** | — | 1-3-4-2 or 1-2-4-3 | sequence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly expects students to differentiate between the **major stationary components** (cylinder block, head, sump) and **moving components** (piston, connecting rod, crankshaft, camshaft, valves).

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot (for Foundation)

$$\text{IC Engines} \begin{cases} \text{Spark Ignition (SI)} \\ \text{Compression Ignition (CI)} \end{cases} \begin{cases} \text{2-Stroke} \\ \text{4-Stroke} \end{cases}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Engine Components — The "Why" and "How"

### 2.1.1 Cylinder Block (Stationary)

- **Material:** Cast iron or aluminum alloy (modern engines use **aluminum** for **40% weight reduction**).
- **Function:** Houses the cylinders where pistons reciprocate; provides rigidity and supports all auxiliaries.
- **Key Feature:** Contains **water jackets** (cooling passages) cast around the cylinder bores.
- **"Why" it matters:** Acts as the structural backbone; any warpage leads to **loss of compression and oil consumption**.

### 2.1.2 Cylinder Head (Stationary)

- **Material:** Cast iron or aluminum; bolted to the block via **head bolts** (torqued in **spiral sequence**).
- **Function:** Closes the top of the cylinder; contains **intake and exhaust ports**, **valve seats**, **spark plug hole** (SI) or **injector bore** (CI), and **combustion chamber**.
- **Head Gasket:** A multi-layer steel (MLS) gasket seals the head to the block — tolerates temperatures up to **300 °C**.

### 2.1.3 Piston (Moving)

- **Material:** Aluminum-silicon alloy (e.g., **Al-Si 12%**) — lightweight, good thermal conductivity.
- **Function:** Receives combustion pressure and transmits force via the connecting rod to the crankshaft.
- **Critical Features:**
  - **Crown (top):** Shape governs combustion characteristics (hemispherical, dished, flat).
  - **Ring Zone:** Houses **compression rings** (top 2) and **oil control ring** (bottom).
  - **Skirt:** Guides the piston in the bore; prevents contact with cylinder wall.

### 2.1.4 Piston Rings (Moving)

| Ring Type | Position | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Compression Ring (Top)** | 1st groove | Seals combustion gases above piston |
| **Compression Ring (Second)** | 2nd groove | Back-up seal; improves oil scraping |
| **Oil Control Ring** | 3rd groove | Scrapes excess oil back to sump |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tangential Pressure:** A typical compression ring exerts **10–20 N/mm²** against the cylinder wall, ensuring gas sealing.

### 2.1.5 Connecting Rod (Moving)

- **Function:** Transmits piston force to the crankshaft; converts reciprocating motion to rotary motion.
- **Material:** Forged steel (e.g., **SAE 4340**) or **powder metallurgy** for high-performance applications.
- **Two Ends:**
  - **Small End:** Connects to piston via **gudgeon pin (wrist pin)**.
  - **Big End:** Connects to crankshaft via **crankpin**; split-type with cap and **big-end bolts**.

### 2.1.6 Crankshaft (Moving)

- **Function:** Converts reciprocating motion of pistons into rotary motion; delivers power to the flywheel.
- **Material:** Forged steel, induction-hardened crankpins.
- **Main Journals:** Supported in main bearings within the block.
- **Crankpin:** Offset from the main axis by **half the stroke length ($r$)**.

### 2.1.7 Camshaft and Valve Train (Moving)

- **Function:** Operates intake and exhaust valves at precise timing.
- **Drive Mechanism:** **Timing chain**, **timing belt**, or **gear train** (all synced to crankshaft).
- **Valve Clearance:** Small gap (typically **0.15–0.30 mm** for SI, **0.30–0.50 mm** for CI) to allow thermal expansion.

### 2.1.8 Flywheel (Moving, Rotational)

- **Function:** Stores rotational kinetic energy; smooths out torque fluctuations.
- **Key Equation:** Kinetic energy stored:
$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$
where $I$ is the moment of inertia (kg·m²) and $\omega$ is angular velocity (rad/s).

### 2.1.9 Intake and Exhaust Manifolds (Stationary)

- **Intake Manifold:** Distributes air (or air-fuel mixture) to each cylinder.
- **Exhaust Manifold:** Collects exhaust gases and directs them to the catalytic converter.
- **Design Goal:** Minimize pressure drop; tuned length for **ram effect**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU board-exam favorite and appear in 80% of ESE questions on this module.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c} = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c}$ | Compression ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$ | Swept volume (per cylinder) | m³ |
| 3 | $r = \dfrac{L}{2}$ | Crank radius (half the stroke) | m |
| 4 | $\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon^{\gamma-1}}$ | Otto cycle efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_{th,Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(r_c - 1)}$ | Diesel cycle efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{th,Atkinson} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_c^{\gamma-1}(r_c - 1)}{(\gamma-1)(\ln r_c)}$ | Atkinson cycle (modern hybrid) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $r_c = \dfrac{V_3}{V_2}$ | Cut-off ratio (Diesel) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $W_{net} = P_{mi} \cdot V_s \cdot \dfrac{N}{2} \cdot n_c$ | Indicated work per cycle (4-stroke) | J |
| 9 | $P_i = \dfrac{W_{net} \cdot n_c \cdot N}{2 \cdot 60}$ for 4-stroke | Indicated power | kW |
| 10 | $P_i = \dfrac{P_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n_c}{60}$ for 2-stroke | Indicated power (2-stroke) | kW |
| 11 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_b}{P_i}$ | Mechanical efficiency | dimensionless |
| 12 | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_f}{P_b}$ | Brake specific fuel consumption | kg/kW·h |
| 13 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{th} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | Overall efficiency | dimensionless |
| 14 | $N = \dfrac{60 \cdot n}{t}$ | RPM calculation (n revs in t sec) | rpm |
| 15 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity | rad/s |
| 16 | $P_b = \tau \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Brake power from torque | W |
| 17 | $SFC_{Volumetric} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Air-fuel ratio (stoichiometric) | dimensionless |
| 18 | $TDC, BDC \Rightarrow V_{max}, V_{min}$ | Stroke end positions | — |

**Where:**
- $V_c$ = clearance volume, $V_s$ = swept volume
- $D$ = bore diameter, $L$ = stroke length
- $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ = piston cross-sectional area
- $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ (≈ **1.4** for air, **1.33** for combustion products)
- $n_c$ = number of cylinders
- $N$ = engine RPM, $P_{mi}$ = mean indicated pressure (bar → Pa conversion: ×10⁵)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These components and formulas are the foundation of:
> - **Automotive Design:** Engineers use $\varepsilon$ to design knock-resistant engines.
> - **Emission Control:** Higher $\eta_{th}$ ⇒ lower CO₂ per km.
> - **Hybrid Powertrains:** Atkinson cycle (used in Toyota Prius) improves real-world efficiency by 10–15%.
> - **Formula-1 Racing:** Modern F1 engines achieve $\varepsilon \approx 18:1$ with direct injection and 50% thermal efficiency (highest ever).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Geometric Compression Ratio

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most-asked derivation in KTU ESE for Module 2.**

### Statement:
Prove that the compression ratio of a reciprocating IC engine is given by:
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c}$$

### Given:
- $V_1$ = total volume at BDC (Bottom Dead Centre) = $V_c + V_s$
- $V_2$ = clearance volume at TDC (Top Dead Centre) = $V_c$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Define the swept volume.**
The swept volume is the volume displaced by the piston as it moves from TDC to BDC:
$$V_s = A \times L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L$$

**Step 2: Identify the volumes at the two dead centres.**
At BDC, the cylinder volume is at its **maximum**:
$$V_1 = V_c + V_s \quad \text{(clearance + swept)}$$

At TDC, the cylinder volume is at its **minimum** (just the clearance):
$$V_2 = V_c$$

**Step 3: Apply the definition of compression ratio.**
By definition, compression ratio is the ratio of maximum to minimum volume in the cylinder:
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$$

**Step 4: Substitute.**
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_c + V_s}{V_c}$$

**Step 5: Expand.**
$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_c}{V_c} + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c} = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c}$$

**Step 6: Final form.**
$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c} = 1 + \dfrac{\pi D^2 L / 4}{V_c}}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU style):
A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has bore $D = 80$ mm, stroke $L = 90$ mm, and clearance volume $V_c = 40$ cm³ per cylinder. Find $\varepsilon$.

**Solution:**
$$V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 (0.090) = 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 452.4 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$\varepsilon = 1 + \dfrac{452.4}{40} = 1 + 11.31 = \boxed{12.31}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory derivation — appears in 95% of KTU ESE question papers.**

### Statement:
Derive the air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle.

### Assumptions (Air-Standard Cycle):
1. Working fluid is **ideal gas** (air) with constant specific heats.
2. All processes are **internally reversible**.
3. Combustion is replaced by **heat addition at constant volume**.

### The Four Processes:
| Process | From → To | Type |
|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | BDC → TDC | Isentropic compression |
| 2 → 3 | TDC (heat in) | Constant volume heat addition |
| 3 → 4 | TDC → BDC | Isentropic expansion (power stroke) |
| 4 → 1 | BDC (heat out) | Constant volume heat rejection |

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Heat added at constant volume (Process 2 → 3).**
$$Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 2: Heat rejected at constant volume (Process 4 → 1).**
$$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 3: Net work done equals net heat transfer (1st Law).**
$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = m C_v [(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$$

**Step 4: Apply the isentropic relations.**
For Process 1 → 2 (isentropic compression):
$$\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \varepsilon^{\gamma-1}$$
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \varepsilon^{\gamma-1}$$

For Process 3 → 4 (isentropic expansion):
$$\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma-1} = \varepsilon^{\gamma-1}$$
$$T_3 = T_4 \cdot \varepsilon^{\gamma-1}$$

**Step 5: Substitute into thermal efficiency formula.**
$$\eta_{th,Otto} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

**Step 6: Using the isentropic relations, simplify.**
Let $r = \varepsilon^{\gamma-1}$. Then $T_2 = T_1 r$ and $T_3 = T_4 r$. Substituting:
$$\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{T_4 r - T_1 r} = 1 - \dfrac{T_4 - T_1}{r(T_4 - T_1)} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r}$$

**Step 7: Final form.**
$$\boxed{\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon^{\gamma-1}}}$$

### Interpretation:
- $\eta_{th}$ increases with **higher compression ratio**.
- $\eta_{th}$ increases with **higher $\gamma$** (monoatomic gases have higher $\gamma$).
- For $\varepsilon = 8$, $\gamma = 1.4$: $\eta_{th} = 1 - 1/8^{0.4} = 1 - 0.435 = \mathbf{56.5\%}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Indicated Power (4-Stroke Engine)

### Statement:
Derive the expression for indicated power of a multi-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine.

### Derivation:

**Step 1: Work done per cycle per cylinder.**
The work done per cycle is:
$$W_c = P_{mi} \times V_s = P_{mi} \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L$$
(where $P_{mi}$ is in Pa, all dimensions in m)

**Step 2: Number of power strokes per second per cylinder (4-stroke).**
In a 4-stroke engine, **one power stroke occurs every 2 revolutions**.
$$\text{Power strokes per second per cylinder} = \dfrac{N}{2 \times 60} = \dfrac{N}{120}$$

**Step 3: Total power for $n_c$ cylinders.**
$$P_i = W_c \times \text{power strokes/sec} \times n_c$$
$$P_i = P_{mi} \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \times \dfrac{N}{120} \times n_c$$

**Step 4: Convert to kW (divide by 1000).**
$$\boxed{P_i = \dfrac{P_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n_c \cdot N}{120 \times 1000} \text{ kW}}$$

### Numerical Example:
A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke engine has $D = 90$ mm, $L = 100$ mm, $P_{mi} = 8$ bar, $N = 3000$ rpm. Find $P_i$.

**Solution:**
$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.09)^2 = 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$P_i = \dfrac{8 \times 10^5 \times 0.100 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 3000}{120 \times 1000}$$
$$P_i = \dfrac{8 \times 10^5 \times 0.100 \times 6.362 \times 10^{-3} \times 12000}{120000}$$
$$P_i = \dfrac{610752}{120} = 5089.6 \text{ W} = \boxed{5.09 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: 2-Stroke vs 4-Stroke

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always include this table when answering 14-mark questions on engine cycles.**

| Parameter | 2-Stroke Engine | 4-Stroke Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Power strokes per revolution** | 1 | 1/2 |
| **Number of revolutions per cycle** | 1 | 2 |
| **Power-to-weight ratio** | **Higher (~1.8×)** | Lower |
| **Thermal efficiency** | Lower (scavenging losses) | Higher |
| **Mechanical complexity** | Simpler | More complex (camshaft) |
| **Cooling requirement** | Higher (more frequent firing) | Moderate |
| **Lubrication** | Total-loss (petrol) or splash (diesel) | Force-fed (pressure lubrication) |
| **Exhaust emissions** | Higher (incomplete scavenging) | Lower |
| **Typical applications** | Mopeds, chainsaws, small generators | Cars, trucks, industrial |
| **Power equation** | $P_i = \dfrac{P_{mi} L A n_c N}{60}$ | $P_i = \dfrac{P_{mi} L A n_c N}{120}$ |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: SI vs CI Engine

| Parameter | SI (Petrol) Engine | CI (Diesel) Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel** | Gasoline, LPG, CNG | Diesel, biodiesel |
| **Ignition** | Spark plug | Compression auto-ignition |
| **Compression ratio** | 6–12 | 14–22 |
| **Air-fuel mixture** | Premixed (carburetor/port injection) | Direct injection into hot air |
| **Throttle** | Required (pumping loss) | Not required |
| **Thermal efficiency** | 25–35% (brake) | 35–45% (brake) |
| **Weight** | Lighter | Heavier (robust construction) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (precision injectors) |
| **Starting under load** | Difficult | Easy |
| **Speed range** | Higher (7000+ rpm) | Lower (4500 rpm max) |
| **Emissions** | CO, unburnt HC | NOx, particulates |
| **Cycle used** | Otto | Diesel (dual/Sabathé for combustion) |

---

## 3.6 Working of a 4-Stroke SI Engine — Exhaustive Step Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most frequently asked 14-mark question in KTU ESE.**

### Stroke 1 — **Intake Stroke** (Crankshaft 0° → 180°)
- Piston moves from **TDC to BDC**.
- **Intake valve:** OPEN; **Exhaust valve:** CLOSED.
- **Camshaft lobe** lifts intake valve via rocker arm.
- **Downward motion** creates low pressure (suction).
- **Carburetor/Port Injector** delivers atomized fuel-air mixture.
- **Process:** Approximately **constant pressure** (slight drop due to throttling).
- **Mixture enters at ~0.9 atm**, T = **30–80 °C**.

### Stroke 2 — **Compression Stroke** (Crankshaft 180° → 360°)
- Piston moves from **BDC to TDC**.
- Both valves **CLOSED**.
- Mixture is compressed to **~1/10th of its original volume** (for $\varepsilon = 10$).
- **Process:** Adiabatic (isentropic) compression.
- **Temperature rise:** From 350 K to **~600–700 K** (well below auto-ignition).
- **Pressure rise:** From ~1 atm to **~10–15 bar**.

### Stroke 3 — **Power (Expansion) Stroke** (Crankshaft 360° → 540°)
- Piston at TDC; **spark plug fires** 5–30° **BTDC** (Before TDC).
- **Combustion:** Rapid, approximately **constant volume** (Otto cycle).
- **Pressure spikes** to **30–50 bar**; **temperature** to **~2500 K**.
- Piston pushed from TDC to BDC by high-pressure gas.
- **Both valves closed** during this stroke.
- This is the **only stroke producing useful work**.

### Stroke 4 — **Exhaust Stroke** (Crankshaft 540° → 720°)
- Piston moves from **BDC to TDC**.
- **Exhaust valve:** OPEN; **Intake valve:** CLOSED**.
- Camshaft lobe lifts exhaust valve just before BDC (typically **10–25° BBDC**).
- **Burnt gases expelled** (CO₂, H₂O, N₂, CO, unburnt HC).
- Pressure drops to slightly above atmospheric.
- **Exhaust valve closes after TDC** (valve overlap prevents reverse flow).
- Cycle repeats at **720°** (one full cycle = **2 revolutions of crankshaft**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Firing Order (4-cyl):** 1-3-4-2 means cylinder #1 fires, then #3, then #4, then #2. This balances the crankshaft by ensuring no two adjacent cylinders fire consecutively.

---

## 3.7 Working of a 2-Stroke Engine — Step Walkthrough

### Operation (Crankcase-scavenged, 2-stroke petrol):
- **Stroke 1 (Up):**
  - Piston at BDC covers **transfer port** and **exhaust port** openings.
  - **Crankcase volume increases** → low pressure draws fresh charge from carburetor through **reed valve** (one-way).
- **Top of piston:**
  - **Combustion** occurs; high pressure pushes piston down (power).
- **Stroke 2 (Down):**
  - Piston moves down; **exhaust port** opens first (top of cylinder) → exhaust gases escape.
  - **Transfer port** then opens; fresh charge from crankcase rushes up, **scavenging** the cylinder.
  - Piston deflects incoming charge using a **deflector crown** to prevent direct loss through exhaust.

### Advantages & Disadvantages:
- ✅ **No valves, no camshaft, no pushrods** — simpler.
- ✅ **Power every revolution** — high power-to-weight ratio.
- ❌ **Scavenging losses** — some fresh charge escapes unburnt.
- ❌ **Higher emissions** and **lower thermal efficiency**.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **IVO** = Intake Valve Opens, **IVC** = Intake Valve Closes, **EVO** = Exhaust Valve Opens, **EVC** = Exhaust Valve Closes. Valve overlap occurs between EVC of one cycle and IVO of the next.
