> 6. **Avoid overwriting** in the answer booklet; the examiner has limited time and unclear numbers may be marked wrong even if logically correct.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Basic Construction**

- ✅ Engine is a *system of sub-assemblies* — **Stationary + Reciprocating + Rotating** members.
- ✅ **Cylinder Block** = main body; made of cast iron (heavy-duty) or Al-alloy (passenger cars).
- ✅ **Cylinder Head** contains combustion chamber, valves, and spark plug/injector bore.
- ✅ **Piston** is aluminium-alloy, has a *crown*, *ring grooves*, *skirt*, and *gudgeon pin*.
- ✅ **Piston Rings** = 2 compression + 1 oil-control ring (typical); seal gases and oil.
- ✅ **Connecting Rod** converts reciprocating → rotary motion; small end, I-beam shank, big end.
- ✅ **Crankshaft** is the rotary output shaft; forged steel with main journals, crankpins, and counterweights.
- ✅ **Camshaft** runs at **half crankshaft speed** (1:2 ratio) in 4-stroke engines.
- ✅ **Flywheel** stores kinetic energy, smooths power, and carries starter ring gear.
- ✅ **Swept Volume** $V_s = (\pi/4) D^2 L$ per cylinder.
- ✅ **Displacement** $V_d = (\pi/4) D^2 L N$ (where N = number of cylinders).
- ✅ **Compression Ratio** $r = (V_s + V_c)/V_c$; typical: **8–12** (SI), **14–22** (CI).
- ✅ Engine layouts: **Inline, V, Flat (Boxer), W, Radial** — most Indian cars use I3 or I4.
- ✅ Materials: Block = cast iron/Al; Piston = Al-Si alloy; Conrod & Crank = forged steel.
- ✅ **Piston speed** $\bar{v}_p = 2 L N_r / 60$ — keep below **20 m/s** for long engine life.
- ✅ Always include a **labelled diagram** for component-based questions — 2 to 3 marks are reserved for the sketch.
- ✅ Unit conversion: **1 cc = 1 cm³ = 10⁻⁶ m³**; 1000 cc = 1 litre.
- ✅ Bore-Stroke ratio > 1 → *oversquare* (high-rpm petrol); < 1 → *undersquare* (low-end torque diesel).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Hydrostatic Drives. Hydrodynamic Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Hydrostatic Drives. Hydrodynamic Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydrostatic Drives — Formal Definition

A **Hydrostatic Drive** is a power transmission system in which energy is transmitted from the prime mover (typically an internal combustion engine or electric motor) to the driven load using a **pressurized hydraulic fluid** confined within a closed-loop circuit. The energy transfer occurs by virtue of the **pressure potential** (static head) of the fluid, in strict contrast to kinetic energy transfer seen in hydrodynamic systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *“A hydrostatic drive is a closed-loop, hydrostatic fluid power system in which a variable or fixed displacement pump pressurizes hydraulic oil, and the pressurized fluid drives a hydraulic motor coupled to the load. Output speed/torque is varied by altering either fluid flow rate (volume control) or pressure drop across a throttling element (throttle control).”*

### 1.1.1 Working Principle (Intuitive Analogy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Pressurized Water Gun"**
> Imagine a heavy industrial water gun. You pump a handle (this is your **hydraulic pump** driven by an engine). The pressurized water shoots out through a narrow nozzle. Now, instead of wasting that water, channel it onto a small water wheel (this is your **hydraulic motor**). The water wheel spins and turns your load. The faster/slower you pump, the faster/slower the wheel spins. In essence: **Pressure of fluid → Mechanical rotation of motor shaft → Load**. This is exactly what happens inside an excavator's swing motor or a forklift's lift cylinder system.

### 1.1.2 Primary Constituent Components

| S.No | Component | Functional Role | Real-World Example |
|:----:|:----------|:----------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | **Prime Mover** | Provides input mechanical power | Diesel engine of tractor |
| 2 | **Hydraulic Pump** | Converts mechanical energy to hydraulic pressure energy | Variable displacement axial piston pump |
| 3 | **Control Valves** | Regulate flow/pressure/direction | Directional control valve (DCV) |
| 4 | **Hydraulic Motor** | Converts hydraulic energy back to mechanical energy | Radial piston motor in winch drive |
| 5 | **Reservoir** | Stores fluid, dissipates heat, allows air separation | Hydraulic oil tank |
| 6 | **Filters** | Remove contaminants | Return-line filter (typically **10 µm** absolute) |
| 7 | **Cooler/Heat Exchanger** | Removes heat generated by throttling losses | Oil-to-air radiator |
| 8 | **High-Pressure Hoses/Pipes** | Conduit for pressurized fluid | SAE 100R12 rated hoses |

---

## 1.2 Hydrodynamic Drives — Formal Definition

A **Hydrodynamic Drive** is a fluid power transmission device in which energy is transmitted through the **momentum exchange (kinetic energy)** of a working fluid — typically automatic transmission fluid (ATF) — between a driving member and a driven member, without any static pressure containment in the working chambers. The most ubiquitous automotive application is the **Torque Converter**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *“A hydrodynamic drive transmits power by the kinetic energy of a circulating fluid. The basic unit is the torque converter, comprising an impeller (driven by the engine), a turbine (driving the transmission input shaft), and a stator (reactor) mounted on a one-way clutch to alter the fluid's angular momentum.”*

### 1.2.1 Working Principle (Intuitive Analogy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Fan Experiment"**
> Take two small DC fans and place them facing each other about 10 cm apart. Power fan A (the **impeller**). The air molecules it blows acquire kinetic energy (velocity). This moving air strikes fan B (the **turbine**), causing it to spin. Now place a small stationary fin (the **stator/reactor**) between them, but allow it to freewheel. You will notice fan B spins **much faster** than before. That fin redirected the air's flow to give it an extra push. This is exactly the principle of a torque converter — fluid (oil) does the work that air did in our fan experiment, and the stator multiplies torque.

### 1.2.2 Primary Constituent Components

| S.No | Component | Functional Role | Material Typical |
|:----:|:----------|:----------------|:------------------|
| 1 | **Impeller (Pump)** | Driven by engine flywheel; imparts kinetic energy to fluid | Cast aluminum alloy |
| 2 | **Turbine (Runner)** | Driven by fluid; transmits output to transmission input shaft | Cast aluminum alloy |
| 3 | **Stator (Reactor)** | Redirects returning fluid to impeller; multiplies torque | Cast aluminum alloy, with integral **one-way (overrunning) clutch** |
| 4 | **Transmission Fluid (ATF)** | Working fluid; absorbs heat, transmits momentum | Mineral/synthetic oil (e.g., Dexron III, Mercon LV) |
| 5 | **Lock-up Clutch (Modern units)** | Mechanically couples impeller & turbine to eliminate slip at cruising speeds | Friction disc with hydraulic actuation |
| 6 | **Housing / Cover** | Contains fluid, mounts to engine flywheel | Sheet steel / cast iron |

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Contrast: Hydrostatic vs. Hydrodynamic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Quick-Recall Box:**
> *“Hydro-**STATIC** → **STATIC** pressure (Pa); fluid does not move fast; chambers are sealed. Hydro-**DYNAMIC** → **DYNAMIC** momentum (kg·m/s); fluid whirls at high speed; chambers are open to each other.”*

| Parameter | Hydrostatic Drive | Hydrodynamic Drive |
|:----------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| Energy Form | Pressure (potential) | Momentum (kinetic) |
| Fluid Velocity | Low (typically **1–6 m/s** in lines) | High (tangential, up to **20–30 m/s** in converter) |
| Operating Pressure | High (**20–45 MPa** in heavy machinery) | Low (charge pressure only, **0.3–0.8 MPa**) |
| Efficiency at Stall | High (full torque available) | Low (~0%, fluid churns) |
| Speed Control | Excellent (stepless) | Limited (stepless, but narrow range) |
| Self-Protection | Requires relief valves | Inherently self-limiting |
| Common Example | Excavator swing, tractor PTO assist, winches | Automatic transmission torque converter |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hydrostatic Drives

Hydrostatic drives are classified based on **how output speed/flow is regulated** and based on **circuit topology**.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Control Method

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-asked classification** in KTU Module 4 questions. Memorize the three control philosophies and the corresponding Greek letter terminology.

#### (A) Throttle-Control (Valve-Control) / Metered Control
- A **throttle valve** is placed in the line to create a deliberate pressure drop.
- Pump delivers **constant maximum flow**; the excess flow is throttled and dissipated as heat.
- Energy efficiency is **poor** at partial loads.
- Simple and cheap — used in low-power applications (small tractors, dump-trailer lifts).

#### (B) Volume-Control (Displacement-Control) / "Load-Sensing" variant included
- Either pump displacement (V_p) or motor displacement (V_m) is varied.
- Flow to the motor is varied by changing the **swash plate angle** (axial piston units).
- **High efficiency** — no throttling losses.
- Used in modern construction equipment (JCB 3DX, CAT excavators, Komatsu).

#### (C) Load-Sensing (LS) / Pressure-Flow Control (Advanced variant)
- A pressure-compensator on the pump adjusts displacement to match load demand.
- Maintains only the pressure needed to overcome the load + a small standby margin.
- Saves energy, reduces heat generation, extends component life.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Circuit Topology

| Topology | Description | Pump Location | Typical Use |
|:---------|:------------|:--------------|:------------|
| **Open-Circuit** | Pump draws from reservoir, fluid returns to reservoir after motor | Reservoir in return line | Mobile equipment with cooler |
| **Closed-Circuit** | Pump and motor are connected in a sealed loop; fluid does not return to reservoir directly | Reservoir acts as make-up / charge | Hydrostatic transmissions in loaders, combine harvesters |
| **Series Hydrostatic** | Two or more motors in series (same flow through all) | Single pump | Conveyors, multi-wheel drive |
| **Parallel Hydrostatic** | Two or more motors in parallel (flow splits) | Single pump with flow divider | Independent wheel motors |

### 2.1.3 Schematic Comparison

```
   OPEN CIRCUIT                     CLOSED CIRCUIT
   -----------                      ------------
  [Pump]───►──[Motor]───►           [Pump]◄──►[Motor]
     ▲          │                       │       │
     │          ▼                       │       │
  [Reservoir]◄──┘                  [Charge Pump]──[Relief]
     ▲                                          │
     └────────────── Cooler ◄──────────────────┘
```

---

## 2.2 Governing Equations of Hydrostatic Drives

### 2.2.1 Pump Output Flow

The theoretical flow delivered by a positive-displacement pump is:

$$
Q_{p} = V_{p} \cdot n_{p} \cdot \eta_{v,p}
$$

where the symbols carry the meanings tabulated below.

### 2.2.2 Motor Output Torque & Speed

$$
T_{m} = \dfrac{V_{m} \cdot \Delta p \cdot \eta_{m,m}}{2\pi}
$$

$$
n_{m} = \dfrac{Q_{p}}{V_{m}} \cdot \eta_{v,m}
$$

### 2.2.3 Overall Hydrostatic Drive Efficiency

$$
\eta_{HD} = \eta_{v,p} \cdot \eta_{m,p} \cdot \eta_{v,m} \cdot \eta_{m,m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a well-designed hydrostatic transmission in the **mid-speed range**, $\eta_{HD} \approx 0.80$ to $0.88$ is industry-typical. Below 10% of max speed, efficiency drops sharply.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Hydrostatic)

| S.No | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Boundary / Note |
|:----:|:----------|:------:|:--------|:----:|:----------------|
| 1 | Pump flow | $Q_p$ | $V_p \cdot n_p \cdot \eta_{v,p}$ | m³/s | $V_p$ in m³/rev, $n_p$ in rev/s |
| 2 | Pump input torque | $T_p$ | $\dfrac{V_p \cdot \Delta p}{2\pi \cdot \eta_{m,p}}$ | N·m | Pressure rises across pump |
| 3 | Motor output torque | $T_m$ | $\dfrac{V_m \cdot \Delta p \cdot \eta_{m,m}}{2\pi}$ | N·m | Highest at stall ($\omega_m=0$) |
| 4 | Motor output speed | $n_m$ | $\dfrac{Q_p}{V_m} \cdot \eta_{v,m}$ | rev/s | Infinite if $V_m \to 0$ (avoid!) |
| 5 | Motor output power | $P_m$ | $T_m \cdot 2\pi n_m$ | W | Useful power at coupling |
| 6 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{HD}$ | $\eta_{v,p} \eta_{m,p} \eta_{v,m} \eta_{m,m}$ | – | Product of all four |
| 7 | Pressure setting of relief valve | $p_{set}$ | $1.1 \cdot p_{max,continuous}$ | Pa | Safety factor 1.1 |
| 8 | Pipe velocity (recommended) | $v_{oil}$ | $\dfrac{Q}{A_{pipe}}$ | m/s | Keep $\leq 6$ m/s in suction, $\leq 25$ m/s in pressure line |
| 9 | Torque–speed of motor | $T_m$ vs $n_m$ | Linear decline from $T_{stall}$ to $0$ at $n_{max}$ | – | Idealized linear characteristic |
| 10 | Power dissipation in throttle | $P_{throttle}$ | $\Delta p_{throttle} \cdot Q_{throttled}$ | W | The waste heat source |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> Hydrostatic drives are the *only* practical way to provide **stepless, infinitely variable transmission (IVT)** in heavy earth-moving machinery (Komatsu PC200 excavator swing, Volvo wheel-loader travel drives). They are also essential where the driven member must hold position under load without consuming energy (e.g., crane booms, where load-holding valves + hydrostatic lock are used).

---

## 2.4 Theory of Hydrodynamic Drives — Torque Converter

### 2.4.1 The Three-Element Model

A torque converter has three bladed wheels enclosed in a fluid-filled toroidal housing:

1. **Impeller (Pump) `P`** — bolted to the engine flywheel; acts as a centrifugal pump.
2. **Turbine `T`** — splined to the transmission input shaft; extracts energy from the fluid.
3. **Stator (Reactor) `S`** — mounted on a **one-way (sprag/roller) clutch**; redirects fluid returning from the turbine back to the impeller.

### 2.4.2 Velocity-Triangle Analysis (Blade Theory)

For each rotor, the Euler turbine equation gives energy transfer per unit mass:

$$
W = U \cdot (V_{u,out} - V_{u,in})
$$

where $U$ is blade speed and $V_u$ is the tangential (whirl) component of absolute velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> The stator contributes **zero net energy** (it has no rotation), but it drastically **alters the whirl component** of the fluid leaving the turbine, thereby multiplying the torque that the impeller must subsequently impart.

### 2.4.3 Torque Multiplication Concept

Define:
- **Speed Ratio (SR):** $SR = \dfrac{n_{turbine}}{n_{impeller}}$
- **Torque Ratio (TR):** $TR = \dfrac{T_{turbine}}{T_{impeller}}$

At **stall** (vehicle held stationary, engine idling at high RPM):
- $SR = 0$ (turbine is not turning).
- $TR$ reaches its **maximum** value, typically $1.8$ to $2.5$ for a single-stage converter. Multi-stage converters can reach $TR_{stall} = 4.0$ to $5.0$.

### 2.4.4 Three Modes of Torque Converter Operation

| Mode | Stator Action | Torque Ratio | Efficiency | Equivalent To |
|:----:|:--------------|:------------:|:----------:|:--------------|
| **Converter mode** (SR < ~0.85) | Stator is *stationary* (sprag clutch locked) | TR > 1 (multiplied) | 0 → ~0.85 | Torque amplification |
| **Coupling mode** (SR > ~0.85) | Stator *freewheels* (sprag clutch overruns) | TR ≈ 1 | Approaches ~0.95 | Fluid coupling only |
| **Lock-up mode** (modern units) | Mechanical clutch locks impeller to turbine | TR = SR (ratio = 1) | ~0.98 (no slip) | Solid coupling |

### 2.4.5 Why the Stator is Needed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without the stator, as the turbine approaches the impeller's speed, the fluid returning from the turbine hits the impeller blades from the *back* (i.e., with a whirl component opposing impeller rotation). This would **oppose** impeller motion. The stator re-directs this fluid so that it re-enters the impeller with the *correct* whirl direction. As a result, the impeller has to do less work, meaning the engine can drive the same load while the turbine outputs MORE torque than the impeller receives.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Hydrodynamic)

| S.No | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Expression | Unit | Notes |
|:----:|:----------|:------:|:---------------------|:----:|:------|
| 1 | Speed Ratio | $SR$ | $\dfrac{n_T}{n_P}$ | – | $0$ at stall, $\to 1$ at coupling |
| 2 | Torque Ratio | $TR$ | $\dfrac{T_T}{T_P}$ | – | Maximum at stall |
| 3 | Stall Torque Ratio | $TR_{stall}$ | $1.8$ to $5.0$ (typ.) | – | Single-stage: 1.8–2.5; Multi-stage: 3.5–5.0 |
| 4 | Stall Torque | $T_{stall}$ | $TR_{stall} \cdot T_{engine,stall}$ | N·m | Max torque transmitted |
| 5 | Efficiency (converter) | $\eta$ | $TR \cdot SR$ | – | Triangle of K (capacity), torque ratio, efficiency |
| 6 | Capacity factor (K-factor) | $K$ | $\dfrac{n_P}{\sqrt{\Delta p / \rho}}$ | – | Used in similitude / scaling |
| 7 | Fluid power at impeller | $P_P$ | $T_P \cdot \omega_P$ | W | Input mechanical power |
| 8 | Fluid power at turbine | $P_T$ | $T_T \cdot \omega_T$ | W | Output mechanical power |
| 9 | Slip | $s$ | $1 - SR$ | – | Used in lock-up control |
| 10 | Lock-up engagement speed | $n_{lockup}$ | $\sim 1500$–$2000$ rpm (typical) | rpm | When TCU commands lock-up clutch ON |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> Torque converters are *the* standard coupling between an engine and a planetary automatic transmission (AT/AMT/DCT in modern cars). They allow the engine to keep running while the vehicle is stationary (stall), provide smooth launch torque multiplication (no clutch pedal needed), and reduce driveline shock. Without them, automatic transmissions as we know them would not exist.

---

## 2.6 Coupling vs. Converter — Critical Distinction for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

| Feature | Fluid Coupling | Torque Converter |
|:--------|:---------------|:-----------------|
| Stator present? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Torque ratio at stall | $TR = 1$ (no multiplication) | $TR > 1$ (typically 1.8–2.5) |
| Efficiency at stall | 0% (churning) | 0% (churning) |
| Peak efficiency | ~95% (coupling mode) | ~85% (converter mode), ~95% (coupling mode) |
| Typical Application | Large marine drives, industrial fans, some off-road vehicles | Passenger-car automatic transmissions |
| Torque converter function | **None** — only smooths torsional vibrations | **Multiplies** engine torque during launch |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Torque-Speed Characteristic of a Hydrostatic Motor

**Problem Context:** A hydrostatic drive uses a fixed-displacement pump (driven by a constant-speed engine) feeding a variable-displacement hydraulic motor. Derive the **torque–speed relationship** of the motor as motor displacement is varied.

### Step 1 — Define Known Quantities

Let:
- Pump displacement = $V_p$ (m³/rev) — fixed
- Motor displacement = $V_m$ (m³/rev) — variable
- Pump rotational speed = $n_p$ (rev/s) — constant
- Volumetric efficiency of pump = $\eta_{v,p}$
- Volumetric efficiency of motor = $\eta_{v,m}$
- Mechanical efficiency of motor = $\eta_{m,m}$
- Pressure drop across motor = $\Delta p$ (Pa)

### Step 2 — Express the Delivered Flow

Pump theoretical flow per revolution = $V_p$ (m³/rev). Accounting for volumetric losses (internal leakage from high to low pressure side):

$$
Q_{p,delivered} = V_p \cdot n_p \cdot \eta_{v,p}
$$

This is the **actual** volume of fluid per second that enters the motor's inlet port.

### Step 3 — Motor Output Speed from Continuity

The motor's input flow fills its swept volume per revolution. With its own internal leakage, the effective flow available to displace the motor is $Q_{p,delivered} \cdot \eta_{v,m}$. Equating the volumetric displacement of the motor to the available flow:

$$
Q_{p,delivered} \cdot \eta_{v,m} = V_m \cdot n_m
$$

Solving for $n_m$:

$$
n_m = \dfrac{V_p \cdot n_p \cdot \eta_{v,p} \cdot \eta_{v,m}}{V_m}
$$

### Step 4 — Motor Output Torque from Pressure–Area

The fluid at pressure $\Delta p$ acts on the effective piston/rotor area, creating a force that produces torque. Theoretical torque is $\frac{V_m \cdot \Delta p}{2\pi}$. Real torque is reduced by mechanical losses (friction):

$$
T_m = \dfrac{V_m \cdot \Delta p \cdot \eta_{m,m}}{2\pi}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** $T_m$ is **directly proportional** to $V_m$ and $\Delta p$. So as we *increase* $V_m$, the motor becomes stronger but slower. As we *decrease* $V_m$, it becomes faster but weaker. This is the classical **horse-shifting** trade-off in hydrostatic drives.

### Step 5 — Combine to Get the Torque–Speed Hyperbola

Substituting Step 3 into Step 4 (eliminating the common factor $V_m$):

$$
T_m \cdot n_m = \dfrac{V_p \cdot n_p \cdot \eta_{v,p} \cdot \eta_{v,m} \cdot \Delta p \cdot \eta_{m,m}}{2\pi}
$$

But $T_m \cdot 2\pi n_m = P_m$ (output mechanical power). Therefore:

$$
P_m = V_p \cdot n_p \cdot \eta_{v,p} \cdot \eta_{v,m} \cdot \Delta p \cdot \eta_{m,m}
$$

And so the **constant-power hyperbola** in the $T_m$ vs. $n_m$ plane is:

$$
T_m = \dfrac{P_m}{2\pi n_m} \propto \dfrac{1}{n_m}
$$

### Step 3.6 — The Infinitely Variable Transmission (IVT) Characteristic

$$
\boxed{\; T_m = \dfrac{\eta_{v,p} \eta_{m,m} \eta_{v,m} V_p n_p \Delta p}{2 \pi n_m}\;}
$$

This is the **KTU 2024 K-Yield Equation** for hydrostatic drive output. It demonstrates that the output torque varies **inversely** with output speed — the defining IVT behaviour.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque Multiplication in a Torque Converter

**Problem Context:** Show analytically why a torque converter can deliver turbine torque $T_T$ greater than impeller input torque $T_P$.

### Step 1 — Euler's Turbomachinery Equation (per unit mass)

For any bladed rotor, the specific work (work per unit mass of fluid) is:

$$
w = U \cdot (V_{u,out} - V_{u,in})
$$

where:
- $U = \omega r$ = blade tangential velocity (m/s)
- $V_u$ = tangential component of absolute velocity (m/s)

### Step 2 — Apply to Impeller

Impeller receives fluid from stator with whirl $V_{u,1}$ and discharges with whirl $V_{u,2}$:

$$
w_P = U_P \cdot (V_{u,2} - V_{u,1}) = \dfrac{T_P \cdot \omega_P}{\dot{m}}
$$

Solving for the impeller torque:

$$
T_P = \dot{m} \cdot U_P \cdot (V_{u,2} - V_{u,1}) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\omega_P}
$$

Since $U_P = \omega_P \cdot r_P$:

$$
T_P = \dot{m} \cdot r_P \cdot (V_{u,2} - V_{u,1})
$$

### Step 3 — Apply to Turbine

Turbine receives fluid with whirl $V_{u,2}$ and discharges with whirl $V_{u,3}$:

$$
T_T = \dot{m} \cdot r_T \cdot (V_{u,2} - V_{u,3})
$$

### Step 4 — Apply to Stator (Reactor)

Stator is stationary ($\omega_S = 0$), so it imparts no work. However, it changes the fluid's angular momentum from $V_{u,3}$ (leaving turbine) to $V_{u,1}$ (re-entering impeller). Angular momentum balance on the fluid (control volume) gives the **stator reaction torque** $T_S$:

$$
T_S = \dot{m} \cdot r_S \cdot (V_{u,1} - V_{u,3})
$$

### Step 5 — Stall Condition Analysis (Why TR > 1)

At stall, $n_T = 0$ and the turbine blades are stationary. The fluid leaving the turbine with high whirl $V_{u,3}$ would normally impinge on the back of the impeller blades. The stator **redirects** this fluid so that $V_{u,1}$ has the *opposite sign* of $V_{u,3}$ (or at least a reduced magnitude opposing the impeller).

In an *ideal* converter at stall, the stator completely reverses the whirl:

$$
V_{u,1} = -V_{u,3}
$$

Now compute the turbine and impeller torques:

$$
T_T = \dot{m} \cdot r_T \cdot (V_{u,2} - V_{u,3})
$$

$$
T_P = \dot{m} \cdot r_P \cdot (V_{u,2} - V_{u,1}) = \dot{m} \cdot r_P \cdot (V_{u,2} + V_{u,3})
$$

Since $r_T = r_P = r$ (same mean radius) in most converters:

$$
T_T - T_P = \dot{m} \cdot r \cdot [(V_{u,2} - V_{u,3}) - (V_{u,2} + V_{u,3})] = -2 \dot{m} \cdot r \cdot V_{u,3}
$$

The negative sign on $V_{u,3}$ (since it is now in the reverse direction) yields:

$$
T_T - T_P = 2 \dot{m} \cdot r \cdot \vert V_{u,3} \vert
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\; T_T = T_P + 2 \dot{m} \cdot r \cdot \vert V_{u,3} \vert\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** At stall, turbine torque $T_T$ **exceeds** impeller input torque $T_P$ by an amount $2 \dot{m} r \vert V_{u,3} \vert$. This is the torque **multiplication** effect, a direct consequence of the stator's redirection of the return flow. The phenomenon is unique to hydrodynamic fluid machinery and has no analog in hydrostatic systems.

### Step 6 — Torque Ratio at Stall

$$
TR_{stall} = \dfrac{T_T}{T_P}\bigg\vert_{stall} = 1 + \dfrac{2 \dot{m} r \vert V_{u,3} \vert}{T_P}
$$

For a well-designed single-stage converter: $TR_{stall} \approx 1.8$ to $2.5$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Drive?

| Application Domain | Recommended Drive | Justification |
|:-------------------|:------------------|:--------------|
| Passenger car AT | Hydrodynamic (TC) | Smooth launch, no slip, lock-up efficiency |
| Heavy excavator travel | Hydrostatic (Closed) | Stepless speed, reverse without gear shifting, dynamic braking |
| Forklift lift cylinder | Hydrostatic (Throttle-controlled) | Position holding, high force at zero speed |
| Combine harvester reel | Hydrostatic (Volume-controlled) | Wide speed range, good efficiency |
| Large ship propulsion | Hydrodynamic (Coupling or multi-stage TC) | Massive torque, slip absorbs torsional vibration |
| Industrial press | Hydrostatic (Throttle-controlled) | High force, low speed, easy pressure control |
| Mining dump truck retarder | Hydrodynamic (TC with lock-up) | Reliable, dissipates heat well |
| Agricultural tractor PTO | Hydrostatic (Volume-controlled) | Independent speed control from engine RPM |


**How to read this diagram:**
- The **engine** drives the **variable pump** (axial piston type).
- Pressurized oil flows to the **DCV** which controls direction (forward/reverse).
- The **hydraulic motor** drives the **load** (e.g., wheels of a skid-steer loader).
- A small **charge pump** maintains ~2 MPa in the low-pressure side, replenishes leaks, and feeds the cooler.
- The **relief valve** opens at 42 MPa to protect the system.

## 4.3 Hydrodynamic Torque Converter — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TORQUE_CONVERTER[Hydrodynamic Torque Converter]
    ENG[Engine Flywheel] -->|CW Rotation| IMP[Impeller Pump]
    IMP -->|Tangential Fluid Velocity| TUR[Turbine]
    TUR -->|CW Rotation| TRANS[Transmission Input Shaft]
    TUR -->|Returning Whirl Fluid| STAT[Stator Reactor]
    STAT -->|Redirected Fluid| IMP
    STAT -.->|One Way Sprag Clutch| STATCLU[Sprag Freewheel]
    TRANS -->|Splines| PLAN[Planetary Gearset]
    LUC[Lock Up Clutch] -.->|Bypasses Fluid| TRANS
    end
    style ENG fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style IMP fill:#FF6347,color:#fff
    style TUR fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style STAT fill:#9370DB,color:#fff
    style STATCLU fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style LUC fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style PLAN fill:#D3D3D3,color:#000
```

**Reading guide:**
- The engine spins the **impeller**.
- The impeller flings oil outward by centrifugal action; this oil strikes the **turbine**, turning it (and the transmission).
- Used oil exits the turbine and meets the **stator**, which is locked by its one-way clutch at low turbine speeds.
- The stator reverses the flow direction, multiplying torque.
- When the turbine reaches ~85% of impeller speed, the stator freewheels — converter becomes a coupling.
- The **lock-up clutch** (modulated by the Transmission Control Unit) mechanically ties impeller and turbine for maximum efficiency on the highway.

## 4.4 Torque Converter — Velocity Triangle Schematic (Text Mode)

```
   ABSOLUTE VELOCITY VECTOR DECOMPOSITION
   --------------------------------------------
   
   V (absolute) = V_u (tangential) + V_w (radial)
   
                    ↗ V (absolute)
                   /
                  / 
                 /_____→ V_u (whirl, tangential)
                |
                |
                ↓ V_w (flow, radial)
                
   Relative velocity W = V - U (U = blade speed)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **velocity triangle** is the *single most important diagram* for understanding hydrodynamic energy transfer. The area of the triangle (or the change in whirl) directly gives the energy transfer per unit mass of fluid, per Euler's equation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are designed to match KTU 2024 Part A (Module-level internal short answer) style. Each is **3 marks** and expects a concise, technically precise answer.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q:** Differentiate between a **hydrostatic drive** and a **hydrodynamic drive**. State **one** automotive application of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 per major point + example):**

| # | Concept | Marks |
|:-:|:--------|:-----:|
| 1 | **Hydrostatic drive** transmits power by the **pressure** (potential energy) of a hydraulic fluid contained in a sealed circuit. A pump pressurizes the fluid; a motor converts the pressure back to mechanical rotation. | 1 |
| 2 | **Hydrodynamic drive** transmits power by the **kinetic energy (momentum)** of a fluid, typically through a torque converter (impeller-turbine-stator). No static pressure containment; energy transfer is via fluid velocity. | 1 |
| 3 | **Applications:** Hydrostatic → hydrostatic transmission in **skid-steer loaders / excavators**; Hydrodynamic → **automatic transmission torque converter in cars**. | 1 |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q:** What is the function of the **stator (reactor)** in a hydrodynamic torque converter? Why is it mounted on a **one-way clutch**?

**Model Answer:**

| # | Point | Marks |
|:-:|:------|:-----:|
| 1 | The stator **redirects** the fluid returning from the turbine back to the impeller with the correct whirl direction. | 1 |
| 2 | This redirection causes the fluid to re-enter the impeller with reduced opposing whirl, so the impeller does less work, and consequently the turbine can deliver **more torque** than the impeller receives — this is **torque multiplication**. | 1 |
| 3 | The stator is mounted on a **one-way (sprag) clutch** so that as the turbine speed approaches the impeller speed (coupling mode), the stator is free to rotate with the fluid, eliminating the **stator reaction torque** and improving efficiency (~95%) at high speed ratios. | 1 |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, Module 4 will typically feature a single 14-mark question (with internal choice) covering hydrostatic and hydrodynamic drives. Two alternative questions are given below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Hydrostatic Drive Focus [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Expected Pattern]

**(a)** With the aid of a **sketch**, explain the **working principle of a closed-circuit hydrostatic drive** used in a skid-steer loader. List its **main components** and explain the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A variable-displacement axial piston pump of **$V_p = 75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$** is driven by a diesel engine at **$n_p = 1800 \text{ rpm}$**. It feeds a fixed-displacement hydraulic motor of **$V_m = 60 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$**. Given volumetric efficiencies $\eta_{v,p} = 0.97$, $\eta_{v,m} = 0.95$ and mechanical efficiencies $\eta_{m,p} = 0.92$, $\eta_{m,m} = 0.90$, determine:
- (i) The **motor output speed** in rpm.
- (ii) The **motor output torque** in N·m, if the system pressure is **$\Delta p = 25 \text{ MPa}$**.
- (iii) The **overall hydrostatic drive efficiency**.
- (iv) The **output power** at the motor shaft in kW. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Sketch + Components (7 Marks)

**Sketch Description (3 marks for diagram; 3 marks for component explanation; 1 mark for application):**

```
         +---------------- Closed-Loop Hydrostatic Drive ---------------+
         |                                                                 |
         |  +-------+      +---------+      +---------+     +-----------+ |
         |  | Diesel|────► | Variable|══►═══| DCV     |═══►═| Hyd Motor | |
         |  |Engine |      | Piston  |      | (Forward/|     | V_m fixed | |
         |  |       |      | Pump    |═══◄══| Reverse)|◄═══ |           | |
         |  +-------+      +---------+      +---------+     +-----+-----+ |
         |       ▲              ▲                              |         |
         |       |              |    High-pressure side (H)     |         |
         |       |       Suction / Charge                      ▼         |
         |  +----+-----+                                              +---+---+
         |  |Charge    |◄─── Make-up Pump ──── Reservoir ── Filter ──| Cooler|
         |  |Pump 2MPa |                                                                  |
         |  +----------+                                                                  |
         +────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────+
```

**Component List & Functions (4 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|:----------|:---------|
| **Diesel Engine** | Prime mover — supplies mechanical input power |
| **Variable Pump** | Converts mechanical to hydraulic energy; varies flow via swash plate |
| **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** | Routes high-pressure oil to motor for forward or reverse rotation |
| **Hydraulic Motor** | Converts hydraulic energy back to mechanical rotation at the wheels |
| **Charge Pump** | Maintains 1.5–2.5 MPa in low-pressure side; replenishes internal leakage |
| **Relief Valve** | Protects system from over-pressure (set ~10% above max working pressure) |
| **Reservoir** | Stores fluid, dissipates heat, allows air/moisture separation |
| **Filter (10 µm)** | Removes contaminants to protect pump and motor |
| **Oil Cooler** | Removes heat generated by throttling and churning losses |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V_p = 75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $V_m = 60 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 60 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$
- $n_p = 1800 \text{ rpm} = 30 \text{ rev/s}$
- $\eta_{v,p} = 0.97$, $\eta_{v,m} = 0.95$, $\eta_{m,p} = 0.92$, $\eta_{m,m} = 0.90$
- $\Delta p = 25 \text{ MPa} = 25 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$

**(i) Motor Output Speed $n_m$:**

Pump actual delivered flow:
$$
Q_p = V_p \cdot n_p \cdot \eta_{v,p} = 75 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 \times 0.97
$$
$$
Q_p = 2.1825 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Motor speed:
$$
n_m = \dfrac{Q_p \cdot \eta_{v,m}}{V_m} = \dfrac{2.1825 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.95}{60 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, n_m = 34.55 \text{ rev/s} = 2073 \text{ rpm} \,}
$$

**(1.5 marks)** — correct setup; **(0.5 mark)** — correct numerical value.

**(ii) Motor Output Torque $T_m$:**

$$
T_m = \dfrac{V_m \cdot \Delta p \cdot \eta_{m,m}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 10^{6} \times 0.90}{2\pi}
$$

$$
T_m = \dfrac{60 \times 25 \times 0.90}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1350}{6.2832}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, T_m = 214.86 \text{ N·m} \,}
$$

**(1.5 marks)** — correct formula; **(0.5 mark)** — correct numerical value.

**(iii) Overall Hydrostatic Drive Efficiency $\eta_{HD}$:**

$$
\eta_{HD} = \eta_{v,p} \cdot \eta_{m,p} \cdot \eta_{v,m} \cdot \eta_{m,m}
$$

$$
\eta_{HD} = 0.97 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.7634
$$

$$
\boxed{\, \eta_{HD} = 76.34\% \,}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**(iv) Output Power $P_m$:**

$$
P_m = T_m \cdot 2\pi n_m = 214.86 \times 2\pi \times 34.55
$$

$$
P_m = 214.86 \times 216.95 = 46{,}621 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, P_m \approx 46.62 \text{ kW} \,}
$$

**(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Hydrodynamic Drive Focus [KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Dec 2024 Expected]

**(a)** With a **neat sketch**, describe the **construction and working** of a **three-element hydrodynamic torque converter**. Define **stall torque ratio**, **speed ratio** and explain the term **"converter mode" vs. "coupling mode"**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A torque converter has a **stall torque ratio of 2.2** when the engine delivers **$T_P = 200 \text{ N·m}$** at the impeller input. The transmission input shaft (turbine) is held stationary (vehicle at rest, brakes applied).
- (i) Compute the **stall torque** transmitted to the turbine.
- (ii) If the engine now speeds up to **$n_P = 2500 \text{ rpm}$** and the turbine reaches a **speed ratio of 0.85** in converter mode, compute the **turbine speed** in rpm and the **turbine torque**, assuming the torque ratio follows the empirical relation $TR = TR_{stall} \cdot (1 - 0.6 \cdot SR)$. **(3 Marks)**
- (iii) If a **lock-up clutch** engages at $SR \geq 0.9$ and eliminates slip completely, calculate the **efficiency improvement** relative to converter mode at $SR = 0.9$ (assume $TR = 1.05$ at $SR = 0.9$ in converter mode). **(4 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 Marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**

A torque converter consists of **three bladed rotors** housed in a fluid-filled toroidal casing:
1. **Impeller (Pump) P** — connected to the engine flywheel. Has curved blades, similar to a centrifugal pump impeller.
2. **Turbine T** — splined to the transmission input shaft. Has blades that are mirror-images of the impeller.
3. **Stator (Reactor) S** — between turbine and impeller, mounted on a **one-way sprag clutch** that allows rotation in only one direction.

**Working (3 marks):**

- Engine rotates the impeller, which flings oil outward (centrifugal action).
- Oil strikes the turbine blades, causing it to rotate.
- Oil exits the turbine and meets the stator. At low turbine speeds, the stator is **stationary** and reverses the oil's whirl, redirecting it back to the impeller with the proper angle.
- This redirection **reduces the work the impeller must do** to turn the same fluid → turbine outputs more torque than impeller receives (torque multiplication).
- As turbine speed approaches impeller speed, the stator **freewheels** (no longer redirects), eliminating its reaction → converter becomes a coupling.

**Definitions (1 mark):**
- **Stall Torque Ratio (TR_stall):** $T_T / T_P$ when the turbine is not rotating ($n_T = 0$).
- **Speed Ratio (SR):** $n_T / n_P$ — fraction of impeller speed at which the turbine turns.
- **Converter mode:** Stator locked, $TR > 1$ (torque multiplication).
- **Coupling mode:** Stator freewheels, $TR \approx 1$, higher efficiency.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**(i) Stall Torque (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_{T,stall} = TR_{stall} \cdot T_P = 2.2 \times 200 = 440 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, T_{T,stall} = 440 \text{ N·m} \,}
$$

**(ii) Turbine Speed & Torque at SR = 0.85 (1.5 marks):**

Turbine speed:
$$
n_T = SR \cdot n_P = 0.85 \times 2500 = 2125 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, n_T = 2125 \text{ rpm} \,}
$$

Torque ratio at SR = 0.85:
$$
TR = TR_{stall} \cdot (1 - 0.6 \cdot SR) = 2.2 \cdot (1 - 0.6 \times 0.85)
$$
$$
TR = 2.2 \cdot (1 - 0.51) = 2.2 \cdot 0.49 = 1.078
$$

Turbine torque:
$$
T_T = TR \cdot T_P = 1.078 \times 200 = 215.6 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, T_T = 215.6 \text{ N·m} \,}
$$

**(iii) Lock-up Efficiency Improvement (4 marks):**

**At SR = 0.9 in converter mode (no lock-up):**
- $TR = TR_{stall} \cdot (1 - 0.6 \cdot SR) = 2.2 \cdot (1 - 0.6 \cdot 0.9) = 2.2 \cdot 0.46 = 1.012$

**Given $TR = 1.05$ at SR = 0.9 (as stated in problem; the two are slightly different — use the given value 1.05).**

Converter mode efficiency:
$$
\eta_{conv} = TR \cdot SR = 1.05 \times 0.9 = 0.945
$$

Lock-up mode efficiency (no slip → 100% mechanical coupling, friction losses ~2%):
$$
\eta_{lockup} \approx 0.98
$$

Efficiency improvement:
$$
\Delta\eta = \eta_{lockup} - \eta_{conv} = 0.98 - 0.945 = 0.035
$$

$$
\boxed{\, \Delta\eta = +3.5\% \text{ (absolute)} \,}
$$

Or in relative terms:
$$
\dfrac{\Delta\eta}{\eta_{conv}} = \dfrac{0.035}{0.945} \approx 3.7\% \text{ improvement}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — $V_p$ in cm³/rev must be converted to m³/rev before computing flow in m³/s. Mixing cm³ with m³ is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers. (Lose up to 2 marks)
> 2. **In torque converter problems**, students often forget that the impeller input torque and the turbine output torque are related through $T_T = TR \cdot T_P$, not $T_T = T_P$ alone. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing up converter mode vs. coupling mode** — students sometimes say the stator is "active" in coupling mode. The stator freewheels in coupling mode; it is *inactive*. (Lose 1 mark in Part A)
> 4. **Forgetting the lock-up clutch** — modern automotive torque converters all have a lock-up clutch, and examiners often ask about it. Mentioning it always gains bonus credit. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **In derivations**, students write $T_m = V_m \cdot \Delta p$ without the $2\pi$ denominator and the efficiency factor. This is the *theoretical* torque, not the *actual* torque. Always include $\eta_{m,m}$ for the real-world answer. (Lose 1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Box — Read this 5 minutes before walking into the KTU exam hall.**

### A. Hydrostatic Drives

- ☐ **Definition:** Energy transfer by **fluid pressure** in a sealed circuit.
- ☐ **Main components:** Pump → DCV → Motor → Reservoir → Filter → Cooler.
- ☐ **Two control types:**
  - **Throttle control** — wasteful (energy lost as heat through throttling).
  - **Volume control** — efficient (swash plate angle varies displacement).
- ☐ **Two circuit types:**
  - **Open circuit** — pump draws from reservoir; motor returns to reservoir.
  - **Closed circuit** — sealed loop; charge pump replenishes leakage.
- ☐ **Key formulas:**
  - $Q_p = V_p \cdot n_p \cdot \eta_{v,p}$
  - $T_m = \dfrac{V_m \cdot \Delta p \cdot \eta_{m,m}}{2\pi}$
  - $n_m = \dfrac{V_p \cdot n_p \cdot \eta_{v,p} \cdot \eta_{v,m}}{V_m}$
  - $\eta_{HD} = \eta_{v,p} \eta_{m,p} \eta_{v,m} \eta_{m,m}$
- ☐ **IVT behaviour:** Output torque inversely proportional to output speed.
- ☐ **Applications:** Skid-steer loaders, excavators, forklifts, harvesters.

### B. Hydrodynamic Drives (Torque Converter)

- ☐ **Definition:** Energy transfer by **fluid momentum** in an open toroidal chamber.
- ☐ **Three elements:** Impeller (P), Turbine (T), Stator (S).
- ☐ **Stator** is mounted on a **one-way clutch** — locks at low turbine speed, freewheels at high turbine speed.
- ☐ **Stall torque ratio (TR_stall):** 1.8–2.5 (single stage), 3.5–5.0 (multi-stage).
- ☐ **Key formulas:**
  - $SR = n_T / n_P$
  - $TR = T_T / T_P$
  - $\eta_{conv} = TR \cdot SR$
  - $T_{T,stall} = TR_{stall} \cdot T_{engine,stall}$
- ☐ **Three operating modes:** Converter mode → Coupling mode → Lock-up mode.
- ☐ **Lock-up clutch** engages at high $SR$ (~0.9) for highway efficiency (~98% vs ~85% in converter mode).
- ☐ **Stator's role:** Reverses the returning fluid's whirl to multiply torque (Euler: $T_T = T_P + 2\dot{m}r|V_{u,3}|$).
- ☐ **Applications:** All automatic-transmission passenger cars, AMT, DCT pre-fill phase, marine drives.

### C. Key Distinctions to Memorize

| Aspect | Hydrostatic | Hydrodynamic |
|:-------|:-----------:|:------------:|
| Energy form | Pressure | Momentum |
| Operating pressure | High (20–45 MPa) | Low (0.3–0.8 MPa) |
| Stall efficiency | 100% (theoretically) | 0% |
| Torque multiplication | Yes (high pressure × displacement) | Yes (stator effect) |
| Continuous slip | Minimal (only leakage) | Yes, except in lock-up |

### D. Examiner's Memory Tricks

- ☐ **"STATIC = stationary-pressure"**; **"DYNAMIC = dynamic-spinning-fluid"**.
- ☐ **"Stator = Stationary = redirects = multiplies"**.
- ☐ **"Lock-up = Like = perfect mechanical coupling"**.
- ☐ **"Volume control = Variable swash = Variable output speed"**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Flywheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fluid Flywheel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fluid Flywheel (Hydrodynamic Coupling):** A fluid flywheel is a hydrodynamic power transmission device that uses a working fluid (typically **automatic transmission fluid / ATF oil**) to transmit rotational torque from a prime mover (engine crankshaft) to a driven shaft (transmission input), without any mechanical contact between the driving and driven members. It consists essentially of an **impeller** (pump / driving member) and a **runner** (turbine / driven member) enclosed in a sealed, oil-filled housing.

It is classified as a **frictionless, slip-type fluid coupling** and forms the simplest member of the hydrodynamic family of automotive drivetrain components. Unlike its advanced cousin — the **torque converter** — the fluid flywheel has **no stator (reaction member)**, which means it cannot multiply torque. Its primary role is to provide **smooth, gradual engagement** between the engine and the gearbox, eliminating the harsh jerk associated with a mechanical friction clutch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Tag — PBMUT404 / M4:** Fluid flywheel is treated as a foundational topic that introduces hydrodynamic principles before progressing to torque converters, centrifugal clutches, and automatic transmission gearboxes in subsequent modules.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine two electric fans placed **face-to-face**, one on a table (the impeller, connected to the engine) and the other on a stool directly in front of it (the runner, connected to the gearbox). When you switch on the first fan, it blows air, which pushes the blades of the second fan, making it spin. There is **no physical contact** between the two fans — only the invisible air transfers the rotational energy.

A **fluid flywheel works on exactly this principle**, except:

- Instead of air, the working medium is a **high-density, incompressible hydraulic oil** (denser → transmits more torque).
- Instead of open blades, both wheels have carefully **curved, radial blades** housed inside a sealed toroidal (doughnut-shaped) casing.
- The oil, once flung outward by the spinning impeller, spirals back inward through the runner, transferring momentum in a **closed circulatory loop**.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Fluid Flywheel" name** comes from the fact that the rotating oil mass itself acts as a **kinetic energy reservoir** (like a mechanical flywheel). When the engine surges, the oil absorbs the shock; when the engine sags, the oil releases its stored momentum — thereby protecting the drivetrain from torsional vibrations.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid density ($\rho$) | **780 – 870 kg/m³** (ATF) | kg/m³ |
| Kinematic viscosity of ATF | **~7 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** at 80 °C | m²/s |
| Typical slip at stall | **~ 1.5 – 5 %** | dimensionless (×100 for %) |
| Typical efficiency at rated load | **~ 95 – 98 %** | dimensionless (×100 for %) |
| Engine-side speed ($N_1$) | measured in **rpm** | rev/min |
| Transmission-side speed ($N_2$) | measured in **rpm** | rev/min |

---

## 1.4 Role Inside a Vehicle Drivetrain

```
Engine Crankshaft → [Fluid Flywheel] → Gearbox Input Shaft → Wheels
```

The fluid flywheel sits between the **engine flywheel** and the **clutch / gearbox** assembly. In some heavy commercial vehicles and marine vessels, it is used as a **standalone starting device** because it allows the engine to be started under load without stalling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details

A fluid flywheel is mechanically simple, yet its fluid dynamics are elegant. The main parts are:

1. **Impeller (Driving Member / Pump)**
   - Attached to the engine flywheel / crankshaft.
   - Has curved radial blades (often semicircular or backward-curved).
   - Functions as a **centrifugal pump**.

2. **Runner (Driven Member / Turbine)**
   - Mounted on the transmission input shaft (or propeller shaft in marine use).
   - Blade geometry is a **mirror image** of the impeller to maintain smooth oil flow.
   - It is **NOT mechanically connected** to the impeller.

3. **Housing / Casing**
   - Toroidal, sealed enclosure welded or bolted together.
   - Holds a precise volume of ATF — usually **~ 80 – 90 % full** (not 100 %, to allow thermal expansion and to minimize air entrainment).
   - A small **filler / breather plug** allows oil top-up.

4. **Sealing & Bearings**
   - Two shaft seals (radial lip seals) prevent oil leakage.
   - A bearing supports the runner shaft concentrically with the impeller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> There is **NO stator** in a fluid flywheel. This is the single most important constructional distinction between a fluid flywheel and a torque converter.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The hydrodynamic energy transfer follows a clean, four-stage cycle:

**Step 1 — Engine Accelerates the Impeller**
- The engine rotates the impeller at speed $N_1$. Oil sitting between the blades experiences **centrifugal force** proportional to $\omega^2 r$ (where $\omega$ is angular velocity and $r$ is blade radius).

**Step 2 — Centrifugal Pumping Action**
- The oil is flung outward toward the casing wall. This converts the impeller's **mechanical energy → kinetic energy of the fluid**. Pressure rises radially outward (Bernoulli effect).

**Step 3 — Momentum Transfer to the Runner**
- The high-velocity oil jet leaving the impeller periphery strikes the **concave side of the runner blades**, exerting a tangential force. This force generates a torque $T_2$ on the runner, causing it to rotate in the **same direction** as the impeller (but at a **slightly lower** speed).

**Step 4 — Return Flow Loop**
- After striking the runner, the oil spirals **inward** along the runner blades, returns to the impeller eye (center), and is re-energized. This establishes a **continuous circulatory vortex** inside the housing.

> [!TIP]
> The **slip** (difference between $N_1$ and $N_2$) is essential, not a defect. Without slip, the runner would have to match the impeller speed, requiring physical contact — defeating the purpose of hydrodynamic transmission. Slip permits smooth torque transfer across a wide speed range.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and aligned with standard hydrodynamic coupling theory. They are the **exact equations** required for KTU 2024 ESE numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle s = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1} \times 100\,\%$ | **Slip (%)** — fundamental performance metric |
| 2 | $\displaystyle \tau = \frac{T_2}{T_1}$ | **Torque ratio** — always $\tau \le 1$ for fluid flywheel (no torque multiplication) |
| 3 | $\displaystyle \eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{T_2 \, N_2}{T_1 \, N_1} = (1 - s)$ | **Hydraulic efficiency** — note: slip appears twice (once in speed, once in torque-via-circulation) so $\eta \ne 1-s$ directly. For an **ideal fluid flywheel with no losses**, $\eta = (1-s)$ when $T_1 = T_2$. |
| 4 | $\displaystyle P = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | **Power transmitted** (Watts), with $N$ in rpm and $T$ in N·m |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho \left(v_1^2 - v_2^2\right)$ | **Pressure rise across impeller** — Euler's turbomachinery equation simplified |
| 6 | $\displaystyle v_t = \omega \, r$ | **Tangential blade velocity** at radius $r$ |
| 7 | $\displaystyle T = \dot{m} \,(v_{t1} r_1 - v_{t2} r_2)$ | **Euler's turbomachine torque equation** |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

| Field | Application of Fluid Flywheel |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Smooth-launch clutches in older British / European cars (e.g., early Humbers, Daimlers). |
| **Marine** | Drives for **diesel-electric** and direct-drive ship propellers (e.g., Voith Schneider systems). |
| **Heavy Industry** | Soft-start coupling for **large centrifugal pumps, compressors, conveyors, crushers** — protects motor from inrush shock. |
| **Power Generation** | Hydro-turbine startup coupling in pumped-storage stations. |
| **Locomotives** | Drive coupling in some diesel-hydraulic locomotives. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Slip Equation from First Principles

We begin with the **operational definition** of slip and reduce it to a dimensionless fraction.

**Given:**
- $N_1$ = impeller (input) rotational speed in rpm
- $N_2$ = runner (output) rotational speed in rpm

**Step 1 — Define the speed difference**

The relative speed lost between the driver and the driven member is:

$$\Delta N = N_1 - N_2$$

**Step 2 — Normalize with respect to input speed**

To express slip as a fraction of the input, divide the speed difference by $N_1$:

$$s = \frac{\Delta N}{N_1} = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage**

Multiplying by 100 yields the KTU-standard percentage form:

$$\boxed{\,s \;=\; \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1}\times 100\;\%\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** When $s = 0$ there is no slip (impossible in practice without mechanical lock-up). When $s = 100\%$ the runner is stationary (stall condition). In real automotive fluid flywheels at cruising speed, $s \approx 1.5$–$3\%$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque Ratio

**Given:**
- $T_1$ = input torque transmitted by the impeller shaft (N·m)
- $T_2$ = output torque transmitted by the runner shaft (N·m)

**Step 1 — Newton's Third Law on the Fluid Circuit**

In a hydrodynamic coupling with **no stator**, the tangential force the impeller imparts on the oil is exactly equal in magnitude (and opposite in direction) to the tangential force the oil exerts on the runner. Therefore the **torque transmitted through the fluid** is the same on both sides:

$$T_{\text{fluid}} = T_1 = T_2$$

**Step 2 — Define torque ratio**

$$\tau = \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the equality**

Since $T_1 = T_2$ for an ideal lossless fluid flywheel:

$$\boxed{\,\tau_{\text{ideal}} = 1\,}$$

**Step 4 — Account for real-world losses**

Friction between the oil and casing, windage, and viscous heating cause the **actual** output torque to be marginally lower:

$$\tau_{\text{actual}} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \approx 0.95 \text{ to } 0.98$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Because there is **no stator to redirect fluid**, the fluid flywheel **cannot multiply torque**. This is the fundamental reason it has been largely replaced by the **torque converter** in modern automatic cars, since torque converters can achieve $\tau > 2$ at stall.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Transmitted and Efficiency

**Step 1 — Write power on the input side**

$$P_{\text{in}} = T_1 \,\omega_1 = T_1 \cdot \frac{2\pi N_1}{60}$$

**Step 2 — Write power on the output side**

$$P_{\text{out}} = T_2 \,\omega_2 = T_2 \cdot \frac{2\pi N_2}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency definition**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{T_2 \, N_2}{T_1 \, N_1}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the no-stator torque equality $T_1 = T_2$**

Canceling the torques (ideal coupling):

$$\eta = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 1 - s$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta = (1 - s) \times 100\,\%\,}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Verification (Worked Example)**

> **Problem:** A fluid flywheel runs with the impeller at $N_1 = 3000$ rpm and the runner at $N_2 = 2880$ rpm. Calculate the slip and the efficiency.

**Solution:**

$$s = \frac{3000 - 2880}{3000} = \frac{120}{3000} = 0.04 = 4\%$$

$$\eta = 1 - s = 1 - 0.04 = 0.96 = 96\%$$

**Step 6 — Power Calculation (continuing the same example)**

Assume $T_1 = T_2 = 200$ N·m. Then:

$$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000 \times 200}{60} = 62\,831.85 \text{ W} \approx 62.83 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = \frac{2\pi \times 2880 \times 200}{60} = 60\,318.58 \text{ W} \approx 60.32 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{\text{loss}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}} = 2.51 \text{ kW (heat generated in oil)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Students often write $\eta = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1}$ — this is **slip**, NOT efficiency. Efficiency is $1 - s$. Do not interchange them in the exam.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Euler's Turbomachinery Equation Applied to the Impeller

**Step 1 — Tangential velocity at blade tip**

$$v_{t1} = \omega_1 \, r_1 = \frac{2\pi N_1}{60}\,r_1$$

**Step 2 — Euler's head equation (specific energy transferred)**

$$\text{Specific Energy } E = u_1 v_{w1} - u_2 v_{w2}$$

where $u$ is blade velocity and $v_w$ is the whirl (tangential) component of absolute fluid velocity.

**Step 3 — Torque developed**

$$T_1 = \dot{m} \,(v_{w1} r_1 - v_{w2} r_2)$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate of oil through the impeller-runner circuit.

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU 2024 exam, you are **not required to derive Euler's equation from scratch**, but you **must recognize and apply** it for any problem asking for torque or power in a fluid flywheel.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Fluid Flywheel vs. Torque Converter vs. Mechanical Clutch

| Feature | Fluid Flywheel | Torque Converter | Mechanical Friction Clutch |
|---|---|---|---|
| Torque multiplication | **No** ($\tau = 1$) | **Yes** ($\tau > 2$ at stall) | No (binary on/off) |
| Wear / maintenance | **Negligible** | Negligible | High (plate wear) |
| Slip control | **Continuous** | Continuous | Abrupt (engage/disengage) |
| Heat dissipation | Excellent (oil cooled) | Excellent | Poor (friction plate overheats) |
| Stalling under load | Possible (slip absorbs) | Possible | Engine stalls |
| Efficiency at cruise | High (~95-98%) | High (~95%) | ~100% (locked) |
| Complexity | Low | High (adds stator + clutch lock-up) | Lowest |
| Modern automotive use | **Obsolete** (replaced by TC) | Dominant in automatics | Dominant in manuals |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Fluid Flywheel

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional flow** between the impeller, runner, oil circuit, and external drivetrain. The actual physical cross-section is a **torus (annular doughnut)** which Mermaid cannot render as a 2-D drawing, so a block-architecture representation is used (in compliance with the protocol's fallback rule).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Engine Crankshaft] --> B[Impeller - Driving Member]
    B --> C[Centrifugal Pumping of ATF Oil]
    C --> D[High Velocity Oil Jet]
    D --> E[Runner Blades - Impact Zone]
    E --> F[Runner - Driven Member]
    F --> G[Transmission Input Shaft]
    G --> H[Gearbox and Propeller Line]
    H --> I[Vehicle Wheels]
    E --> J[Return Flow - Inward Spiral]
    J --> K[Impeller Eye - Re-entry Zone]
    K --> B
    B --> L[Torque Sensor T1 on Input]
    F --> M[Torque Sensor T2 on Output]
    L --> N[Slip Calculator s = N1 - N2 / N1]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Efficiency Display eta = 1 - s]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Flow & Losses

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Mechanical Power In - Engine] --> P2[Impeller - Converts to Kinetic Energy of Oil]
    P2 --> P3[Oil Circuit - High Velocity Vortex]
    P3 --> P4[Runner - Recovers Kinetic Energy]
    P4 --> P5[Mechanical Power Out - Transmission]
    P2 --> L1[Windage Loss]
    P3 --> L2[Viscous Friction Loss]
    P3 --> L3[Oil Heating Loss]
    P4 --> L4[Bearing Friction Loss]
    L1 --> H[Heat Dissipated to Casing]
    L2 --> H
    L3 --> H
    L4 --> H
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Operational States of the Fluid Flywheel

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle : Engine Off
    Idle --> Priming : Engine Starts - Oil Settles
    Priming --> Acceleration : N1 increases - Slip high
    Acceleration --> Coupling : N2 approaches N1
    Coupling --> SteadyState : Slip stabilizes at 1-3 percent
    SteadyState --> Stall : Sudden load - N2 drops to zero
    Stall --> SteadyState : Load removed
    Coupling --> [*] : Engine Off
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Comparison: Fluid Flywheel vs. Torque Converter

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph FluidFlywheel
        FF1[Impeller] --> FF2[Runner]
        FF1 -.- FFOil1[Oil Jet - No Reaction]
    end
    subgraph TorqueConverter
        TC1[Impeller] --> TC2[Runner]
        TC1 -.- TCOil[Oil Jet]
        TC2 --> TC3[Stator - Reaction Member]
        TC3 -.- TCOil
    end
    FluidFlywheel -.-> Result1[No Torque Multiplication - tau equals 1]
    TorqueConverter -.-> Result2[Torque Multiplied - tau greater than 1]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define a fluid flywheel. Mention its main components.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A fluid flywheel is a **hydrodynamic coupling** that transmits torque from the engine to the gearbox using a working fluid (ATF oil) without any mechanical contact between the driving and driven shafts. Its main components are: **(1) Impeller** (driving member attached to the engine), **(2) Runner** (driven member attached to the transmission input shaft), and **(3) Sealed oil-filled housing** that encloses both members.

---

**Q2. Why is there always a slip in a fluid flywheel? What is its typical range?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Slip exists because the runner can never reach the exact speed of the impeller — the oil must **continuously re-circulate** between the two members to transmit torque. Without this relative motion, there would be no oil flow and no momentum transfer. In a properly designed fluid flywheel, slip at normal operating speed ranges from **1.5% to 5%**, and can rise to nearly **100%** at stall (when the runner is held stationary against full engine torque).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 / Model Pattern]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style Question]**

**a)** With the help of a neat functional diagram, explain the **construction and working of a fluid flywheel**. List its **advantages and limitations** compared to a mechanical friction clutch. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A fluid flywheel is supplied with **25 kW of mechanical power** at an input speed of **2400 rpm**. If the slip is **4%**, calculate the **(i) output speed, (ii) output torque, and (iii) power lost as heat** in the coupling. Assume $T_1 = T_2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Construction:** A fluid flywheel consists of an **impeller (1)** keyed to the engine flywheel, a **runner (2)** mounted on a separate concentric shaft leading to the gearbox, and a **sealed toroidal casing (3)** filled to about 80–90% with automatic transmission fluid. A bearing and lip seals isolate the two shafts.

**Working:**
1. The engine rotates the impeller.
2. Centrifugal force flings the oil outward — the impeller acts as a **centrifugal pump**.
3. The high-velocity oil jet enters the runner blade channels, exerting a tangential force that turns the runner.
4. Oil returns inward along the runner blades back to the impeller eye, forming a **closed vortex loop**.

**Advantages over friction clutch:**
- No mechanical wear → virtually **maintenance-free**.
- **Smooth, gradual** engagement — no jerking.
- Acts as a **torsional damper**, protecting the drivetrain from engine vibrations.
- Engine can be **started under load** without stalling.
- Self-adjusting to load changes.

**Limitations:**
- **No torque multiplication** (unlike torque converter).
- Small but real **power loss as heat** due to slip.
- Requires a **continuous oil supply** and sealing.
- Higher initial cost than a simple friction plate.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Given data:**

$$P_{\text{in}} = 25 \text{ kW} = 25\,000 \text{ W}, \quad N_1 = 2400 \text{ rpm}, \quad s = 4\% = 0.04$$

**Step 1 — Output speed**

$$N_2 = N_1 (1 - s) = 2400 \times (1 - 0.04) = 2400 \times 0.96 = \mathbf{2304 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 2 — Input torque $T_1$**

$$T_1 = \frac{P_{\text{in}} \times 60}{2\pi N_1} = \frac{25\,000 \times 60}{2\pi \times 2400}$$

$$T_1 = \frac{1\,500\,000}{15\,079.6} = \mathbf{99.47 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 3 — Output torque $T_2$**

Given $T_1 = T_2$:

$$T_2 = 99.47 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4 — Output power $P_{\text{out}}$**

$$P_{\text{out}} = \frac{2\pi N_2 T_2}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 2304 \times 99.47}{60}$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = \frac{1\,439\,950}{60} = \mathbf{23\,999 \text{ W} \approx 24.0 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 5 — Power lost as heat**

$$P_{\text{loss}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}} = 25.0 - 24.0 = \mathbf{1.0 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly compute $P_{\text{out}}$ as $P_{\text{in}} \times (1 - s)$ which gives the right number **by accident** here, but is conceptually wrong if $T_1 \ne T_2$. The **correct** sequence is: compute $T_1$ from $P_{\text{in}}$, then compute $P_{\text{out}}$ from $T_2$ and $N_2$ separately. Marks will be docked if you skip steps.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**a)** Explain the **difference between a fluid flywheel and a torque converter**. Why is the fluid flywheel considered obsolete in modern automatic cars? **(7 marks)**

**b)** A fluid flywheel has an impeller running at **1800 rpm** delivering a torque of **150 N·m**. The runner rotates at **1740 rpm**. Determine the **(i) percentage slip, (ii) efficiency, (iii) output power, and (iv) power dissipated as heat** in the fluid. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (a)

| Aspect | Fluid Flywheel | Torque Converter |
|---|---|---|
| Stator (reaction member) | **Absent** | **Present** (between runner & impeller) |
| Torque multiplication | **No** ($\tau = 1$) | **Yes** ($\tau = 2$ to $3.5$ at stall) |
| Efficiency at stall | Very low (~0%) | Higher (60–80%) |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex (also has lock-up clutch in modern units) |
| Cost | Low | High |

**Why fluid flywheel is obsolete in modern automatics:**
- It cannot multiply torque → engine must be sized larger to launch the vehicle.
- Slip is always present, causing continuous heat generation and marginal fuel loss.
- The torque converter, with its **stator + lock-up clutch**, can multiply torque at stall (so smaller engines can launch heavier cars) and lock up at cruising speed for **100% efficiency** (no slip).

> [!NOTE]
> Fluid flywheels **are still in use** in marine propulsion, heavy industrial soft-start couplings, and some vintage automobiles.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Given:**

$$N_1 = 1800 \text{ rpm}, \quad T_1 = 150 \text{ N·m}, \quad N_2 = 1740 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 1 — Slip**

$$s = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1800 - 1740}{1800} = \frac{60}{1800} = 0.0333 = \mathbf{3.33\%}$$

**Step 2 — Output torque**

$$T_2 = T_1 = 150 \text{ N·m (no stator, no torque multiplication)}$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency**

$$\eta = 1 - s = 1 - 0.0333 = 0.9667 = \mathbf{96.67\%}$$

**Step 4 — Output power**

$$P_{\text{out}} = \frac{2\pi N_2 T_2}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1740 \times 150}{60}$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = \frac{1\,639\,491}{60} = \mathbf{27\,324.85 \text{ W} \approx 27.32 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 5 — Input power (for verification)**

$$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{2\pi N_1 T_1}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1800 \times 150}{60} = 28\,274.33 \text{ W} \approx 28.27 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Power dissipated**

$$P_{\text{loss}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}} = 28.27 - 27.32 = \mathbf{0.95 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** A very common mistake is to compute efficiency as $\eta = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_2}$ (inverted denominator). The correct form is **always** $\eta = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 1 - s$. Cross-check by computing both $P_{\text{in}}$ and $P_{\text{out}}$ and dividing — the answers should match within rounding error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [x] Fluid flywheel = **hydrodynamic coupling** (no stator, no torque multiplication).
- [x] **Three components only:** Impeller, Runner, Sealed oil-filled housing.
- [x] **Working fluid:** Automatic Transmission Fluid (ATF), density $\approx$ 800 kg/m³.
- [x] Housing is filled to **~85% capacity** — never 100%.
- [x] **Slip equation:** $s = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1} \times 100\%$.
- [x] **Efficiency equation:** $\eta = 1 - s$ (ideal case, $T_1 = T_2$).
- [x] **Torque ratio:** $\tau = 1$ (always, for fluid flywheel — no multiplication).
- [x] **Power formula:** $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ (use $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m → $P$ in Watts).
- [x] Lost power is **converted to heat in the oil** → oil must have a cooler in heavy-duty applications.
- [x] Slip **range at cruising:** 1.5% – 5%; at stall: ~100%.
- [x] **No wear** of mechanical parts → maintenance-free.
- [x] Engine can be **started under load** (unlike a manual friction clutch).
- [x] **Replaced by torque converter** in modern automatics due to inability to multiply torque.
- [x] **Still used in:** marine drives, soft-start industrial couplings, some vintage cars.
- [x] **Key difference from torque converter:** presence vs. absence of a **stator (reaction member)**.
- [x] Direction of rotation: **impeller and runner rotate in the SAME direction**.
- [x] Oil flow forms a **closed toroidal vortex** — never escapes the housing under normal operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque Convertor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Torque Convertor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Torque Converter:**
> A *torque converter* is a type of **fluid coupling** (hydrodynamic device) used in automatic transmission systems of automobiles. It transmits rotating mechanical power from the **prime mover (engine/crankshaft)** to the **driven shaft (transmission input shaft)** using a hydraulic working fluid (automatic transmission fluid — ATF). Unlike a simple fluid coupling, it can **multiply torque** during low-speed, high-load conditions (e.g., vehicle starting from rest or climbing a hill) by employing a third element called the **stator (reactor)** mounted on a one-way (overrunning) clutch.

The torque converter is the modern, active replacement of the traditional mechanical clutch in vehicles equipped with **automatic transmissions (AT)**, **continuously variable transmissions (CVT)**, and **dual-clutch automated gearboxes** that use hydraulic launch.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two Fans in a Box" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine two electric table fans placed face-to-face inside a sealed cardboard box, both completely immersed in a thin pool of oil.
> 1. The first fan (**Impeller / Pump**) is plugged into the wall socket — it is the *engine*.
> 2. The second fan (**Turbine**) has its blades freed — it is the *transmission input shaft*.
> 3. When fan 1 spins, it pushes oil onto the blades of fan 2, which makes fan 2 also rotate — *this is torque transmission via fluid*.
> 4. Now, here is the clever part: we insert a **small stationary third fan (Stator)** between them, mounted on a hinge that only allows it to spin in ONE direction (a one-way ratchet). When the engine is spinning fast but the wheels (fan 2) are stationary (e.g., you are stopped at a red light holding the brake), the stationary stator redirects the returning oil back into the impeller with extra force — **multipifying the torque** to the wheels. As the vehicle reaches cruising speed, the stator stops doing this redirecting (it freewheels), and the system behaves like a simple coupling.
> 5. The "extra boost" given by the stator is the **torque multiplication** — a force amplifier, like a hydraulic jack.

This is exactly what happens inside the torque converter of your automatic car — sealed, oil-filled, no mechanical contact, smooth and self-adjusting.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities (SI Units):**
> - **Torque (T):** Newton-metre (**N·m**)
> - **Angular Velocity (ω):** Radians per second (**rad/s**) — or **rpm** in practical automotive practice
> - **Power (P):** Watts (**W**) or Kilowatts (**kW**) — given by $P = T \times \omega$
> - **Speed Ratio (SR):** $SR = \dfrac{N_{turbine}}{N_{impeller}}$ — dimensionless
> - **Torque Ratio (TR):** $TR = \dfrac{T_{turbine}}{T_{impeller}}$ — dimensionless
> - **Stall Speed:** Typically **1,200 – 2,000 rpm** for the impeller when turbine is locked (vehicle stationary)
> - **Maximum Torque Multiplication:** Typically **2.0:1 to 2.5:1** in passenger cars; up to **3.5:1** in heavy-duty applications
> - **Working Fluid:** Automatic Transmission Fluid (ATF) — typically **Dexron III / Dexron VI / Mercon LV** specification
> - **Efficiency at Coupling Point:** Maximum ~**85–90%** (some loss to churning & slip)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details — The Three Working Members

A torque converter is a **sealed, donut-shaped (toroidal) housing** containing three primary rotating elements, all immersed in transmission oil:

| S.No. | Element | Role | Connection |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Impeller (Pump)** | Driven by the engine flywheel/crankshaft. Acts as a centrifugal pump that throws oil outward by centrifugal force. | Rigidly bolted to the **engine flywheel** (housing rotates with it). |
| 2 | **Turbine (Runner)** | Receives the kinetic energy of the oil leaving the impeller and converts it back to mechanical rotation on the transmission input shaft. | Splined to the **transmission input shaft** (drives the gearbox). |
| 3 | **Stator (Reactor)** | A stationary (or freewheeling) reaction member placed in the path of the returning oil. It redirects the oil flow back to the impeller to **amplify torque** during stall/acceleration. | Mounted on a **one-way roller clutch (overrunning clutch)** fixed to the stationary transmission housing via a stationary shaft. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Supplementary Components Inside the Torque Converter:**
> - **Front Cover / Pump Housing:** Welded or riveted cover that encloses the impeller; bolted to the engine flex-plate/flywheel.
> - **Stator Shaft (Stub Shaft):** A non-rotating shaft on which the stator is mounted via a one-way clutch.
> - **One-Way (Overrunning) Clutch:** Allows the stator to remain stationary when oil pushes backward on it, but to freewheel (rotate with the oil flow) when the oil flow reverses direction at higher vehicle speeds.
> - **Seal Rings, Bearings, and Damper Springs** for smooth operation.

## 2.2 Working Principle — The Three Operating Phases

The torque converter operates in **three distinct phases** as the vehicle accelerates from rest to cruising speed:

### Phase 1 — Stall Condition (Torque Multiplication Phase)
- **Vehicle is stationary**, brake applied, engine idling at **stall speed (≈ 1,800 rpm)**.
- Turbine is **locked** (output shaft stationary), so it is NOT rotating.
- The impeller spins but cannot push oil into the spinning turbine effectively. Oil is forced through the narrow gaps and returns strongly toward the impeller.
- The **stator, held stationary by the one-way clutch**, redirects this returning oil **back into the impeller** in the direction of impeller rotation.
- This produces a **reaction torque** on the impeller, supplementing the engine torque → **Torque Multiplication occurs**.
- Typical torque ratio at stall: **TR = 2.0 to 2.5** (engine torque is amplified 2–2.5× at the turbine).

### Phase 2 — Acceleration Phase (Variable Multiplication)
- As the vehicle starts moving, the turbine begins to spin.
- The torque multiplication **gradually reduces** as turbine speed increases.
- Stator remains **locked stationary** as long as oil is still trying to push it backward.
- Torque ratio **TR decreases smoothly** from stall value (~2.5) toward unity (1.0).

### Phase 3 — Coupling Point (Fluid Coupling Mode)
- At approximately **85–90% of impeller speed**, the oil flow direction at the stator **reverses**.
- The one-way clutch now **frees the stator** to rotate with the oil (freewheel mode).
- The stator is now carried along by the fluid and contributes **no torque reaction**.
- The torque converter now behaves like a simple **fluid coupling** with a small (~5–10%) slip.
- **TR = 1.0**; the turbine receives exactly the same torque the impeller absorbs (minus minor fluid losses).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Use a Torque Converter Instead of a Fluid Coupling?**
> A pure fluid coupling has a torque ratio TR = 1.0 at all speeds. It CANNOT multiply torque. This is why a car using only a fluid coupling (e.g., certain vintage designs) needs a separate "fluid flywheel" with auxiliary starting means. The stator solves this elegantly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula No. | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F-1** | $P = T \times \omega$ | Power = Torque × Angular velocity | **W = N·m × rad/s** |
| **F-2** | $SR = \dfrac{N_{turbine}}{N_{impeller}}$ | Speed Ratio (always between 0 and 1) | dimensionless |
| **F-3** | $TR = \dfrac{T_{turbine}}{T_{impeller}}$ | Torque Ratio (≥ 1 in stall, = 1 in coupling) | dimensionless |
| **F-4** | $TR_{stall}$ (max) = **2.0 – 2.5** (cars), up to **3.5** (trucks) | Maximum torque multiplication at stall | dimensionless |
| **F-5** | $\eta_{converter} = TR \times SR$ | Efficiency of torque converter (always < 1, peaks ≈ 0.85–0.90) | dimensionless |
| **F-6** | Slip = $N_{impeller} - N_{turbine}$ | Rotational speed difference (rpm) | **rpm** |
| **F-7** | Slip% = $\dfrac{N_{impeller} - N_{turbine}}{N_{impeller}} \times 100$ | Percentage slip | **%** |
| **F-8** | $Q \propto N_{impeller} \times D^3$ | Theoretical oil flow rate (Q) ∝ impeller speed × cube of diameter | m³/s |
| **F-9** | $H \propto N^2 D^2$ | Theoretical head (H) ∝ (speed)² × (diameter)² | metres of oil |
| **F-10** | $P_{fluid} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Hydraulic power transferred by the fluid | **W** (ρ = density, g = 9.81 m/s²) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Exam Insight — The Three Product Laws (Pump / Turbine Affinity Laws):**
> 1. **Flow Law:** $Q \propto ND^3$ → flow rate is proportional to impeller speed × diameter cubed
> 2. **Head Law:** $H \propto N^2 D^2$ → pressure/head is proportional to speed squared × diameter squared
> 3. **Power Law:** $P \propto N^3 D^5$ → input power is proportional to speed cubed × diameter to the fifth power
