> These three laws are derived by dimensional analysis from Euler's turbomachinery equation and are absolutely critical for KTU numerical problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Passenger Cars:** Nearly all automatic transmission cars (Toyota, Honda, Hyundai, BMW, Mercedes-Benz) use torque converters for smooth launch.
- **Heavy Earthmoving Equipment:** Wheel loaders, dump trucks, bulldozers — high torque multiplication (3.5:1) needed to move massive loads from rest.
- **Marine Propulsion:** Boat drives use large torque converters for the propeller.
- **Locomotive & Military Vehicles:** Some diesel-electric and hydrostatic drives use torque converter stages.
- **Industrial Drives:** Large conveyors, crushers, mixers in cement, mining, and process industries.
- **Hybrid Vehicles:** Modern Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive uses a **lock-up clutch** in the torque converter to mechanically couple engine to wheels at cruising speed, eliminating slip loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lock-Up Clutch (Modern Enhancement):** Most post-1990s torque converters include a **lock-up clutch** — a friction plate controlled by hydraulic pressure via the Transmission Control Module (TCM). At higher vehicle speeds (typically above 60 km/h), the lock-up clutch engages, mechanically locking the impeller to the turbine. This eliminates the 5–10% slip loss, improving fuel economy by **5–10%** and reducing fluid heating.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Three Affinity Laws (Pump Laws)

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundation:** The torque converter is fundamentally a **turbomachine** — specifically a centrifugal pump (impeller) and a centrifugal turbine operating in a closed fluid loop. Its behaviour obeys the classic Euler turbomachinery equations, which can be non-dimensionalised to yield three universal "affinity laws."

### Derivation 1: The Flow Law ($Q \propto N D^3$)

**Step 1 — Define the volumetric flow rate Q in the meridian (radial) plane.**

The oil leaving the impeller blade tip has a radial velocity component that scales linearly with impeller tip speed:

$$
V_{radial} \propto U = \pi D N
$$

where $D$ is the impeller diameter and $N$ is the rotational speed in rps (revolutions per second).

**Step 2 — Relate Q to radial velocity and flow area.**

The flow area $A$ at the impeller exit is proportional to $D^2$ (annular area $\propto D^2$):

$$
A \propto D^2
$$

**Step 3 — Combine velocity and area.**

$$
Q = A \times V_{radial} \propto D^2 \times (\pi D N) \propto N D^3
$$

**Result — Flow Law:**

$$
\boxed{Q_1 / Q_2 = (N_1 / N_2) \times (D_1 / D_3)^3}
$$

### Derivation 2: The Head Law ($H \propto N^2 D^2$)

**Step 1 — Euler's turbomachine equation for head.**

The head developed by an ideal impeller is:

$$
H = \dfrac{U_2 \cdot V_{u2}}{g}
$$

where $U_2$ is the impeller tip speed and $V_{u2}$ is the tangential (whirl) component of absolute velocity at exit.

**Step 2 — Note the velocity ratios.**

For a geometrically similar impeller, all velocity components scale with tip speed $U = \pi D N$:

$$
U_2 \propto D N, \quad V_{u2} \propto D N
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the head equation.**

$$
H \propto U_2 \cdot V_{u2} \propto (D N) \times (D N) \propto N^2 D^2
$$

**Result — Head Law:**

$$
\boxed{H_1 / H_2 = (N_1 / N_2)^2 \times (D_1 / D_2)^2}
$$

### Derivation 3: The Power Law ($P \propto N^3 D^5$)

**Step 1 — Recall the hydraulic power equation.**

$$
P = \rho g Q H
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the scaling laws for Q and H.**

$$
P \propto Q \times H \propto (N D^3) \times (N^2 D^2) = N^3 D^5
$$

**Result — Power Law:**

$$
\boxed{P_1 / P_2 = (N_1 / N_2)^3 \times (D_1 / D_2)^5}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Torque Converter Efficiency Equation

**Step 1 — Recall the definitions of Speed Ratio and Torque Ratio.**

$$
SR = \dfrac{N_{turbine}}{N_{impeller}} = \dfrac{\omega_{turbine}}{\omega_{impeller}}
$$

$$
TR = \dfrac{T_{turbine}}{T_{impeller}}
$$

**Step 2 — Recall the power equation at the impeller and turbine.**

$$
P_{impeller} = T_{impeller} \times \omega_{impeller}
$$

$$
P_{turbine} = T_{turbine} \times \omega_{turbine}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the efficiency ratio (output power / input power).**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{turbine}}{P_{impeller}} = \dfrac{T_{turbine} \times \omega_{turbine}}{T_{impeller} \times \omega_{impeller}}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange the ratio as (TR × SR).**

$$
\eta = \left( \dfrac{T_{turbine}}{T_{impeller}} \right) \times \left( \dfrac{\omega_{turbine}}{\omega_{impeller}} \right)
$$

**Result — Efficiency Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{converter} = TR \times SR}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight from this Equation:**
> - At **stall** (start of motion): $SR = 0$, so $\eta = 0$ (all energy goes into fluid motion and heat; useful output is zero).
> - At **coupling** (high speed): $TR = 1$, and $SR$ is high (≈ 0.85–0.95), so $\eta$ is high (≈ 0.85–0.95).
> - The product $TR \times SR$ **peaks at the "sweet spot"** — the operating point the transmission ECU tries to keep the converter near, just before lock-up clutch engagement.

## 3.3 KTU Numerical Problem — Worked Example

**Problem:** A torque converter has an impeller speed of 2,400 rpm. At a particular instant, the turbine speed is 1,800 rpm. The impeller absorbs 80 N·m of torque. The torque ratio is 1.6 at this operating point.

**Find:** (a) Speed Ratio, (b) Turbine torque, (c) Turbine power output, (d) Converter efficiency.

### Step 1 — Compute the Speed Ratio (SR).

$$
SR = \dfrac{N_{turbine}}{N_{impeller}} = \dfrac{1{,}800}{2{,}400} = 0.75
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Turbine Torque.

$$
T_{turbine} = TR \times T_{impeller} = 1.6 \times 80 = 128 \; N\!\cdot\!m
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Turbine Power Output.

First, convert 1,800 rpm to rad/s:

$$
\omega_{turbine} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1{,}800}{60} = 188.5 \; rad/s
$$

Now apply the power equation:

$$
P_{turbine} = T_{turbine} \times \omega_{turbine} = 128 \times 188.5 = 24{,}128 \; W \approx 24.13 \; kW
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Converter Efficiency.

$$
\eta = TR \times SR = 1.6 \times 0.75 = 1.20 \;\;(\text{or } 120\%)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner Trap:** A value of $\eta > 1$ (i.e., 120%) is **mathematically correct** here. It means the converter is in its **torque multiplication regime** — the stator is feeding energy back into the impeller. However, the **true thermodynamic efficiency** (mechanical power out / chemical energy in fuel) is far less because of engine inefficiencies. In KTU theory, we report the **fluid/kinematic efficiency** as $TR \times SR$, which CAN exceed 1. Do NOT be tempted to "correct" it. Always show the formula.

For comparison, **Impeller power input**:

$$
\omega_{impeller} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 2{,}400}{60} = 251.3 \; rad/s
$$

$$
P_{impeller} = 80 \times 251.3 = 20{,}106 \; W \approx 20.11 \; kW
$$

**Power gain from the stator** $= 24.13 - 20.11 = 4.02 \; kW$ (this energy comes from the **fluid's kinetic energy** redirected by the stator back into the impeller — accounting for $TR > 1$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Torque Converter — Internal Construction Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the functional architecture of a torque converter, showing the three main members, the direction of oil flow, and the role of the one-way clutch.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine Crankshaft and Flywheel] -->|Mechanical Drive| IMP[Impeller Pump Housing Rotating]
    IMP -->|High Velocity Oil Jet| TUR[Turbine Splined to Transmission Input Shaft]
    TUR -->|Returning Oil Flow| STA[Stator Reactor Mounted on One Way Clutch]
    STA -->|Redirected Oil Back to Impeller| IMP
    STA -.->|At Coupling Point Freewheel| OW[One Way Overrunning Clutch Releases Stator]
    TUR -->|Mechanical Output| GB[Transmission Gearbox Input Shaft]
    OW -->|Reaction Force During Stall| HS[Stationary Transmission Housing Stator Shaft]
    IMP -->|Waste Heat to Oil| OIL[Automatic Transmission Fluid ATF Reservoir and Cooler]
    TUR -->|Waste Heat to Oil| OIL
    LUC[Lock Up Clutch Friction Disc TCM Controlled] -.->|Engages at High Speed| IMP
    LUC -.->|Locks| TUR
```

## 4.2 State Diagram — The Three Phases of Torque Converter Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StallPhase : Vehicle at rest brake applied engine idling
    StallPhase : Stall Phase TR 2.0 to 2.5 Stator LOCKED one way clutch engaged
    StallPhase --> AccelerationPhase : Driver releases brake throttle applied
    AccelerationPhase : Acceleration Phase TR gradually falls from 2.5 to 1.0 Stator still LOCKED
    AccelerationPhase --> CouplingPoint : Turbine speed reaches 85 to 90 percent of impeller speed
    CouplingPoint : Coupling Point TR equals 1.0 Oil flow direction reverses at stator
    CouplingPoint --> FreewheelMode : One way clutch releases stator freewheels with oil
    FreewheelMode : Freewheel Mode TR equals 1.0 Small slip 5 to 10 percent
    FreewheelMode --> LockUpEngaged : Vehicle speed above 60 km per hour TCM signal
    LockUpEngaged : Lock Up Mode Mechanical coupling zero slip maximum efficiency
    LockUpEngaged --> StallPhase : Vehicle brakes to a stop torque cycle restarts
    FreewheelMode --> StallPhase : Vehicle brakes to a stop torque cycle restarts
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Power Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT [POWER INPUT]
        A1[Engine Combustion Torque] --> A2[Flex Plate and Flywheel]
    end
    subgraph CONV [TORQUE CONVERTER SEALED UNIT]
        A2 --> B1[Front Cover and Impeller Pump]
        B1 -->|Oil Kinetic Energy| B2[Turbine Runner]
        B2 -->|Reaction Oil| B3[Stator Reactor]
        B3 -->|Redirected Reaction Oil| B1
        B2 -.->|Freewheel| B3
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT [POWER OUTPUT TO DRIVETRAIN]
        B2 --> C1[Transmission Input Shaft]
        C1 --> C2[Planetary Gear Set]
        C2 --> C3[Output Shaft to Propeller Shaft]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL [ELECTRONIC CONTROL]
        D1[Transmission Control Module TCM] -->|Hydraulic Pressure Signal| B3
        D1 -->|Lock Up Command| E1[Lock Up Clutch Solenoid]
        E1 -->|Engages Friction Disc| B1
    end
```

## 4.4 Performance Characteristic Curves — Qualitative Description

The following Mermaid quadrant chart represents the four key performance curves of a torque converter as a function of **Speed Ratio (SR)** on the horizontal axis:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph XAXIS [Speed Ratio SR increasing from 0 to 1]
        direction LR
    end
    subgraph CURVES [Performance Curves]
        direction LR
        Q1[TR Curve starts at 2.5 at SR equals 0 falls to 1.0 at SR equals 0.9] -->|Concave Decreasing| Q2[TR equals 1.0 from SR equals 0.9 onwards]
        Q3[Efficiency Curve starts at 0 at SR equals 0 rises to peak 0.86 at SR equals 0.8] -->|Concave Increasing then Plateau| Q4[Efficiency holds near 0.86 to 0.9 till lock up]
        Q5[Slip Curve starts at 100 percent at SR equals 0 falls to 5 to 10 percent at SR equals 0.9] -->|Hyperbolic Decreasing| Q6[Slip near zero after lock up]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The **efficiency curve peaks just before SR = 0.85**. This is the **"sweet spot"** — the operating point at which the transmission ECU tries to maintain the converter before commanding the lock-up clutch to engage for further efficiency gains.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define torque converter. List its three main components and state the function of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** A torque converter is a hydrodynamic fluid coupling device that transmits engine torque to the transmission input shaft using automatic transmission fluid (ATF) and is capable of **multipifying torque** during stall conditions.
>
> **Three components (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Impeller (Pump):** Centrifugal pump connected to the engine flywheel; it imparts kinetic energy to the oil.
> 2. **Turbine (Runner):** Receives the high-velocity oil from the impeller and drives the transmission input shaft; converts fluid energy back to mechanical rotation.
> 3. **Stator (Reactor):** Stationary reaction member mounted on a one-way clutch; redirects returning oil back into the impeller to provide torque multiplication.
>
> **Function of stator specifically (0.5 mark):** The stator increases the torque transmitted to the turbine by changing the direction of fluid flow back onto the impeller blades, thereby adding reaction torque to the engine torque.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between a fluid coupling and a torque converter.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Fluid Coupling | Torque Converter |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Number of working members** | Two (impeller + turbine) | Three (impeller + turbine + stator) |
> | **Torque ratio (TR)** | Always **1.0** | **2.0 – 2.5** (variable, max at stall) |
> | **Torque multiplication** | **Not possible** | **Possible** via stator |
> | **Efficiency at stall** | Zero (SR = 0 ⇒ no output) | Non-zero turbine torque, but fluid losses |
> | **Application** | Older "fluid flywheel" designs, some marine | All modern automatic transmission vehicles |
> | **Stator?** | Absent | Present, mounted on one-way clutch |
>
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** A torque converter is essentially a fluid coupling **with an added stationary reactor (stator)** that enables torque multiplication, making it suitable for vehicle launch.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a torque converter. Describe its three operating phases: stall, acceleration, and coupling.**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Construction (3 marks):**
> A torque converter consists of a sealed toroidal housing containing three blade wheels (impeller, turbine, stator) immersed in automatic transmission fluid (ATF).
> - **Impeller:** Driven by the engine via the flex-plate. Acts as a centrifugal pump.
> - **Turbine:** Splined to the transmission input shaft; receives oil and rotates.
> - **Stator:** Mounted on a stationary stub shaft via a **one-way (overrunning) clutch**; its role is reaction.
> - **Front cover, bearings, seals, and lock-up clutch** complete the assembly.
>
> **Working (2 marks):**
> The impeller throws oil outward by centrifugal force; this oil strikes the turbine blades, causing the turbine to rotate. Oil then returns through the stator core, which — when stationary — redirects the flow back to the impeller, adding reaction torque and thus multiplying output torque. At higher speeds, the stator freewheels and torque multiplication ceases.
>
> **Three Operating Phases (2 marks):**
> 1. **Stall Phase:** Vehicle stationary. Turbine locked. Impeller spins at ~1,800 rpm. Stator is locked by one-way clutch, redirecting oil. TR = 2.0–2.5.
> 2. **Acceleration Phase:** Vehicle starts moving. TR drops smoothly from 2.5 to 1.0 as turbine accelerates.
> 3. **Coupling Point:** At SR ≈ 0.85–0.90, oil flow direction at stator reverses; one-way clutch releases stator, which freewheels. TR = 1.0. System now acts as a simple fluid coupling.
>
> *(Examiner's Note: Neat sketch of three-blade arrangement is mandatory for full marks.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A torque converter has an impeller speed of 2,000 rpm. At an operating point, the turbine speed is 1,500 rpm. The impeller input torque is 75 N·m, and the torque ratio at this point is 1.8. Determine: (i) Speed Ratio, (ii) Turbine torque, (iii) Power output at turbine, (iv) Power input at impeller, (v) Converter efficiency.**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Given:**
> - $N_{impeller} = 2{,}000$ rpm; $N_{turbine} = 1{,}500$ rpm
> - $T_{impeller} = 75$ N·m; $TR = 1.8$
>
> **(i) Speed Ratio (1 mark):**
>
> $$SR = \dfrac{N_{turbine}}{N_{impeller}} = \dfrac{1{,}500}{2{,}000} = 0.75$$
>
> **(ii) Turbine Torque (1 mark):**
>
> $$T_{turbine} = TR \times T_{impeller} = 1.8 \times 75 = 135 \; N\!\cdot\!m$$
>
> **(iii) Turbine Power Output (1.5 marks):**
> Convert turbine speed to rad/s:
>
> $$\omega_{turbine} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1{,}500}{60} = 157.08 \; rad/s$$
>
> $$P_{turbine} = 135 \times 157.08 = 21{,}205.8 \; W \approx 21.21 \; kW$$
>
> **(iv) Impeller Power Input (1.5 marks):**
> Convert impeller speed to rad/s:
>
> $$\omega_{impeller} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 2{,}000}{60} = 209.44 \; rad/s$$
>
> $$P_{impeller} = 75 \times 209.44 = 15{,}708 \; W \approx 15.71 \; kW$$
>
> **(v) Converter Efficiency (2 marks):**
>
> $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{turbine}}{P_{impeller}} = \dfrac{21{,}205.8}{15{,}708} = 1.35 \;(\text{or } 135\%)$$
>
> Cross-check using the formula:
>
> $$\eta = TR \times SR = 1.8 \times 0.75 = 1.35 \; ✓$$

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the role of the stator (reactor) in a torque converter with a neat diagram. Why is it mounted on a one-way (overrunning) clutch? Describe the flow of oil through the three members during the stall condition.**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Role of Stator (3 marks):**
> The stator is the third and most critical element that distinguishes a torque converter from a simple fluid coupling. It is a bladed reaction member placed in the path of oil returning from the turbine to the impeller.
> - When the turbine is slow (or stalled), the oil leaves the turbine and tries to enter the impeller **against the direction of impeller rotation**.
> - The stator's curved blades redirect this returning oil **in the same direction as impeller rotation**, adding a **reaction force** on the impeller.
> - This reaction augments the engine torque, producing a **torque boost** at the turbine (TR > 1).
>
> **One-Way Clutch Justification (2 marks):**
> - During stall and acceleration: oil pushes the stator in one direction; the one-way clutch **locks** it stationary to provide reaction.
> - At the coupling point (~85% of impeller speed), oil flow direction at the stator **reverses**. The one-way clutch now **releases the stator**, allowing it to **freewheel** with the oil. Without this release, the stator would oppose impeller motion and cause severe efficiency loss and heat.
> - The one-way clutch therefore allows the stator to function as a **two-state device**: locked (stall) or free (coupling).
>
> **Oil Flow During Stall (2 marks):**
> 1. Engine rotates the **impeller** (pump) via the flywheel.
> 2. Impeller blades fling oil outward centrifugally into the **turbine** at high velocity.
> 3. Oil strikes the curved turbine blades, attempting to drive the turbine (which is locked because the vehicle is stationary).
> 4. Oil exits the turbine and enters the **stator core** in a direction **opposing impeller rotation**.
> 5. The stationary stator's curved blades redirect this flow **back into the impeller** in its direction of rotation.
> 6. This redirected flow exerts a **reaction force** on the impeller, adding to the engine torque.
> 7. **Net effect:** Torque at turbine > Torque at impeller ⇒ Torque Multiplication (TR = 2.0 to 2.5).
>
> *(Neat diagram of three-blade arrangement with oil flow arrows is mandatory.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Using the affinity (similarity) laws of turbomachinery, derive the three governing relations: (i) $Q \propto N D^3$, (ii) $H \propto N^2 D^2$, (iii) $P \propto N^3 D^5$. State one practical use of these laws in torque-converter design.**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(i) Flow Law: $Q \propto N D^3$ (2.5 marks):**
> - The radial velocity of oil leaving the impeller tip is proportional to tip speed:
>   $V_r \propto U = \pi D N$
> - The flow cross-sectional area at impeller exit is proportional to $D^2$:
>   $A \propto D^2$
> - Therefore, $Q = A \times V_r \propto D^2 \times (D N) = N D^3$.
> - **Result:** $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} \times \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^3$
>
> **(ii) Head Law: $H \propto N^2 D^2$ (2.5 marks):**
> - Euler's head equation: $H = \dfrac{U_2 \cdot V_{u2}}{g}$
> - Both $U_2$ and $V_{u2}$ are proportional to tip speed $\pi D N$.
> - Therefore, $H \propto (D N) \times (D N) = N^2 D^2$.
> - **Result:** $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 \times \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2$
>
> **(iii) Power Law: $P \propto N^3 D^5$ (1.5 marks):**
> - Hydraulic power: $P = \rho g Q H$
> - Substituting the scaling laws: $P \propto (N D^3) \times (N^2 D^2) = N^3 D^5$.
> - **Result:** $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3 \times \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^5$
>
> **Practical Use (0.5 mark):**
> - In torque-converter design, the affinity laws are used to **predict performance at different operating speeds** from model tests. For example, if a small prototype torque converter (small $D$) is tested at high speed to avoid cavitation, the full-scale torque converter performance (large $D$) at lower speed can be predicted precisely. They are also used in **scaling up** torque converters for trucks and heavy equipment from passenger-car designs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Torque Ratio > 1" with impossible physics:** Many students think $\eta > 1$ means the converter is a "perpetual motion machine." It is not. The fluid/kinematic efficiency $TR \times SR$ can exceed 1 because the stator feeds energy back. Always clarify this in your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s:** Power $P = T \omega$ requires $\omega$ in **rad/s**, not rpm. A direct multiplication of N·m × rpm yields a meaningless number. KTU examiners specifically dock 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Skipping the sketch:** KTU Part B questions on torque converter **require a neat labelled diagram** for full marks. Even a rough freehand sketch showing impeller, turbine, stator, oil flow arrows, and one-way clutch fetches 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Mislabelling the stator's role:** Saying "stator rotates" or "stator drives the turbine" is wrong. The stator is **stationary** (during stall) and **only redirects oil**. It never directly drives the turbine.
> 5. **Forgetting the lock-up clutch:** If the question asks about modern enhancements or fuel economy, mention the **lock-up clutch** that engages at highway speeds. This is a frequently asked supplementary point.
> 6. **Wrong unit for head/pressure:** The head $H$ in affinity laws is in **metres of oil (or fluid)**, not pressure units. Convert using $P = \rho g H$ if needed.
> 7. **Not mentioning one-way clutch in the explanation:** This is the single most important supporting component. Any explanation of the stator's two states (locked vs freewheel) without mentioning the one-way clutch is considered incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Torque Converter (Module 4):**
>
> - **Definition:** Hydrodynamic fluid device that transmits AND multiplies engine torque to the transmission input shaft using ATF.
> - **Three Members:** Impeller (engine-driven pump) → Turbine (transmission-driven) → Stator (stationary reactor on one-way clutch).
> - **Stator is the key differentiator** from a simple fluid coupling.
> - **Three Operating Phases:** Stall (TR max ≈ 2.0–2.5), Acceleration (TR falls), Coupling (TR = 1, stator freewheels).
> - **Speed Ratio:** $SR = N_{turbine}/N_{impeller}$ (0 to 1).
> - **Torque Ratio:** $TR = T_{turbine}/T_{impeller}$ (≥ 1 in stall).
> - **Efficiency:** $\eta = TR \times SR$ (can exceed 1.0 in stall region, but real efficiency is dominated by slip loss).
> - **Stall speed:** ~1,200–2,000 rpm; **Maximum torque multiplication:** 2.0–2.5 (cars), up to 3.5 (trucks).
> - **One-way (Overrunning) Clutch:** Mounts the stator — locked during stall, freewheels at coupling point.
> - **Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto ND^3$, $H \propto N^2 D^2$, $P \propto N^3 D^5$ — derive using dimensional analysis of tip speed $U = \pi D N$ and Euler's head equation.
> - **Lock-Up Clutch:** Modern feature that mechanically couples impeller and turbine above ~60 km/h, eliminating slip and improving fuel economy by 5–10%.
> - **Working Fluid:** Automatic Transmission Fluid (ATF) — Dexron III / Dexron VI / Mercon LV.
> - **Common Applications:** Automatic transmission cars, heavy earthmoving equipment, marine propulsion, industrial drives, hybrid vehicles (with lock-up).
> - **Examiner's Favourite Numerical:** Always expects (a) SR, (b) Turbine Torque, (c) Power Output, (d) Efficiency — remember to convert rpm → rad/s!
> - **Examiner's Favourite Theory:** "Role of stator and why one-way clutch is needed" — answer this with a labelled diagram showing oil flow during stall.
> - **Always draw the diagram** in Part B answers — minimum 2 marks reserved for it in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuously Varying Transmission: Components of CVT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Continuously Varying Transmission: Components of CVT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)** is a class of automotive transmission that can seamlessly vary the effective gear ratio across a continuous, infinite range between its minimum and maximum limits, as opposed to the discrete, fixed step-ratios of a conventional manual or automatic gearbox.

Mathematically, the transmission ratio $\tau$ can assume any real value within the operating envelope:
$$\tau \in [\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}] \quad \text{where} \quad \tau_{min} < \tau < \tau_{max}$$

The **CVT components** collectively form an integrated mechatronic powertrain sub-system, comprising:
1. **Mechanical Power Transmission Subsystem** – variator, pulleys, belt/chain, planetary gear set, torque converter.
2. **Hydraulic Actuation Subsystem** – oil pump, pressure regulator, control valves, pulley actuators.
3. **Electronic Control Subsystem** – ECU, throttle position sensor, vehicle speed sensor, CVT fluid temperature sensor, engine speed sensor.
4. **Thermal Management Subsystem** – cooler, oil pan, filter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The variator (primary pulley + secondary pulley + belt/chain) is the *defining* component of a CVT. The hydraulic and electronic subsystems are *enabling* components that make the variator function.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap-Water Analogy"

Imagine a kitchen tap delivering water. Instead of a tap that you can only turn in *discrete clicks* (like a traditional gearbox with 5 or 6 fixed gears), imagine a **smooth, infinitely variable knob**. You can stop it *anywhere* between fully closed and fully open, choosing any flow rate in between. 

A CVT behaves exactly like this for engine torque. Instead of being forced to choose between, say, 1st, 2nd, or 3rd gear, the CVT continuously morphs its internal ratio so the engine always operates at its **Most Efficient or Most Powerful RPM point** for the current driving demand. The driver feels seamless, step-less acceleration — like a continuously dimming light rather than a blinking one.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Conceptual Takeaway:** A CVT is not "one more gear." It is an *infinite-number-of-gears* device. The "components" therefore exist not to provide gear steps, but to *continuously vary* one single ratio, *control* that variation intelligently, and *transmit* the resulting torque to the wheels.

## 1.3 Why CVTs Exist — The Engineering Motivation

Conventional multi-speed transmissions have a finite number of ratios. Between gears, the engine RPM either **overshoots** (during upshift) or **undershoots** (during downshift) the optimal operating line. This creates two penalties:
- A **torque hole** during the shift transient.
- A **departure from the ideal engine operating point** at any given vehicle speed.

The CVT eliminates both penalties by allowing the engine to stay glued to its optimal **Ideal Operating Line** (Best Fuel Economy line or Maximum Power line) on the Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) map at all times.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard metric used to quantify the *range* of a CVT is the **Span Ratio** $\rho_{span} = \tau_{max} / \tau_{min}$. Modern automotive CVTs achieve $\rho_{span}$ in the range of **2.4 to 7.0**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Block Decomposition of a CVT

The CVT is logically broken into the following subsystems, each with a defined engineering role.

### A. Mechanical Power Path
| Sub-Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Primary (Drive) Pulley** | Receives torque from engine (via torque converter in auto-CVTs). Has one *fixed* flange and one *movable* flange. Movable flange slides axially to vary the working diameter $D_1$. |
| **Secondary (Driven) Pulley** | Delivers torque to the final drive. Also has a fixed and a movable flange. Its movable flange is axially loaded to vary the working diameter $D_2$. |
| **Belt / Chain / Toroidal Traction Unit** | Transfers tangential force from primary to secondary. Must withstand high tensile and bending stresses. |
| **Planetary Gear Set** | Provides forward and reverse gear reduction. The variator itself is non-reversing. |
| **Torque Converter** | Provides fluid coupling and torque multiplication at low speeds (used in most modern automotive CVTs). |
| **Final Drive & Differential** | Perpendiculars the drive and transmits to wheels with final reduction ratio. |

### B. Hydraulic Actuation Subsystem
| Sub-Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Oil Pump** | Generates pressurized CVT fluid (often at **300–700 kPa** primary line pressure). |
| **Pressure Regulator Valve** | Maintains constant line pressure despite changing engine speed. |
| **Ratio Control Valve** | Modulates pulley actuator pressure to change the variator ratio. |
| **Pulley Actuators (Cylinders)** | Convert hydraulic pressure to axial force on the movable flanges. |
| **CVT Fluid (ATF/CVTF)** | Serves as hydraulic fluid, lubricant, and friction modifier. Typical viscosity **6–8 cSt at 100°C**. |

### C. Electronic Control Unit (ECU) Subsystem
| Sub-Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)** | Reports driver demand. |
| **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)** | Reports actual road speed. |
| **Engine Speed Sensor (RPM)** | Reports engine crankshaft RPM. |
| **CVTF Temperature Sensor** | Monitors fluid temperature for viscosity-dependent pressure compensation. |
| **Line Pressure Sensor** | Closed-loop feedback for hydraulic control. |
| **ECU / TCU** | Computes target ratio, target line pressure, and lock-up clutch state. |

### D. Traction Unit Options
| Type | Traction Element | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Belt-Driven CVT** (Van Doorne) | Steel push belt with segmented elements | Small/medium cars (Nissan, Honda, Toyota) |
| **Chain-Driven CVT** (Audi / Subaru Lineartronic) | Push-block chain (Link-Belt) | Higher torque vehicles (2.0 L+) |
| **Toroidal CVT** (Extroid by Nissan) | Rollers + discs | Commercial vehicles (limited) |
| **Hydrostatic CVT** | Variable-displacement pump + motor | Heavy machinery, tractors |
| **Epicyclic / Infinitely Variable (IVT)** | Compound epicyclic + variator | Industrial, hybrid vehicles |

## 2.2 Operating Principle — The "Variator" Logic

The variator is the heart of the CVT. It works on the **pushing-belt-between-cones principle**.

- The **primary pulley** effective radius $r_1$ is *forced* by the engine to a specific value dictated by the demanded torque.
- The **secondary pulley** effective radius $r_2$ is *sensed* and adjusted by the ECU to maintain the target vehicle speed.
- Because the belt cannot stretch, any change in $r_1$ forces an *equal-and-opposite* change in $r_2$ such that the geometric belt length remains constant.

The instantaneous transmission ratio is given by:
$$\tau = \frac{\omega_{in}}{\omega_{out}} = \frac{r_2}{r_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

where $\omega$ are angular velocities and $N$ are rotational speeds (RPM).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula required to solve KTU 2024 Scheme problems on CVT components and their operation.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = r_2 / r_1$ | Instantaneous transmission ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $i_{overall} = \tau \cdot i_{F} \cdot i_{R}$ | Overall drivetrain ratio (F=final, R=reverse) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V = \omega_{out} \cdot r_{wheel} \cdot (60/1000)$ | Vehicle speed from output RPM | km/h |
| 4 | $F_{clamp} = \dfrac{2 T}{D_{eff} \cdot \mu}$ | Required belt clamping force | Newtons [N] |
| 5 | $L_{belt} = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}$ | Effective belt length on variator | mm |
| 6 | $P_{line} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{piston}}$ | Required hydraulic line pressure | Pa |
| 7 | $\tau_{max} = T_{engine,max} \cdot r_2$ | Belt tangential force / torque check | N·m |
| 8 | $\rho_{span} = \tau_{max} / \tau_{min}$ | CVT span ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $v_{belt} = \pi D_1 N_1$ | Tangential belt velocity (cross-check) | m/s |
| 10 | $P_{fric} = \mu \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot v_{belt}$ | Friction power loss in variator | W |
| 11 | $T_{cap} = \mu \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot D_{eff} / 2$ | Torque capacity of variator | N·m |
| 12 | $\eta_{CVT} \approx 0.85 - 0.92$ | Typical efficiency range | dimensionless |

> **Notation used:**
> $r_1, r_2$ = effective pulley radii [m]
> $D_1, D_2$ = effective pulley diameters [m]
> $C$ = center-to-center pulley distance [m]
> $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between belt and pulley (typically **0.07 – 0.12** for steel-on-steel with traction fluid)
> $T$ = transmitted torque [N·m]
> $A_{piston}$ = actuator piston area [m²]

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Passenger Cars:** Achieves **15–20% fuel-economy improvement** over 4-speed automatics in city driving, by holding the engine on the BSFC sweet spot.
- **Scooters / Motorcycles:** Centrifugal automatic CVT in scooters (Honda Activa, TVS Jupiter) — no electronics, purely weight-and-RPM-based.
- **Hybrid Electric Vehicles:** Toyota's e-CVT (THS) uses a planetary gearset + MG1 + MG2 to achieve an *electrical* continuously variable ratio, with no friction variator.
- **Agricultural & Earthmoving:** Hydrostatic CVTs (John Deere, CASE) provide seamless speed control for varying load conditions.
- **Industrial Drives:** Mechanical PIV (Positive Infinitely Variable) drives use conical disks for low-power industrial applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Subaru's Lineartronic CVT and Audi's multitronic CVT use a **chain** instead of a belt to handle higher torque (~400 N·m) from larger engines. The chain has 2× the torque capacity but 2–3% lower mechanical efficiency than a steel push belt.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Belt Length Equation on a Variator

The steel push-belt is wrapped between two variable-diameter pulleys separated by a center distance $C$. The total effective belt length $L$ is the sum of:
1. Two straight (tangential) segments running between the pulleys.
2. Two arc segments wrapping around the pulleys.

The two straight segments have total length $2C$.
Each arc spans an angle of $(\pi - 2\alpha)$, where $\alpha = \sin^{-1}[(D_1 - D_2)/(2C)]$ for a typical geometry (D₁ > D₂ assumed).

The two arc lengths combined equal $\frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2)$.
The "take-up" of length due to the radius difference is $\frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}$.

$$\boxed{\; L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C} \;}$$

**Step-by-step algebraic verification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= 2 \cdot (\text{tangent length}) + (\text{arc}_1 + \text{arc}_2) \\
  &= 2C + \left[\pi D_1 \cdot \frac{(\pi - 2\alpha)}{2\pi} + \pi D_2 \cdot \frac{(\pi - 2\alpha)}{2\pi}\right] \\
  &= 2C + \left(\frac{D_1 + D_2}{2}\right)(\pi - 2\alpha) \\
\end{aligned}$$

Using the identity $\pi - 2\alpha = \pi - 2\sin^{-1}[(D_1 - D_2)/(2C)]$, and for small angles $2\alpha \approx (D_1 - D_2)/C$ (i.e., the chord length approximation), we get:

$$\begin{aligned}
L &\approx 2C + \left(\frac{D_1 + D_2}{2}\right)\left[\pi - \frac{D_1 - D_2}{C}\right] \\
  &= 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) - \frac{(D_1 + D_2)(D_1 - D_2)}{2C} \\
  &= 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) - \frac{(D_1^2 - D_2^2)}{2C} \\
\end{aligned}$$

For the operational case where $D_1 \approx D_2$ (mid-ratio) and using the exact geometric identity, the corrected term becomes the cross-product $\frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}$, yielding the canonical belt-length formula above. This is a key formula tested in KTU 2024 board examinations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Clamping Force on the Belt

The variator transmits torque by **friction** between the belt and the pulley sheave faces. Consider the primary pulley. Let:
- $T$ = torque to be transmitted [N·m]
- $D_{eff}$ = effective diameter of the variator [m]
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between belt and sheave
- $F_{clamp}$ = total axial clamping force on the belt [N]

The belt tangential force is $F_t = T / (D_{eff}/2) = 2T / D_{eff}$.

The maximum frictional force available is $F_{fric} = \mu \cdot F_{clamp}$.

For non-slipping, $F_{fric} \geq F_t \cdot k$ where $k$ is a safety factor (typically **1.25 to 1.5**).

Setting equality with the safety factor:

$$F_{clamp} = \frac{2 T \cdot k}{\mu \cdot D_{eff}}$$

The **hydraulic line pressure** required from the oil pump is then:

$$P_{line} = \frac{F_{clamp}}{A_{piston}} = \frac{2 T \cdot k}{\mu \cdot D_{eff} \cdot A_{piston}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU 2024 typical problem):**

A CVT is required to transmit $T = 180$ N·m at the primary pulley. Effective diameter $D_{eff} = 0.15$ m, $\mu = 0.10$, $A_{piston} = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $k = 1.3$.

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp} &= \frac{2 \times 180 \times 1.3}{0.10 \times 0.15} \\
          &= \frac{468}{0.015} \\
          &= 31{,}200 \text{ N} \\
P_{line} &= \frac{31{,}200}{8 \times 10^{-4}} \\
         &= 3.9 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
         &= 39 \text{ MPa (approx.)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

This is the required hydraulic line pressure — well within the **30–50 MPa** range of typical CVT pumps.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transmission Ratio and Vehicle Speed Coupling

Given the engine torque $T_e$ and the engine speed $N_e$ (in RPM), the **vehicle speed** $V$ in km/h is:

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D_{wheel} \cdot N_e}{1000 \cdot 60 \cdot \tau \cdot i_{final}}$$

Substituting the relationship $\tau = r_2/r_1$:

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D_{wheel} \cdot N_e \cdot r_1}{1000 \cdot 60 \cdot r_2 \cdot i_{final}}$$

Differentiating with respect to time, the **vehicle acceleration** is:

$$a = \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{\pi \cdot D_{wheel}}{1000 \cdot 60 \cdot i_{final}} \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[\frac{N_e \cdot r_1}{r_2}\right]$$

This is the fundamental dynamic equation that the CVT's ECU solves *continuously* to determine the optimal $r_1 / r_2$ ratio for the current driving demand.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component-by-Component Failure Modes

| Component | Failure Mode | Symptom in Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| Steel Push Belt | Segment pin wear / fatigue | Jerky ratio changes, metallic clatter |
| Primary Pulley Bearing | Seizure due to inadequate lubrication | Loss of drive, whining noise |
| CVT Fluid | Oxidation / contamination | Slipping, overheating, judder |
| Line Pressure Solenoid | Stuck open / closed | Belt slip or no ratio change |
| Ratio Control Solenoid | Stiction / contamination | Ratio stuck at one extreme |
| Torque Converter Clutch | Worn friction material | Vibration at highway speed, fuel waste |
| Stepper Motor (Subaru) | Loss of position feedback | P17xx DTC, "limp mode" |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical (KTU Exam Practice)

**Problem:** A CVT has $r_{1,min} = 0.03$ m, $r_{1,max} = 0.075$ m. The center distance $C = 0.18$ m. Engine peak torque is $T_{max} = 220$ N·m at $N_e = 4500$ RPM. Calculate:
(a) The span ratio of the variator.
(b) The maximum belt length.
(c) The required clamping force for max torque.

**Solution:**

**(a) Span ratio:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_{span} &= \frac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{min}} = \frac{r_{2,max}/r_{1,min}}{r_{2,min}/r_{1,max}} \\
           &= \frac{r_{2,max}}{r_{2,min}} \cdot \frac{r_{1,max}}{r_{1,min}} \\
\end{aligned}$$

If the variator is symmetric ($r_{1,max} = r_{2,max} = 0.075$ m, $r_{1,min} = r_{2,min} = 0.03$ m):

$$\rho_{span} = \frac{0.075}{0.03} \cdot \frac{0.075}{0.03} = 2.5 \times 2.5 = 6.25$$

**(b) Maximum belt length** (occurs when one pulley is at min, other at max):

$$\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 2 \times 0.075 = 0.15 \text{ m}, \quad D_2 = 2 \times 0.03 = 0.06 \text{ m} \\
L &= 2(0.18) + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.15 + 0.06) + \frac{(0.15 - 0.06)^2}{4 \times 0.18} \\
  &= 0.36 + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.21) + \frac{(0.09)^2}{0.72} \\
  &= 0.36 + 0.3299 + 0.01125 \\
  &= 0.7012 \text{ m} \approx 701.2 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) Required clamping force** (assuming $\mu = 0.10$, $k = 1.25$):

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp} &= \frac{2 \times 220 \times 1.25}{0.10 \times 0.15} \\
          &= \frac{550}{0.015} \\
          &= 36{,}667 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of a CVT (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[ENGINE CRANKSHAFT] --> TC[TORQUE CONVERTER]
    TC --> LP[LOCK UP CLUTCH]
    LP --> PP[PRIMARY PULLEY DRIVE]
    PP --> MS[MOVABLE SHEAVE PRIMARY]
    PP --> FS1[FIXED SHEAVE PRIMARY]
    MS -- Hydraulic Axial Force --> PISTON1[PRIMARY ACTUATOR PISTON]
    
    PP --> BELT[STEEL PUSH BELT OR CHAIN]
    BELT --> SP[SECONDARY PULLEY DRIVEN]
    SP --> MS2[MOVABLE SHEAVE SECONDARY]
    SP --> FS2[FIXED SHEAVE SECONDARY]
    MS2 -- Hydraulic Axial Force --> PISTON2[SECONDARY ACTUATOR PISTON]
    
    SP --> PG[PLANETARY GEAR SET]
    PG --> FD[FINAL DRIVE AND DIFFERENTIAL]
    FD --> WH[ROAD WHEELS]
    
    PUMP[HYDRAULIC OIL PUMP] --> PRV[PRESSURE REGULATOR VALVE]
    PRV --> PISTON1
    PRV --> PISTON2
    PUMP --> LCV[LINE CONTROL VALVE]
    LCV --> PRV
    
    ECU[TRANSMISSION CONTROL UNIT TCU] --> LCV
    ECU --> PRV
    ECU --> LUC[LOCK UP CLUTCH SOLENOID]
    LUC --> LP
    
    TPS[THROTTLE POSITION SENSOR] --> ECU
    VSS[VEHICLE SPEED SENSOR] --> ECU
    RPM[ENGINE RPM SENSOR] --> ECU
    CTEMP[CVT FLUID TEMP SENSOR] --> ECU
    
    OILP[CVT FLUID COOLER] --> PUMP

    class TPS,VSS,RPM,CTEMP sens
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRIMARY[PRIMARY PULLEY VARIATOR]

    class FSB,MSB,SPRINGB,PISTONB,OILB secondarybox
```

## 4.3 Control Loop Logic — The ECU Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([DRIVER PRESSES ACCELERATOR]) --> READSENS[READ TPS VSS RPM CVTF TEMP]
    READSENS --> CALC[CALCULATE TARGET RATIO FOR DESIRED ACCELERATION]
    CALC --> LOOKUP[LOOKUP OPTIMAL ENGINE RPM FROM BSFC MAP]
    LOOKUP --> RATIOCHG[SEND COMMAND TO RATIO CONTROL VALVE]
    RATIOCHG --> PISTONACT[ACTUATE PRIMARY AND SECONDARY PISTONS]
    PISTONACT --> RATCHK[RATIO REACHED]
    RATCHK -- NO --> PISTONACT
    RATCHK -- YES --> STABLE[HOLD RATIO LINE PRESSURE CONSTANT]
    STABLE --> MONITOR[CONTINUOUSLY MONITOR SENSORS]
    MONITOR --> READSENS
    
    MONITOR --> SLIP[BELT SLIP DETECTED]
    SLIP --> INCRM[INCREASE LINE PRESSURE]
    INCRM --> MONITOR
    
    classDef proc fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    class READSENS,CALC,LOOKUP,MONITOR,SLIP,INCRM proc
    class RATIOCHG,PISTONACT,STABLE act
    class START,RATCHK out
```

## 4.4 Schematic Interpretation Notes (KTU Board Standard)

In a KTU 2024 board answer, when asked to "draw and label a CVT system," the examiner expects:
1. **Engine → Torque Converter → Primary Pulley → Belt → Secondary Pulley → Final Drive** as a *linear* power path.
2. A *secondary* feedback path showing the **Hydraulic Pump → Control Valves → Pulley Actuators**.
3. A *tertiary* electronic path showing **Sensors → ECU → Control Valves**.
4. A *belt/pulley half-section* in a separate box to illustrate the variator geometry (movable sheave + fixed sheave + belt contact points).

> [!NOTE]
> Always label the **two straight runs** of the belt, the **two wrap arcs**, the **effective diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$**, the **center distance $C$**, and the **hydraulic actuator piston** on your diagram. These four labels alone are worth **2 marks** in a typical 7-mark sub-question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT). List its three major sub-systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A CVT is a transmission that can vary its effective gear ratio seamlessly over a continuous range, with no discrete gear steps. The three major sub-systems are:
1. **Mechanical Power Transmission** (variator, pulleys, belt/chain, planetary gearset, torque converter).
2. **Hydraulic Actuation** (oil pump, regulator valve, ratio control valve, pulley pistons).
3. **Electronic Control** (TCU/ECU, sensors — TPS, VSS, RPM, CVTF temperature).

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correct listing with brief role.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the function of a "variator" in a CVT. Name the two types of traction elements used in modern automotive CVTs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **variator** is the core mechanical component that physically varies the transmission ratio by altering the effective running diameters of two pulleys connected by a traction element.

Two types of traction elements:
1. **Steel Push Belt** (Van Doorne design — used by Nissan, Honda, Toyota for sub-2.0 L engines).
2. **Push-Block Chain / Link Belt** (used by Audi multitronic, Subaru Lineartronic, and modern chain-driven CVTs for higher torque capacities up to **400 N·m**).

*(1.5 marks for variator function + 1.5 marks for the two traction elements.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a labeled sketch, describe the **construction and working of a belt-driven CVT**. Explain how the ratio is varied by the movement of the movable sheaves. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **effective belt length** on a variator in terms of the center distance $C$ and the effective diameters $D_1, D_2$. A CVT has $C = 180$ mm, $D_{max} = 150$ mm, $D_{min} = 60$ mm. Calculate the maximum and minimum belt lengths. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
A belt-driven CVT (Van Doorne type) consists of:
- A **primary (drive) pulley** keyed to the input shaft (connected to the engine via a torque converter).
- A **secondary (driven) pulley** coupled to the output shaft through a planetary gear set for forward/reverse.
- A **steel push belt** with hundreds of segmented elements (typically **300–800** segments) that ride on the pulley sheaves.
- Each pulley has a **fixed sheave** and a **movable sheave** that can slide axially.
- The movable sheaves are loaded by **hydraulic pistons** that receive pressurized CVT fluid from a pump.
- A **TCU** (Transmission Control Unit) processes signals from TPS, VSS, RPM, and CVTF temperature sensors.

**Working:**
- The input shaft turns the primary pulley at engine speed $N_e$.
- The push belt transmits tangential force $F_t$ via **friction** between the belt segments and the pulley sheave faces.
- The clamping force is generated hydraulically: $F_{clamp} = P_{line} \cdot A_{piston}$.
- To **increase ratio** (low gear, high torque): Primary pulley is squeezed, reducing $D_1$ to a small value. Simultaneously, the secondary pulley is unclamped slightly, allowing its movable sheave to be pushed out by belt tension, increasing $D_2$.
- The ratio changes from $\tau = D_2 / D_1 = \text{large}$ to $\tau = \text{small}$ as acceleration progresses.
- The TCU continuously adjusts to keep the engine at its **optimal BSFC point** for the given load.

**Sketch (expected):**
A full-sectional block diagram showing the variator with belt wrap, two sheaves per pulley, hydraulic actuator, and belt length geometry.

*(Distribution: 2 marks construction list, 2 marks working explanation, 1 mark sketch, 1 mark ratio-variation logic, 1 mark role of TCU.)*

---

**(b) Belt Length Derivation (7 marks):**

**Derivation:** (As derived in Section 3.1)

$$\boxed{\; L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C} \;}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $C = 180$ mm, $D_{max} = 150$ mm, $D_{min} = 60$ mm.

**Maximum belt length** (occurs when one pulley is at $D_{max}$ and the other is at $D_{min}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= 2(180) + \frac{\pi}{2}(150 + 60) + \frac{(150 - 60)^2}{4 \times 180} \\
        &= 360 + \frac{\pi}{2}(210) + \frac{(90)^2}{720} \\
        &= 360 + 329.87 + 11.25 \\
        &= 701.12 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Minimum belt length** (occurs when both pulleys are at the same effective diameter, i.e., $D_1 = D_2 = D_{mid}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{mid} &= \frac{D_{max} + D_{min}}{2} = \frac{150 + 60}{2} = 105 \text{ mm} \\
L_{min} &= 2(180) + \frac{\pi}{2}(105 + 105) + 0 \\
        &= 360 + \pi \times 105 \\
        &= 360 + 329.87 \\
        &= 689.87 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}$$

*(Distribution: 3 marks derivation, 2 marks numerical for $L_{max}$, 2 marks for $L_{min}$.)*

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **hydraulic control system** of an automotive CVT in detail. Include the functions of the line pressure regulator, ratio control valve, and pulley actuators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CVT is required to transmit a maximum engine torque of $T = 250$ N·m. The effective diameter of the primary pulley is $D_{eff} = 0.16$ m, the coefficient of friction between belt and sheave is $\mu = 0.10$, and the piston area is $A = 9 \times 10^{-4}$ m². Using a safety factor of $k = 1.4$, calculate:
  (i) the required belt clamping force,
  (ii) the required hydraulic line pressure, and
  (iii) the maximum torque capacity if the line pressure is limited to 4 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hydraulic Control System (7 marks):**

The hydraulic subsystem is the "muscle" of the CVT. It has four functional blocks:

1. **Oil Pump (Engine-driven, internal-external gear type):**
   - Draws CVT fluid (CVTF) from the sump.
   - Generates primary line pressure typically between **300 kPa and 800 kPa** under normal conditions, but the pressure regulator can boost it to **30–50 MPa** for clamping during high-torque events.

2. **Line Pressure Regulator Valve (Spring-loaded spool valve):**
   - Maintains a constant primary line pressure regardless of engine RPM.
   - Receives feedback pressure on one face and spring force on the other.
   - Excess fluid is dumped back to the sump via a relief passage.

3. **Ratio Control Valve (Proportional solenoid valve):**
   - Modulated by the TCU via a duty-cycle PWM signal.
   - Directs oil to the **primary pulley actuator** to change $D_1$.
   - The secondary pulley actuator is governed by a separate solenoid or by a mechanical feedback (Honda's design).
   - Acts as a "variable orifice" — varying the flow to the primary piston changes the axial position of the movable sheave, which changes the ratio.

4. **Pulley Actuators (Double-acting hydraulic pistons):**
   - Mounted behind the movable sheaves of the primary and secondary pulleys.
   - Convert hydraulic pressure to **axial clamping force** on the belt.
   - Force balance: $F_{clamp} = P_{line} \cdot A_{piston} - F_{spring}$.
   - The clamping force is critical — too little = belt slip, too much = excessive friction loss.

**Additional components:**
- **CVTF Cooler**: Heat exchanger (often integrated with the radiator) that prevents fluid from exceeding **~120°C**.
- **CVTF Filter**: Removes wear debris from the segmented belt.

*(Distribution: 1.5 marks pump, 1.5 marks regulator, 2 marks ratio control valve, 2 marks actuators + cooler.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T = 250$ N·m, $D_{eff} = 0.16$ m, $\mu = 0.10$, $A = 9 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $k = 1.4$.

**(i) Required clamping force:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp} &= \frac{2 T \cdot k}{\mu \cdot D_{eff}} \\
          &= \frac{2 \times 250 \times 1.4}{0.10 \times 0.16} \\
          &= \frac{700}{0.016} \\
          &= 43{,}750 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Required line pressure:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{line} &= \frac{F_{clamp}}{A} \\
         &= \frac{43{,}750}{9 \times 10^{-4}} \\
         &= 4.861 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
         &= 48.61 \text{ MPa} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Maximum torque capacity at $P_{line} = 4$ MPa:**

The maximum clamping force is:
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp,max} &= P_{line} \cdot A \\
              &= 4 \times 10^{6} \times 9 \times 10^{-4} \\
              &= 3{,}600 \text{ N} \\
\end{aligned}$$

The maximum transmittable torque:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{F_{clamp,max} \cdot \mu \cdot D_{eff}}{2 \cdot k} \\
        &= \frac{3{,}600 \times 0.10 \times 0.16}{2 \times 1.4} \\
        &= \frac{57.6}{2.8} \\
        &= 20.57 \text{ N·m} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Inference:** With a 4 MPa line pressure, the variator can only transmit ~20.6 N·m — far less than the 250 N·m demanded. This means the CVT would **slip and fail** under full engine load. The line pressure must be increased to at least the 48.6 MPa calculated in part (ii).

*(Distribution: 1.5 marks formula + answer (i), 1.5 marks (ii), 2 marks (iii), 1 mark inference comment, 1 mark units consistency.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in CVT Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor $k$:** Many students write $F_{clamp} = 2T/(\mu D)$. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** if $k$ is omitted. Always state $k = 1.25$ to $1.5$ explicitly.
> 
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in $L_{belt}$ formula:** Mixing mm and m in $C$, $D_1$, $D_2$ is a guaranteed **1-mark loss**. Convert all to mm OR all to m, and be consistent.
> 
> 3. **Confusing $D_{eff}$ and $D_{wheel}$:** In a vehicle-speed problem, $D_{eff}$ is the *variator* diameter (small, ~150 mm) and $D_{wheel}$ is the *tire* diameter (large, ~600 mm). Mixing them up gives a wrong answer by 4×.
> 
> 4. **Not drawing the belt geometry:** In a 7-mark sketch question, the variator cross-section is mandatory. Skipping it costs **2 marks** outright.
> 
> 5. **Miscounting sub-systems:** A CVT has THREE primary sub-systems (mechanical, hydraulic, electronic). Writing "2 sub-systems" is an immediate **0.5 mark loss**.
> 
> 6. **Wrong symbol for ratio:** Use $\tau$ (tau) or $i$ for ratio — *never* use the Greek letter $\rho$ (rho), which is reserved for span ratio.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 4 — CVT Components):**
> 
> - [x] **CVT Definition:** Transmission with infinite, continuously variable gear ratio between $\tau_{min}$ and $\tau_{max}$.
> - [x] **Three Sub-Systems:** Mechanical (variator + belt), Hydraulic (pump + valves + actuators), Electronic (TCU + sensors).
> - [x] **Variator =** Primary Pulley + Secondary Pulley + Traction Element (belt or chain).
> - [x] **Traction Elements:** Steel Push Belt (Van Doorne) and Push-Block Chain (Audi/Subaru).
> - [x] **Key Ratio Formula:** $\tau = r_2 / r_1$ (or $D_2 / D_1$).
> - [x] **Span Ratio:** $\rho_{span} = \tau_{max} / \tau_{min}$ (typically 2.4 to 7.0).
> - [x] **Belt Length:** $L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}$.
> - [x] **Clamping Force:** $F_{clamp} = \frac{2 T k}{\mu D_{eff}}$, where $k$ is the safety factor (1.25 to 1.5).
> - [x] **Line Pressure:** $P_{line} = F_{clamp} / A_{piston}$ (typically 30 to 50 MPa for full clamping).
> - [x] **Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$):** 0.07 to 0.12 for steel-on-steel with traction fluid.
> - [x] **CVT Fluid:** Acts as hydraulic fluid, lubricant, and traction fluid. Typical viscosity **6–8 cSt @ 100°C**.
> - [x] **CVT Efficiency:** 85% to 92% (chain CVTs are 2–3% lower than belt CVTs).
> - [x] **BSFC Map Connection:** CVT keeps engine on the best-BSFC curve for any speed/load.
> - [x] **TCU Inputs:** TPS, VSS, RPM, CVTF Temperature Sensor.
> - [x] **Hydraulic Components to Memorize:** Oil Pump, Line Pressure Regulator, Ratio Control Valve, Pulley Actuator Pistons, CVTF Cooler, CVTF Filter.
> - [x] **Planetary Gearset:** Used for forward/reverse — the variator itself is non-reversing.
> - [x] **Torque Converter:** Present in most modern auto-CVTs for low-speed torque multiplication.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of CVT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of CVT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)** is a type of automatic transmission that can change seamlessly through a continuous range of effective gear ratios, as opposed to the discrete steps of a conventional geared transmission. It achieves this by varying the **effective diameter** of the input and output pulleys (or equivalent traction elements) during operation, thereby allowing the engine to operate at its most efficient rotational speed for a given vehicle speed and load condition.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a CVT is classified as a **friction-drive, traction-drive, or hydraulic transmission** in which the **transmission ratio $i$ varies continuously** between a minimum and maximum bound rather than in fixed steps.

The general transmission ratio is given by:

$$i = \frac{N_{in}}{N_{out}} = \frac{r_{out}}{r_{in}}$$

where:
- $N_{in}$ = rotational speed of input (driver) pulley
- $N_{out}$ = rotational speed of output (driven) pulley
- $r_{in}$ = effective radius of input pulley
- $r_{out}$ = effective radius of output pulley

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bicycle on a Hill**
> Imagine you are cycling up a hill. As the slope steepens, you manually shift to a lower gear. With only **3 or 4 gears**, you often find your pedaling cadence is either too fast (gear too low) or too slow with too much force (gear too high). Now imagine a bicycle with **infinitely variable gears** — the chainring (front gear) could smoothly grow larger while the rear sprocket smoothly shrinks. You could always pedal at the **perfect comfortable rate** regardless of the slope. **A CVT is exactly this for a car engine — it keeps the engine in its "sweet spot" (best specific fuel consumption) at every vehicle speed.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Numerical Benchmarks to Memorize:**
> - **Standard automotive CVT ratio span (spread)**: typically **4.5 : 1 to 7.0 : 1** (e.g., Nissan Xtronic CVT ≈ 7.0:1, Jatco JF011E ≈ 6.0:1)
> - **V-belt CVT efficiency**: **85% – 92%** at rated load
> - **Toroidal CVT efficiency**: **90% – 95%**
> - **Maximum traction coefficient (μ)** for traction fluid: **0.08 – 0.12** (Newton-Raphson traction fluids)
> - **Operating fluid pressure (V-belt CVT)**: **0.5 MPa – 4 MPa**
> - **Hydraulic pump/motor CVT efficiency**: **80% – 85%**
> - **Engine sweet spot (BSFC minimum)**: typically **1800 – 2500 rpm** for petrol, **1200 – 1800 rpm** for diesel

## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Classification of CVT Types

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically groups CVTs into the following families:

1. **Variable Diameter Pulley V-Belt CVT** (Van Doorne / Push-belt)
2. **Toroidal CVT** (Half-toroidal and Full-toroidal)
3. **Hydrostatic CVT**
4. **Hydrodynamic CVT** (Voith / Torotrak / Extroid)
5. **Cone CVT**
6. **Ratcheting / Infinitely Variable Transmission (IVT)**
7. **Electric / Hybrid CVT (e-CVT – Toyota THS)**

Each of these manipulates a different physical principle — **friction, traction, hydrostatic pressure, hydrodynamic shear, or electromagnetic coupling** — to achieve the ratio variation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Operating Principle

All CVTs, despite mechanical differences, share a **three-block functional architecture**:

- **Block A — Input Traction Element (Driver)**: Converts the engine's torque into a movable contact zone.
- **Block B — Coupling Medium**: Transfers motion without discrete teeth — by belt, chain, traction rollers, fluid film, or magnetic field.
- **Block C — Output Traction Element (Driven)**: Collects motion at a variable effective radius and delivers to the driveshaft.

The **"shift"** occurs by changing the ratio of effective radii, **not by engaging different gear pairs**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table lists **every formula you are expected to know** for the KTU University Examination on Types of CVT. Memorize the units, boundary conditions, and the sign convention.

| # | Formula | Variables & Meaning | Units | Boundary / Applicability |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{r_2}{r_1}$ | Ratio of input/output speeds = ratio of effective radii | dimensionless | All friction/traction CVTs |
| 2 | $i_{min} = \dfrac{r_{2,min}}{r_{1,max}}$ | Minimum ratio (overdrive) | dimensionless | High-speed cruise |
| 3 | $i_{max} = \dfrac{r_{2,max}}{r_{1,min}}$ | Maximum ratio (start-up) | dimensionless | Hill launch, towing |
| 4 | $T_{out} = i \cdot \eta \cdot T_{in}$ | Torque transformation with efficiency | N·m | Power flow direction |
| 5 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Power equation | W | Energy balance |
| 6 | $\mu = \dfrac{F_t}{F_n}$ | Traction coefficient | dimensionless | Hertzian contact in traction drives |
| 7 | $F_t = \mu \cdot F_n$ | Tangential (driving) force capacity | N | Limit of torque transfer |
| 8 | $F_n = p \cdot A_c$ | Clamping force from hydraulic actuator | N | $p$ = line pressure, $A_c$ = piston area |
| 9 | $F_n = \dfrac{T}{r} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | Required clamping force to prevent slip | N | $T$ = transmitted torque, $r$ = radius |
| 10 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{belt} \cdot \eta_{hyd} \cdot \eta_{bear}$ | Combined efficiency | dimensionless | Typically 0.85–0.92 |
| 11 | $Q = \dfrac{V_D \cdot n}{1000}$ | Pump displacement flow (hydrostatic CVT) | L/min | $V_D$ in cc/rev, $n$ in rpm |
| 12 | $T_{pump} = \dfrac{p \cdot V_D}{2\pi}$ | Pump torque | N·m | Hydrostatic units |
| 13 | $n_{motor} = \dfrac{Q}{V_{D,m}}$ | Motor speed (hydrostatic) | rpm | Lossless approximation |
| 14 | $i_{hydro} = \dfrac{V_{D,pump}}{V_{D,motor}}$ | Hydrostatic ratio | dimensionless | Variable by swashplate angle |
| 15 | $\Delta p = \dfrac{T_{motor} \cdot 2\pi}{V_{D,motor}}$ | Pressure rise in hydrostatic motor | Pa | Energy balance |
| 16 | $\omega_{e} = \omega_{r} \cdot (1 + s)$ | Hydrodynamic coupling slip relation | rad/s | $s$ = slip (typically 1%–5%) |
| 17 | $P_{loss} = s \cdot P_{in}$ | Hydrodynamic slip loss | W | Voith / fluid coupling |
| 18 | $R_{spread} = \dfrac{i_{max}}{i_{min}}$ | Ratio spread (span) | dimensionless | 4.5:1 – 7.0:1 typical |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does KTU emphasize CVTs in automotive engineering?**
> 1. **Fuel Economy**: 5%–15% better than equivalent AT due to operation near BSFC minimum.
> 2. **Emissions Compliance**: Crucial for Euro 6 / BS-VI / CAFÉ norms.
> 3. **Simpler Software Control**: No discrete shift events to schedule.
> 4. **Lower Production Cost**: Fewer parts than planetary ATs (≈ 200 parts vs. 500+).
> 5. **Smoother Drive**: No torque interruption during "shifts" — critical for EVs and hybrids.
> 6. **Limitations**: Torque capacity is lower than geared ATs (limited to ≈ **400 N·m** in modern push-belt units), and the "rubber-band effect" can feel unnatural to drivers accustomed to stepped gears.

## 2.4 Physical Constraint: The Fundamental Trade-off

All CVTs must satisfy **Euler's belt-tension equation** (or its traction equivalent):

$$\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where:
- $T_1$ = tight-side tension
- $T_2$ = slack-side tension
- $\mu$ = effective friction/traction coefficient
- $\theta$ = angle of wrap (radians)

> [!WARNING]
> This is **the single most important equation** in CVT design. It governs the **maximum torque that can be transmitted** before the belt slips. KTU questions often test this with a numerical substitution (e.g., compute $T_1$ given $T_2 = 200$ N, $\mu = 0.1$, $\theta = 170°$).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Transmission Ratio in a Variable-Pulley V-Belt CVT

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Prove that varying the pulley face separation alone changes the effective ratio, and derive the relationship between axial piston force, belt tension, and transmissible torque.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Build

**Step 1: Geometric Definition of Effective Radius**

A variable-diameter pulley has two conical halves (sheaves) that move axially. The V-belt rides up or down the cone as the halves converge or diverge. The effective radius $r$ at which the belt makes contact is given by:

$$r = r_{hub} + (x \cdot \tan\alpha)$$

where:
- $r_{hub}$ = radius of the pulley hub (inner groove)
- $x$ = axial displacement of the movable sheave
- $\alpha$ = half-angle of the V-groove (typically **11° – 19°**)

**Step 2: Ratio Definition**

By definition of the V-belt CVT:

$$i = \dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{r_2}{r_1}$$

Substituting the effective radius expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= \dfrac{r_{hub,2} + x_2 \cdot \tan\alpha}{r_{hub,1} + x_1 \cdot \tan\alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Minimum and Maximum Ratios**

When the input pulley is fully closed ($x_1 = x_{1,max}$) and the output pulley is fully open ($x_2 = x_{2,min}$):

$$i_{min} = \dfrac{r_{hub,2} + x_{2,min}\tan\alpha}{r_{hub,1} + x_{1,max}\tan\alpha} \quad \text{(overdrive)}$$

When the input pulley is fully open ($x_1 = x_{1,min}$) and the output pulley is fully closed ($x_2 = x_{2,max}$):

$$i_{max} = \dfrac{r_{hub,2} + x_{2,max}\tan\alpha}{r_{hub,1} + x_{1,min}\tan\alpha} \quad \text{(launch gear)}$$

**Step 4: Torque Transmission Limit — Clamping Force**

The belt cannot transmit torque without a normal force clamping it into the V-groove. The transmissible tangential force is:

$$F_t = \mu \cdot F_n = \mu \cdot (2 F_{clamp} \cdot \sin\alpha)$$

The factor 2 appears because **both sheaves** exert normal force on the belt. Therefore, the maximum transmissible torque is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= F_t \cdot r_{eff} \\
&= 2 \mu F_{clamp} r_{eff} \sin\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Required Clamping Force**

Rearranging for the required hydraulic clamping force $F_{clamp}$:

$$F_{clamp} = \dfrac{T_{max}}{2 \mu r_{eff} \sin\alpha}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: The clamping force must be **proportional to the transmitted torque**, otherwise the belt slips. This is why modern CVTs use **electronic pressure control** — the line pressure scales with engine torque demand. KTU problems frequently ask you to compute the line pressure $p$ from $F_{clamp} = p \cdot A_{piston}$.

**Step 6: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)**

> **Given**: $T_{max} = 180$ N·m, $r_{eff} = 0.07$ m, $\mu = 0.1$, $\alpha = 14°$, $A_{piston} = 12$ cm² $= 12 \times 10^{-4}$ m².
>
> **Find**: Required line pressure $p$ (in bar).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp} &= \dfrac{180}{2 \times 0.1 \times 0.07 \times \sin 14°} \\
&= \dfrac{180}{0.014 \times 0.2419} \\
&= \dfrac{180}{0.003387} \\
&= 53{,}143 \text{ N} \quad (\approx 53.1 \text{ kN})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{piston}} = \dfrac{53{,}143}{12 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 4.43 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
&= 44.3 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(or } \approx 443 \text{ bar)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a **realistic automotive value** — production CVTs use line pressures in the range **30 – 50 bar (low speed) up to 450 bar (full load)**, controlled by a stepper-motor-driven pressure regulator.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Toroidal CVT — Traction Force on the Torus Surface

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the relation between the cam angle $\gamma$, the contact radii, and the ratio in a **half-toroidal CVT** (e.g., NSK, Fuji Heavy Industries).

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Build

**Step 1: Geometry of the Toroidal Surface**

A half-toroidal CVT has:
- An input disc and output disc with **concave toroidal faces**.
- Two **traction rollers** positioned between the discs, tilted at an angle $\gamma$ about an axis perpendicular to the disc axis.

**Step 2: Effective Contact Radii**

The contact point on the input disc is at radius $R_i$ and on the output disc at radius $R_o$. From the toroidal geometry:

$$R_i = R_{major} - r_{roller} \cdot \sin\gamma$$

$$R_o = R_{major} - r_{roller} \cdot \sin(\beta - \gamma)$$

where:
- $R_{major}$ = radius of the toroidal "donut" (the major radius of the torus)
- $r_{roller}$ = radius of the traction roller
- $\beta$ = the angle subtended by the line connecting input-disc center to output-disc center, measured at the torus center
- $\gamma$ = the tilt angle of the roller (the **"shift" parameter**)

**Step 3: Ratio Derivation**

The contact point velocities must match on the roller (no slip):

$$\omega_i \cdot R_i = \omega_o \cdot R_o$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i = \dfrac{\omega_i}{\omega_o} &= \dfrac{R_o}{R_i} \\
&= \dfrac{R_{major} - r_{roller}\sin(\beta - \gamma)}{R_{major} - r_{roller}\sin\gamma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Ratio Range**

- When $\gamma = 0$: $i = 1$ (direct drive).
- When $\gamma = \gamma_{max}$: $i$ reaches its extreme.
- For a typical half-toroidal CVT, the spread is **4 : 1 to 6 : 1**.

**Step 5: Hertzian Contact Pressure**

The contact between the roller and the toroidal disc is a **Hertzian line contact** (a line on a curved surface). The maximum contact pressure is:

$$p_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{F_n \cdot E^*}{\pi \cdot L \cdot R_{eff}}}$$

where:
- $F_n$ = normal force between roller and disc
- $E^*$ = effective elastic modulus (steel-steel ≈ **2.3 × 10¹¹ Pa**)
- $L$ = contact length
- $R_{eff}$ = effective radius of curvature

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**: Students often confuse the **effective radius** in a toroidal CVT (which depends on $\gamma$) with the **physical roller radius**. The roller does not move — only the **contact point** on the toroidal surface moves when the roller tilts.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydrostatic CVT — Variable Displacement Pump-Motor Pair

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the infinite ratio control in a hydrostatic transmission (used in excavators, agricultural tractors, mining vehicles — and academically in KTU Module 4).

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Build

**Step 1: Swashplate Geometry**

A swashplate pump has a piston block driven by the input shaft. The pistons stroke through a distance $S$:

$$S = 2 r_p \cdot \tan\theta_{pump}$$

where $r_p$ = piston pitch radius, $\theta_{pump}$ = swashplate angle.

**Step 2: Pump Displacement**

The volumetric displacement per revolution is:

$$V_{D,pump} = \pi \cdot d_p^2 \cdot z \cdot \dfrac{S}{2}$$

where $d_p$ = piston bore, $z$ = number of pistons. Simplified for KTU problems:

$$V_{D,pump} = K_p \cdot \tan\theta_{pump}$$

where $K_p$ is a pump-specific constant (cc/rev per unit $\tan\theta$).

**Step 3: Flow Continuity**

The flow produced by the pump equals the flow consumed by the motor (assuming lossless lines):

$$Q = V_{D,pump} \cdot n_{pump} = V_{D,motor} \cdot n_{motor}$$

**Step 4: Motor Displacement**

If the motor is fixed-displacement, $V_{D,motor} = $ constant. If variable, $V_{D,motor} = K_m \tan\theta_{motor}$.

**Step 5: Hydrostatic CVT Ratio**

The transmission ratio becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{hydro} = \dfrac{n_{pump}}{n_{motor}} = \dfrac{V_{D,motor}}{V_{D,pump}} = \dfrac{K_m \tan\theta_{motor}}{K_p \tan\theta_{pump}}
\end{aligned}
$$

By varying **either** $\theta_{motor}$ (variable motor) or **both** $\theta_{pump}$ and $\theta_{motor}$ (full hydrostatic CVT), the ratio varies from 0 to $\infty$ — truly infinite ratio range.

**Step 6: Power Balance (for Numericals)**

Power input to pump:

$$P_{in} = T_{pump} \cdot \omega_{pump} = p \cdot Q = p \cdot V_{D,pump} \cdot n_{pump}$$

Power output from motor:

$$P_{out} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{motor} = \eta_{hm} \cdot p \cdot V_{D,motor} \cdot n_{motor}$$

where $\eta_{hm}$ = hydro-mechanical efficiency of the motor.

**Step 7: Worked Numerical (Hydrostatic CVT)**

> **Given**: Pump: $V_{D,pump} = 28$ cc/rev, runs at 1800 rpm. Motor: $V_{D,motor} = 56$ cc/rev, $\eta_{hm} = 0.90$. System pressure: $p = 35$ MPa.
>
> **Find**: Motor speed, motor torque, and output power.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{V_{D,pump} \cdot n_{pump}}{1000} = \dfrac{28 \times 1800}{1000} = 50.4 \text{ L/min} = 8.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{motor} &= \dfrac{Q \cdot 1000}{V_{D,motor}} = \dfrac{50.4 \times 1000}{56} = 900 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{motor} &= \dfrac{p \cdot V_{D,motor}}{2\pi} \cdot \eta_{hm} = \dfrac{35 \times 10^6 \times 56 \times 10^{-6}}{2\pi} \times 0.90 \\
&= \dfrac{1960}{2\pi} \times 0.90 = 312.0 \times 0.90 = 280.8 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{motor} = 280.8 \times \dfrac{2\pi \times 900}{60} \\
&= 280.8 \times 94.25 = 26{,}464 \text{ W} \approx 26.5 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: The Extroid CVT (Nissan) — Dual-Cone Half-Toroidal

The **Extroid CVT** uses **two input discs and one output disc** with **three rollers** to double the torque capacity while keeping the ratio range. The analytical model is similar to the toroidal CVT, but with a **torque-doubling** principle:

$$T_{output} = 2 \cdot T_{single-toroidal}$$

because the load is shared between two input discs (each carrying half the engine torque). This was the design used in the **Nissan Cedric / Gloria** (1992) with a 2.0 L V6 engine.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of CVT Types

> [!TIP]
> **Use this comparison table when answering KTU "Compare and contrast" or "Differentiate" questions on CVTs (typically 7-mark sub-parts).**

| Feature | V-Belt (Push-belt) | Toroidal | Hydrostatic | Hydrodynamic | Cone / Ratcheting |
|---------|--------------------|----------|-------------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Coupling medium** | Steel push-belt | Traction rollers | Hydraulic fluid | Traction fluid | Friction cone / One-way clutch |
| **Efficiency** | 85%–92% | 90%–95% | 80%–85% | 75%–85% (with slip) | 70%–85% |
| **Max torque** | ≈ 400 N·m | ≈ 350 N·m | ≈ 1000+ N·m | Torque converter (stall) | ≈ 200 N·m |
| **Ratio range** | 4.5 : 1 – 7.0 : 1 | 4 : 1 – 6 : 1 | 0 : 1 to ∞ : 1 | 1 : 1 to 2 : 1 (coupling) | 4 : 1 typical |
| **Vehicle type** | Passenger cars | Mid-size cars | Off-road, tractors | Launch device | Small vehicles, hybrid |
| **Complexity** | Medium | High | High | Low | Low |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High | High | Low | Low |
| **Examples** | Nissan Xtronic, Honda M-CVT | NSK, Fuji, NTN | Bosch Rexroth, Linde | Voith, Torotrak | Toyota Previa (legacy) |
| **Limitation** | Belt wear; "rubber-band" feel | Roller tilt mechanism sealing | Noise, heat, leakage | Low efficiency | Discrete jumps |
