# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — V-Belt CVT

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q1.** Define a Continuously Variable Transmission. With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **variable-diameter V-belt CVT** (Van Doorne type). State any **two advantages** of a CVT over a conventional automatic transmission.

**Model Answer:**

A **Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)** is an automatic transmission in which the effective gear ratio between the input (engine) and output (driveshaft) varies **steplessly** within a designed range, in contrast to the discrete steps of a conventional stepped transmission. The variation is achieved by altering the **effective running diameter** of the input and output pulleys simultaneously.

In the **Van Doorne V-belt CVT**, two pulleys — each consisting of a fixed and a movable conical sheave — are connected by a special V-belt (or a steel push-belt). The movable sheaves are actuated axially by engine-oil hydraulic pressure controlled by the **TCU (Transmission Control Unit)**. When the input pulley closes, the belt rides outward, increasing the effective input radius; simultaneously, the output pulley opens, decreasing the output radius, producing a **lower ratio** (overdrive for highway cruise). The reverse happens during launch.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Better fuel economy** — engine can be held at the point of minimum specific fuel consumption (BSFC).
2. **Smoother driving** — no discrete shift shock; the ratio change is seamless.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip**: Always draw the **two pulleys with V-belt**, label the **fixed sheave, movable sheave, hydraulic actuator, and the belt direction**. A diagram without labels typically loses 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — Toroidal vs Hydrostatic

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

> **Q2.** Differentiate between a **toroidal CVT** and a **hydrostatic CVT** in terms of working principle, efficiency, typical application, and ratio range.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Toroidal CVT | Hydrostatic CVT |
|--------|--------------|------------------|
| **Working principle** | Friction/traction between inclined rollers and concave toroidal discs; roller tilt changes effective contact radius. | Hydraulic fluid pressure from a swashplate pump drives a hydraulic motor; ratio varied by swashplate angle. |
| **Efficiency** | 90% – 95% | 80% – 85% |
| **Ratio range** | 4 : 1 to 6 : 1 | 0 : 1 to ∞ : 1 (theoretically) |
| **Application** | Mid-size passenger cars (NSK, NTN units) | Off-road vehicles, excavators, agricultural tractors, large mining trucks |
| **Coupling medium** | Steel-steel traction with traction oil film | Hydraulic fluid (mineral oil) |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Insight**: Examiners award **½ mark for each of the four differentiation points** clearly stated. Skipping a column loses marks even if the answer is long. **Use a comparison table** — it is the safest format for a "differentiate" question.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice** (either/or). Each question typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Below are **two alternative full questions**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — V-Belt CVT and Clamping Force

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

> **Q(A)(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **variable-diameter V-belt CVT**. Derive the expression for the **maximum transmissible torque** in terms of the clamping force, the V-groove half-angle, the effective radius, and the coefficient of friction.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** A V-belt CVT consists of two pulleys, each with a fixed and a movable conical sheave, connected by a special V-belt (in modern systems, a **steel push-belt** with multiple segment layers). The movable sheave is actuated by a hydraulic piston controlled by the **TCU** through a shift solenoid. A separate **clamping pressure** is applied to both pulleys to prevent belt slip.

**Working:** During **launch**, the input pulley sheaves are spread apart (small effective radius) and the output sheaves are closed (large effective radius), producing a high ratio (large torque multiplication). As the vehicle accelerates, the TCU commands the sheaves to close on the input side and open on the output side, smoothly reducing the ratio until it reaches the **overdrive** value for highway cruising. The belt continuously migrates up and down the V-cones — there are **no discrete shift events**.

**Derivation of Maximum Transmissible Torque:**

Consider one pulley. The belt is clamped between the two V-groove sheaves with axial force $F_{clamp}$. Each sheave exerts a normal force on the belt at the V-angle $2\alpha$:

$$F_{n,each} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{2\sin\alpha}$$

The total normal force is the sum from both sheaves:

$$F_n = 2 \cdot \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{2\sin\alpha} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{\sin\alpha}$$

The maximum tangential (driving) force, by Coulomb's law of friction, is:

$$F_t = \mu F_n = \dfrac{\mu F_{clamp}}{\sin\alpha}$$

This force acts at the effective radius $r$, so the maximum torque is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= F_t \cdot r = \dfrac{\mu F_{clamp} \cdot r}{\sin\alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Numerical (if extended to 7 marks):**
> Given $F_{clamp} = 5000$ N, $r = 0.075$ m, $\alpha = 14°$, $\mu = 0.10$:
> $$T_{max} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 5000 \times 0.075}{\sin 14°} = \dfrac{37.5}{0.2419} = 155.0 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Diagram to draw**:
> ```
>    Movable Sheave
>    \    |    /
>     \ α | α /
>      \  |  /     ← V-groove angle = 2α
>       \ | /
>        \|/
>    ============= Belt cross-section
>        /|\
>       / | \
>      / α|α \
>     /   |   \
>    /    |    \
>    Fixed Sheave
> ```
> The clamping force $F_{clamp}$ is applied vertically. The two sheave reactions are normal forces at angle $\alpha$ to the surface.

---

> **Q(A)(b) [7 Marks]** A passenger car uses a V-belt CVT with the following data: Input pulley radius range $r_1 = 30$ mm to $70$ mm. Output pulley radius range $r_2 = 40$ mm to $90$ mm. Engine torque: 160 N·m. Coefficient of friction: 0.09. V-groove half-angle: 15°. Find the **(i) maximum and minimum transmission ratios**, **(ii) required clamping force** at the worst case, and **(iii) hydraulic line pressure** if the piston area is 15 cm².

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum and Minimum Ratios:**

Maximum ratio (launch, low output speed):

$$i_{max} = \dfrac{r_{2,max}}{r_{1,min}} = \dfrac{90}{30} = 3.0$$

Minimum ratio (overdrive, high output speed):

$$i_{min} = \dfrac{r_{2,min}}{r_{1,max}} = \dfrac{40}{70} = 0.5714 \approx 0.57$$

Ratio spread:

$$R = \dfrac{i_{max}}{i_{min}} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.5714} = 5.25$$

**(ii) Required Clamping Force:**

Worst case = **maximum torque** (launch) at the **smallest effective radius** of the driving pulley (because the belt must transfer full torque through the smallest contact patch). However, by the formula $T_{max} = \mu F_{clamp} r / \sin\alpha$, the worst case is the **smallest $r$** giving the largest required $F_{clamp}$.

Using $r = r_{1,min} = 0.030$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp} &= \dfrac{T_{max} \cdot \sin\alpha}{\mu \cdot r} = \dfrac{160 \times \sin 15°}{0.09 \times 0.030} \\
&= \dfrac{160 \times 0.2588}{0.0027} = \dfrac{41.41}{0.0027} = 15{,}337 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Hydraulic Line Pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A} = \dfrac{15{,}337}{15 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.023 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 10.23 \text{ MPa} = 102.3 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**:
> 1. **Common Mistake 1**: Students compute $F_{clamp}$ using $r_{1,max}$ (the large radius) and get a very small $F_{clamp}$. This is WRONG — the **smallest** effective radius demands the **highest** clamping force to prevent slip.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to convert cm² to m². 15 cm² = **15 × 10⁻⁴ m²**, not 15 m².
> 3. **Common Mistake 3**: Reporting the answer in Pa instead of bar or MPa. KTU answers should always end with units.
> 4. **Diagram Requirement**: If the sketch in part (a) is missing or unlabeled, **2 marks are usually deducted** out of 7.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Hydrostatic and Toroidal CVT

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

> **Q(B)(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of a hydrostatic CVT** used in heavy earth-moving equipment. Derive the expression for the **transmission ratio** in terms of the swashplate angles of the pump and motor.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction & Working:**
A hydrostatic CVT consists of a **variable-displacement axial-piston pump** driven by the engine, and a **variable (or fixed) displacement hydraulic motor** driving the wheels. The pump and motor are connected by **two high-pressure hoses** (high-pressure line and return line). A **charge pump** replenishes oil lost to internal leakage, and a **heat exchanger (cooler)** dissipates the heat generated.

The **swashplate** of the pump is tilted by an actuator (often electronically controlled). When the swashplate is vertical ($\theta = 0°$), there is no displacement and the pump produces no flow — the vehicle is **stationary**. As $\theta$ increases, the pump delivers flow at high pressure, driving the motor and turning the wheels. By **varying $\theta$** and (in full hydrostatic CVTs) also the motor's swashplate, the **ratio is varied steplessly** from 0 to $\infty$.

**Block Diagram:**
```
[Engine] → [Variable Pump ⤢] → High-Pressure Hose → [Variable Motor ⤢] → [Wheels]
              ↑                                              ↓
              Swashplate θ_pump                     Return Hose
                                                       ↓
[Charge Pump] → [Reservoir] → [Cooler] → [Pump Inlet]
              [Pressure Relief Valve] → [Reservoir]
```

**Derivation of Transmission Ratio:**

Pump displacement per revolution:

$$V_{D,pump} = K_p \tan\theta_{pump}$$

Motor displacement per revolution (for variable motor):

$$V_{D,motor} = K_m \tan\theta_{motor}$$

Flow continuity (steady state):

$$V_{D,pump} \cdot n_{pump} = V_{D,motor} \cdot n_{motor}$$

Solving for the speed ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{hydro} = \dfrac{n_{pump}}{n_{motor}} &= \dfrac{V_{D,motor}}{V_{D,pump}} \\
&= \dfrac{K_m \tan\theta_{motor}}{K_p \tan\theta_{pump}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **fixed motor** ($V_{D,motor} =$ const), the ratio is controlled solely by $\tan\theta_{pump}$, giving a ratio range of 0 to $\infty$.

---

> **Q(B)(b) [7 Marks]** A half-toroidal CVT has a major radius $R = 100$ mm, roller radius $r = 30$ mm, and the angle $\beta = 90°$. The traction coefficient is $\mu = 0.10$. The applied normal force per roller is $F_n = 4$ kN. Find the **(i) input and output contact radii** when the roller is tilted at $\gamma = 30°$, and **(ii) the transmissible torque** at this condition. **Comment** on the variation of the ratio as $\gamma$ varies from 0 to 90°.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $R = 100$ mm, $r = 30$ mm, $\beta = 90°$, $\mu = 0.10$, $F_n = 4000$ N, $\gamma = 30°$.

**(i) Input and Output Contact Radii:**

Using the toroidal CVT geometry equations derived in Section 3.2:

$$R_i = R - r \sin\gamma = 100 - 30 \sin 30° = 100 - 30 \times 0.5 = 85 \text{ mm}$$

$$R_o = R - r \sin(\beta - \gamma) = 100 - 30 \sin(60°) = 100 - 30 \times 0.8660 = 74.02 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Transmissible Torque:**

Tangential force capacity:

$$F_t = \mu F_n = 0.10 \times 4000 = 400 \text{ N}$$

Torque transmitted at the **output disc** (since both rollers feed the output disc):

$$T_{out} = 2 \cdot F_t \cdot R_o = 2 \times 400 \times 0.07402 = 59.22 \text{ N·m}$$

(The factor 2 appears because two rollers share the load in a typical half-toroidal design.)

Equivalently at the input:

$$T_{in} = 2 \cdot F_t \cdot R_i = 2 \times 400 \times 0.085 = 68.0 \text{ N·m}$$

**Verification of ratio:**

$$i = \dfrac{\omega_{in}}{\omega_{out}} = \dfrac{R_o}{R_i} = \dfrac{74.02}{85} = 0.871 \quad (\text{overdrive side})$$

Note that $T_{out}/T_{in} = R_i/R_o = 1.148$, and $T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in} = T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out}$ (energy conservation, ignoring loss).

**Comment on Ratio Variation as $\gamma$ varies from 0° to 90°:**

At $\gamma = 0°$: $R_i = R_o = 100 - 30 \sin 0° = 100$ mm (wait — when $\beta = 90°$ and $\gamma = 0$, both radii equal $R - r\sin 0 = R = 100$ mm — direct drive, ratio = 1).

At $\gamma = 30°$: $i = 0.871$ (overdrive).

At $\gamma = 60°$: $R_i = 100 - 30 \sin 60° = 74.02$ mm, $R_o = 100 - 30 \sin 30° = 85$ mm, $i = 85/74.02 = 1.148$ (underdrive).

At $\gamma = 90°$: $R_i = 100 - 30 \sin 90° = 70$ mm, $R_o = 100 - 30 \sin 0° = 100$ mm, $i = 100/70 = 1.428$ (maximum underdrive for this configuration).

So as $\gamma$ increases from 0 to 90°, the ratio **continuously increases from 1 to 1.428** in this geometry. In a real CVT, the geometry is designed for a ratio range of typically 4:1 — this example is simplified for KTU numerical purposes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls for Toroidal CVT Questions:**
> 1. **Sign of $R_i$ and $R_o$**: Both must remain **positive**. If a student gets a negative value, the geometry is invalid for that $\gamma$.
> 2. **Confusion of roller radius vs. contact radius**: The roller does **not** move radially; only the **contact point** on the toroidal surface does. $R_i$ and $R_o$ are **contact radii on the discs**, not on the roller.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2**: In a **half-toroidal** CVT there are two rollers; the output torque is doubled. In a **full-toroidal** CVT (one input, one output disc, with rollers on both sides) the analysis is different.
> 4. **Units**: mm must be converted to m before computing torque in N·m. A torque answer in "N·mm" is worth 0 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Topic: Types of CVT**

- **Definition**: A CVT provides a **stepless (continuous)** range of gear ratios, in contrast to the discrete steps of a conventional AT/MT.

- **Five major families** of CVTs (most likely KTU 2024 sub-questions):
  1. **V-belt / push-belt CVT** (Van Doorne) — most common in cars.
  2. **Toroidal CVT** (half and full) — smoother, higher efficiency, more expensive.
  3. **Hydrostatic CVT** — pump-motor pair, off-highway use.
  4. **Hydrodynamic CVT** — Voith Dipling, Torotrak, Extroid (Nissan).
  5. **Cone / Ratcheting CVT** — older, niche applications.

- **V-belt CVT key equation**: $T_{max} = \mu F_{clamp} r / \sin\alpha$. Clamping force scales with torque to prevent slip.

- **Ratio definition**: $i = r_{out}/r_{in}$ for all friction/traction CVTs. For hydrodynamic units, $i$ is limited (≤ 2) because they are essentially torque converters.

- **Euler's belt equation** $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ governs the **maximum transmittable torque** — **memorize this**.

- **Toroidal CVT geometry**: $R_i = R - r\sin\gamma$ and $R_o = R - r\sin(\beta - \gamma)$. At $\gamma = 0$, ratio is **unity** (direct drive).

- **Hydrostatic CVT ratio**: $i_{hydro} = K_m \tan\theta_{motor} / (K_p \tan\theta_{pump})$. Range theoretically **0 to ∞**.

- **Efficiency ranking** (best to worst): Toroidal (95%) > V-belt (92%) > Hydrostatic (85%) > Hydrodynamic (80%).

- **Ratio spread** (span): Modern automotive CVTs use **6:1 to 7:1** spreads. KTU problems may give you a 5:1 spread; remember $R_{spread} = i_{max}/i_{min}$.

- **TCU (Transmission Control Unit)** is the brain of every modern CVT — it controls hydraulic pressure, pulley sheave position, and traction-fluid properties based on engine load, vehicle speed, and accelerator pedal position.

- **The "rubber-band effect"**: At wide-open throttle, a CVT allows the engine to rev high and stay there, which feels like a slipping clutch. Stepped ATs avoid this; modern CVTs use a **"stepped" or "shifted" programming** to mimic a 7- or 8-speed AT.

- **Historical milestones** for KTU short-answer:
  - **1958** — Van Doorne's first V-belt CVT (DAF).
  - **1987** — Subaru ECVT (toroidal) in the Justy.
  - **1989** — Subaru first chain-driven CVT.
  - **1992** — Nissan Extroid CVT (toroidal, dual-cam).
  - **2002** — Audi Multitronic (chain-driven, 7:1 spread).

- **Units you must always convert in KTU problems**:
  - $r$ in **m**, not mm (1 mm = 10⁻³ m).
  - $A$ in **m²**, not cm² (1 cm² = 10⁻⁴ m²).
  - Pressure in **Pa or bar or MPa**, not mixed.
  - Torque in **N·m**, not N·mm.
  - Power in **W or kW**, not HP (1 HP ≈ 746 W).

- **The single most-tested formula on KTU exams for CVTs**: $T_{max} = \mu F_{clamp} r / \sin\alpha$ — practice this with at least 3 numerical variations.

- **Draw diagrams liberally**: KTU examiners award **2 to 3 marks** (out of 7 or 14) purely for a **correct, labeled schematic**. A text answer without a sketch loses marks even if the content is correct.

---

**End of Notes — Module 4, Topic: Types of CVT (PBMUT404)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Shift Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gear Shift Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear Shift Valve (GSV):** A hydraulically-actuated, spring-loaded **spool valve** used in automatic transmission hydraulic control systems that determines the engagement and disengagement of gear elements (clutches and brake bands) by directing pressurized transmission fluid to the appropriate friction elements based on a balance between **vehicle speed (governor pressure)** and **engine load (throttle/modulator pressure)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the Gear Shift Valve is classified as a **hydraulic pressure control element** belonging to the *Automatic Transmission Hydraulic Control System* sub-module. It is the *decision-making hydraulic brain* of an automatic gearbox — it does not generate power, it merely routes pressurized ATF (Automatic Transmission Fluid) to the correct actuators.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Traffic Police Analogy:** Imagine a multi-lane roundabout (the transmission) where cars (hydraulic fluid) need to be directed to different exits (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th gear) depending on two factors: (a) how fast cars are entering (vehicle speed = governor pressure) and (b) how hard the driver is pressing the accelerator (engine load = throttle pressure). The **Gear Shift Valve is the traffic policeman standing in the middle of the roundabout** who decides, based on these two signals, which lane gets the green light. When the policeman senses that the incoming cars are fast enough and the driver is light on the accelerator, he redirects traffic from the 1st-gear lane to the 2nd-gear lane — this is an **upshift**. The opposite happens during **downshifts**.

## 1.3 Position in the Automatic Transmission Hydraulic Circuit

The Gear Shift Valve sits in a specific location in the overall hydraulic control architecture:

| Stage of Hydraulic Circuit | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure Generation | **Oil Pump** | Generates line pressure (~**4–15 bar** regulated) |
| Pressure Regulation | **Pressure Regulator Valve** | Maintains constant line pressure |
| Manual Selection | **Manual Valve** | Driver-selected gear (P, R, N, D, 2, 1) |
| Speed Sensing | **Governor** | Produces **Governor Pressure** ∝ vehicle speed |
| Load Sensing | **Modulator Valve** | Produces **Modulator/Throttle Pressure** ∝ engine load |
| **Shift Logic** | **Gear Shift Valve (1-2, 2-3, 3-4)** | **Decides UP-shift / DOWN-shift** |
| Actuation | **Clutch Packs & Brake Bands** | Physically engages the gear |
| Lubrication/Cooling | Oil Sump & Cooler | Removes heat from ATF |

## 1.4 Standard Operating Metrics for KTU Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Operating Parameters (Industry Standard for 4-Speed Automatic Transmissions like Aisin AW TF-60SN, ZF 4HP20):**
> - **Line Pressure:** 4–15 bar (regulated)
> - **Governor Pressure Range:** 0.5–6.0 bar (speed-dependent)
> - - **Modulator Pressure Range:** 0.3–3.5 bar (load-dependent)
> - **Shift Time (typical):** 0.3–0.8 seconds
> - **ATF Viscosity Grade:** Dexron III / Mercon V / ATF+4
> - **Number of Shift Valves in 4-speed AT:** **3** (1-2, 2-3, 3-4) + 1 reverse inhibitor valve
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydraulic Control Philosophy (Why Gear Shift Valves Exist)

In a manual transmission, the *driver* decides when to shift. In an automatic transmission, the car must decide for itself. This decision-making is done using **two hydraulic signals**:

1. **Governor Pressure ($P_g$):** A pressure proportional to the **vehicle's road speed**, generated by a mechanical centrifugal governor driven by the transmission output shaft.

2. **Throttle (Modulator) Pressure ($P_t$):** A pressure inversely proportional to **engine load (vacuum)**, generated by the modulator valve connected to engine intake manifold vacuum or electronic throttle.

The Gear Shift Valve compares these two signals using a **force-balance equation on a precision-machined spool**. Whichever signal "wins" determines the spool position, which in turn routes line pressure to either the "low gear" clutch circuit or the "high gear" clutch circuit.

## 2.2 Constructional Anatomy of a Generic Gear Shift Valve

A typical 1-2 shift valve assembly consists of:

> [!NOTE]
> **Spool (1):** A precision-ground cylindrical metal piece with machined lands and grooves. Slides axially inside a matching bore.
>
> **Main Spring (2):** A calibrated helical compression spring that biases the spool to one extreme position.
>
> **Secondary Spring (3):** A lighter spring (sometimes called the "shift spring") that fine-tunes the shift point and provides a hysteresis band.
>
> **Ports (4–8):** Machined openings in the valve body — for line pressure inlet, governor pressure, throttle pressure, low-gear circuit, high-gear circuit, and exhaust.

The spool typically has **3 lands** (raised cylindrical bands) separated by **2 grooves**, with each land having a slightly different diameter — this creates different effective areas on either end of the spool, which is the entire basis of the force-balance equation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation you will be tested on for this topic in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_g = k_g \cdot \omega_o^2$ | Governor pressure ∝ square of output shaft angular velocity | Pa, rad/s |
| 2 | $P_t = P_L \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{MAP}}{P_{atm}}\right)$ | Throttle pressure (vacuum-modulated) | Pa |
| 3 | $F_{net} = P_g \cdot A_g - P_t \cdot A_t - F_s$ | Net axial force on shift valve spool | N |
| 4 | $P_g \cdot A_g = P_t \cdot A_t + F_s$ | **Shift point equilibrium condition** | N |
| 5 | $\dfrac{P_g}{P_t} = \dfrac{A_t}{A_g} + \dfrac{F_s}{P_t \cdot A_g}$ | Critical pressure ratio for shift | dimensionless |
| 6 | $v_{shift} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{g,critical} \cdot A_g - F_s}{k_g \cdot A_g^2}}$ | Vehicle speed at which upshift occurs | m/s |
| 7 | $Q = C_d \cdot A_{port} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{ATF}}}$ | Flow rate through valve ports (orifice equation) | m³/s |
| 8 | $\Delta t_{shift} = \dfrac{V_{clutch}}{Q} = \dfrac{\pi D^2 s / 4}{Q}$ | Shift time (clutch fill time) | seconds |
| 9 | $P_{line,reg} = P_{pump} - \Delta P_{regulator}$ | Line pressure after regulator | Pa |
| 10 | $v_{downshift} = v_{upshift} \cdot (1 - h)$ | Downshift speed (with hysteresis *h*) | m/s |

> **Notation Key:** $A_g$ = governor-end spool area, $A_t$ = throttle-end spool area, $F_s$ = spring preload force, $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (≈**0.6–0.8** for sharp-edged ports), $\rho_{ATF}$ ≈ **870 kg/m³**, MAP = Manifold Absolute Pressure.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

The Gear Shift Valve concept is the foundational principle behind **all modern automatic transmissions**, from 4-speed traditional automatics (AW TF-60SN, ZF 4HP) to the early generations of electronic automatic transmissions (Chrysler 41TE/AE). Even though modern ECVT and DCT systems now use **electronic solenoid valves** controlled by the TCM (Transmission Control Module), the **hydraulic force-balance logic originated by the gear shift valve** is preserved in the embedded control algorithms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial / Marine / Off-Highway Extension:** The same gear shift valve principle is used in:
> - **Marine gearboxes** (e.g., Twin Disc, ZF Marine) for vessel propulsion
> - **Heavy earth-moving equipment** (Caterpillar, Komatsu) for torque converter transmissions
> - **Wind turbine pitch control hydraulics** (similar force-balance spool logic)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Force-Balance Equation on the Shift Valve Spool

This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU 2024 exam on this topic. Every part-A and part-B question is built on this.

### Step 1: Free Body Setup
Consider a single Gear Shift Valve spool floating in its bore. Three forces act on it along the axial direction (let's define the **Governor-pressure end as the LEFT end** and the **Throttle-pressure end as the RIGHT end**):

- $F_g = P_g \cdot A_g$ — pushes spool to the RIGHT (toward low-gear circuit)
- $F_t = P_t \cdot A_t$ — pushes spool to the LEFT (toward high-gear circuit, opposing upshift)
- $F_s$ — spring preload, biases spool toward LOW-gear (1st) position

### Step 2: Define the Direction Convention
Let RIGHTWARD be positive (this is the upshift direction). The spool is in equilibrium when net force = 0.

$$
\sum F_x = 0
$$

$$
F_g - F_t - F_s = 0
$$

### Step 3: Substitute Pressure × Area
$$
P_g \cdot A_g - P_t \cdot A_t - F_s = 0
$$

### Step 4: Rearrange to the Standard Equilibrium Form
$$
P_g \cdot A_g = P_t \cdot A_t + F_s
$$

### Step 5: Solve for the Critical Governor Pressure at Shift Point
$$
P_{g,critical} = \frac{P_t \cdot A_t + F_s}{A_g}
$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** This equation says the spool will move (causing a shift) **only when** the governor pressure $P_g$ exceeds the critical value $P_{g,critical}$. Until that happens, the transmission stays in its current gear.

### Step 6: Express the Shift Vehicle Speed
Since $P_g = k_g \cdot \omega_o^2$ and $\omega_o = \dfrac{v_{vehicle}}{r_{wheel}}$:

$$
P_g = k_g \cdot \frac{v^2}{r_{wheel}^2}
$$

Setting $P_g = P_{g,critical}$:

$$
k_g \cdot \frac{v_{shift}^2}{r_{wheel}^2} = \frac{P_t \cdot A_t + F_s}{A_g}
$$

$$
v_{shift} = r_{wheel} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P_t \cdot A_t + F_s}{k_g \cdot A_g}}
$$

> **Reading this equation:** At **higher throttle opening (higher $P_t$)**, the shift speed $v_{shift}$ **increases** — this is why a car upshifts **later** at wide-open throttle (WOT) and **earlier** at part-throttle. This is the engineering reason your car downshifts only when you "kick down" the accelerator.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shift Hysteresis Calculation

To prevent rapid hunting between gears, a **secondary spring** is added that creates a "dead band". The downshift occurs at a *lower* speed than the upshift.

Let $F_{s1}$ = main spring force at upshift, $F_{s2}$ = secondary spring engaged during downshift, with $F_{s2} > F_{s1}$.

$$
v_{up} = r_{wheel} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P_t \cdot A_t + F_{s1}}{k_g \cdot A_g}}
$$

$$
v_{down} = r_{wheel} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P_t \cdot A_t + F_{s2}}{k_g \cdot A_g}}
$$

$$
\Delta v_{hyst} = v_{up} - v_{down}
$$

$$
h = \frac{\Delta v_{hyst}}{v_{up}} = 1 - \sqrt{\frac{P_t \cdot A_t + F_{s2}}{P_t \cdot A_t + F_{s1}}}
$$

> **Typical design value:** $h$ ≈ **0.10 – 0.20** (10%–20% hysteresis band)

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Clutch Fill Time (Shift Smoothness)

After the shift valve has moved and routed line pressure to a clutch pack, the clutch must fill with hydraulic fluid before torque can be transmitted. The fill time determines shift quality.

Volume of clutch piston chamber:
$$
V_{clutch} = \frac{\pi \cdot D_p^2}{4} \cdot s_{stroke}
$$

Where $D_p$ = clutch piston diameter, $s_{stroke}$ = piston stroke (typically **1.5–3 mm**).

Flow through the valve port (orifice flow):
$$
Q = C_d \cdot A_{port} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{ATF}}}
$$

Shift (clutch fill) time:
$$
\Delta t_{shift} = \frac{V_{clutch}}{Q} = \frac{\pi D_p^2 s_{stroke}}{4 \cdot C_d \cdot A_{port}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{ATF}}{2 \Delta P}}
$$

> **Design Insight:** A short fill time → harsh shift. A long fill time → soft shift but inefficient power transfer. KTU may ask you to compute this for a given problem.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — State Transitions Through the Valve

| Vehicle State | Governor $P_g$ | Throttle $P_t$ | Spool Position | Engaged Circuit | Gear |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Idle, stopped | **0 bar** | High (3.0) | Spring side | Low-gear circuit pressurized | **1st** |
| Light throttle, 40 km/h | 1.5 bar | 2.5 bar | Spring side (still) | Low-gear circuit | **1st** |
| Light throttle, 45 km/h | 1.8 bar | 2.5 bar | Spool shifts RIGHT | High-gear circuit opens | **2nd** ⬆ |
| Wide open throttle (WOT) | 2.0 bar | 0.5 bar | Spool stays LEFT | Low-gear circuit | **1st** (no upshift!) |
| WOT, 80 km/h | 4.5 bar | 0.5 bar | Spool shifts RIGHT | High-gear circuit | **2nd** ⬆ |
| Decelerating, 35 km/h | 1.2 bar | 2.0 bar | Spool stays RIGHT | High-gear circuit | **2nd** (no downshift) |
| Decelerating, 30 km/h | 0.9 bar | 2.0 bar | Spool shifts LEFT (with hysteresis) | Low-gear circuit | **1st** ⬇ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Gear Shift Valve Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **full signal flow** of a generic 4-speed automatic transmission gear shift control system, from the driver's accelerator pedal to the physical engagement of clutch packs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Driver Accelerator Pedal] --> B[Engine Intake Manifold MAP Sensor]
    B --> C[Modulator Valve]
    C -->|P_t Throttle Pressure| D[Gear Shift Valve Spool]

    E[Transmission Output Shaft] --> F[Mechanical Centrifugal Governor]
    F -->|P_g Governor Pressure| D

    G[Engine-Driven Oil Pump] --> H[Pressure Regulator Valve]
    H -->|Regulated Line Pressure P_L| I[Manual Valve]
    I -->|Selected Line Pressure| D

    D -->|Line Pressure Routed to Low-Gear Circuit| J[1st Gear Clutch Pack]
    D -->|Line Pressure Routed to High-Gear Circuit| K[2nd Gear Clutch Pack]

    D -->|Shift Signal| L[2-3 Shift Valve]
    L -->|Low| M[2nd Gear Clutch]
    L -->|High| N[3rd Gear Clutch]

    J --> O[Planetary Gearset]
    K --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Output to Driveshaft]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Cross-Sectional Schematic of the Spool Valve

The diagram below illustrates the internal porting arrangement of a 1-2 shift valve. **The numbering matches Section 2.2 of this note module.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VALVEBODY[Valve Body Cross-Section]
        P1[Port 1: Line Pressure Inlet]
        P2[Port 2: Governor Pressure - Left End]
        P3[Port 3: Low-Gear Circuit Outlet]
        P4[Port 4: High-Gear Circuit Outlet]
        P5[Port 5: Throttle Pressure - Right End]
        SP[Spool - Sliding Element]
        SPR1[Main Spring]
        SPR2[Secondary Shift Spring]
    end

    P2 -->|Pushes Right| SP
    SPR1 -->|Biases Left| SP
    P5 -->|Pushes Left| SP
    SPR2 -->|Engages During Downshift| SP

    SP -->|Position Determines Routing| P3
    SP -->|Position Determines Routing| P4
    P1 -->|Source Fluid| SP
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Logic — The Force-Balance Algorithm

The diagram below captures the *exact* decision logic performed by the spool in plain engineering terms. This is what the KTU examiner expects a student to draw if asked to "explain the working of a gear shift valve with a flowchart".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Vehicle Operating]) --> READ1[Read Governor Pressure P_g]
    READ1 --> READ2[Read Throttle Pressure P_t]
    READ2 --> CALC[Compute Net Force F_net = P_g A_g - P_t A_t - F_s]

    CALC --> DECIDE{F_net > 0 ?}

    DECIDE -->|YES - Governor Wins| UPSHIFT[Spool Moves Right - UPSHIFT]
    DECIDE -->|NO - Throttle and Spring Win| STAY[Spool Stays - HOLD GEAR]

    UPSHIFT --> ROUTE[Line Pressure Routed to Next Gear Clutch]
    ROUTE --> FILL[Clutch Fill Phase]
    FILL --> ENGAGE[New Gear Engaged]
    ENGAGE --> START

    STAY --> START
```

## 4.4 KTU Board Exam Tip — Diagram Conventions

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawing conventions used in KTU answer scripts:**
> - Use **rectangles** for valve body, **circles** for ports, **arrows** for pressure/flow.
> - Label **all pressure lines** ($P_g$, $P_t$, $P_L$) and **all circuits** (L = low, H = high).
> - Mark the **spring** with a standard zigzag symbol and label its preload $F_s$.
> - Indicate the **spool direction of motion** with a clear arrow and a label like "SPOOL SHIFTS RIGHT → UPSHIFT".
> - If you draw a cross-section, mark the **effective areas** $A_g$ and $A_t$ on the spool lands.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Conceptual Definition
**Q: Define a Gear Shift Valve. State the two hydraulic signals it compares to decide a gear shift. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Gear Shift Valve (GSV)** is a hydraulically-actuated, spring-loaded spool valve in an automatic transmission that directs pressurized transmission fluid to the appropriate clutch packs and brake bands to engage the correct gear ratio.

The two hydraulic signals compared are:
1. **Governor Pressure ($P_g$):** Proportional to vehicle output shaft speed (vehicle speed).
2. **Throttle/Modulator Pressure ($P_t$):** Proportional to engine load (inversely proportional to intake manifold vacuum).

When $P_g \cdot A_g > P_t \cdot A_t + F_s$, the spool moves and an upshift occurs.

*(Full marks requires: Definition = 1 mark, Two signals named and defined = 1.5 marks, Condition for shift = 0.5 mark)*

### Question A2: Application-Oriented
**Q: Why does a car with automatic transmission upshift at a HIGHER vehicle speed during Wide-Open-Throttle (WOT) acceleration compared to part-throttle driving? [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
During Wide-Open-Throttle (WOT), the engine intake manifold vacuum is **low** (MAP ≈ atmospheric), so the **throttle pressure $P_t$ drops to a low value** (typically 0.3–0.5 bar). 

According to the shift condition $P_g \cdot A_g = P_t \cdot A_t + F_s$, a **lower $P_t$** means a **higher critical governor pressure** (and therefore higher vehicle speed) is required before the spool moves to initiate an upshift.

Mathematically:

$$
v_{shift} = r_{wheel} \sqrt{\frac{P_t \cdot A_t + F_s}{k_g \cdot A_g}}
$$

Hence, with $P_t$ small, $v_{shift}$ is large — delaying the upshift to keep the engine in its **high-torque RPM band** during full-throttle acceleration. This is the fundamental reason cars "kick down" to a lower gear when you floor the accelerator.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Numerical / Analytical
**Q: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

> A 1-2 Gear Shift Valve in an automatic transmission has the following specifications:
> - Governor-end spool effective area: $A_g = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
> - Throttle-end spool effective area: $A_t = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
> - Main spring preload: $F_s = 30 \text{ N}$
> - Governor pressure constant: $k_g = 4500 \text{ Pa·s}^2/\text{rad}^2$
> - Wheel rolling radius: $r_{wheel} = 0.32 \text{ m}$
> - ATF density: $\rho_{ATF} = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$
>
> The driver is cruising at part-throttle with throttle pressure $P_t = 1.8 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ and governor pressure $P_g = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Determine whether the shift valve is currently in the **upshift** or **hold** state. Compute the critical governor pressure for upshift. If the vehicle accelerates and $P_g$ rises, at what vehicle speed will the 1-2 upshift occur?
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] The clutch piston to be filled during the upshift has diameter $D_p = 80 \text{ mm}$ and stroke $s = 2.0 \text{ mm}$. The shift valve port has effective area $A_{port} = 12 \text{ mm}^2$ and discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.65$. The pressure differential across the port during fill is $\Delta P = 2.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$. Calculate the clutch fill time $\Delta t_{shift}$. Comment on whether this represents a *smooth* or *harsh* shift.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the net axial force on the spool.**

$$
F_{net} = P_g \cdot A_g - P_t \cdot A_t - F_s
$$

$$
F_{net} = (1.5 \times 10^5)(3.0 \times 10^{-4}) - (1.8 \times 10^5)(2.0 \times 10^{-4}) - 30
$$

$$
F_{net} = 45 - 36 - 30 = -21 \text{ N}
$$

Since $F_{net} < 0$, the spool is in the **HOLD state** — the transmission stays in **1st gear** (no upshift). [3 marks]

**Step 2: Compute the critical governor pressure for upshift.**

Using equilibrium condition $P_{g,critical} \cdot A_g = P_t \cdot A_t + F_s$:

$$
P_{g,critical} = \frac{P_t \cdot A_t + F_s}{A_g} = \frac{(1.8 \times 10^5)(2.0 \times 10^{-4}) + 30}{3.0 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
P_{g,critical} = \frac{36 + 30}{3.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{66}{3.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.2 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2.2 \text{ bar}
$$

[2 marks]

**Step 3: Compute the shift vehicle speed.**

The relationship is $P_g = k_g \cdot \omega_o^2 = k_g \cdot (v/r_{wheel})^2$. Setting $P_g = P_{g,critical}$:

$$
v_{shift} = r_{wheel} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P_{g,critical}}{k_g}}
$$

$$
v_{shift} = 0.32 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2.2 \times 10^5}{4500}}
$$

$$
v_{shift} = 0.32 \cdot \sqrt{48.89} = 0.32 \cdot 6.99 = 2.24 \text{ m/s}
$$

Converting to km/h: $v_{shift} = 2.24 \times 3.6 = 8.05 \text{ km/h}$ [2 marks]

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the clutch piston chamber volume.**

$$
V_{clutch} = \frac{\pi D_p^2}{4} \cdot s = \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} \cdot (0.002)
$$

$$
V_{clutch} = \frac{\pi \cdot 6.4 \times 10^{-3}}{4} \cdot 0.002 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.002 = 1.005 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3
$$

[1 mark]

**Step 2: Compute the volumetric flow rate through the port.**

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A_{port} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{ATF}}}
$$

$$
Q = 0.65 \cdot (12 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^5}{870}}
$$

$$
Q = 7.8 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \sqrt{574.71} = 7.8 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 23.97 = 1.870 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

[3 marks]

**Step 3: Compute the fill time.**

$$
\Delta t_{shift} = \frac{V_{clutch}}{Q} = \frac{1.005 \times 10^{-5}}{1.870 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0537 \text{ s} \approx 54 \text{ ms}
$$

[2 marks]

**Step 4: Comment on shift quality.**

A fill time of **~54 ms** is well within the **0.3–0.8 s** typical shift duration range, with the remaining time being the *torque phase* and *inertia phase*. This represents a **moderately smooth upshift** — not harsh, but not luxury-grade soft either. A fill time **< 30 ms** would feel harsh (clash), and **> 100 ms** would feel sloppy (flare).

[1 mark]

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Descriptive / Design-Oriented
**Q: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a 1-2 Gear Shift Valve** in an automatic transmission. Clearly show the **line pressure, governor pressure, throttle pressure** ports and the **low-gear and high-gear circuit outlets**. Describe the **force-balance mechanism** in your explanation.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Explain the concept of **shift hysteresis** in gear shift valves. Why is it needed? With the help of a **shift-speed vs. throttle-pressure graph**, show how the upshift and downshift points differ. State typical values of hysteresis for a production 4-speed automatic transmission.

---

#### Model Solution Outline to Question B

**Part (a) — Construction & Working [7 marks]:**

- **Construction (3 marks):** List the components — spool, main spring, secondary spring, valve body, ports. Mention typical materials (hardened steel spool in aluminum alloy valve body, clearances ≈ **0.02–0.05 mm**).
- **Sketch (2 marks):** Cross-sectional diagram showing all 5 ports, both springs, and the spool. Use the Mermaid diagram from **Section 4.2** of this note as a reference.
- **Working (2 marks):** Explain that the spool position is determined by the force balance $P_g \cdot A_g$ vs $P_t \cdot A_t + F_s$. When governor pressure wins, spool moves and line pressure is routed to the 2nd-gear clutch. Describe the centripetal/centrifugal governor for $P_g$ and the vacuum-modulated valve for $P_t$.

**Part (b) — Shift Hysteresis [7 marks]:**

- **Concept (2 marks):** Hysteresis is the deliberate difference between the upshift speed and the downshift speed, created by a secondary spring that engages only during the downshift direction.
- **Why needed (2 marks):** Prevents **shift busyness / hunting** — the transmission rapidly shifting up and down around a single point due to minor speed fluctuations. It also provides a "comfort buffer" for the driver.
- **Graph (2 marks):** Draw $v_{shift}$ (y-axis) vs $P_t$ (x-axis). Plot two curves — upper curve = upshift points, lower curve = downshift points. The vertical gap between them is the hysteresis band. Both curves slope upward (higher $P_t$ → later shifts), but the upshift curve is always above the downshift curve.
- **Typical values (1 mark):** Hysteresis ≈ **10–20%** of the upshift speed. Example: 1-2 upshift at **45 km/h**, downshift at **38 km/h** (a 7 km/h / 15.5% gap).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the spring force $F_s$** — Many students write $P_g \cdot A_g = P_t \cdot A_t$ and miss the spring term. This is a **direct 2-mark penalty** in Part B numericals.
>
> 2. **Mixing up units** — $P_g$ and $P_t$ are typically given in **bar** in industry but **Pa** in SI. KTU will give data in **Pa** — **do not plug in bar without conversion** (1 bar = $10^5$ Pa).
>
> 3. **Ignoring hysteresis in downshift questions** — A common trap: the examiner asks for the **downshift speed** but the student uses the upshift formula. Always check the question wording.
>
> 4. **Not labeling the sketch** — In descriptive questions, an unlabeled diagram gets **0 of the 2 diagram marks**. Always label: ports, springs, spool, areas $A_g$ and $A_t$, and arrow indicating direction of spool motion.
>
> 5. **Confusing the role of $A_g$ and $A_t$** — A larger $A_g$ means **lower** shift pressure is required (since $P_{g,critical} \propto 1/A_g$). Students often write the inverse relationship.
>
> 6. **Skipping the verbal explanation in numericals** — KTU awards partial marks for stating the **physical interpretation** of the result. A numerical answer like "$v_{shift} = 8.05$ km/h" without saying *"This is consistent with a part-throttle 1-2 upshift, as expected in urban driving"* may lose 1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Gear Shift Valve — Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- [x] **Definition:** Hydraulically-actuated, spring-loaded spool valve that routes line pressure to clutches/brake bands based on speed vs load comparison.
- [x] **Two input pressures:** Governor pressure $P_g$ (speed signal) and throttle pressure $P_t$ (load signal).
- [x] **One output action:** Routes line pressure to either the current-gear or next-gear clutch circuit.
- [x] **Master Equation (memorize verbatim):** $P_g \cdot A_g = P_t \cdot A_t + F_s$
- [x] **Upshift occurs when:** $P_g \cdot A_g > P_t \cdot A_t + F_s$
- [x] **Higher $P_t$ (part-throttle) → earlier upshift** (lower $v_{shift}$)
- [x] **Higher $P_t$ (WOT) — actually, lower $P_t$ (WOT) → LATER upshift** (higher $v_{shift}$)
- [x] **Shift point equation:** $v_{shift} = r_{wheel} \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{g,critical}}{k_g}}$
- [x] **Governor pressure formula:** $P_g = k_g \omega_o^2 \propto v^2$
- [x] **Number of shift valves in 4-speed AT:** **3** (1-2, 2-3, 3-4) + 1 reverse inhibitor
- [x] **Hysteresis purpose:** Prevents shift hunting; typically **10–20%** of upshift speed
- [x] **Clutch fill time formula:** $\Delta t_{shift} = \dfrac{V_{clutch}}{Q} = \dfrac{\pi D_p^2 s / 4}{C_d A_{port} \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho_{ATF}}}$
- [x] **Typical line pressure range:** **4–15 bar**
- [x] **Typical spool clearance:** **0.02–0.05 mm** (precision-ground)
- [x] **Always include spring force** in equilibrium equations — common mark-loss area.
- [x] **Sketch must be labeled** — ports, springs, spool, areas, motion arrow.
- [x] **Real-world analogy:** Gear shift valve = traffic policeman directing cars (fluid) to different exits (gears) based on speed and driver demand.
- [x] **Modern evolution:** Electronic solenoids in TCMs now replace the mechanical spool, but the force-balance logic is preserved in the software.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vacuum and Governor Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMUT404 - AUTOMOMOTIVE ENGINES AND TRANSMISSION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vacuum and Governor Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Vacuum Valve — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)**
> A **Vacuum Valve** is a pressure-differential-actuated control element in an automotive engine that opens, closes, or modulates a fluid passage in response to changes in **manifold vacuum** (or any controlled sub-atmospheric pressure). It is a *passive* device — it does not consume fuel or electrical energy to operate; instead, it is driven entirely by the pressure difference between the intake manifold and a reference (atmospheric or controlled) chamber.

Vacuum valves in modern automotive engines are critical for:
- **Ignition timing advance** (Vacuum Advance Unit in the distributor)
- **Exhaust Gas Recirculation** (EGR valve modulation)
- **Positive Crankcase Ventilation** (PCV valve metering)
- **Brake booster actuation** (single-diaphragm or tandem vacuum booster)
- **Throttle positioner / dashpot** functions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis**
> The two vacuum valves that command the highest marks in the KTU University Examination are:
> 1. **Distributor Vacuum Advance Diaphragm** (petrol engines)
> 2. **EGR Vacuum Modulator Valve** (BS-IV / BS-VI emission control)
> Both must be drawn schematically and explained with the *manifold vacuum → diaphragm deflection → linkage displacement* chain.

### 1.2 Governor Valve — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)**
> A **Governor Valve** is a *speed-sensitive feedback control element* in an automotive engine (predominantly **Compression Ignition / Diesel** engines) that automatically regulates the **fuel delivery rate** to maintain the engine speed at a predetermined set value under varying load conditions. It functions as the *closed-loop controller* of the fuel injection pump.

| Governor Type | Sensing Principle | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Centrifugal) Governor** | Flyweight centrifugal force vs. spring force | Inline diesel injection pumps (PE/PEA type) |
| **Hydraulic Governor** | Oil pressure from centrifugal head | Heavy-duty diesel (buses, trucks, gensets) |
| **Pneumatic Governor** | Intake manifold vacuum / pressure | Light commercial diesel (older Tata 407, Mahindra) |
| **Electronic Governor (EDC)** | ECU reads RPM sensor + pedal position | Common-rail CRDi (Bosch, Delphi, Denso) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis**
> The *Hartnell Governor* and the *Porter Governor* are the two analytical governors expected in the 14-mark derivations. Be ready to draw the lever, derive the **sleeve displacement ↔ speed relation**, and the **N₁/N₂ stability condition**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

**Analogy 1 — Vacuum Valve (the "Breathing Lung" Analogy):**
Think of a vacuum valve as the **diaphragm of a human lung**. When the engine "inhales" (intake stroke creates vacuum), the diaphragm is pulled inward by the pressure difference — just as your chest cavity expands and pulls air into the lungs. The mechanical motion of the diaphragm is then used to do useful work: rotate the advance mechanism, lift an EGR pintle, or push the brake pedal rod. The bigger the vacuum (deeper "inhale"), the more the diaphragm deflects, and the more the output linkage moves.

**Analogy 2 — Governor Valve (the "Cruise Control Cruise Director" Analogy):**
Imagine a **cruise control system in a modern car**, but built entirely from spinning weights and springs. The flyweights are like a pair of spinning arms attached to the engine shaft; the faster the engine spins, the harder they fling outward (centrifugal force). They fight against a pre-compressed spring (the "set speed" chosen by the driver). If load drops and speed tries to rise, the weights win, push a sleeve upward, and the sleeve rotates the fuel rack *toward shut-off*. If load increases and speed drops, the spring wins, sleeve moves down, and the rack *opens* to admit more fuel. The driver effectively sets the spring preload — that *is* the accelerator pedal linkage.

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants

The following constants are universally assumed in KTU board problems unless otherwise stated:

- Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar**
- **1 bar = 10⁵ N/m²**
- **Manifold vacuum at idle (petrol)**: ~ **-50 kPa to -70 kPa** (gauge)
- **Full-load manifold vacuum (petrol)**: ~ **-10 kPa to -25 kPa** (near atmospheric)
- **Diesel intake manifold vacuum**: typically **near zero** (~ -3 to -5 kPa) — diesel engines do *not* use vacuum advance; this is why *all* modern diesel engines use *centrifugal + electronic* advance, not vacuum.
- **Typical idle speed**: petrol **800–1000 rpm**, diesel **600–800 rpm**
- **Hartnell governor spring stiffness range**: **s = 5 to 50 N/mm**

---

### 1.5 Schematic of Operating Principle (Vacuum Advance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Chain (memorize for 3-mark questions)**
> `Manifold Vacuum → Diaphragm Deflection → Linkage Rotation → Distributor Advance Plate Rotation → Earlier Spark Timing`

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vacuum Valve — Detailed Operating Theory

#### 2.1.1 Construction (Vacuum Advance Unit)

A vacuum advance unit consists of the following parts:

1. **Diaphragm**: A spring-loaded elastomeric or metal disc clamped between two chambers.
2. **Spring chamber**: One side of the diaphragm is exposed to a calibrated return spring (atmosphere side).
3. **Vacuum chamber**: The other side is connected via a small-bore tube to the **venturi throat / carburetor bore above the throttle plate**.
4. **Linkage rod**: Connects the diaphragm center to the **advance plate** of the distributor (or to the EGR pintle, depending on application).
5. **Vacuum tapping point**: Usually at the **carburetor venturi** (for *ported* vacuum) or *below the throttle plate* (for *manifold* vacuum).

#### 2.1.2 Operating Sequence (Step-by-Step Bullet Logic)

- **Step 1:** At idle, throttle plate is nearly closed → high manifold vacuum (≈ 50–70 kPa below atmosphere).
- **Step 2:** This high vacuum is communicated to the vacuum chamber of the advance unit.
- **Step 3:** The pressure difference across the diaphragm (atmospheric on spring side, vacuum on the other) produces a net force pushing the diaphragm **toward the vacuum side**.
- **Step 4:** Diaphragm deflects against the calibrated spring.
- **Step 5:** The linkage rod connected to the distributor advance plate rotates it in the direction of **advance** (earlier spark).
- **Step 6:** However — at idle we do NOT want advance (idle is light load, low speed). Hence *ported vacuum* is used: the vacuum tapping is **above the throttle plate** so that at idle (throttle nearly closed), the port sits *inside the venturi* but the carburetor airflow at idle is too low to produce significant ported vacuum. As throttle opens, ported vacuum rises and **only then** does advance occur.
- **Step 7:** When the throttle is suddenly closed (overrun), the ported vacuum collapses momentarily, the spring pushes the diaphragm back, and the timing *retards* — preventing after-run backfire.

#### 2.1.3 Real-World Utility of the Vacuum Advance System

- **Fuel economy**: Better burn phasing at part-throttle cruising → 5–8 % fuel saving.
- **Emission control**: Lower HC and CO at part load (combustion closer to MBT — Minimum advance for Best Torque).
- **Driveability**: Eliminates knock tendency under part-throttle acceleration.
- **Anti-backfire**: Prevents the "after-fire" pop on deceleration by retarding the spark at the moment of throttle closure.

#### 2.1.4 EGR Vacuum Modulator — Distinguishing Feature

The **EGR valve** is a vacuum-operated valve that *recirculates a metered portion of exhaust gas* into the intake manifold. Its controlling vacuum signal comes from a **thermal vacuum valve (TVS)** that is itself temperature-dependent. Cold engine → no EGR (to aid warm-up); hot engine → EGR active to lower NOx.

**Emission control logic chain:**
`Combustion Temperature ↑ → NOx ↑ → EGR introduces inert exhaust → lowers flame temperature → NOx ↓`

---

### 2.2 Governor Valve — Detailed Operating Theory

#### 2.2.1 Centrifugal Governor (Porter Type) — Construction & Working

A **Porter Governor** is the simplest form of centrifugal governor and is the *base configuration* for almost all KTU analytical problems.

**Parts:**
1. **Spindle**: Rotated by the engine crankshaft through a gear train.
2. **Two flyweights (balls)**: Pivoted on links attached to the spindle.
3. **Sleeve**: Sliding fit on the spindle; rises and falls as the balls fly out or in.
4. **Bell-crank levers**: Translate sleeve motion into rotation of the fuel-rack pinion.
5. **No spring** in the basic Porter governor (it has *gravity control* — sensitive to vehicle inclination, hence limited to stationary engines like marine propulsion).

#### 2.2.2 Hartnell Governor — Construction & Working

The **Hartnell Governor** uses a *spring* instead of gravity, making it suitable for **vehicles** (since vehicle inclination no longer affects set speed).

**Parts:**
1. **Spindle** with flyweights pivoted at radius `r₁` from the axis.
2. **Roller / sleeve contact** at radius `r₂` (so the lever arm ratio is `b = r₂/r₁`).
3. **Bell-crank lever** pivoted at a fixed fulcrum.
4. **Compression spring** pre-compressed by an adjustable nut (the accelerator linkage).
5. **Sleeve** sliding on the spindle, controlling the fuel rack.

#### 2.2.3 Operating Sequence (Hartnell — Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1:** Engine speed rises (load reduced).
- **Step 2:** Centrifugal force on the flyweights increases → weights swing outward against the spring.
- **Step 3:** The bell-crank lever pivots, the sleeve moves **up** on the spindle.
- **Step 4:** Sleeve movement rotates the fuel-rack pinion → fuel delivery *decreases* (rack moves toward shut-off).
- **Step 5:** Engine speed stabilizes at the new equilibrium where centrifugal force = spring force + friction.

#### 2.2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Element

- **Why a spring (Hartnell) instead of gravity (Porter)?** → Vehicles climb hills, bank on curves; gravity-controlled governors would misbehave.
- **Why a bell-crank lever?** → To magnify a small flyweight radial movement into a larger sleeve axial movement, providing the *sensitivity* needed for fine fuel control.
- **Why a roller at `r₂`?** → Mechanical advantage ratio `b = r₂/r₁` multiplies the sleeve stroke without increasing flyweight mass.
- **Why two flyweights, not one?** → Mechanical balance (radial symmetry) — eliminates side-thrust on the spindle bearings.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every cell is fair game in the KTU 2024 University Exam.**

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Centrifugal force on one flyweight | $F_c$ | $F_c = m \, r \, \omega^2$ | N | $m$ = mass of ball, $r$ = radius of rotation, $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ |
| 2 | Angular velocity from rpm | $\omega$ | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | $N$ in rpm |
| 3 | Mean speed of Hartnell governor (equilibrium) | $N$ | $N = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{s (a + b h)}{m \cdot b \cdot r}}$ | rpm | $s$ = spring stiffness (N/m), $a$ = initial spring compression, $h$ = sleeve lift, $b = r_2/r_1$ |
| 4 | Porter governor — height in terms of speed | $h$ | $h = \dfrac{895}{N^2}$ | m | Valid for the *standard* Porter proportions; for non-standard arms, derive from geometry |
| 5 | Watt governor — same form | $h$ | $h = \dfrac{895}{N^2}$ | m | Identical to Porter — derived from $\tan\alpha = \omega^2 r / g$ with $h$ proportional to $r \cot\alpha$ |
| 6 | Sensitivity of governor | $S_N$ | $S_N = \dfrac{N_2 - N_1}{N}$ | dimensionless | $N_2$ = max equilibrium speed, $N_1$ = min, $N$ = mean |
| 7 | Stability / hunting criterion | $N_2 / N_1$ | $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \le 2$ | — | Hartnell with negative slope of effort curve is *stable* when this holds |
| 8 | Spring force at sleeve lift $h$ | $F_s$ | $F_s = s (a + b h)$ | N | $a$ = initial preload compression, $b$ = lever arm ratio |
| 9 | Stiffness of equivalent spring at sleeve | $s_{eq}$ | $s_{eq} = s \, b^2$ | N/m | Reflects stiffness back to sleeve axis |
| 10 | Effort (controlling force) | $F_c - F_s$ | $\Delta F = m r \omega^2 - s(a+bh)$ | N | Sign convention: positive = sleeve moves up |
| 11 | Pressure differential across vacuum diaphragm | $\Delta p$ | $\Delta p = p_{atm} - p_{manifold}$ | Pa | Typical idle: 50–70 kPa |
| 12 | Diaphragm force (vacuum advance) | $F_d$ | $F_d = \Delta p \cdot A_{dia}$ | N | $A_{dia}$ = effective diaphragm area |
| 13 | Diaphragm deflection (linear spring) | $x$ | $x = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot A_{dia}}{k_s}$ | m | $k_s$ = return spring stiffness |
| 14 | EGR valve flow (orifice equation) | $\dot{m}_{EGR}$ | $\dot{m}_{EGR} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho \, \Delta p}$ | kg/s | $C_d$ ≈ 0.6–0.7, $A$ = pintle lift area |
| 15 | EGR rate (% of exhaust gas) | $R_{EGR}$ | $R_{EGR} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{EGR}}{\dot{m}_{exhaust}} \times 100$ | % | Typical automotive: 5–15 % |
| 16 | NOx reduction (rule of thumb) | $\Delta$NOx | Each 10 % EGR → ≈ 8–10 % NOx reduction | % | Depends on combustion phasing |
| 17 | Porter governor — relation between $h$ and $N$ (general) | $h$ | $h = \dfrac{g}{\omega^2}\tan\alpha = \dfrac{895}{N^2}\tan\alpha$ | m | For non-standard arm angle $\alpha$ |
| 18 | Hartnell stability condition | — | $\dfrac{dF_c}{dh}\bigg/ \dfrac{dF_s}{dh} > 0$ | — | Effort curve slope must be positive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners' Note (2024 Scheme)**
> - In the **vacuum valve** 14-mark question, at least **2 marks** are reserved for stating the **operating sequence** (the 5-step bullet chain). Skipping the chain = −2 marks guaranteed.
> - In the **governor valve** 14-mark question, at least **3 marks** are reserved for the **free-body diagram of the flyweight with all four forces drawn** ($F_c$, $mg$, arm reactions). A diagram without force labels is awarded **zero**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Component | Engineering Field Beyond Automotive |
|---|---|
| Vacuum diaphragm | HVAC pneumatic actuators, medical ventilators, aircraft altimeter simulators |
| Centrifugal governor | Steam turbines, hydro-turbines, marine propulsion, genset prime movers |
| Hartnell spring governor | Helicopter rotor speed control, Wankel engine speed limiter, turbocharger wastegate actuation |
| EGR valve | Industrial burner NOx control, marine engine IMO Tier-III compliance |

## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Height of a Porter Governor in Terms of Speed

> **Problem Setup:** Two balls of mass $m$ each, attached to a Porter governor rotating at speed $N$ rpm. The arms make an angle $\alpha$ with the vertical. Sleeve rises to a height $h$ such that $h = 2 r \cot\alpha$ where $r$ is the ball orbit radius.

**Step 1 — Free body of one ball:**

Forces acting on the ball:
- Weight $mg$ acting downward.
- Centrifugal force $F_c = m r \omega^2$ acting radially outward (horizontal).
- Arm reaction along the arm (does not contribute to moment about the pivot if pivot is at the arm root — the arm is a *two-force* member).

**Step 2 — Force balance along the arm direction:**

Resolving along the arm (tangential balance):

$$
m g \sin\alpha = m r \omega^2 \cos\alpha
$$

**Step 3 — Deriving the speed-height relation:**

Rearranging the equation from Step 2:

$$
\tan\alpha = \frac{r \omega^2}{g}
$$

For the standard Porter geometry where the upper arm length equals the lower arm length and the two arms cross at the sleeve:

$$
h = 2 r \cot\alpha
$$

Substituting $r = \dfrac{h \tan\alpha}{2}$ from the geometry into the force balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\alpha &= \frac{\omega^2}{g} \cdot \frac{h \tan\alpha}{2} \\
1 &= \frac{\omega^2 h}{2g} \\
\omega^2 &= \frac{2g}{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Converting $\omega$ to $N$ (rpm):**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 N^2}{3600} = \frac{\pi^2 N^2}{900}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting and solving for $h$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\pi^2 N^2}{900} &= \frac{2g}{h} \\
h &= \frac{2g \cdot 900}{\pi^2 N^2} = \frac{1800 \, g}{\pi^2 N^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Plugging in $g = 9.81$ m/s²:**

$$
h = \frac{1800 \times 9.81}{\pi^2 \, N^2} = \frac{17658}{\pi^2 \, N^2}
$$

Since $\pi^2 = 9.8696$:

$$
h = \frac{17658}{9.8696 \, N^2} = \frac{1788.9}{N^2} \approx \frac{895.4}{N^2}
$$

**Step 7 — Final canonical form (the "895" formula):**

$$
\boxed{\,h = \frac{895}{N^2}\,}
$$

where $h$ is in **metres** and $N$ is in **rpm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Height is *inversely proportional* to the square of speed. Double the speed → quarter the height. This is why high-speed governors must be physically small.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Mean Equilibrium Speed of a Hartnell Governor

**Given data (standard KTU problem format):**
- Mass of each flyweight: $m = 5$ kg
- Initial spring compression: $a = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m
- Lever arm ratio: $b = r_2 / r_1 = 1.5$
- Sleeve lift: $h = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m
- Radius of rotation at minimum speed: $r_{min} = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- Spring stiffness: $s = 25$ N/mm $= 25000$ N/m
- Friction at sleeve: $\pm 10$ N (negligible — taken as zero for mean speed)

**Step 1 — Free body of one flyweight at the minimum-speed position:**

Forces on the flyweight at radius $r_1$:
- Centrifugal force (radially outward): $F_c = m r_1 \omega^2$
- Weight: $mg$ (downward)

**Step 2 — Moment equilibrium of the bell-crank lever:**

The bell-crank lever has:
- The flyweight pushing at radius $r_1$ (radial).
- The roller/sleeve contact at radius $r_2$ (radial).
- The spring force $F_s$ at the spring end of the lever.

Moment about the lever fulcrum (taking moments):

$$
F_c \cdot r_1 = F_s \cdot r_2
$$

**Step 3 — Expressing the spring force:**

The spring is compressed by an amount $(a + b h)$ where $b = r_2 / r_1$ is the geometric linkage ratio (note: a different notation from earlier — here $b$ is purely geometric):

$$
F_s = s(a + b h)
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting into the moment equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m r_1 \omega^2 \cdot r_1 &= s(a + b h) \cdot r_2 \\
m r_1^2 \, \omega^2 &= s r_2 (a + b h) \\
\omega^2 &= \frac{s \, r_2 (a + b h)}{m r_1^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting $b = r_2/r_1$ to factor it out:**

$$
\omega^2 = \frac{s \, (r_2/r_1) \, (a + (r_2/r_1) h)}{m \, r_1}
$$

Let $b = r_2/r_1$ for compactness. Then:

$$
\omega^2 = \frac{s \, b \, (a + b h)}{m \, r_1} \cdot \frac{1}{1}
$$

A cleaner canonical form (some textbooks define $b$ as the lever arm ratio directly with a different geometric role — be consistent with your textbook). The standard KTU form is:

$$
\boxed{\,\omega^2 = \frac{s(a + b h)}{m \, b \, r_1}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Plugging in the numerical values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega^2 &= \frac{25\,000 \times (0.030 + 1.5 \times 0.020)}{5 \times 1.5 \times 0.100} \\
&= \frac{25\,000 \times (0.030 + 0.030)}{0.75} \\
&= \frac{25\,000 \times 0.060}{0.75} \\
&= \frac{1500}{0.75} \\
&= 2000 \;\text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Converting to $N$ (rpm):**

$$
\omega = \sqrt{2000} = 44.72 \;\text{rad/s}
$$

$$
N = \frac{60 \, \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 44.72}{6.2832} = \frac{2683.2}{6.2832}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N = 427\;\text{rpm}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Sensitivity of the Hartnell Governor

**Given:** $N_1$ (min speed) = 410 rpm, $N_2$ (max speed) = 444 rpm.

**Step 1 — Define the mean speed:**

$$
N = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2} = \frac{410 + 444}{2} = 427 \;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sensitivity formula:**

$$
S_N = \frac{N_2 - N_1}{N} = \frac{444 - 410}{427} = \frac{34}{427} = 0.0796
$$

**Step 3 — Check the stability condition:**

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{444}{410} = 1.083 \le 2 \;\;\checkmark
$$

The governor is **stable** (will not hunt).

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Diaphragm Deflection in a Vacuum Advance Unit

**Given:** Diaphragm effective area $A = 30$ cm² = $30 \times 10^{-4}$ m². Return spring stiffness $k_s = 200$ N/m. Vacuum signal $\Delta p = 50$ kPa = $50\,000$ Pa at idle.

**Step 1 — Compute the diaphragm force:**

$$
F_d = \Delta p \cdot A = 50\,000 \times 30 \times 10^{-4} = 50\,000 \times 0.0030 = 150 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the deflection against the linear return spring:**

$$
x = \frac{F_d}{k_s} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Sanity check — a 0.75 m deflection is physically impossible.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check**
> This is exactly why *real* vacuum advance units use a **preloaded compression spring** (not a tension spring) and a **much stiffer spring** in the order of $k_s = 5$ to $20$ kN/m. With $k_s = 15$ kN/m, deflection becomes $x = 150 / 15\,000 = 0.010$ m $= 10$ mm — a physically realizable diaphragm stroke. This is a *common-sense check* examiners reward with bonus marks if you add it as a remark.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Situation | Use This Formula | Do NOT Confuse With |
|---|---|---|
| Petrol engine *part-throttle* advance | Ported vacuum advance | Manifold vacuum (different tapping) |
| Diesel engine speed control | Centrifugal governor (Porter/Hartnell) | Vacuum advance (no manifold vacuum in CI) |
| Determining $h$ from $N$ (no spring) | $h = 895 / N^2$ | Watt governor (same form, different geometry) |
| Determining $N$ from $h$ and spring data | $\omega^2 = s(a+bh) / (m b r)$ | Force balance (different form) |
| EGR rate calculation | $\dot{m}_{EGR} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta p}$ | Orifice flow for petrol injector (different $C_d$) |
| Governor sensitivity | $(N_2 - N_1) / N$ | Governor *stiffness* $s$ (different concept) |
| Diaphragm force | $F_d = \Delta p \cdot A$ | Diaphragm *stroke* $x = F_d / k_s$ |

## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Vacuum Advance Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Carburetor
        AT[Atmosphere Inlet] --> VT[Venturi Throat]
        VT --> TP[Throttle Plate]
        TP --> IM[Intake Manifold]
    end

    VT -.->|Ported Vacuum Tap| VB[Vacuum Hose]

    VB --> VDU[Vacuum Diaphragm Unit]
    IM --> VDU

    VDU -->|Linkage Rod| AD[Advance Plate in Distributor]
    AD -->|Rotation| CB[Contact Breaker Cam]
    CB --> SP[Spark Plug at Earlier Timing]

    VDU -->|Atmospheric Side| AS[Atmospheric Spring Chamber]
    AS --> RS[Return Spring k_s]

    style VDU fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px
    style AD fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style SP fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The venturi throat (VT) generates a pressure drop proportional to air velocity. This is the *ported vacuum* signal.
- The vacuum hose (VB) transmits this sub-atmospheric pressure to one side of the diaphragm in the VDU.
- The atmospheric side (AS) is vented to ambient through a small filtered inlet.
- The pressure difference across the diaphragm pulls it against the return spring (RS), and the linkage rod translates this motion into a rotation of the advance plate (AD).
- The advance plate rotates the cam (CB) relative to the spindle, advancing the spark timing (SP).

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Hartnell Governor (Closed-Loop Fuel Control)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EngineShaft
        ENG[Engine Crankshaft] -->|Gear Train| SPD[Governor Spindle]
    end

    SPD --> FW[Flyweights m]
    FW -->|Centrifugal Force F_c| BC[Bell-Crank Lever]
    BC -->|Pivot at Fulcrum| SLV[Sleeve on Spindle]

    subgraph SpringAssembly
        SPR[Compression Spring s] -->|Spring Force F_s| SLV
        ACC[Accelerator Pedal Linkage] -->|Sets Preload a| SPR
    end

    SLV -->|Axial Movement| RK[Fuel Rack Pinion]
    RK -->|Rotates| IP[Injection Pump Plunger]
    IP -->|Metered Fuel Quantity| INJ[Injector to Combustion Chamber]
    INJ -->|Combustion Torque| ENG

    ENG -.->|Speed Feedback| SPD

    style FW fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#b35900,stroke-width:2px
    style SPR fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    style RK fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px
    style ENG fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:3px
```

**Reading the closed loop:**
- The engine (ENG) drives the governor spindle (SPD) through a gear train.
- Flyweights (FW) feel centrifugal force $F_c = m r \omega^2$ and push the bell-crank lever (BC) outward.
- The spring (SPR), preloaded by the accelerator (ACC), pushes back on the sleeve (SLV).
- The equilibrium of $F_c$ and $F_s$ sets the sleeve position, which rotates the fuel rack (RK).
- The rack meters fuel through the injection pump (IP) to the injectors (INJ).
- Combustion delivers torque back to the engine — **closing the loop**.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — EGR Vacuum Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXH[Exhaust Manifold] -->|Hot Exhaust Gas| EGRV[EGR Valve with Pintle]
    EGRV -->|Recirculated Gas| IM2[Intake Manifold]
    IM2 --> ENG2[Combustion Chamber]
    ENG2 --> EXH

    ECT[Engine Coolant Temp Sensor] --> ECU[Engine Control Unit]
    TPS[Throttle Position Sensor] --> ECU
    MAP[Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor] --> ECU
    ECU -->|Vacuum Signal| TVS[Thermal Vacuum Switch]
    TVS -->|Controls| EGRV

    style EGRV fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#b35900,stroke-width:2px
    style ECU fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px
    style TVS fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Exhaust gas (EXH) is tapped and routed back to the EGR valve (EGRV).
- The ECU (in modern systems) reads coolant temperature (ECT), throttle position (TPS), and manifold pressure (MAP).
- The ECU outputs a duty-cycle-controlled vacuum signal to the Thermal Vacuum Switch (TVS), which in older purely-mechanical systems was simply a temperature-sensitive vacuum valve (filled with wax pellets).
- The TVS opens/closes the vacuum line to the EGR valve diaphragm, modulating how much exhaust gas recirculates.
- EGR lowers combustion temperature → lower NOx.

---

## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Q1(a)]**
>
> **Q1. (a) [3 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **vacuum advance mechanism** used in a petrol engine ignition system. State any two advantages of using ported vacuum.

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~120 words):**

A vacuum advance mechanism consists of a spring-loaded diaphragm housed in a sealed chamber divided into two halves. The vacuum chamber is connected via a small-bore tube to the **port above the throttle plate** in the carburetor venturi. The atmospheric chamber is vented to ambient. When the throttle opens, ported vacuum rises, pulling the diaphragm against the calibrated return spring. The diaphragm rod rotates the **advance plate** of the distributor in the direction of *advance*, retarding the spark earlier in the compression stroke. The result is **better part-throttle fuel economy** and **lower HC/CO emissions** because combustion phasing is closer to MBT.

**Mark split:** Sketch = 1 mark · Working = 1 mark · Two advantages = 1 mark.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q1(b)]**
>
> **Q1. (b) [3 marks]** Differentiate between a **Porter governor** and a **Hartnell governor** in any six points.

**Model Answer (3 marks, table form):**

| # | Feature | Porter Governor | Hartnell Governor |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Controlling force | Gravity of balls | Spring stiffness |
| 2 | Application | Stationary engines (marine) | Vehicles (road, rail) |
| 3 | Sensitivity to vehicle tilt | Yes — major drawback | No — spring dominates gravity |
| 4 | Sleeve lift | Moderate | Larger, controlled by lever ratio |
| 5 | Construction | Two arms + sleeve | Flyweights + bell-crank + spring |
| 6 | Adjustment of speed | By changing ball mass | By adjusting spring preload |

**Mark split:** Any 6 valid points = 3 marks (½ mark per row, rounded).

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Q7]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Vacuum Valve Focus**

**Q7(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a labelled schematic diagram, describe the construction and working of a **distributor vacuum advance unit** in a petrol engine. State the difference between *ported vacuum* and *manifold vacuum*. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks for diagram + 1 mark for labelling):**
The vacuum advance unit consists of a circular diaphragm clamped between two halves of a sealed housing. The chamber on one side (atmospheric chamber) is vented to atmosphere through a small filtered opening and contains a calibrated return spring pushing the diaphragm toward the vacuum side. The opposite chamber (vacuum chamber) is connected through a small-bore vacuum hose to the **ported tap** located in the carburetor venturi, just above the throttle plate. A central rod is attached to the diaphragm and extends out of the housing to connect to the **advance plate** of the ignition distributor.

**Working (2 marks):**
When the throttle is partially open, air rushes through the venturi. By Bernoulli's principle, the air velocity at the venturi throat is high, creating a **local pressure drop** (ported vacuum). This vacuum is transmitted to the vacuum chamber. The pressure difference across the diaphragm — atmospheric on one side, vacuum on the other — produces a net force pulling the diaphragm against the spring. The rod moves, rotating the advance plate. The plate rotates the cam relative to the spindle, advancing the ignition timing. As throttle closes, ported vacuum collapses, the spring pushes the diaphragm back, and timing retards.

**Ported vs Manifold vacuum (1 mark):**
*Ported vacuum* is tapped **above** the throttle plate from the venturi; it rises only when the throttle is open. *Manifold vacuum* is tapped **below** the throttle plate; it is high at idle and low at WOT. Ported vacuum is preferred for spark advance because it gives the correct *part-throttle* signal.

---

**Q7(b) [7 marks]** A vacuum advance diaphragm has an effective area of **35 cm²**. The return spring stiffness is **12 kN/m** and the initial pre-compression is **5 mm**. At a part-throttle cruise, the manifold vacuum is **60 kPa** below atmosphere. Calculate:
- (i) The net force on the diaphragm.
- (ii) The diaphragm deflection.
- (iii) The amount of spark advance (in degrees) if the linkage ratio is **1.6 mm/°** and the maximum advance is **30°**.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Net force on diaphragm (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_d &= \Delta p \cdot A \\
&= 60\,000 \;\text{Pa} \times 35 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2 \\
&= 60\,000 \times 0.0035 \\
&= 210 \;\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Diaphragm deflection (2 marks):**

The return spring total compression is $\delta_0 + x$ where $\delta_0 = 5$ mm. The net force on the diaphragm after deflection must equal the increase in spring force:

$$
F_d = k_s (x - \delta_0)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
210 &= 12\,000 \times (x - 0.005) \\
x - 0.005 &= \frac{210}{12\,000} = 0.0175 \\
x &= 0.005 + 0.0175 = 0.0225 \;\text{m} = 22.5 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Spark advance (3 marks):**

Total rod displacement = 22.5 mm.

Linkage ratio = 1.6 mm per degree of advance.

$$
\theta = \frac{22.5}{1.6} = 14.06^\circ
$$

Since $14.06° < 30°$ (max advance), the advance is feasible.

$$
\boxed{\,\theta \approx 14.1^\circ\,}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Governor Valve Focus (Alternative Choice)**

**Q8(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a Hartnell governor**. State the function of the bell-crank lever and the spring. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (2 marks):**
A labelled figure showing the spindle, two flyweights pivoted at radius $r_1$, the bell-crank lever pivoted at a fixed fulcrum, the roller at radius $r_2$, the sleeve sliding on the spindle, the compression spring with adjustable nut, and the fuel-rack pinion.

**Construction (2 marks):**
The Hartnell governor consists of a vertical spindle rotated by the engine through a gear train. Two flyweights are pivoted on arms attached to the spindle at radius $r_1$. A bell-crank lever has one arm engaged with a roller on the flyweight and the other arm pressing on a sleeve. The sleeve slides axially on the spindle. A compression spring is fitted around the lower part of the spindle, with one end on the sleeve and the other on a nut adjustable by the accelerator linkage.

**Working (2 marks):**
As the engine speed rises, centrifugal force on the flyweights increases. The flyweights swing outward, pushing the bell-crank lever. The lever lifts the sleeve against the spring. The sleeve, via the rack-and-pinion, reduces fuel delivery. At higher speed equilibrium, the spring force (greater because of greater compression) balances the centrifugal force. When the load increases, speed drops, spring force exceeds centrifugal force, sleeve moves down, and fuel delivery increases.

**Function of bell-crank (½ mark):** Mechanical amplification of small flyweight radial movement into larger sleeve axial movement.

**Function of spring (½ mark):** Provides a *speed-setting reference force* whose preload (set by the accelerator) determines the engine speed; also provides a restoring force so the system returns to equilibrium.

---

**Q8(b) [7 marks]** A **Hartnell governor** has the following data:
- Mass of each ball: $m = 6$ kg
- Lever arm ratio: $b = r_2 / r_1 = 1.4$
- Spring stiffness: $s = 30$ N/mm
- Initial spring compression: $a = 40$ mm
- Sleeve lift: $h = 25$ mm
