So the final KTU-grade equation is:

$$\boxed{V = \dfrac{0.377 \cdot N_e \cdot r}{i_{gear} \cdot i_{final}}}$$

*Explanation*: $0.377$ is a unit-conversion constant that emerges naturally from rpm → m/min → km/h, combined with the rolling circumference. This is the **standard equation** KTU examiners expect students to memorize for any module covering vehicle propulsion, gear ratios, or DCT / manual / automatic transmission design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Clutch Torque Capacity (Uniform-Wear Assumption)

The **uniform-wear assumption** is more realistic than uniform-pressure for a clutch operating in a DCT, because the friction lining wears faster where pressure is highest, leading to a pressure distribution that decreases with radius.

### Step 1 — Define pressure distribution

Under uniform wear, the product $p(r) \cdot r = \text{constant} = p_{max} \cdot R_i$, so:

$$p(r) = \dfrac{p_{max} \cdot R_i}{r}$$

*Explanation*: At the inner radius $R_i$, the pressure is maximum $p_{max}$. As radius increases, pressure falls hyperbolically such that the linear wear rate $\dot{w} \propto p \cdot v \propto p \cdot r$ is constant (Archard's wear law).

### Step 2 — Differential normal force on a ring element

Consider a thin annular ring of radius $r$, width $dr$, on one friction face. The normal force on it is:

$$dF_N = p(r) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr = \dfrac{p_{max} \cdot R_i}{r} \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr = 2 \pi \, p_{max} \, R_i \, dr$$

*Explanation*: $2 \pi r dr$ is the area of the annulus. The $r$ in the denominator cancels with the $r$ in the area, leaving a constant in $r$ — this is the **defining simplification of the uniform-wear assumption**.

### Step 3 — Integrate over the friction surface

Total normal force on one friction face:

$$F_N = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} 2 \pi \, p_{max} \, R_i \, dr = 2 \pi \, p_{max} \, R_i \, (R_o - R_i)$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by friction coefficient and mean radius to get torque

The frictional force on each annular element is $\mu \cdot dF_N$, acting at radius $r$. The torque contribution is $\mu \cdot dF_N \cdot r$:

$$dT = \mu \cdot dF_N \cdot r = \mu \cdot 2 \pi \, p_{max} \, R_i \, dr \cdot r$$

Wait — recall that under uniform wear, $dF_N$ was already simplified (the $r$ cancelled). We must integrate properly:

$$T_{\text{one face}} = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \mu \cdot p(r) \cdot 2 \pi r^2 \, dr = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \mu \cdot \dfrac{p_{max} R_i}{r} \cdot 2 \pi r^2 \, dr = 2 \pi \mu \, p_{max} \, R_i \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r \, dr$$

$$T_{\text{one face}} = 2 \pi \mu \, p_{max} \, R_i \cdot \left[\dfrac{r^2}{2}\right]_{R_i}^{R_o} = \pi \mu \, p_{max} \, R_i \, (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$$

### Step 5 — Express in mean-radius form for engineering compactness

Using the identity $R_o^2 - R_i^2 = (R_o - R_i)(R_o + R_i) = (R_o - R_i) \cdot 2 R_m$ where $R_m = \dfrac{R_o + R_i}{2}$:

$$T_{\text{one face}} = \pi \mu \, p_{max} \, R_i \cdot 2 R_m \cdot (R_o - R_i) = 2 \pi \mu \, p_{max} \, R_m \, R_i \, (R_o - R_i)$$

For **$n$ friction surfaces**:

$$\boxed{T_c = 2 \pi \mu \, p_{max} \, R_m \, R_i \, (R_o - R_i) \cdot n}$$

A more commonly cited **equivalent** form (derived by substituting $R_i = 2 R_m - R_o$, useful when only $R_m$ and the radial width are given) is:

$$T_c = \mu \cdot p_{max} \cdot \pi \cdot R_m^2 \cdot (R_o - R_i) \cdot n \quad \text{(approximate, used in many texts)}$$

*Explanation*: This is the form in the KTU Formula Sheet (Formula 4). The strict derivation above (Formula 4 with $R_i$ retained) is more accurate.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (typical 7-mark sub-part)**: A 7-speed DSG in a 1.4 L turbocharged petrol car has the following data in 3rd gear: $N_e = 3000$ rpm, $i_{3rd} = 1.46$, $i_{final} = 4.06$, tyre size 205/55 R16, rolling radius $r = 0.316$ m. Calculate (a) the vehicle speed and (b) the engine torque at the wheels if engine torque is 220 Nm and DCT efficiency is 94 %.

**Part (a) — Vehicle speed**

Apply the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$V = \dfrac{0.377 \cdot N_e \cdot r}{i_{gear} \cdot i_{final}}$$

Substitute the given values:

$$V = \dfrac{0.377 \cdot 3000 \cdot 0.316}{1.46 \cdot 4.06}$$

Compute numerator:

$$0.377 \cdot 3000 = 1131; \quad 1131 \cdot 0.316 = 357.396$$

Compute denominator:

$$1.46 \cdot 4.06 = 5.9276$$

Compute the quotient:

$$V = \dfrac{357.396}{5.9276} \approx 60.29 \;\text{km/h}$$

**Part (a) answer**: $V \approx 60.3$ km/h.

**Part (b) — Torque at the wheels**

The total gear ratio in 3rd gear, including final drive:

$$i_{total} = i_{3rd} \cdot i_{final} = 1.46 \cdot 4.06 = 5.9276$$

Wheel torque before losses:

$$T_{wheel,ideal} = T_e \cdot i_{total} = 220 \cdot 5.9276 = 1304.07 \;\text{Nm}$$

With DCT mechanical efficiency $\eta_{DCT} = 0.94$:

$$T_{wheel,actual} = T_{wheel,ideal} \cdot \eta_{DCT} = 1304.07 \cdot 0.94 = 1225.83 \;\text{Nm}$$

**Part (b) answer**: $T_{wheel} \approx 1226$ Nm.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DCT vs Competing Transmission Architectures

| **Parameter** | **Manual MT** | **Single-Clutch AMT** | **Conventional AT (Torque Converter)** | **CVT** | **DCT / DSG** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Driver effort** | High (clutch + shift) | Low | Low | Low | Low |
| **Shift speed** | Slowest (driver) | Slow (500–800 ms) | Moderate (300–500 ms) | Seamless (no discrete shifts) | **Fastest (50–400 ms)** |
| **Torque interruption** | Yes (noticeable) | Yes (noticeable) | **No** (torque converter slip) | No (no shifts) | **Near-zero (engine load-shed)** |
| **Mechanical efficiency** | 95–97 % | 94–96 % | 85–92 % | 85–90 % | **92–96 %** |
| **Torque capacity (typical)** | Up to 1000+ Nm | Up to 350 Nm | Up to 1000+ Nm | Limited (~300 Nm typical) | Up to 1000 Nm (wet) |
| **Complexity** | Low | Low–Medium | High (planetary + TC) | Medium | **High (mechatronic)** |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Low | High | Medium | **Medium–High** |
| **Creep behaviour** | Poor (driver skill) | Poor (judder) | **Excellent (TC stall)** | Acceptable | **Good (controlled slip)** |
| **Hill-hold ease** | Manual handbrake | Tricky | Excellent | Good | **Good (auto-hold)** |
| **Fuel economy** | **Best (skilled driver)** | Good | Worst (slip loss) | Good (synthetic gearing) | **Best (automated)** |
| **Sporty shift feel** | Depends on driver | Jerky | Slushy | Rubbery | **Excellent** |
| **Reliability concerns** | Low | Clutch wear | TC wear, ATF degradation | Belt/chain wear | Mechatronic, dry-clutch wear |
| **Representative users** | Enthusiasts | Entry-level hatchbacks (Maruti, Tata) | Lexus, classic Mercedes, Ford | Nissan, Honda (older) | VW, Audi, Porsche, Ferrari, Ford, Hyundai/Kia |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**: A common Part A question (3 marks) is: *"Compare DCT and CVT with respect to efficiency, shift feel, and torque capacity."* Use the table above as the source for your model answer — it captures **all 3** of those axes in one line per row.

## 3.5 Energy Balance During a Single DCT Upshift — Step-by-Step

This derivation is the foundation of **shift-quality engineering** and is a frequent KTU 14-mark Part B question.

**Setup**: Engine is at $N_{e,pre}$ rpm, output shaft at $N_{out}$ rpm. The next gear has ratio $i_{next}$ which will require engine speed to drop to $N_{e,post} = N_{out} \cdot i_{next}$.

### Step 1 — Compute pre- and post-shift engine speeds

$$N_{e,pre} = N_{out} \cdot i_{current}$$

$$N_{e,post} = N_{out} \cdot i_{next}$$

The mismatch that must be absorbed is:

$$\Delta N_e = N_{e,pre} - N_{e,post} = N_{out} \cdot (i_{current} - i_{next})$$

*Explanation*: Because the current gear has a higher ratio (lower numerical value in most cases, but actually lower ratio for upshift — wait, careful — for an upshift to a higher-numbered gear, the ratio is numerically lower, e.g., 1.46 in 3rd drops to 1.20 in 4th, so the engine must slow down, hence $\Delta N_e > 0$).

### Step 2 — Kinetic energy to be dissipated in the synchronizer / clutch slip

The rotating masses with effective inertia $I_e$ (engine + clutch K1 inertia, referred to engine side) carry the kinetic energy:

$$E_{kin,pre} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_e \cdot \omega_{e,pre}^2, \quad \text{where} \; \omega_{e,pre} = \dfrac{2 \pi N_{e,pre}}{60}$$

After the shift:

$$E_{kin,post} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_e \cdot \omega_{e,post}^2$$

The **energy dissipated** during the shift (assuming output speed stays constant during the brief shift event):

$$\boxed{E_{diss} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_e \cdot \left(\omega_{e,pre}^2 - \omega_{e,post}^2\right) = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_e \cdot \left(\omega_{e,pre} - \omega_{e,post}\right)\left(\omega_{e,pre} + \omega_{e,post}\right)}$$

### Step 3 — Power dissipation rate and thermal check

If the shift occurs in time $t_{shift}$, average power dissipated is:

$$P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_{diss}}{t_{shift}}$$

*Explanation*: A typical dry DCT in a 200 Nm application dissipates ~ 1.5–2.5 kJ per shift. If $t_{shift} = 0.2$ s, then $P_{avg} \approx 7.5–12.5$ kW. A wet DCT's ATF cooling can handle this; a dry DCT cannot dissipate such heat continuously — this is why dry DCTs are limited to **moderate-torque, urban-duty** applications.

### Step 4 — Why "engine load-shed" matters

If the engine delivers torque $T_e$ during the shift, the clutch must absorb **mechanical power** in addition to the kinetic energy. To minimize this, the TCU commands a **fuel-cut / torque-reduction request** to the engine ECU over the CAN bus, momentarily dropping $T_e$ by 60–80 % during the shift, so that the **clutch slip work is minimized**. This is the engineering reason DCTs feel "sporty" — the engine momentarily idles into the next gear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trivia Point**: In Volkswagen's DSG, the **engine torque request reduction** is called the *E-Gas intervention* or *MSR/Momentenreduzierung*. Students who mention this German-specific term in a 14-mark answer often earn an **extra insight mark** from examiners.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied**:
> - All node IDs are **alphanumeric** (no reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph` used as IDs).

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Power Flow Architecture of a 7-Speed DSG / DCT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[ENGINE AND DUAL MASS FLYWHEEL] --> CLUTCH_HOUSING[DUAL CLUTCH PACK HOUSING]
    CLUTCH_HOUSING --> K1[CLUTCH K1 ODD]
    CLUTCH_HOUSING --> K2[CLUTCH K2 EVEN]
    K1 --> SHAFT_OUTER[HOLLOW OUTER INPUT SHAFT]
    K2 --> SHAFT_INNER[SOLID INNER INPUT SHAFT]
    SHAFT_OUTER --> LAYSHAFT_1[ODD LAYSHAFT GEARS 1 3 5 R]
    SHAFT_INNER --> LAYSHAFT_2[EVEN LAYSHAFT GEARS 2 4 6]
    LAYSHEF1[LAYSHAFT 1]:::hidden --> SHIFT_FORKS_1[SHIFT FORKS AND SYNCHROMESH ASSEMBLY ODD]
    LAYSHAFT_2 --> SHIFT_FORKS_2[SHIFT FORKS AND SYNCHROMESH ASSEMBLY EVEN]
    SHIFT_FORKS_1 --> OUTPUT[COMMON OUTPUT SHAFT]
    SHIFT_FORKS_2 --> OUTPUT
    OUTPUT --> FINAL_DRIVE[FINAL DRIVE DIFFERENTIAL]
    FINAL_DRIVE --> WHEELS[DRIVEN WHEELS]

    classDef hidden display:none;
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- The **engine + dual-mass flywheel** is the single driving source.
- The **dual-clutch pack** routes torque to **two independent input shafts** (K1 → odd, K2 → even).
- The two layshafts each carry their assigned gear pairs and a set of **synchronizer sleeves** operated by **shift forks**.
- The **common output shaft** collects torque from whichever gear is currently engaged and feeds the final drive.
- Notice that **only one clutch + one layshaft + one gear** is active at any instant — the other is *preloaded and synchronized* but torque-free.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Sequential Control State Machine for an Upshift 1→2

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STATE_1ST_ENGAGED
    STATE_1ST_ENGAGED : STATE A K1 CLOSED 1ST GEAR ACTIVE K2 OPEN
    STATE_1ST_ENGAGED --> STATE_2ND_PRESELECTED : TCU MOVES 2ND GEAR SYNCHRO TO PRE ENGAGED POSITION
    STATE_2ND_PRESELECTED : STATE B K1 STILL CLOSED 1ST GEAR DRIVING 2ND GEAR SYNCHRONIZED
    STATE_2ND_PRESELECTED --> STATE_TORQUE_REDUCED : ENGINE ECU FUEL CUT TORQUE REQUEST 80 PERCENT
    STATE_TORQUE_REDUCED : STATE C ENGINE LOAD SHED TO MINIMIZE SLIP WORK
    STATE_TORQUE_REDUCED --> STATE_CLUTCH_SWAP : K1 OPENS AND K2 CLOSES OVERLAP CONTROL
    STATE_CLUTCH_SWAP : STATE D CLUTCH TO CLUTCH TORQUE TRANSFER IN 200 TO 250 MS
    STATE_CLUTCH_SWAP --> STATE_2ND_ENGAGED : K1 FULLY OPEN K2 FULLY CLOSED
    STATE_2ND_ENGAGED : STATE E K2 CLOSED 2ND GEAR DRIVING 3RD GEAR PRESELECTED ON K1
    STATE_2ND_ENGAGED --> [*]
```

**Reading the state diagram**:
- Each **rectangular state** represents a distinct physical configuration of clutches, gears, and engine torque.
- The **arrows** are triggered by TCU commands (solenoid actuations, ECU fuel-cut requests).
- The transition `STATE_CLUTCH_SWAP` is the **only** step where torque interruption can be perceived — and modern DSG calibrations compress this to < 30 ms of perceptible slack.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mechatronic Control System)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_SENSORS [INPUT SENSOR LAYER]
        S1[APPS ACCELERATOR PEDAL]
        S2[ENGINE RPM SENSOR]
        S3[VEHICLE SPEED SENSOR]
        S4[CLUTCH POSITION SENSORS K1 AND K2]
        S5[GEAR SELECTOR POSITION]
        S6[ATF TEMPERATURE SENSOR]
        S7[BRAKE PEDAL SWITCH]
    end

    subgraph TCU_CORE [TCU TRANSMISSION CONTROL UNIT]
        PROC[SHIFT LOGIC AND ADAPTIVE MAPS]
        CLUTCH_CTRL[CLUTCH PRESSURE CONTROLLER PID]
        SHIFT_CTRL[SHIFT FORK ACTUATION CONTROLLER]
        DIAG[ON BOARD DIAGNOSTICS OBD]
        BUS[CAN AND FLEXRAY BUS INTERFACE]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_ACTUATORS [OUTPUT ACTUATOR LAYER]
        A1[PROPORTIONAL PRESSURE SOLENOIDS FOR K1 AND K2]
        A2[SHIFT FORK HYDRAULIC ACTUATORS]
        A3[PARKING LOCK ACTUATOR]
        A4[ATF PUMP MOTOR]
    end

    subgraph ENGINE_LINK [ENGINE ECU LINKAGE]
        ENG_ECU[ENGINE ECU]
        THROTTLE[ELECTRONIC THROTTLE BODY]
    end

    S1 --> PROC
    S2 --> PROC
    S3 --> PROC
    S4 --> CLUTCH_CTRL
    S5 --> PROC
    S6 --> A4
    S7 --> PROC
    PROC --> CLUTCH_CTRL
    PROC --> SHIFT_CTRL
    PROC --> BUS
    BUS --> ENG_ECU
    ENG_ECU --> THROTTLE
    CLUTCH_CTRL --> A1
    SHIFT_CTRL --> A2
    PROC --> A3
    PROC --> DIAG
```

**Reading the architecture diagram**:
- The system is layered into **sensors → TCU → actuators**, with a **feedback loop** through the engine ECU for torque coordination.
- The **PID clutch pressure controller** is the heart of shift quality — it modulates K1 and K2 pressure profiles in real time to achieve torque transfer without shudder.
- The **CAN/FlexRay bus** is what makes the engine "cooperate" with the gearbox during shifts.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Failure Mode and Limp-Home Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NORMAL[NORMAL OPERATION FULL DSG FUNCTIONALITY] --> SENSOR_FAULT{SENSOR FAULT DETECTED}
    SENSOR_FAULT -- CLUTCH POS SENSOR OUT OF RANGE --> LIMP1[ENTER LIMP HOME LIMIT TO 3RD GEAR ONLY KEEP CLUTCH K1 ACTIVE]
    SENSOR_FAULT -- ATF OVER TEMP --> LIMP2[REDUCE LINE PRESSURE LOCK OUT HIGH GEARS ENABLE COOLING PUMP MAX]
    SENSOR_FAULT -- SHIFT FORK STUCK --> LIMP3[DISABLE THAT GEAR LOG FAULTY GEAR IN TCU CONTINUE WITH REMAINING GEARS]
    SENSOR_FAULT -- PRESSURE SOLENOID FAILURE --> LIMP4[LATCH K1 OR K2 OPEN VEHICLE MUST BE TOWED OR NEUTRAL]
    LIMP1 --> RECOVERY[AUTO RECOVERY ON SENSOR RECONNECT AFTER KEY CYCLE]
    LIMP2 --> RECOVERY
    LIMP3 --> RECOVERY
    LIMP4 --> TOW[TOW REQUIRED NO MOBILITY]
```

**Reading the failure-mode diagram**:
- A DCT is **highly sensor-dependent**; loss of clutch position feedback is the most critical failure.
- The limp-home strategy in most production DSGs is to **freeze the gearbox in 2nd or 3rd gear** to allow slow driving to a workshop (Volkswagen calls this *Notlauf*).
- A **complete pressure loss** disables the transmission entirely — the mechatronic unit cannot even actuate the parking lock reliably, so the vehicle must be towed.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme)**:
> - **Part A**: 3-mark short-answer questions (typically 4–5 per paper, 2-module choice).
> - **Part B**: 14-mark long-answer questions with **internal choice** (typically 2 questions per module, choose 1).
> - Each 14-mark question has **2 sub-parts of 7 marks each**, often with internal sub-division into (i) and (ii).
> - Bloom's levels: Part A targets *Remember / Understand*; Part B targets *Apply / Analyze / Evaluate*.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (with Model Answers)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"Differentiate between a single-clutch AMT and a dual-clutch transmission (DCT) with respect to shift mechanism, shift time, and torque interruption." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer**:
A single-clutch Automated Manual Transmission (AMT) uses **one friction clutch** and **one layshaft** to sequentially disengage the current gear, synchronize the next gear, and re-engage the clutch, resulting in a relatively **slow shift time of 500–800 ms** and a **noticeable torque interruption** at the driveline. A Dual-Clutch Transmission (DCT) uses **two clutches and two layshafts** to **preselect the next gear** while the current gear is still engaged, enabling **clutch-to-clutch shifting in 200–400 ms** (or 50–120 ms in performance DCTs) with **near-zero torque interruption** because the engine load is simultaneously reduced during the brief overlap. (3 marks: 1 mark for clutch configuration, 1 for shift time, 1 for torque interruption.)

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**"What is a mechatronic module in a DSG transmission? List its main inputs and outputs." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer**:
The mechatronic module is the **electro-hydraulic control unit** of a DSG/DCT, integrating a **Transmission Control Unit (TCU)**, **hydraulic block**, and **solenoid valves** within a single housing attached to the gearbox.
- **Main inputs (sensors)**: accelerator pedal position (APPS), engine and vehicle speed, clutch position sensors (K1, K2), shift fork position sensors, gear selector position, ATF temperature, brake pedal switch, steering angle, and **CAN-bus** data from the engine ECU.
- **Main outputs (actuators)**: proportional pressure control solenoids for clutches K1 and K2, shift-fork hydraulic actuators, parking lock actuator, and the ATF pump motor. (1 mark for definition, 1 for inputs, 1 for outputs.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!TIP]
> **Internal choice format**: KTU typically presents 2 alternative 14-mark questions for the same module; the student answers ONE. Below are TWO fully-independent alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) covering different sub-topics of the DCT module, so you can practice both.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks): Detailed DCT Construction & Operation

**[KTU University Exam Model – May 2024 Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a 7-speed dual-clutch transmission (DSG)**. Explain the role of the **dual-clutch pack**, the **concentric input shafts**, and the **mechatronic control unit**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **sequential power flow** during an upshift from 3rd to 4th gear, clearly identifying the state of clutches K1 and K2, the active layshaft, and the role of **engine torque reduction** during the shift. Explain why a DCT achieves a **faster shift** than a single-clutch AMT. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Construction overview (3 marks)**

A 7-speed DSG consists of:
- **Dual-clutch assembly**: two friction clutches (K1 and K2) sharing a common driving plate connected to the engine flywheel (DMF).
  - In a **dry DCT** (e.g., VW DQ200): two single-plate dry clutches; K1 controls odd gears 1, 3, 5, 7, R; K2 controls even gears 2, 4, 6.
  - In a **wet DCT** (e.g., VW DQ500, Porsche PDK): two multi-plate wet clutches immersed in ATF; higher torque capacity (≥ 600 Nm).
- **Two concentric input shafts** (1 mark):
  - **Outer/hollow shaft** → driven by K1, carries odd-gear pinions to the odd layshaft.
  - **Inner/solid shaft** passing through the hollow shaft → driven by K2, carries even-gear pinions to the even layshaft.
  - *Why concentric?* → To keep the gearbox axial length comparable to a manual unit, minimize packaging volume, and share a common clutch housing.
- **Two layshafts with gear pairs and synchronizer sleeves** (1 mark):
  - Each layshaft has fixed pinions for its assigned gears.
  - Each gear pair is paired with a **synchronizer sleeve + dog clutch** operated by a shift fork.
- **Mechatronic control unit** (1 mark):
  - Electro-hydraulic valve block + TCU.
  - Receives inputs from APPS, engine speed, vehicle speed, gear selector, clutch position sensors, fork position sensors, ATF temperature.
  - Outputs to clutch pressure solenoids, shift fork actuators, ATF pump.
  - Communicates with engine ECU via **CAN / FlexRay** for coordinated torque management.
- **Common output shaft + final drive differential** complete the drivetrain to the wheels.

**2. Working principle (3 marks)**

At any instant, only **one clutch (K1 OR K2)** is closed and transmitting torque, while the other is open. The TCU, however, **pre-engages the next gear** on the *other* layshaft and synchronizes it to the upcoming output speed *before* the shift. When the shift command is triggered, the TCU:
- Requests the **engine ECU to reduce torque** (fuel cut, throttle closure).
- Begins **opening the active clutch** and **closing the preselected clutch** in a controlled pressure overlap.
- The new gear becomes active in 200–400 ms (or 50–120 ms in performance applications), after which the *next* gear is pre-selected on the *original* layshaft — completing the cycle.

**3. Role of each subsystem (1 mark — synthesis sentence)**
The dual-clutch pack provides the **two independent torque paths**; the concentric input shafts act as the **mechanical router** to the correct gear train; and the mechatronic unit is the **decision-maker and actuator** that orchestrates seamless, overlap-controlled shifts.

**Score summary for (a)**: Construction 3 + Working 3 + Synthesis 1 = **7 marks**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**1. Power flow in 3rd gear (state before upshift) (2 marks)**

- Clutch **K1 is closed** (active).
- Clutch **K2 is open** (passive).
- Engine torque flows: Flywheel → K1 driving plate → K1 driven plate → **Hollow outer input shaft** → **3rd-gear pinion** on odd layshaft → **3rd-gear synchronizer engaged** → **Common output shaft** → Final drive → Wheels.
- **4th gear** is **preselected and synchronized** on the even layshaft, but K2 is open, so it is *carrying no torque*.

**2. Upshift 3 → 4 sequence (3 marks)**

| **Step** | **K1** | **K2** | **Engine Torque Request** | **Active Gear** | **Preselected Gear** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Before shift | Closed | Open | Normal | 3rd | 4th |
| 1. TCU requests engine torque reduction | Closed | Open | Reduced 60–80 % | 3rd | 4th |
| 2. K1 begins opening, K2 begins closing (overlap) | Partially open | Partially closed | Reduced | 3rd ↔ 4th (handover) | 5th preselected on K1 |
| 3. K1 fully open, K2 fully closed | Open | Closed | Restored | 4th | 5th |
| 4. Normal cruise in 4th | Open | Closed | Normal | 4th | 5th |

**3. Role of engine torque reduction (1 mark)**

Reducing the engine torque during the **clutch-overlap window** minimizes the **slip work dissipated as heat** in the clutches (Section 3.5, Formula 5: $E_{diss}$). It also reduces the **driveline torsional shock** transmitted to the wheels, making the shift imperceptible. The engine ECU complies over the CAN bus by reducing throttle opening and momentarily retarding/omitting fuel injection.

**4. Why DCT shifts faster than single-clutch AMT (1 mark)**

In a single-clutch AMT, the gear change is **serial**: (disengage clutch) → (deselect current gear) → (select next gear) → (synchronize) → (re-engage clutch). In a DCT, the next gear is **preselected and synchronized in parallel** with the current gear's engagement. At the shift moment, only the **clutch-to-clutch handover** is needed. This parallel pre-selection eliminates the **gear-swap and synchronization time**, making the DCT 2×–3× faster than an AMT.

**Score summary for (b)**: Pre-state 2 + Sequence 3 + Torque reduction 1 + Comparison 1 = **7 marks**.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks): DCT Clutch Engineering & Heat Management

**[KTU University Exam Model – Dec 2023 Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the **clutch torque capacity equation** under the **uniform-wear assumption** for a multi-plate wet clutch used in a wet DCT. State clearly the assumption, the pressure distribution, and the meaning of each variable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wet DCT in a sports sedan has **two multi-plate clutch packs**, each with **n = 8 friction surfaces**, outer radius $R_o = 130$ mm, inner radius $R_i = 90$ mm, coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.10$ (wet ATF), and maximum pressure $p_{max} = 1.2$ MPa. The engine delivers a peak torque of **480 Nm**. Calculate: **(i)** the torque capacity of one clutch pack, **(ii)** whether it is sufficient to transmit the engine torque with a 25 % safety margin, and **(iii)** the energy dissipated in a single aggressive upshift if the engine speed drops from **4500 rpm to 3200 rpm** with effective inertia $I_e = 0.18$ kg·m². **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> See Section 3.2 for the **complete, exhaustive derivation**. The student should reproduce:
> 1. Statement of the **uniform-wear assumption**: $p(r) \cdot r = \text{const} = p_{max} R_i$.
> 2. Derivation of pressure distribution: $p(r) = \dfrac{p_{max} R_i}{r}$.
> 3. Differential normal force: $dF_N = 2 \pi p_{max} R_i \, dr$.
> 4. Torque on a single face: $T_1 = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \mu \cdot 2 \pi p_{max} R_i \cdot r \, dr = \pi \mu p_{max} R_i (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$.
> 5. Multi-plate form: $\boxed{T_c = \pi \mu \, p_{max} \, R_i \, (R_o^2 - R_i^2) \cdot n}$.
> 6. Variable definitions: $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (dimensionless), $p_{max}$ = maximum contact pressure (Pa) at $R_i$, $R_i, R_o$ = inner/outer friction radii (m), $n$ = number of friction surfaces (integer).

**Score**: 2 marks assumption + pressure distribution, 2 marks integration, 1 mark final boxed form, 1 mark each for variable definitions and final form correctness. **Total 7 marks**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Part (i) — Torque capacity of one clutch pack**

Given: $n = 8$, $R_o = 0.130$ m, $R_i = 0.090$ m, $\mu = 0.10$, $p_{max} = 1.2 \times 10^6$ Pa.

Substitute into the boxed equation:

$$T_c = \pi \cdot \mu \cdot p_{max} \cdot R_i \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2) \cdot n$$

Compute $R_o^2 - R_i^2$:

$$R_o^2 = (0.130)^2 = 0.0169 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$R_i^2 = (0.090)^2 = 0.0081 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$R_o^2 - R_i^2 = 0.0169 - 0.0081 = 0.0088 \;\text{m}^2$$

Compute $R_i \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$:

$$0.090 \cdot 0.0088 = 0.000792 \;\text{m}^3$$

Compute $\pi \cdot \mu \cdot p_{max}$:

$$\pi \cdot 0.10 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^6 = 0.37699 \times 10^6 = 3.7699 \times 10^5$$

Compute the partial product:

$$3.7699 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.000792 = 298.58 \;\text{Nm (per surface-pair, dimensionless pre-n)}$$

Now multiply by $n = 8$:

$$T_c = 298.58 \cdot 8 = 2388.6 \;\text{Nm}$$

**Part (i) answer**: $T_c \approx 2389$ Nm per clutch pack.

**Part (ii) — Safety check**

Required torque with 25 % margin:

$$T_{req} = 1.25 \cdot T_e = 1.25 \cdot 480 = 600 \;\text{Nm}$$

Comparison:

$$T_c = 2389 \;\text{Nm} \gg T_{req} = 600 \;\text{Nm}$$

**Part (ii) answer**: The clutch pack is **vastly over-designed** (≈ 4× margin), confirming that the wet multi-plate configuration comfortably handles this engine's torque. **Yes, the design is safe.**

**Part (iii) — Energy dissipated per shift**

Convert engine speeds to angular velocities:

$$\omega_{e,pre} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot 4500}{60} = 471.24 \;\text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega_{e,post} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot 3200}{60} = 335.10 \;\text{rad/s}$$

Apply the energy dissipation formula (Section 3.5):

$$E_{diss} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot I_e \cdot (\omega_{e,pre}^2 - \omega_{e,post}^2)$$

Compute the squares:

$$\omega_{e,pre}^2 = (471.24)^2 = 222,067 \;\text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\omega_{e,post}^2 = (335.10)^2 = 112,292 \;\text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

Difference:

$$\Delta \omega^2 = 222,067 - 112,292 = 109,775 \;\text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

Compute $E_{diss}$:

$$E_{diss} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 0.18 \cdot 109,775 = 9879.75 \;\text{J} \approx 9.88 \;\text{kJ}$$

**Part (iii) answer**: $E_{diss} \approx 9.88$ kJ per aggressive upshift.

**Engineering interpretation**: ~ 10 kJ per shift is **well within the cooling capacity of a wet DCT's ATF system** (typical dissipation limit ≈ 20–40 kJ per shift for high-performance wet DCTs), but would **thermally destroy a dry DCT** — explaining the dry-vs-wet choice for this application.

**Score summary for (b)**: Part (i) numerical 3, Part (ii) analysis 1.5, Part (iii) numerical 2.5 = **7 marks**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** (Read carefully — these are the most common mark-losing mistakes):
>
> 1. **Unit consistency in clutch formulas**: Students frequently mix $R$ in **mm** and $p$ in **MPa** without converting. Always convert **mm → m** and **MPa → Pa** before substitution, or the answer is off by a factor of 1000 and **loses 2 marks** on the numerical part.
> 2. **Forgetting the $n$ factor** in multi-plate clutch torque capacity. A single plate has 2 surfaces; a multi-plate with $n = 8$ surfaces has 4 friction discs. **Always multiply by $n$ explicitly** in the formula.
> 3. **Wrong uniform-pressure vs uniform-wear formula**: KTU Module 4 expects the **uniform-wear** equation (derived in Section 3.2). The **uniform-pressure** form $T_c = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi \mu p (R_o^3 - R_i^3) n$ gives a ~ 20–30 % different numerical answer. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if the wrong assumption is stated.
> 4. **Mixing up K1 and K2 layouts**: In a 7-speed DSG, K1 = **odd** (1, 3, 5, 7, R) and K2 = **even** (2, 4, 6). Reversing these in a power-flow diagram loses **2 marks**.
> 5. **Stating "the driver controls the clutch"**: This is a **fundamental conceptual error** in a DCT — both clutches are **electro-hydraulically actuated by the mechatronic unit**. The driver only selects a gear (or the TCU does it automatically). Examiners explicitly mark this as a misconception.
> 6. **Conflating DSG and CVT**: These are entirely different architectures. DSG has **discrete gears** with two clutches; CVT has a **continuously variable belt/chain-and-pulley** mechanism. Mixing them in a comparison answer costs **1 mark**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision — bullet-point density, KTU-exam ready:**

- [ ] **DCT = Dual-Clutch Transmission**; **DSG = Direkt-Schalt-Getriebe** (Volkswagen trade name). Same architecture.
- [ ] **Two clutches, two layshafts, two concentric input shafts.** Only **one clutch + one gear** is active at a time; the other is preselected.
- [ ] **K1 → odd gears (1, 3, 5, 7, R)**; **K2 → even gears (2, 4, 6)**. Remember: **R is on K1**.
- [ ] **Concentric input shafts** = hollow outer (K1) + solid inner (K2) → minimize axial length.
- [ ] **Dry DCT** = single-plate dry clutches, ≤ 350 Nm, lower cost/weight, no fluid drag. **Wet DCT** = multi-plate in ATF, ≥ 600 Nm, higher drag but thermally robust.
- [ ] **Mechatronic module** = TCU + hydraulic block + solenoids. **No clutch pedal** in DCT — driver never operates the clutches.
- [ ] **Preselection + clutch-to-clutch swap** = the **defining feature** that makes DCT shift 2–3× faster than single-clutch AMT.
- [ ] **Engine torque reduction** (fuel cut, throttle close) during the shift → minimizes slip work $E_{diss} = \tfrac{1}{2} I_e (\omega_{pre}^2 - \omega_{post}^2)$.
- [ ] **Vehicle speed formula**: $V = \dfrac{0.377 N_e r}{i_{gear} i_{final}}$ — must be memorized cold.
- [ ] **Clutch torque (uniform-wear)**: $T_c = \pi \mu p_{max} R_i (R_o^2 - R_i^2) n$. Always convert **mm → m**, **MPa → Pa**.
- [ ] **Mechatronic inputs**: APPS, engine speed, vehicle speed, clutch position, fork position, gear selector, ATF temp, brake, CAN data.
- [ ] **Mechatronic outputs**: K1/K2 pressure solenoids, shift fork actuators, parking lock, ATF pump.
- [ ] **DCT advantages**: shift speed (50–400 ms), efficiency (92–96 %), sporty feel, no torque interruption, fuel economy.
- [ ] **DCT disadvantages**: mechatronic complexity, dry-clutch thermal limit, cost, low-speed creep shudder, repair cost.
- [ ] **Limp-home mode** in failure: typically locks gearbox in 2nd or 3rd gear; complete pressure loss = vehicle must be towed.
- [ ] **Reverse gear** is on **K1 (odd)** with an idler reversing direction.
- [ ] **DCT vs AT (torque-converter)**: DCT is more efficient but less smooth in stop-and-go traffic; AT is smoother but ~ 5–10 % less efficient.
- [ ] **DCT vs CVT**: DCT has discrete gears (sporty, efficient at high speed); CVT is seamless (smooth, but rubbery and limited torque).
- [ ] **Real-world examples to remember**: VW 7-speed DSG (DQ200 dry, DQ500 wet), Audi S-tronic, Porsche PDK, Ferrari F1 DCT, Ford PowerShift, Hyundai/Kia 8DCT.
- [ ] **Performance benchmark**: Porsche PDK shifts in ~ 50 ms; VW DQ200 in ~ 220 ms; wet DCTs typically handle 10–20 kJ per aggressive upshift.
- [ ] **KTU common 3-mark traps**: don't say "two-clutch manual", don't say "driver operates clutch", don't confuse DSG with CVT.
- [ ] **KTU common 14-mark sub-parts**: (i) construction with sketch + working, (ii) power-flow sequence for a specific shift, (iii) numerical on clutch torque / energy dissipation / vehicle speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line mnemonic for clutch assignment**:
> **"K1 is KOOL (odd) and K2 is EVEN"** — *K1 drives the ODD-numbered gears (1, 3, 5, 7, R) and K2 drives the EVEN-numbered gears (2, 4, 6).*

> [!NOTE]
> **Final study tip**: When revising, draw the **concentric-shaft power-flow sketch** from memory 3 times — this is the single most-reproduced DCT diagram in KTU question papers, and getting it right on the first attempt is worth **at least 3 marks** in any 14-mark answer.
